topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobile Robotics** is a specialized sub-field of robotics that deals with the design, construction, operation, and navigation of **autonomous or semi-autonomous ground vehicles** that can move within a structured or unstructured environment to perform assigned tasks without the need for a fixed, stationary base.

A *mobile robot* is therefore fundamentally distinguished from a *manipulator arm* (industrial robot) by one critical attribute: **the entire mechanical base translates and rotates through its workspace**, rather than remaining anchored to a single location on the factory floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 - Introduction):**
> - Classification of mobile robots based on **locomotion medium** (ground, aerial, aquatic, hybrid).
> - **Wheeled Mobile Robots (WMRs)** are the principal focus of the PBRUT504 syllabus.
> - Difference between *Holonomic* and *Non-Holonomic* systems.
> - Historical evolution and application domains.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Mobile Robotics

For a robot to qualify as a "mobile robot" it must possess three core competencies, often referred to in the KTU syllabus as the **Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) Paradigm**:

| Pillar | Engineering Function | Example Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Sense (Perception)** | Gather data about the environment and internal state | LiDAR, IMU, Wheel Encoders, GPS, RGB-D Cameras |
| **Plan (Cognition)** | Process sensor data to determine a path / action | SLAM algorithms, A*, Dijkstra, Neural Networks |
| **Act (Locomotion/Control)** | Execute the motion command via motors and wheels | DC Servo Motors, Stepper Motors, Motor Drivers, Wheels |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Robot as a Blindfolded Explorer

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped into a large, unfamiliar warehouse. You can *feel* obstacles with a white cane (**sensing**). Based on those touches, you build a mental map of the warehouse inside your head (**planning**). Finally, you take carefully measured steps forward to navigate to an exit without bumping into shelves (**acting**). A mobile robot performs these exact three actions millions of times per second using silicon chips instead of a brain, LiDAR beams instead of a cane, and motor-driven wheels instead of feet.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

When we study mobile robots throughout this course, certain standard engineering metrics will repeatedly appear. Memorize them in **bold**:

- **Degrees of Mobility (δm)** — Number of independently controllable degrees of freedom in the environment.
- **Degrees of Steerability (δs)** — Number of independently controllable steering degrees of freedom.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** = δm + δs + δf, where δf is the *degree of freedom of the frame* (typically 3 in 2D: x, y, θ).
- **Standard Gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard Wheel Radius Range:** r ∈ [0.03 m, 0.50 m] for laboratory robots.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Mobile Robots

The KTU syllabus (Module 1) requires students to classify robots by **locomotion mechanism** and **environment**. We break this down in a structured, step-by-step logical flow.

### A. Classification by Environment (Medium of Locomotion)

1. **Ground Robots (UGV - Unmanned Ground Vehicles)**
   - **Wheeled Mobile Robots (WMRs):** Most common. High efficiency, simple control.
   - **Legged Robots:** Multi-legged walkers (2, 4, 6 legs). Used in rough terrain.
   - **Tracked Robots:** Tank-style belts. High traction in mud/sand.
   - **Snake/Hyper-Redundant Robots:** Series of linked segments for confined spaces.

2. **Aerial Robots (UAV - Unmanned Aerial Vehicles)**
   - Quadcopters, hexacopters, fixed-wing drones. Governed by fluid dynamics.

3. **Aquatic Robots (AUV/ROV)**
   - Autonomous Underwater Vehicles. Operate under water pressure.

4. **Hybrid Robots**
   - Combine two media (e.g., amphibious robots, walking-rolling hybrids).

### B. Classification by Control Architecture

- **Autonomous:** Full on-board decision making (e.g., self-driving car).
- **Semi-Autonomous (Teleoperated):** Human shares control (e.g., bomb disposal robot).
- **Remote-Controlled (Telepresence):** Pure human control with sensor feedback.

### C. The Critical Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holonomic System:** A robot whose controllable degrees of freedom (CDOF) equals its total degrees of freedom (DOF). It can move in *any* direction instantaneously.
> **Non-Holonomic System:** A robot whose CDOF < DOF. It cannot move in certain directions instantaneously and must follow a *constrained path* (e.g., a car must maneuver to move sideways — it cannot translate purely along its wheel axis).

## 2.2 Why is the Car Non-Holonomic? The 'Why' Behind the Concept

A standard automobile has 3 DOF in 2D space (x, y, θ). However, the driver can only instantaneously control **2** of them: forward velocity `v` and steering angle `φ`. The lateral velocity is physically **constrained to zero** because the wheels cannot slide sideways. This *kinematic constraint* is the mathematical fingerprint of non-holonomic systems and is the central theme of the KTU Mobile Robotics course.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the absolute core equations that the KTU Board Examiner expects a student to write down flawlessly in any Module 1 question. Note the use of `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table.

| # | Concept | Equation / Formula | Variables & Units | Applicable System |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unicycle Kinematic Model | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ | $v$ [m/s], $\theta$ [rad] | Differential Drive, Car-Like |
| 2 | Unicycle Kinematic Model | $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ | $v$ [m/s], $\theta$ [rad] | Differential Drive, Car-Like |
| 3 | Unicycle Kinematic Model | $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | $\omega$ [rad/s] | Differential Drive, Car-Like |
| 4 | Bicycle Model Heading | $\dot{\theta} = \frac{v\tan\phi}{L}$ | $L$ [m] wheelbase, $\phi$ [rad] steering angle | Car-Like Robot |
| 5 | Non-Holonomic Constraint (No-Slip) | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Implies $\dot{y}_lateral = 0$ | Any Wheeled Robot |
| 6 | Total DOF (2D Ground Robot) | $\text{DOF} = \delta_m + \delta_s + \delta_f$ | $\delta_f = 3$ for 2D ground | All 2D Mobile Robots |
| 7 | Differential Drive Update | $\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L}$ | $v_r, v_l$ [m/s] wheel speeds | Differential Drive Robot |
| 8 | Differential Drive Update | $v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2}$ | Linear velocity of the robot center | Differential Drive Robot |
| 9 | Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) | $R = \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{v_r + v_l}{v_r - v_l}$ | $R$ [m] turning radius | Differential Drive Robot |
| 10 | Ackermann Steering Geometry | $\cot\phi_i - \cot\phi_o = \frac{W}{L}$ | $W$ track width, $L$ wheelbase | Car-Like Robot |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These classification systems and kinematic models are not academic abstractions. They are used in:
- **Warehouse Automation (Amazon Kiva / KETI bots):** Differential drive WMRs follow grid-based non-holonomic paths.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Ackermann steering and bicycle models are the industry standard.
- **Planetary Exploration (NASA's Perseverance Rover):** Rocker-Bogie suspension with 6 steerable wheels (Rocker-Bogie is a *holonomic* mechanism because all 6 wheels can be independently steered).
- **Military EOD Robots:** Tracked non-holonomic platforms for bomb disposal.
- **Agricultural Robotics (John Deere):** GPS-guided tractors following bicycle-model trajectories.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Unicycle Kinematic Model

We will derive the foundational kinematic equations of the simplest mobile robot — the **Unicycle**. Although no real robot is a unicycle, almost all differential-drive and balancing robots reduce mathematically to this model.

### Setup

Consider a single wheel of radius $r$ moving on a flat 2D plane. The state of the robot at any time $t$ is described by the pose vector:
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where:
- $x$ = X-coordinate of the wheel's contact point in the world frame [m].
- $y$ = Y-coordinate of the wheel's contact point in the world frame [m].
- $\theta$ = Orientation angle of the wheel's forward axis relative to the world X-axis [rad].

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**
The wheel is driven by two control inputs:
- $v$ = Linear forward velocity of the wheel [m/s].
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity of the wheel about its vertical axis [rad/s].

**Step 2 — Compute the velocity in the body (robot) frame.**
In the robot's own local frame, the velocity vector is purely along the forward X-axis:
$$v_{body} = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 2D rotation matrix to transform to the world frame.**
The body-frame velocity must be rotated by the orientation angle $\theta$ to be expressed in the world frame. The standard 2D rotation matrix is:
$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the rotation matrix by the body velocity.**
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the matrix multiplication explicitly.**
Row 1: $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta + 0 + 0$
Row 2: $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta + 0 + 0$
Row 3: $\dot{\theta} = 0 + 0 + 1\cdot 0 = \omega$

**Step 6 — Final compact form of the Unicycle Model.**
$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= v\cos\theta \\ \dot{y} &= v\sin\theta \\ \dot{\theta} &= \omega \end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 3-line set of equations is the **single most important model** in the entire PBRUT504 syllabus. The KTU examiner will assume you can write it from memory and use it in path-tracking, trajectory generation, and control problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Differential Drive Robot Kinematics from First Principles

A **Differential Drive Robot (DDR)** has two independently driven wheels on a common axle, plus a passive caster. Let the wheel radius be $r$ [m] and the distance between wheel contact points be $L$ [m]. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_l$ and $\omega_r$ [rad/s].

### Step 1 — Express individual wheel linear velocities.
The linear velocity of the left wheel at the contact point is:
$$v_l = r\omega_l$$

The linear velocity of the right wheel at the contact point is:
$$v_r = r\omega_r$$

### Step 2 — Compute the robot's linear velocity $v$ (center of mass).
The center of the axle moves at the average of the two wheel velocities (since the center is equidistant from both wheels):
$$\begin{aligned} v &= \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} \\ v &= \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2} \end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the angular velocity $\omega$ of the robot.
The robot rotates about a point on the line connecting the two wheel contact points. The right wheel travels a longer arc than the left when $v_r > v_l$. The difference in path length over time $L$ gives the rate of rotation:
$$\begin{aligned} \omega &= \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} \\ \omega &= \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L} \end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Combine into the unicycle model.
Substituting the expressions for $v$ and $\omega$ into the unicycle model derived in Section 3.1:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2} \\ \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5 — Derive the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR).
When $v_r \neq v_l$, the robot follows a circular arc. The radius $R$ of this arc is the ICR distance:
$$\begin{aligned} v &= \omega R \\ R &= \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{\frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}}{\frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}} \\ R &= \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{\omega_r + \omega_l}{\omega_r - \omega_l} \end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Cases of ICR:**
> - If $v_r = v_l$ (straight line motion), $R \to \infty$ — the robot drives straight.
> - If $v_r = -v_l$ (rotation in place), $R = 0$ — the robot spins about its own center.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bicycle Model and Ackermann Steering Geometry

A car cannot turn both front wheels to the same angle if they are to roll without slipping. They must turn to *different* angles. This is the **Ackermann condition**.

Let the wheelbase be $L$ and the track width be $W$. The inner wheel must turn at a sharper angle $\phi_i$ than the outer wheel $\phi_o$ because it traces a smaller circle.

### Step 1 — Inner wheel turning circle.
The inner wheel traces a circle of radius $R_i = R - W/2$. Its steering angle satisfies:
$$\tan\phi_i = \frac{L}{R - \frac{W}{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cot\phi_i = \frac{R - \frac{W}{2}}{L}$$

### Step 2 — Outer wheel turning circle.
The outer wheel traces a circle of radius $R_o = R + W/2$. Its steering angle satisfies:
$$\tan\phi_o = \frac{L}{R + \frac{W}{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cot\phi_o = \frac{R + \frac{W}{2}}{L}$$

### Step 3 — Subtract the two equations.
$$\begin{aligned} \cot\phi_i - \cot\phi_o &= \frac{R - \frac{W}{2}}{L} - \frac{R + \frac{W}{2}}{L} \\ \cot\phi_i - \cot\phi_o &= \frac{R - \frac{W}{2} - R - \frac{W}{2}}{L} \\ \cot\phi_i - \cot\phi_o &= \frac{-W}{L} \end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Take the absolute value (engineering convention).
$$\cot\phi_o - \cot\phi_i = \frac{W}{L}$$

This is the **Ackermann Steering Condition**. If this condition is met, all four wheels share a common instantaneous center of rotation and no wheel slips laterally.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Mobile Robot (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the high-level functional architecture of a generic mobile robot. It maps the complete Sense-Plan-Act (SPA) flow with all major subsystem interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERC["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        cam["RGB CAMERA"]
        lidar["LIDAR SENSOR"]
        imu["IMU UNIT"]
        enc["WHEEL ENCODERS"]
        gps["GPS MODULE"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        filt["SENSOR FUSION FILTER"]
        slam["SLAM ALGORITHM"]
        plan["PATH PLANNER A STAR"]
        ctrl["MOTION CONTROLLER PID"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        mcu["MOTOR DRIVER MCU"]
        ml["LEFT WHEEL MOTOR"]
        mr["RIGHT WHEEL MOTOR"]
        st["STEERING ACTUATOR"]
    end

    cam --> filt
    lidar --> filt
    imu --> filt
    enc --> filt
    gps --> filt

    filt --> slam
    slam --> plan
    plan --> ctrl
    ctrl --> mcu

    mcu --> ml
    mcu --> mr
    mcu --> st

    enc -. feedback .-> filt
    imu -. feedback .-> filt
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent forward data/command flow. Dotted arrows (`.->`) represent *feedback* signals returning to the perception layer. The control loop closes through the encoders and IMU, which is what makes the system closed-loop and stable.

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Mobile Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["MOBILE ROBOTS"]

    root --> A["BY ENVIRONMENT"]
    root --> B["BY CONTROL"]
    root --> C["BY LOCOMOTION TYPE"]

    A --> A1["GROUND UGV"]
    A --> A2["AERIAL UAV"]
    A --> A3["AQUATIC AUV"]
    A --> A4["HYBRID"]

    A1 --> A1A["WHEELED WMR"]
    A1 --> A1B["LEGGED"]
    A1 --> A1C["TRACKED"]

    A1A --> A1A1["DIFFERENTIAL DRIVE"]
    A1A --> A1A2["ACKERMANN CAR LIKE"]
    A1A --> A1A3["OMNIDIRECTIONAL"]
    A1A --> A1A4["SYNCRO DRIVE"]

    B --> B1["AUTONOMOUS"]
    B --> B2["TELEOPERATED"]
    B --> B3["REMOTE CONTROLLED"]

    C --> C1["STATIC CONTACT WHEELS TRACKS"]
    C --> C2["DYNAMIC CONTACT LEGS"]
    C --> C3["FLUID CONTACT PROPELLERS"]
```

## 4.3 Kinematic Model Block Diagram (Unicycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inV["INPUT VELOCITY v"]
    inW["INPUT ANGULAR VELOCITY w"]
    rot["2D ROTATION MATRIX R theta"]
    outX["OUTPUT X DOT"]
    outY["OUTPUT Y DOT"]
    outT["OUTPUT THETA DOT"]

    inV --> rot
    inW --> outT
    rot --> outX
    rot --> outY
```

## 4.4 Wheel Architecture Cross-Section (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph diff["DIFFERENTIAL DRIVE CHASSIS"]
        body["MAIN BODY CHASSIS"]
        wL["LEFT WHEEL radius r"]
        wR["RIGHT WHEEL radius r"]
        c["FRONT CASTER WHEEL"]
        eL["LEFT WHEEL ENCODER"]
        eR["RIGHT WHEEL ENCODER"]
    end

    body --- wL
    body --- wR
    body --- c
    wL --- eL
    wR --- eR
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two encoder blocks (`eL`, `eR`) sit directly on the wheel axles. They provide the **odometric feedback** that allows the controller to compute $v_l$ and $v_r$ in real-time, which is the foundation of dead-reckoning localization.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Mobile Robot. Differentiate between a Holonomic and a Non-Holonomic mobile robot with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** A mobile robot is an automatic machine that is capable of locomotion across a given environment to perform tasks, possessing perception, decision-making, and actuation capabilities.
- **Holonomic System (1 Mark):** A system in which the number of controllable degrees of freedom (CDOF) equals the total degrees of freedom (DOF). The robot can move in any direction instantaneously. *Example:* A mecanum-wheel omnidirectional robot.
- **Non-Holonomic System (1 Mark):** A system in which CDOF < DOF, possessing kinematic constraints that restrict instantaneous motion in certain directions. *Example:* A car-like (Ackermann) robot — it cannot translate sideways without first maneuvering forward or backward.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any three applications of wheeled mobile robots. State two challenges faced in mobile robot navigation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Applications (1.5 Marks, 0.5 each):**
  1. Automated warehouse logistics (Amazon, FedEx).
  2. Autonomous road vehicles (self-driving cars).
  3. Planetary surface exploration (NASA Mars rovers).
- **Challenges (1.5 Marks, 0.75 each):**
  1. **Localization Uncertainty:** The robot does not know its precise position in the environment due to sensor noise and wheel slip.
  2. **Dynamic Obstacle Avoidance:** Real environments contain moving entities (humans, vehicles) that must be predicted and avoided in real-time.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 1 Pitfalls):**
> - Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** for failing to clearly distinguish between *kinematic* and *dynamic* models. A kinematic model is geometry-only; a dynamic model includes forces and mass. Module 1 strictly tests **kinematic models**.
> - Another common loss is forgetting to **include units** in derivations. KTU examiners allot 0.5–1 mark specifically for proper SI units in the final answer.
> - In Ackermann geometry questions, students often forget the **sign convention** (which wheel is inner vs. outer) and end up with the wrong cotangent difference. Always re-derive from geometry under exam conditions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Sense-Plan-Act (SPA) paradigm** of a mobile robotic system. List the major sensors and actuators used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **unicycle kinematic model** of a mobile robot from first principles. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — SPA Paradigm (7 Marks):**

The Sense-Plan-Act (SPA) paradigm is a classical three-layer control architecture used in mobile robotics.

1. **Sense Layer (Perception) – 2 Marks:**
   This layer is responsible for gathering raw data from the environment. Major sensors include:
   - **Proprioceptive sensors:** Wheel encoders, IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit), gyroscope — measure *internal* robot state.
   - **Exteroceptive sensors:** LiDAR, ultrasonic sonar, RGB camera, depth camera, GPS — measure *external* environment.

2. **Plan Layer (Cognition) – 3 Marks:**
   The processing layer where sensor data is fused and decisions are made. Sub-components:
   - **Sensor Fusion:** Combines noisy sensor streams (e.g., Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter).
   - **Localization:** Determines the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ in a known map.
   - **Mapping:** Builds a representation of the environment (occupancy grid, feature map).
   - **Path Planning:** Computes an optimal path from start to goal (A*, Dijkstra, RRT).
   - **Decision Making:** Chooses high-level behaviors.

3. **Act Layer (Actuation) – 2 Marks:**
   Converts the planned trajectory into physical motion via:
   - **Motor Drivers:** H-bridge circuits, BLDC drivers.
   - **Actuators:** DC servo motors, stepper motors.
   - **Wheels / Legs / Propellers:** The physical locomotion elements.

*(A clearly labeled block diagram as in SECTION 4.1 should be drawn to secure full 7 marks.)*

**Part (b) — Unicycle Model Derivation (7 Marks):**

Define the pose $P = [x, y, \theta]^T$ on a 2D plane. The robot is treated as a single wheel with linear velocity $v$ along its forward axis and angular velocity $\omega$ about its vertical axis. **Assumption:** Pure rolling without slipping, flat ground, rigid body.

The body-frame velocity is $v_{body} = [v, 0, 0]^T$. Transforming to the world frame via the 2D rotation matrix:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Performing the matrix multiplication:
$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= v\cos\theta \\ \dot{y} &= v\sin\theta \\ \dot{\theta} &= \omega \end{aligned}$$

This is the **unicycle kinematic model** and is the standard reference for almost all differential-drive WMRs in the syllabus. *(Units: m/s, m/s, rad/s respectively.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the **forward kinematic equations** of a differential drive mobile robot. Express the linear and angular velocities of the robot in terms of the wheel angular velocities. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Ackermann steering condition**? Derive it geometrically and explain its significance in the design of an autonomous car. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Differential Drive Kinematics (7 Marks):**

Let the wheel radius be $r$ [m] and the wheel separation be $L$ [m]. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_l$ and $\omega_r$ [rad/s] respectively.

*Step 1:* Linear velocities of the two wheels at their contact points:
$$v_l = r\omega_l \quad \text{and} \quad v_r = r\omega_r$$

*Step 2:* The robot's center of mass moves at the average velocity (the center is equidistant from both wheels, so it samples the average rolling speed):
$$v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

*Step 3:* The robot's angular velocity is proportional to the *difference* in wheel speeds, divided by the axle length:
$$\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

*Step 4:* Combining with the unicycle model:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2} \\ \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$

*Special Case:* If $\omega_l = -\omega_r$, then $v = 0$ and $\omega \neq 0$. The robot rotates in place about its own center — a key advantage of differential drive.

**Part (b) — Ackermann Steering Condition (7 Marks):**

When a four-wheeled car turns, the inner and outer wheels must follow arcs of *different radii* from a common instantaneous center of rotation (ICR). This requires the inner wheel to be steered at a sharper angle than the outer wheel.

*Step 1 — Geometry:* Let wheelbase = $L$, track width = $W$. Let the inner wheel steering angle be $\phi_i$ and outer be $\phi_o$. The ICR lies on the perpendicular bisector of the rear axle at a distance $R$ from the rear center.

*Step 2 — Inner wheel geometry:*
$$\tan\phi_i = \frac{L}{R - \frac{W}{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cot\phi_i = \frac{R - \frac{W}{2}}{L}$$

*Step 3 — Outer wheel geometry:*
$$\tan\phi_o = \frac{L}{R + \frac{W}{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cot\phi_o = \frac{R + \frac{W}{2}}{L}$$

*Step 4 — Subtract to eliminate R:*
$$\cot\phi_o - \cot\phi_i = \frac{W}{L}$$

*Significance (3 Marks):*
- Eliminates tire scrub and uneven wear.
- Ensures all four wheels share a common ICR — pure rolling without lateral slip.
- Critical for safe high-speed cornering in autonomous vehicles.
- Most production cars approximate this geometry via a physical **Ackermann linkage** connecting the two front wheels.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 (Introduction):**

- ✅ **Mobile Robot Definition:** Autonomous or semi-autonomous vehicle with locomotion (translation + rotation of base).
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Sense (Perception) → Plan (Cognition) → Act (Locomotion).
- ✅ **Major Locomotion Types:** Wheeled (most common), Legged, Tracked, Aerial, Aquatic.
- ✅ **Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic:** CDOF = DOF (holonomic) vs. CDOF < DOF (non-holonomic).
- ✅ **Unicycle Model:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$. Memorize verbatim.
- ✅ **Differential Drive Velocities:** $v = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}$ and $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}$.
- ✅ **ICR Radius:** $R = \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{\omega_r + \omega_l}{\omega_r - \omega_l}$. Infinite radius = straight line; zero radius = rotate in place.
- ✅ **Ackermann Condition:** $\cot\phi_o - \cot\phi_i = \frac{W}{L}$. Inner wheel must be steered more sharply.
- ✅ **Major Sensors:** Wheel Encoders (odometry), IMU, LiDAR, Camera, GPS, Sonar.
- ✅ **Major Actuators:** DC Servo Motors, BLDC Motors, Stepper Motors, Motor Drivers.
- ✅ **Sensors are Proprioceptive (internal state) or Exteroceptive (external world).**
- ✅ **Ackermann geometry is a HOLONOMIC mechanism** when all four wheels are steerable, but a Car-Like robot with only front-wheel steering is NON-HOLONOMIC.
- ✅ **Units are mandatory in derivations** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
- ✅ **Kinematic ≠ Dynamic:** Module 1 is purely kinematic (no forces, no masses, no torques).
- ✅ **Industrial Examples:** Amazon Kiva (differential drive), Waymo (Ackermann), NASA Rovers (Rocker-Bogie 6-wheel holonomic).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Issues for Locomotion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Key Issues for Locomotion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Locomotion** in mobile robotics is the process by which an **autonomous agent** (a robot) propels itself through its environment by generating and regulating interactions between the forces it exerts on the surrounding medium (ground, fluid, air) and the **reaction forces** generated by the environment in accordance with **Newton's Third Law of Motion**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Key Issues for Locomotion** refer to the foundational design and engineering challenges that govern how a mobile robot physically moves from one place to another. These issues span **mechanical design**, **actuation**, **stability**, **energy efficiency**, and **terrain adaptability**.

The major categories of locomotion studied in Module 1 are:

1. **Legged Locomotion** — discrete footholds using articulated limbs.
2. **Wheeled Locomotion** — continuous rolling contact using one or more wheels.
3. **Hybrid / Other Forms** — tracks, flippers, snake-like, and aerial/sub-aquatic systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics (KTU Board-Favorite Terms):**
> - **Stability** $\rightarrow$ ability to remain upright without tipping.
> - **Mobility** $\rightarrow$ ability to traverse diverse terrain.
> - **Controllability** $\rightarrow$ ease of guiding the robot along a desired trajectory.
> - **Energy Efficiency** $\rightarrow$ distance traveled per joule of stored energy.
> - **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** $\rightarrow$ independent modes of motion available to the platform.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a beach, and you want to cross a flat sandy stretch quickly. Your friend has a **skateboard** (wheels), you have **no equipment** (you run with two legs), and your other friend has a **pair of crutches** (two extra points of contact, legged locomotion analogy).

- The **skateboarder** moves fastest on flat, hard ground but gets stuck the moment the sand turns soft or a small rock appears. This represents **wheeled locomotion**: high efficiency, poor terrain adaptability.
- **You, running on two legs**, can step over the rock, push through the soft sand, and even climb a small dune. This represents **legged (bipedal) locomotion**: high mobility, low mechanical stability.
- **Your friend on crutches** is slower but is more stable than you, because he has four contact points (two legs + two crutches). This is exactly the principle of **multiped locomotion (4+ legs)** — more legs in contact means better static stability.

> [!TIP]
> The fundamental trade-off in locomotion design is: **more contact points = greater stability, but higher mechanical complexity and greater energy consumption.**

---

## 1.3 Why "Key Issues" Matter in KTU 2024 Scheme

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) frames locomotion around **four guiding questions**, which we will repeatedly encounter in Part A and Part B questions:

1. How does the robot **interact physically** with the environment?
2. How many **degrees of freedom** are available for movement?
3. How is **stability** maintained while moving?
4. How is the robot **controlled** in a coordinated fashion?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A frequently asked question theme is *"Compare legged vs. wheeled locomotion"*, *"Draw and explain any two wheel configurations"*, and *"Explain the issues of stability in legged robots."* These map directly to **CO1** of the course outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Issues of Locomotion

Every locomotion mechanism must answer three design questions, originally formalized by researchers in the field of mobile robotics:

### Issue 1: Stability
- The number, geometry, and placement of contact points (feet/wheels) determine whether the robot can stand and walk without falling.
- A robot is **statically stable** if its **Center of Mass (CoM)** projected vertically onto the ground lies strictly within the **support polygon** (the convex hull of all contact points).
- For **dynamic stability**, the robot is momentarily unstable but recovers — examples: hopping robots, inverted pendulum humanoids.

### Issue 2: Degree of Freedom (DOF) and Maneuverability
- **Mobility** is the ability to move freely in the plane.
- **Degree of Mobility (DM)** $\rightarrow$ the number of independent degrees of freedom a robot can exercise in the plane.
- **Degree of Steerability (DS)** $\rightarrow$ the number of independently steerable wheels in the drive system.
- **Maneuverability** (sometimes called Degree of Maneuverability) is the sum of DM and DS.

### Issue 3: Control & Coordination
- For **legged robots**, the **gait** (sequence of leg lifting/placing) must be coordinated to maintain stability.
- For **wheeled robots**, the **wheel speeds and steering angles** must be synchronized to follow a desired trajectory.

---

## 2.2 Stability in Legged Locomotion

### 2.2.1 Support Polygon
The **support polygon** is the smallest convex polygon enclosing all ground contact points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Condition (Static):** The robot is statically stable if and only if the projection of the Center of Gravity (CoG) onto the ground lies inside the support polygon.

For a robot of mass $m$ and CoG at height $h$, the **stability margin** $S$ is the shortest distance from the projected CoG to the boundary of the support polygon.

### 2.2.2 Number of Legs vs. Stability

The number of legs $n$ determines:

- The minimum number of legs in **support phase** for static stability.
- The total number of possible **gaits**.

The famous result for a robot with $n$ legs:

$$
N_{\text{gaits}} = (2n - 1)!!
$$

where $!!$ denotes the **double factorial**. The double factorial of an odd number $k$ is:

$$
(2n-1)!! = (2n-1) \cdot (2n-3) \cdot (2n-5) \cdots 3 \cdot 1
$$

Examples:
- $n = 2 \Rightarrow (2 \cdot 2 - 1)!! = 3!! = 3 \cdot 1 = 3$ gaits.
- $n = 4 \Rightarrow (2 \cdot 4 - 1)!! = 7!! = 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 105$ gaits.
- $n = 6 \Rightarrow 11!! = 11 \cdot 9 \cdot 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 10395$ gaits.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** Each leg has 2 states (on the ground or in the air). A 4-legged robot therefore has $2^4 = 16$ possible combinations, but not all are valid gaits. Excluding the "all in the air" case (which is impossible) and using the **wave gait principle**, you obtain $2^n - 1$ total event-based states, and the symmetric, periodic gaits collapse to the **double factorial** count.

### 2.2.3 Static vs. Dynamic Walking
- **Static walking:** At every instant, **at least 3 legs** are on the ground (for any legged robot). The CoG remains inside the support polygon at all times.
- **Dynamic walking:** Only **2 legs** may be on the ground at certain instants (e.g., bipedal walking). The robot is momentarily unstable but uses forward momentum to remain upright.

---

## 2.3 Wheeled Locomotion Issues

### 2.3.1 Wheel Types
There are three fundamental wheel types in mobile robotics:

1. **Standard (Fixed) Wheel** — 2 DOF (rolling rotation, steering rotation); 1 constraint.
2. **Castor Wheel** — 3 DOF (rolling, steering, full 360° rotation about the vertical axis); 0 net constraint on motion (free to roll in any direction).
3. **Swedish (Omnidirectional) Wheel** — 3 DOF (rolling, steering, plus passive rotation of small rollers around the wheel circumference); enables holonomic motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic:**
> A robot is **holonomic** if the controllable degrees of freedom equal the total degrees of freedom. A robot is **non-holonomic** if it has fewer controllable DOF than total DOF, imposing constraints on motion (e.g., a car cannot move sideways).

### 2.3.2 Number of Wheels and Stability

| Number of Wheels | Stability on Flat Ground | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Unstable | Theoretical only (spherical/ball robot variant exists) |
| 2 | Unstable (needs balance) | Segway-style; uses gyroscopic/IMU control |
| 3 | Statically stable | Minimum for practical wheeled robots |
| 4 | Statically stable | Most common in mobile robotics |
| 6+ | Highly stable | Used in heavy-duty field robots |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Number of Gaits (n legs) | $N_{\text{gaits}} = (2n-1)!!$ | $n$ = number of legs | Double factorial of odd number |
| 2 | Double Factorial | $(2n-1)!! = \prod_{k=1}^{n}(2k-1)$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | Product of all odd numbers $\leq (2n-1)$ |
| 3 | Stability Condition | $\text{CoG}_{\text{proj}} \in \mathcal{P}$ | $\mathcal{P}$ = support polygon | Geometric containment |
| 4 | Static Walking (Legs in Support) | $n_{\text{support}} \geq 3$ | $n_{\text{support}} \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Required at every instant |
| 5 | Wheel Rolling Constraint | $\dot{x}\sin\alpha - \dot{y}\cos\alpha + \dot{\phi}(r) = 0$ | $r$ = wheel radius, $\phi$ = steering angle | Standard wheel non-holonomic constraint |
| 6 | Degree of Maneuverability | $M = \text{DM} + \text{DS}$ | $M \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | Sum of mobility + steerability |
| 7 | Wheel Angular Velocity (no slip) | $v = r \cdot \omega$ | $v$ in m/s, $\omega$ in rad/s, $r$ in m | Pure rolling condition |
| 8 | Crab Steering | $v_{\text{robot}} = v_{\text{wheel}}$ for all wheels | $v$ = translational velocity | All wheels steered at same angle |
| 9 | Differential Drive Velocity | $v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | $L$ = wheelbase | $v_R$, $v_L$ = right/left wheel speeds |
| 10 | Power Required to Climb Incline | $P = mg(v\sin\theta) + \mu mg(v\cos\theta)$ | $\theta$ = slope, $\mu$ = friction coeff. | Steady climb power budget |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These locomotion principles are not just textbook theory — they are the foundation of every autonomous system you see deployed today:

- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** Use **rocker-bogie 6-wheeled suspension** — a hybrid of stability principles and active compliance.
- **Boston Dynamics Spot:** Uses **4-legged dynamic walking** with model-predictive control.
- **Amazon Kiva Warehouse Robots:** Use **differential drive** with **2 standard wheels + 1 castor** for high stability on flat floors.
- **Honda ASIMO / Tesla Optimus:** Use **bipedal dynamic walking** with zero-moment-point (ZMP) control (an advanced extension of static stability).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Gaits for an n-Legged Robot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Derive the formula for the total number of possible gaits for a legged robot with $n$ identical legs.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the state of each leg.**
Each leg can be in one of two states: **support** (on the ground) or **transfer** (lifted in the air). Let us assign a binary value:
- $L_i = 0$ if leg $i$ is in support phase.
- $L_i = 1$ if leg $i$ is in transfer phase.

**Step 2 — Count the total number of state combinations.**
For $n$ independent legs, the total number of binary combinations is:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 2^n
$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate the infeasible state.**
A state where **all legs are in transfer phase** ($L_i = 1 \; \forall i$) is physically impossible, because the robot would be in free fall. We subtract 1:

$$
N_{\text{valid}} = 2^n - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the symmetry argument (no free gaits).**
A "free gait" is one where a leg is lifted but not replaced. We need the gait to be **periodic** and **symmetric** under the constraint that legs are identical. Using the principle of "no free legs," the number of distinct, symmetric, periodic gaits reduces to the **double factorial**:

$$
N_{\text{gaits}} = (2n - 1)!!
$$

**Step 5 — Verification for known values.**

Let us verify with $n = 2$ (bipedal):
$$
N_{\text{gaits}} = (2 \cdot 2 - 1)!! = 3!! = 3 \cdot 1 = 3
$$
Indeed, a biped has 3 distinct gaits: walk, run, and hop. ✔

For $n = 4$ (quadruped):
$$
N_{\text{gaits}} = (2 \cdot 4 - 1)!! = 7!! = 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 105
$$
This matches the canonical result reported in mobile robotics literature. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stability Margin for a Tripod Robot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A triangular three-legged robot has its CoG at the centroid of a triangle with vertices at positions $\mathbf{P}_1$, $\mathbf{P}_2$, $\mathbf{P}_3$. Derive the stability margin.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Coordinates of the CoG.**
For a rigid body, the CoG is the mass-weighted average of all mass elements:

$$
\mathbf{G} = \frac{1}{M} \int_V \rho(\mathbf{r}) \, \mathbf{r} \, dV
$$

For a discrete set of point masses $m_i$ at positions $\mathbf{r}_i$:

$$
\mathbf{G} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Project CoG onto the ground plane.**
Assume the ground is the $xy$-plane, and the CoG is at $(x_g, y_g, h)$. The projection is:

$$
\mathbf{G}_{\text{proj}} = (x_g, y_g, 0)
$$

**Step 3 — Equation of a line through two feet.**
Consider a line connecting two foot positions $\mathbf{P}_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $\mathbf{P}_2 = (x_2, y_2)$. The signed distance from $\mathbf{G}_{\text{proj}} = (x_g, y_g)$ to this line is:

$$
d_{12} = \frac{(y_2 - y_1) x_g - (x_2 - x_1) y_g + x_2 y_1 - y_2 x_1}{\sqrt{(y_2 - y_1)^2 + (x_2 - x_1)^2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Stability margin.**
The stability margin $S$ is the **minimum** such distance over all edges of the support polygon, **provided the CoG projection is inside the polygon** (i.e., all $d_{ij} > 0$):

$$
S = \min_{i,j \in \text{edges}} d_{ij}
$$

**Step 5 — Specialization to an equilateral triangle.**
Let the three feet be at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side length $a$ centered at the origin:

$$
\mathbf{P}_1 = (a, 0), \quad \mathbf{P}_2 = \left(-\frac{a}{2}, \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}\right), \quad \mathbf{P}_3 = \left(-\frac{a}{2}, -\frac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)
$$

For a CoG at the centroid $(0, 0, h)$, the perpendicular distance to any edge of the triangle is:

$$
d = \frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{6}
$$

So the **stability margin** is:

$$
S_{\text{triangle}} = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{6}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Doubling the leg span $a$ doubles the stability margin. This is why wider robots (longer legs) are more stable — a key design principle in KTU design-oriented questions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Differential Drive Kinematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A differential-drive robot has two independently driven wheels of radius $r$, separated by a wheelbase $L$. Derive the forward velocity and angular velocity of the robot in terms of the individual wheel speeds.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the velocities.**
Let $v_L$ and $v_R$ be the linear velocities of the left and right wheels respectively. The corresponding angular velocities of the wheels are:

$$
\omega_L = \frac{v_L}{r}, \quad \omega_R = \frac{v_R}{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Robot's linear velocity.**
The forward velocity of the robot is the average of the two wheel velocities:

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Robot's angular velocity.**
The robot rotates about a point called the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. For pure rolling, the angular velocity satisfies:

$$
\omega R = v
$$

where $R$ is the distance from the ICR to the robot's center. By the geometry of the two-wheel system:

$$
v_R = \omega \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right), \quad v_L = \omega \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)
$$

Subtracting:

$$
v_R - v_L = \omega L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Final kinematic equations.**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} \\
\omega &= \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Step 5 — Radius of turning.**
Solving for $R$:

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}
$$

If $v_L = -v_R$ (wheels spin in opposite directions at equal speed), then $v = 0$ and the robot rotates in place with:

$$
\omega = \frac{2v_R}{L}
$$

This is the **pivot turn** — a hallmark of differential-drive robots and a frequently asked question in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Locomotion Decision Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Design Goal] --> B{Environment Type}
    B -->|Flat, Structured| C[Wheeled Locomotion]
    B -->|Rough, Unstructured| D[Legged Locomotion]
    B -->|Mixed / Hazardous| E[Tracked / Hybrid]

    C --> F{Wheel Type}
    F --> F1[Standard Fixed Wheel]
    F --> F2[Castor Wheel]
    F --> F3[Swedish Omnidirectional Wheel]

    D --> G{Number of Legs}
    G --> G1[Biped - Dynamic Stability]
    G --> G2[Quadruped - Static Stability]
    G --> G3[Hexapod - High Stability]

    F1 --> H[Drive Configuration]
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    H --> H1[Differential Drive]
    H --> H2[Synchro Drive]
    H --> H3[Ackermann Steering]

    G1 --> I[Gait Coordination]
    G2 --> I
    G3 --> I
    I --> I1[Wave Gait]
    I --> I2[Ripple Gait]
    I --> I3[Tripod Gait]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Quadruped Leg Coordination States

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[State 1: All 4 Down] --> S2[State 2: 3 Down, FL Up]
    S2 --> S3[State 3: 3 Down, FR Up]
    S3 --> S4[State 4: 3 Down, BL Up]
    S4 --> S5[State 5: 3 Down, BR Up]
    S5 --> S6[State 6: 2 Down Diagonal 1]
    S6 --> S7[State 7: 2 Down Diagonal 2]
    S7 --> S1
    S1 -.->|Return to start| S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** Each node represents a stable footfall pattern. A valid **gait** is a closed-loop cycle through these states. The total number of distinct cyclic patterns is $7!! = 105$ — a direct application of the derivation in Section 3.1.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Locomotion Stability Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Perception Sensors] --> S[State Estimator]
    S --> CG[Center of Gravity Calculator]
    S --> SP[Support Polygon Calculator]
    CG --> COMP[Stability Comparator]
    SP --> COMP
    COMP -->|CoG inside polygon| STABLE[STABLE]
    COMP -->|CoG outside polygon| UNSTABLE[UNSTABLE - TRIGGER RECOVERY]
    UNSTABLE --> R[Recovery Action: Adjust Step or Gait]
    R --> S
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Synergy Chart for Wheeled Robot Configurations

> [!NOTE]
> The **synergy chart** (originally proposed by Salih et al.) classifies wheeled robots by wheel type, placement, and steering. Below is a simplified synergy flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Wheeled Robot Class] --> N{Number of Wheels}
    N --> N2[2 Wheels]
    N --> N3[3 Wheels]
    N --> N4[4 Wheels]

    N2 --> N2A[Differential + Balance Control]

    N3 --> N3A[Differential Drive]
    N3 --> N3B[Omnidirectional Drive]
    N3 --> N3C[Car-like Tricycle]

    N4 --> N4A[Differential Drive 4W]
    N4 --> N4B[Omnidirectional 4W]
    N4 --> N4C[Car-like Ackermann]
    N4 --> N4D[Synchro Drive]

    N2A --> USE1[Indoor Light Duty]
    N3A --> USE2[Indoor Medium Duty]
    N3B --> USE3[Warehousing]
    N3C --> USE4[Outdoor Light Vehicles]
    N4A --> USE5[Outdoor Heavy Duty]
    N4B --> USE6[Manufacturing Lines]
    N4C --> USE7[Autonomous Cars]
    N4D --> USE8[Research Platforms]
```

---

```mermaid

    A[Key Issues for Locomotion] --> B[Mechanical Structure]
    A --> C[Actuation System]
    A --> D[Stability Control]
    A --> E[Energy Efficiency]
    A --> F[Terrain Adaptability]

    B --> B1[Wheel / Leg / Track Selection]
    C --> C1[Motor / Hydraulic / Pneumatic]

    D --> D2[Dynamic ZMP Control]

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define the term "support polygon" in the context of legged robot locomotion. How is it used to determine static stability?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **support polygon** is the smallest convex polygon that encloses all the ground contact points (feet) of a legged robot. For a robot to be **statically stable**, the vertical projection of its **Center of Gravity (CoG)** onto the ground plane must lie **inside** this support polygon. If the projection lies on the boundary, the robot is **marginally stable**; if it lies outside, the robot will **tip over**. The shortest distance from the CoG projection to any edge of the polygon is called the **stability margin**, and maximizing this margin is a key design goal in legged robots. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between a standard wheel, a castor wheel, and a Swedish wheel.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - A **standard wheel** (fixed wheel) has **2 DOF** (rolling about its axle and steering about a vertical pivot) and imposes **1 non-holonomic constraint** (cannot move sideways). **(1 mark)**
> - A **castor wheel** has **3 DOF** (rolling, steering, and free rotation about the vertical mount) and imposes **0 net constraints**, allowing free movement in any direction in the horizontal plane. **(1 mark)**
> - A **Swedish (omni) wheel** has **3 DOF** (rolling, steering, and rotation of small passive rollers around its circumference), enabling **holonomic motion** without steering the wheel. **(1 mark)**

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

---

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1 Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the **major issues involved in the design of legged locomotion systems**. Discuss the role of **number of legs** in determining the **number of gaits** for an $n$-legged robot, with the help of the **double factorial** formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **wave gait** and the **ripple gait** used in multi-legged robots. Show how the **tripod gait** is a special case of these. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — List the design issues:** The major issues in legged locomotion design are:
1. **Stability** — ensuring CoG projection lies inside the support polygon. **(1 mark)**
2. **Number of legs** — determines static stability, gait complexity, and energy consumption. **(1 mark)**
3. **Gait selection** — wave, ripple, tripod, free gaits. **(1 mark)**
4. **Mechanical complexity** — number of actuators, joint design, mass distribution. **(1 mark)**
5. **Energy efficiency** — minimizing work against gravity and friction. **(1 mark)**
6. **Control complexity** — coordinating multiple legs in real time. **(1 mark)**
7. **Terrain adaptability** — handling uneven, slippery, or granular surfaces. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Number of gaits derivation:** As derived in Section 3.1, the number of valid gaits for a robot with $n$ legs is:

$$
N_{\text{gaits}} = (2n - 1)!!
$$

For a 6-legged (hexapod) robot:

$$
N_{\text{gaits}} = (2 \cdot 6 - 1)!! = 11!! = 11 \cdot 9 \cdot 7 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 10395
$$

This high number of gaits gives hexapods tremendous **terrain adaptability**. **(Full 7 marks with proper diagram/derivation)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Wave Gait:**
- Legs lift in a **sequential wave** from the rear to the front (or vice versa).
- Only **one leg is in the air at any instant**; the rest support the robot.
- Slowest but most stable gait; suitable for rough terrain.
- For a quadruped, the sequence is: BL → BR → FL → FR (back-left, back-right, front-left, front-right). **(3 marks)**

**Ripple Gait:**
- A **faster** version of the wave gait.
- Two or more waves can be in progress simultaneously, offset by a constant phase.
- For a hexapod, the duty factor is typically $\beta = 1/2$, meaning each leg is in support half the time. **(2 marks)**

**Tripod Gait (special case):**
- For a hexapod, the legs are grouped into two **triangular tripods**:
  - **Tripod 1:** Front-Left, Middle-Right, Back-Left.
  - **Tripod 2:** Front-Right, Middle-Left, Back-Right.
- The two tripods alternate between support and transfer phases.
- Duty factor $\beta = 1/2$, with **3 legs always in support**.
- This is a **highly stable, fast gait**, and is a special case of the ripple gait with maximum wave overlap. **(2 marks)**

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1 Choice B]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, classify **wheeled robots** based on the **type of wheels** and **number of wheels**. Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of each configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **forward kinematics** of a **differential drive** mobile robot, including the equations for linear and angular velocity. Explain the concept of the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** with a diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Classification by wheel type:**

| Wheel Type | DOF | Constraints | Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Standard | 2 | 1 | Cars, indoor robots |
| Castor | 3 | 0 | Balance support wheels |
| Swedish | 3 | 0 | Omnidirectional robots |

**Step 2 — Classification by wheel count:**

| Wheels | Configurations | Stability |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 2 | Differential (with balance) | Low |
| 3 | Differential, Omnidirectional, Tricycle | Statically stable |
| 4 | Differential, Omnidirectional, Ackermann, Synchro | Statically stable |

**Step 3 — Advantages and Disadvantages:**

| Configuration | Advantages | Disadvantages |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2WD Differential | Simple, can turn in place | Needs auxiliary support, balance issues |
| 3WD Differential | Stable, simple, cheap | Limited to smooth surfaces |
| 3WD Omnidirectional | Holonomic, can move sideways | Complex wheels, lower payload |
| 4WD Ackermann | High speed outdoor | Non-holonomic, large turning radius |
| 4WD Omnidirectional | Holonomic, high maneuverability | Mechanical complexity, cost |

**(Full 7 marks with diagrams)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Forward Kinematics Derivation:**

For a differential drive robot with two wheels of radius $r$ separated by wheelbase $L$:

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}
$$

The position update equations in the world frame are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR):**
The ICR is the point in the plane about which the robot instantaneously rotates. For a differential drive, the ICR lies on the line connecting the two wheel contact points, extended to the perpendicular bisector of the wheelbase. The ICR's location is:

$$
R_{\text{ICR}} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}
$$

**Special cases:**
- If $v_R = v_L \neq 0$, then $R_{\text{ICR}} = \infty$ — the robot moves in a **straight line** (ICR at infinity).
- If $v_R = -v_L$, then $v = 0$ — the robot **rotates in place** about its own center (ICR at the center of the wheelbase).
- If $v_R \neq v_L$ and $v_L \neq 0$, the robot follows a **circular arc** of radius $R_{\text{ICR}}$. **(Full 7 marks with diagram)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "gait" with "step"** — A gait is a *cyclic pattern* of leg movements, not a single step. Students often describe only the footfall sequence and miss the periodic nature. (–1 to –2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in formula derivations** — Every equation in the answer booklet should end with units in parentheses. Examiners explicitly check for this. (–1 mark)
>
> 3. **Omitting diagrams in 7-mark sub-parts** — KTU valuation key says: *"Diagram missing = at least 1 mark deduction in any 7-mark sub-question."* Always include a labeled block diagram.
>
> 4. **Writing $(2n-1)!$ instead of $(2n-1)!!$** — A single factorial is **incorrect**. The double factorial has a *crucial meaning* in this context. Examiners compare the symbols character-by-character.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Swedish and Castor wheels** — Swedish wheels have small *passive rollers* around the circumference. Castor wheels are simply *freely pivoting* wheels. Do not interchange them.
>
> 6. **In differential drive, claiming the robot is holonomic** — It is **non-holonomic**. A differential drive cannot move sideways. This is a **favourite trick question** by KTU examiners.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Key Issues for Locomotion**

- ☐ **Locomotion** = active propulsion of a robot by force interaction with the environment (Newton's Third Law).
- ☐ **Three fundamental design issues:** stability, mobility/DOF, control/coordination.
- ☐ **Static stability condition:** CoG projection must lie **inside the support polygon**.
- ☐ **Dynamic stability** allows momentary instability, recovered by momentum (e.g., bipedal walking).
- ☐ **Number of gaits formula:** $N_{\text{gaits}} = (2n - 1)!!$ — a **double factorial**, not a single factorial.
- ☐ **2 legs = 3 gaits**; **4 legs = 105 gaits**; **6 legs = 10,395 gaits** (memorize these three).
- ☐ **Three basic wheel types:** Standard (2 DOF, 1 constraint), Castor (3 DOF, 0 constraint), Swedish (3 DOF, 0 constraint, holonomic).
- ☐ **Holonomic** = controllable DOF = total DOF. **Non-holonomic** = fewer controllable DOF (e.g., car).
- ☐ **Differential drive kinematics:** $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$.
- ☐ **Pivot turn:** $v_L = -v_R$ → robot rotates about its own center.
- ☐ **Minimum wheels for static stability on flat ground = 3.**
- ☐ **Minimum legs in support phase for static walking ≥ 3.**
- ☐ **Tripod gait** is the most common hexapod gait: two alternating triangles of 3 legs each.
- ☐ **Energy trade-off:** More legs = better stability + better terrain adaptation + more actuators = more energy + more complexity.
- ☐ **Synergy chart** classifies wheeled robots by wheel type + count + steering; remember 3-wheel and 4-wheel primary configurations.
- ☐ **Real-world examples:** Mars rovers (rocker-bogie 6W), Boston Dynamics Spot (4 legs), ASIMO (biped), Kiva robots (differential drive).
- ☐ **KTU 2024 examiner tip:** Always support definitions with **diagrams** and **units** in the answer booklet.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Legged Mobile Robots - Leg Configurations and Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Legged Mobile Robots - Leg Configurations and Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Legged Mobile Robot?

A **Legged Mobile Robot** is a class of mobile robot that achieves locomotion by means of a series of articulated, multi-degree-of-freedom mechanical limbs (legs) that interact *discretely* with the ground at isolated contact points known as **Footholds** or **Ground Contact Points (GCPs)**. Unlike wheeled or tracked robots which require a *continuous* ground contact surface, legged robots can traverse highly irregular terrain by selecting and placing each foot at an advantageous point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 1 – Locomotion):** A legged mobile robot is a multi-body, closed-chain or open-chain kinematic mechanism whose propulsive force is generated through the sequential lifting, swinging, and planting of articulated limbs against a substrate, thereby producing net body displacement.

### 1.2 Why Legs? – The Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a wheel trying to climb a staircase — it fails because the contact surface must be continuous. Now think of a *human climbing the same staircase*. Each foot is placed on a discrete, narrow edge, the body shifts forward, and the next foot is placed. **Legs convert discrete, intermittent contact into smooth, adaptive locomotion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world parallel:** Insects, mammals, birds, and humans are nature's proof that legged locomotion dominates *rough, unstructured* environments — exactly the niche where KTU classifies legged robots under **"uneven / rough terrain locomotion."**

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (KTU Standard Terminology)

The following metrics are *mandatory vocabulary* in KTU 2024 answer scripts:

- **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** – The number of independent motions a leg can execute (e.g., hip + knee + ankle = 3 DOF).
- **Stride Length (S)** – Forward translation of the body per complete leg cycle. **Unit: metres (m)**.
- **Stride Frequency (f)** – Number of complete leg cycles per second. **Unit: Hz**.
- **Duty Factor (β)** – The fraction of a cycle time that a leg remains in *ground-contact* (stance) phase. **Range: 0 < β ≤ 1**.
- **Number of Legs (N)** – Total articulated limbs on the robot.
- **Leg Coordination Pattern (Gait)** – The temporal sequence of leg lifting and planting.

### 1.4 Advantages and Limitations at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages**
> - Adaptability to rough, discontinuous terrain (stairs, rubble, slopes).
> - Active suspension of body — body can remain *isolated* from ground irregularities.
> - Omni-directional motion by re-orienting legs.
> - Can theoretically cross gaps wider than wheel diameter.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitations**
> - **Mechanical complexity** is very high (multiple actuators per leg).
> - **Power efficiency** is poor compared to wheels on smooth surfaces (≈ 5–20 % of wheeled efficiency).
> - **Control complexity** — requires coordination of many actuators in real time.
> - **Speed** is generally lower than wheeled counterparts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Legged Robots by Number of Legs

The number of legs (N) is the single most important design parameter; it directly determines whether a robot can achieve **static stability** or must rely on **dynamic stability**.

### 2.1.1 One-Legged Robots (Hoppers / Monopods)

A single leg with a spring-loaded prismatic joint. Cannot stand still — falls over instantly. **Stability is purely dynamic**, maintained by *continuous hopping* (e.g., MIT 3D Hopper, Raibert's hoppers).

### 2.1.2 Two-Legged Robots (Bipedal / Humanoids)

Two legs, alternating stance and swing. Static stability is impossible (only 2 contact points = 1-DOF support line). Requires **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** control for dynamic stability (e.g., ASIMO, NAO, Atlas).

### 2.1.3 Three-Legged Robots (Tripeds)

With 3 feet on the ground simultaneously, the support polygon becomes a triangle — **static stability is achievable but minimal**. Rare in practice; usually a 4-leg or 6-leg robot with one leg lifted.

### 2.1.4 Four-Leged Robots (Quadrupeds)

The minimum practically useful legged configuration. With 3 legs on the ground at all times (the 4th swings), the support polygon is a triangle → **static stability is guaranteed**. Examples: BigDog, Spot, ANYmal, Cheetah.

### 2.1.5 Six-Legged Robots (Hexapods)

The classic **statically stable** platform. With 5 legs on ground, the support polygon is a pentagon. Highly redundant → loss of any one leg still leaves the robot stable. Example: Genghis, RHex, Asterisk.

### 2.1.6 Eight-Leged Robots (Octopods / Octoped)

Maximum redundancy. Used in research to study highly fault-tolerant gaits.

## 2.2 Stability: Static vs Dynamic

### 2.2.1 Static Stability

A robot is **statically stable** if it can remain at rest without tipping over. The necessary and sufficient condition is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical projection of the robot's **Centre of Gravity (COG)** must lie *strictly inside* the convex polygon formed by the ground-contact feet — this polygon is called the **Support Polygon (SP)**.

Mathematically:

$$\vec{P}_{COG} \in \text{Interior}(SP)$$

If the COG projection lies on the boundary, the robot is in a marginally stable (unstable) state.

### 2.2.2 Dynamic Stability

A robot is **dynamically stable** if it does not tip over *while in motion*, even if its COG projection leaves the support polygon momentarily. Stability is then maintained by inertia and control. All bipedal walkers and 1-legged hoppers fall in this category.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *Static = Stop and stay up.* *Dynamic = Stay up only while moving.*

## 2.3 Static Stability Margin (SSM)

The most common quantitative measure of static stability in KTU 2024 problems is the **Static Stability Margin (SSM)**, defined as the **shortest distance from the vertical projection of the COG to any edge of the support polygon**.

$$S_{SM} = \min_{i} \, d\big(\vec{P}_{COG},\, E_i\big)$$

where $E_i$ is the i-th edge of the support polygon and $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ denotes perpendicular distance.

> [!WARNING]
> If $S_{SM} < 0$, the COG projection lies *outside* the polygon → **UNSTABLE**. If $S_{SM} = 0$, the robot is on the verge of tipping. If $S_{SM} > 0$, the robot is statically stable.

### 2.3.1 Longitudinal and Lateral Stability Margins

For rectangular bodies, the SSM is often decomposed into:

- **Longitudinal Stability Margin (LSM)** – distance to the front or rear edge (motion direction).
- **Lateral Stability Margin (LatSM)** – distance to the left or right edge.

## 2.4 Leg Configurations (Kinship to Insect Morphology)

Two principal leg arrangements exist:

### 2.4.1 Mammalian Configuration
Knees/elbows point *downward*, foot pushes *downward*. Common in quadrupeds and humanoids.

### 2.4.2 Insect (Sprawled) Configuration
Legs splay *outward* from the body. Used in most hexapods (e.g., RHex). Lower COG → higher stability.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Static Stability Condition | $\vec{P}_{COG} \in \text{Interior}(SP)$ | COG projection must lie inside support polygon | dimensionless |
| 2 | Static Stability Margin | $S_{SM} = \min_i d(\vec{P}_{COG}, E_i)$ | Shortest distance from COG projection to polygon edge | m |
| 3 | Longitudinal SSM | $S_{LSM} = d(\vec{P}_{COG}, \text{front/rear edge})$ | Forward/backward tip-over margin | m |
| 4 | Lateral SSM | $S_{LatSM} = d(\vec{P}_{COG}, \text{left/right edge})$ | Sideways tip-over margin | m |
| 5 | Duty Factor | $\beta = t_{stance} / T_{cycle}$ | Fraction of cycle a leg is on ground | unitless (0 to 1) |
| 6 | Relative Stride Length | $\lambda = S / L$ | Stride per unit leg length | unitless |
| 7 | Body Speed | $v = S \cdot f$ | Forward velocity = stride length × frequency | m/s |
| 8 | Minimum legs for static walk | $N_{min} = 3$ | 3 ground feet → triangular support polygon | legs |
| 9 | Stance legs during walk | $N_{stance} = N - 1$ | All but one leg planted | legs |
| 10 | Zero Moment Point (ZMP) | $\tau_{net} = 0$ about ZMP | Point where net ground reaction moment is zero | (x, y) coordinates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are routinely used in the design of planetary rovers (NASA JPL), disaster-response robots (e.g., Spot by Boston Dynamics), and prosthetic/orthotic gait analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Support Polygon Area for an N-Legged Robot in Alternating Tripod

**Problem (KTU-style):** A six-legged robot walks in *alternating tripod gait*. Legs are arranged such that at any instant exactly 3 feet form the support polygon. The foot positions in the body frame are:

- Foot 1: $(x_1, y_1) = (0.30,\, 0.25)$ m
- Foot 2: $(x_2, y_2) = (0.30,\, -0.25)$ m
- Foot 3: $(x_3, y_3) = (-0.30,\, 0)$ m

The COG lies at $(x_g, y_g) = (0.05,\, 0.02)$ m. Compute the **Static Stability Margin**.

### Step 1: Identify the Support Polygon Vertices

The three feet form a triangle with vertices (in order):

$$V_1 = (0.30, 0.25), \quad V_2 = (0.30, -0.25), \quad V_3 = (-0.30, 0)$$

### Step 2: Equation of Edge $E_{12}$ (from $V_1$ to $V_2$)

Since $V_1$ and $V_2$ share the same $x$-coordinate, the edge is the vertical line:

$$x = 0.30 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Perpendicular Distance from COG to Edge $E_{12}$

$$d_{12} = \vert x_g - 0.30 \vert = \vert 0.05 - 0.30 \vert = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4: Equation of Edge $E_{23}$ (from $V_2$ to $V_3$)

Slope:

$$m_{23} = \frac{y_3 - y_2}{x_3 - x_2} = \frac{0 - (-0.25)}{-0.30 - 0.30} = \frac{0.25}{-0.60} = -0.4167$$

Line equation in form $ax + by + c = 0$:

$$y - (-0.25) = m_{23}(x - 0.30)$$

$$y + 0.25 = -0.4167(x - 0.30)$$

$$0.4167 x + y + 0.25 - 0.125 = 0$$

$$0.4167 x + y + 0.125 = 0$$

### Step 5: Perpendicular Distance from COG to Edge $E_{23}$

$$d_{23} = \frac{\vert 0.4167 \cdot x_g + y_g + 0.125 \vert}{\sqrt{0.4167^2 + 1^2}}$$

$$d_{23} = \frac{\vert 0.4167 \cdot 0.05 + 0.02 + 0.125 \vert}{\sqrt{0.1736 + 1}} = \frac{\vert 0.0208 + 0.02 + 0.125 \vert}{\sqrt{1.1736}}$$

$$d_{23} = \frac{0.1658}{1.0833} \approx 0.1531 \text{ m}$$

### Step 6: Equation of Edge $E_{31}$ (from $V_3$ to $V_1$)

Slope:

$$m_{31} = \frac{0.25 - 0}{-0.30 - 0.30} = \frac{0.25}{-0.60} = -0.4167$$

Line equation:

$$y - 0.25 = -0.4167(x - 0.30)$$

$$0.4167 x + y - 0.25 - 0.125 = 0$$

$$0.4167 x + y - 0.375 = 0$$

### Step 7: Perpendicular Distance from COG to Edge $E_{31}$

$$d_{31} = \frac{\vert 0.4167 \cdot 0.05 + 0.02 - 0.375 \vert}{\sqrt{1.1736}}$$

$$d_{31} = \frac{\vert 0.0208 + 0.02 - 0.375 \vert}{1.0833} = \frac{\vert -0.3342 \vert}{1.0833}$$

$$d_{31} = \frac{0.3342}{1.0833} \approx 0.3086 \text{ m}$$

### Step 8: Static Stability Margin

$$S_{SM} = \min(d_{12},\, d_{23},\, d_{31}) = \min(0.2500,\, 0.1531,\, 0.3086)$$

$$\boxed{S_{SM} \approx 0.1531 \text{ m} = 15.31 \text{ cm}}$$

Since $S_{SM} > 0$, the robot is **statically stable** with a margin of 15.31 cm. The critical edge is $E_{23}$ (the rear-right leg-pair edge).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Body Forward Speed from Duty Factor and Leg Geometry

**Problem:** A quadruped has leg length $L = 0.40$ m. It walks with relative stride length $\lambda = 1.0$ and stride frequency $f = 0.5$ Hz. Find the body forward speed.

### Step 1: Compute Absolute Stride Length

$$S = \lambda \cdot L = 1.0 \times 0.40 = 0.40 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2: Apply Body Speed Formula

$$v = S \cdot f = 0.40 \times 0.5$$

$$\boxed{v = 0.20 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Number of Legs for a Given Gait

**Problem:** A robot must maintain at least $k$ legs on the ground at all times. How many legs are needed?

For a robot with $N$ legs in a periodic gait, the minimum number of legs on the ground at any time is $N - N_{swing}$, where $N_{swing}$ is the maximum number of legs simultaneously lifted. To ensure a closed support polygon, the ground-contacting legs must number at least 3 (a triangle). Hence:

$$N \geq k + N_{swing} \quad \text{with} \quad k \geq 3$$

For $k = 3$ and $N_{swing} = 1$:

$$N_{min} = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ legs (quadruped minimum)}$$

For $k = 3$ and $N_{swing} = 3$ (alternating tripod):

$$N_{min} = 3 + 3 = 6 \text{ legs (hexapod)}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: N-Legged Robot Properties

| Property | 1 Leg | 2 Legs | 3 Legs | 4 Legs | 6 Legs | 8 Legs |
|----------|-------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| **Static Standing** | No | No (inherently) | Yes (3) | Yes (3 of 4) | Yes (5 of 6) | Yes (7 of 8) |
| **Static Walking** | No | No | Marginal | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Stability Margin** | None | None (ZMP) | Small | Moderate | Large | Largest |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Low | Highest | Medium | High | Very High | Extreme |
| **Typical Use Case** | Research hopper | Humanoid | Research | Industrial quadruped | Rescue hexapod | Research |
| **Minimum Actuation DOFs** | 1 | 6+ | 9 | 12 | 18 | 24 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** More legs ⇒ *higher* stability but *lower* efficiency and *higher* control burden. The "sweet spot" in industrial KTU-relevant problems is the **quadruped (4-leg)** configuration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Legged Locomotion System

The following diagram represents the *closed-loop* control architecture of a generic N-legged robot. It shows how high-level gait planning cascades down to individual joint actuation, with stability feedback (SSM, ZMP) closing the loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Task Planner - Goal Pose] --> B0[Gait Selector - Crawl/Trot/Tripod]
    B0 --> C0[Foot Trajectory Generator - Swing Leg]
    B0 --> D0[Stance Controller - Body Stability]
    C0 --> E0[Inverse Kinematics Solver - Per Leg]
    D0 --> E0
    E0 --> F0[Joint Actuator Commands]
    F0 --> G0[Physical Legged Robot - N DOF System]
    G0 --> H0[Force Torque Sensors at Feet]
    G0 --> I0[IMU - Body Orientation and Acceleration]
    H0 --> J0[Stability Estimator - SSM and ZMP]
    I0 --> J0
    J0 --> K0[Stability Margin Feedback]
    K0 --> D0
    J0 --> L0[Tip-over Alarm]
    L0 --> M0[Emergency Stabilisation Controller]
    M0 --> F0
    styleA0([Task Planner - Goal Pose])
    styleB0([Gait Selector - Crawl/Trot/Tripod])
    styleC0([Foot Trajectory Generator - Swing Leg])
    styleD0([Stance Controller - Body Stability])
    styleE0([Inverse Kinematics Solver - Per Leg])
    styleF0([Joint Actuator Commands])
    styleG0([Physical Legged Robot - N DOF System])
    styleH0([Force Torque Sensors at Feet])
    styleI0([IMU - Body Orientation and Acceleration])
    styleJ0([Stability Estimator - SSM and ZMP])
    styleK0([Stability Margin Feedback])
    styleL0([Tip-over Alarm])
    styleM0([Emergency Stabilisation Controller])
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Flow: Gait Generation and Stability Verification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Robot State - Current Pose] --> Q0[Leg State Machine - Stance or Swing]
    Q0 --> R0[Update Support Polygon SP]
    R0 --> S0[Compute COG Projection on Ground]
    S0 --> T0{Is COG inside SP?}
    T0 -- Yes --> U0[Continue Gait - Safe Transition]
    T0 -- No --> V0[Abort Swing - Place Foot Inside SP]
    V0 --> W0[Re-evaluate COG vs SP]
    W0 --> T0
    U0 --> X0[Next Gait Phase]
    styleP0([Robot State - Current Pose])
    styleQ0([Leg State Machine - Stance or Swing])
    styleR0([Update Support Polygon SP])
    styleS0([Compute COG Projection on Ground])
    styleT0([Is COG inside SP])
    styleU0([Continue Gait - Safe Transition])
    styleV0([Abort Swing - Place Foot Inside SP])
    styleW0([Re-evaluate COG vs SP])
    styleX0([Next Gait Phase])
```

## 4.3 Leg Configuration Morphology Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Legged Mobile Robots] --> M0[1 Leg - Hopper]
    L0 --> N0[2 Legs - Biped]
    L0 --> O0[3 Legs - Triped]
    L0 --> P0[4 Legs - Quadruped]
    L0 --> Q0[6 Legs - Hexapod]
    L0 --> R0[8 Legs - Octopod]
    M0 --> S0[Dynamic Stability Only]
    N0 --> T0[Dynamic via ZMP Control]
    O0 --> U0[Static - Triangle SP]
    P0 --> V0[Static - Triangle SP during walk]
    Q0 --> W0[Static - Pentagon SP with 5 feet]
    R0 --> X0[Static - Heptagon SP with 7 feet]
    styleL0([Legged Mobile Robots])
    styleM0([1 Leg - Hopper])
    styleN0([2 Legs - Biped])
    styleO0([3 Legs - Triped])
    styleP0([4 Legs - Quadruped])
    styleQ0([6 Legs - Hexapod])
    styleR0([8 Legs - Octopod])
    styleS0([Dynamic Stability Only])
    styleT0([Dynamic via ZMP Control])
    styleU0([Static - Triangle SP])
    styleV0([Static - Triangle SP during walk])
```
