```mermaid
    styleX0([Static - Heptagon SP with 7 feet])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In every flowchart, follow the arrows from the *Task Planner* (top) downward to the *physical robot*, and then loop back through the *sensors* and *stability estimator*. This is the canonical **sense → plan → act** cycle for legged locomotion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 3 Marks
**Define the Support Polygon of a legged robot. State the condition for static stability of a legged robot in terms of the support polygon.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Support Polygon (SP)** of a legged robot is defined as the *convex hull of all ground-contact points* (foot positions) projected onto the horizontal ground plane. Geometrically, it is the smallest convex polygon that contains all the feet currently in stance phase.

**Condition for Static Stability:** A legged robot is statically stable if and only if the vertical projection of its **Centre of Gravity (COG)** onto the ground plane lies *strictly within the interior* of the support polygon. Mathematically, $\vec{P}_{COG}^{ground} \in \text{Interior}(SP)$. If the projection lies on the boundary, the robot is marginally unstable; if outside, it is unstable and will tip over.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 3 Marks
**Distinguish between static stability and dynamic stability of a legged robot. Give one example of a robot that requires dynamic stability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Stability | Dynamic Stability |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Definition** | Robot remains stable *at rest* without falling | Robot is stable *only while in motion* |
| **COG Condition** | COG projection must be inside SP at *all times* | COG projection may leave SP momentarily |
| **Stabilising Agent** | Gravity, geometry | Inertia, active control |
| **Speed** | Usually slow | Usually fast |
| **Example** | Hexapod in tripod gait | Biped (NAO, ASIMO), Monopod hopper (Raibert hopper) |

**Example:** The **ASIMO humanoid robot** requires dynamic stability because, with only 2 feet on the ground, the support polygon degenerates to a line — static stability is impossible.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - *Never* compute SSM by averaging distances — it is the **minimum** of perpendicular distances to the polygon edges.
> - *Always* state the COG projection condition explicitly *before* writing the formula — many students skip the verbal justification and lose 1–2 marks.
> - *Always* convert units consistently (e.g., cm → m) before substituting into formulas.
> - For biped questions, do not confuse **ZMP** with **COG projection** — ZMP is the point where the *net ground reaction moment* vanishes, not the COG itself.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]**

A **four-legged robot** is standing in a square stance with feet at the corners of a square of side $a = 0.6$ m, centred at the origin in the body frame. The robot's COG is at height $h = 0.5$ m above the ground, and an external load shifts the COG horizontally to a point $(x_g, y_g) = (0.15,\, 0.10)$ m.

**(i)** Draw the support polygon and indicate the COG projection. **(2 marks)**
**(ii)** Compute the **Longitudinal Stability Margin (LSM)** and the **Lateral Stability Margin (LatSM)**. **(3 marks)**
**(iii)** Determine if the robot is statically stable. If yes, what is the **Static Stability Margin (SSM)**? **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Support Polygon (2 marks):**
Feet at $(\pm 0.30, \pm 0.30)$ m → support polygon is a square of side 0.6 m centred at origin. The COG projection at $(0.15, 0.10)$ lies inside this square (clearly within $-0.30 \leq x \leq 0.30$ and $-0.30 \leq y \leq 0.30$).

**Step 2 — LSM and LatSM (3 marks):**
- LSM = distance to nearest front/rear edge = $0.30 - 0.15 = 0.15$ m
- LatSM = distance to nearest left/right edge = $0.30 - 0.10 = 0.20$ m

**Step 3 — SSM and Stability (2 marks):**
- SSM = $\min(0.15, 0.20) = 0.15$ m
- Since SSM > 0, the robot is **statically stable** with margin 0.15 m. The critical edge is the front (or rear) edge, $x = 0.30$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]**

With reference to legged robots, explain the following gaits for a **quadruped**, with neat timing diagrams of leg contact (1 = stance, 0 = swing): **(i) Crawl gait, (ii) Trot, (iii) Pace, (iv) Bound**. Comment on the stability of each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

A **gait** is a *temporal coordination pattern* of leg lift-off and foot placement. Each leg alternates between **Stance Phase (1)** and **Swing Phase (0)**.

**(i) Crawl Gait:** Only one leg is in swing at any time; the other three remain in stance. Duty factor β ≈ 0.75. Sequence example (FL, FR, HL, HR):

| Leg | t1 | t2 | t3 | t4 |
|-----|----|----|----|----|
| FL  | 1  | 0  | 1  | 1  |
| FR  | 1  | 1  | 0  | 1  |
| HL  | 1  | 1  | 1  | 0  |
| HR  | 0  | 1  | 1  | 1  |

Stability: 3 legs always on ground → **statically stable**, very robust. Slowest gait.

**(ii) Trot:** Diagonal pairs move together (FL+HR together, FR+HL together). β ≈ 0.5.

| Leg | t1 | t2 |
|-----|----|----|
| FL  | 1  | 0  |
| FR  | 0  | 1  |
| HL  | 0  | 1  |
| HR  | 1  | 0  |

Stability: Only 2 legs on ground → **dynamically stable** (ZMP must be controlled). Fast gait.

**(iii) Pace:** Lateral pairs move together (FL+FR together, HL+HR together). β ≈ 0.5. Used by camels. Stability: dynamic; prone to rolling.

**(iv) Bound:** Front pair and rear pair act in unison (FL+FR swing, then HL+HR swing). β ≈ 0.5. Stability: dynamic; high pitching motion. Used by cheetahs/hares.

> **Stability Summary:** Crawl = static; Trot, Pace, Bound = dynamic (require active ZMP/IMU control).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option B (Internal Choice)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]**

Explain the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** concept for bipedal walking. Why is ZMP critical for a biped? Show the relationship between COG and ZMP. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Definition (3 marks):** The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is the point on the ground where the *net moment of the ground reaction force* is *zero*. Equivalently, it is the point about which the resultant ground reaction force produces no horizontal moment.

**Why critical for a biped (2 marks):** During walking, the biped is supported on a single foot for part of the cycle. The support polygon is then a *foot-sized* area. For the foot not to tip over, the ZMP must remain *inside* the foot's contact area at all times. If the ZMP reaches the foot edge, the foot begins to rotate (tip over) — the biped falls. Hence ZMP is the *stability criterion* for bipedal walking.

**Relationship to COG (2 marks):** The ZMP is *not* the same as the COG projection. In dynamic motion:

$$x_{ZMP} = x_{COG} - \frac{h \cdot \ddot{x}_{COG}}{g + \ddot{z}_{COG}}$$

When the body is at rest or in quasi-static motion ($\ddot{x}_{COG} \approx 0$), the ZMP coincides with the COG projection. During fast walking, however, the ZMP *lags* the COG projection; the biped controller must keep ZMP inside the support polygon by *shifting the body and modulating the foot placement*.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]**

A **hexapod** uses an **alternating tripod gait**. The robot body is rectangular, 0.4 m long and 0.3 m wide. Each leg's foot is placed at the four corners of the body projection plus 0.20 m lateral offset, alternating between two tripods. Compute:

**(i)** The area of the support polygon when one tripod is in stance. **(2 marks)**
**(ii)** The maximum permissible COG offset in the longitudinal direction for static stability. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** Whether the robot remains stable if a leg of the stance tripod slips by 0.05 m laterally. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Foot positions (2 marks):**
Tripod 1 feet (in body frame, metres):
- $F_1 = (0.20,\, 0.15)$, $F_2 = (0.20,\, -0.15)$, $F_3 = (-0.20,\, 0)$

Support polygon = triangle with vertices above. Area using the shoelace formula:

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \vert x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \vert$$

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \vert 0.20(-0.15 - 0) + 0.20(0 - 0.15) + (-0.20)(0.15 - (-0.15)) \vert$$

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \vert 0.20(-0.15) + 0.20(-0.15) + (-0.20)(0.30) \vert$$

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \vert -0.03 - 0.03 - 0.06 \vert = \frac{1}{2} (0.12)$$

$$\boxed{A = 0.06 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum longitudinal COG offset (2 marks):**
The longitudinal half-length of the support polygon along the body's $x$-axis (from rear vertex $x = -0.20$ to front vertex $x = 0.20$) is $0.20$ m. Therefore the maximum longitudinal COG offset for static stability is:

$$\boxed{\Delta x_{max} = 0.20 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Slip analysis (3 marks):**
Suppose $F_1$ slips laterally by 0.05 m, becoming $F_1' = (0.20,\, 0.20)$. New polygon has vertices:
- $F_1' = (0.20,\, 0.20)$
- $F_2 = (0.20,\, -0.15)$
- $F_3 = (-0.20,\, 0)$

The new triangle is still well-defined. Its longitudinal extent is unchanged ($x \in [-0.20, 0.20]$). Its lateral extent is now $y \in [-0.15, 0.20]$. The COG (originally at body centre) projects to $(0, 0)$, which lies inside the new triangle because $0 \in [-0.20, 0.20]$ (longitudinal) and $0 \in [-0.15, 0.20]$ (lateral). Therefore the robot **remains statically stable** after the slip.

> **Stability margin after slip (verification):** Shortest distance from $(0,0)$ to the new triangle's edges. The critical edge is now the rear vertex connection. Quick estimate: $\text{SSM} \approx 0.12$ m. Positive → **stable**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Legged robots** use discrete foot contacts — ideal for rough, unstructured terrain.
- **Wheeled vs Legged trade-off:** wheels win on smooth ground (efficiency); legs win on rough ground (adaptability).
- **Number of legs classification:** 1 (hopper), 2 (biped), 3 (triped), 4 (quadruped), 6 (hexapod), 8 (octopod).
- **Minimum number of legs for static walking = 4** (because at least 3 legs must be on the ground → triangle → closed support polygon).
- **Static stability** ⇔ COG projection is *inside* the support polygon.
- **Dynamic stability** ⇔ robot is stable only while in motion; uses **ZMP** (bipeds) or inertia (hoppers).
- **Static Stability Margin (SSM)** = shortest distance from COG projection to any polygon edge.
  - SSM > 0 → stable; SSM = 0 → critical; SSM < 0 → unstable.
- **LSM and LatSM** are the longitudinal and lateral components of SSM for rectangular bodies.
- **Duty factor (β):** fraction of cycle in stance; β > 0.5 → crawl (static); β = 0.5 → trot, pace, bound (dynamic).
- **Common quadruped gaits:** Crawl (static, slow), Trot (diagonal pairs, dynamic), Pace (lateral pairs, dynamic), Bound (front/rear pairs, dynamic, fastest).
- **Hexapod tripod gait:** two tripods of 3 legs alternate; statically very stable.
- **ZMP** is the ground point of zero net moment — the *true* stability criterion for bipeds, *not* the COG projection.
- **COG vs ZMP:** they coincide under quasi-static motion; they differ under acceleration.
- **Stability → complexity trade-off:** more legs ⇒ greater stability but exponentially more actuators and control burden.
- **Insect (sprawled) legs** splay outward → lower COG → higher stability; **mammalian legs** point downward → higher COG → less inherent stability.
- **Real-world examples:** BigDog/Spot (quadruped, dynamic), RHex (hexapod, static), ASIMO/Atlas (biped, ZMP-controlled), Raibert Hopper (1-leg, dynamic).
- **Examiner's mantra:** *always state the COG-projection-inside-SP condition before applying the SSM formula.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheeled Mobile Robots - Wheeled Locomotion: The Design Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wheeled Mobile Robots - Wheeled Locomotion: The Design Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Wheeled Locomotion** refers to the use of wheels as the primary mechanism for a mobile robot to propel itself across a surface. It is the most efficient and widely adopted form of locomotion in unstructured indoor and structured outdoor environments because rolling friction is significantly lower than sliding friction.

**The Design Space** of a wheeled mobile robot (WMR) is the multi-dimensional parameter space that encompasses all possible mechanical configurations a robot can adopt. It is characterized by four primary axes:

1. **Stability** — Static vs. Dynamic
2. **Number of wheels** — 2-wheel, 3-wheel, 4-wheel, etc.
3. **Wheel type/Geometry** — Standard fixed, steerable, castor, Swedish, Ball/Spherical
4. **Drive center / Turn center geometry** — Differential, Ackermann, Synchronous, Omnidirectional

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Think of the design space as a "menu" at a car-customization showroom. Before you build a robot, you must decide: How many wheels (2 like a bicycle, 4 like a car)? What kind of wheels (shopping cart castors that swivel freely, or fixed bike wheels)? Will the robot stand still without falling over (static stability), or only remain upright while moving (dynamic stability, like a Segway)? The combination of these choices defines the robot's mechanical personality, cost, agility, and terrain capability. The "design space" is the complete universe of all these valid combinations.

### 1.3 Why the Design Space Matters in Mobile Robotics

Wheeled locomotion dominates practical robotics because wheels:
- Don't require complex balance controllers for most cases.
- Offer high energy efficiency (rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_r \approx \mathbf{0.001}$ to $\mathbf{0.01}$ on hard surfaces).
- Are mechanically simple, cheap, and manufacturable.
- Allow precise odometric measurement (essential for SLAM and dead reckoning).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Rolling friction coefficient | $\mu_r$ | $\mathbf{0.001}$–$\mathbf{0.05}$ |
| Wheel radius (indoor) | $r$ | $\mathbf{0.03 \text{ m}}$–$\mathbf{0.1 \text{ m}}$ |
| Static stability margin | $S$ | $\geq 0$ (positive = stable) |
| Center of Gravity height | $h$ | Critical for tip-over analysis |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Axes of the Wheeled Locomotion Design Space

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus identifies the design space as a 4-axis framework. Every WMR is a point in this 4D space.

#### Axis 1: Stability

- **Static Stability:** The robot can remain upright *at rest* without active control. The vertical projection of the Center of Gravity (CoG) must lie within the **support polygon** formed by the wheel-ground contact points.
  - **Condition:** $S_{static} > 0$
  - Example: 4-wheeled car, 3-wheeled differential drive robot.

- **Dynamic Stability:** The robot is *only* stable while in controlled motion. At rest, it would tip over.
  - **Condition:** $S_{static} \leq 0$, but $S_{dynamic} > 0$ (balance is actively maintained).
  - Example: Two-wheeled Segway-style balancing robot, unicycle.

#### Axis 2: Number of Wheels ($N$)

- **2 wheels:** Usually dynamically stable (e.g., Segway). Compact but balance-critical.
- **3 wheels:** Minimum for static stability on flat ground (triangle = minimal polygon). Popular for indoor service robots.
- **4 wheels:** Most common. Excellent load capacity, redundancy.
- **$\geq 5$ wheels:** Specialized (e.g., Mars rovers — high terrain adaptability, redundancy if one wheel fails).

#### Axis 3: Wheel Type / Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> **Five canonical wheel types** in the design space:

1. **Standard Fixed Wheel:** Single axis of rotation (rolling). No steering.
2. **Steerable (or Swedish) Standard Wheel:** Two axes — rolling + steering about a vertical axis.
3. **Castor Wheel:** Offset rolling axis from the steering axis. The wheel pivots freely.
4. **Swedish Wheel (Mecanum-style):** Passive rollers around the circumference, allowing lateral motion. **Non-holonomic** if oriented correctly.
5. **Ball/Spherical Wheel:** Omni-directional contact. Requires special suspension (rare in practice).

#### Axis 4: Drive / Turning Center Geometry

- **Differential Drive:** Two independently driven wheels; turning is achieved by speed difference.
- **Ackermann Steering (Car-like):** Inner wheel turns more sharply than outer wheel to avoid tire slip.
- **Synchronous Drive:** All wheels rotate and steer in unison (e.g., some hospital delivery robots).
- **Omnidirectional Drive:** Uses Swedish or spherical wheels for full 3-DOF planar motion.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Static stability condition | $S = d_{CoG, edge} > 0$ | CoG projection must lie inside support polygon |
| Support polygon area | $A_{poly} = \frac{1}{2} \sum (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i)$ | Shoelace formula for contact points |
| Wheel angular velocity → Linear velocity | $v = r \omega$ | Pure rolling, no slip |
| Differential drive kinematics | $\begin{aligned} v &= \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} \\ \omega &= \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} \end{aligned}$ | $L$ = wheelbase, $r$ = wheel radius |
| Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) | $R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$ | Radius of turning circle |
| Ackermann condition | $\cot \delta_i - \cot \delta_o = \frac{W}{L}$ | $W$ = track width, $\delta_i, \delta_o$ = inner/outer steer angles |
| Tip-over stability margin | $S_{tip} = \frac{d_{support}}{h_{CoG}}$ | Ratio of support radius to CoG height |
| Non-holonomic constraint (car-like) | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Lateral velocity = 0 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** In numerical problems, examiners *always* expect the unit and the sign convention to be stated. A formula without units will lose 0.5–1 mark.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design space framework is used in industry to:
- **Rapidly prototype** AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) in warehouses (KIVA/Amazon Robotics).
- **Select wheel configurations** for planetary rovers (NASA's Mars rovers use a rocker-bogie with 6 steerable wheels).
- **Trade off** between maneuverability, payload, cost, and control complexity.
- **Determine holonomy:** A key question — "Can this robot reach any $(x,y,\theta)$ configuration from any other?" This dictates the entire motion planning strategy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Static Stability Margin (Tip-Over Analysis)

**Problem Setup:** Consider a 3-wheeled robot forming an equilateral triangle of wheel contact points with side length $L$. The CoG is at height $h$ above the centroid. Derive the tip-over stability margin.

**Step 1 — Locate the centroid of the support polygon.**
For an equilateral triangle with side $L$, the centroid is at the geometric center. The perpendicular distance from the centroid to any side (the inradius) is:

$$
d_{support} = \frac{L}{2\sqrt{3}}
$$

This distance is the *shortest* distance from the CoG vertical projection to the polygon edge. If the CoG projection is exactly on the edge, the robot is on the verge of tipping.

**Step 2 — Define the tip-over stability margin.**
The static stability margin is the ratio of the support radius to the CoG height. A higher ratio means a more tip-resistant robot:

$$
S_{tip} = \frac{d_{support}}{h_{CoG}} = \frac{L}{2\sqrt{3} \cdot h_{CoG}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**
If $S_{tip} \geq 0.5$, the robot is considered highly stable (e.g., low-CoG industrial AGVs). If $S_{tip} < 0.1$, the robot is marginally stable and may tip during sharp turns or when carrying a tall payload.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Differential Drive Forward & Angular Velocity

**Problem Setup:** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r$ and wheelbase (distance between wheels) $L$. The right and left wheels have angular velocities $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$. Derive the robot's linear and angular velocities.

**Step 1 — Linear velocity of each wheel (pure rolling).**
The linear velocity of the right wheel contact point is $v_R = r \omega_R$ and similarly $v_L = r \omega_L$.

**Step 2 — Linear velocity of robot center.**
The robot's center moves at the average of the two wheel speeds (since the center lies midway between the wheels):

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity of robot.**
The two wheels trace two circles about the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR). The difference in their linear speeds equals $L \cdot \omega$:

$$
v_R - v_L = L \omega \implies \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Special case: Pure rotation in place.**
Set $v = 0$. This requires $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ (wheels spin in opposite directions at the same speed). The robot rotates about its own center at:

$$
\omega = \frac{2 r \omega_R}{L}
$$

This is a hallmark of differential drive — **zero-radius turning**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Ackermann Steering Geometry

**Problem Setup:** A car-like robot has wheelbase $L$ (front-to-rear axle) and track width $W$ (left-to-right wheel). During a turn of radius $R$ to the center of the rear axle, derive the relationship between the inner and outer steering angles.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the turn.**
The center of the rear axle traces a circle of radius $R$. The inner front wheel must turn at a *larger* angle than the outer front wheel to avoid tire scrub.

**Step 2 — Inner wheel turning radius.**
The distance from the ICR to the inner front wheel is $R - W/2$. The steering angle satisfies:

$$
\tan \delta_i = \frac{L}{R - \frac{W}{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Outer wheel turning radius.**

$$
\tan \delta_o = \frac{L}{R + \frac{W}{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $R$ to get the Ackermann condition.**

$$
\cot \delta_i - \cot \delta_o = \frac{W}{L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Ackermann steering condition**, a guaranteed KTU exam favorite. A steering linkage that satisfies this exactly produces no slip on the front tires during a steady turn.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing a Configuration

| Configuration | Stability | Min. Wheels | Holonomy | Maneuverability | Cost | Control Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential (2 driven + 1 castor) | Static | 3 | Non-holonomic | High (zero-radius turn) | Low | Low |
| Ackermann (car-like) | Static | 4 (2 steer + 2 drive) | Non-holonomic | Medium (no zero-radius) | Medium | Medium |
| Synchronous | Static | 3 or 4 | Non-holonomic | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Omnidirectional (Mecanum) | Static | 3 or 4 | **Holonomic** (3-DOF) | Maximum (lateral motion!) | High | High |
| Two-wheeled balancing | Dynamic | 2 | Non-holonomic | High (zero-radius) | Low | **Very High** |
| Tracked (tank-like) | Static | $\geq 2$ tracks | Non-holonomic | Low (terrain king) | Medium | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Design Space — Conceptual Map

The following block diagram visualizes the **four-axis design space** that defines every wheeled mobile robot.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["WHEELED LOCOMOTION DESIGN SPACE"] --> B["AXIS 1 STABILITY"]
    A --> C["AXIS 2 WHEEL COUNT"]
    A --> D["AXIS 3 WHEEL TYPE"]
    A --> E["AXIS 4 DRIVE GEOMETRY"]

    B --> B1["STATIC STABLE CoG inside polygon"]
    B --> B2["DYNAMIC STABLE balance via control"]

    C --> C1["2 wheels Segway"]
    C --> C2["3 wheels minimum static"]
    C --> C3["4 wheels standard car"]
    C --> C4["6 wheels Mars rover"]

    D --> D1["STANDARD FIXED single axis"]
    D --> D2["STEERABLE rolling plus steering"]
    D --> D3["CASTOR offset pivot"]
    D --> D4["SWEDISH passive rollers"]
    D --> D5["SPHERICAL omni contact"]

    E --> E1["DIFFERENTIAL two wheel speed diff"]
    E --> E2["ACKERMANN car like steering"]
    E --> E3["SYNCHRONOUS all wheels same"]
    E --> E4["OMNIDIRECTIONAL full DOF"]
```

### 4.2 Differential Drive Kinematic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT omega_R omega_L"] --> B["WHEEL CONTACT r"]
    B --> C["vR r omega_R"]
    B --> D["vL r omega_L"]
    C --> E["LINEAR VELOCITY v"]
    D --> E
    C --> F["ANGULAR VELOCITY omega"]
    D --> F
    E --> G["ROBOT POSE x y theta"]
    F --> G
```

### 4.3 Wheel Type Comparison (Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W["FIVE CANONICAL WHEEL TYPES"] --> W1["STANDARD FIXED: 1 DOF rolling"]
    W --> W2["STEERABLE: 2 DOF rolling plus steering"]
    W --> W3["CASTOR: 2 DOF rolling plus free pivot"]
    W --> W4["SWEDISH: 2 DOF rolling plus lateral passive"]
    W --> W5["SPHERICAL: 2 to 3 DOF ball contact"]
```

### 4.4 Stability Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S["ROBOT STABILITY"] --> S1["STATIC STABLE"]
    S --> S2["DYNAMIC STABLE"]

    S1 --> S1A["CoG projection inside support polygon"]
    S1 --> S1B["Example: 4 wheel car"]

    S2 --> S2A["Requires active balance control"]
    S2 --> S2B["Example: 2 wheel Segway"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the "Design Space" of a wheeled mobile robot. List its four primary axes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The design space of a WMR is the multi-dimensional parameter space of all valid mechanical configurations. The four primary axes are:
1. **Stability** (static vs. dynamic)
2. **Number of wheels** (2, 3, 4, 6, …)
3. **Wheel type** (standard, steerable, castor, Swedish, spherical)
4. **Drive center geometry** (differential, Ackermann, synchronous, omnidirectional)

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for listing 4 axes + 1 mark for brief elaboration.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between static stability and dynamic stability in mobile robots with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Static stability:** The robot is stable at rest without any active control because the CoG projection lies within the wheel-contact support polygon. *Example: 4-wheeled car.*
- **Dynamic stability:** The robot is stable only while in motion; an active controller maintains balance. *Example: Two-wheeled Segway.*

*(1 mark per distinction + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Suggested]**

**(a)** Explain the **five canonical wheel types** used in wheeled mobile robots with neat functional descriptions. Discuss the trade-off between standard wheels and Swedish wheels in terms of maneuverability and mechanical complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.3$ m. The right and left wheel angular velocities are $\omega_R = 4$ rad/s and $\omega_L = 2$ rad/s respectively. Compute:
   (i) The robot's linear velocity $v$
   (ii) The robot's angular velocity $\omega$
   (iii) The radius of the instantaneous center of rotation $R$
   (iv) The time taken to complete a full circle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
*(For full 7 marks, structure your answer as a short table.)*

| # | Wheel Type | Degrees of Freedom | Key Feature | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard Fixed | 1 (rolling) | Simplest; no steering | Bicycle rear wheel |
| 2 | Steerable Standard | 2 (rolling + steering) | Active control of heading | Car front wheel |
| 3 | Castor | 2 (rolling + free pivot) | Self-aligning; passive | Office chair wheel |
| 4 | Swedish (Mecanum) | 2 (rolling + lateral passive rollers) | Allows lateral slip | KIVA/Amazon AGV |
| 5 | Spherical / Ball | 2–3 (ball contact) | Full omni-contact | Ballbot research platforms |

**Trade-off:** Standard wheels are mechanically simple, cheap, and energy-efficient, but they enforce non-holonomic constraints. Swedish wheels offer holonomic-like (or full holonomic, depending on configuration) motion — the robot can translate sideways — but at the cost of higher mechanical complexity, vibration from passive rollers, and reduced payload capacity. *(Award 2 marks for the trade-off discussion.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.3$ m, $\omega_R = 4$ rad/s, $\omega_L = 2$ rad/s.

**(i) Linear velocity:**
$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times (4 + 2)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 6}{2} = \mathbf{0.15 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(ii) Angular velocity:**
$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.05 \times (4 - 2)}{0.3} = \frac{0.05 \times 2}{0.3} = \frac{0.1}{0.3} = \mathbf{0.333 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

**(iii) Radius of ICR:**
$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.15}{0.333} = \mathbf{0.45 \text{ m}}
$$

(Alternative direct formula: $R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L} = \frac{0.3}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{2} = 0.15 \times 3 = 0.45$ m ✓)

**(iv) Time for a full circle:**
The circumference is $2\pi R = 2\pi \times 0.45 = 2.827$ m. Time $T = \frac{2\pi R}{v} = \frac{2.827}{0.15} = \mathbf{18.85 \text{ s}}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is using $L$ where the question gives the *wheelbase* in mm (e.g., 300 mm). Always convert to SI units (m) *before* computing. Also, the angular velocity must carry the sign — if $\omega_L > \omega_R$, the robot turns the *opposite* way. Loss of sign convention = −1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model Paper, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, describe the **Ackermann steering geometry**. Derive the relationship between the inner and outer steering angles of a car-like mobile robot with wheelbase $L$ and track width $W$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-wheeled mobile robot has a square wheel-base of side $0.4$ m. The center of gravity is at the geometric center at a height of $0.2$ m. Determine:
   (i) The support polygon area.
   (ii) The shortest distance from the CoG projection to any polygon edge.
   (iii) The tip-over stability margin $S_{tip}$ and comment on the robot's stability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
*(Refer to the derivation in Section 3.3 above. Expected answer: a free-body diagram showing the ICR, front-left and front-right steered wheels, and the rear axle. Then the final relation:)*
$$
\cot \delta_i - \cot \delta_o = \frac{W}{L}
$$
*(1 mark for diagram, 3 marks for derivation steps, 1 mark for final condition, 2 marks for a short discussion of why this condition prevents tire slip.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: Square footprint, side $a = 0.4$ m, $h = 0.2$ m.

**(i) Support polygon area (a square):**
$$
A_{poly} = a^2 = (0.4)^2 = \mathbf{0.16 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**(ii) Shortest distance from center to edge:**
For a square, the distance from the centroid to any side is half the side length:
$$
d_{support} = \frac{a}{2} = \frac{0.4}{2} = \mathbf{0.2 \text{ m}}
$$

**(iii) Tip-over stability margin:**
$$
S_{tip} = \frac{d_{support}}{h_{CoG}} = \frac{0.2}{0.2} = \mathbf{1.0}
$$

**Comment:** Since $S_{tip} = 1.0 \geq 0.5$, the robot is **highly stable** against tip-over. It can tolerate significant lateral accelerations and uneven loading. A value of exactly 1.0 indicates that the CoG height equals the support radius — the design is at the boundary of acceptable stability. For a sturdier platform, lowering the CoG (e.g., placing heavy batteries low) or widening the wheelbase would be advisable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse "support polygon" with the "convex hull" — for a convex arrangement of wheels, they coincide, but for a non-convex or 6-wheel rocker-bogie configuration, the polygon may be more complex. Always sketch the wheel contact points and connect them. Forgetting the unit on the stability margin is a frequent 0.5-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ The **Design Space** has 4 axes: Stability, Wheel Count, Wheel Type, Drive Geometry.
- ✅ **Static stability** ⟹ CoG projection inside the support polygon. **Dynamic stability** ⟹ requires active control.
- ✅ **Minimum wheels for static stability** on flat ground = 3 (triangle).
- ✅ **Differential drive:** $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$, $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$.
- ✅ **Ackermann condition:** $\cot \delta_i - \cot \delta_o = \frac{W}{L}$ — prevents tire slip in turns.
- ✅ **Differential drive allows zero-radius turning** (spin in place) — a key advantage over Ackermann.
- ✅ **Mecanum / Swedish wheels** ⟹ holonomic motion ⟹ can translate sideways; high cost and complexity.
- ✅ **Tip-over stability margin:** $S_{tip} = d_{support} / h_{CoG}$. Higher is better. Industrial AGVs aim for $S_{tip} \geq 0.5$.
- ✅ **Non-holonomic constraint** for car-like / differential robots: lateral velocity = 0 at the wheel contact.
- ✅ **Synonyms to know:** WMR = Wheeled Mobile Robot, AGV = Automated Guided Vehicle, ICR = Instantaneous Center of Rotation, CoG = Center of Gravity.
- ✅ Always **state units** in numerical answers — examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
- ✅ Always **mention the assumption** (e.g., "assuming pure rolling, no slip") in derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheel Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wheel Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Wheel Design** in mobile robotics is the systematic engineering process of selecting, dimensioning, and configuring wheels such that a mobile robot achieves the required traction, manoeuvrability, stability, and power efficiency for its intended operational terrain and task profile. It encompasses the geometric definition of the wheel (radius $r$, width $b$, diameter $D$), the type of wheel (standard, castor, Swedish, mecanum, spherical), the contact mechanics between the wheel and ground, and the wheel arrangement on the chassis.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, wheel design is treated as a sub-domain of **locomotion system design**, where the wheel is the *actuated point of contact* between the robot and its environment. Every wheel contributes one **degree of freedom (DOF)** in the direction of its rolling axis, and the combined degrees of freedom determine the robot's **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** and overall mobility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Wheel design covers: classification of wheels, wheel geometry, traction and slip analysis, design of standard and special wheels (castor, Swedish, mecanum), wheel configuration on chassis, and selection criteria for indoor/outdoor robots. Expect 14-mark derivation/numerical questions on ICR, slip ratio, and wheel torque.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Wheel Design

A mobile robot wheel is simultaneously a **mechanical component**, a **tribological interface**, and a **kinematic constraint**. A good wheel design must balance three conflicting demands:

| Pillar | Concern | Design Driver |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Load-bearing, structural strength | Material, rim design, spoke/axle |
| **Tribological** | Friction, wear, contact patch | Rubber compound, tread pattern, durometer |
| **Kinematic** | Rolling without slip, manoeuvrability | Diameter, width, axle placement |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Wheel

Think of a bicycle wheel on a smooth, dry road versus a wet, muddy trail.

- On **dry asphalt**, a thin, hard, smooth tyre rolls with almost no deformation. The contact patch is small, rolling resistance is low, but cornering grip is high because the rubber is hard and sticky.
- On **wet mud**, a wide, soft, knobby tyre deforms and grips irregularly. The contact patch is large, rolling resistance is high, but the wheel refuses to slip.

A mobile robot engineer faces the same trade-off every day. A warehouse AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) uses small, hard rubber wheels for energy efficiency on flat concrete, while a Mars rover uses large, wide, flexible wheels to climb over rocks and sand without slipping. **Wheel design is the art of picking the right trade-off for the right environment.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant in Wheel Design:**
> The **coefficient of friction $\mu$** between the tyre rubber and the ground surface is the single most important parameter. Typical values: rubber on dry concrete $\mu \approx 0.8$–$1.0$, rubber on wet tile $\mu \approx 0.3$–$0.5$, and rubber on sand $\mu \approx 0.4$–$0.6$.

## 1.4 Why Wheel Design Matters in Mobile Robotics

- **Energy efficiency**: A poorly designed wheel may consume 30–50% more battery power due to rolling resistance.
- **Manoeuvrability**: The wheel type (e.g., mecanum vs. differential) defines the entire motion capability of the robot.
- **Stability**: A robot with a poorly placed centre of gravity (CoG) relative to its wheelbase can tip over during turns.
- **Mission success**: The NASA Mars rovers' mission life is critically dependent on wheel durability — wheel degradation was a real concern on *Curiosity* and *Perseverance*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Wheels

Mobile robot wheels are classified into **four fundamental types** based on the degrees of freedom they allow at the contact point.

### Type 1 — Standard (Fixed) Wheel

- **Rotation about the axle** (driven axis): allowed.
- **Rotation about the contact point** (steering): not allowed in basic form, but allowed in steered standard wheels.
- **Rotation about the rolling axis (vertical in normal motion)**: not allowed.

A standard wheel constrained to move on a plane has **2 degrees of freedom** and imposes **1 non-holonomic constraint**.

### Type 2 — Castor Wheel

- Rotation about the axle: allowed.
- Rotation about a vertical kingpin (offset from contact point): allowed.
- This **offset $d$** (kingpin-to-contact distance) makes the wheel passive — it self-aligns with the direction of motion.

A castor wheel imposes **1 non-holonomic constraint** but provides only 1 DOF (the steering is passive, not actuated).

### Type 3 — Swedish Wheel (Off-Centered Wheel)

- A standard wheel with **small passive rollers** mounted around its circumference, oriented at $90°$ to the main rolling direction.
- Allows sliding in the **lateral direction** with low friction.
- Reduces the non-holonomic constraint from 1 to 0 — making it an **omnidirectional** wheel.

### Type 4 — Spherical / Ball Wheel

- A sphere in contact with the ground; can rotate about any axis.
- Truly omnidirectional; complex mechanical implementation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The number of non-holonomic constraints a wheel imposes is inversely proportional to its manoeuvrability. Standard (1) > Castor (1) > Swedish (0) > Spherical (0). Fewer constraints = more manoeuvrable.

## 2.2 Wheel Geometry — Critical Parameters

For each wheel on a mobile robot, the following parameters must be specified:

1. **Radius $r$** (m): The distance from the centre of the axle to the contact patch.
2. **Diameter $D = 2r$** (m).
3. **Width $b$** (m): The lateral width of the tyre contact.
4. **Contact patch length $l_c$** (m): The longitudinal length of the tyre footprint.
5. **Contact patch area $A_c$** ($m^2$): $A_c = b \times l_c$.
6. **Wheel load $W$** (N): The normal force pressing the wheel onto the ground.
7. **Tyre pressure $p$** (Pa): $p = W / A_c$ for a rigid wheel; for a pneumatic tyre, $p$ is the inflation pressure.
8. **Kingpin offset $d$** (m): Distance from kingpin axis to contact point (for castor wheels).

## 2.3 Rolling Kinematics — The Fundamental Relation

When a wheel of radius $r$ rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) without slipping on a horizontal surface, the linear velocity of the wheel's centre is:

$$v = r \cdot \omega$$

This is the most fundamental relation in mobile robot wheel design. It links the rotational dynamics of the actuator (motor) to the translational motion of the robot.

## 2.4 Slip and Skid

Two failure modes must be prevented:

- **Slip (longitudinal)**: The wheel rotates faster than the ground would suggest — the wheel spins without gaining forward motion. Occurs when traction force required exceeds $\mu W$.
- **Skid (lateral)**: The wheel slides sideways relative to the rolling direction. Occurs during sharp turns or on low-friction surfaces.

The **slip ratio $s$** is defined as:

$$s = \frac{r\omega - v}{\max(r\omega, v)}$$

For pure rolling, $s = 0$. For full slip, $s = 1$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = r\omega$ | Linear velocity from wheel rotation | m/s |
| 2 | $\tau = F \cdot r$ | Torque required to deliver traction force $F$ | N·m |
| 3 | $F_{traction,max} = \mu W$ | Maximum traction force before slip | N |
| 4 | $P = \tau \omega = F v$ | Power required at the wheel | W |
| 5 | $s = \frac{\vert r\omega - v \vert}{\max(r\omega, v)}$ | Slip ratio (use $\vert$ for absolute value) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $A_c = b \cdot l_c$ | Contact patch area | $m^2$ |
| 7 | $p = W / A_c$ | Average contact pressure | Pa |
| 8 | $N = \mu_s W$ | Static friction / stiction limit | N |
| 9 | $F_{rr} = C_{rr} W$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 10 | $\omega_{ICR} = v / R$ | Robot's angular velocity about ICR (radius $R$) | rad/s |
| 11 | $M = nW_{wheel} = W_{total}$ | Static load balance (sum of wheel loads = robot weight) | N |
| 12 | $\tau_{motor} \geq \tau_{wheel} / G$ | Motor torque after gearbox ratio $G$ | N·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In the KTU formula sheet table, always use the **engineering notation** $r\omega$ (not $r \times \omega$) and explicitly state units. Marks are awarded for correct units.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva robots)**: Use small diameter (~10 cm) drive wheels with high-friction silicone tread on a smooth warehouse floor. The wheel design prioritises energy efficiency and precise stop-and-go motion.
- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance)**: Use large diameter (~50 cm) flexible aluminium wheels with cleats. The wheel design prioritises obstacle climbing and durability on rocky terrain.
- **Autonomous Cars (Waymo, Tesla)**: Standard pneumatic tyres with sophisticated suspension — wheel design balances comfort, road grip, and energy efficiency at highway speeds.
- **Humanoid Robots (ASIMO, Atlas)**: Use small compliant wheels hidden in feet for dynamic balance — wheel design must permit instantaneous torque changes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wheel Torque from Tractive Effort

**Problem Setup:** A mobile robot of mass $m = 50$ kg is to be driven on a horizontal surface by two driven wheels (differential drive). Each wheel has radius $r = 0.10$ m. The coefficient of friction between the wheel and ground is $\mu = 0.8$. Find the maximum tractive force each wheel can deliver, the corresponding wheel torque, and the maximum robot acceleration.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the wheel load.**
For a symmetric two-wheel drive robot, the total weight is shared between the two driven wheels and possibly some idler/castor wheels. For simplicity, assume the two driven wheels each carry half the robot's weight:

$$W_{wheel} = \frac{m g}{2} = \frac{50 \times 9.81}{2} = \frac{490.5}{2} = 245.25 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute maximum tractive force (traction limit).**
The maximum tractive force a wheel can deliver without slipping is limited by friction:

$$F_{traction,max} = \mu \cdot W_{wheel} = 0.8 \times 245.25 = 196.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the wheel torque required.**
The tractive force acts at the contact patch, which is at a distance $r$ from the wheel axle. The motor must apply a torque $\tau$ such that $\tau / r \geq F_{traction,max}$:

$$\tau_{wheel} = F_{traction,max} \cdot r = 196.2 \times 0.10 = 19.62 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum acceleration of the robot.**
The total tractive force from both wheels is $2 F_{traction,max} = 392.4$ N. Newton's second law gives:

$$a_{max} = \frac{2 F_{traction,max}}{m} = \frac{392.4}{50} = 7.848 \text{ m/s}^2$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{F_{traction,max} = 196.2 \text{ N}, \quad \tau_{wheel} = 19.62 \text{ N·m}, \quad a_{max} = 7.848 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A 50 kg robot accelerating at 0.8g (since $\mu = 0.8$ and we used the full traction limit) is a very high-performance figure. In practice, designers use a **safety factor of 2 to 3**, meaning the actual commanded acceleration is limited to $\sim 0.3g$ to avoid wheel slip during transients.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR) for Differential Drive

**Problem Setup:** A differential drive robot has two wheels separated by a track width $L = 0.4$ m. The left wheel rotates at $\omega_L = 5$ rad/s and the right wheel at $\omega_R = 3$ rad/s, both in the forward direction. Each wheel has radius $r = 0.08$ m. Find: (a) the linear velocity of each wheel, (b) the ICR location, and (c) the robot's angular velocity.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the linear velocity of each wheel using $v = r\omega$.**

$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L = 0.08 \times 5 = 0.40 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R = 0.08 \times 3 = 0.24 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Establish the geometry of the ICR.**
For a differential drive robot, the ICR lies on the line passing through the two wheel contact points, extended outward from the side of the **slower wheel**. Let the ICR be at a distance $R$ from the right wheel (slower) and $R + L$ from the left wheel.

Since both wheel contact points share the same angular velocity $\omega_{robot}$ about the ICR:

$$v_L = \omega_{robot} \cdot (R + L)$$

$$v_R = \omega_{robot} \cdot R$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R$ and $\omega_{robot}$ by dividing the two equations.**

$$\frac{v_L}{v_R} = \frac{R + L}{R}$$

$$v_L \cdot R = v_R \cdot (R + L)$$

$$R (v_L - v_R) = v_R \cdot L$$

$$R = \frac{v_R \cdot L}{v_L - v_R} = \frac{0.24 \times 0.4}{0.40 - 0.24} = \frac{0.096}{0.16} = 0.60 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\omega_{robot}$ from the right wheel equation.**

$$\omega_{robot} = \frac{v_R}{R} = \frac{0.24}{0.60} = 0.40 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{R = 0.60 \text{ m (ICR is 0.6 m to the right of the right wheel)}, \quad \omega_{robot} = 0.40 \text{ rad/s}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Because $\omega_L > \omega_R$, the robot is turning to the **right** (the ICR is on the right-hand side). If $\omega_L = \omega_R$, then $v_L = v_R$, the denominator $v_L - v_R = 0$, and $R \to \infty$ — meaning the ICR is at infinity, and the robot moves in a **straight line**. This is the special case of pure translation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wheel Diameter Selection for Obstacle Climbing

**Problem Setup:** A mobile robot must climb a step of height $h = 0.05$ m. The wheel has a static friction coefficient $\mu = 0.7$ with the step edge. Find the minimum wheel diameter $D_{min}$ such that the robot can climb the step.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Set up the force balance at the step edge.**
When the wheel contacts the vertical face of the step, three forces act at the contact point: the normal reaction $N$ (horizontal), the friction force $f = \mu N$ (vertical, supporting the robot weight), and the robot weight $W = mg$ (downward).

**Step 2 — Vertical equilibrium for climbing.**
For the wheel to climb, the friction force must support the weight (or the applied motor torque must lift the wheel):

$$f \geq W \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu N \geq W$$

**Step 3 — Geometric constraint on the normal force.**
The wheel contacts the step edge at a point where the wheel radius is perpendicular to the step face. By taking moments about the wheel centre, the normal force $N$ is related to the wheel radius $r$ and the applied forward force $F_{push}$:

$$N = F_{push} \cdot \frac{r - h}{r} \quad \text{(when } h < r\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Combine and solve for the minimum radius.**
For the wheel to *just* climb the step, the geometry requires that the contact point with the step is at the top of the wheel — this happens when $r \geq h$. For a margin of safety, designers typically choose:

$$D_{min} = 2 r_{min} \quad \text{where} \quad r_{min} \geq h + \text{safety margin}$$

A common engineering rule of thumb is:

$$D_{min} \geq 2 h \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_{min} \geq 2 \times 0.05 = 0.10 \text{ m}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{D_{min} = 0.10 \text{ m (i.e., a wheel diameter of at least 10 cm for a 5 cm step)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Wheel Selection Decision Table

| Robot Type | Wheel Diameter | Wheel Type | Friction ($\mu$) | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indoor AGV (warehouse) | 8–15 cm | Standard + castor | $\geq 0.7$ | Energy efficiency |
| Outdoor rover (planetary) | 30–50 cm | Standard ruggedised | $\geq 0.5$ | Obstacle climbing |
| Humanoid robot | 5–10 cm | Compliant + compliant feet | $\geq 0.6$ | Dynamic stability |
| Omnidirectional robot | 10–15 cm | Mecanum / Swedish | $\geq 0.4$ | Lateral manoeuvrability |
| Underwater robot | N/A (thrusters) | — | — | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Wheel Design Decision Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **complete functional design flow** for selecting and dimensioning a wheel for a mobile robot. It is rendered as a clean block-level architecture per the KTU safety guidelines.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Robot Task Specification] --> B[Identify Terrain Type]
    B --> C{Is terrain smooth and flat?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Standard Drive Wheel]
    C -- No --> E[Use Ruggedised or Swedish Wheel]
    D --> F[Compute Wheel Load W per wheel]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Traction Limit Fmax equals mu times W]
    G --> H[Compute Required Torque tau equals Fmax times r]
    H --> I[Select Motor and Gearbox to Match tau]
    I --> J[Compute Wheel Slip Ratio s]
    J --> K{Is s less than 0.1?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Design Acceptable]
    K -- No --> M[Increase Wheel Diameter or Surface Friction]
    M --> F
    L --> N[Check Obstacle Climbing: D greater than 2h]
    N --> O{Is climbing requirement met?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Finalise Wheel Geometry]
    O -- No --> Q[Increase Wheel Diameter]
    Q --> P
    P --> R[End: Wheel Design Finalised]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Wheel Forces and Free Body Diagram (FBD)

The following Mermaid block diagram presents the **force interactions on a single driven wheel** of a mobile robot. It maps every contact force, axle reaction, and applied motor torque in a clear, labelled architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Wheel_Assembly
        M[DC Motor / Actuator] -->|Torque tau| G[Gearbox with ratio G]
        G -->|Torque tau wheel| A[Wheel Axle]
        A --> W[Wheel Tyre of radius r]
    end
    subgraph Contact_Patch
        W -->|Normal force N| CP[Ground Contact Patch]
        W -->|Traction Force F equals mu N| CP
    end
    subgraph Robot_Chassis
        W -->|Vertical Load W robot| C[Chassis of mass m]
        C -->|Weight m times g| W
    end
    CP -->|Reaction R equals N plus F| W
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Differential Drive ICR Geometry

The following block diagram shows how the two wheel velocities map to the Instantaneous Centre of Rotation in a differential drive robot.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Left Wheel: velocity vL equals r times omega L] --> ICR[ICR Computation]
    R[Right Wheel: velocity vR equals r times omega R] --> ICR
    ICR --> O1[Distance R from slow wheel]
    ICR --> O2[Distance R plus L from fast wheel]
    ICR --> O3[Robot Angular Velocity omega robot equals vR over R]
    O1 --> Final[Robot Follows Circular Arc]
    O2 --> Final
    O3 --> Final
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Wheel Type Comparison Matrix

The following block diagram maps the four canonical wheel types used in mobile robotics and the degrees of freedom they allow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Wheel Classification] --> T1[Standard Fixed Wheel]
    Start --> T2[Castor Wheel with kingpin offset d]
    Start --> T3[Swedish Wheel with rollers]
    Start --> T4[Spherical Wheel]
    T1 --> C1[DOF 2, Constraints 1]
    T2 --> C2[DOF 2, Constraints 1, passive steering]
    T3 --> C3[DOF 3, Constraints 0, omnidirectional]
    T4 --> C4[DOF 3, Constraints 0, fully omnidirectional]
    C1 --> Use1[Used in differential drive]
    C2 --> Use2[Used as idler support wheel]
    C3 --> Use3[Used in omnidirectional platforms]
    C4 --> Use4[Used in research prototypes]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** All four block diagrams above are rendered using the **Mermaid** syntax which is fully compatible with KTU 2024 Scheme learning management systems. Each node uses pure alphanumeric identifiers prefixed with letters (e.g., `Wheel_Assembly`, `Contact_Patch`, `T1`, `C3`) and all labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown formatting inside quoted labels — to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rendering safeguards.
