<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
*Define the terms: (i) Slip ratio, (ii) Castor wheel kingpin offset, (iii) Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR).*

**Model Answer (3 × 1 mark):**

**(i) Slip Ratio $s$:** A dimensionless quantity that quantifies the deviation of a wheel from pure rolling. It is defined as:

$$s = \frac{\vert r\omega - v \vert}{\max(r\omega, v)}$$

For pure rolling, $s = 0$. For full slip, $s = 1$.

**(ii) Kingpin Offset $d$:** In a castor wheel, the kingpin (vertical steering axis) is offset from the contact point by a perpendicular distance $d$. This offset is what makes the wheel passive — it self-aligns with the direction of motion under the action of friction.

**(iii) ICR:** The Instantaneous Centre of Rotation is the unique point in the robot's reference frame about which the entire rigid body (chassis) is rotating at a given instant. For a differential drive, the ICR lies on the line through the two wheel contact points.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*List any three factors that affect the traction force available at a mobile robot wheel, and state the formula for maximum tractive force.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three factors affecting traction force:**

1. **Coefficient of friction $\mu$** between the wheel material and the ground surface.
2. **Normal load $W$** pressing the wheel onto the ground (i.e., the weight distribution on the wheel).
3. **Contact patch area and shape** — a larger contact patch provides higher traction up to a saturation limit.

**Formula for maximum tractive force:**

$$F_{traction,max} = \mu \cdot W$$

This is the upper bound; the actual tractive force is limited by the motor torque as $\tau / r$ and the design always chooses the lower of the two.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Question A]**

A four-wheeled mobile robot of mass **$m = 80$ kg** uses two driven rear wheels (differential drive) and two front castor idler wheels. Each driven wheel has radius **$r = 0.12$ m**. The wheel-ground friction coefficient is **$\mu = 0.75$**. The track width (distance between the two driven wheels) is **$L = 0.5$ m**.

**(a) [7 marks]** Compute the maximum tractive force per driven wheel, the wheel torque required, and the maximum linear acceleration of the robot. Assume the two driven wheels share the robot's weight equally and ignore rolling resistance.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the left wheel motor is commanded to spin at $\omega_L = 6$ rad/s and the right wheel at $\omega_R = 4$ rad/s, find the location of the Instantaneous Centre of Rotation and the robot's angular velocity. Also compute the radius of the circular arc the robot follows.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Compute weight per driven wheel.**

$$W_{wheel} = \frac{mg}{2} = \frac{80 \times 9.81}{2} = \frac{784.8}{2} = 392.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute maximum tractive force per wheel.**

$$F_{traction,max} = \mu \cdot W_{wheel} = 0.75 \times 392.4 = 294.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute wheel torque required.**

$$\tau_{wheel} = F_{traction,max} \cdot r = 294.3 \times 0.12 = 35.316 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute maximum robot acceleration.**

$$a_{max} = \frac{2 F_{traction,max}}{m} = \frac{2 \times 294.3}{80} = \frac{588.6}{80} = 7.3575 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Final Answer (a):** $F_{traction,max} = 294.3$ N, $\tau_{wheel} = 35.32$ N·m, $a_{max} = 7.36$ m/s².

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute linear velocities of each wheel.**

$$v_L = r \omega_L = 0.12 \times 6 = 0.72 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_R = r \omega_R = 0.12 \times 4 = 0.48 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ICR geometry.**
Since $v_L > v_R$, the robot is turning right; the ICR is to the right of the right wheel.

$$R = \frac{v_R \cdot L}{v_L - v_R} = \frac{0.48 \times 0.5}{0.72 - 0.48} = \frac{0.24}{0.24} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute robot angular velocity.**

$$\omega_{robot} = \frac{v_R}{R} = \frac{0.48}{1.0} = 0.48 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Radius of circular arc.**
The robot follows an arc of radius **$R = 1.0$ m** about the ICR.

**Final Answer (b):** ICR is 1.0 m to the right of the right wheel; $\omega_{robot} = 0.48$ rad/s; arc radius = 1.0 m.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Common Mark Loss Zone):**
> - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to divide the weight by 2 (using total weight in the friction equation). **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to use the radius $r$ when converting traction force to wheel torque (writing $\tau = F$ instead of $\tau = Fr$). **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Mistake 3:** In the ICR problem, placing the ICR on the wrong side (left of the left wheel when it should be right of the right wheel). **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Mistake 4:** Not stating units in the final answer. **Loss: 0.5–1 mark per sub-question.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 1, Question B]**

A mobile robot uses **mecanum wheels** for omnidirectional motion. Each mecanum wheel has a radius **$r = 0.075$ m** and is fitted with **8 peripheral rollers** at $45°$ to the wheel plane. The robot's mass is **$m = 30$ kg**, and the coefficient of friction between the rubber roller and the floor is **$\mu = 0.65$**.

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat sketch (or block diagram) how a mecanum wheel generates force in the lateral direction. Show the roller orientation and the resulting force vector decomposition.

**(b) [7 marks]** If all four mecanum wheels are arranged in the standard "+" configuration, derive the wheel speed commands ($\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4$) required to produce a pure sideways translation of the robot at **$v_y = 0.5$ m/s** with no forward velocity and no rotation. Assume wheel 1 is front-left, wheel 2 is front-right, wheel 3 is rear-right, wheel 4 is rear-left. The rollers on the left wheels are oriented at $+45°$ and on the right wheels at $-45°$ (standard convention).

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Working Principle of a Mecanum Wheel:**

A mecanum wheel consists of a central hub tyre (driven by the motor) surrounded by a ring of small free-spinning **passive rollers** mounted at $45°$ to the wheel plane. The rollers are typically barrel-shaped and made of hard rubber or polyurethane.

- When the **hub rotates**, the wheel rolls forward or backward.
- Because the rollers are at $45°$, the contact force between the roller and the ground has **two components**:
  - A **longitudinal** component (along the wheel rolling direction) — this drives the robot forward.
  - A **lateral** component (perpendicular to the wheel rolling direction) — this is the **omnidirectional force**.

By **rotating the rollers at 45° in opposite directions** on the left and right wheels, the lateral force components from the four wheels can be made to either **add up** (for sideways motion) or **cancel out** (for pure forward motion).

**Force Decomposition Diagram (textual):**
If the wheel rotates forward with motor torque producing a traction force $F$ at the contact patch, and the roller is at $45°$, the ground reaction on the roller splits into:

$$F_{longitudinal} = F \cos(45°) = \frac{F}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$F_{lateral} = F \sin(45°) = \frac{F}{\sqrt{2}}$$

When four wheels are arranged in the "+" configuration with rollers on left wheels at $+45°$ and right wheels at $-45°$, the lateral components of all four wheels add up to produce a net sideways force, while the longitudinal components cancel in pairs.

**Final Answer (a):** The mecanum wheel produces omnidirectional force because the $45°$ rollers split the contact force into longitudinal and lateral components. By coordinating all four wheels, the robot can translate in any direction without changing wheel orientation.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Recall the mecanum drive inverse kinematics.**

For a mecanum robot with wheel radius $r$ and half-wheelbase $a$ (front-back) and half-track $b$ (left-right), the wheel speeds for a desired body twist $(v_x, v_y, \omega_z)$ are given by the matrix equation. For the standard configuration with rollers at $\pm 45°$:

$$\omega_1 = \frac{1}{r}(v_x - v_y - (a+b)\omega_z)$$

$$\omega_2 = \frac{1}{r}(v_x + v_y + (a+b)\omega_z)$$

$$\omega_3 = \frac{1}{r}(v_x - v_y + (a+b)\omega_z)$$

$$\omega_4 = \frac{1}{r}(v_x + v_y - (a+b)\omega_z)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the desired motion.**
We want pure sideways motion: $v_x = 0$, $v_y = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega_z = 0$.

$$\omega_1 = \frac{1}{r}(0 - 0.5 - 0) = \frac{-0.5}{0.075} = -6.667 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_2 = \frac{1}{r}(0 + 0.5 + 0) = \frac{0.5}{0.075} = +6.667 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_3 = \frac{1}{r}(0 - 0.5 + 0) = \frac{-0.5}{0.075} = -6.667 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_4 = \frac{1}{r}(0 + 0.5 - 0) = \frac{0.5}{0.075} = +6.667 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the signs.**
- $\omega_1 = -6.667$ rad/s (front-left wheel spins **backward**).
- $\omega_2 = +6.667$ rad/s (front-right wheel spins **forward**).
- $\omega_3 = -6.667$ rad/s (rear-right wheel spins **backward**).
- $\omega_4 = +6.667$ rad/s (rear-left wheel spins **forward**).

**Final Answer (b):** All four wheels must spin at $|\omega| = 6.667$ rad/s, with the two right wheels (2 and 4) spinning in the opposite direction to the two left wheels (1 and 3).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing the roller orientation sign convention. The KTU paper expects the **standard convention** (left wheels at $+45°$, right wheels at $-45°$). If the student uses the opposite convention, all signs flip. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to substitute the correct sign of $v_y$ in the inverse kinematics. The lateral velocity enters with opposite signs for left vs. right wheels. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Mistake 3:** Not writing the matrix equation explicitly before substituting. KTU examiners award partial credit for showing the formula; jumping straight to numbers loses method marks. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wheel Design — Rapid Revision Checklist (for KTU 2024 ESE preparation):**

- **Core Kinematic Identity:** Always start with $v = r\omega$ — this is the single most-tested equation.
- **Slip Ratio:** $s = \frac{\vert r\omega - v \vert}{\max(r\omega, v)}$. Pure rolling: $s = 0$. Full slip: $s = 1$. **Never** write $s$ without the absolute value brackets.
- **Traction Limit:** $F_{traction,max} = \mu W$. The motor can only deliver what friction allows — this is the **first** constraint to check.
- **Wheel Torque:** $\tau = F \cdot r$. This links motor selection to wheel geometry.
- **ICR Location for Differential Drive:** $R = \dfrac{v_{slow} \cdot L}{v_{fast} - v_{slow}}$. The ICR is on the side of the slower wheel. Pure straight motion: $R \to \infty$.
- **Obstacle Climbing Rule:** $D \geq 2h$ for a wheel to climb a step of height $h$. This is a **rule of thumb** used by KTU examiners.
- **Wheel Type Hierarchy:** Standard < Castor < Swedish < Spherical (in terms of manoeuvrability and complexity).
- **Mecanum Wheel Inverse Kinematics:** Memorise the four-wheel matrix equation — it is a guaranteed 14-mark question in KTU ESE.
- **Units Discipline:** Always state units in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
- **Safety Factor:** Real designs use a safety factor of 2–3 on traction and 1.5–2 on torque to avoid slip during transients.
- **Material Selection:** Rubber (high $\mu$, low wear on hard floor) vs. Polyurethane (medium $\mu$, high wear resistance). For Mars rovers: aluminium with cleats (very high durability, lower $\mu$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** Wheel design questions in the ESE typically combine a **derivation** (5–7 marks) with a **numerical** (5–7 marks) and a **short concept** (2–3 marks). Always show the formula before substituting numbers, draw a clear FBD (free body diagram) using a block diagram if a sketch is required, and explicitly state the units in the final answer. The KTU examiner's valuation key rewards **method** over a final numerical answer — a correct method with an arithmetic slip still earns 80–90% marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheel Geometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wheel Geometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Wheel Geometry** in mobile robotics is the formal kinematic study of how different wheel types interact with the ground to constrain and define the possible motion of a wheeled mobile robot (WMR). It characterizes the geometric relationship between a wheel's contact point with the ground, the wheel's mounting position on the robot chassis, and the resulting constraints on the robot's instantaneous motion in the plane.

The three fundamental elements of wheel geometry are:

1. **Wheel Type** — the physical/mechanical design of the wheel (standard, castor, Swedish, spherical, mecanum).
2. **Wheel Position and Orientation** — where the wheel is mounted on the robot and at what angle it is attached.
3. **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** — the unique point in the world frame about which the robot must instantaneously rotate to satisfy all wheel constraints simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental theorem of wheel geometry states that at any instant, a rigid-body mobile robot can have **at most one** Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR). All wheel constraints must converge to a common ICR for the motion to be physically realizable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy office chair with two fixed wheels on a polished floor. The chair **cannot** move sideways — it can only go forward, backward, or turn. Why? Because each fixed wheel forces the chair to "pivot" about a single point on the floor (the ICR). If you tried to push the chair sideways, the wheels would scrape — meaning the chair is **kinematically constrained**.

Now imagine a supermarket trolley. The rear wheels are fixed, but the front wheels can **pivot freely** (castor wheels). The trolley is still constrained — it cannot translate sideways — but the ICR is now adjustable because the castor wheels can rotate. This is the **degree of steerability** of the robot.

If you replace the castor wheels with a **Swedish 90° wheel** (a small wheel whose rim is covered in tiny perpendicular rollers), the trolley suddenly *can* move sideways! This is because the Swedish wheel introduces **zero kinematic constraints** — it can roll in any direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy Summary:**
> - **Fixed Wheel** = Train wheel on a rail (only forward/backward).
> - **Castor Wheel** = Office chair front wheel (pivots freely, but still cannot move sideways).
> - **Swedish Wheel** = Ball-bearing drawer slide (slips easily in any direction).
> - **Ball/Spherical Wheel** = Marble rolling on glass (truly omnidirectional).

### 1.3 Standard Reference Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom of a rigid body in the plane | $DOF$ | **3** |
| Number of fixed standard wheels | $N_f$ | non-negative integer |
| Number of steering standard wheels | $N_s$ | non-negative integer |
| Number of castor wheels | $N_c$ | non-negative integer |
| Number of special (Swedish/Spherical) wheels | $N_{sp}$ | non-negative integer |
| Wheel orientation angle | $\alpha$ or $\beta$ | radians |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Wheels

The four primary wheel categories in mobile robotics are:

**(A) Standard Fixed Wheel**
- Two Degrees of Freedom (DOF): rotation about the axle, rotation about the contact point.
- Two constraints imposed on the robot: (1) rolling constraint (no sliding along the wheel plane) and (2) no lateral sliding at the contact point.
- The ICR must lie on the line passing through the wheel axle and perpendicular to the wheel plane.

**(B) Steering Standard Wheel**
- Same as the fixed standard wheel, but the entire wheel assembly can **rotate** about a vertical axis attached to the robot chassis.
- This adds an extra **steerability DOF** that the robot can actively control.
- Contributes 1 mobility constraint + 1 steerability DOF.

**(C) Castor Wheel**
- A standard wheel mounted on a vertical pivot (like the front wheel of a shopping cart).
- It adds 1 mobility constraint (the wheel-plane direction is still constrained).
- It does **not** add steerability because the castor wheel's orientation is passive.

**(D) Special Wheels (Swedish, Mecanum, Ball/Spherical)**
- These wheels employ passive rollers or spherical geometry to allow motion in **multiple directions**.
- They impose **zero mobility constraints** (omnidirectional in their contact plane).
- A Swedish 45° or 90° wheel, a mecanum wheel, and a ball wheel all belong to this class.

### 2.2 The Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) — Geometric Principle

For a rigid robot at any instant, every point on the robot moves along a circle centered at the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. Therefore, every wheel's contact point must have a velocity vector **perpendicular** to the line joining that contact point to the ICR.

The geometric implication is that **the ICR must lie on the line defined by the wheel axle of every standard wheel**. The locus of permissible ICR positions for a single standard wheel is therefore an **infinite straight line** in the plane.

- Two standard wheels → ICR is uniquely determined (intersection of two lines).
- Three or more standard wheels → the robot is over-constrained unless all ICR-locus lines meet at a single point.

### 2.3 The Five Wheel Constraints (Kinematic)

For a standard wheel at position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\beta$ with respect to the x-axis, the ICR $(x_{ICR}, y_{ICR})$ must satisfy the **rolling-sliding constraint equation**:

$$
x_{ICR} \sin(\beta) - y_{ICR} \cos(\beta) + d = 0
$$

where the constant $d = y \cos(\beta) - x \sin(\beta)$. This single linear equation encodes both the rolling and lateral-sliding constraints of the standard wheel.

### 2.4 Mobility, Steerability, and Maneuverability

| Concept | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Degree of Mobility** | $\delta_m$ | Number of independent translational/rotational DOF the robot **possesses** in the plane. |
| **Degree of Steerability** | $\delta_s$ | Number of additional DOF the robot has from **steerable** wheels. |
| **Degree of Maneuverability** | $MM$ | Total controllable DOF of the robot, $MM = \delta_m + \delta_s$. |

The **Mobility** is reduced by each fixed constraint imposed by the wheels:

$$
\delta_m = 3 - N_c
$$

where $N_c$ is the total number of independent mobility constraints (one per fixed standard wheel, one per steering standard wheel, and one per castor wheel).

The **Steerability** is the number of controllable steering axes:

$$
\delta_s = N_s
$$

Therefore, the **Maneuverability Theorem** of Wheeled Mobile Robots is:

$$
MM = 3 - N_c + N_s
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula | Equation | Use |
|---|---|---|
| DOF in plane | $3$ | Foundation of all WMR analysis |
| Mobility | $\delta_m = 3 - N_c$ | Translational/rotational freedom left |
| Steerability | $\delta_s = N_s$ | Number of steerable standard wheels |
| Maneuverability | $MM = 3 - N_c + N_s$ | Total controllable robot DOF |
| ICR Constraint (standard wheel) | $x_{ICR} \sin\beta - y_{ICR} \cos\beta + d = 0$ | Locus of permissible ICR for one wheel |
| Position of ICR | $x_{ICR}, y_{ICR}$ satisfies all wheel constraints | Geometric robot analysis |
| Robot body angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{v_x}{R} = \frac{v_y}{R}$ where $R$ = ICR distance | Kinematic link |
| Differential drive DOF | $MM = 1$ (since $N_c = 2, N_s = 0$) | Two fixed standard wheels |
| Synchronous drive DOF | $MM = 2$ (since $N_c = 2, N_s = 1$) | Two steering + castor |
| Omnidirectional (4 mecanum) | $MM = 3$ (since $N_c = 0, N_s = 0$) | Full planar motion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Wheel geometry is the **first step** in designing any autonomous WMR — warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), autonomous cars, planetary rovers (NASA's Mars rovers use rocker-bogie with steerable wheels), and robotic wheelchairs. The choice of wheel type directly determines whether the robot can navigate a given environment (e.g., parallel parking requires high steerability; tight indoor maneuvers benefit from omnidirectional mecanum wheels).

### 2.6 Real-World Applications

- **Differential Drive (2 fixed + 1 castor)** — Roomba vacuum cleaners, Mars Sojourner rover.
- **Tricycle / Ackermann Steering** — Autonomous cars, Toyota, Waymo.
- **Synchronous Drive (3 or 4 steering)** — Indoor hospital delivery robots.
- **Mecanum / Swedish (4 omnidirectional)** — Heavy-payload industrial AGVs, KUKA omniMove platforms.
- **Ball Wheel (3 spherical)** — Research prototypes requiring zero turn radius.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the ICR Constraint Equation for a Standard Wheel

**Setup:** Consider a standard wheel whose center is at position $(x, y)$ in the world frame, with the wheel plane making an angle $\beta$ with the x-axis. The wheel touches the ground at a point directly below its center.

**Step 1 — Velocity of the contact point.** The velocity of the wheel's contact point is determined by the rigid-body motion of the robot. If the robot has an angular velocity $\omega$ about the ICR at $(x_{ICR}, y_{ICR})$, then the contact point at $(x, y)$ has velocity:

$$
\vec{v} = \omega \, \hat{k} \times \vec{r} = \omega \, (- (y - y_{ICR}), \, x - x_{ICR})
$$

The components are $v_x = -\omega(y - y_{ICR})$ and $v_y = \omega(x - x_{ICR})$.

**Step 2 — No-sliding (rolling) constraint.** The wheel can only roll in the direction of its plane (i.e., along the unit vector $(\cos\beta, \sin\beta)$). Therefore, the velocity of the contact point must be **parallel** to this direction. This means the velocity component perpendicular to the wheel plane is zero:

$$
v_x \sin\beta - v_y \cos\beta = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the velocity expressions:**

$$
-\omega(y - y_{ICR}) \sin\beta - \omega(x - x_{ICR}) \cos\beta = 0
$$

Divide through by $\omega$ (assuming the robot is moving, so $\omega \neq 0$):

$$
-(y - y_{ICR}) \sin\beta - (x - x_{ICR}) \cos\beta = 0
$$

Rearrange to obtain the **ICR constraint line equation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-y \sin\beta + y_{ICR} \sin\beta - x \cos\beta + x_{ICR} \cos\beta &= 0 \\
x_{ICR} \cos\beta + y_{ICR} \sin\beta &= x \cos\beta + y \sin\beta
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying both sides to convert to a standard linear form and rearranging:

$$
x_{ICR} \sin\beta - y_{ICR} \cos\beta + (y \cos\beta - x \sin\beta) = 0
$$

This is the **rolling-sliding constraint line** for a standard wheel.

**Step 4 — Geometric interpretation.** The set of points $(x_{ICR}, y_{ICR})$ satisfying the equation above is a **straight line** in the plane, perpendicular to the wheel's direction of travel, and passing through the wheel's center. This line is called the **axle-line** of the wheel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Maneuverability Formula

**Setup:** A rigid robot has **3 DOF** in the plane: translation along $x$, translation along $y$, and rotation about its center.

**Step 1 — Counting constraints.** Each fixed standard wheel adds **1 kinematic constraint** (the no-sliding condition at the contact point). Each steering standard wheel adds the same **1 kinematic constraint** but also **1 steerable DOF** (the steering axis). Each castor wheel adds **1 kinematic constraint** but **0 steerable DOF** (it is passively self-aligning). Special wheels (Swedish, mecanum, ball) add **0 kinematic constraints**.

**Step 2 — Effective mobility.** The number of constraints $N_c$ imposed on the robot is:

$$
N_c = N_f + N_s + N_c^{castor}
$$

The mobility $\delta_m$ of the robot (the number of independent DOF left) is then:

$$
\delta_m = 3 - N_c
$$

**Step 3 — Effective steerability.** The number of steerable standard wheels $N_s$ gives the degree of steerability:

$$
\delta_s = N_s
$$

**Step 4 — Total maneuverability.** The total maneuverability is the sum of mobility and steerability, because steering effectively unlocks an additional DOF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MM &= \delta_m + \delta_s \\
&= (3 - N_c) + N_s \\
&= 3 - N_f - N_s - N_c^{castor} + N_s \\
&= 3 - N_f - N_c^{castor}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental maneuverability formula** for a wheeled mobile robot.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Differential Drive Robot

A differential drive robot has **2 fixed standard wheels** and **1 castor wheel**. Determine its maneuverability.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:** $N_f = 2$, $N_s = 0$, $N_c^{castor} = 1$.

**Step 2 — Compute the number of constraints:**

$$
N_c = N_f + N_s + N_c^{castor} = 2 + 0 + 1 = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mobility:**

$$
\delta_m = 3 - N_c = 3 - 3 = 0
$$

A mobility of **0** is a special case. The robot has *no* free DOF — every motion is controlled by the wheel actuators.

**Step 4 — Compute the steerability:**

$$
\delta_s = N_s = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Total maneuverability:**

$$
MM = \delta_m + \delta_s = 0 + 0 = 0 \quad \text{(effectively a single controllable DOF since the wheels are coupled)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle KTU Pitfall:** A differential drive robot has $MM = 0$ in the strict sense (using the formula above with 3 constraints), yet it can still translate forward/backward and rotate. This is because the **two drive wheels are coupled through their actuators**, providing a *single* effective control input (the difference of wheel speeds) that produces rotational motion. KTU examiners often ask: "Why does a differential drive robot have only 1 controllable DOF despite having 2 motors?" — the answer is the **non-holonomic constraint** between the wheels, not the constraint count.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Wheeled Robot Configurations

| Robot Type | Wheels | $N_f$ | $N_s$ | $N_c^{castor}$ | $N_{sp}$ | $N_c$ (constraints) | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $MM$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Drive | 2 fixed + 1 castor | 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 0 | 0 (effectively 1 controllable) |
| Tricycle (Ackermann) | 1 steering + 2 fixed | 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| Synchronous Drive | 3 steering + 0 fixed | 0 | 3 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | 3 |
| Omnidirectional (4 mecanum) | 4 special | 0 | 0 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 |
| Swedish 90° (4 wheels) | 4 special | 0 | 0 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 |
| Car (Ackermann) | 2 steering + 2 fixed | 2 | 2 | 0 | 0 | 4 | -1 (impossible) | 2 | inconsistent → over-constrained, resolved by Ackermann linkage |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wheel Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WHEEL TYPES IN MOBILE ROBOTICS] --> B[STANDARD WHEELS]
    A --> C[SPECIAL WHEELS]
    B --> B1[FIXED STANDARD WHEEL]
    B --> B2[STEERING STANDARD WHEEL]
    B --> B3[CASTOR WHEEL]
    C --> C1[SWEDISH 45 DEG WHEEL]
    C --> C2[SWEDISH 90 DEG WHEEL]
    C --> C3[MECANUM WHEEL]
    C --> C4[BALL OR SPHERICAL WHEEL]
    B1 --> D1[CONSTRAINTS = 1 MOBILITY, 0 STEERABILITY]
    B2 --> D2[CONSTRAINTS = 1 MOBILITY, 1 STEERABILITY]
    B3 --> D3[CONSTRAINTS = 1 MOBILITY, 0 STEERABILITY]
    C1 --> E1[CONSTRAINTS = 0 OMNIDIRECTIONAL]
    C2 --> E2[CONSTRAINTS = 0 OMNIDIRECTIONAL]
    C3 --> E3[CONSTRAINTS = 0 OMNIDIRECTIONAL]
    C4 --> E4[CONSTRAINTS = 0 OMNIDIRECTIONAL]
```

### 4.2 ICR and Axle-Line Geometric Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ROBOT CHASSIS] --> B[WHEEL 1 AT X1 Y1 BETA1]
    A --> C[WHEEL 2 AT X2 Y2 BETA2]
    A --> D[WHEEL 3 AT X3 Y3 BETA3]
    B --> E[AXLE LINE L1]
    C --> F[AXLE LINE L2]
    D --> G[AXLE LINE L3]
    E --> H[ICR = INTERSECTION]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[UNIQUE INSTANTANEOUS CENTER OF ROTATION]
    I --> J[ALL WHEELS ROLL WITHOUT SLIDING]
    I --> K[ROBOT ANG VEL OMEGA ABOUT ICR]
    I --> L[EACH CONTACT VELOCITY PERPENDICULAR TO LINE TO ICR]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of Wheel Geometry Constraints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: WHEEL TYPE AND POSITION] --> B[COMPUTE N_F, N_S, N_CAST, N_SP]
    B --> C[TOTAL CONSTRAINTS N_C]
    C --> D[MOBILITY DELTA M = 3 MINUS N_C]
    B --> E[STEERABILITY DELTA S = N_S]
    D --> F[MANEUVERABILITY MM = DELTA M PLUS DELTA S]
    E --> F
    F --> G{OVER CONSTRAINED?}
    G -->|YES| H[ACKERMANN LINKAGE OR FLEXIBLE SUSPENSION REQUIRED]
    G -->|NO| I[MOTION IS FEASIBLE]
    F --> J[TYPE OF ROBOT]
    J --> J1[DIFFERENTIAL DRIVE]
    J --> J2[ACKERMANN CAR]
    J --> J3[OMNIDIRECTIONAL MECANUM]
    J --> J4[SYNCHRONOUS DRIVE]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram of ICR Geometric Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[WHEEL CENTER P_X P_Y] --> B[COMPUTE BETA ANGLE]
    B --> C[FORM LINEAR EQ: XICR SIN BETA MINUS YICR COS BETA PLUS D = 0]
    C --> D[PLOT LINE IN PLANE]
    D --> E[REPEAT FOR ALL WHEELS]
    E --> F[FIND COMMON INTERSECTION POINT]
    F --> G[THIS IS THE ICR]
    G --> H[VALIDATE ALL WHEELS HAVE NON SLIPPING ROLLING]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) for a wheeled mobile robot. Why must all standard wheel axle-lines intersect at a common ICR?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is the unique point in the plane about which a rigid mobile robot instantaneously rotates at a given moment, assuming the robot's motion can be described as a pure rotation.

For a standard wheel, the contact point with the ground cannot have a velocity component along the wheel's axle — it must be perpendicular to the axle. This implies that the ICR must lie on the line passing through the wheel center and perpendicular to the wheel plane (the **axle-line**). If the robot is a rigid body, all its points rotate about the same ICR at that instant, so the axle-lines of **all** standard wheels must pass through a common ICR. Otherwise, the no-sliding constraint cannot be satisfied simultaneously for all wheels, and the motion is kinematically infeasible.

**(Marking: 1 mark definition + 1 mark axle-line logic + 1 mark rigidity argument.)**

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a fixed standard wheel and a castor wheel with respect to the constraints they impose on robot motion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed Standard Wheel | Castor Wheel |
|---|---|---|
| Mounting | Rigidly fixed to chassis | Mounted on a vertical pivot |
| Orientation | Constant (defined at design) | Self-aligns passively with motion direction |
| Mobility Constraints | 1 (no-sliding at contact) | 1 (no-sliding at contact) |
| Steerability DOF | 0 | 0 |
| Net Contribution | 1 constraint, no added DOF | 1 constraint, no added DOF |

The key **practical** difference is that a castor wheel can **passively rotate** to align with the direction of motion, which reduces wheel scrub and tire wear during turns. A fixed standard wheel cannot rotate, so the robot must either slide the wheel sideways (infeasible) or rotate about a point on the wheel's axle-line.

**(Marking: 1 mark each for constraint comparison and 1 mark for practical distinction.)**

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> KTU students choose **one** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, classify the different types of wheels used in mobile robotics. Explain the kinematic constraints each type imposes on the robot's motion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot has 2 fixed standard wheels and 1 castor wheel. Compute its degree of mobility, degree of steerability, and maneuverability. Explain why the robot is still able to move forward and rotate despite having zero maneuverability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wheel Classification (7 marks):**

Draw a block diagram showing the four major wheel classes (as in Section 4.1 above) and explain:

- **Standard Fixed Wheel (1 mark):** Two DOF (spin + contact rotation). Imposes 1 mobility constraint (no sliding at contact). Contributes 0 to steerability.
- **Steering Standard Wheel (2 marks):** Adds 1 steerability DOF. Same constraint as fixed standard wheel, but the wheel can be reoriented actively.
- **Castor Wheel (1 mark):** Mounted on a vertical pivot, passively self-aligning. Imposes 1 mobility constraint, 0 steerability.
- **Special Wheels — Swedish, Mecanum, Ball (3 marks):** Passive rollers on the rim allow omnidirectional motion. Impose 0 mobility constraints. A Swedish 90° wheel uses perpendicular rollers, while a mecanum wheel uses 45° rollers; both can move in any direction in the plane.

**(b) Differential Drive Kinematics (7 marks):**

Given: $N_f = 2$, $N_s = 0$, $N_c^{castor} = 1$, $N_{sp} = 0$.

- **Mobility constraints (1 mark):** $N_c = N_f + N_s + N_c^{castor} = 2 + 0 + 1 = 3$.
- **Mobility (2 marks):** $\delta_m = 3 - N_c = 3 - 3 = 0$.
- **Steerability (1 mark):** $\delta_s = N_s = 0$.
- **Maneuverability (1 mark):** $MM = \delta_m + \delta_s = 0$.

**Why the robot still moves (2 marks):** Although the formal maneuverability is 0, the two drive wheels are **actuator-coupled**. The robot's motion is described by a **non-holonomic constraint** that relates the forward velocity $v$ and the angular velocity $\omega$:

$$
\dot{x} \sin\theta - \dot{y} \cos\theta = 0
$$

This single non-holonomic constraint reduces the controllable DOF from 2 (translation) to 1, allowing the robot to move along a path defined by the difference of the two wheel speeds. The robot can still drive forward and rotate, but **not** move sideways — the maneuverability being 0 simply means all 3 planar DOF are now *coupled* to a single control input (the differential wheel speeds), not that no motion is possible.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** What is the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)? Derive the constraint equation for a standard wheel and explain its geometric meaning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An omnidirectional robot is equipped with 4 mecanum wheels. Compute its degree of mobility, steerability, and maneuverability. Mention two real-world applications where such a configuration is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ICR and Constraint Derivation (7 marks):**

- **Definition of ICR (2 marks):** The ICR is the unique point in the world frame about which the rigid robot instantaneously rotates. For a standard wheel, the ICR must lie on the line passing through the wheel center and perpendicular to the wheel plane (the axle-line).

- **Derivation of constraint equation (4 marks):** Following the derivation in Section 3.1, the velocity of the contact point $(x, y)$ about the ICR $(x_{ICR}, y_{ICR})$ is:

$$
v_x = -\omega (y - y_{ICR}), \quad v_y = \omega (x - x_{ICR})
$$

Imposing the no-sliding condition $v_x \sin\beta - v_y \cos\beta = 0$, simplifying and dividing by $\omega$:

$$
x_{ICR} \sin\beta - y_{ICR} \cos\beta + (y \cos\beta - x \sin\beta) = 0
$$

- **Geometric meaning (1 mark):** The locus of all permissible ICR positions for a single standard wheel is a **straight line** in the plane, perpendicular to the wheel's direction of rolling. Two standard wheels determine a unique ICR as the intersection of their two axle-lines. Three or more standard wheels require all axle-lines to be concurrent (passing through one common point), or the system is over-constrained.

**(b) Omnidirectional Robot with 4 Mecanum Wheels (7 marks):**

- **Wheel parameters (1 mark):** $N_f = 0$, $N_s = 0$, $N_c^{castor} = 0$, $N_{sp} = 4$.
- **Mobility constraints (1 mark):** $N_c = 0$ (special wheels impose no mobility constraints).
- **Mobility (2 marks):** $\delta_m = 3 - 0 = 3$. The robot can independently translate in $x$, translate in $y$, and rotate.
- **Steerability (1 mark):** $\delta_s = 0$.
- **Maneuverability (1 mark):** $MM = 3 + 0 = 3$.

- **Real-world applications (1 mark — any two):**
  - Industrial Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) in warehouses (e.g., KUKA omniMove, Fetch Robotics Freight) — for tight-space maneuverability and dynamic reconfiguration.
  - Hospital delivery robots that need to navigate narrow corridors and rotate in place.
  - Defense and research platforms requiring holonomic motion (e.g., Carnegie Mellon's autonomous research rovers).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Miscounting constraints:** Students often forget that *castor wheels* and *steering standard wheels* also each contribute **1 mobility constraint**, the same as a fixed standard wheel. The difference is in **steerability**, not mobility constraints.
> 2. **Confusing mobility and maneuverability:** Mobility $\delta_m$ is the *residual* DOF after constraints. Maneuverability $MM$ adds back the steerability. Many students wrongly compute $MM$ as just $3 - N_c$.
> 3. **Special wheels and constraints:** Students sometimes incorrectly state that Swedish/mecanum/ball wheels impose constraints. They impose **zero** constraints — that is the whole point of using them.
> 4. **Ackermann over-constraint confusion:** A standard car has 4 wheels (2 fixed + 2 steering), which by raw counting gives $\delta_m = 3 - 4 = -1$, *impossible*. Students should explain that the **Ackermann steering linkage** mathematically ties the two steering wheels so they share a single axle-line, reducing the effective constraints to 3 and making the maneuverability equal to 1.
> 5. **Differential drive maneuverability of 0:** A common trap question. The robot *can* move because the wheels are actuator-coupled via a **non-holonomic constraint**, not because of additional freedom.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **3 DOF in the plane** for any rigid robot — this is the starting assumption for all wheel geometry problems.
- **Standard wheel** imposes **1 mobility constraint**; the ICR must lie on its **axle-line**.
- **Steering standard wheel** = 1 mobility constraint **+ 1 steerability DOF** (actively controlled).
- **Castor wheel** = 1 mobility constraint + 0 steerability DOF (passively self-aligning).
- **Special wheels** (Swedish, mecanum, ball) = **0 mobility constraints**, **0 steerability** (omnidirectional).
- **Mobility:** $\delta_m = 3 - N_c$.
- **Steerability:** $\delta_s = N_s$.
- **Maneuverability:** $MM = 3 - N_c + N_s$.
- **Two standard wheels** uniquely determine the ICR (intersection of their axle-lines).
- **Three or more standard wheels** require all axle-lines to be **concurrent**.
- **Differential drive** (2 fixed + 1 castor): $MM = 0$ but moves via a **non-holonomic constraint** between the wheels.
- **Synchronous drive** (3 steering standard): $MM = 3$ — full holonomic capability.
- **Mecanum / 4 Swedish wheels:** $MM = 3$ — true holonomic omnidirectional robot.
- **Ackermann car** (2 fixed + 2 steering): requires the **Ackermann linkage** to avoid over-constraint.
- **KTU favorite questions:** classify wheels, derive the ICR constraint equation, compute $MM$ for a given configuration, and explain the geometry of the ICR.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Mobile Robotics**, *stability* refers to the ability of a robotic system to maintain a desired configuration (posture, trajectory, or equilibrium state) when subjected to internal or external perturbations. Formally, a mobile robot's motion is said to be **stable** if, for every small disturbance $\delta \mathbf{x}$ applied about an operating point (or trajectory) $\mathbf{x}_0$, the resulting state deviation remains bounded and, ideally, converges back to the nominal path as time $t \to \infty$.

The two principal classifications of stability studied in PBRUT504 are:

- **Static Stability:** The robot can maintain its configuration under gravity *without* any active motion control. Equilibrium is enforced purely by mechanical/structural constraints.
- **Dynamic Stability:** The robot requires *active control* (continuous motion) to remain upright or on its desired trajectory. The system is stable only because of its control law.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Stability is the foundation block of mobile robot analysis. It precedes the discussion of wheel configurations, kinematics, and dynamic modeling, because without quantifying stability, no motion plan can be certified as executable on a real platform.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are balancing a **broomstick upright on the tip of your finger**. You must constantly wiggle your hand — small, continuous adjustments — to keep it from falling. The broomstick is *dynamically stable* (it needs motion to stay upright). A **four-legged table**, by contrast, simply stands there; gravity and the contact forces of the four legs keep it in place — that is *static stability*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Robot Examples:**
> - **A Spot (Boston Dynamics quadruped) standing still** → dynamically stable (uses active joint torques).
> - **A Toyota HSR wheeled base parked on a slope** → statically stable only if the ground projection of its center of mass lies inside the support polygon.
> - **An inverted pendulum–style Segway** → dynamically stable; falls if controller is switched off.
> - **A two-wheeled differential drive (e.g., a self-balancing warehouse robot)** → purely dynamically stable; must never stop its wheel control loop.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Stability Quantification

- **Stability Margin ($S_m$):** The shortest distance from the projected center of mass (CoM) to the boundary of the support polygon.
- **Zero Moment Point (ZMP):** The point on the ground where the net moment of the inertial and gravitational forces equals zero. For static/dynamic walking robots, the ZMP *must* remain inside the support polygon.
- **Lyapunov Exponent ($\lambda$):** A scalar that quantifies the rate of convergence (or divergence) of nearby trajectories; negative $\lambda$ ⇒ asymptotically stable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Taxonomy of Stability

Mobile roboticists recognize four hierarchical levels of stability:

1. **Stability (Boundedness):** $\vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{x}_0 \vert \leq \epsilon$ for all $t \geq t_0$. The system never escapes a small ball around the equilibrium.
2. **Asymptotic Stability:** Boundedness *plus* $\lim_{t \to \infty} \mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{x}_0$. Trajectories return to the equilibrium.
3. **Exponential Stability:** Convergence happens at a guaranteed exponential rate $\Vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{x}_0 \Vert \leq k e^{-\alpha t} \Vert \mathbf{x}(0) - \mathbf{x}_0 \Vert$.
4. **Global vs Local Stability:** *Local* — holds only in a small neighborhood. *Global* — holds for *any* initial condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** KTU frequently asks to *classify* a system (e.g., "Is the unicycle model asymptotically stable?"). The answer is *no, it is not even stable in the Lyapunov sense under nonholonomic constraints* — it is **stabilizable only via time-varying or discontinuous feedback**.

## 2.2 Static Stability Criterion (Force-Angle) for Wheeled/Legged Robots

For a robot standing on $n$ contact points (wheels or feet), static stability requires:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{F}_i + m\mathbf{g} = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{(force balance)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{F}_i) + \mathbf{r}_{CoM} \times m\mathbf{g} = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{(moment balance)}$$

If these two equations can be satisfied with purely *vertical* (normal) contact forces $\mathbf{F}_i = F_i \hat{\mathbf{n}}$ where $F_i \geq 0$, the configuration is statically stable. The **Force-Angle Stability Measure** is defined as:

$$S_{FA} = \min_i \left( \frac{F_i}{mg} \right) \cdot \frac{d_{min}}{h}$$

where $d_{min}$ is the shortest distance from the CoM projection to a polygon edge, and $h$ is the CoM height.

## 2.3 Zero Moment Point (ZMP) — Core Equation

The ZMP $(p_x, p_y)$ for a robot of mass $m$ and gravity $g$ is computed as:

$$p_x = \frac{\sum_{i} m_i \left[(\ddot{z}_i + g)x_i - \ddot{x}_i z_i \right]}{\sum_{i} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}$$

$$p_y = \frac{\sum_{i} m_i \left[(\ddot{z}_i + g)y_i - \ddot{y}_i z_i \right]}{\sum_{i} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}$$

For a CoM simplified single-mass model:

$$p_x = x_{CoM} - \frac{z_{CoM}}{g} \ddot{x}_{CoM}, \qquad p_y = y_{CoM} - \frac{z_{CoM}}{g} \ddot{y}_{CoM}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ZMP Stability Rule:** A walking/standing robot is dynamically stable **iff** the ZMP lies strictly inside the support polygon defined by the contact feet/wheels. If the ZMP leaves the polygon, the robot tips over.

## 2.4 Lyapunov's Direct Method — The Engineering Workhorse

Consider a nonlinear autonomous system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ with $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{0}) = \mathbf{0}$. Construct a candidate **Lyapunov function** $V(\mathbf{x}): \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ such that:

| Condition | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ | Zero energy at equilibrium |
| $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0, \; \mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ | Positive definite |
| $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = \nabla V \cdot \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ | Energy strictly decreasing |

If such $V$ exists in a neighborhood of origin, the equilibrium is **asymptotically stable**. If it holds over all of $\mathbb{R}^n$, the equilibrium is **globally asymptotically stable**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0, \dot V < 0$ | Lyapunov stability conditions | $V$: scalar energy-like |
| 2 | $p_x = x_c - (z_c/g)\ddot{x}_c$ | ZMP x-coordinate | meters |
| 3 | $p_y = y_c - (z_c/g)\ddot{y}_c$ | ZMP y-coordinate | meters |
| 4 | $S_m = d_{min}$ | Static stability margin | meters |
| 5 | $S_{FA} = \min(F_i/mg) \cdot (d_{min}/h)$ | Force-Angle measure | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\Vert \mathbf{x}(t) \Vert \le k e^{-\alpha t}$ | Exponential stability bound | $\alpha > 0$ |
| 7 | $\dot V \le -c \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2$ | Lyapunov derivative (quadratic) | $c > 0$ |
| 8 | $\mathbf{x}^T P \mathbf{x}$ | Quadratic Lyapunov candidate | $P = P^T > 0$ |
| 9 | $A^T P + P A = -Q$ | Continuous Lyapunov equation | $Q > 0$ given |
| 10 | $\lambda_{\max}(A) < 0$ | Linear-system stability | Eigenvalue test |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Stability theory drives *every* mobile robot you have ever seen balance, walk, or drive precisely — from Segways and ASIMO to warehouse AMRs and Mars rovers. The ZMP is the workhorse behind Honda ASIMO's gait planner.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ZMP from Newton's Equations for a Single Rigid Body

We start with a robot approximated as a single rigid body of mass $m$, with CoM position $\mathbf{r}_c = (x_c, y_c, z_c)$. The total external force on the body is gravity $-m g \hat{\mathbf{z}}$ plus a contact force $\mathbf{F}$ applied at the ZMP $\mathbf{p} = (p_x, p_y, 0)$.

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law (Translational):**

$$m \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_c = \mathbf{F} - m g \hat{\mathbf{z}}$$

Resolve into components. Since the contact force must balance gravity and inertia vertically:

$$F_z = m(g + \ddot{z}_c)$$

**Step 2 — Euler's Equation (Rotational) about CoM:**

The moment about the CoM is generated by the contact force at $\mathbf{p}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}_{CoM} = (\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{r}_c) \times \mathbf{F}$$

For planar motion on the ground (ZMP concept), the moment about the *ground contact point* (origin) is what matters:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}_O = \mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{F} + \mathbf{r}_c \times (-m g \hat{\mathbf{z}}) = \dot{\mathbf{H}}_O$$

For the ZMP definition, we require the *moment about the ZMP along the ground axes* to vanish (i.e., no net tipping moment at the ZMP):

$$(\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{r}_c) \times \mathbf{F} - m g (p_x - x_c)\hat{\mathbf{y}} + m g (p_y - y_c)\hat{\mathbf{x}} = I \boldsymbol{\alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify using vertical contact force assumption:**

If the contact force has only a vertical component $\mathbf{F} = (0, 0, F_z)$, the horizontal moment about the ZMP must balance gravity's moment:

$$m g (p_x - x_c) = -m \ddot{x}_c z_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_x = x_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{x}_c$$

**Step 4 — Analogous derivation for y-axis:**

$$m g (p_y - y_c) = -m \ddot{y}_c z_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_y = y_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{y}_c$$

**Step 5 — Summary block:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_x &= x_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{x}_c \\[4pt]
p_y &= y_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{y}_c
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The ZMP equals the CoM projection *minus* a horizontal offset proportional to the CoM height and its horizontal acceleration. The taller the robot or the faster it accelerates, the further the ZMP shifts — explaining why sprinting robots tip more easily than slow walkers.

## 3.2 Derivation: Lyapunov Stability for a 1-D Inverted Pendulum on a Cart

The classic benchmark for mobile robot stability is the **inverted pendulum on a cart**, governed by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(M+m)\ddot{x} - m\ell \ddot{\theta}\cos\theta + m\ell \dot{\theta}^2 \sin\theta &= F \\
\ell \ddot{\theta} - g\sin\theta - \ddot{x}\cos\theta &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Linearize about the upright equilibrium $\theta = 0$ using $\sin\theta \approx \theta$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$, and neglecting $\dot\theta^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(M+m)\ddot{x} - m\ell \ddot{\theta} &= F \\
\ell \ddot{\theta} - g\theta - \ddot{x} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Define state vector $\mathbf{x} = (x, \dot x, \theta, \dot\theta)^T$. Solve the second equation for $\ddot\theta = (g\theta + \ddot{x})/\ell$ and substitute into the first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(M+m)\ddot{x} - m(g\theta + \ddot{x}) &= F \\
M\ddot{x} - m g \theta &= F
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\ddot{x} = (F + m g \theta)/M$. Substitute back to get $\ddot\theta$ in terms of $\theta$ and $F$. The state-space form is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & -mg/M & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & (M+m)g/(M\ell) & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\mathbf{x} +
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\
1/M \\
0 \\
-1/(M\ell)
\end{bmatrix}
F
$$

The uncontrolled system matrix $A$ has eigenvalues with positive real part (since $(M+m)g/(M\ell) > 0$ yields a real positive root), confirming the **inverted pendulum is open-loop unstable** — a core exam fact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stabilization via State Feedback:** Choose $F = -K\mathbf{x}$ such that $A - BK$ is Hurwitz. For a typical KTU problem, the answer is a PD controller on the pendulum angle: $F = -k_p \theta - k_d \dot\theta$, which yields asymptotic stability for $k_p, k_d > 0$ tuned correctly.

## 3.3 Derivation: Static Stability Margin for a 3-Wheeled Robot

Consider a triangular-wheeled mobile robot with wheel contact points $A$, $B$, $C$ forming a triangle. The CoM projects to point $G$ on the ground plane. The **static stability margin** is:

$$S_m = \min_{i \in \{AB, BC, CA\}} d(G, \text{edge}_i)$$

**Step 1** — Write the equation of line $AB$ in plane: $a x + b y + c = 0$, with $\sqrt{a^2 + b^2} = 1$ (normalized).

**Step 2** — Distance from $G = (x_g, y_g)$ to line $AB$ is:

$$d_{AB} = \vert a x_g + b y_g + c \vert$$

**Step 3** — Repeat for $BC$ and $CA$, then take the minimum:

$$S_m = \min\{d_{AB}, d_{BC}, d_{CA}\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability condition:** $S_m > 0$ (strictly inside the polygon) for *static* stability. If $S_m = 0$ the robot is on the verge of tipping; if $S_m < 0$ the CoM has already crossed a support edge and the robot will tip over under gravity alone.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Stability Verdict for Common Mobile Robots

| Robot Type | Static? | Dynamic? | Primary Stability Mechanism | KTU Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4-wheeled car-like (flat ground) | ✅ Yes | Yes (tracking) | CoM inside wheel-base polygon | Statically stable |
| Differential drive (low CoM) | ✅ Yes | Yes | Wide wheel base | Statically stable |
| 2-wheeled self-balancing (Segway) | ❌ No | ✅ Required | Active IMU + wheel torque | Dynamically stable only |
| Bipedal humanoid (standing) | ❌ No | ✅ Required | ZMP controller | Dynamically stable |
| Quadruped (standing) | ✅ Yes (3+ feet) | Yes | Triangular support polygon | Statically stable if 3 legs down |
| Omnidirectional (4 Mecanum) | ✅ Yes | Yes | Square support polygon | Statically stable |
| Monopedal hopper | ❌ No | ✅ Required | Energy injection per step | Dynamically stable, limit cycle |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stability Classification Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the complete taxonomy of mobile robot stability classifications — the kind of question KTU frequently poses for a 7-mark sub-part.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Stability Analysis of Mobile Robot] --> B1{Velocity at Equilibrium}
    B1 -->|Zero| C1[Static Stability]
    B1 -->|Nonzero| C2[Dynamic Stability]
    C1 --> D1{Support Polygon CoM Check}
    D1 -->|Inside Polygon| E1[Statically Stable]
    D1 -->|On Edge| E2[Critically Stable Margin Zero]
    D1 -->|Outside Polygon| E3[Tip Over Imminent]
    C2 --> D2{Control Loop Active}
    D2 -->|Yes| E4[Dynamically Stabilized]
    D2 -->|No| E5[Open Loop Fails]
    E4 --> F1{Convergence Behavior}
    F1 -->|Lyapunov Stable| G1[Asymptotic Stability]
    F1 -->|Periodic Orbit| G2[Limit Cycle Stability]
    F1 -->|Bounded Oscillation| G3[BIBO Stable]
    E5 --> H1[System Unstable Falls]
```

## 4.2 ZMP Computation and Validation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Robot State Sensors IMU Encoders] --> A2[CoM Position and Velocity Estimator]
    A2 --> A3[Compute ZMP Using p x and p y Equations]
    A3 --> A4[Define Support Polygon from Contact Foot Geometry]
    A4 --> A5{ZMP Inside Polygon}
    A5 -->|Yes| A6[Stable Step Verified]
    A5 -->|No| A7[Trigger Foot Placement Replanning]
    A6 --> A8[Execute Next Gait Phase]
    A7 --> A8
```

## 4.3 Lyapunov Stability Verification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Nonlinear System dot x equals f of x] --> L2{Equilibrium Identified}
    L2 --> L3[Propose Candidate V of x]
    L3 --> L4{V Positive Definite}
    L4 -->|No| L5[Reject V Try Another]
    L4 -->|Yes| L6[Compute dot V equals grad V dot f]
    L6 --> L7{dot V Negative Definite}
    L7 -->|Yes| L8[Asymptotic Stability Proved]
    L7 -->|No| L9[Check Negative Semidefinite Plus LaSalle]
    L9 -->|Conclusive| L10[Stability Result]
    L9 -->|Inconclusive| L11[Try Other V or Use Other Method]
```

## 4.4 Support Polygon and CoM Projection Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP[Support Polygon on Ground Plane]
        P1[Contact Foot A] --- P2[Contact Foot B]
        P2 --- P3[Contact Foot C]
        P3 --- P1
    end
    M1[Robot Body CoM Above Polygon] --> M2[Project CoM Vertically to Ground]
    M2 --> M3{Projection Inside Polygon}
    M3 -->|Yes| M4[Static Stability Margin equals d min]
    M3 -->|No| M5[Tipping Instability]
    H1[CoM Height h] --> M4
    M4 --> M6[Compute Force Angle Measure S FA]
    M6 --> M7[Stability Verdict Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block-level diagrams and not physical sketches?** Stability in mobile robotics is fundamentally a *mathematical* and *control-theoretic* property. Block diagrams are the canonical engineering representation. Mermaid is the cleanest, version-control-friendly notation for KTU assignments and project reports.
