<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define static stability margin for a mobile robot. How is it computed for a three-wheeled robot?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The static stability margin $S_m$ is the *shortest distance* from the vertical projection of the robot's center of mass (CoM) onto the ground plane to the nearest edge of the support polygon formed by the contact points (wheels/feet).

For a three-wheeled robot with contact points $A$, $B$, $C$ forming a triangle on the ground, the support polygon is $\triangle ABC$. If the CoM projects to point $G = (x_g, y_g)$, the margin is:

$$S_m = \min\{d(G, \overline{AB}),\; d(G, \overline{BC}),\; d(G, \overline{CA})\}$$

where $d(G, \overline{XY})$ is the perpendicular distance from $G$ to edge $\overline{XY}$. A positive $S_m$ means static stability; a zero or negative value indicates imminent tipping. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Zero Moment Point (ZMP)? State the condition for dynamic stability of a biped robot using ZMP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is the specific point on the ground (or support surface) at which the net moment of all active forces — gravity and inertial forces — about the horizontal axes is equal to zero. **(1 mark)**

For a single-CoM model, the ZMP coordinates are:

$$p_x = x_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{x}_c, \qquad p_y = y_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{y}_c$$

**(1 mark)**

**Stability condition:** A biped robot is dynamically stable *if and only if* the computed ZMP lies strictly inside the support polygon defined by the contact feet. If the ZMP exits the polygon, the robot will tip over. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the ZMP equations for a mobile robot modeled as a single rigid body. Show all the steps from Newton's second law and Euler's rotational equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A humanoid robot has its CoM at height $z_c = 0.9$ m, with $x_c = 0$ m, $y_c = 0$ m. During forward walking, the CoM accelerates horizontally at $\ddot{x}_c = 0.5$ m/s² and $\ddot{y}_c = 0$ m/s². The support polygon is a rectangle with corners at $(\pm 0.10, \pm 0.05)$ m. Determine if the robot is dynamically stable. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Translational equilibrium (Newton's second law):** The robot is approximated as a single rigid body of mass $m$ with CoM at $\mathbf{r}_c$. External forces are gravity $-m g \hat{\mathbf{z}}$ and a contact force $\mathbf{F}$ applied at the ZMP $\mathbf{p}$:

$$m \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_c = \mathbf{F} - m g \hat{\mathbf{z}}$$

**Step 2 — Vertical force balance:** The vertical component must balance gravity and vertical inertia:

$$F_z = m(g + \ddot{z}_c)$$

**Step 3 — Moment equation about origin:** Total moment about the ZMP must vanish by definition (ZMP is the point of zero horizontal moment):

$$\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{F} + \mathbf{r}_c \times (-m g \hat{\mathbf{z}}) = I_O \boldsymbol{\alpha} + \mathbf{r}_c \times m \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_c$$

**Step 4 — Take x-component and assume $\mathbf{F}$ has only a vertical component (i.e., $F_x = F_y = 0$):**

$$m g (p_x - x_c) = -m \ddot{x}_c z_c$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $p_x$:**

$$p_x = x_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{x}_c$$

**Step 6 — Repeat for y-axis:**

$$p_y = y_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{y}_c$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{p_x = x_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{x}_c, \qquad p_y = y_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{y}_c}
$$

This derivation (Steps 1–6) is worth the full 7 marks. **Distribution:** Step 1 (1), Step 2 (1), Step 3 (1), Step 4 (1), Step 5 (1), Step 6 (1), Step 7 (1).

---

**(b) Numerical verification (7 marks):**

**Given:** $z_c = 0.9$ m, $x_c = 0$, $y_c = 0$, $\ddot{x}_c = 0.5$ m/s², $\ddot{y}_c = 0$ m/s², $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Compute ZMP x-coordinate:**

$$p_x = x_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{x}_c = 0 - \frac{0.9 \times 0.5}{9.81} = -\frac{0.45}{9.81}$$

$$p_x \approx -0.0459 \text{ m} \approx -4.59 \text{ cm} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ZMP y-coordinate:**

$$p_y = y_c - \frac{z_c}{g} \ddot{y}_c = 0 - \frac{0.9 \times 0}{9.81} = 0 \text{ m} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Identify support polygon boundary:** The rectangle spans $x \in [-0.10, +0.10]$ m, $y \in [-0.05, +0.05]$ m.

**Step 4 — Check if ZMP is inside:** $p_x = -0.0459$ m is within $[-0.10, +0.10]$ ✓. $p_y = 0$ is within $[-0.05, +0.05]$ ✓. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Compute stability margins:**

$$S_{mx} = \min(p_x - (-0.10),\; 0.10 - p_x) = \min(0.0541, 0.1459) = 0.0541 \text{ m} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

$$S_{my} = 0.05 - 0 = 0.05 \text{ m} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** Since $(p_x, p_y) = (-0.046, 0)$ lies *inside* the support polygon with positive margin in both axes, the robot is **dynamically stable** during this walking phase. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice). `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** State and explain Lyapunov's direct method of stability. Construct a Lyapunov function for the linear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$ where $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$ and prove asymptotic stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-wheeled self-balancing robot (inverted pendulum on a cart) has the linearized dynamics matrix:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -9.81 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 19.62 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Verify that the open-loop system is unstable. Design a state-feedback gain $K = [k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4]$ so that the closed-loop system $A - BK$ is asymptotically stable, choosing $B = [0, 0.5, 0, -0.5]^T$. Suggest one valid set of gains. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Lyapunov's direct method (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Statement:** Lyapunov's direct method states that for an autonomous system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ with equilibrium at $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$, if there exists a continuously differentiable scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ such that $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$, $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$, and $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ in some neighborhood, then the equilibrium is **asymptotically stable**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Quadratic candidate:** For linear systems, choose $V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T P \mathbf{x}$ with $P = P^T > 0$. Then:

$$\dot{V} = \dot{\mathbf{x}}^T P \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{x}^T P \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x}^T(A^T P + P A)\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 3 — Lyapunov equation:** Choose any $Q = Q^T > 0$ and solve $A^T P + P A = -Q$. A convenient choice is $Q = I$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Solve for $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$:** Let $P = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix}$. Compute $A^T P + P A$:

$$
A^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A^T P = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 p_{12} & -2 p_{22} \\ p_{11} - 3 p_{12} & p_{12} - 3 p_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P A = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 p_{12} & p_{11} - 3 p_{12} \\ -2 p_{22} & p_{12} - 3 p_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A^T P + P A = \begin{bmatrix} -4 p_{12} & p_{11} - 3 p_{12} - 2 p_{22} \\ p_{11} - 3 p_{12} - 2 p_{22} & 2 p_{12} - 6 p_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Set equal to $-I$:

$$-4 p_{12} = -1 \Rightarrow p_{12} = 0.25$$

$$2 p_{12} - 6 p_{22} = -1 \Rightarrow 0.5 - 6 p_{22} = -1 \Rightarrow p_{22} = 0.25$$

$$p_{11} - 3(0.25) - 2(0.25) = 0 \Rightarrow p_{11} = 1.25$$

**Step 5 — Verify positive definiteness of $P$:**

$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 1.25 & 0.25 \\ 0.25 & 0.25 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(P) = 1.25 \times 0.25 - 0.0625 = 0.3125 - 0.0625 = 0.25 > 0$$

Since $p_{11} > 0$ and $\det(P) > 0$, $P > 0$. **Therefore the equilibrium is asymptotically stable.** **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Self-balancing robot analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Eigenvalues of open-loop $A$:** The system decouples into a cart-mode and pendulum-mode. The pendulum block is:

$$A_p = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 19.62 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Characteristic equation: $\lambda^2 - 19.62 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \pm \sqrt{19.62} = \pm 4.43$ rad/s. **(1 mark)**

Since one eigenvalue is positive (4.43), the system is **unstable in open loop**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Closed-loop with $B = [0, 0.5, 0, -0.5]^T$:** Choose $K = [k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4]$. Then:

$$A - BK = A - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.5 \\ 0 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix} [k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4]$$

The new third-row contribution: $-(-0.5) k_3 = 0.5 k_3$ added to $A(3,3) = 0$, and $0.5 k_4$ added to $A(3,4) = 1$. Similarly for other rows. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Pick gains for stability (try $k_1 = 10$, $k_2 = 10$, $k_3 = -40$, $k_4 = -10$):** With these gains, the closed-loop pendulum block becomes $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 19.62 + 20 & -5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 39.62 & -5 \end{bmatrix}$. **(1 mark)**

Characteristic equation: $\lambda^2 + 5\lambda - 39.62 = 0$. This has discriminant $25 + 158.48 = 183.48 > 0$, giving one positive root — still unstable. Re-tune: choose $k_3 = -100$, $k_4 = -15$. Then the block becomes $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -30.38 & -8.5 \end{bmatrix}$. **(1 mark)**

Characteristic equation: $\lambda^2 + 8.5\lambda + 30.38 = 0$. Discriminant $= 72.25 - 121.52 = -49.27 < 0$, yielding complex roots with negative real part $-4.25$. **Stable!** **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Conclude:** A valid stabilizing gain is $K = [10, 10, -100, -15]$; the closed-loop system is asymptotically stable. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse static stability with dynamic stability.** Many students write "the robot is stable" without specifying *which* stability class. KTU examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for this imprecision.
> 2. **In ZMP derivations, students frequently forget the $z_c/g$ factor.** Always double-check the sign convention: $\ddot{x}_c$ *forward* shifts ZMP *backward* relative to CoM.
> 3. **Lyapunov function choice is critical.** A wrong $V$ (not positive definite, or with $\dot V$ not negative) means *zero marks* for the stability proof step, even if the rest of the derivation is correct.
> 4. **For the inverted pendulum, do not claim the linearized system is the same as the nonlinear one.** Mention "linearized about $\theta = 0$" explicitly.
> 5. **Units, units, units.** ZMP and stability margin must be in meters. $g$ in m/s². Show units in *every* numerical substitution.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Static stability** = gravity + support polygon sufficient; **Dynamic stability** = requires active control.
- ✅ **Static stability margin** = shortest distance from CoM projection to support polygon edge; must be > 0.
- ✅ **ZMP formulas (CoM model):** $p_x = x_c - (z_c/g)\ddot{x}_c$, $p_y = y_c - (z_c/g)\ddot{y}_c$.
- ✅ **ZMP must lie strictly inside the support polygon** for dynamic walking stability.
- ✅ **Lyapunov's direct method:** $V > 0$ and $\dot V < 0$ ⇒ asymptotic stability.
- ✅ **Lyapunov equation:** $A^T P + P A = -Q$ with $Q > 0$ guarantees $P > 0$ ⇒ origin stable.
- ✅ **Force-Angle measure:** $S_{FA} = \min(F_i/mg) \cdot d_{min}/h$ — combines force and geometric criteria.
- ✅ **Inverted pendulum on cart is open-loop unstable** — must use feedback (PD or LQR) to stabilize.
- ✅ **Two-wheeled balancing robots are *purely* dynamically stable** — never statically stable.
- ✅ **Limit cycle stability** (Hopf, Poincaré) is the formal mechanism behind stable walking gaits.
- ✅ **Linearization validity** for pendulum requires small angles ($\theta \ll 1$ rad); KTU expects this disclaimer.
- ✅ **Hurwitz criterion** for linear systems: all eigenvalues of $A$ in left half-plane ⇒ asymptotically stable.
- ✅ **Exponential vs asymptotic:** exponential is stronger; rate constant $\alpha > 0$ matters in practice.
- ✅ **Local vs global stability:** nonlinear systems (e.g., unicycle) are often only *locally* stabilizable.
- ✅ **Exam mantra for stability proofs:** "Define V → Show V > 0 → Compute $\dot V$ → Show $\dot V < 0$ → Conclude."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maneuverability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maneuverability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maneuverability** of a wheeled mobile robot is formally defined as the *combined capacity of the robot to achieve arbitrary motions in its workspace*, quantified as the **sum of its Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$)** and **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$)**.

Mathematically, the **maneuverability index** is expressed as:

$$M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$

where:
- **$\delta_m$ (Degree of Mobility)**: The number of degrees of freedom (DOF) of the robot's *motion* that can be **instantaneously** achieved in the plane (ignoring steering actuation). It is dictated purely by the rolling and sliding constraints imposed by the wheels.
- **$\delta_s$ (Degree of Steerability)**: The number of degrees of freedom corresponding to the *steering angles* of the wheels that can be independently controlled to alter the robot's maneuverability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic**: A robot is **holonomic** when $\delta_m = 3$ (it can translate in any direction and rotate independently at any instant). If $\delta_m < 3$, it is **non-holonomic** — meaning it cannot move sideways or achieve arbitrary configurations instantaneously; it must perform complex maneuvers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy: The Supermarket Trolley vs. A Car**
>
> Imagine pushing a **supermarket trolley** (which has 4 castor wheels that swivel freely). You can push it straight, sideways, diagonally — almost in *any* direction, and it can rotate about its own center. It has **high maneuverability**.
>
> Now imagine a **car** (Ackermann-steered). It has fixed front and rear wheels that cannot slide sideways. The car *cannot* move sideways directly — to move left, it must go forward, turn the steering, and then straighten. Its **maneuverability is restricted**, even though it has steering.
>
> This difference is exactly what **Mobility ($\delta_m$)** captures. The trolley has $\delta_m = 3$ (holonomic); the car has $\delta_m = 2$ (it can only translate forward/backward and rotate — not move sideways).

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Notation

| Symbol | Meaning | Range / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $M$ | Maneuverability index | Integer (0 to 5 typical) |
| $\delta_m$ | Degree of Mobility | 0, 1, 2, or **3** (DOF) |
| $\delta_s$ | Degree of Steerability | 0, 1, 2, or **3** |
| $N$ | Total number of wheels | Dimensionless (count) |
| $N_f$ | Number of fixed standard wheels | Dimensionless |
| $N_s$ | Number of steerable standard wheels | Dimensionless |
| $N_{sw}$ | Number of Swedish (omni) wheels | Dimensionless |
| $N_c$ | Number of castor wheels | Dimensionless |
| $C_f$ | Total number of non-redundant fixed constraints | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard ceiling:** For any robot in a 2D plane, $\delta_m \le 3$ and $\delta_s \le 3$, so the **theoretical maximum maneuverability is $M \le 6$**. No real robot achieves $M = 6$ in practice due to mechanical and actuation constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wheel Classification — The Foundation of Maneuverability

Every wheel type imposes a specific number of **kinematic constraints** on the robot's motion. These constraints directly determine $\delta_m$ and $\delta_s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Favorite Wheel Types (4 Mandatory Variants):**

### A. Fixed Standard Wheel
- **Configuration**: Wheel axle is *rigidly fixed* to the chassis; the wheel plane cannot rotate.
- **Constraints Imposed**: **2 constraints** (one for rolling without slip, one preventing lateral sliding).
- **Example use**: Rear wheels of a car.

### B. Steerable Standard Wheel
- **Configuration**: Wheel axle is mounted on a steering mechanism that can rotate the wheel plane about a vertical axis.
- **Constraints Imposed**: **2 constraints** (same as fixed wheel), **BUT** the steering rotation contributes **1 extra DOF** to $\delta_s$.
- **Example use**: Front wheels of a car, bicycle front wheel.

### C. Swedish Wheel / Mecanum Wheel
- **Configuration**: Standard wheel with small passive rollers around its circumference (at $0°$ or $45°$).
- **Constraints Imposed**: **1 constraint** (only rolling — lateral motion is permitted by the free-spinning rollers).
- **Example use**: Omnidirectional robots (KUKA youBot, etc.).

### D. Castor Wheel
- **Configuration**: Steerable wheel where the steering axis is *offset* from the contact point and is *passively* free to rotate (not actuated).
- **Constraints Imposed**: **1 constraint** (passive steering releases the lateral constraint).
- **Example use**: Office chair wheels, support wheels on differential drives.

## 2.2 The Mobility Equation (Siegwart's Formulation)

The **Degree of Mobility** is calculated using the *fixed constraint* approach:

$$\delta_m = 3 - C_f$$

where $C_f$ is the total number of effective non-redundant constraints:

$$C_f = 2N_f + 2N_s + N_{sw} + N_c$$

The **Degree of Steerability** is:

$$\delta_s = \min(3, N_s)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A negative $\delta_m$ indicates the robot is **over-constrained** and physically cannot move. Some constraints may also be **redundant** (linearly dependent) — students must verify using the rank of the Jacobian matrix.

## 2.3 The Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The ICR is the unique point in the plane about which the *entire robot body* is instantaneously rotating. It may lie *inside* or *outside* the robot's footprint.

For a **fixed standard wheel** at position $(x, y)$ with wheel plane at angle $\alpha$, the ICR must lie on the line **perpendicular to the wheel plane** through the contact point. The geometric condition is:

$$(X - x)\cos\alpha + (Y - y)\sin\alpha = 0$$

where $(X, Y)$ are the coordinates of the ICR.

For a robot with **multiple fixed standard wheels**, the ICR is the **intersection point** of all such perpendicular lines.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

| Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Total maneuverability index | Dimensionless |
| $\delta_m = 3 - C_f$ | Degree of mobility | DOF in plane |
| $\delta_s = N_s$ | Degree of steerability (actuated only) | DOF |
| $C_f = 2N_f + 2N_s + N_{sw} + N_c$ | Total fixed constraints | Count |
| $\dot{x}\sin\alpha - \dot{y}\cos\alpha = 0$ | Rolling constraint (fixed wheel) | Velocity eqn. |
| $(X - x)\cos\alpha + (Y - y)\sin\alpha = 0$ | ICR perpendicular line | Geometric eqn. |
| $\vec{v} = \omega \hat{k} \times \vec{r}_{ICR \to P}$ | Velocity of any point P on robot | Cross-product form |
| $\delta_m = 3$ | Condition for holonomic | Full plane mobility |
| $v_{lateral} = 0$ | Non-holonomic condition | No sideways motion |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Trick:** *"**T**wo for **F**ixed, **T**wo for **S**teerable, **O**ne for **S**wedish, **O**ne for **C**astor"* — **TFTOSO** (the constraint counts).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva)**: Uses differential drives with $\delta_m = 2$ — high reliability, simple control, sufficient for structured aisles.
- **Omnidirectional Hospital Robots**: Use Swedish/Mecanum wheels ($\delta_m = 3$) to navigate cluttered spaces with strict motion constraints.
- **Autonomous Cars (Waymo, Tesla)**: Ackermann steering with $\delta_m = 2$ and $\delta_s = 1$ — favors speed and stability over maneuverability.
- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance)**: Rocker-bogie with steerable wheels — high $\delta_s$ for terrain adaptability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rolling Constraint of a Fixed Standard Wheel

> **Objective:** Show that the velocity of the wheel contact point must be tangent to the wheel plane (no lateral slip).

**Setup:**
- Let the wheel contact point have world coordinates $(x, y)$.
- The wheel plane is oriented at angle $\alpha$ with respect to the world X-axis.
- The wheel plane direction unit vector is $\hat{u} = (\cos\alpha, \sin\alpha)$.
- The lateral (perpendicular) direction unit vector is $\hat{n} = (-\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha)$.

**Step 1:** The instantaneous velocity of the contact point is:

$$\vec{v}_P = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** The lateral (no-slip) condition requires that there is no velocity component perpendicular to the wheel plane:

$$\vec{v}_P \cdot \hat{n} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the vectors:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{v}_P \cdot \hat{n} &= (\dot{x}, \dot{y}) \cdot (-\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha) \\
&= -\dot{x}\sin\alpha + \dot{y}\cos\alpha
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Setting this equal to zero gives the **rolling constraint equation**:

$$\boxed{\;-\dot{x}\sin\alpha + \dot{y}\cos\alpha = 0\;}$$

> This is the *first* of two constraints for a fixed standard wheel. The second (sliding constraint) prevents motion along the lateral direction — already implied here.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Constraint of a Steerable Standard Wheel

> **Objective:** Extend the fixed wheel derivation to include the steering angle $\beta(t)$.

**Setup:**
- The wheel plane is at angle $\alpha + \beta(t)$, where $\beta$ is the time-varying steering angle.
- Let the wheel center be at offset $l$ from the robot's reference point (rotates as robot turns).
- The robot's angular velocity is $\dot{\theta}$.

**Step 1:** Replace $\alpha$ with $(\alpha + \beta)$ in the rolling constraint:

$$-\dot{x}\sin(\alpha + \beta) + \dot{y}\cos(\alpha + \beta) = 0$$

**Step 2:** The steering rotation rate $\dot{\beta}$ contributes a term proportional to the offset:

$$-\dot{x}\sin(\alpha + \beta) + \dot{y}\cos(\alpha + \beta) + l\,\dot{\beta} = 0$$

**Step 3:** In matrix form, this becomes a constraint on the robot's configuration velocity vector $\vec{\xi} = (\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})^T$:

$$J_1(\beta)\, \vec{\xi} + J_2(\beta)\, \dot{\beta} = 0$$

where $J_1$ and $J_2$ are the appropriate Jacobian rows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** For a steerable wheel, the orientation $\alpha$ becomes *configuration-dependent* — this is what introduces the steering DOF and reduces the *effective* fixed constraint count from 2 to 1 for the purpose of mobility analysis.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: ICR Position from Wheel Geometry

> **Objective:** Find the ICR coordinates $(X, Y)$ given a single fixed standard wheel.

**Step 1:** The wheel is at $(x_w, y_w)$ with orientation $\alpha$. The wheel can only move in an arc, so the ICR lies on a line **perpendicular to the wheel plane** passing through the contact point.

**Step 2:** The line equation is:

$$(X - x_w)\cos\alpha + (Y - y_w)\sin\alpha = 0$$

**Step 3:** For a *second* fixed wheel at $(x_w', y_w')$ with orientation $\alpha'$, the ICR must also satisfy:

$$(X - x_w')\cos\alpha' + (Y - y_w')\sin\alpha' = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solving this $2 \times 2$ linear system:

$$\begin{aligned}
X\cos\alpha + Y\sin\alpha &= x_w\cos\alpha + y_w\sin\alpha \\
X\cos\alpha' + Y\sin\alpha' &= x_w'\cos\alpha' + y_w'\sin\alpha'
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Using Cramer's rule (or matrix inversion), we get the unique ICR location:

$$X = \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta}, \quad Y = \frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta}$$

where $\Delta = \cos\alpha\sin\alpha' - \cos\alpha'\sin\alpha = \sin(\alpha' - \alpha)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Singularity Alert:** If $\alpha' = \alpha$ (parallel wheels), the determinant $\Delta = 0$ and the ICR is at **infinity** — the robot undergoes **pure translation** along the wheel plane direction.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Maneuverability of Standard Robot Configurations

| Robot Type | $N_f$ | $N_s$ | $N_{sw}$ | $N_c$ | $C_f$ | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $M$ | Holonomic? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Differential Drive (2 fixed + 1 castor) | 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **5** | 2 | 0 | **2** | No |
| Differential Drive (2 fixed + 2 castor) | 2 | 0 | 0 | 2 | **6** | -2 (over) | 0 | -2 | No motion* |
| Tricycle (1 fixed + 2 steerable) | 1 | 2 | 0 | 0 | **5** | 2 | 2 | **4** | No |
| Car (Ackermann, 2 fixed + 2 steerable) | 2 | 2 | 0 | 0 | **8** | -1 (over) | 2 | 1 | No** |
| Omnidirectional (3 Swedish, 120°) | 0 | 0 | 3 | 0 | **3** | 0 | 0 | **0** | No (stationary) |
| Omnidirectional (4 Mecanum, 90°) | 0 | 0 | 4 | 0 | **4** | 3 | 0 | **3** | **Yes** |
| Synchro Drive (3 steerable) | 0 | 3 | 0 | 0 | **6** | -3 (over) | 3 | 0 | No*** |
| Omnidirectional (3 Swedish + 1 fixed) | 1 | 0 | 3 | 0 | **5** | 2 | 0 | **2** | No |

> \* **Over-constrained** — this configuration cannot move; the 4th castor adds a redundant constraint.
> \** **Resolved by Ackermann geometry:** The 2 steerable wheels are *coupled* to satisfy 1 constraint, so effective $C_f = 7$, giving $\delta_m = 2$ with $\delta_s = 1$.
> \*** **Synchro drive** is feasible because all wheels rotate and steer synchronously, making many constraints dependent. Effective $C_f = 3$, so $\delta_m = 0$ and $\delta_s = 3$.

## 3.5 Worked Example: 3-Wheel Omnidirectional Robot (120° Swedish)

**Given:** A robot has 3 Swedish wheels arranged at 120° apart, each oriented at angles $0°, 120°, 240°$.

**Step 1:** Calculate $C_f$:

$$C_f = 2N_f + 2N_s + N_{sw} + N_c = 2(0) + 2(0) + 3(1) + 0 = 3$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $\delta_m$:

$$\delta_m = 3 - C_f = 3 - 3 = 0$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $\delta_s$:

$$\delta_s = N_s = 0$$

**Step 4:** Total maneuverability:

$$M = 0 + 0 = 0$$

**Interpretation:** The robot has $M = 0$, which seems contradictory for an "omnidirectional" robot. This is a **classic KTU trap**!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction:** The 3-Swedish-wheel robot is in fact **fully omnidirectional**. The above calculation fails because the 3 rolling constraints are *not* independent — they are **linearly dependent** (their sum equals zero). So the *effective* $C_f$ is **2**, not 3. The corrected maneuverability is $M = 3 - 2 = 1$ motion DOF (the rotation), and the steering is provided by differential wheel speeds.
>
> **Moral:** Always verify constraint independence using the rank of the constraint matrix.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Maneuverability Analysis Architecture (Flowchart)

The following block diagram shows the complete analysis pipeline from robot description to maneuverability index:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mobile Robot Description] --> B[Identify Wheel Count and Types]
    B --> C1[Fixed Standard Wheels]
    B --> C2[Steerable Standard Wheels]
    B --> C3[Swedish or Mecanum Wheels]
    B --> C4[Castor Wheels]
    C1 --> D[Count Constraints per Type]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E["Compute Cf = 2Nf + 2Ns + Nsw + Nc"]
    E --> F{Is Cf less than 3?}
    F -->|No, Cf greater than 3| G[Over-Constrained Check]
    G --> H{Constraints Independent?}
    H -->|No, Redundant| I[Reduce Cf by Rank]
    H -->|Yes, all Independent| J[Mobility equals 3 minus Cf]
    I --> J
    F -->|Yes, Cf less than 3| J
    J --> K["delta_m equals 3 minus Cf_effective"]
    K --> L["delta_s equals min of 3 and Ns"]
    L --> M["M equals delta_m plus delta_s"]
    M --> N{Is delta_m equals 3?}
    N -->|Yes| O[HOLONOMIC Robot]
    N -->|No| P[NON-HOLONOMIC Robot]
    O --> Q[Can achieve any motion instantly]
    P --> R[Requires maneuver for lateral motion]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style O fill:#d4f4d4
    style P fill:#ffe1e1
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]
**Define maneuverability of a mobile robot. Differentiate between degree of mobility and degree of steerability.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Maneuverability** is the combined ability of a wheeled mobile robot to achieve arbitrary motions in its workspace, quantified as $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$. **[1 mark]**
>
> **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$)** is the number of degrees of freedom of the *robot's motion* in the plane that can be achieved instantaneously (ignoring steering). It depends on the rolling and sliding constraints of the wheels. **[1 mark]**
>
> **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$)** is the number of independently controllable steering DOFs of the wheels. It depends on actuated steering mechanisms. **[1 mark]**
>
> *Example: A differential drive robot has $\delta_m = 2$ (forward/backward + rotation) and $\delta_s = 0$, giving $M = 2$.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**What is an Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)? Why is it important in mobile robot kinematics?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is the unique point in the plane about which the entire robot body is instantaneously rotating. It may lie inside or outside the robot's footprint. **[1.5 marks]**
>
> **Importance in Kinematics:** **[1.5 marks]**
> 1. It determines the *direction* of velocity of every point on the robot (perpendicular to the line joining the point to the ICR).
> 2. The ICR must lie on a line perpendicular to each fixed standard wheel's plane.
> 3. It is used to analyze whether the robot can move in a desired direction without reorienting its wheels.
> 4. For a holonomic robot, the ICR can be at *any* location (including at infinity for pure translation).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Convention:** Students must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the kinematic rolling constraint equation for a **fixed standard wheel** and a **steerable standard wheel**. Clearly state all assumptions and explain how these constraints affect the mobility of the robot.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A four-wheeled mobile robot is designed with **two fixed standard wheels** at the rear and **two Swedish wheels** at the front (arranged symmetrically). Calculate the degree of mobility ($\delta_m$) and degree of steerability ($\delta_s$) of this robot. Comment on whether the robot is holonomic and discuss its maneuverability in practical applications.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Assumptions:**
- Robot moves in a 2D plane (3 DOF: $x, y, \theta$).
- No slip at the wheel-ground contact for rolling constraint.
- The wheel is a rigid disk.

**Derivation for Fixed Standard Wheel:**

Let the wheel contact point be at $(x, y)$ in the world frame, with the wheel plane oriented at angle $\alpha$.

The wheel plane unit vector is $\hat{u} = (\cos\alpha, \sin\alpha)$.
The lateral (perpendicular) unit vector is $\hat{n} = (-\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha)$.

For no lateral slip, the velocity component perpendicular to the wheel plane must vanish:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{v}_P \cdot \hat{n} &= (\dot{x}, \dot{y}) \cdot (-\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha) = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad -\dot{x}\sin\alpha + \dot{y}\cos\alpha &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**[2 marks]** for the derivation.

Additionally, the rolling without slip condition relates wheel rotation $\dot{\phi}$ to the translation along the wheel plane:

$$\dot{x}\cos\alpha + \dot{y}\sin\alpha = r\dot{\phi}$$

where $r$ is the wheel radius. **[1 mark]**

So a fixed standard wheel imposes **2 independent constraints**.

**Derivation for Steerable Standard Wheel:**

For a steerable wheel, the orientation becomes $(\alpha + \beta(t))$ where $\beta$ is the steering angle. **[1 mark]**

The constraint equations become:

$$-\dot{x}\sin(\alpha + \beta) + \dot{y}\cos(\alpha + \beta) + l\dot{\beta} = 0$$

where $l$ is the offset of the wheel center from the robot's reference point. **[2 marks]**

**Effect on Mobility:** Each fixed standard wheel removes 1 DOF from the robot's motion. A steerable wheel has 2 constraints, but introduces 1 extra steering DOF, effectively contributing only 1 net constraint to mobility (plus 1 to steerability). **[1 mark]**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $N_f = 2$ (two fixed standard wheels at the rear)
- $N_s = 0$ (no steerable standard wheels)
- $N_{sw} = 2$ (two Swedish wheels at the front)
- $N_c = 0$ (no castor wheels)

**Step 1: Calculate $C_f$.** **[2 marks]**

$$C_f = 2N_f + 2N_s + N_{sw} + N_c = 2(2) + 2(0) + 2 + 0 = 6$$

**Step 2: Check for redundancy.** **[1 mark]**

For a 4-wheel robot, $C_f = 6$ exceeds the total 3 DOF. We must check the rank of the constraint matrix. Assuming the wheels are arranged symmetrically, exactly **1 constraint is redundant** (since the Swedish wheels share the same axis symmetry).

Effective $C_f = 6 - 1 = 5$ ... *Wait, this still exceeds 3!*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction:** Re-examine — Swedish wheels each add 1 constraint, fixed wheels each add 2. For 2 fixed + 2 Swedish:
> - Fixed wheels: $2 \times 2 = 4$ constraints
> - Swedish wheels: $2 \times 1 = 2$ constraints
> - Total raw: $6$ constraints
> - Since the system has 3 DOF, the *maximum* effective constraints is 3 (any more would be redundant or over-constraining).
> - For symmetric 4-wheel layout: 2 of the 6 are redundant. **Effective $C_f = 4$.**

**Step 3: Compute $\delta_m$.** **[1.5 marks]**

$$\delta_m = 3 - C_f = 3 - 4 = -1$$

**Step 4: Re-examination.** **[1.5 marks]**

A negative $\delta_m$ indicates over-constraining. However, with the 2 Swedish wheels permitting lateral motion, the effective configuration is similar to a **differential drive with omni-support wheels**:
- Forward/backward translation: ✓
- Rotation: ✓
- Pure lateral translation: ✓ (due to Swedish wheels)
- Thus, the practical $\delta_m = 3$ (holonomic), but the standard formula gives a lower bound.

> **Conclusion:** The robot is **practically holonomic** with $\delta_m = 3$ and $\delta_s = 0$, giving $M = 3$. It is suitable for **warehouse automation** where omnidirectional motion is required. **[1 mark]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Define **holonomic** and **non-holonomic** mobile robots. Provide **two examples** of each type with diagrams and explain the practical significance of this distinction.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, classify the different types of wheels used in mobile robotics. For each type, specify the **number of kinematic constraints** imposed and explain the physical reasoning using force/motion analysis.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Holonomic Robot:** **[2 marks]**

A robot is holonomic if the *number of controllable DOF* equals the *total DOF* of the robot in its workspace. For a 2D mobile robot:

$$\delta_m = 3 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \text{Holonomic}$$

A holonomic robot can move to any $(x, y, \theta)$ configuration in finite time without reorientation maneuvers.

**Examples:**
1. **3-Mecanum wheel robot** (omnidirectional) — can move in any planar direction. **[1 mark]**
2. **4-Swedish wheel robot (orthogonal arrangement)** — full planar mobility. **[1 mark]**

**Non-Holonomic Robot:** **[2 marks]**

A non-holonomic robot has $\delta_m < 3$. It cannot move instantaneously in all directions; lateral motion is restricted.

**Examples:**
1. **Differential drive robot** ($\delta_m = 2$) — can move forward/backward and rotate, but cannot move sideways without an arc trajectory. **[1 mark]**
2. **Ackermann-steered car** ($\delta_m = 2$) — same restriction; must use parallel parking maneuvers. **[1 mark]**

**Practical Significance:** **[0 marks — covered in examples, but add note]**

> **Note:** Holonomic robots are *easier to control* for path-tracking (no complex maneuvers needed), but they are *mechanically more complex* (require omni/special wheels) and have *lower payload capacity*. Non-holonomic robots are *simpler, more robust*, and favored for outdoor/large-scale applications (cars, rovers).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

| Wheel Type | Diagram Description | No. of Constraints | Physical Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Fixed Standard** | Disk with axle rigidly fixed to chassis | **2** | Wheel rolls along its plane (1 constraint on $\dot{\phi}$); cannot slide laterally (1 constraint on $v_\perp$). |
| **Steerable Standard** | Disk mounted on a rotating fork | **2** + 1 steering DOF | Same 2 constraints, but the steering rotation adds 1 controllable DOF. |
| **Swedish Wheel** | Disk with small passive rollers at $0°$ or $45°$ on circumference | **1** | The free-spinning rollers permit lateral sliding, removing 1 constraint. |
| **Mecanum Wheel** | Disk with $45°$ rollers around periphery | **1** | Same as Swedish — rollers allow motion perpendicular to the main wheel plane. |
| **Castor Wheel** | Steerable wheel with offset pivot (passive) | **1** | The passive steering releases the lateral constraint; only rolling constraint remains. |
| **Ball Wheel** | Spherical ball as contact | **0** | Ball can roll in any direction, imposing no constraint (but hard to control). |

**Physical Reasoning — Detailed (per type):**

**Fixed Standard Wheel** **[1.5 marks]**
- *Motion permitted*: Translation along wheel plane direction $(\cos\alpha, \sin\alpha)$.
- *Motion forbidden*: Any motion with a component along $(-\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha)$ (lateral).
- *Constraint count*: 2 (rolling + lateral no-slip).

**Steerable Standard Wheel** **[1.5 marks]**
- *Same as fixed*, but the orientation $(\alpha + \beta)$ is *time-varying*.
- *Additional DOF*: 1 (the steering angle $\beta$).
- *Net effect on mobility*: 2 constraints, but adds 1 to $\delta_s$.

**Swedish Wheel** **[1.5 marks]**
- *Motion permitted*: Along wheel plane direction (main rollers) AND perpendicular direction (small passive rollers).
- *Motion forbidden*: None perpendicular to the wheel's main plane.
- *Constraint count*: 1 (only the rolling constraint).

**Mecanum Wheel** **[0.5 marks]**
- *Functionally equivalent* to Swedish wheel in terms of constraint count.
- *Difference*: Roller angle ($45°$) gives a unique force vector decomposition.

**Castor Wheel** **[1.5 marks]**
- *Special steerable wheel*: The steering axis is *offset* from the contact point and is *passively* free to rotate (like a ball joint).
- *Effect*: The wheel automatically aligns with the direction of motion, releasing the lateral constraint.
- *Constraint count*: 1.

**Ball Wheel** **[0.5 marks]**
- *Universal joint behavior*: The ball can roll in any direction.
- *Constraint count*: 0 (fully passive omni-motion).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to count steering DOF** when computing $\delta_s$ for a robot with steerable wheels. $\delta_s = N_s$, not the wheel count.
> 2. **Ignoring constraint redundancy** — assuming all constraints are independent. Always check the rank of the constraint matrix.
> 3. **Confusing holonomic with omnidirectional.** Holonomic = $\delta_m = 3$ (DOF count). Omnidirectional = can translate in any direction (achieved via $\delta_m = 3$ *and* proper wheel arrangement).
> 4. **Forgetting the ICR singularity:** If $\alpha' = \alpha$ (parallel wheels), the ICR is at infinity and the robot undergoes pure translation.
> 5. **Mixing up $C_f$ formula terms:** Students often write $C_f = 2N_f + N_s + N_{sw}$ — this is **wrong**. The correct term for steerable wheels is $2N_s$ (not $N_s$).
> 6. **Negative $\delta_m$ interpretation:** A negative value is *physically meaningless* for motion — it indicates over-constraint. Always report $\delta_m = \max(0, 3 - C_f)$ if you are unsure about redundancy.
> 7. **Wheel orientation conventions:** The angle $\alpha$ is measured from the *world X-axis* to the wheel plane — not from the robot's heading.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- [ ] **Maneuverability** = $\delta_m + \delta_s$, maximum theoretical value = 6.
- [ ] **Holonomic condition**: $\delta_m = 3$.
- [ ] **Non-holonomic condition**: $\delta_m < 3$ (most cars, differential drives).
- [ ] **Constraint count per wheel**: Fixed = 2, Steerable = 2 (+1 DOF), Swedish/Mecanum = 1, Castor = 1, Ball = 0.
- [ ] **Mobility formula**: $\delta_m = 3 - C_f$ where $C_f = 2N_f + 2N_s + N_{sw} + N_c$.
- [ ] **Steerability formula**: $\delta_s = N_s$ (number of *actuated* steerable wheels).
- [ ] **Rolling constraint (fixed wheel)**: $-\dot{x}\sin\alpha + \dot{y}\cos\alpha = 0$.
- [ ] **ICR lies on perpendicular line to wheel plane** through contact point: $(X-x)\cos\alpha + (Y-y)\sin\alpha = 0$.
- [ ] **ICR at infinity** = pure translation; **ICR inside robot** = rotation in place.
- [ ] **Differential drive**: $\delta_m = 2$, $\delta_s = 0$, $M = 2$ (non-holonomic).
- [ ] **3-Mecanum / 4-Mecanum robots**: $\delta_m = 3$ (holonomic, omnidirectional).
- [ ] **Ackermann car**: $\delta_m = 2$, $\delta_s = 1$ (constrained by geometry).
- [ ] **Tricycle** (1 fixed + 2 steerable): $\delta_m = 2$, $\delta_s = 2$, $M = 4$.
- [ ] Always check **constraint independence** using the rank of the Jacobian before finalizing $\delta_m$.
- [ ] **Memorization trick for constraints**: *Two-Fixed, Two-Steerable, One-Swedish, One-Castor* (TFTOSO).
- [ ] **ICR singularity**: Parallel wheels → $\sin(\alpha' - \alpha) = 0$ → ICR at infinity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# MODULE 1 — TOPIC: CONTROLLABILITY IN MOBILE ROBOTICS

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> **Controllability** of a mobile robotic system is the property that characterizes whether a robot can be driven from any **initial configuration** $\mathbf{q}_0$ to any arbitrary **desired final configuration** $\mathbf{q}_f$ in its configuration space $\mathcal{C}$, using a finite sequence of admissible control inputs $u(t)$ over a finite time horizon $T$.

More formally, for a nonlinear driftless affine control system of the form:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathbf{g}_i(\mathbf{q}) \, u_i$$

the system is said to be **controllable** if the reachable set $\mathcal{R}(\mathbf{q}_0, T)$ equals the entire configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ for some finite $T > 0$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a **car** in a two-dimensional parking lot. The car has two control inputs — **steering angle** and **throttle/brake**. Intuitively, even though you cannot slide sideways (a constraint imposed by the wheels), you can still reach *any* point on the parking lot by a clever sequence of forward-reverse maneuvers and steering corrections. This is **controllability** — the system has enough "maneuverability" through combinations of inputs to overcome kinematic restrictions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics used in Controllability Analysis:**
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Usually denoted as $n = \dim(\mathcal{C})$.
> - **Number of Independent Inputs:** Denoted as $m$.
> - **Holonomic vs Non-holonomic:** A mobile robot with $m < n$ *can still be controllable* through nonlinear coupling (Lie brackets) — this is the central paradox of mobile robotics.

### What is *NOT* Controllability

It is critical to distinguish **controllability** from related concepts that students often confuse:

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Controllability $\neq$ Stabilizability $\neq$ Path Planning.
> - *Stabilizability* asks whether there exists a control that drives the state to a specific equilibrium.
> - *Path planning* assumes controllability and computes an explicit trajectory.
> - *Controllability* is a *structural* property of the system — independent of any specific controller.

---

## 2. WHY MOBILITY & MANEUVERABILITY MATTER

In mobile robotics, almost every real-world wheeled platform (unicycles, differential drives, car-like robots, tricycles) is **non-holonomic**. A non-holonomic system has fewer control inputs than configuration-space degrees of freedom, but generates motion through **non-integrable** velocity constraints. Controllability is the mathematical assurance that despite these constraints, **full maneuverability is preserved** in the configuration space.

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Two Main Classes of Controllability

In mobile robotics, KTU examiners consistently test the following classification:

| Class | Definition | Applicable System |
|---|---|---|
| **Small-Time Local Controllability (STLC)** | Robot can reach an open neighbourhood of any configuration in arbitrarily small time | Smooth non-holonomic systems (unicycle, car) |
| **Global Controllability** | Robot can reach *any* configuration in $\mathcal{C}$ from *any* initial condition | Unicycle, differential drive (with no saturation) |
| **Pointwise Controllability** | At a specific configuration $\mathbf{q}_0$, the system is controllable to a neighbourhood | Used for local analysis only |
| **Non-Controllable** | There exists at least one direction $\delta \mathbf{q}$ in $\mathcal{C}$ that cannot be reached | Car with fixed front wheels (no reverse) |

### 2.2 The Controllability Rank Condition (CRC) — *Chow–Rashevskii Theorem*

This is the **single most important theorem** for the KTU 2024 ESE question bank. It applies to driftless affine systems:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathbf{g}_i(\mathbf{q}) \, u_i, \quad \mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

**Step-by-step logic of the Lie-algebraic controllability test:**

1. **Step 1 — Identify Vector Fields.** Compute the $m$ input vector fields $\mathbf{g}_1(\mathbf{q}), \dots, \mathbf{g}_m(\mathbf{q})$ that map control inputs to velocities.
2. **Step 2 — Form the Involutive Closure.** Compute all iterated Lie brackets $[\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j]$, $[\mathbf{g}_i, [\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j]]$, $[\mathbf{g}_j, [\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j]]$, and so on, until no new linearly independent vectors are produced.
3. **Step 3 — Form the Distribution.** Define the **Lie algebra generated distribution**:

$$\Delta(\mathbf{q}) = \operatorname{span} \{ \mathbf{g}_i, [\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j], [\mathbf{g}_i, [\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j]], \ldots \}_{\mathbf{q}}$$

4. **Step 4 — Apply the Rank Test.** The system is controllable if and only if:

$$\dim \Delta(\mathbf{q}) = n \quad \forall \mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C}$$

If the dimension equals $n$ at every point, the system is **STLC** (and often globally controllable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The Lie bracket $[\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j]$ represents motion in a direction that is **NOT** directly available through any single input. It is the *nonlinear coupling* between inputs that gives non-holonomic systems their maneuverability.

### 2.3 Canonical KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Property |
|---|---|---|
| $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Generalized velocity vector | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathbf{g}_i(\mathbf{q}) u_i$ |
| $[\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j]$ | Lie bracket of two vector fields | $[\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j] = \frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_j}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_i - \frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_i}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_j$ |
| $\Delta(\mathbf{q})$ | Controllability distribution | $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{g}_i, [\mathbf{g}_i,\mathbf{g}_j], \ldots\}_{\mathbf{q}}$ |
| $\dim \Delta(\mathbf{q})$ | Controllability rank | Must equal $n = \dim(\mathcal{C})$ for full controllability |
| $\mathcal{L}(\Delta)$ | Lie algebra generated by $\Delta$ | Smallest Lie algebra containing all $\mathbf{g}_i$ |
| $n, m$ | DOF and input counts | If $m < n$, system may still be controllable via Lie brackets |
| $v, \omega$ | Linear and angular velocities | Standard unicycle controls |
| $\theta$ | Heading angle | $\theta \in (-\pi, \pi]$ for the unicycle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Controllability analysis is the gatekeeper for autonomous vehicle deployment. Before designing any path-tracking controller (PID, MPC, feedback linearization), engineers must verify controllability — otherwise no controller can ever achieve the desired maneuver. This test is also applied to spacecraft attitude dynamics, underwater ROVs, and quadrotor motion planning.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Controllability of the Unicycle Mobile Robot

The unicycle model is the **canonical example** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on controllability. We will derive every step rigorously.

#### Step A — Write the Kinematic Equations

The unicycle has configuration $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^\top \in \mathbb{R}^3$ where $(x, y)$ is the planar position and $\theta$ is the heading. The controls are linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\dot{x} \\[4pt]
\dot{y} \\[4pt]
\dot{\theta}
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos(\theta) \\
\sin(\theta) \\
0
\end{bmatrix} v
+
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\
0 \\
1
\end{bmatrix} \omega
$$

#### Step B — Identify the Two Input Vector Fields

The system is in driftless affine form $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{g}_1(\mathbf{q}) v + \mathbf{g}_2(\mathbf{q}) \omega$ with:

$$
\mathbf{g}_1(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos(\theta) \\
\sin(\theta) \\
0
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
\mathbf{g}_2(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\
0 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** $\mathbf{g}_1$ is the direction of forward motion (driven by $v$), and $\mathbf{g}_2$ is the pure rotation direction (driven by $\omega$).

#### Step C — Note the Direct Span

The direct span of the two vector fields is:

$$
\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2\} =
\left\{
\alpha
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0
\end{bmatrix}
+
\beta
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\ 0 \\ 1
\end{bmatrix}
\right\}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This span has dimension only **2**, not 3. At first glance, it appears that the unicycle cannot move sideways — only forward/backward and rotate. So $\dim \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{g}_1,\mathbf{g}_2\} = 2 < 3 = n$, which would naïvely suggest *non-controllability*.

#### Step D — Compute the Lie Bracket $[\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2]$

Using the formula:

$$
[\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2] = \frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_2}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_1 - \frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_1}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_2
$$

Compute each term separately.

**Term 1: $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{g}_2}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_1$**

The Jacobian of $\mathbf{g}_2$ with respect to $\mathbf{q} = [x,y,\theta]^\top$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_2}{\partial \mathbf{q}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

because $\mathbf{g}_2 = [0, 0, 1]^\top$ has no $\mathbf{q}$-dependence. Multiplying by $\mathbf{g}_1$ gives the zero vector.

**Term 2: $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{g}_1}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_2$**

The Jacobian of $\mathbf{g}_1$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_1}{\partial \mathbf{q}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & -\sin(\theta) \\
0 & 0 & \cos(\theta) \\
0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying by $\mathbf{g}_2 = [0, 0, 1]^\top$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_1}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_2 =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & -\sin(\theta) \\
0 & 0 & \cos(\theta) \\
0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\ 0 \\ 1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-\sin(\theta) \\
\cos(\theta) \\
0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final Lie bracket:**

$$
[\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2] = \mathbf{0} - \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta \\ -\cos\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This Lie bracket is **orthogonal to** $\mathbf{g}_1$. Geometrically, it represents the **lateral (sideways) motion** generated by the sequence "drive forward, rotate, drive forward in reverse, rotate back" — the famous parallel-parking maneuver.

#### Step E — Verify the Lie Algebra Rank Condition

Now we form the three vectors:

$$
\mathbf{g}_1 =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
\mathbf{g}_2 =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\ 0 \\ 1
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
[\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2] =
\begin{bmatrix}
\sin\theta \\ -\cos\theta \\ 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

We check linear independence by evaluating the determinant of the matrix $[\mathbf{g}_1 \;\; \mathbf{g}_2 \;\; [\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2]]$:

$$
\det
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\
\sin\theta & 0 & -\cos\theta \\
0 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Expanding along the second column (which contains two zeros):

$$
= 1 \cdot (-1)^{3+2} \det
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & \sin\theta \\
\sin\theta & -\cos\theta
\end{bmatrix}
= -1 \cdot (-\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta) = -(-1) = 1
$$

Since the determinant equals $1 \neq 0$, the three vectors are **linearly independent** for all $\theta$. Therefore:

$$
\dim \Delta(\mathbf{q}) = 3 = n \quad \forall \, \mathbf{q}
$$

**Conclusion:** The unicycle satisfies the Lie Algebra Rank Condition and is therefore **Small-Time Locally Controllable**, and in fact globally controllable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Insight:** Even though the unicycle has *only two* inputs and a *three-dimensional* configuration space, the nonlinear Lie bracket $[\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2]$ fills the missing direction — restoring full controllability. This is the *hallmark* of non-holonomic controllability in mobile robotics.

---

### 3.2 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — Controllability of Common Mobile Robots

| Robot Type | Configuration $\mathbf{q}$ | Vector Fields $\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2$ | Lie Bracket $[\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2]$ | Rank | Controllable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unicycle** | $[x, y, \theta]^\top$ | $[\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0]^\top$, $[0,0,1]^\top$ | $[\sin\theta, -\cos\theta, 0]^\top$ | 3 | **YES** |
| **Differential Drive** | $[x, y, \theta]^\top$ | $[\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0]^\top$, $[0,0,1]^\top$ (after transformation) | $[\sin\theta, -\cos\theta, 0]^\top$ | 3 | **YES** |
| **Car-Like (with reverse)** | $[x, y, \theta, \phi]^\top$ | $[\cos\theta, \sin\theta, \tan\phi/L, 0]^\top$, $[0,0,0,1]^\top$ | $[\sin\theta \tan\phi/L, \ldots]$ | 4 | **YES** (if reverse allowed) |
| **Car-Like (no reverse)** | $[x, y, \theta, \phi]^\top$ | Same as above, with $v \geq 0$ | Same | 4 | **YES** (locally), but **constrained globally** |
| **Tricycle (no reverse)** | $[x, y, \theta]^\top$ | $[\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0]^\top$ only | — | 1 | **NO** |

**Conversion Logic for Tricycle:** With only one control (steering) and no reverse, the robot can only move forward in a curved arc — it cannot rotate in place, and cannot translate sideways. Hence uncontrollable.

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Controllability Verification Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the algorithmic pipeline for verifying controllability of a mobile robot in a KTU examination context.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Kinematic Model q-dot equals sum of gi u] --> B[Extract Input Vector Fields g1 to gm]
    B --> C[Compute Direct Span of g1 to gm]
    C --> D{Dimension equals n?}
    D -- Yes --> E[System is Trivially Controllable]
    D -- No --> F[Compute Lie Brackets g_i_g_j]
    F --> G[Compute Higher Order Lie Brackets]
    G --> H[Form Controllability Distribution Delta q]
    H --> I[Evaluate rank of Delta q at all q]
    I --> J{rank equals n at all q?}
    J -- Yes --> K[System is Small Time Locally Controllable]
    J -- No --> L[System is NOT Controllable]
    E --> M[Document Verdict for KTU Report]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

**Conversion Logic:** The pipeline first checks the *trivial* condition (direct span of inputs). If that fails (typical for non-holonomic systems), it escalates to Lie-bracket based analysis — exactly mirroring the KTU 2024 syllabus treatment of non-holonomic controllability.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Geometric Meaning of Lie Bracket Motion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Drive Forward v positive] --> P2[Step 2 Rotate by delta phi omega positive]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Drive Backward v negative]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Rotate by negative delta phi omega negative]
    P4 --> P5[Net Effect Pure Lateral Displacement along y]
    P5 --> P6[Equivalent to Lie bracket g1 g2 motion]
```

**Conversion Logic:** This four-step "parking maneuver" generates *sideways motion* without ever commanding a sideways velocity directly. The net displacement is proportional to the Lie bracket $[\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2]$ — the cornerstone of non-holonomic controllability.

---

### 4.3 Schematic — Controllability State Map for Wheeled Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Unicycle Model] --> Q1{Forward plus Reverse plus Steering}
    Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Controllable Globally]
    Q1 -- No --> S2[Not Controllable]
    R2[Differential Drive] --> Q2{Two Independent Wheel Velocities}
    Q2 -- Yes --> S1
    Q2 -- No --> S2
    R3[Car-Like Robot] --> Q3{Steering plus Throttle plus Reverse}
    Q3 -- Yes --> S1
    Q3 -- No --> S3[Only STLC Locally]
    R4[Tricycle No Reverse] --> Q4{Only One Control Input}
    Q4 -- Yes --> S2
```

**Conversion Logic:** This schematic acts as a quick visual decision tree for KTU students to rapidly identify whether a given mobile robot platform is controllable based on its actuation structure.

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

Define **controllability** of a nonlinear driftless control system of the form $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \sum_{i=1}^m \mathbf{g}_i(\mathbf{q}) u_i$.

**Model Answer:**

Controllability of a nonlinear driftless system refers to the ability of the system to be steered from any initial configuration $\mathbf{q}_0$ to any desired final configuration $\mathbf{q}_f$ in the configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ using admissible control inputs $u(t)$ over a finite time interval $T$. Mathematically, the reachable set $\mathcal{R}(\mathbf{q}_0, T)$ must equal the entire configuration space $\mathcal{C}$. For driftless systems, controllability is verified using the **Lie Algebra Rank Condition (LARC)** which states that the dimension of the controllability distribution $\Delta(\mathbf{q}) = \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{g}_i, [\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j], \ldots\}$ must equal $n = \dim(\mathcal{C})$ for all $\mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

What is the **Lie bracket** of two vector fields, and why is it important for controllability of non-holonomic systems?

**Model Answer:**

The Lie bracket of two vector fields $\mathbf{g}_i$ and $\mathbf{g}_j$ is defined as:

$$
[\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j] = \frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_j}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_i - \frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_i}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_j
$$

It is important for controllability because, in non-holonomic systems, the number of independent inputs $m$ is less than the configuration dimension $n$. The Lie bracket represents motion in a *direction not directly commanded* by any single input — it captures the nonlinear coupling between controls. By computing iterated Lie brackets, one can determine whether the system *indirectly* generates motion in all $n$ directions. This is encapsulated in the **Chow–Rashevskii theorem** which uses Lie brackets to verify the Lie Algebra Rank Condition.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]*

**(a)** Derive the kinematic model of a unicycle mobile robot and identify the two input vector fields $\mathbf{g}_1$ and $\mathbf{g}_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Lie Algebra Rank Condition**, prove that the unicycle is controllable. Show all intermediate Lie bracket computations explicitly. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Solution to (a):

The unicycle has configuration $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^\top$ and control inputs $(v, \omega)$. The kinematic equations are obtained from rigid-body rolling without slipping:

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

Rewriting in driftless affine form:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} =
\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} v + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \omega
$$

Therefore the two vector fields are:

$$
\mathbf{g}_1(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{g}_2(\mathbf{q}) =
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Direct span check — $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2\}$ has dimension 2 (the vector $[0,0,1]^\top$ has zero contribution to the $(x,y)$-plane basis). Since $n = 3$, direct span is insufficient.

**Step 2:** Compute the Lie bracket $[\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2]$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_2}{\partial \mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}, \quad
\frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_1}{\partial \mathbf{q}} =
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_1}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_2 =
\begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore:

$$
[\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2] =
\begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta \\ -\cos\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Form the matrix $[\mathbf{g}_1 \;\; \mathbf{g}_2 \;\; [\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2]]$ and compute its determinant:

$$
\det
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\
\sin\theta & 0 & -\cos\theta \\
0 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
= -(-\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta) = 1
$$

Since the determinant equals $1 \neq 0$, the three vectors are linearly independent everywhere, so $\dim \Delta(\mathbf{q}) = 3 = n$.

**Conclusion:** By the **Chow–Rashevskii theorem**, the unicycle satisfies the Lie Algebra Rank Condition and is **Small-Time Locally Controllable**, and hence globally controllable.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Explain the **Chow–Rashevskii theorem** for controllability of driftless nonlinear systems. State its hypothesis and conclusion precisely. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has configuration $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^\top$ and inputs $(v_R, v_L)$ — the right and left wheel velocities. The kinematic model is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} =
\frac{1}{2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0
\end{bmatrix}
(v_R + v_L) +
\frac{1}{2b}
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\ 0 \\ 1
\end{bmatrix}
(v_R - v_L)
$$

where $b$ is the wheelbase half-width. Verify its controllability using the LARC. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Solution to (a):

**Chow–Rashevskii Theorem Statement:**

*Let $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathbf{g}_i(\mathbf{q}) u_i$ be a driftless control-affine system with $\mathbf{g}_i$ analytic vector fields on a connected manifold $\mathcal{C}$. If the Lie algebra generated by $\{\mathbf{g}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{g}_m\}$ has dimension $n$ at every point $\mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C}$, then the system is controllable — i.e., for any $\mathbf{q}_0, \mathbf{q}_f \in \mathcal{C}$, there exists a piecewise-constant admissible control that drives $\mathbf{q}_0$ to $\mathbf{q}_f$ in finite time.*

The hypothesis is the **Lie Algebra Rank Condition (LARC)** — that the involutive closure of the input vector fields spans the full tangent space at every configuration. The conclusion is that the reachable set equals the configuration space.

#### Solution to (b):

Define $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$ and $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/(2b)$. The model reduces to the unicycle form:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0
\end{bmatrix} v + \begin{bmatrix}
0 \\ 0 \\ 1
\end{bmatrix} \omega
$$

This is identical to the unicycle — therefore the same Lie bracket computation yields:

$$
[\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2] =
\begin{bmatrix}
\sin\theta \\ -\cos\theta \\ 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The determinant of the matrix $[\mathbf{g}_1 \;\; \mathbf{g}_2 \;\; [\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2]]$ equals $1 \neq 0$, so LARC is satisfied and the differential-drive robot is **controllable**.

---

### ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Lie bracket** — Many students stop at the direct span $\{\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2\}$ and incorrectly conclude that non-holonomic systems are *not controllable*. This is the **#1 deduction** on KTU valuation keys (typically a 4–5 mark penalty).
> 2. **Sign errors in $[\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j]$** — The order matters: $[\mathbf{g}_i, \mathbf{g}_j] = -[\mathbf{g}_j, \mathbf{g}_i]$. Always use the convention $\frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_j}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_i - \frac{\partial \mathbf{g}_i}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}_j$.
> 3. **Confusing controllability with stabilizability** — A common 2-mark trap.
> 4. **Skipping the determinant step** — Examiners expect explicit verification of linear independence, not just a statement that the vectors "look independent".
> 5. **Ignoring the case $\theta = 0$ or $\theta = \pi/2$** — Always confirm the determinant is non-zero *uniformly*.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Controllability** = ability to reach *any* configuration in $\mathcal{C}$ from *any* $\mathbf{q}_0$ in finite time.
- **Non-holonomic systems** have $m < n$ but can still be controllable through **nonlinear Lie-bracket coupling**.
- The **Lie Algebra Rank Condition (LARC)** is the central tool: $\dim \Delta(\mathbf{q}) = n$.
- **Chow–Rashevskii theorem** establishes that LARC $\Rightarrow$ controllability for driftless systems.
- The **unicycle** is the canonical controllable system; its Lie bracket $[\mathbf{g}_1, \mathbf{g}_2] = [\sin\theta, -\cos\theta, 0]^\top$ represents lateral motion.
- **Differential drives** are kinematically equivalent to unicycles after the linear/angular velocity transformation.
- **Car-like robots** (with reverse) are controllable; without reverse, they are only **STLC** locally.
- **Tricycles without reverse** are *not* controllable.
- The **determinant test** on the matrix $[\mathbf{g}_1 \;\; \mathbf{g}_2 \;\; \ldots \;\; \text{brackets}]$ must give a *non-zero constant* (or non-zero function of $\mathbf{q}$) for uniform controllability.
- **Practical engineering utility**: This analysis is mandatory before designing any motion controller — without controllability, no feedback law can realize the desired motion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aerial Robots - Advantages of UAVs Over Conventional Manned Aircraft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aerial Robots - Advantages of UAVs Over Conventional Manned Aircraft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) / Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS):**
> An **Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)**, also referred to as an **Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS)** or **Remotely Piloted Aircraft System (RPAS)**, is a powered, aerial vehicle that does not carry a human operator onboard, uses aerodynamic forces to provide vehicle lift, can fly autonomously or be remotely piloted, and is reusable. (Source: **U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) / ICAO Standard**)

A **UAV system** is a complete assembly consisting of:
- The **aerial vehicle (airframe)**
- The **ground control station (GCS)**
- **Command, Control, Communications, Computers, Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (C4ISR)** links
- The **launch and recovery subsystem** (where applicable)

A **conventional manned aircraft** is, by contrast, an aerial platform with a **human pilot physically present in the cockpit**, performing direct flight control and decision-making in real time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Letter vs. The Telephone Call"**
> 
> Imagine you need to deliver a message to a house on top of a steep, dangerous cliff.
> 
> - **Conventional Manned Aircraft** = *You must physically climb the cliff and deliver it yourself.* You are exposed to **falls, weather, fatigue, and risk to life**. The journey requires life-support equipment (oxygen, food, rest cycles).
> - **Aerial Robot (UAV)** = *You fly a small remote-controlled messenger drone to the cliff top.* If it crashes, you simply **build another one**. The drone can **hover, fold into small spaces, work at night, and perform repetitive tasks** without getting tired.
> 
> The fundamental paradigm shift: **Risk is transferred from a human being to a machine**, while the mission still gets accomplished.

## 1.3 Aerial Robot Classification (Quick Reference)

| Class | Lift Mechanism | Typical Use | Hover Capable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fixed-Wing UAV** | Aerodynamic wings (forward speed) | Long-range surveillance, mapping | **No** |
| **Multirotor UAV** | Multiple rotors (vertical lift) | Inspection, cinematography, search | **Yes** |
| **Single-Rotor Helicopter UAV** | Main + tail rotor | Heavy payload, long endurance hover | **Yes** |
| **Hybrid VTOL** | Tilt-rotor / tilt-wing | Reconnaissance + hover transition | **Yes** |
| **Flapping-Wing / Bio-inspired** | Oscillating wings | Stealth reconnaissance, indoor | **Limited** |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU PBRUT504

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 1 of PBRUT504 lays the foundation for understanding **why mobile aerial platforms are a paradigm shift** in robotics. The advantages of UAVs are **not just "no pilot"** — they unlock fundamentally new mission profiles that manned flight **cannot economically, ethically, or physically execute**. This forms the motivation for studying the **dynamics, control, sensing, and navigation** of aerial robots in the remaining modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Comparison: The 15 Core Advantages of UAVs

Below is a structured, exam-ready analysis of each major advantage. These form the **frequently-tested content** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examinations).

### A. Human Safety & Risk Mitigation
- **No loss-of-life penalty:** In hostile, hazardous, or contaminated environments (nuclear fallout, chemical spills, mine-clearing, anti-terror operations), the failure of a UAV is a **material loss, not a casualty**. This single fact makes UAVs ethically and operationally superior.
- **G-force tolerance:** Human pilots black out beyond **+5 Gz** sustained, and fatal g-force injuries occur above **+10 Gz**. UAVs can sustain **40+ G** manoeuvres with no physiological penalty.

### B. Cost Economics
- **Acquisition cost:** A light observation helicopter (e.g., Bell 407) costs approximately **\$2 to \$5 million**, while an equivalent-capability UAV (e.g., MQ-8 Fire Scout) costs **\$10–25 million** but supports **high-risk missions**. Small commercial drones (DJI Matrice series) cost **\$5,000–\$20,000** for civilian work.
- **Operating cost:** Manned aircraft have **high recurring crew costs** (pilot salary, training, insurance, life-support). UAVs eliminate most of these.
- **Maintenance ratio:** Military rotary-wing aircraft require **3–5 maintenance hours per flight hour**. UAVs are typically **1–2 hours per flight hour**.

### C. Operational Endurance & Persistence
- **No human fatigue factor:** A pilot's effective flight time before mandatory rest is regulated (**maximum 8–12 hours**). UAVs can be **swapped mid-mission** or operate continuously with relay stations.
- **Persistence:** Solar-powered UAVs (e.g., Facebook Aquila, Google Loon) achieve **months of continuous flight**, a feat impossible for manned platforms.

### D. Maneuverability & Access
- **Hover capability:** Multirotor UAVs can **hover, fly backwards, sideways, and rotate in place** — moves impossible or extremely dangerous in fixed-wing manned aircraft.
- **Confined-space access:** UAVs can fly **indoors, in caves, urban canyons, and through windows**; manned aircraft cannot.
- **Low-altitude operation:** UAVs routinely fly **below 5 m AGL** for inspection; manned aircraft have **regulatory minimum altitudes (typically 150 m / 500 ft)** for safety.

### E. Environmental & Logistic Footprint
- **No runway requirement:** VTOL UAVs launch from **truck beds, ship decks, rooftops** — eliminating expensive runway infrastructure (a typical airfield costs **\$50M–\$1B+** to build).
- **Lower emissions per mission hour:** Electric multirotor UAVs have **near-zero direct emissions**.
- **Lower acoustic/visual signature (stealth):** Small UAVs have a small radar cross-section and can use electric motors for **stealth reconnaissance**.

### F. Mission Flexibility & Reconfigurability
- **Modular payload:** A single UAV platform can be reconfigured for **EO/IR cameras, LiDAR, multispectral sensors, cargo delivery, or signal intelligence** by swapping the payload.
- **Swarm capability:** Multiple UAVs can cooperate as a **swarm** (collective intelligence) — a capability **physically impossible with manned aircraft** (one pilot = one aircraft).

### G. Data Quality & Sensor Placement
- **Sensor proximity:** UAVs can place sensors **centimetres from the target** (e.g., bridge crack inspection, turbine blade scanning), giving **sub-millimetre resolution** impossible from manned aircraft at safe standoff distances.

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Metric Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The topic is **qualitative-dominant**, but quantitative metrics are examinable. Memorize the following table.

| Metric | Equation / Definition | Unit | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{acq} + (C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{crew}) \cdot T_{hr}$ | \$ | Justifies UAV over manned choice |
| **Operating Cost per Flight Hour** | $OCPH = \dfrac{C_{op} + C_{crew} + C_{ins}}{T_{flight}}$ | \$/hr | Fleet budgeting |
| **Cost-per-Mission** | $CPM = \dfrac{C_{acq}}{N_{missions}} + CPH \cdot t_{mission}$ | \$/mission | Mission economics |
| **Risk-Adjusted Cost** | $RAC = C_{mission} + P_{loss} \cdot C_{human\text{-}life}$ | \$ | Justifies high-risk UAV use |
| **Payload-to-Empty-Weight Ratio** | $\eta_p = \dfrac{m_{payload}}{m_{empty}}$ | dimensionless | Design efficiency |
| **Endurance (Battery)** | $E = \dfrac{C_{batt} \cdot V_{batt}}{P_{hover}}$ | hours | Mission planning |
| **Maximum G-Tolerance (UAV)** | $a_{max} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{m_{total}}$ | m/s² | Structural design |
| **Manhours per Flight Hour (MPFH)** | $MPFH = \dfrac{T_{maint}}{T_{flight}}$ | hr/hr | Logistics planning |

Where:
- $C_{acq}$ = acquisition cost
- $C_{op}$ = operating cost (fuel/electricity, consumables)
- $C_{maint}$ = maintenance cost
- $C_{crew}$ = pilot/operator cost
- $C_{ins}$ = insurance cost
- $T_{hr}$ = total service hours
- $P_{loss}$ = probability of catastrophic failure
- $C_{human\text{-}life}$ = statistical value of human life (used in risk analysis)

## 2.3 The "Why" — Engineering Utility Beyond the Obvious

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **most important** insight for KTU examinations: UAVs do not merely replace manned aircraft — **they enable new missions that were previously impossible**:
> 1. **Precision Agriculture** — cm-level multispectral crop health mapping
> 2. **Disaster Response** — entering collapsed buildings before human rescuers
> 3. **Last-Mile Logistics** — Amazon Prime Air, Zipline medical delivery
> 4. **Perceptive Inspection** — confined industrial spaces (chimneys, boilers, turbine interiors)
> 5. **Cooperative Swarms** — distributed sensing and manipulation
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — UAV vs Manned Aircraft: Total Cost of Ownership

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark model question** for this topic. We compare a manned helicopter with a multirotor UAV for a 5-year, 500 flight-hour/year pipeline inspection programme.

### 3.1.1 Given Data

| Parameter | Manned Helicopter | Surveillance UAV |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Acquisition cost $C_{acq}$ | **\$4,000,000** | **\$150,000** |
| Operating cost $C_{op}$ | **\$1,500 / hr** | **\$50 / hr** |
| Crew cost $C_{crew}$ (2 pilots) | **\$400 / hr** | **\$30 / hr** (1 operator) |
| Insurance $C_{ins}$ | **\$300 / hr** | **\$10 / hr** |
| Maintenance $C_{maint}$ | **\$700 / hr** | **\$80 / hr** |
| Probability of loss $P_{loss}$ | **0.0001 / hr** | **0.001 / hr** |
| Statistical value of life | **\$10,000,000** | **\$0** (no human) |
| Annual flight hours | **500 hr** | **500 hr** |
| Programme duration | **5 years** | **5 years** |

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Calculation for the Manned Helicopter

**Step 1 — Total flight hours over 5 years:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= 500 \,\text{hr/yr} \times 5 \,\text{yr} \\
&= 2500 \,\text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Hourly recurring cost (excluding acquisition):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{hour} &= C_{op} + C_{crew} + C_{ins} + C_{maint} \\
&= 1500 + 400 + 300 + 700 \\
&= 2900 \,\$/\text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total recurring cost over 5 years:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{recur} &= C_{hour} \times T_{total} \\
&= 2900 \times 2500 \\
&= 7{,}250{,}000 \,\$
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Expected risk-adjusted loss cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{risk} &= P_{loss} \times C_{life} \times T_{total} \\
&= 0.0001 \times 10{,}000{,}000 \times 2500 \\
&= 2{,}500{,}000 \,\$
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Cost of Ownership (Manned):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{manned} &= C_{acq} + C_{recur} + C_{risk} \\
&= 4{,}000{,}000 + 7{,}250{,}000 + 2{,}500{,}000 \\
&= 13{,}750{,}000 \,\$
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Calculation for the UAV

**Step 1 — Total flight hours:** Same as above, **2500 hr**.

**Step 2 — Hourly recurring cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{hour}^{UAV} &= 50 + 30 + 10 + 80 \\
&= 170 \,\$/\text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total recurring cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{recur}^{UAV} &= 170 \times 2500 \\
&= 425{,}000 \,\$
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Risk cost (no human on board, but include asset loss + collateral):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{risk}^{UAV} &= 0.001 \times 0 + \text{(insurance covers loss)} \\
&= 0 \,\$
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Statistical value of human life = \$0 because no human is exposed.)*

**Step 5 — Total Cost of Ownership (UAV):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{UAV} &= 150{,}000 + 425{,}000 + 0 \\
&= 575{,}000 \,\$
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Cost Savings Comparison

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= TCO_{manned} - TCO_{UAV} \\
&= 13{,}750{,}000 - 575{,}000 \\
&= 13{,}175{,}000 \,\$
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings \%} &= \dfrac{13{,}175{,}000}{13{,}750{,}000} \times 100 \\
&\approx 95.8 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
