> The UAV delivers the **same mission** (pipeline inspection) at **~4.2% of the manned cost**, while completely **eliminating the human-risk component**. This is why UAV adoption is accelerating in **surveillance, inspection, and dangerous-environment missions**.

## 3.2 Endurance Comparison — Why "No Pilot" Unlocks Long-Duration Missions

For a **fixed-wing UAV** with solar augmentation, the endurance $E$ is bounded only by structural fatigue, not human physiology:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{UAV} &= \min\left( E_{batt}, \, E_{fuel}, \, E_{solar}, \, E_{fatigue} \right) \\
E_{manned} &= \min\left( E_{batt}, \, E_{fuel}, \, T_{pilot\text{-}limit} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $T_{pilot\text{-}limit} \approx 8$ to $12$ hours (regulatory), the **manned aircraft's effective endurance is capped by the pilot**, whereas the UAV is capped by physics alone. Solar UAVs (e.g., Zephyr S) have demonstrated **> 64 days of continuous flight** — a feat **literally impossible for any manned aircraft**.

## 3.3 G-Force Tolerance Derivation

Maximum acceleration of an aerial platform is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{max} = \dfrac{F_{max} - m g \cos\theta}{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a human pilot, the survivable sustained acceleration is **~5 Gz**. For a UAV with no human, the structural limit applies, typically **20–40 G**. The **maneuverability ratio** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{ratio} = \dfrac{a_{max}^{UAV}}{a_{max}^{human}} \approx \dfrac{30}{5} = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

This means UAVs can execute **manoeuvres 6× more aggressive** than human-piloted aircraft, opening combat and aerobatic mission profiles impossible for manned flight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UAV System Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete functional architecture of a typical aerial robot system and contrasts its decision-loop with a manned aircraft.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GND["GROUND CONTROL STATION"]
        OPR["Human Operator / Mission Planner"]
        GCS["Ground Control Software"]
        DLINK["Data Link Transceiver"]
    end

    subgraph AIR["AERIAL VEHICLE AIRFRAME"]
        FC["Flight Controller Pixhawk-class"]
        IMU["IMU 6-axis Accelerometer Gyro"]
        GPS["GPS GNSS Module"]
        BATT["Battery BMS"]
        ESCT["ESC Motor Drivers"]
        MTR["Brushless Motors x4 to x8"]
        PAY["Payload Camera LiDAR Multispectral"]
        UPLINK["Telemetry Uplink"]
    end

    OPR --> GCS
    GCS -->|Command| DLINK
    DLINK -->|RC / Telemetry| FC
    FC --> IMU
    FC --> GPS
    FC --> BATT
    FC --> ESCT
    ESCT --> MTR
    FC --> PAY
    FC --> UPLINK
    UPLINK -->|Video / State| DLINK
    DLINK --> GCS
    GCS --> OPR

    style OPR fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style FC fill:#93c47d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style MTR fill:#6fa8dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PAY fill:#e69138,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Advantage Taxonomy — Block-Level Categorization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ADV["ADVANTAGES OF UAVs OVER MANNED AIRCRAFT"]

    ADV --> S1["SAFETY"]
    ADV --> S2["COST"]
    ADV --> S3["PERFORMANCE"]
    ADV --> S4["FLEXIBILITY"]
    ADV --> S5["ACCESS"]

    S1 --> S1A["No loss of human life"]
    S1 --> S1B["Higher G-tolerance 40g vs 5g"]
    S1 --> S1C["No pilot fatigue blackouts"]

    S2 --> S2A["Lower acquisition cost"]
    S2 --> S2B["Lower operating cost per hour"]
    S2 --> S2C["No crew salary or insurance"]

    S3 --> S3A["Hover capability"]
    S3 --> S3B["Long endurance days to months"]
    S3 --> S3C["Aggressive manoeuvres"]

    S4 --> S4A["Modular payload swap"]
    S4 --> S4B["Swarm cooperative operation"]
    S4 --> S4C["Day night all weather ops"]

    S5 --> S5A["Indoor and confined space access"]
    S5 --> S5B["Low altitude flight below 5m"]
    S5 --> S5C["No runway required VTOL"]

    style ADV fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style S1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S3 fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S4 fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S5 fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic: When to Choose UAV vs Manned Aircraft

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Mission Defined"] --> B{"Risk to Human\nPilot Acceptable?"}
    B -->|NO| C["Choose UAV\nMandatory"]
    B -->|YES| D{"Mission Duration\nGreater than 12 hours?"}
    D -->|YES| C2["Choose UAV\nManned pilot fatigue"]
    D -->|NO| E{"Confined or\nIndoor Space?"}
    E -->|YES| C3["Choose UAV\nManned cannot access"]
    E -->|NO| F{"Cost Budget\nCritical?"}
    F -->|YES| C4["Choose UAV\nLower TCO"]
    F -->|NO| G["Manned or UAV\nacceptable"]

    style C fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C2 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C3 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C4 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Any mission that is **high-risk, long-duration, confined-space, or cost-critical** automatically selects a UAV. Manned aircraft remain competitive only for **medium-risk, short-duration, open-sky missions** (e.g., commercial passenger transport, where payload (people) is the mission itself).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any six advantages of UAVs over conventional manned aircraft.**

**Model Answer:**

The six key advantages of UAVs over conventional manned aircraft are:
1. **Human Safety:** No risk of loss of life in dangerous missions (combat, CBRN, disaster zones).
2. **Lower Operating Cost:** No pilot salary, training, life-support, or insurance costs.
3. **Higher Endurance:** No pilot fatigue limit; can operate continuously for days or months.
4. **Higher G-Tolerance:** Can sustain 20–40 G manoeuvres, far beyond human physiological limits (~5 Gz).
5. **Greater Access:** Can fly indoors, in confined spaces, and at very low altitudes (below 5 m).
6. **Swarm Capability:** Multiple UAVs can cooperate as a distributed system, a capability impossible for manned aircraft.

*(Award 0.5 marks per correct point, full credit for any 6 valid points.)*

---

### **Question 1.2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between a UAV and a UAS.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | UAV (Unmanned Aerial Vehicle) | UAS (Unmanned Aircraft System) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scope** | Refers only to the aerial vehicle itself (the airframe) | Refers to the complete system including ground station, links, and personnel |
| **Components** | Airframe, motors, flight controller, payload | UAV + Ground Control Station (GCS) + C2 link + Launch/recovery system + Operators |
| **Analogy** | The "car" | The "car + driver + garage + remote" |

In short, **the UAV is a subset of the UAS**. The UAV is the flying platform, while the UAS is the operational system required to put the UAV in the air and complete a mission.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Module questions for 14 marks have an **internal choice**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **safety and human-risk advantages** of UAVs over conventional manned aircraft, with reference to specific mission scenarios.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, derive the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** for a UAV and a manned aircraft performing the **same mission**, and comment on the result.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Safety and Human-Risk Advantages:**

1. **No loss-of-life penalty:**
   - In **combat operations**, the failure of a UAV results in a material loss that can be replaced.
   - In **CBRN (Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear) reconnaissance**, a UAV can fly into contaminated zones without exposing personnel to toxic substances.
   - In **disaster response** (e.g., Fukushima nuclear accident, post-earthquake collapsed buildings), UAVs conduct damage assessment before human rescue teams are deployed.

2. **Higher G-force tolerance:**
   - Human pilots black out at sustained accelerations above **+5 Gz**, and fatal injuries occur above **+10 Gz**.
   - UAVs have no physiological limit. Airframe structural limits of **20–40 G** enable aggressive combat manoeuvres (e.g., banked turns at 80°+ angle in the Bayraktar TB2).

3. **No pilot fatigue:**
   - Aviation authorities (DGCA, FAA, EASA) mandate **maximum 8–12 flight hours** with mandatory rest cycles.
   - UAVs can be **swapped between operators** mid-mission, enabling **24/7 persistent surveillance**.

4. **No physiological vulnerability:**
   - UAVs are unaffected by **hypoxia, dehydration, vibration, spatial disorientation**, or **g-induced loss of consciousness (G-LOC)**.
   - High-altitude reconnaissance (above 60,000 ft, e.g., Global Hawk) is possible without pressurized suits.

5. **Ethical and political risk reduction:**
   - Loss of a pilot becomes an **international incident** (search-and-rescue, repatriation). A lost UAV is a logistics issue.

*(Each point: 1 mark, plus 2 marks for cohesive explanation and mission scenario examples.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Derivation:**

The TCO over a programme duration is given by:

$$
TCO = C_{acq} + (C_{op} + C_{crew} + C_{ins} + C_{maint}) \times T_{total} + P_{loss} \times C_{life} \times T_{total}
$$

**Example: Pipeline Inspection Mission (5 years, 500 hr/yr)**

For the **manned helicopter** (values as computed in Section 3.1.2):

$$
TCO_{manned} = 4{,}000{,}000 + 2900 \times 2500 + 0.0001 \times 10{,}000{,}000 \times 2500 = 13{,}750{,}000 \,\$
$$

For the **UAV** (values as computed in Section 3.1.3):

$$
TCO_{UAV} = 150{,}000 + 170 \times 2500 + 0 = 575{,}000 \,\$
$$

**Cost saving:**

$$
\Delta TCO = 13{,}750{,}000 - 575{,}000 = 13{,}175{,}000 \,\$ \quad (\approx 95.8\%)
$$

**Comments:**
- The UAV delivers the **same mission** at less than **5% of the manned cost**.
- The **risk-adjusted cost** for the manned aircraft is significant because of the high statistical value of human life.
- The result justifies UAV adoption in **inspection, surveillance, and dangerous-environment missions**.
- However, the UAV requires a **higher initial investment per flight hour in command infrastructure** and is **limited in payload mass** for this example (camera + LiDAR only, no passengers).

*(Award 2 marks for the formula, 3 marks for correct substitution and arithmetic, 2 marks for the final comparison and engineering comment.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify UAVs based on **wing/rotor configuration**, and explain how each configuration gives different operational advantages over manned aircraft.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **swarm and cooperative operation** capability of UAVs, and explain why this is **physically impossible for manned aircraft**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**UAV Classification by Configuration:**

| Class | Lift Principle | Hover | Key Advantage over Manned |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fixed-Wing UAV** | Forward airspeed → aerodynamic lift | No | **Long endurance** (16–48 hr); suited for mapping, long-range ISR |
| **Multirotor UAV** | Multiple vertical rotors | Yes | **Hover + confined-space flight**; perfect for inspection |
| **Single-Rotor Helicopter UAV** | Main + tail rotor | Yes | **High payload capacity** in a small footprint |
| **Hybrid VTOL** | Tilt-rotor / lift-thrust-vectoring | Yes | **Best of both worlds**: hover take-off + fixed-wing cruise |
| **Flapping-Wing / Bio-inspired** | Oscillating wings | Limited | **Stealth + indoor access**; silent, no exposed rotors |

**Operational Advantage Discussion:**
- A **manned fixed-wing aircraft** cannot hover, ruling out inspection of vertical structures (turbines, towers). A **multirotor UAV** hovers and orbits at 2 m from a wind-turbine blade.
- A **manned helicopter** has a **rotor diameter of 10–15 m** and cannot fly indoors. A **sub-250 g micro-UAV** can fly through window gaps.
- A **manned aircraft has minimum flight altitude of 150 m (FAA Part 91)** for safety. A **UAV routinely operates at 1–5 m AGL**, enabling ultra-high-resolution imaging.

*(Award 1.5 marks per class for correct classification + principle; 2 marks for the comparison table and inference.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Swarm and Cooperative Operation:**

A **UAV swarm** is a group of multiple UAVs that coordinate their actions **without centralized human control**, using:
- **Inter-UAV communication** (mesh radio, e.g., MAVLink over XBee)
- **Shared localization** (relative GPS, UWB, or visual relative pose)
- **Distributed consensus algorithms** (e.g., leader-follower, virtual-structure, potential-field)

**Capabilities Unlocked:**
1. **Coverage:** A swarm of N UAVs can cover an area **N× faster** than a single UAV, and can self-heal coverage if one unit fails.
2. **Manipulation:** Cooperative aerial transport of payloads heavier than any single UAV can lift (e.g., multiple UAVs lifting a structural beam).
3. **Distributed sensing:** Multiple viewpoints for **Structure-from-Motion (SfM)** photogrammetry and SLAM.
4. **Robustness:** No single point of failure — if one UAV fails, the swarm continues.

**Why Manned Aircraft Cannot Do This:**
- **Pilot limitation:** One pilot = one aircraft. A "swarm of manned aircraft" would require **one pilot per aircraft** with real-time voice coordination — operationally impossible beyond 2–3 aircraft.
- **Communication bandwidth:** Pilots use voice radio; coordinated multi-aircraft manoeuvres need **machine-speed machine-to-machine links**.
- **Safety regulations:** Civil aviation authorities (FAA, DGCA) **prohibit uncoordinated formations** beyond a few aircraft.
- **Physiology:** Pilots black out at different rates under stress — coordinated manoeuvres are humanly impossible.

**Examples of UAV Swarms:**
- **U.S. DARPA OFFSET Program:** 250+ UAV swarms for urban reconnaissance.
- **Indian Army Swarm Drone Demonstration (2021):** 75 UAVs executing synchronized surveillance and strike patterns.
- **Skydio, Parrot, DJI commercial swarms** for light shows and agricultural spraying.

*(Award 2 marks for definition, 3 marks for capabilities, 2 marks for the "impossible for manned" justification with examples.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "UAV" and "UAS":** Examiners specifically test this. Always write the **expanded acronym** in the first instance.
> 2. **Listing advantages without elaboration:** Saying "UAVs are cheaper" is **1 mark**. Showing the **TCO calculation with numbers** is **3 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring the "mission" context:** Always tie an advantage to a **specific mission scenario** (combat, agriculture, inspection). Generic answers lose marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the limitations:** UAVs have drawbacks too — **limited payload, regulatory restrictions, range, weather sensitivity**. A 14-mark answer that ignores limitations appears one-sided.
> 5. **Mis-using the pipe symbol `|` in tables:** This breaks the markdown table and is a **common formatting mistake** in KTU answer sheets (when converted to PDF).
> 6. **Forgetting units:** Always include **\$/hr, hr, m, kg, m/s²**. The KTU marking scheme deducts marks for missing units in numerical answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **UAV** = Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (the flying platform).
- [x] **UAS** = Unmanned Aircraft System = UAV + GCS + C2 link + operators + launch/recovery.
- [x] Five major UAV classes: **Fixed-wing, Multirotor, Single-rotor, Hybrid VTOL, Flapping-wing**.
- [x] **Safety advantage:** No human risk; tolerates 20–40 G vs human 5 G.
- [x] **Cost advantage:** 4–25× lower TCO due to eliminated crew, training, insurance.
- [x] **Endurance advantage:** Capped by physics (months), not pilot fatigue (8–12 hr).
- [x] **Access advantage:** Hover, indoor, low-altitude (< 5 m) flight impossible for manned.
- [x] **Flexibility advantage:** Modular payload + swarm cooperation.
- [x] **TCO formula:** $TCO = C_{acq} + C_{recur} \cdot T_{total} + P_{loss} \cdot C_{life} \cdot T_{total}$.
- [x] **OCPH formula:** $OCPH = (C_{op} + C_{crew} + C_{ins} + C_{maint}) / T_{flight}$.
- [x] UAVs **enable new missions**, not just replace old ones (precision agriculture, swarm reconnaissance, last-mile delivery).
- [x] Manned aircraft remain preferred only for **passenger transport** and **medium-risk, short-duration, open-sky missions**.
- [x] Always use **$\vert$ or $\mid$** in tables — never bare `|` for absolute value.
- [x] Always quote **units** in numerical answers.
- [x] Examiners reward **mission-linked examples** over generic statements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of UAVs - Fixed-Wing UAV or Airplane - Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of UAVs - Fixed-Wing UAV or Airplane - Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ✈️ Fixed-Wing UAV — Definition, Intuition & Operating Philosophy

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fixed-Wing UAV** (Unmanned Aerial Vehicle), also called an **Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS)** in its complete form, is an aerial robotic platform that generates aerodynamic lift primarily through the forward motion of **rigid, non-rotating wings** — exactly as a conventional manned airplane does. Unlike rotary-wing platforms (quadcopters, helicopters), the fixed-wing UAV cannot hover; lift is produced by the dynamic pressure created when the airframe moves forward through the air at a minimum airspeed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A Fixed-Wing UAV is a type of Unmanned Aerial Vehicle that uses static (non-rotating) lifting surfaces (wings) to generate lift through forward airspeed. It typically includes a fuselage, fixed wings, tail assembly (empennage), propulsion system, and an autopilot/mission computer.

The vehicle is a part of the broader classification tree of UAVs shown below:

| Classification | Lift Mechanism | Hover Capable? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed-Wing** | Forward speed over rigid wings | ❌ No | Cessna 172 UAV, MQ-1 Predator |
| **Rotary-Wing** | Spinning rotor blades | ✅ Yes | Quadcopter, Helicopter |
| **Hybrid VTOL** | Tilting rotors/wings | ✅ (limited) | Tilt-rotor aircraft |
| **Lighter-Than-Air** | Buoyancy (gas) | ✅ (passive) | Blimp, weather balloon |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sky Paper Glider 🛩️

Imagine a **paper aeroplane** you throw in your classroom. It glides across the room for a long time, but it can **never stop in mid-air and stay there**. It *must* keep moving forward, or it falls. A fixed-wing UAV works on the same physical principle — the harder and faster it pushes through the air, the more lift the wings create. The trade-off is clear: in exchange for **incredibly long flight times (endurance) and high speed**, you sacrifice the ability to **hover and stand still in one place** like a quadcopter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The One-Line Truth:** *Fixed-wing UAV = "Always moving forward; never standing still."*

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics Used in KTU Evaluation

When describing a fixed-wing UAV, examiners expect students to use these exact standard metrics:

- **Endurance (E):** Maximum total flight time on a single fuel/battery charge — typically **0.5 to 40+ hours** depending on platform class.
- **Range (R):** Maximum horizontal distance covered in a single mission — typically **10 km to 2000+ km**.
- **Service Ceiling:** Maximum operational altitude — usually **500 m to 25,000 m** (HALE class).
- **Maximum Take-Off Weight (MTOW):** Total weight at takeoff, including payload, fuel, and airframe.
- **Wing Loading (W/S):** Weight per unit wing area, expressed in **N/m²**.
- **Aspect Ratio (AR):** Ratio of wingspan squared to wing area — a measure of aerodynamic efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy — The Six Sub-Systems of a Fixed-Wing UAV

A fixed-wing UAV is not just an airframe; it is an **integrated aerial robot**. KTU expects you to understand the function of every major subsystem.

1. **Airframe (Fuselage + Wings + Empennage):** Provides structural shape and creates lift/drag.
2. **Propulsion System (Engine + Propeller or Jet):** Generates forward thrust; commonly electric (BLDC), internal combustion (gasoline), or turbojet for high-altitude platforms.
3. **Powerplant & Energy Source:** Li-Po battery (electric), heavy fuel (HFE), or kerosene (jet).
4. **Avionics & Flight Control Computer (FCC):** Houses the **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**, GPS, barometer, and the autopilot algorithm (e.g., Pixhawk, Ardupilot).
5. **Communication Datalink:** C2 (Command & Control) link + payload data link; typically operating at **2.4 GHz, 5.8 GHz, or 900 MHz**.
6. **Payload (Sensor Suite):** EO/IR camera, LiDAR, multispectral sensor, SAR radar, or mission-specific instrument.

## 2.2 The Four Fundamental Forces in Steady Level Flight

For a fixed-wing UAV in **unaccelerated, level, straight-and-level flight**, four forces are in equilibrium:

| Force | Symbol | Direction | Generated By | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lift** | $L$ | Upward (⊥ to flight path) | Wings (pressure differential) | $L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$ |
| **Weight** | $W$ | Downward (toward Earth) | Gravity ($W=mg$) | $W = m \cdot g$ |
| **Thrust** | $T$ | Forward (along flight path) | Engine + Propeller | $T = \eta_p \cdot P_{prop}$ |
| **Drag** | $D$ | Backward (‖ to flight path) | Air friction + form drag | $D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_D$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Conditions (Examiner's Favourite):**
> $L = W$  (vertical balance)      and      $T = D$ (horizontal balance)

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the formulas in the table below — they appear in **almost every fixed-wing question** in KTU End-Semester Examinations. Pay special attention to the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent table-breakage.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dynamic Pressure** | $q = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$ | Pa (N/m²) | Kinetic energy per unit volume of oncoming air |
| 2 | **Lift Force** | $L = q \cdot S \cdot C_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$ | N | Upward force supporting the UAV |
| 3 | **Drag Force** | $D = q \cdot S \cdot C_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_D$ | N | Resistance to forward motion |
| 4 | **Lift-to-Drag Ratio** | $E_{\text{ratio}} = L/D = C_L / C_D$ | dimensionless | Aerodynamic efficiency (key for endurance & range) |
| 5 | **Stall Speed** | $V_{\text{stall}} = \sqrt{2W / (\rho S C_{L,\max})}$ | m/s | Minimum airspeed before wing stalls |
| 6 | **Power Required** | $P_{\text{req}} = D \cdot V$ | W | Power needed to overcome drag |
| 7 | **Thrust-Specific Fuel Consumption** | $c_T = \dot{m}_f / T$ | kg/(N·s) | Fuel mass flow per unit thrust |
| 8 | **Breguet Range Equation** | $R = (V / c_T) \cdot (C_L / C_D) \cdot \ln(W_0 / W_1)$ | m | Maximum range for jet-propelled fixed-wing |
| 9 | **Breguet Endurance Equation** | $E = (C_L^{3/2} / C_D) \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho S} \cdot (\eta_p \cdot b_f) / W$ | s | Maximum endurance (propeller-driven) |
| 10 | **Aspect Ratio** | $AR = b^2 / S$ | dimensionless | Long, thin wings ⇒ high efficiency |
| 11 | **Wing Loading** | $W/S = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\text{stall}}^2 C_{L,\max}$ | N/m² | Constraint on minimum flight speed |
| 12 | **Climb Rate** | $R/C = (T - D) \cdot V / W$ | m/s | Vertical speed capability |

**Where the standard symbols mean:**

- $\rho$ = air density, **≈ 1.225 kg/m³ at sea level, ISA standard**
- $V$ = true airspeed (m/s)
- $S$ = wing planform area (m²)
- $C_L$, $C_D$ = lift and drag coefficients (dimensionless)
- $C_{L,\max}$ = maximum lift coefficient at stall
- $\eta_p$ = propeller efficiency (typically 0.7 to 0.85)
- $b_f$ = fuel-specific energy (≈ 43 MJ/kg for gasoline)
- $W_0$, $W_1$ = initial and final aircraft weight
- $b$ = wingspan (m)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The **Breguet Range & Endurance Equations** are not just academic exercises. Aerospace engineers at Boeing, Airbus, and DRDO (India) use them as the **first-cut sizing tool** when designing any new aircraft. The ratio $L/D$ is the single most important design parameter — increasing it by just **10%** can extend range by **10%** without adding a single drop of fuel. This is why gliders have extremely long, thin wings (high AR) and why modern airliners use **winglets**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners reward **complete derivations** with full marks. Skipping a step costs 1–2 marks per question. Below are the two derivations most frequently asked in the 2024 scheme.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stall Speed Equation $V_{\text{stall}}$

**Starting Principle:** The wing stalls when the local angle of attack reaches the critical value beyond which the airflow can no longer remain attached to the upper surface. At this point, the lift coefficient reaches its maximum permissible value, $C_{L,\max}$.

**Step 1 — Write the fundamental lift equation.**

$$L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$$

**Step 2 — Apply the equilibrium condition at the moment of stall.**
For a UAV flying in straight and level flight, lift must equal weight:

$$L = W$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the maximum lift coefficient.**
At the moment just before stall, the lift coefficient is at its peak value, $C_{L,\max}$, and the velocity is the lowest that can support the weight — the stall speed $V_{\text{stall}}$:

$$W = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\text{stall}}^2 \, S \, C_{L,\max}$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $V_{\text{stall}}$.**

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\text{stall}}^2 \, S \, C_{L,\max} = W$$

$$V_{\text{stall}}^2 = \frac{2 W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L,\max}}$$

**Step 5 — Take the positive square root (speed is non-negative).**

$$\boxed{V_{\text{stall}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L,\max}}}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Higher weight $W$ requires a higher stall speed (heavier aircraft fly faster). Larger wing area $S$ and higher $C_{L,\max}$ reduce stall speed. This is the reason high-lift devices like **flaps and slats** are used during takeoff and landing — they temporarily raise $C_{L,\max}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Breguet Range Equation

This is the *most repeated* derivation question in KTU Mobile Robotics for fixed-wing UAVs. We assume a jet-propelled, propeller-driven fixed-wing UAV cruising at constant altitude with constant $C_L$, $C_D$, and $L/D$.

**Step 1 — Relate fuel mass flow to thrust and TSFC.**
The specific fuel consumption parameter $c_T$ is defined as fuel mass flow per unit thrust:

$$\dot{m}_f = c_T \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Apply the cruise condition $T = D$.**
In level cruise, all available thrust is used purely to overcome drag:

$$T = D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_D$$

**Step 3 — Express the weight as a function of fuel burned.**
As the UAV burns fuel, total weight decreases. The rate of weight change equals the negative of the fuel flow rate (lift supports the weight, so $L = W$):

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = - \dot{m}_f = -c_T \cdot D = -c_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_D$$

**Step 4 — Relate drag to weight using the lift-to-drag ratio.**
Since $L = W = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$, we can write:

$$D = \frac{C_D}{C_L} \cdot W = \frac{W}{(L/D)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $D$ into the weight-rate equation.**

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = - c_T \cdot \frac{W}{(L/D)}$$

**Step 6 — Separate variables $W$ and $t$, then integrate.**

$$\frac{dW}{W} = - c_T \cdot \frac{dt}{(L/D)}$$

Integrating from initial weight $W_0$ to final weight $W_1$, and from $t=0$ to $t = E$ (endurance):

$$\int_{W_0}^{W_1} \frac{dW}{W} = - \frac{c_T}{(L/D)} \int_{0}^{E} dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{W_1}{W_0}\right) = - \frac{c_T \cdot E}{(L/D)}$$

$$E = \frac{(L/D)}{c_T} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{W_0}{W_1}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Convert endurance $E$ into range $R$ by multiplying by cruise speed $V$.**
Since $R = V \cdot E$:

$$\boxed{R = V \cdot \frac{(L/D)}{c_T} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{W_0}{W_1}\right) = \frac{V}{c_T} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_D} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{W_0}{W_1}\right)}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fixed-Wing vs. Rotary-Wing

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison is the *most-asked* 7-mark sub-question in KTU ESE under the module "Types of UAVs". Memorize the entries and the supporting physics.

| Parameter | **Fixed-Wing UAV** | **Rotary-Wing UAV** |
|---|---|---|
| **Lift Generation** | Forward speed over fixed wings | Spinning rotor blades |
| **Hovering Ability** | ❌ Not possible | ✅ Inherently capable |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Very high (aerodynamic) | Low (constant rotor power) |
| **Endurance** | High (1 – 40+ hours) | Low (20 – 45 minutes) |
| **Speed** | High (50 – 500+ km/h) | Low to moderate (≤ 100 km/h) |
| **Take-off / Landing** | Needs runway, catapult, or net | Vertical (no runway) |
| **Payload Capacity** | High (10 – 1000+ kg) | Limited (≤ 25 kg typical) |
| **Maneuverability** | Wide turns, large radius | Tight turns, point hover |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Lower (no swashplate) | Higher (rotor hub, cyclic/collective) |
| **Mission Profile** | Survey, mapping, long-range ISR | Inspection, surveillance, precision delivery |
| **Susceptibility to Wind** | High (forward motion disrupted) | Moderate (can stabilise in hover) |
| **Stall Risk** | Yes — must maintain $V > V_{\text{stall}}$ | No stall in hover |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Fixed-Wing UAV

Below is a detailed **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping the interactions between every subsystem of a fixed-wing UAV. This is the kind of block diagram KTU examiners expect in a 7-mark "Explain the architecture" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([MISSION COMMAND STATION]) --> A2[Ground Control Station GCS]
    A2 --> A3[C2 DATALINK RADIO 2.4 GHz]

    A3 --> B1[ONBOARD AVIONICS BAY]
    B1 --> B2[Autopilot FCU e.g. Pixhawk]
    B2 --> B3[IMU 9 axis sensor]
    B2 --> B4[GPS Receiver]
    B2 --> B5[Air Pressure Sensor]

    B1 --> C1[FLIGHT CONTROL SURFACES]
    C1 --> C2[Aileron Roll control]
    C1 --> C3[Elevator Pitch control]
    C1 --> C4[Rudder Yaw control]
    C1 --> C5[Throttle Engine control]

    B1 --> D1[PROPULSION SUBSYSTEM]
    D1 --> D2[BLDC Motor or Piston Engine]
    D2 --> D3[Propeller generates Thrust T]
    D3 --> D4[Forward motion creates Dynamic Pressure q]

    D4 --> E1[AERODYNAMIC FORCES ON WINGS]
    E1 --> E2[Wing generates Lift L]
    E1 --> E3[Airframe generates Drag D]
    E1 --> E4[Gravity creates Weight W]
    E2 --> F1([UAV FLIGHT PATH])
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    D3 --> F1

    F1 --> G1[PAYLOAD SENSORS]
    G1 --> G2[EO IR Camera]
    G1 --> G3[LiDAR Scanner]
    G1 --> G4[Multispectral Imager]
    G1 --> G5[SAR Radar optional]

    G1 --> A2
    G2 --> A2
    G3 --> A2
    G4 --> A2
    G5 --> A2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The ground operator sends commands via the C2 datalink. The autopilot reads sensor data (IMU, GPS, barometer) and commands the control surfaces. The engine produces thrust, the airframe produces drag, the wings produce lift, and gravity produces weight. Payload sensors stream data back to the GCS.

## 4.2 Force Balance Free-Body Diagram (Textual Schematic)

```
            LIFT (L)  ↑
                       |         
        __________________________
       /                          \
      /        WING (S)             \
     /                                \
    /__________________________________\
                       |         
            WEIGHT (W) ↓

     ←—— DRAG (D)        THRUST (T) ——→
            ←—————————→        
              VELOCITY (V)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **steady, level, unaccelerated flight**, the four arrows form a closed vector rectangle: $L = W$ (vertical) and $T = D$ (horizontal). Any deviation from this state causes the UAV to climb, descend, accelerate, or decelerate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]
**"Differentiate between fixed-wing and rotary-wing UAVs with respect to lift generation and hovering capability."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed-Wing UAV | Rotary-Wing UAV |
|---|---|---|
| Lift Generation | Produced by **forward motion** of rigid wings through air | Produced by **spinning rotor blades** |
| Hovering | **Not possible** — requires continuous forward speed | **Inherently capable** of stationary hover |
| Energy Source for Lift | Kinetic energy of forward motion | Continuous rotational mechanical power |

A fixed-wing UAV must maintain a minimum airspeed equal to the **stall speed** $V_{\text{stall}}$ to stay airborne, whereas a rotary-wing UAV can remain stationary in the air by balancing the rotor thrust against gravity.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**"Define the aspect ratio of a fixed-wing UAV and state its effect on aerodynamic efficiency."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Aspect Ratio (AR)** of a fixed-wing UAV is defined as the ratio of the **square of the wingspan (b²)** to the **wing planform area (S)**:

$$AR = \frac{b^2}{S}$$

**Effect on aerodynamic efficiency:** A higher aspect ratio (long, narrow wings) significantly reduces the **induced drag** $D_i$ at the wingtips, thereby increasing the **lift-to-drag ratio (L/D)**. A high L/D directly improves both endurance and range of the UAV. This is why gliders, high-altitude surveillance drones (e.g., Global Hawk), and long-endurance solar UAVs all use very high aspect ratio wings.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, describe the **major subsystems of a fixed-wing UAV** and explain the role of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Breguet Range Equation** for a jet-propelled fixed-wing UAV in cruise, clearly stating all assumptions. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

A fixed-wing UAV is composed of **six major functional subsystems**, each performing a critical role:

**1. Airframe (1 mark)** — Comprises the fuselage, wings, and empennage. Provides structural shape, generates lift via the wings, and houses all other subsystems.

**2. Propulsion System (1 mark)** — Consists of the engine (electric BLDC, piston, or turbojet) coupled to a propeller or jet nozzle. Generates forward thrust to overcome drag.

**3. Powerplant & Energy Source (1 mark)** — Li-Po battery, gasoline, HFE, or kerosene. Stores the energy required for sustained flight.

**4. Avionics & Flight Control Unit (FCU) (1.5 marks)** — The "brain" of the UAV. Includes autopilot (e.g., Pixhawk, Ardupilot), IMU (gyroscope + accelerometer + magnetometer), GPS, and barometer. Implements closed-loop PID control of the aircraft.

**5. Communication Datalink (1 mark)** — Bi-directional radio link (C2 command link + payload telemetry link) connecting the UAV to the Ground Control Station (GCS).

**6. Payload (1.5 marks)** — Mission-specific sensors such as EO/IR camera, LiDAR, multispectral imager, or SAR radar.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **Ground Control Station (GCS)** as a part of the Unmanned Aircraft **System (UAS)**. KTU expects the full UAS definition: UAV + GCS + Datalink. Missing this costs 1 mark.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Cruise flight at constant altitude (no climb/descent).
2. Constant $C_L$, $C_D$, and $L/D$ throughout the cruise.
3. Thrust-specific fuel consumption $c_T$ is constant.
4. Steady level flight: $L = W$ and $T = D$.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from the definition of specific fuel consumption:

$$\dot{m}_f = c_T \cdot T$$

In level cruise, $T = D$:

$$\dot{m}_f = c_T \cdot D$$

Since lift equals weight in level flight:

$$D = \frac{W}{(L/D)}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = -\dot{m}_f = -c_T \cdot \frac{W}{(L/D)}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $W_0$ to $W_1$ over endurance $E$:

$$\int_{W_0}^{W_1} \frac{dW}{W} = -\frac{c_T}{(L/D)} \int_0^E dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{W_0}{W_1}\right) = \frac{c_T \cdot E}{(L/D)}$$

$$E = \frac{(L/D)}{c_T} \ln\left(\frac{W_0}{W_1}\right)$$

Multiplying by cruise velocity $V$ to obtain range $R$:

$$\boxed{R = V \cdot \frac{(L/D)}{c_T} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{W_0}{W_1}\right)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write $W_1/W_0$ instead of $W_0/W_1$ inside the logarithm, or forget to multiply by $V$ at the end, yielding endurance instead of range. This single sign error costs 1–2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare the **characteristics of fixed-wing and rotary-wing UAVs** across at least eight parameters. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A small fixed-wing UAV has a **mass of 5 kg**, **wing area of 0.6 m²**, and a **maximum lift coefficient $C_{L,\max} = 1.4$**. Calculate the **stall speed** at sea level. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

A comprehensive comparison across **eight parameters** is given in the table below (1 mark per two well-explained parameters; minimum four pairs for full marks):

| Parameter | Fixed-Wing | Rotary-Wing |
|---|---|---|
| Lift Generation | Forward speed over wings | Spinning rotor blades |
| Hovering | Cannot hover | Can hover in place |
| Endurance | High (hours) | Low (minutes) |
| Speed | High (50–500 km/h) | Low to moderate (≤100 km/h) |
| Take-off/Landing | Runway or launcher | Vertical |
| Energy Efficiency | High aerodynamic efficiency | Low due to rotor downwash losses |
| Mechanical Complexity | Lower (fewer moving parts) | Higher (rotor hub, swashplate) |
| Payload Capacity | High (10–1000+ kg) | Limited (≤25 kg) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing only 3–4 parameters will cap your marks at 4. The KTU 2024 scheme requires **a minimum of six to eight parameters** with a brief justification for full 7 marks. Do not present a one-line answer.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Mass $m = 5$ kg
- Wing area $S = 0.6$ m²
- Maximum lift coefficient $C_{L,\max} = 1.4$
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Calculate the weight of the UAV (1 mark).**

$$W = m \cdot g = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Write the stall speed equation (1 mark).**

$$V_{\text{stall}} = \sqrt{\frac{2W}{\rho \, S \, C_{L,\max}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (1 mark).**

$$V_{\text{stall}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 49.05}{1.225 \times 0.6 \times 1.4}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator (1 mark).**

$$1.225 \times 0.6 \times 1.4 = 1.029$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator and divide (1 mark).**

$$\frac{2 \times 49.05}{1.029} = \frac{98.10}{1.029} = 95.335$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root (1 mark).**

$$\boxed{V_{\text{stall}} = \sqrt{95.335} \approx 9.76 \text{ m/s} \approx 35.1 \text{ km/h}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is forgetting to multiply mass by $g$ to obtain weight in Newtons. Writing $V_{\text{stall}} = \sqrt{2m / (\rho S C_{L,\max})}$ directly is dimensionally wrong and costs 1 mark. Always convert mass to weight first.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist — Fixed-Wing UAVs**

- ✅ A **Fixed-Wing UAV** generates lift through **forward motion over rigid wings** — it **cannot hover**.
- ✅ The **four fundamental forces** are: Lift $L$, Weight $W$, Thrust $T$, Drag $D$.
- ✅ **Equilibrium condition** in level cruise: $L = W$ and $T = D$.
- ✅ **Lift equation:** $L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$
- ✅ **Drag equation:** $D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_D$
- ✅ **Stall speed:** $V_{\text{stall}} = \sqrt{2W / (\rho S C_{L,\max})}$
- ✅ **Breguet Range:** $R = (V/c_T) \cdot (C_L/C_D) \cdot \ln(W_0/W_1)$
- ✅ **Breguet Endurance:** $E = (C_L/C_D) \cdot \ln(W_0/W_1) / c_T$
- ✅ **Aspect Ratio:** $AR = b^2 / S$ → high AR = high efficiency
- ✅ **Wing Loading:** $W/S = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{\text{stall}}^2 C_{L,\max}$
- ✅ **Six subsystems:** Airframe, Propulsion, Powerplant, Avionics, Datalink, Payload (+ GCS for full UAS).
- ✅ **Strengths:** Long endurance, high speed, large payload, high altitude.
- ✅ **Weaknesses:** No hover, needs runway/catapult, vulnerable to strong crosswinds, stall risk.
- ✅ **Typical use cases:** Aerial mapping, long-range ISR, agricultural survey, border patrol, atmospheric research.
- ✅ **Standard air density at sea level:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** before any derivation — KTU awards 1–2 marks for this alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotary-Wing UAV - Multi-Rotor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rotary-Wing UAV - Multi-Rotor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Rotary-Wing Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)** is an aerial robotic platform that achieves lift, propulsion, and control through the rotation of multiple rigid blades mounted on powered rotors, without requiring any fixed aerodynamic surfaces (wings, ailerons, or rudders). A **Multi-Rotor UAV** is a specific class of rotary-wing UAV that uses **three or more** independently actuated rotor assemblies to generate the aerodynamic forces and moments required for sustained flight.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** A Multi-Rotor UAV is defined as a Vertical Take-Off and Landing (VTOL) rotorcraft whose flight envelope is controlled by modulating the angular velocities of its multiple fixed-pitch rotors. The vehicle is **under-actuated** (typically 4 control inputs for 6 Degrees of Freedom) and **dynamically unstable** in open-loop, requiring closed-loop feedback for stable flight.

### Key Operational Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Quadrotor) |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor Count | $N_r$ | 3 to 8 |
| Total Degrees of Freedom | DOF | 6 (3 Translational + 3 Rotational) |
| Independent Control Inputs | $U$ | 4 (Thrust, Roll, Pitch, Yaw) |
| Actuator Type | - | BLDC Motor + Fixed-Pitch Propeller |
| Hover Thrust | $T_{hov}$ | 2$\times$ to 3$\times$ vehicle weight |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine balancing a **single broomstick on the tip of your finger**. You must constantly make tiny adjustments to keep it upright. Now imagine trying to do this while simultaneously wanting to walk in any direction and rotate — that is essentially what a quadrotor must do, **400 to 2000 times per second**!
>
> A multi-rotor works like a **flying Swiss clock**: every rotor is a gear, and by changing the speed of just the right gear combinations, the entire clock (the aircraft) moves precisely the way you want — up, down, left, right, forward, backward, or spinning.

### Why Multi-Rotor and Not a Helicopter?

A traditional helicopter uses a **swashplate** — a complex mechanical assembly that physically tilts each rotor blade as it spins to change lift direction. A multi-rotor eliminates the swashplate entirely. Instead, it uses **multiple rotors spinning at variable speeds**. By simply making one rotor spin faster than another, the imbalance creates the torque/moment needed to tilt the aircraft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Highlight:** The trade-off is *mechanical simplicity for electronic complexity*. A multi-rotor is mechanically simple (no swashplate, no tail rotor) but demands an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** running a high-rate feedback loop. This is the fundamental inversion of complexity between helicopters and multi-rotors.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Multi-Rotor UAVs

Multi-rotors are classified strictly by **rotor count** (this is the KTU-mandated taxonomy):

> [!NOTE]
> - **Tricopter** ($N_r = 3$) — Uses a tilting rear rotor for yaw authority.
> - **Quadrotor / Quadcopter** ($N_r = 4$) — The most ubiquitous configuration.
> - **Hexacopter** ($N_r = 6$) — Higher payload, redundancy, fault tolerance.
> - **Octocopter** ($N_r = 8$) — Heavy-lift, cinema-grade, maximum redundancy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often confuse "rotor" with "propeller". In KTU 2024 terminology, a **rotor** refers to the complete rotating assembly (hub + blades + motor), while a **propeller** refers to the blade set only. The aerodynamic theory treats them as equivalent in momentum/actuator-disc analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — How a Multi-Rotor Flies

A multi-rotor achieves all six degrees of freedom using **only four control inputs** by exploiting the **asymmetric thrust** principle. The four control inputs map as follows:

1. **Throttle (Collective / Heave):** All rotors spin at the *same incremental speed*. Sum of all thrusts changes.
2. **Roll (Lateral Tilt):** Motors on one side spin faster than the other side. The vehicle tilts left/right.
3. **Pitch (Longitudinal Tilt):** Front motors differ from rear motors. The vehicle tilts forward/backward.
4. **Yaw (Heading Rotation):** Clockwise-spinning rotors are sped up while counter-clockwise rotors are slowed (or vice-versa). Conservation of angular momentum causes the body to rotate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does a multi-rotor have a yaw problem?** Newton's Third Law: every spinning rotor creates a **reaction torque** on the body in the *opposite* direction. If all rotors spin the same way, the body would spin uncontrollably. The solution (used in every multi-rotor) is to use **counter-rotating** rotor pairs. For example, in an X-config quadrotor, motors $M_1$ and $M_3$ spin CCW, while $M_2$ and $M_4$ spin CW. Yaw control works by breaking this balance temporarily.

---

## 2.2 Standard Configurations of a Quadrotor

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Students must know all four configurations and the corresponding motor speed control laws.

### (a) Plus (+) Configuration
- Motors aligned with body axes: Front, Right, Back, Left.
- Pitch control = Front/Back motors.
- Roll control = Left/Right motors.
- Simpler control law; lower agility.

### (b) Cross (X) Configuration
- Motors placed at 45° to body axes: Front-Right, Front-Left, Back-Left, Back-Right.
- Pitch = (Front pair) − (Rear pair).
- Roll = (Right pair) − (Left pair).
- **Industry standard** — best maneuverability.

### (c) H Configuration
- Two front arms angled outward; two rear arms parallel.
- Asymmetric inertia — used in specific research platforms.

### (d) Y / Y-I Configuration
- Front arm has a single motor (like Tricopter with 2 rear).
- Hybrid layout.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE (Pipeline-Safe):** Vertical pipe $\vert$ is used instead of $\vert$ symbol inside table rows to prevent markdown parsing errors. Use \vert for absolute value notation.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single-Rotor Thrust (Actuator Model) | $T_i = b \, \omega_i^2$ | $b$ = thrust coefficient, $\omega_i$ = rotor angular speed | N (Newton) |
| 2 | Single-Rotor Reaction Torque | $\tau_i = d \, \omega_i^2$ | $d$ = drag/torque coefficient | N·m |
| 3 | Total Thrust (Quadrotor) | $U_1 = b\left(\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2\right)$ | Collective thrust along body $z$-axis | N |
| 4 | Roll Moment (X-Config) | $U_2 = \dfrac{bL}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\omega_4^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_1^2 - \omega_3^2\right)$ | About body $x$-axis | N·m |
| 5 | Pitch Moment (X-Config) | $U_3 = \dfrac{bL}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\omega_1^2 + \omega_4^2 - \omega_2^2 - \omega_3^2\right)$ | About body $y$-axis | N·m |
| 6 | Yaw Moment (X-Config) | $U_4 = d\left(\omega_2^2 + \omega_4^2 - \omega_1^2 - \omega_3^2\right)$ | About body $z$-axis | N·m |
| 7 | Air Density (ISA Sea-Level) | $\rho = 1.225$ | **Bold** standard constant | kg/m³ |
| 8 | Hover Induced Velocity | $v_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{2 \rho A}}$ | $A$ = rotor disc area = $\pi R^2$ | m/s |
| 9 | Hover Power (Momentum Theory) | $P_{hov} = T \cdot v_0 = \dfrac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}$ | Minimum power for hover | W (Watt) |
| 10 | Figure of Merit (Efficiency) | $FM = \dfrac{P_{ideal}}{P_{actual}} \leq 0.7$ | $\vert FM \vert \leq 1$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Translational DOF | $\dot{\xi} = R \cdot V_b$ | $R$ = rotation matrix (Earth to Body) | m/s |
| 12 | Rotational DOF (Euler) | $\dot{\eta} = W \cdot \Omega$ | $W$ = Euler rate matrix | rad/s |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The $T_i = b\omega_i^2$ relationship is the **most important equation** in multi-rotor dynamics. Every flight controller (PX4, ArduPilot, Betaflight) takes desired thrust, inverts this to get desired $\omega_i$, and sends that to the **Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)**. The coefficient $b$ is identified experimentally and stored in firmware.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Multi-Rotor? |
|---|---|
| Precision Agriculture | Hover for spraying; VTOL from confined farmyards |
| Cinematography | Stable gimbal-friendly platform; ultra-smooth flight |
| Search & Rescue | Vertical penetration into collapsed structures |
| Warehouse Logistics | Indoor flight; precise pickup/drop |
| 5G/6G Aerial Base Stations | Hovering at fixed altitude as relays |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Thrust from Momentum Theory (Actuator-Disc Model)

> **Goal:** Derive the relationship $T = 2 \rho A v_0^2$ for a hovering rotor and show how thrust scales with rotational speed.

### Step 1: Define the Control Volume
Consider an **actuator disc** of area $A = \pi R^2$ where $R$ is the rotor radius. The rotor induces a uniform axial velocity $v_0$ (called *induced velocity* or *downwash*) through the disc.

### Step 2: Apply Conservation of Mass
Mass flow rate through the disc:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A v_0
$$

The rotor pulls air from above at velocity $v_0$ and ejects it below at velocity $v_0 + w$, where $w$ is the *far-wake velocity*. For minimum induced power, $w = v_0$, giving an effective exit velocity of $2v_0$.

### Step 3: Apply Conservation of Momentum (Thrust Equation)
Thrust = mass flow rate $\times$ change in velocity:

$$
T = \dot{m} \cdot (2v_0) = \rho A v_0 \cdot 2v_0
$$

Therefore:

$$
T = 2 \rho A v_0^2
$$

### Step 4: Solve for Induced Velocity

$$
v_0 = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}}
$$

### Step 5: Link to Rotational Speed via Blade Element Theory
For a single rotor, the thrust also depends on $\omega^2$:

$$
T = b \, \omega^2
$$

Equating the two expressions and substituting $A = \pi R^2$:

$$
b \, \omega^2 = 2 \rho \pi R^2 v_0^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_0 = \omega \sqrt{\frac{b}{2 \rho \pi R^2}}
$$

This proves that **induced velocity is directly proportional to rotor angular velocity** — the foundation of all motor-mixing equations in flight controllers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Hover Power (Momentum Theory)

### Step 1: Define the Work Done on the Air
The rate of work (power imparted to air) = Force $\times$ Velocity of force application. The force is the thrust $T$ applied at the disc where the velocity is $v_0$:

$$
P_{ideal} = T \cdot v_0
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Induced Velocity
From Derivation I, $v_0 = \sqrt{T / (2 \rho A)}$:

$$
P_{ideal} = T \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}}
$$

### Step 3: Final Form

$$
P_{ideal} = \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** This is the famous **"$T$ to the three-halves" law** of rotorcraft hover. It means that if you double the payload (and thus the thrust), the required hover power increases by a factor of $2^{3/2} \approx 2.83$, **not 2**. This is why heavy-lift drones have disproportionately short flight times.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Newton-Euler Equations of Motion for a Quadrotor

### Step 1: Define Coordinate Frames
- **Earth frame (E):** Inertial, fixed to ground, axes $X_E, Y_E, Z_E$ (Up positive).
- **Body frame (B):** Fixed to the vehicle CG, axes $X_B$ (forward), $Y_B$ (right), $Z_B$ (down — aerospace convention).

### Step 2: Translational Dynamics
Apply Newton's Second Law in the body frame, then transform to Earth frame using the rotation matrix $R$:

$$
m \ddot{\xi} = R \cdot F_{total}
$$

Where $F_{total}$ is the body-frame force vector. The only aerodynamic force in hover flight is thrust along $Z_B$:

$$
F_{total} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -U_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Expanding with the full Euler-angle rotation matrix $R(\phi, \theta, \psi)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m \ddot{x} &= U_1 \left( \cos\phi \sin\theta \cos\psi + \sin\phi \sin\psi \right) \\
m \ddot{y} &= U_1 \left( \cos\phi \sin\theta \sin\psi - \sin\phi \cos\psi \right) \\
m \ddot{z} &= U_1 \left( \cos\phi \cos\theta \right) - m g
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Rotational Dynamics
Apply Euler's equation $M = I \dot{\Omega} + \Omega \times (I \Omega)$. For small angles and diagonal inertia matrix $I = \text{diag}(I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} &= U_2 - \dot{\theta}\dot{\psi}(I_{zz} - I_{yy}) \\
I_{yy} \ddot{\theta} &= U_3 - \dot{\phi}\dot{\psi}(I_{xx} - I_{zz}) \\
I_{zz} \ddot{\psi} &= U_4 - \dot{\phi}\dot{\theta}(I_{yy} - I_{xx})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Decoupled Small-Angle Approximation
For low-speed hover and small attitude angles ($\phi, \theta \ll 1$), the gyroscopic coupling terms $\dot{\theta}\dot{\psi}$, $\dot{\phi}\dot{\psi}$, $\dot{\phi}\dot{\theta}$ become negligible, yielding the **decoupled linearized model**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} &= U_2 \\
I_{yy} \ddot{\theta} &= U_3 \\
I_{zz} \ddot{\psi} &= U_4
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU exam:** The linearized decoupled form is what every PID flight controller in industry is actually based on. The four control inputs $U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4$ are independently designed, then re-mixed to motor speeds. This is the core of **cascade control architecture**.

### Step 5: Total Thrust Expression in Terms of Motor Speeds
Summing contributions of all four rotors:

$$
U_1 = b \left( \omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2 \right)
$$

The single most important engineering fact: **Thrust scales with the square of motor RPM**, not linearly. This is why motor RPM changes are computed using square-root operations in flight controllers.

---

## 3.4 Verification of Hover Condition

For steady hover, all accelerations are zero: $\ddot{x} = \ddot{y} = \ddot{z} = 0$ and $\ddot{\phi} = \ddot{\theta} = \ddot{\psi} = 0$.

From the $z$-equation:

$$
U_1 = \frac{m g}{\cos\phi \cos\theta}
$$

For small angles:

$$
U_1 \approx m g
$$

Therefore, in hover, the total thrust equals the weight of the vehicle. The **Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (TWR)** is defined as:

$$
TWR = \frac{T_{max}}{m g}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** A TWR $< 1$ means the vehicle **cannot** hover. A TWR $= 1$ gives *zero* margin. Practical multi-rotors use $TWR \approx 2.0$ to $2.5$ for agility and $TWR \approx 1.3$ for efficient long-endurance flight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Quadrotor Control Architecture (X-Configuration)

This is a complete functional block diagram of an X-configuration quadrotor's control loop. Every flight controller in production (PX4, ArduPilot, DJI A3/N3) implements exactly this architecture.

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeP[Ground Station or Remote Pilot] --> nodeG[Desired Trajectory Xd Yd Zd Yawd]
    nodeG --> nodeA[Outer Position Controller PID]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Desired Attitude Phid Thetad]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Inner Attitude Controller PID]
    nodeH[IMU Gyro and Accelerometer] --> nodeI[State Estimator Complementary Filter]
    nodeI --> nodeC
    nodeI --> nodeA
    nodeC --> nodeD[Rate Controller PD]
    nodeH --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Motor Mixer Matrix M]
    nodeE --> nodeF1[ESC 1 Motor M1 Front Right CW]
    nodeE --> nodeF2[ESC 2 Motor M2 Rear Right CCW]
    nodeE --> nodeF3[ESC 3 Motor M3 Rear Left CW]
    nodeE --> nodeF4[ESC 4 Motor M4 Front Left CCW]
    nodeF1 --> nodeJ[Vehicle Dynamics 6DOF Newton Euler]
    nodeF2 --> nodeJ
    nodeF3 --> nodeJ
    nodeF4 --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeH
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Output Pose X Y Z Roll Pitch Yaw]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows show the flow of information, not physical force. The IMU is the only feedback sensor in the inner loop (running at **1 kHz to 8 kHz**). GPS is only added to the outer loop at **50 Hz to 100 Hz**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Multi-Rotor Family Tree and Configuration Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeRoot[Rotary Wing UAV]
    nodeRoot --> nodeA1[Single Rotor Helicopter]
    nodeRoot --> nodeA2[Multi Rotor UAV]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB1[With Swashplate]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB2[Tricopter 3 rotors]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB3[Quadcopter 4 rotors]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB4[Hexacopter 6 rotors]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB5[Octocopter 8 rotors]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC1[Plus Configuration]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC2[Cross X Configuration Industry Standard]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC3[H Configuration]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC4[Y Configuration]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD1[M1 FR CW]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD2[M2 RR CCW]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD3[M3 RL CW]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD4[M4 FL CCW]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Control Mixing Matrix for X-Configuration Quadrotor

The diagram below shows how the four high-level control inputs (Collective, Roll, Pitch, Yaw) are **mixed** into four motor commands. The signs are KTU-examinable.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeU1[U1 Collective Thrust Positive] --> nodeMix
    nodeU2[U2 Roll Left Right] --> nodeMix
    nodeU3[U3 Pitch Forward Back] --> nodeMix
    nodeU4[U4 Yaw Heading] --> nodeMix
    nodeMix[Mixer Matrix] --> nodeM1[M1 omega1sq]
    nodeMix --> nodeM2[M2 omega2sq]
    nodeMix --> nodeM3[M3 omega3sq]
    nodeMix --> nodeM4[M4 omega4sq]
```

**The Algebraic Mixing Equations (X-Configuration):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_1^2 &= \frac{1}{4b}\left(U_1 - U_3 + U_4\right) \\
\omega_2^2 &= \frac{1}{4b}\left(U_1 - U_2 - U_4\right) \\
\omega_3^2 &= \frac{1}{4b}\left(U_1 + U_3 + U_4\right) \\
\omega_4^2 &= \frac{1}{4b}\left(U_1 + U_2 - U_4\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Mastery (Frequently Tested):**
> - Positive **Roll** ($U_2 > 0$): $M_2$ and $M_4$ speeds **decrease**; $M_1$ and $M_3$ speeds **increase** → vehicle rolls **right** (body $X$-axis convention).
> - Positive **Pitch** ($U_3 > 0$): $M_3$ and $M_4$ speeds **increase**; $M_1$ and $M_2$ speeds **decrease** → vehicle pitches **forward**.
> - Positive **Yaw** ($U_4 > 0$): CCW rotor pair **speeds up**; CW pair **slows down** → body yaws **CCW**.

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram: Open-Loop Instability vs. Closed-Loop Stability

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeOL[Open Loop Multi Rotor] --> nodeOLY[Inherently Unstable Poles in RHP]
    nodeOLY --> nodeOLF[Tumbles Within 0.2s Without Control]
    nodeFB[Feedback Path IMU 1kHz] --> nodeFBY[Stabilizes Poles to LHP]
    nodeFBY --> nodeFBF[Stable Hover Achieved]
    nodeOL --> nodeFB
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Dec 2024]**

### Q1. Define a Multi-Rotor UAV. Differentiate it from a fixed-wing UAV in terms of lift generation and flight envelope.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Multi-Rotor UAV** is an unmanned aerial vehicle that uses **three or more fixed-pitch rotors** to generate lift and control. The lift is produced entirely by the **momentum imparted to the air** by the rotating blades (actuator-disc principle), without any forward airspeed required.

**Differentiation Table:**

| Aspect | Multi-Rotor | Fixed-Wing |
|---|---|---|
| Lift Source | Rotor thrust (hover) | Forward speed over wings |
| Take-off | Vertical (VTOL) | Runway or catapult |
| Hover Capability | Yes (native) | No |
| Energy Efficiency | Low (3–5× higher power in hover) | High (glide possible) |
| Mechanization | Variable RPM motors | Control surfaces |

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1M) + Lift mechanism (1M) + Comparison (1M).

---

### Q2. List and briefly explain the four basic control movements of a quadrotor.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Throttle (Heave):** All four motors increase or decrease RPM in unison. This changes the total thrust $U_1$ and moves the vehicle vertically. (0.75M)

2. **Roll:** A differential RPM command is sent to the left and right motor pairs. This creates a moment about the body $x$-axis, tilting the vehicle and producing lateral acceleration. (0.75M)

3. **Pitch:** A differential RPM command is sent to the front and rear motor pairs. This creates a moment about the body $y$-axis, tilting the vehicle and producing longitudinal acceleration. (0.75M)

4. **Yaw:** The speeds of the CW and CCW rotor pairs are unbalanced. By Newton's third law, the *reaction torque* on the body changes, causing rotation about the body $z$-axis. (0.75M)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, May 2024]**

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Derive the thrust equation for a single rotor using momentum theory. Show that hover power is proportional to $T^{3/2}$. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Control Volume Definition (1 Mark):**
Define a stream-tube of cross-section $A = \pi R^2$ enclosing the rotor. Let $v_0$ be the uniform induced velocity at the disc, and $w$ be the far-wake velocity below the rotor.

**Step 2 — Mass Conservation (1 Mark):**
$$
\dot{m} = \rho A v_0
$$

**Step 3 — Momentum Conservation (2 Marks):**
Air enters at $v_0$ and leaves at $(v_0 + w)$. For minimum induced power, $w = v_0$, so exit velocity is $2v_0$.

$$
T = \dot{m} \cdot 2v_0 = 2 \rho A v_0^2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Induced Velocity (1 Mark):**
$$
v_0 = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}}
$$

**Step 5 — Hover Power (2 Marks):**
$$
P_{ideal} = T \cdot v_0 = T \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}} = \frac{T^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A}}
$$

This proves the **three-halves power law**: $P_{hov} \propto T^{3/2}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Students frequently write the thrust equation as $T = \rho A v_0^2$ (forgetting the factor of 2). The factor of 2 arises from the *velocity increment* across the disc being $2v_0$ (from $v_0$ to $2v_0$), not $v_0$. **Loses 1 full mark.**
> Also, $A = \pi R^2$ must be substituted, not left as $A$, for full credit.

---

#### (b) Explain the four basic control movements of a quadrotor in the X-configuration. With the help of a control mixing table, show how each movement maps to motor RPM changes. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Configuration Setup (1 Mark):**
In the X-configuration, motors are placed at the corners of an "X" relative to the pilot's view:
- $M_1$ (Front-Right) — **CW**
- $M_2$ (Rear-Right) — **CCW**
- $M_3$ (Rear-Left) — **CW**
- $M_4$ (Front-Left) — **CCW**

**Step 2 — Control Movements (4 Marks):**

| Movement | Mechanical Effect | Motor Speed Change |
|---|---|---|
| **Throttle ↑** | All motors increase RPM by $\Delta\omega$ | $M_1, M_2, M_3, M_4$ all $\uparrow$ |
| **Roll → (Right)** | Right side lifts, left side drops | $M_1, M_2 \downarrow$; $M_3, M_4 \uparrow$ |
| **Pitch ↓ (Forward)** | Front drops, back lifts | $M_1, M_4 \downarrow$; $M_2, M_3 \uparrow$ |
| **Yaw → (CW)** | Reaction torque increases CCW | $M_1, M_3 \uparrow$; $M_2, M_4 \downarrow$ |

**Step 3 — Mixing Equations (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_1^2 &= \frac{1}{4b}\left(U_1 - U_3 + U_4\right) \\
\omega_2^2 &= \frac{1}{4b}\left(U_1 - U_2 - U_4\right) \\
\omega_3^2 &= \frac{1}{4b}\left(U_1 + U_3 + U_4\right) \\
\omega_4^2 &= \frac{1}{4b}\left(U_1 + U_2 - U_4\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> Sign errors in the mixing table are the **#1 reason students lose marks** on this question. The signs depend on the rotation direction convention (CW/CCW). Always draw a free-body diagram of the body-frame moment directions before writing signs. Mark deducted: 2 marks per incorrect sign.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) Explain the different multi-rotor configurations (Tricopter, Quadcopter, Hexacopter, Octocopter) with their advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Tricopter (3 Rotors) — 1.5 Marks:**
- Three rotors at the corners of a triangle; one rear rotor is **tiltable** (using a servo) for yaw control.
- **Advantage:** Lightest, cheapest, agile.
- **Limitation:** Yaw servo failure is catastrophic; limited payload.

**Quadcopter (4 Rotors) — 2 Marks:**
- The most common configuration. Two sub-types: **Plus (+)** and **Cross (X)**.
- **X-configuration** is industry standard due to better maneuverability and symmetric motor loading.
- **Advantage:** Mechanically simple, well-understood dynamics, vast controller ecosystem.
- **Limitation:** Zero rotor-failure redundancy. Lose one motor → crash guaranteed.

**Hexacopter (6 Rotors) — 2 Marks:**
- Six rotors arranged symmetrically. **Critical advantage:** Can survive the loss of *one* motor through reallocation of control authority.
- **Advantage:** Higher payload, fault tolerance, smoother flight (more averaging).
- **Limitation:** 50% higher power consumption, heavier, more expensive.

**Octocopter (8 Rotors) — 1.5 Marks:**
- Eight rotors in co-axial or flat configuration. Used in **cinema-grade heavy lift** (e.g., DJI S800, Freefly Alta X).
- **Advantage:** Highest payload, can survive loss of two motors in co-axial setup.
- **Limitation:** Highest cost, lowest efficiency, requires professional pilot.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:**
> Students often confuse "co-axial" with "octo-X". A **co-axial octocopter** has 8 motors on 4 arms (2 stacked per arm). A **flat octocopter** has 8 arms. These are *not* equivalent for fault tolerance. **Loses 0.5–1 mark.**

---

#### (b) Derive the dynamic equations of motion of a quadrotor using the Newton-Euler formulation. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assumptions (1 Mark):**
1. Rigid body.
2. Body $z$-axis points downward (aerospace convention).
3. Origin at the center of gravity.
4. Inertia matrix is diagonal: $I = \text{diag}(I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz})$.
5. Small-angle approximation for hover: $\phi, \theta \approx 0$, $\sin x \approx x$, $\cos x \approx 1$.
6. Gyroscopic coupling terms $\dot{\theta}\dot{\psi}$, $\dot{\phi}\dot{\psi}$, $\dot{\phi}\dot{\theta}$ are negligible.

**Step 2 — Newton Equation for Translation (2 Marks):**
$$
m \ddot{\xi} = R \cdot F_b + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -mg \end{bmatrix}
$$

Expanded:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m \ddot{x} &= U_1 (\cos\phi \sin\theta \cos\psi + \sin\phi \sin\psi) \\
m \ddot{y} &= U_1 (\cos\phi \sin\theta \sin\psi - \sin\phi \cos\psi) \\
m \ddot{z} &= U_1 \cos\phi \cos\theta - mg
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Euler Equation for Rotation (2 Marks):**

$$
I \dot{\Omega} + \Omega \times (I \Omega) = M_b
$$

For the diagonal inertia assumption and small angles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} \ddot{\phi} &= U_2 \\
I_{yy} \ddot{\theta} &= U_3 \\
I_{zz} \ddot{\psi} &= U_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Control Inputs in Terms of Motor Speeds (1 Mark):**
$$
U_1 = b(\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2)
$$
$$
U_2 = \frac{bL}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\omega_4^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_1^2 - \omega_3^2\right)
$$
$$
U_3 = \frac{bL}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\omega_1^2 + \omega_4^2 - \omega_2^2 - \omega_3^2\right)
$$
$$
U_4 = d\left(\omega_2^2 + \omega_4^2 - \omega_1^2 - \omega_3^2\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Discussion (1 Mark):**
The system is **under-actuated** (4 inputs, 6 DOF) and **nonlinear** in the general case. In the small-angle hover regime, it decouples into four **independent second-order systems**, enabling classical PID control. The position controller outputs attitude setpoints; the attitude controller outputs moments; the motor mixer allocates to RPM.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #4:**
> Forgetting the **gravity term** $-mg$ in the $z$-equation loses **1 full mark**. Forgetting to state assumptions loses another **0.5–1 mark**. The gravity term is a *body-force* coupling — it appears because the body frame rotates relative to the Earth frame.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024**

- ✅ A **multi-rotor** uses $\geq 3$ fixed-pitch rotors; lift is generated by **actuator-disc** momentum transfer.
- ✅ A quadrotor is **under-actuated** (4 inputs, 6 DOF) and **open-loop unstable** — closed-loop IMU feedback is mandatory.
- ✅ The **four control inputs** are: Throttle ($U_1$), Roll ($U_2$), Pitch ($U_3$), Yaw ($U_4$).
- ✅ Thrust equation: $T_i = b\omega_i^2$ — *quadratic* dependence on RPM (most important equation in flight control).
- ✅ Yaw control works by **breaking the balance** of counter-rotating rotor pairs; principle: **conservation of angular momentum**.
- ✅ **X-configuration** is the industry standard; **Plus-configuration** is the simplest to teach.
- ✅ Hover thrust = $mg$ (for small angles); Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (TWR) should be **1.3 to 2.5**.
- ✅ Hover power scales as $T^{3/2}$ — the **"three-halves power law"** — explains why heavy lift is so inefficient.
- ✅ Hexacopter and Octocopter provide **rotor-failure redundancy**; quadcopters do not.
- ✅ The dynamic model is the **Newton-Euler formulation**: 6 coupled ODEs (3 translational + 3 rotational).
- ✅ Linearization (small-angle, low-speed) gives **decoupled double-integrator** dynamics → enables classical PID.
- ✅ Standard air density at sea level: $\rho = 1.225$ **kg/m³** (memorize).
- ✅ BLDC motors + Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs) + fixed-pitch propellers replace the **swashplate** of helicopters.
- ✅ Inertia matrix is **diagonal** for symmetric X-config quadrotors: $I = \text{diag}(I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz})$.
- ✅ Coordinate convention: **Body $z$-axis points DOWN** (aerospace/DCM convention used in KTU 2024 syllabus).

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Final Exam Tip:** Questions on multi-rotors in KTU 2024 are almost always in pairs: a **definition/configuration** question (Part A) and a **derivation + control mixing** question (Part B). Master the four mixing equations and the Newton-Euler derivation, and you can secure **17/20 marks** on this topic alone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tricopter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tricopter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tricopter (Tri-rotor Helicopter / Y-4 Configuration UAV)**
> A **Tricopter** is a rotary-wing Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) that generates lift and thrust using **three independently controlled brushless DC motors** driving fixed-pitch propellers, arranged in a triangular airframe. Unlike a quadcopter, the tricopter uses a **mechanically tilted rear rotor (via a yaw-servo)** to control the **yaw (ψ) axis**, since three fixed rotors cannot individually balance reaction torques in the horizontal plane without cyclic variation or differential thrust strategies.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBRUT504 – Mobile Robotics)** syllabus, the Tricopter is studied as a canonical **under-actuated multi-rotor MAV (Micro Aerial Vehicle)** platform within Module 1 (Introduction to Mobile Robots / Aerial Mobile Robotics). It serves as a pedagogical benchmark for analyzing:

- **Thrust vectoring** mechanisms
- **Coupled nonlinear dynamics** in 6-DOF rigid body motion
- **Control allocation (Mixing Matrix)** for non-square actuator configurations
- **Yaw control redundancy** via servo-tilting

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Spinning Eater with One Tilting Fork"** 🍴
> Imagine you are sitting at a round dining table with **three forks** stuck into a rotating Lazy Susan. Each fork (rotor) spins and pushes air downward. Now, the Susan naturally wants to spin the *opposite way* due to Newton's Third Law. If all three forks spun at equal speed, the table would still rotate.
> 
> To stop the rotation, you cannot tilt the front two forks easily, but you **CAN tilt the back fork sideways** using a hinge. This tilting is exactly what the **rear yaw-servo** does on a tricopter — it redirects one rotor's thrust sideways, creating a horizontal force that counteracts the unwanted yaw torque. 🎯

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics for Tricopters:**

- **Standard Mass (m):** 0.5 kg – 2.0 kg (typical hobby/MAV class)
- **Gravity (g):** 9.81 m/s²
- **Propeller Diameter:** 8" – 12" (203 mm – 305 mm)
- **Motor KV Rating:** 800 – 1000 RPM/V
- **Hover Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (TWR):** ≈ **2:1** (minimum safe value)
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** 6 (3 translational + 3 rotational)
- **Actuators:** 4 (3 motors + 1 yaw servo)
- **Under-actuation Index:** 6 DOF / 4 actuators = **1.5** (under-actuated)

## 1.3 Standard Tricopter Configurations

| Configuration | Arm Layout | Yaw Control Location | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Y-3 (Tricopter)** | 120° between front arms, rear arm | Rear rotor tilt | Most common hobby/MAV |
| **T-Tail Tricopter** | Two front + tail boom | Tail rotor tilt | Cinematic drones |
| **Coaxial Tricopter** | Stacked rotors | Variable | Research prototypes |

---

## 1.4 Why Study Tricopters? (Engineering Relevance)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Cost-effective** aerial platforms for surveillance and mapping
> - **Minimal actuator count** → reduced failure probability and lower weight
> - Benchmarks **non-symmetric control allocation** problems in mobile robotics
> - Foundation for understanding **VTOL transitions** in hybrid UAVs (e.g., tailsitter concepts)
> - Direct application of **Newton-Euler** and **Lagrangian** rigid-body dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The "How & Why" Behind Tricopter Flight

A tricopter achieves **6-DOF motion control using only 4 actuators** through a clever mapping called the **Control Mixing Matrix**. Let us break this down step-by-step.

### Step 1 — The Four Control Inputs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A tricopter pilot (or flight controller) commands **four virtual inputs**:
> 1. **Throttle (U₁)** → collective vertical thrust
> 2. **Roll (U₂)** → rotation about body-X axis
> 3. **Pitch (U₃)** → rotation about body-Y axis
> 4. **Yaw (U₄)** → rotation about body-Z axis

### Step 2 — The Four Physical Actuators

The system physically possesses:
- **ω₁, ω₂, ω₃** → angular speeds of the three rotors (rad/s)
- **δ_r** → tilt angle of the rear rotor (rad), controlled by the yaw-servo

### Step 3 — Force and Torque Generation

Each rotor produces:
- **Thrust along its spin axis:** $F_i = k_f \cdot \omega_i^2$
- **Reaction torque about its spin axis:** $\tau_i = k_m \cdot \omega_i^2$

where:
- **$k_f$** = thrust coefficient (N·s²/rad²)
- **$k_m$** = moment/drag coefficient (N·m·s²/rad²)
- **$C_T = k_m / k_f$** = torque-to-thrust ratio (typically **≈ 0.01 – 0.03 m**)

### Step 4 — Why Three Rotors Need a Servo

> [!NOTE]
> In a quadcopter, two rotors spin **CW** and two **CCW**, so the net reaction torque is zero at hover. A **tricopter with three rotors has an ODD count** → reaction torques CANNOT be balanced symmetrically. Therefore, one rotor's thrust vector must be **tilted laterally** by angle $\delta_r$ to produce a **horizontal force component** that creates the counter-yaw moment.

The tilted rear rotor produces:
- **Vertical thrust component:** $F_3 \cos(\delta_r)$
- **Horizontal thrust component:** $F_3 \sin(\delta_r)$ → used for yaw control

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Equations for Tricopter — Memorize These!**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_i = k_f \omega_i^2$ | Rotor thrust | N |
| 2 | $\tau_i = k_m \omega_i^2$ | Rotor reaction torque | N·m |
| 3 | $T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} F_i \cos(\delta_i)$ | Total vertical thrust | N |
| 4 | $F_{yaw,rear} = F_3 \sin(\delta_r)$ | Yaw-controlling horizontal force | N |
| 5 | $m\ddot{z} = T_{total} - mg$ | Altitude dynamics (NED frame) | m/s² |
| 6 | $I_{xx}\ddot{\phi} = \tau_\phi$ | Roll dynamics | rad/s² |
| 7 | $I_{yy}\ddot{\theta} = \tau_\theta$ | Pitch dynamics | rad/s² |
| 8 | $I_{zz}\ddot{\psi} = \tau_\psi$ | Yaw dynamics | rad/s² |
| 9 | $\begin{bmatrix} U_1 \\ U_2 \\ U_3 \\ U_4 \end{bmatrix} = M \begin{bmatrix} \omega_1^2 \\ \omega_2^2 \\ \omega_3^2 \\ \delta_r \end{bmatrix}$ | Control Mixing Matrix equation | Mixed |
| 10 | $C_T = k_m / k_f$ | Torque-to-thrust coefficient ratio | m |
| 11 | $\omega_{hover} = \sqrt{(mg/3)/k_f}$ | Hover angular velocity (all equal) | rad/s |
| 12 | $\delta_{r,max} \approx \pm 15°$ | Typical servo tilt range limit | rad |

## 2.3 Control Mixing Matrix (M) for Y-3 Tricopter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most examination-relevant equation** for tricopters. The matrix **M** maps the **4 virtual control inputs (U₁, U₂, U₃, U₄)** to the **4 physical actuator commands**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{tricopter} &= \begin{bmatrix}
k_f & k_f & k_f & 0 \\
0 & -k_f L & 0 & 0 \\
L\cos(30°)k_f & 0 & -L\cos(30°)k_f & 0 \\
-k_m & k_m & 0 & k_f L_{tail} \sin(\delta_{r,ref})
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- **L** = arm length (distance from CG to motor)
- **L_tail** = distance from CG to rear motor
- The fourth column (servo column) **only** affects the yaw channel (U₄)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The tricopter's under-actuated control allocation strategy is **identical in principle** to:
> - **Quadcopter X-configuration control mixing** (most consumer drones)
> - **Hexacopter / Octocopter** actuator redundancy resolution
> - **Spacecraft reaction wheel + thruster** hybrid control
> - **Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs)** with vectored thrusters
> 
> Understanding the tricopter mixing matrix is therefore a **gateway skill** for all modern multi-rotor and aerospace robotic systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix
