## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hover Condition (Equilibrium Thrust)

> **Problem:** A tricopter has a total mass **m = 1.5 kg** with three identical rotors, each with thrust coefficient **$k_f = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s²/rad²**. Determine the required **hover angular velocity** $\omega_{hover}$ for each motor (assume the rear rotor tilt $\delta_r = 0$ at hover).

**Given:**
- $m = 1.5$ kg
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $k_f = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s²/rad²
- 3 rotors

**Step 1 — Total weight to be supported:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= m \cdot g \\
  &= 1.5 \times 9.81 \\
  &= 14.715 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — At hover, total thrust equals weight, and all three motors spin at equal speed:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= 3 \cdot k_f \cdot \omega_{hover}^2 = mg
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $\omega_{hover}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{hover}^2 &= \frac{mg}{3 k_f} \\
\omega_{hover} &= \sqrt{\frac{mg}{3 k_f}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{hover} &= \sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 9.81}{3 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5}}} \\
              &= \sqrt{\frac{14.715}{3.6 \times 10^{-5}}} \\
              &= \sqrt{408{,}750} \\
              &\approx 639.3 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to RPM (commonly used in drone specs):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{hover,RPM} &= 639.3 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \\
                   &= 639.3 \times 9.5493 \\
                   &\approx 6105 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $\omega_{hover} \approx 639.3$ rad/s $\approx 6105$ RPM. This is a realistic figure for a **920 KV motor on a 11.1V (3S) LiPo** at hover.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Yaw Control via Rear Rotor Tilt

> **Problem:** Calculate the **rear rotor tilt angle $\delta_r$** required to generate a yaw moment of **$\tau_\psi = 0.15$ N·m**, given the rear motor is at $L_{tail} = 0.25$ m from the CG and produces a thrust $F_3 = 5.0$ N.

**Given:**
- $\tau_\psi = 0.15$ N·m
- $L_{tail} = 0.25$ m
- $F_3 = 5.0$ N
- Assumption: yaw from rear tilt dominates (other torques balanced)

**Step 1 — The horizontal force component from the tilted rear rotor is:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{horizontal} &= F_3 \cdot \sin(\delta_r)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — This horizontal force creates a moment about the CG (yaw axis) at distance $L_{tail}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_\psi &= F_{horizontal} \cdot L_{tail} = F_3 \cdot \sin(\delta_r) \cdot L_{tail}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\delta_r$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin(\delta_r) &= \frac{\tau_\psi}{F_3 \cdot L_{tail}} \\
\delta_r &= \arcsin\left( \frac{\tau_\psi}{F_3 \cdot L_{tail}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin(\delta_r) &= \frac{0.15}{5.0 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.15}{1.25} = 0.12 \\
\delta_r &= \arcsin(0.12) \\
\delta_r &\approx 0.1203 \text{ rad} \\
\delta_r &\approx 6.89° \text{ (degrees)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A modest **6.89°** tilt produces a meaningful yaw moment. The servo must therefore be precise within **±0.5°** for fine heading control.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Roll Moment (Coupled Dynamics)

> **Problem:** A tricopter with arm length **L = 0.25 m** experiences a roll command. If motors 1 and 2 (on the roll axis) have thrust **$F_1 = 5.2$ N** and **$F_2 = 4.8$ N**, find the net roll moment.

**Step 1 — The roll moment is generated by the **thrust difference** between left and right motors:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_\phi &= L \cdot (F_1 - F_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_\phi &= 0.25 \times (5.2 - 4.8) \\
         &= 0.25 \times 0.4 \\
         &= 0.10 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A small thrust differential (0.4 N) creates a useful 0.10 N·m roll moment, which accelerates the body. This is the **same principle** as in quadcopters, but here one axis (yaw) is decoupled to the servo.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Table

| Feature | Tricopter | Quadcopter | Hexacopter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **No. of Actuators** | 4 (3 motors + 1 servo) | 4 (4 motors) | 6 (6 motors) |
| **Yaw Control** | Servo tilt (mechanical) | Differential torque (electronic) | Differential torque |
| **Symmetry** | Asymmetric (Y-shape) | Symmetric (X or +) | Symmetric |
| **Failure Tolerance** | Low (1 motor loss = crash) | Low (1 motor loss = crash) | High (can lose 1–2 motors) |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Low | High |
| **Efficiency** | High (3 motors) | Medium | Lowest (6 motors) |
| **Control Mixing** | 4×4 with servo column | 4×4 symmetric | 4×6 over-actuated |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Simplified Closed-Loop Yaw Dynamics

For a **PD controller** on the yaw channel, the closed-loop dynamics are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_\psi &= I_{zz}\ddot{\psi} + c_\psi \dot{\psi} \\
\tau_{cmd} &= K_p(\psi_{ref} - \psi) + K_d(-\dot{\psi}) \\
\ddot{\psi} + \frac{(c_\psi + K_d)}{I_{zz}}\dot{\psi} + \frac{K_p}{I_{zz}}\psi &= \frac{K_p}{I_{zz}}\psi_{ref}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **2nd-order linear ODE** with natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_p/I_{zz}}$ and damping ratio $\zeta = (c_\psi + K_d)/(2\sqrt{K_p \cdot I_{zz}})$. For a tricopter with $I_{zz} \approx 0.02$ kg·m², choosing $K_p = 0.4$ and $K_d = 0.1$ yields $\omega_n \approx 4.47$ rad/s and $\zeta \approx 0.67$ — well-damped.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Tricopter Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reference Commands phi_ref, theta_ref, psi_ref, z_ref] --> B[PD / PID Flight Controller]
    B --> C[Control Mixing Matrix M]
    C --> D1[Throttle to motor 1]
    C --> D2[Throttle to motor 2]
    C --> D3[Throttle to motor 3]
    C --> D4[Yaw servo command delta_r]
    D1 --> E1[M1 BLDC with CCW prop]
    D2 --> E2[M2 BLDC with CW prop]
    D3 --> E3[M3 BLDC with CCW prop]
    D4 --> E4[Servo tilting rear arm]
    E1 --> F[Rigid Airframe Y-3 Frame]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[6-DOF Rigid Body Dynamics]
    G --> H[IMU Sensor Feedback]
    H --> I[State Estimator]
    I --> B
    G --> J[External World Environment]
    J --> I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Tricopter Rotor Configuration (Y-3 Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Frame[Y-3 Tricopter Airframe - Top View]
        direction LR
        M1[Motor 1 - Front Left - CCW]:::motor
        M2[Motor 2 - Front Right - CW]:::motor
        M3[Motor 3 - Rear Center - CCW with Tilt Servo]:::motorTilt
        CG((Center of Gravity)):::cg
    end
    M1 --- CG
    M2 --- CG
    M3 --- CG
    M1 -. 120 deg arm .-> M2
    M2 -. 120 deg arm .-> M3
    M3 -. 120 deg arm .-> M1
    classDef motor fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef motorTilt fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef cg fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Yaw Control Mechanism (Force Vector Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rear Motor Spin Axis] --> B[Original Thrust Vector F3 - Vertical]
    A --> C[Servo Tilts Arm by delta_r Degrees]
    C --> D[Tilted Thrust Vector]
    D --> E[Vertical Component F3 cos delta_r - LIFT]
    D --> F[Horizontal Component F3 sin delta_r - YAW FORCE]
    F --> G[Creates Yaw Moment about CG]
    G --> H[Rotates Tricopter in Yaw Axis]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Control Signal Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pilot / Autonomy Commands] --> Q1[U1 Throttle]
    P1 --> Q2[U2 Roll]
    P1 --> Q3[U3 Pitch]
    P1 --> Q4[U4 Yaw]
    Q1 --> MX[Mixing Matrix M]
    Q2 --> MX
    Q3 --> MX
    Q4 --> MX
    MX --> R1[omega1 squared]
    MX --> R2[omega2 squared]
    MX --> R3[omega3 squared]
    MX --> R4[delta_r - servo angle]
    R1 --> ESC1[ESC1 Motor1]
    R2 --> ESC2[ESC2 Motor2]
    R3 --> ESC3[ESC3 Motor3]
    R4 --> SRV[Servo Driver]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PBRUT504]**
> Explain the role of the **rear yaw-servo** in a tricopter. Why is it not required in a quadcopter?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A tricopter has **three rotors**, an **odd number**, so the reaction torques (CW and CCW) cannot be inherently balanced like in a quadcopter (which has two CW and two CCW rotors). The yaw-servo **mechanically tilts the rear rotor's thrust vector** by an angle $\delta_r$, producing a **horizontal force component** $F_3 \sin(\delta_r)$. This horizontal force creates a **yaw moment** $\tau_\psi = F_3 \sin(\delta_r) \cdot L_{tail}$ about the center of gravity, enabling active yaw control. A quadcopter does not need this because its symmetric CW/CCW pair naturally cancels yaw torque, and yaw is controlled electronically via **differential motor speeds** ($\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_4^2$).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PBRUT504]**
> Define the **control mixing matrix** for a tricopter. What is its dimension and what does each row represent?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The control mixing matrix **M** of a tricopter is a **4×4 matrix** that maps the **four physical actuator commands** (three motor speeds $\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \omega_3^2$ and the servo tilt $\delta_r$) to the **four virtual control inputs**: collective thrust (U₁), roll (U₂), pitch (U₃), and yaw (U₄). 
> - **Row 1** maps to **U₁ (throttle/altitude)** — sums all vertical thrust components.
> - **Row 2** maps to **U₂ (roll)** — thrust difference along the X-axis.
> - **Row 3** maps to **U₃ (pitch)** — thrust difference along the Y-axis.
> - **Row 4** maps to **U₄ (yaw)** — combines motor reaction torques and the servo-generated horizontal force.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PBRUT504]**
> (a) Derive the **equations of motion for a tricopter** in the body frame using the Newton-Euler approach. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
> 
> (b) A tricopter has total mass $m = 1.2$ kg, arm length $L = 0.3$ m, $k_f = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s²/rad², and $k_m = 1.3 \times 10^{-7}$ N·m·s²/rad². Compute the **hover angular velocity** and the **servo tilt angle** needed to produce a yaw moment of 0.08 N·m. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a) — Newton-Euler Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions:**
- Rigid body, symmetric airframe (X-Y plane symmetric)
- Small angle approximation: $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$, $\cos(\theta) \approx 1$
- Aerodynamic drag on frame is negligible
- Ground effect is ignored

**Step 1 — Translational Dynamics (Newton's Second Law):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
m\ddot{x} &= (\cos\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi + \sin\psi \sin\phi) \cdot U_1 \\
m\ddot{y} &= (\sin\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi - \cos\psi \sin\phi) \cdot U_1 \\
m\ddot{z} &= (\cos\theta \cos\phi) \cdot U_1 - mg
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rotational Dynamics (Euler's Equation):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{xx}\ddot{\phi} &= \tau_\phi - (I_{yy} - I_{zz})\dot{\theta}\dot{\psi} \\
I_{yy}\ddot{\theta} &= \tau_\theta - (I_{zz} - I_{xx})\dot{\phi}\dot{\psi} \\
I_{zz}\ddot{\psi} &= \tau_\psi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Control Allocation for Tricopter (Y-3 Configuration):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= k_f(\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 \cos\delta_r) \\
U_2 &= k_f L (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2) \\
U_3 &= k_f L \cos(30°) (\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 - 2\omega_3^2 \cos\delta_r)/2 \\
U_4 &= k_m(-\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2) + k_f L_{tail} \omega_3^2 \sin\delta_r
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **complete Newton-Euler model** used in KTU-evaluated problems. The yaw equation $U_4$ has TWO terms: (i) reaction torque difference between motors 1 and 2, and (ii) the servo-generated horizontal thrust moment.

#### Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $m = 1.2$ kg
- $L = 0.3$ m, $L_{tail} = 0.3$ m
- $k_f = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s²/rad²
- $k_m = 1.3 \times 10^{-7}$ N·m·s²/rad²
- $\tau_\psi = 0.08$ N·m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Hover angular velocity:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{hover} &= \sqrt{\frac{mg}{3 k_f}} \\
              &= \sqrt{\frac{1.2 \times 9.81}{3 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-5}}} \\
              &= \sqrt{392{,}400} \\
              &\approx 626.4 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Hover thrust per motor:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_3 = F_1 = F_2 &= k_f \cdot \omega_{hover}^2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 392400 = 3.924 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Yaw moment from reaction torques (M1 and M2 assumed equal at hover → cancels):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{reaction} &= k_m(-\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Therefore, the entire 0.08 N·m must come from the servo tilt:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_\psi &= k_f L_{tail} \omega_3^2 \sin\delta_r \\
0.08 &= 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3 \times 392400 \times \sin\delta_r \\
0.08 &= 1.1772 \times \sin\delta_r \\
\sin\delta_r &= 0.06795 \\
\delta_r &\approx 0.0680 \text{ rad} \\
\delta_r &\approx 3.895°
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** $\omega_{hover} \approx 626.4$ rad/s and $\delta_r \approx 3.9°$ — well within typical servo limits.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PBRUT504]**
> (a) Compare the **mechanical, electronic, and control complexity** of a tricopter vs. a quadcopter. List two advantages and two disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)**
> 
> (b) For a tricopter, the **control mixing matrix** is non-symmetric. With reference to the equation $\mathbf{U} = M \boldsymbol{\omega}^2$, explain why the **fourth column of M** is non-zero only in the **fourth row (yaw channel)** and the **first row (throttle)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)

| Aspect | Tricopter | Quadcopter |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Complex (tilt mechanism, servo linkage) | Simple (rigid arms) |
| **Electronic** | 3 ESCs + 1 servo driver (4 channels) | 4 ESCs (4 channels) |
| **Control Mixing** | Non-symmetric 4×4 matrix | Symmetric 4×4 matrix |

**Tricopter Advantages:**
1. **Lower cost** — only 3 BLDC motors and 3 ESCs needed
2. **Longer flight time** (less mass, less power) for the same payload
3. **Highly maneuverable** in tight spaces due to the asymmetric thrust vector

**Tricopter Disadvantages:**
1. **Lower failure tolerance** — loss of any rotor is fatal
2. **Mechanical wear** on the yaw servo (servo failure = crash)
3. **Harder to tune** — coupling between yaw and altitude

**Quadcopter Advantages:** Symmetric dynamics, easier PID tuning, mature open-source firmware (PX4, ArduPilot).
**Quadcopter Disadvantages:** Higher power consumption, heavier, more expensive.

#### Solution to Part (b) — Why the 4th Column of M is Special (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recap the structure of the mixing matrix:**
The matrix **M** is 4×4. The first three columns correspond to the three motors ($\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \omega_3^2$). The **fourth column** corresponds to the **servo tilt angle $\delta_r$**.

**Step 2 — The servo only changes the **direction** of motor 3's thrust vector, not the speed:**
- **Throttle (Row 1):** $U_1$ sums vertical components. Since $F_3$ is now $F_3 \cos\delta_r$, the servo angle affects the **net vertical thrust**, so the (1,4) entry is **non-zero** (specifically $k_f \omega_3^2 \cos\delta_r \cdot \partial/\partial\delta_r$ or its small-angle linearization).
- **Roll (Row 2):** Roll is generated by differential thrust between M1 and M2 only. The servo tilts M3, which lies on the roll axis, but symmetric tilting produces no net roll torque (in small-angle approximation, the X-component of M3's thrust is $F_3 \sin\delta_r$, which appears at the same X-arm as M1/M2 — but cancels in symmetric flight). Therefore, the (2,4) entry is **zero**.
- **Pitch (Row 3):** Pitch is generated by the front-to-rear thrust difference. Tilting the rear rotor by $\delta_r$ DOES create a small pitch coupling, but in **decoupled control design**, this is treated as a **disturbance** and compensated separately. So in the simplified matrix, the (3,4) entry is often **zero or a small constant**.
- **Yaw (Row 4):** The primary purpose of the servo is yaw. The horizontal force $F_3 \sin\delta_r$ at distance $L_{tail}$ creates a yaw moment — this is the **dominant** entry in row 4, column 4. Hence, the (4,4) entry is **non-zero and large**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The fourth column of **M** appears strongly in **Row 1 (throttle)** and **Row 4 (yaw)** because the servo tilt changes the thrust vector's **magnitude component** (vertical) and creates a **horizontal force** for yaw. The decoupling of roll and pitch from servo angle is what makes the matrix **non-square in effect**, distinguishing the tricopter from a quadcopter.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Tricopter Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $\cos\delta_r$ in the throttle equation.** Many students write $U_1 = k_f(\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2)$ ignoring the tilt. **Penalty: -1 to -2 marks.**
> 2. **Mixing up the sign convention** for CW vs. CCW motor directions. The $k_m$ coefficients in row 4 of M alternate in sign; reversing them flips the yaw control sign.
> 3. **Wrong units in $\omega$.** If $\omega$ is in RPM, the formulas $F = k_f \omega^2$ and $\tau = k_m \omega^2$ become **invalid**. Always convert to **rad/s** before applying.
> 4. **Assuming symmetric matrix** for a tricopter. Unlike a quadcopter, the M matrix is **NOT symmetric**. Saying it is symmetric will lose full credit in Part (b) derivation questions.
> 5. **Skipping the Newton-Euler step.** Examiners specifically allocate marks for the EOM derivation. Jumping directly to numerical substitution loses **3-4 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Mixing up the body vs. world frame.** The $m\ddot{x}$ equation uses world frame orientation (roll-pitch-yaw Euler angles), while $\tau_\phi, \tau_\theta, \tau_\psi$ are body-frame moments. Clearly labeling frames is mandatory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tricopter (PBRUT504 Module 1)**
> 
> ✅ **Definition:** 3-rotor UAV with a yaw-servo for heading control (4 actuators total)
> 
> ✅ **Configuration:** Y-3 (most common), 120° between front arms, rear arm with tilting motor
> 
> ✅ **Under-actuated:** 6 DOF controlled by 4 inputs
> 
> ✅ **Key Thrust Equation:** $F_i = k_f \omega_i^2$ and Torque $\tau_i = k_m \omega_i^2$
> 
> ✅ **Yaw Mechanism:** Rear rotor tilt $\delta_r$ produces horizontal force $F_3 \sin\delta_r$ at arm $L_{tail}$
> 
> ✅ **Mixing Matrix M:** 4×4, **non-symmetric**, with the 4th column non-zero in rows 1 (throttle) and 4 (yaw)
> 
> ✅ **Hover Condition:** $\omega_{hover} = \sqrt{mg/(3k_f)}$
> 
> ✅ **Newton-Euler Model:** Standard 6-DOF equations with the tricopter's specific $U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4$ mapping
> 
> ✅ **Euler Angle Convention:** Roll (φ) about X, Pitch (θ) about Y, Yaw (ψ) about Z
> 
> ✅ **Frame:** Body frame for torques; World (NED) frame for translational acceleration
> 
> ✅ **PD Controller:** $\tau_{cmd} = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ — yields 2nd-order closed-loop dynamics
> 
> ✅ **Comparison:** Tricopter = cheaper, lighter, asymmetric; Quadcopter = symmetric, easier to tune
> 
> ✅ **Failure Mode:** Servo failure → loss of yaw control → crash (critical safety issue)
> 
> ✅ **TWR:** Thrust-to-weight ratio ≥ 2:1 for safe operation
> 
> ✅ **Exam Trap:** Always use **rad/s** for $\omega$ in formulas, **not RPM**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quadcopter or Quadrotor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quadrotor (Quadcopter):** A quadrotor is a multi-rotor aerial vehicle belonging to the class of *Vertical Take-Off and Landing* (VTOL) Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). It is mechanically a rigid cross-shaped airframe actuated by **four independent fixed-pitch rotors** mounted symmetrically at the ends of two perpendicular arms. It is fundamentally classified as a **6-DOF rigid body with 4 control inputs**, making it a *Class II under-actuated mechanical system*.

The four rotors are paired into two counter-rotating sets (two ClockWise and two Counter-ClockWise) to cancel the net reactive aerodynamic torque in hover, since fixed-pitch propellers cannot vary blade pitch to vary thrust direction.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Cross-Shaped Broomstick in your Palm":**
> Imagine a cross-shaped rigid stick resting on your fingertip. To keep it balanced:
> - You can push it **UP** by applying equal force on all four ends (this is **throttle**).
> - You can tilt the **front** by spinning the front motors **faster** and the rear motors **slower** (this is **pitch**).
> - You can tilt it **sideways** by speeding up the right pair and slowing the left pair (this is **roll**).
> - You can **spin it in place** by speeding up the CW pair and slowing the CCW pair (this is **yaw**).
>
> A quadrotor continuously does this balancing act, ~400 to ~8000 times per second, using an onboard flight controller with IMU feedback.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | **6** | 3 translational + 3 rotational |
| **Number of Actuators (Inputs)** | **4** | 4 brushless DC motors |
| **Actuation Type** | Under-actuated | 6 DOF > 4 inputs |
| **Airframe Mass (m)** | 0.5 kg – 5 kg | Commercial drones |
| **Rotor Arm Length (l)** | 0.1 m – 0.4 m | Distance from CoM to rotor |
| **Hover Thrust-to-Weight Ratio** | ≈ **2 : 1** | Allows maneuvering margin |
| **Standard Earth Gravity (g)** | **9.81 m/s²** | Used in vertical dynamics |

## 1.4 Why a Quadrotor? (Engineering Motivation)

A quadrotor is preferred over a single-rotor helicopter because:
1. **Mechanical Simplicity:** No swashplate, no variable-pitch mechanism, no tail rotor.
2. **High Fault Tolerance:** Can hover and land safely even if one rotor fails (in certain configurations).
3. **Symmetry & Control Decoupling:** Symmetric geometry simplifies the derivation of the dynamic model.
4. **Low Cost, High Agility:** Ideal platform for research, agriculture, surveillance, and swarm robotics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Degrees of Freedom & Reference Frames

The quadrotor, treated as a **rigid body in 3D space**, possesses exactly **6 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**:

**Translational DOF (Position of Centre of Mass in Inertial Frame)**
- $x$ — **Surge** (forward/backward)
- $y$ — **Sway** (left/right)
- $z$ — **Heave** (up/down)

**Rotational DOF (Orientation via Euler Angles: ZYX Convention)**
- $\phi$ — **Roll** (rotation about body x-axis)
- $\theta$ — **Pitch** (rotation about body y-axis)
- $\psi$ — **Yaw** (rotation about body z-axis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Under-actuated"?** A quadrotor has 6 DOF but only 4 control inputs. Hence 2 DOF (lateral translation x and y) cannot be commanded directly — they are controlled *indirectly* by first tilting the airframe (changing $\phi$ and $\theta$) and then using the resulting horizontal thrust component.

## 2.2 The Four Control Inputs

| Input | Symbol | Effect on Quadrotor | Motors Affected |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Throttle (U₁)** | $U_1$ | Total vertical thrust | All 4 motors equally |
| **Roll (U₂)** | $U_2$ | Tilts quadrotor sideways | Right vs. Left pair |
| **Pitch (U₃)** | $U_3$ | Tilts quadrotor forward/back | Front vs. Rear pair |
| **Yaw (U₄)** | $U_4$ | Spins quadrotor in place | CW pair vs. CCW pair |

## 2.3 Aerodynamic Forces and Moments

Each rotor $i$ ($i = 1,2,3,4$) generates a **thrust force** $F_i$ and a **reaction torque** $\tau_i$, both proportional to the **square** of the rotor angular velocity $\omega_i$:

$$F_i = b \, \omega_i^2$$

$$\tau_i = d \, \omega_i^2$$

where $b$ and $d$ are the **thrust coefficient** and **drag (torque) coefficient** respectively, which are functions of air density, rotor blade geometry, and chord profile.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every KTU-exam-relevant equation** for a quadrotor. Memorise this — it covers 95% of Module 1 derivations.

| # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Single Rotor Thrust | $F_i = b \, \omega_i^2$ | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | Single Rotor Drag Torque | $\tau_i = d \, \omega_i^2$ | N·m |
| 3 | Total Thrust Input (U₁) | $U_1 = b \left(\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2 \right)$ | N (acts along +z body axis) |
| 4 | Roll Moment (U₂) — X-config | $U_2 = b \, l \left(\omega_4^2 - \omega_2^2 \right)$ | N·m, about body x-axis |
| 5 | Pitch Moment (U₃) — X-config | $U_3 = b \, l \left(\omega_3^2 - \omega_1^2 \right)$ | N·m, about body y-axis |
| 6 | Yaw Moment (U₄) — X-config | $U_4 = d \left(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_4^2 \right)$ | N·m, about body z-axis |
| 7 | Translational X-dynamics | $\ddot{x} = \frac{U_1}{m} \left( \cos\phi \sin\theta \cos\psi + \sin\phi \sin\psi \right)$ | m/s² |
| 8 | Translational Y-dynamics | $\ddot{y} = \frac{U_1}{m} \left( \cos\phi \sin\theta \sin\psi - \sin\phi \cos\psi \right)$ | m/s² |
| 9 | Translational Z-dynamics | $\ddot{z} = \frac{U_1}{m} \left( \cos\phi \cos\theta \right) - g$ | m/s², with $g = 9.81$ |
| 10 | Rotational Roll Dynamics | $\ddot{\phi} = \dot{\theta}\dot{\psi} \left( \frac{I_y - I_z}{I_x} \right) + \frac{U_2}{I_x}$ | rad/s² |
| 11 | Rotational Pitch Dynamics | $\ddot{\theta} = \dot{\phi}\dot{\psi} \left( \frac{I_z - I_x}{I_y} \right) + \frac{U_3}{I_y}$ | rad/s² |
| 12 | Rotational Yaw Dynamics | $\ddot{\psi} = \dot{\phi}\dot{\theta} \left( \frac{I_x - I_y}{I_z} \right) + \frac{U_4}{I_z}$ | rad/s² |
| 13 | State Vector (12-state) | $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi, \dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{z}, \dot{\phi}, \dot{\theta}, \dot{\psi}]^T$ | Standard for MPC & LQR |
| 14 | Inertia Matrix (Symmetric) | $I = \mathrm{diag}(I_x, I_y, I_z)$ | kg·m² |
| 15 | Power Consumption (single rotor) | $P_i = c \, \omega_i^3$ | Watts; $c$ = power coefficient |
| 16 | Hover Thrust | $T_{hover} = m g$ | N; at hover $\ddot{z} = 0$ |
| 17 | Thrust-to-Weight Ratio | $\mathrm{TWR} = \frac{b \sum \omega_i^2}{m g}$ | Must be $\geq 1$ to fly |
| 18 | Battery Endurance (approx.) | $t_{end} = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{hover}}$ | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Notation:**
> - $m$ = total mass of the quadrotor (kg)
> - $l$ = arm length — distance from Centre of Mass (CoM) to each rotor (m)
> - $I_x, I_y, I_z$ = moments of inertia about body x, y, z axes (kg·m²)
> - $b$ = thrust coefficient (N·s²)
> - $d$ = drag coefficient (N·m·s²)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The model derived above is the **backbone of every commercial flight controller** (PX4, Ardupilot, Betaflight). The control laws (PID, LQR, MPC) all require these equations to convert pilot stick commands (in 4 channels) into per-motor PWM signals (also 4 channels). The complete chain is:

> **Pilot Stick Input → Reference Trajectory → State Estimator (EKF with IMU+GPS) → Control Law (PID/LQR) → Allocation (U₁,U₂,U₃,U₄) → Mixer (ω₁²,ω₂²,ω₃²,ω₄²) → ESC PWM → Motor RPM**

This is the standard architecture you will see in industry 4.0 autonomous drone deployments (delivery, surveying, agriculture).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Newton's Second Law to Translational Dynamics

**Step 1 — Identify the Body-Frame Thrust Vector.**

The total thrust generated by all four rotors acts along the **body z-axis** (perpendicular to the rotor plane, pointing up in body frame). In the body frame, this vector is:

$$\mathbf{F}_{thrust}^{body} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Rotation Matrix from Body to Inertial Frame.**

Using the **ZYX Euler angle convention**, the rotation matrix $R$ that transforms a vector from the body frame $\{B\}$ to the inertial frame $\{I\}$ is:

$$
R(\phi,\theta,\psi) =
\begin{bmatrix}
c\theta c\psi & s\phi s\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi & c\phi s\theta c\psi + s\phi s\psi \\
c\theta s\psi & s\phi s\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi & c\phi s\theta s\psi - s\phi c\psi \\
-s\theta & s\phi c\theta & c\phi c\theta
\end{bmatrix}
$$

For brevity, $c\alpha = \cos\alpha$ and $s\alpha = \sin\alpha$.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law in the Inertial Frame.**

The translational dynamics are governed by:

$$m \ddot{\mathbf{p}} = R \cdot \mathbf{F}_{thrust}^{body} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ mg \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\mathbf{p} = [x, y, z]^T$ is the position vector of the CoM in the inertial frame, and the last term is gravity acting along $-z_I$.

**Step 4 — Expand the Matrix Multiplication.**

Multiplying $R$ with the thrust vector, only the **third column** of $R$ survives (because the thrust has only a z-component in the body frame):

$$
R \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix} = U_1 \begin{bmatrix} c\phi s\theta c\psi + s\phi s\psi \\ c\phi s\theta s\psi - s\phi c\psi \\ c\phi c\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Equate Component-wise.**

Substituting back into Newton's law and rearranging:

$$
\ddot{x} = \frac{U_1}{m} \left( c\phi \, s\theta \, c\psi + s\phi \, s\psi \right)
$$

$$
\ddot{y} = \frac{U_1}{m} \left( c\phi \, s\theta \, s\psi - s\phi \, c\psi \right)
$$

$$
\ddot{z} = \frac{U_1}{m} \left( c\phi \, c\theta \right) - g
$$

This is the **complete translational model** of a quadrotor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: From Euler's Rotational Equation to Angular Dynamics

**Step 1 — State Euler's Equation for a Rigid Body.**

For a rigid body, the rotational dynamics are given by Euler's equation:

$$I \dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (I \boldsymbol{\omega}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

where $I$ is the inertia matrix, $\boldsymbol{\omega} = [p, q, r]^T$ is the body angular velocity, and $\boldsymbol{\tau} = [U_2, U_3, U_4]^T$ is the applied torque vector.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Inertia Matrix (Diagonal Assumption).**

For a quadrotor with symmetric geometry, the inertia matrix is diagonal:

$$
I = \begin{bmatrix} I_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & I_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & I_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Cross Product Term.**

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega} \times (I \boldsymbol{\omega}) =
\begin{bmatrix}
p \\ q \\ r
\end{bmatrix}
\times
\begin{bmatrix}
I_x p \\ I_y q \\ I_z r
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
q r (I_z - I_y) \\
r p (I_x - I_z) \\
p q (I_y - I_x)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Relate Body Rates $(p,q,r)$ to Euler Rates $(\dot{\phi},\dot{\theta},\dot{\psi})$.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} p \\ q \\ r \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -s\theta \\
0 & c\phi & s\phi c\theta \\
0 & -s\phi & c\phi c\theta
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\psi} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine and Simplify (assuming small-angle / symmetric case $I_x \approx I_y$).**

Substituting and dividing by the respective moments of inertia:

$$
\ddot{\phi} = \dot{\theta} \dot{\psi} \left( \frac{I_y - I_z}{I_x} \right) + \frac{U_2}{I_x}
$$

$$
\ddot{\theta} = \dot{\phi} \dot{\psi} \left( \frac{I_z - I_x}{I_y} \right) + \frac{U_3}{I_y}
$$

$$
\ddot{\psi} = \dot{\phi} \dot{\theta} \left( \frac{I_x - I_y}{I_z} \right) + \frac{U_4}{I_z}
$$

These three equations form the **complete rotational model** of a quadrotor.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: From Motor Speeds to Body Moments (X-Configuration)

**Step 1 — Define Motor Numbering Convention.**

For the **X-configuration**, motors are numbered 1, 2, 3, 4 starting from the **front-right** rotor going **counter-clockwise**:
- Motor 1: front-right (CCW rotation)
- Motor 2: rear-right (CW rotation)
- Motor 3: rear-left (CCW rotation)
- Motor 4: front-left (CW rotation)

**Step 2 — Express Thrust from Each Motor.**

Using $F_i = b \, \omega_i^2$:

$$
F_1 = b \, \omega_1^2, \quad F_2 = b \, \omega_2^2, \quad F_3 = b \, \omega_3^2, \quad F_4 = b \, \omega_4^2
$$

**Step 3 — Sum Thrusts Along Body z-axis.**

All four thrusts point in the same direction (+z body), so:

$$
U_1 = F_1 + F_2 + F_3 + F_4 = b \left( \omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2 \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Roll Torque (about body x-axis).**

Motors 2 and 4 are on the right and left of the airframe respectively, each at distance $l$ from the CoM. The roll torque is:

$$
U_2 = l \left( F_4 - F_2 \right) = b \, l \left( \omega_4^2 - \omega_2^2 \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Pitch Torque (about body y-axis).**

Motors 1 and 3 are at the front and rear of the airframe. The pitch torque is:

$$
U_3 = l \left( F_3 - F_1 \right) = b \, l \left( \omega_3^2 - \omega_1^2 \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Yaw Torque (about body z-axis).**

Reaction torques from CCW motors (1,3) push airframe CW (negative), and CW motors (2,4) push airframe CCW (positive). Net yaw torque:

$$
U_4 = d \left( \omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_4^2 \right)
$$

This completes the **control allocation matrix**, which is the core of the *mixer* in any flight controller.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Linearised Hover Model (for PID/LQR Design)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At hover, $\phi = \theta = \psi = 0$ and $\dot{\phi} = \dot{\theta} = \dot{\psi} = 0$. The gyroscopic terms $\dot{\theta}\dot{\psi}$, $\dot{\phi}\dot{\psi}$, $\dot{\phi}\dot{\theta}$ all vanish. The trigonometric terms simplify to $\sin 0 = 0$ and $\cos 0 = 1$.

**Step 1 — Linearise the Translational Equations.**

$$
\ddot{x} \approx g \, \theta
$$

$$
\ddot{y} \approx -g \, \phi
$$

$$
\ddot{z} \approx \frac{U_1}{m} - g
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This shows beautifully why a quadrotor is under-actuated. To accelerate forward (increase $\ddot{x}$), we **cannot increase $x$-thrust directly**. We must tilt the body by **pitching forward** ($\theta > 0$), and gravity then converts part of the vertical thrust into horizontal motion.

**Step 2 — Linearise the Rotational Equations.**

$$
\ddot{\phi} \approx \frac{U_2}{I_x}, \qquad \ddot{\theta} \approx \frac{U_3}{I_y}, \qquad \ddot{\psi} \approx \frac{U_4}{I_z}
$$

**Step 3 — State-Space Form for Altitude Channel.**

Define altitude state $z_1 = z$, $z_2 = \dot{z}$, and control $u_z = U_1$. The decoupled altitude subsystem is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{z}_1 \\ \dot{z}_2 \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} z_1 \\ z_2 \end{bmatrix} +
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{m} \end{bmatrix} u_z +
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -g \end{bmatrix}
$$

This canonical **double-integrator form** is why classical PID works so well for quadrotor altitude control.

---

# SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 X-Configuration Motor Numbering (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Airframe[QUADROTOR AIRFRAME - X CONFIGURATION]
        direction TB
        subgraph FRight[FRONT RIGHT]
            M1Block[BLOCK M1\nCCW ROTOR\nFORCE F1 = b w1 squared]
        end
        subgraph RRight[REAR RIGHT]
            M2Block[BLOCK M2\nCW ROTOR\nFORCE F2 = b w2 squared]
        end
        subgraph RLeft[REAR LEFT]
            M3Block[BLOCK M3\nCCW ROTOR\nFORCE F3 = b w3 squared]
        end
        subgraph FLeft[FRONT LEFT]
            M4Block[BLOCK M4\nCW ROTOR\nFORCE F4 = b w4 squared]
        end
        CoM[(BLOCK CENTRE OF MASS\nMASS m\nINERTIA Ix Iy Iz)]
        M1Block --ARM LENGTH l-- CoM
        M2Block --ARM LENGTH l-- CoM
        M3Block --ARM LENGTH l-- CoM
        M4Block --ARM LENGTH l-- CoM
    end

    subgraph Inputs[BLOCK CONTROL INPUTS]
        U1[BLOCK U1 TOTAL THRUST]
        U2[BLOCK U2 ROLL]
        U3[BLOCK U3 PITCH]
        U4[BLOCK U4 YAW]
    end

    subgraph Mixer[BLOCK CONTROL ALLOCATION MIXER]
        Alloc1[BLOCK W1 squared plus W2 squared plus W3 squared plus W4 squared times b]
        Alloc2[BLOCK W4 squared minus W2 squared times b times l]
        Alloc3[BLOCK W3 squared minus W1 squared times b times l]
        Alloc4[BLOCK W1 squared minus W2 squared plus W3 squared minus W4 squared times d]
    end

    U1 --> Alloc1
    U2 --> Alloc2
    U3 --> Alloc3
    U4 --> Alloc4
```

## 4.2 Complete Quadrotor Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RefGen[BLOCK REFERENCE GENERATOR]
        A[BLOCK PILOT COMMAND\nDESIRED TRAJECTORY Xd Yd Zd PHId THETAd PSId]
    end

    subgraph Estim[BLOCK STATE ESTIMATOR EKF]
        B[BLOCK FUSION OF IMU GPS BAROMETER\nPOSITION VELOCITY ATTITUDE]
    end

    subgraph Plant[BLOCK QUADROTOR DYNAMICS]
        C[BLOCK 6 DOF RIGID BODY\nF = m a\nTAU = I ALPHA plus OMEGA CROSS I OMEGA]
    end

    subgraph Mixer[BLOCK MIXER]
        D[BLOCK CONVERT U1 U2 U3 U4\nTO W1 W2 W3 W4]
    end

    subgraph MotorSys[BLOCK MOTOR ACTUATION]
        E[BLOCK ESC PLUS BRUSHLESS DC\nFOUR ROTORS]
    end

    A --DESIRED STATE--> F[BLOCK CONTROL LAW\nPID OR LQR OR MPC]
    B --MEASURED STATE--> F
    F --CONTROL VECTOR U1 U2 U3 U4--> Mixer
    Mixer --> D
    D --> E
    E --> Plant
    Plant --ACTUAL FLIGHT--> B
    Plant --SENSORS IMU GPS--> B
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of Decoupled Control Channels

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InnerLoop[BLOCK INNER LOOP HIGH RATE 1 KHZ]
        RollLoop[BLOCK ROLL CONTROLLER\nPD GAINS Kp phi Kd phi]
        PitchLoop[BLOCK PITCH CONTROLLER\nPD GAINS Kp theta Kd theta]
        YawLoop[BLOCK YAW CONTROLLER\nPD GAINS Kp psi Kd psi]
    end

    subgraph OuterLoop[BLOCK OUTER LOOP LOWER RATE 100 HZ]
        AltLoop[BLOCK ALTITUDE CONTROLLER\nPID GAINS Kp z Ki z Kd z]
        PosXLoop[BLOCK X POSITION CONTROLLER\nP GAIN Kp x]
        PosYLoop[BLOCK Y POSITION CONTROLLER\nP GAIN Kp y]
    end

    subgraph Outputs[BLOCK OUTPUTS]
        Out1[BLOCK U2 ROLL TORQUE]
        Out2[BLOCK U3 PITCH TORQUE]
        Out3[BLOCK U4 YAW TORQUE]
        Out4[BLOCK U1 TOTAL THRUST]
    end

    PosXLoop --> PitchLoop
    PosYLoop --> RollLoop
    AltLoop --> Out4
    RollLoop --> Out1
    PitchLoop --> Out2
    YawLoop --> Out3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Architecture (KTU Valuation Insight):**
> - The **inner loop** (attitude) runs at ~1 kHz on the flight controller and uses **PD** control.
> - The **outer loop** (position) runs at ~100 Hz and uses **P** or **PID** control.
> - This **cascaded structure** (position → attitude → motor RPM) is the **universal standard** for quadrotor control, and you are expected to draw and label it in the KTU ESE.

# SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of under-actuation in a quadrotor. Why is a quadrotor classified as an under-actuated system?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Total ~60 words):**

A quadrotor has **6 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — three translational $(x, y, z)$ and three rotational $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ — but possesses only **4 independent control inputs** $(U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4)$, corresponding to total thrust, roll, pitch, and yaw torques.

Since **$6 > 4$**, the system is under-actuated. This means the **lateral translations** $x$ and $y$ cannot be commanded directly. They are controlled **indirectly** by first tilting the airframe (changing $\phi$ or $\theta$) to convert a portion of the vertical thrust into horizontal motion.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark: Definition of DOF vs. control inputs.
- 1 mark: Statement that quadrotor has 6 DOF and 4 inputs.
- 1 mark: Consequence — lateral motion is achieved via tilting.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between X-configuration and +-configuration of a quadrotor. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Total ~70 words):**

| Feature | X-Configuration | +-Configuration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Motor Placement | At the four *diagonal* corners of an X | Aligned with body axes (+x, −x, +y, −y) |
| Symmetry | Motors 1,3 and 2,4 on body diagonals | Motors on body x and y axes |
| Control Coupling | Roll/Pitch act on diagonal pairs | Roll/Pitch act on aligned pairs |
| **Advantage** | Higher stability, larger effective control arm, more payload capacity | **Simpler control allocation** and easier mathematical modeling for beginners |

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark: X-configuration description + advantage.
- 1 mark: +-configuration description + advantage.
- 1 mark: Clear tabular comparison or distinguishing statement.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from Module 1. Each full question carries 14 marks and contains sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a quadrotor UAV. Discuss the role of fixed-pitch rotors and counter-rotation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the complete dynamic model of a quadrotor in 3D space, clearly stating all assumptions, reference frames, and the rotation matrix. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A quadrotor is a multi-rotor UAV with **four fixed-pitch rotors** symmetrically mounted at the ends of two perpendicular arms, forming a cross or X-shape. It is a Vertical Take-Off and Landing (VTOL) vehicle that achieves all motion through **variation of rotor speeds alone**.

**Construction (3 Marks):**
- **Frame:** Lightweight carbon-fibre or aluminium cross-shaped structure.
- **Rotors & Motors:** Four brushless DC motors with fixed-pitch propellers.
  - Motors 1 & 3 rotate **Counter-ClockWise (CCW)**.
  - Motors 2 & 4 rotate **ClockWise (CW)**.
- **Sensors:** IMU (3-axis gyroscope + 3-axis accelerometer), magnetometer, GPS, barometer.
- **Flight Controller:** Microcontroller (STM32 / Pixhawk) running control algorithms at 1 kHz.
- **Power:** Lithium-Polymer (LiPo) battery + 4 Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs).

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
1. **Hover:** All four motors spin at equal speed. Total upward thrust equals weight ($U_1 = mg$).
2. **Vertical Motion (Throttle):** Increase/decrease all four motor speeds equally → climb/descend.
3. **Roll:** Increase motors 1,3 and decrease motors 2,4 → tilts about body x-axis.
4. **Pitch:** Increase motors 2,3 and decrease motors 1,4 → tilts about body y-axis.
5. **Yaw:** Increase CCW pair (1,3) and decrease CW pair (2,4) → spins about body z-axis due to imbalance in reaction torques.

**Role of Counter-Rotation (1 Mark):**
Without counter-rotation, the airframe would **continuously spin** in the opposite direction to the rotors (Newton's Third Law — conservation of angular momentum). By pairing CW and CCW rotors, the net reactive torque cancels out, allowing stable controlled yaw via *differential* torque.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
1. The quadrotor is a **rigid body** with constant mass $m$ and symmetric inertia matrix.
2. The structure is **symmetric** ($I_{xy} = I_{yz} = I_{xz} = 0$).
3. Thrust and drag are proportional to the **square** of rotor speed.
4. The Earth frame is **inertial** (negligible rotational effects).
5. The ZYX Euler angle convention is used.

**Reference Frames (1 Mark):**
- **Inertial Frame $\{I\}$:** Fixed to Earth, axes $(x_I, y_I, z_I)$ with $z_I$ pointing up.
- **Body Frame $\{B\}$:** Fixed to the quadrotor, origin at CoM, axes $(x_B, y_B, z_B)$ aligned with the airframe.

**Rotation Matrix from Body to Inertial (2 Marks):**

$$
R(\phi,\theta,\psi) =
\begin{bmatrix}
c\theta c\psi & s\phi s\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi & c\phi s\theta c\psi + s\phi s\psi \\
c\theta s\psi & s\phi s\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi & c\phi s\theta s\psi - s\phi c\psi \\
-s\theta & s\phi c\theta & c\phi c\theta
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Translational Dynamics (2 Marks):**

Applying Newton's Second Law in the inertial frame, with the body thrust vector $\mathbf{F}_{thrust}^B = [0, 0, U_1]^T$:

$$
m\ddot{x} = U_1 \left( c\phi s\theta c\psi + s\phi s\psi \right)
$$

$$
m\ddot{y} = U_1 \left( c\phi s\theta s\psi - s\phi c\psi \right)
$$

$$
m\ddot{z} = U_1 \left( c\phi c\theta \right) - mg
$$

**Rotational Dynamics (1 Mark):**

Applying Euler's rotational equation $I\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (I\boldsymbol{\omega}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$:

$$
I_x \ddot{\phi} = \dot{\theta}\dot{\psi}(I_y - I_z) + U_2
$$

$$
I_y \ddot{\theta} = \dot{\phi}\dot{\psi}(I_z - I_x) + U_3
$$

$$
I_z \ddot{\psi} = \dot{\phi}\dot{\theta}(I_x - I_y) + U_4
$$

This constitutes the **complete 6-DOF dynamic model** of the quadrotor.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With reference to a quadrotor, define the following terms clearly: (i) Thrust, (ii) Roll, (iii) Pitch, (iv) Yaw, (v) Throttle. **(3 Marks)**

**(b)** A quadrotor with arm length $l = 0.25$ m and mass $m = 1.5$ kg is hovering in steady state. Each rotor produces a thrust of $F = 4$ N. Calculate the total thrust, the thrust-to-weight ratio, and the vertical acceleration if the pilot suddenly increases the thrust of each motor by 20%. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and thrust coefficient $b = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s². **(4 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

| Term | Definition (KTU Standard) |
| :--- | :--- |
| (i) **Thrust** | The total upward force produced by all four rotors, given by $U_1 = b(\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2)$. It acts along the body z-axis. |
| (ii) **Roll** | Rotational motion of the quadrotor about the body x-axis, denoted by angle $\phi$. Achieved by differential thrust between left and right motor pairs. |
| (iii) **Pitch** | Rotational motion about the body y-axis, denoted by angle $\theta$. Achieved by differential thrust between front and rear motor pairs. |
| (iv) **Yaw** | Rotational motion about the body z-axis, denoted by angle $\psi$. Achieved by differential reaction torque between CW and CCW rotor pairs. |
| (v) **Throttle** | The pilot input command corresponding to $U_1$, which commands the total collective thrust. |

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for table format + 5 correct definitions at 0.4 each (or 1 mark for any 3, 2 marks for all 5).

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Step 1 — Total Thrust at Hover (1 Mark):**

$$
U_1 = F_1 + F_2 + F_3 + F_4 = 4 \times 4 \text{ N} = 16 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{TWR} = \frac{U_1}{m g} = \frac{16}{1.5 \times 9.81} = \frac{16}{14.715} \approx 1.087
$$

**Step 3 — New Thrust after 20% Increase (1 Mark):**

New thrust per motor $= 1.2 \times 4 = 4.8$ N.
New total thrust $U_1' = 4 \times 4.8 = 19.2$ N.

**Step 4 — Net Vertical Force and Acceleration (1 Mark):**

$$
F_{net} = U_1' - m g = 19.2 - 14.715 = 4.485 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\ddot{z} = \frac{F_{net}}{m} = \frac{4.485}{1.5} = 2.99 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 3 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Conclusion:** The quadrotor accelerates vertically upward at approximately $3 \text{ m/s}^2$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Sign errors in the rotation matrix** — the ZYX convention is strict; swapping row/column order costs the entire 4-mark derivation.
> 2. **Forgetting the $-mg$ gravity term** in the $\ddot{z}$ equation. Many students write $\ddot{z} = (U_1/m) \cos\phi \cos\theta$ and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up the gyroscopic terms** $\dot{\theta}\dot{\psi}(I_y - I_z)$ — the subscripts follow a cyclic pattern; mismatching them loses full marks for rotational dynamics.
> 4. **Drawing a "+" configuration but calling it X-configuration**, or vice-versa. Always label motors 1, 2, 3, 4 explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems (always write N, N·m, m/s², rad/s², kg·m²).
> 6. **Confusing $b$ (thrust coefficient) with $d$ (drag coefficient)** — $b$ has units of N·s² and $d$ has units of N·m·s².

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 : Quadrotor**

- [x] A quadrotor is a **6-DOF, 4-input, under-actuated** VTOL UAV.
- [x] It uses **four fixed-pitch rotors** — no swashplate.
- [x] **Two CCW and two CW rotors** are used to cancel reactive torque.
- [x] Control is achieved solely by **varying motor speeds** (RPMs).
- [x] The four inputs are: **$U_1$ (throttle/thrust), $U_2$ (roll), $U_3$ (pitch), $U_4$ (yaw)**.
- [x] Thrust equation: $F_i = b \omega_i^2$ — quadratic in rotor speed.
- [x] Drag torque: $\tau_i = d \omega_i^2$ — also quadratic in rotor speed.
- [x] **X-configuration is preferred** for stability; +-configuration is simpler for theory.
- [x] The **rotation matrix $R(\phi,\theta,\psi)$** is in the **ZYX Euler convention** — must be memorised in full.
- [x] Translational dynamics have **no Coriolis term**; rotational dynamics have **gyroscopic coupling terms** $\dot{\theta}\dot{\psi}(I_y - I_z)$.
- [x] **Linearised hover model** simplifies to $\ddot{x} \approx g\theta$ and $\ddot{y} \approx -g\phi$ — this is the foundation of under-actuation.
- [x] The 12-state vector for full state-space control is: $[x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi, \dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{z}, \dot{\phi}, \dot{\theta}, \dot{\psi}]^T$.
- [x] A typical control architecture is **cascaded**: outer position loop (P) → inner attitude loop (PD) → mixer → motors.
- [x] At hover, **TWR ≥ 1**; for agile manoeuvring, **TWR ≈ 2**.
- [x] Standard earth gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is always used unless otherwise stated.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hexacopter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hexacopter # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Hexacopter** (also written as **Hex-rotor UAV** or **6-DOF Multirotor**) is a rotary-wing Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) classified under the *Multi-Rotor Aerial Mobile Robot* family. It is a holonomic, under-actuated mechanical system equipped with **six (6) fixed-pitch brushless DC (BLDC) motors** symmetrically mounted on a rigid airframe. Each motor drives a two-bladed propeller (rotor), producing aerodynamic thrust (Tᵢ) and reactive torque (Qᵢ) along its respective rotation axis. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** > A hexacopter is a six-rotor Vertical Take-Off and Landing (VTOL) mobile robot whose state is described by 6 Degrees of Freedom (3 translational: x, y, z; and 3 rotational: φ (roll), θ (pitch), ψ (yaw)) but actuated by only 4 independent control inputs (Total Thrust, Roll, Pitch, Yaw), making it an **under-actuated system** with **6th-degree redundancy** compared to a quadcopter. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Six-Pedal Hover-Bicycle":** > Imagine you are sitting on an invisible bicycle floating in mid-air. Instead of two wheels, you have **6 spinning fans** mounted on rigid arms. To climb up, you spin all 6 fans faster (more thrust). To move forward, you slow down the **back two fans** and speed up the **front two fans**, tilting the entire frame forward — gravity then pulls you forward. To spin (yaw), you make 3 fans spin clockwise slightly faster than the other 3 counter-clockwise fans. The hexacopter is essentially a **6-legged mechanical insect** that obeys Newton's 2nd & 3rd Laws. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards | Parameter | Standard Metric | | :--- | :--- | | **Number of Rotors (n)** | **6 (Hex = 6)** | | **Default Frame Size (motor-to-motor)** | **550 mm** (5-inch class) to **960 mm** (heavy-lift) | | **Standard Air Density (ρ)** | **1.225 kg/m³** (ISA Sea Level) | | **Propeller Pair Count** | **3 CW + 3 CCW** (for net-zero reactive torque) | | **Standard Take-Off Weight (TOW)** | **1.5 kg – 15 kg** (commercial/research) | | **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | **6 (3 Translation + 3 Rotation)** | | **Independent Control Inputs** | **4 (Total Thrust U₁, Roll U₂, Pitch U₃, Yaw U₄)** | | **Actuator Redundancy** | **6 rotors for 4 inputs = 2 redundant actuators** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Hexa" and not "Six"?** The KTU 2024 syllabus uses the Greek root *hexa* (ἕξ = six) because in aerial robotics nomenclature, multirotors follow the Greek numerical system: **Tri (3) → Quad (4) → Hexa (6) → Octa (8)**. The configuration with 6 rotors offers an excellent balance between mechanical simplicity and **fault tolerance** — the aircraft can continue controlled flight even after the loss of **one (sometimes two)** rotor(s), a property quadcopters lack. ## 1.4 Hexacopter vs. Quadcopter vs. Octocopter — Quick Comparison | Feature | Quadcopter | **Hexacopter** | Octocopter | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Rotor Count | 4 | **6** | 8 | | Payload Capacity | Low | **Medium-High** | Very High | | Fault Tolerance | 0 (any loss = crash) | **1 (often 2) rotor loss** | 1–2 rotor loss | | Mechanical Complexity | Low | **Medium** | High | | Power Efficiency | High | **Medium** | Low | | Cost | Low | **Medium** | High | | Common Use | Hobby, photography | **Survey, agriculture, research** | Cinema, heavy-lift | --- # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Rotor Configurations (KTU High-Yield Topic) There are **two** principal mechanical arrangements of the six rotors on the central hub: 1. **Hex-X (or X6):** The 6 rotors are placed at the vertices of a **regular hexagon**. The front of the aircraft is aligned along the X-axis (between rotors 1 and 4). This is the most common configuration. 2. **Hex-+ (or +6):** The 6 rotors are placed at the vertices of a hexagon, with the front aligned along the line through a single rotor. Less common in practice. 3. **Y6 (Coaxial Hexacopter):** 6 rotors arranged in 3 coaxial pairs (12 motors worth of thrust in a 6-arm frame). Used in heavy-lift applications. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Hex-X Motor Numbering Convention (KTU Standard):** > Motors 1, 3, 5 rotate **Counter-Clockwise (CCW)**. Motors 2, 4, 6 rotate **Clockwise (CW)**. Adjacent motors always rotate in **opposite** directions to cancel reactive torque (Newton's 3rd Law). ## 2.2 Aerodynamic Thrust & Torque Generation Each rotor is a thin-disk actuator that converts electrical power into aerodynamic force. For a single rotor $i$ spinning at angular velocity $\omega_i$ (in rad/s): **Step 1 — Thrust Force produced by rotor $i$ (along its rotation axis, +Z body frame):** $$T_i = b \cdot \omega_i^2$$ where $b$ is the **thrust coefficient** (N·s²/rad²), a function of air density $\rho$, propeller disk area $A$, blade chord, and pitch angle. **Step 2 — Reactive Drag Torque produced by rotor $i$ (about its own shaft):** $$Q_i = d \cdot \omega_i^2$$ where $d$ is the **drag coefficient** (N·m·s²/rad²). **Step 3 — Physical Relationship between $b$ and $d$:** $$\frac{b}{d} \approx \frac{C_T}{C_D} \cdot \frac{1}{R}$$ where $R$ is the propeller radius. This ratio is what engineers tune when selecting propellers. ## 2.3 The Four Control Inputs (Allocation of 6 Motors → 4 Commands) The 4 control inputs $U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4$ are obtained by mixing the 6 individual rotor commands: **U₁ — Total Vertical Thrust (climb/hover/descend):** $$U_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{6} T_i = b \cdot (\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2 + \omega_5^2 + \omega_6^2)$$ **U₂ — Roll Torque (rotation about X-axis, banks left/right):** $$U_2 = b \cdot L \cdot \left(-\omega_2^2 + \omega_5^2 + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_1^2 - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_4^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_3^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_6^2\right)$$ **U₃ — Pitch Torque (rotation about Y-axis, tilts forward/back):** $$U_3 = b \cdot L \cdot \left(\omega_1^2 - \omega_4^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_2^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_5^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_3^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\omega_6^2\right)$$ **U₄ — Yaw Torque (rotation about Z-axis, heading change):** $$U_4 = d \cdot \left(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_4^2 + \omega_5^2 - \omega_6^2\right)$$ where $L$ is the **arm length** (distance from center of mass to each rotor). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Scheme exam-ready and frequently appear in Part A and Part B questions.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $T_i = b \cdot \omega_i^2$ | Single rotor thrust | Newtons (N) | | 2 | $Q_i = d \cdot \omega_i^2$ | Single rotor reactive torque | N·m | | 3 | $U_1 = b \sum_{i=1}^{6} \omega_i^2$ | Total thrust (climb) | N | | 4 | $U_4 = d \sum_{i=1}^{6} (-1)^{i+1} \omega_i^2$ | Yaw torque (CW vs CCW split) | N·m | | 5 | $F_{\text{hover}} = m \cdot g$ | Hover condition: total thrust equals weight | N | | 6 | $\omega_{\text{hover}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m g}{6 b}}$ | Per-rotor speed required to hover | rad/s | | 7 | $P_{\text{hover}} = 6 \cdot b \cdot \omega_{\text{hover}}^3$ | Total hover power | Watts | | 8 | $M = \begin{bmatrix} m I_{3 \times 3} & 0 \\ 0 & I \end{bmatrix}$ | Mass/Inertia matrix (6×6) | kg, kg·m² | | 9 | $\dot{\xi} = R \cdot v_b$ | Translational kinematics (world ← body) | m/s | | 10 | $\dot{\eta} = W^{-1} \cdot \omega_b$ | Rotational kinematics (Euler) | rad/s | | 11 | $\tau_i = I_i \cdot \dot{\omega_i} + \omega_i \times (I_i \cdot \omega_i)$ | Newton-Euler for rotating arm | N·m | | 12 | $\dot{P}_{\text{batt}} = - \sum I_{\text{motor}} \cdot V_{\text{motor}}$ | Battery discharge equation | W | > [!NOTE] > **Substitution Rule (for altitude/hover problems):** In KTU exam problems, when the hexacopter is in **steady hover**, set $U_1 = mg$ and $U_2 = U_3 = U_4 = 0$, then solve the **linear system** of 4 equations in 6 unknowns to find the individual rotor speeds. Because the system is **over-determined (redundant)**, the pseudo-inverse allocation matrix $A^+$ is used in practice. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Where is the Hexacopter used in Industry?** > 1. **Precision Agriculture** — Spraying pesticides over 50-acre fields (DJI Agras T30 is a hexacopter). > 2. **Search & Rescue (SAR)** — Thermal-imaging over disaster zones where one rotor failure must not abort the mission. > 3. **Aerial Surveying & LiDAR Mapping** — Carrying 2–5 kg of heavy sensors. > 4. **Industrial Inspection** — Power lines, wind turbines, bridges. > 5. **Military ISR** (Intelligence, Surveillance, Reconnaissance) — Redundant propulsion is a safety requirement. > 6. **KTU Research Platform** — Most KTU Kerala robotics labs use the **F450/550 hexacopter frame with Pixhawk flight controller** as a teaching platform for Mobile Robotics (PBRUT504). --- # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hexacopter Allocation Matrix (Full Derivation) The allocation matrix converts the 4 desired virtual control inputs $(U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4)^T$ into the 6 individual rotor speeds squared $(\omega_1^2, \ldots, \omega_6^2)^T$. In matrix form, using the standard Hex-X geometry (motor angles: 30°, 90°, 150°, 210°, 270°, 330°): **Step 1 — Define the geometry.** Place 6 motors on a circle of radius $L$. Motor $i$ is at angle $\alpha_i = (i-1) \cdot 60°$ from the front (X-axis). The horizontal position of motor $i$ in the body frame is: $$\mathbf{p}_i = \begin{bmatrix} L \cos \alpha_i \\ L \sin \alpha_i \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 — Write the moment arm cross product.** The moment (torque) produced by thrust $T_i$ about the body center of mass is: $$\mathbf{M}_i = \mathbf{p}_i \times T_i \hat{\mathbf{z}} = T_i \begin{bmatrix} -L \sin \alpha_i \\ L \cos \alpha_i \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3 — Sum over all 6 rotors (roll and pitch contributions).** Roll torque $U_2$ is the X-component of the moment vector, and pitch torque $U_3$ is the Y-component: $$U_2 = b \sum_{i=1}^{6} -L \sin \alpha_i \cdot \omega_i^2$$ $$U_3 = b \sum_{i=1}^{6} L \cos \alpha_i \cdot \omega_i^2$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the 6 angles** $(\alpha_1 = 0°, \alpha_2 = 60°, \alpha_3 = 120°, \alpha_4 = 180°, \alpha_5 = 240°, \alpha_6 = 300°)$: $$\begin{aligned} U_2 &= bL \left( 0 \cdot \omega_1^2 - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_2^2 - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_3^2 + 0 \cdot \omega_4^2 + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_5^2 + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_6^2 \right) \\ &= bL \left( -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_2^2 - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_3^2 + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_5^2 + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\omega_6^2 \right) \\ U_3 &= bL \left( L \cdot \omega_1^2 + \tfrac{L}{2}\omega_2^2 - \tfrac{L}{2}\omega_3^2 - L \cdot \omega_4^2 - \tfrac{L}{2}\omega_5^2 + \tfrac{L}{2}\omega_6^2 \right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Assemble the full 4×6 allocation matrix $A$** such that $\mathbf{U} = A \cdot \boldsymbol{\Omega}$, where $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = (\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \omega_3^2, \omega_4^2, \omega_5^2, \omega_6^2)^T$: $$A = \begin{bmatrix} b & b & b & b & b & b \\ 0 & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} bL & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} bL & 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} bL & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} bL \\ bL & \tfrac{1}{2} bL & -\tfrac{1}{2} bL & -bL & -\tfrac{1}{2} bL & \tfrac{1}{2} bL \\ d & -d & d & -d & d & -d \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 6 — Solve for individual rotor speeds using Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse:** $$\boldsymbol{\Omega} = A^{+} \mathbf{U} = A^{T} (A A^{T})^{-1} \mathbf{U}$$ This gives the 6 individual squared rotor speeds, which are then square-rooted to obtain $\omega_i$. This is the equation implemented inside the Pixhawk / ArduPilot flight controller at **400 Hz**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hover Condition & Per-Rotor Thrust **Given:** A hexacopter of total mass $m$ in **steady-level hover** (no translation, no rotation, no acceleration). **Step 1 — Apply Newton's 2nd Law in the vertical (Z) direction:** $$\sum F_z = m \ddot{z} = 0 \quad \text{(steady hover)}$$ **Step 2 — Sum the thrust forces (upward positive):** $$U_1 - m g = 0 \implies U_1 = m g$$ **Step 3 — Express $U_1$ in terms of the 6 rotors:** $$b (\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2 + \omega_5^2 + \omega_6^2) = m g$$ **Step 4 — Assume symmetric hover (all 6 rotors spin at the same speed $\omega_h$):** $$6 b \omega_h^2 = m g$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the hover rotor speed:** $$\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{m g}{6 b}}$$ **Step 6 — Solve for the hover thrust per rotor:** $$T_h = b \omega_h^2 = \frac{m g}{6}$$ This is the **per-rotor lift requirement**. A 6 kg hexacopter needs only 1 kg of thrust per rotor at hover, but flight controllers typically command **30%–50% throttle margin** to maintain agility. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Translational Equations of Motion (Newton-Euler) **Step 1 — Define the world frame $E = \{O_E, \vec{x}_E, \vec{y}_E, \vec{z}_E\}$** and **body frame $B = \{O_B, \vec{x}_B, \vec{y}_B, \vec{z}_B\}$** fixed at the center of mass. **Step 2 — Express the position of the CG in the world frame:** $$\boldsymbol{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}, \quad \dot{\boldsymbol{\xi}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{z} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3 — Express orientation using ZYX Euler angles:** $$\boldsymbol{\eta} = \begin{bmatrix} \phi \\ \theta \\ \psi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{roll} \\ \text{pitch} \\ \text{yaw} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the rotation matrix $R$ from body to world (using $\phi \to \theta \to \psi$ ZYX convention):** $$R = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi c\theta & c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi \\ s\psi c\theta & s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix}$$ where $c\alpha = \cos\alpha$ and $s\alpha = \sin\alpha$. **Step 5 — Apply Newton's 2nd Law in the body frame** (translation): $$m \dot{\mathbf{v}}_b + \mathbf{\omega}_b \times (m \mathbf{v}_b) = \mathbf{F}_b$$ **Step 6 — Decompose forces** (thrust acts along body Z, gravity acts along world -Z): $$m \dot{\mathbf{v}}_b = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ U_1 \end{bmatrix} - m g R^{T} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} - \mathbf{\omega}_b \times m \mathbf{v}_b$$ **Step 7 — Write the three scalar equations in world frame:** $$\begin{aligned} \ddot{x} &= \frac{U_1}{m} (c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi) \\ \ddot{y} &= \frac{U_1}{m} (s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi) \\ \ddot{z} &= \frac{U_1}{m} (c\theta c\phi) - g \end{aligned}$$ These three equations govern the **translational dynamics** of the hexacopter. Notice that $U_1$ and $\phi, \theta$ together determine the position — this is why a hexacopter is **under-actuated** (you cannot directly command $x$ motion; you must first tilt, then thrust). ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Rotational Equations of Motion **Step 1 — Apply Euler's rotational equation** in the body frame: $$I \dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_b + \boldsymbol{\omega}_b \times (I \boldsymbol{\omega}_b) = \boldsymbol{\tau}_b$$ where $I$ is the **inertia matrix** (3×3, symmetric): $$I = \begin{bmatrix} I_{xx} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & I_{yy} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & I_{zz} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(diagonal for symmetric hexacopter)}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the gyroscopic term** $\boldsymbol{\omega}_b \times I \boldsymbol{\omega}_b$: $$\begin{aligned} I_{xx} \dot{p} &= (I_{yy} - I_{zz}) q r + U_2 \\ I_{yy} \dot{q} &= (I_{zz} - I_{xx}) p r + U_3 \\ I_{zz} \dot{r} &= (I_{xx} - I_{yy}) p q + U_4 \end{aligned}$$ where $p, q, r$ are the body-frame angular rates (roll-rate, pitch-rate, yaw-rate). **Step 3 — Use the kinematic relation** $\dot{\boldsymbol{\eta}} = W(\boldsymbol{\eta}) \boldsymbol{\omega}_b$: $$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\psi} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & s\phi t\theta & c\phi t\theta \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ 0 & s\phi / c\theta & c\phi / c\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p \\ q \\ r \end{bmatrix}$$ where $t\theta = \tan\theta$. This is **singular at $\theta = \pm 90°$**, which is why hexacopters are restricted to small pitch angles during normal flight (typical max $\theta \approx 30°$). > [!NOTE] > **Summary of the 6-DOF Hexacopter Model:** > The complete nonlinear model consists of 6 second-order ODEs (3 translation + 3 rotation) = **12 state variables**: $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi, \dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{z}, p, q, r)$. The flight controller linearizes these around the hover equilibrium and uses **PD / PID control** on each axis. --- # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hex-X Hexacopter — Top-View Motor Layout (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph CentralHub["Central Avionics Bay FC + IMU + GPS + Battery"] FC["Flight Controller Pixhawk"] end subgraph Arms["6 Carbon-Fiber Arms at 60 degree spacing"] M1["Motor 1 Front-Left

The matrix $A_5$ is obtained by deleting the column of the failed rotor from $A$ and re-computing the pseudo-inverse. --- # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > Define a **hexacopter**. List any **two advantages** of a hexacopter over a quadcopter for mobile robotics applications. **Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key):** *(1 mark)* A hexacopter is a six-rotor VTOL unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) that uses six fixed-pitch propellers mounted on a rigid frame to generate lift and control forces. It is an under-actuated 6-DOF mobile robot with 4 independent control inputs (total thrust, roll, pitch, yaw). *(2 marks)* Advantages over a quadcopter: 1. **Fault tolerance / redundancy** — Can continue controlled flight after the failure of one (or in some configurations, two) rotor(s); a quadcopter crashes immediately on any single rotor failure. (1 mark) 2. **Higher payload capacity** — Distributed thrust across 6 motors (instead of 4) allows it to carry heavier sensors (LiDAR, thermal cameras) — typically 2× to 3× the payload of an equivalent-sized quadcopter. (1 mark) --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > State the **thrust equation** $T_i = b \omega_i^2$ for a single rotor of a hexacopter. What does the coefficient $b$ physically represent? **Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key):** *(2 marks)* The thrust $T_i$ generated by rotor $i$ spinning at angular velocity $\omega_i$ is given by: $$T_i = b \cdot \omega_i^2$$ where $b$ is the **thrust coefficient** (in units of N·s²/rad²). *(1 mark)* The coefficient $b$ physically represents the **aerodynamic lift capability of the propeller**, which depends on air density $\rho$, the propeller disk area $A = \pi R^2$ (where $R$ is the propeller radius), the blade pitch angle, the airfoil's lift coefficient, and the number of blades. It is a constant for a given propeller–airframe combination. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Important (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Part B questions carry an **Internal Choice**. The two choices are *completely independent*; you should attempt only the one for which you are best prepared. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Hexacopter Modeling & Control Allocation** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** > **(a)** Draw the block diagram of a hexacopter control system and explain the function of each block. (7 marks) > **(b)** Derive the **complete allocation matrix $A$ (4×6)** for a Hex-X hexacopter with arm length $L$ and motor angles 0°, 60°, 120°, 180°, 240°, 300°. Hence, show how the four control inputs $(U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4)$ are obtained from the six rotor speeds. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram of Hexacopter Control System (7 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.3** above. The student must draw the cascade control architecture: 1. **Trajectory Generator** — produces desired $(x_d, y_d, z_d, \psi_d)$ — 1 mark 2. **Outer Position Controller (PID)** — converts position error to desired roll/pitch/thrust — 1 mark 3. **Inner Attitude Controller (PD)** — converts attitude error to desired torques $U_2, U_3, U_4$ — 1 mark 4. **Control Allocation Block** — uses allocation matrix $A$ to convert virtual inputs to 6 rotor commands — 2 marks 5. **Plant (Hexacopter Dynamics)** — 6-DOF Newton-Euler, with feedback from IMU and GPS — 1 mark 6. **Sensor Feedback (IMU + GPS + Barometer) and EKF Estimator** — 1 mark **(b) Derivation of the 4×6 Allocation Matrix (7 marks):** Step 1 (1 mark): Define motor positions. Motor $i$ at angle $\alpha_i = (i-1) \cdot 60°$ at distance $L$ from CG. So position vectors are: $$\mathbf{p}_i = L[\cos\alpha_i, \sin\alpha_i, 0]^T$$ Step 2 (1 mark): Compute thrust vector. The thrust on rotor $i$ is $T_i \hat{\mathbf{z}}_B = [0, 0, b\omega_i^2]^T$. Step 3 (1 mark): Compute moment arm. The torque about CG is: $$\mathbf{M}_i = \mathbf{p}_i \times T_i \hat{\mathbf{z}}_B = b\omega_i^2 L[-\sin\alpha_i, \cos\alpha_i, 0]^T$$ Step 4 (2 marks): Sum over all 6 rotors to get the X- and Y- components (roll and pitch) plus the yaw component from drag torques: $$U_2 = bL \sum_{i=1}^{6} -\sin\alpha_i \omega_i^2$$ $$U_3 = bL \sum_{i=1}^{6} \cos\alpha_i \omega_i^2$$ $$U_4 = d \sum_{i=1}^{6} \sigma_i \omega_i^2 \quad \text{where } \sigma_i = +1 \text{ (CCW) or } -1 \text{ (CW)}$$ Step 5 (2 marks): Substitute the six angles and write the final matrix form $\mathbf{U} = A\boldsymbol{\Omega}$: $$A = \begin{bmatrix} b & b & b & b & b & b \\ 0 & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}bL & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}bL & 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}bL & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}bL \\ bL & \tfrac{1}{2}bL & -\tfrac{1}{2}bL & -bL & -\tfrac{1}{2}bL & \tfrac{1}{2}bL \\ d & -d & d & -d & d & -d \end{bmatrix}$$ with $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = [\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \omega_3^2, \omega_4^2, \omega_5^2, \omega_6^2]^T$. Step 6 (bonus 1 mark for extra detail): To find individual $\omega_i$ for a given $\mathbf{U}$, use the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse: $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = A^+ \mathbf{U} = A^T(AA^T)^{-1}\mathbf{U}$, then $\omega_i = \sqrt{\Omega_i}$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Hexacopter Hover & Translational Dynamics** > **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** > **(a)** A hexacopter has the following parameters: total mass $m = 6$ kg, thrust coefficient $b = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s²/rad², arm length $L = 0.275$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Calculate the **per-rotor angular velocity required for steady hover** and the **total hover thrust**. (7 marks) > **(b)** Derive the **three translational equations of motion** of a hexacopter in the world frame using the Newton-Euler formulation. Clearly define the rotation matrix $R$. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Hover Calculation (7 marks):** Given: $m = 6$ kg, $b = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s²/rad², $g = 9.81$ m/s², $n = 6$ rotors. Step 1 (2 marks): Total hover thrust equals weight: $$U_1 = m g = 6 \times 9.81 = 58.86 \text{ N}$$ Step 2 (2 marks): In symmetric hover, all 6 rotors spin at the same speed $\omega_h$: $$U_1 = 6 b \omega_h^2$$ $$58.86 = 6 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times \omega_h^2$$ $$\omega_h^2 = \frac{58.86}{7.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 817{,}500 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$ Step 3 (1 mark): Take the square root: $$\omega_h = \sqrt{817{,}500} \approx 904.15 \text{ rad/s}$$ Step 4 (1 mark): Convert to RPM (optional but often expected): $$\text{RPM}_h = \omega_h \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 904.15 \times 9.549 \approx 8{,}635 \text{ RPM}$$ Step 5 (1 mark): Per-rotor hover thrust: $$T_h = b \omega_h^2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 817{,}500 = 9.81 \text{ N}$$ (Check: $6 \times 9.81 = 58.86$ N ✓) > [!WARNING] > **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to take the **square root** and write $\omega_h = 817{,}500$ rad/s instead of $\omega_h \approx 904$ rad/s. **−1 mark**. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Using $U_1 = 4 b \omega_h^2$ (quadcopter formula) instead of $6 b \omega_h^2$ (hexacopter). **−2 marks**. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Not stating units explicitly. KTU examiners deduct **½ mark per missing unit** in Part B numerical problems. > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing $b$ (thrust coefficient) with $d$ (drag coefficient). They are **different constants**. **(b) Translational Equations of Motion (7 marks):** Step 1 (1 mark): Define the world frame $\xi = [x, y, z]^T$ and body frame $v_b = [u, v, w]^T$. They are related by the rotation matrix $R$: $$\dot{\xi} = R v_b$$ Step 2 (2 marks): The ZYX Euler rotation matrix (yaw $\psi$ → pitch $\theta$ → roll $\phi$): $$R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi \cos\theta & \cos\psi \sin\theta \sin\phi - \sin\psi \cos\phi & \cos\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi + \sin\psi \sin\phi \\ \sin\psi \cos\theta & \sin\psi \sin\theta \sin\phi + \cos\psi \cos\phi & \sin\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi - \cos\psi \sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \sin\phi & \cos\theta \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$ Step 3 (2 marks): Apply Newton's 2nd Law in the body frame (with gyroscopic term): $$m (\dot{v}_b + \omega_b \times v_b) = F_b$$ Step 4 (2 marks): Decompose the body force $F_b = [0, 0, U_1]^T - m g R^T [0, 0, 1]^T$ and convert to world frame: $$\begin{aligned} \ddot{x} &= \frac{1}{m} \big[ U_1 (\cos\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi + \sin\psi \sin\phi) \big] \\ \ddot{y} &= \frac{1}{m} \big[ U_1 (\sin\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi - \cos\psi \sin\phi) \big] \\ \ddot{z} &= \frac{1}{m} \big[ U_1 \cos\theta \cos\phi - mg \big] \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning for Part (b):** > 1. **Sign error in $\ddot{z}$:** Students often forget the $-mg$ term. KTU strictly requires the gravity term to appear in the world-frame equation. **−1 mark** if missing. > 2. **Wrong rotation order:** The matrix $R$ above uses ZYX (yaw → pitch → roll). Some textbooks use XYZ. KTU 2024 syllabus **explicitly mandates ZYX**. Mentioning the order is mandatory for full marks. > 3. **Forgetting the gyroscopic term** $\omega_b \times v_b$ in the body frame derivation: **−1 mark**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam!)** ### **Core Concepts** - ☐ A **hexacopter** is a **6-rotor** VTOL UAV with **6 DOF** (3 translation + 3 rotation). - ☐ It has only **4 independent control inputs** ($U_1, U_2, U_3, U_4$) → **under-actuated** system. - ☐ **6 rotors for 4 inputs = 2 redundant actuators** → fault tolerance advantage. - ☐ Standard configuration: **Hex-X** with motor angles **0°, 60°, 120°, 180°, 240°, 300°**. - ☐ Motors alternate **CW (2, 4, 6) and CCW (1, 3, 5)** to cancel reactive torque. ### **Critical Formulas (Memorize These)** - ☐ $T_i = b \omega_i^2$ — single rotor thrust. - ☐ $Q_i = d \omega_i^2$ — single rotor drag torque. - ☐ $U_1 = 6 b \omega_h^2 = m g$ — hover condition (per-rotor $\omega_h = \sqrt{mg / 6b}$). - ☐ $U_4 = d \sum \sigma_i \omega_i^2$ — yaw control via CW/CCW speed differential. - ☐ Allocation matrix $A$ is **4×6**, and the pseudo-inverse $A^+ = A^T(AA^T)^{-1}$ is used to solve for individual rotor commands. ### **Dynamic Equations** - ☐ **Translation:** $m \ddot{\xi} = R \cdot F_b - m g \hat{z}_E$ → 3 scalar ODEs. - ☐ **Rotation:** $I \dot{\omega}_b + \omega_b \times I \omega_b = \tau_b$ → 3 scalar ODEs. - ☐ **Kinematics:** $\dot{\eta} = W(\eta) \omega_b$ — singular at $\theta = \pm 90°$. - ☐ **Total state dimension:** 12 (positions, Euler angles, linear velocities, angular rates). ### **Sensors & Control** - ☐ **IMU** (accelerometer + gyroscope) — measures $\omega_b$ and linear acceleration. - ☐ **GPS + Barometer** — measures world-frame position and altitude. - ☐ **EKF (Extended Kalman Filter)** — fuses sensor data into state estimate. - ☐ **Cascade control:** Outer (position) PID → Inner (attitude) PD → Allocation → Motors. ### **Key Numerical Values to Know** - ☐ Air density at sea level: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. - ☐ Gravity: $g = 9.81$ m/s². - ☐ Typical $b$ range: $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ N·s²/rad² (depends on propeller). - ☐ Typical arm length $L$: 0.225 m (F450) to 0.480 m (F960). ### **Exam-Day Strategy** - ☐ If a numerical problem says "**steady hover**" → use $U_1 = mg$ first, then $U_2 = U_3 = U_4 = 0$. - ☐ If asked "**derivation of allocation matrix**" → start from geometry, then moment cross-product, then summation. - ☐ Always include **units** in numerical answers. - ☐ Always **state the assumption** (e.g., "assuming symmetric hover"). ---

topics.title: Y3/Y6
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Y3/Y6

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Y3 and Y6 Wheeled Robot Configurations

In the foundational study of **Wheeled Mobile Robots (WMRs)**, the kinematic and structural configuration of wheels defines the robot's maneuverability, stability, and application domain. The notations **Y3** and **Y6** refer to specific wheel-arrangement geometries where the wheels are placed in a **Y-shaped geometric pattern** (i.e., equally spaced at **120°** angular intervals around the robot's center).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of wheeled robots based on the number of wheels and their geometric arrangement is a fundamental topic in Module 1. Y3 and Y6 configurations represent the most practically deployed omnidirectional platforms in service, warehouse, and planetary robotics.

### 1.1.1 Formal Definition: Y3 Configuration

A **Y3 mobile robot** is a three-wheeled omnidirectional platform where the three **centered (fixed-orientation) or off-centered omni-wheels** (Mecanum or Swedish wheels) are mounted at **120° angular spacing**, forming a Y-shape when viewed from above. Each wheel is independently actuated, granting the robot **3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** on a planar surface: $x$, $y$, and $\theta$ (orientation).

### 1.1.2 Formal Definition: Y6 Configuration
