In mobile robotics, **representing robot position** means describing the geometric state of the robot — its location and orientation — at any given instant. This state is formally called the **configuration** of the robot, and the space of all possible configurations is termed the **Configuration Space (C-space)**.

Mathematically, the position (or pose) of a mobile robot moving on a plane is represented as:

$$
q = (x, y, \theta) \in SE(2)
$$

where:
- $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ is the **position** of a reference point on the robot (typically the center of the wheelbase).
- $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ is the **orientation** (heading angle) of the robot with respect to the global X-axis.
- $SE(2)$ is the **Special Euclidean Group in 2D** — the mathematical manifold governing rigid-body motion in the plane.

For a robot moving in 3D space, the pose is extended to:

$$
q = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi) \in SE(3)
$$

where $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ are the **roll, pitch, and yaw** Euler angles.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a remote-controlled toy car in a parking lot.** To fully describe *where* the car is at any moment, you need **three pieces of information**:
> 1. **Where on the map?** → (x, y) coordinates
> 2. **Which way is it facing?** → Heading angle θ
>
> Together, these three numbers form the car's **pose**. A car can be facing North (0°) at the same (x, y) location but with entirely different "states" — like a driver pointing one way and the car pointing another.

### Why is Position Representation Important?

- It is the **fundamental input** to navigation, path planning, and control algorithms.
- It allows a robot to **localize** itself (know where it is).
- It enables **transformation** of sensor data from robot-frame to world-frame.
- It defines the **degrees of freedom (DOF)** the robot possesses.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Configuration (q)** | Complete specification of robot's position | $q = (x, y, \theta)$ |
| **Configuration Space (C-space)** | Set of all possible configurations | $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ |
| **Workspace** | The physical region the robot can reach | A 10m × 10m room |
| **Pose** | Position + Orientation | $(2.5, 3.0, 45°)$ |
| **DOF** | Number of independent parameters needed to define pose | 3 (for planar) |
| **Holonomic** | All DOF are controllable | Omni-directional robot |
| **Non-holonomic** | Some DOF are not directly controllable | Car (cannot move sideways) |

## 1.4 Why Kinematics, Not Dynamics?

> [!NOTE]
> **Kinematics** studies **motion** (geometry of motion), while **dynamics** studies **forces causing motion**.
>
> For a first-pass analysis of robot motion — like a KTU exam problem — kinematics is sufficient. Dynamics (involving mass, inertia, motor torques) belongs to a higher-level course.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Position Representation

### Step 1: Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Determination

The DOF of a rigid body in 3D space is **6** (3 translations + 3 rotations). When constrained to move on a 2D plane, the DOF reduces to **3** (2 translations + 1 rotation).

The **Grübler-Kutzbach formula** (mobility formula) for planar mechanisms:

$$
M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

where:
- $M$ = mobility (DOF) of the mechanism
- $n$ = total number of links (including ground)
- $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (1-DOF joints)
- $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (2-DOF joints)

### Step 2: Forward Kinematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forward Kinematics** computes the robot's pose $q(t)$ given the control inputs (wheel velocities, steering rates).

For a **differential-drive robot** (two independently driven wheels on a common axis):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos(\theta) \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin(\theta) \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

where the control inputs are:
- $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ → **linear velocity**
- $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ → **angular velocity**
- $r$ = wheel radius, $L$ = distance between wheels, $\omega_R, \omega_L$ = right/left wheel angular velocities.

### Step 3: Pose Representation via Homogeneous Transformation

A pose in 2D can be encoded as a $3 \times 3$ **homogeneous transformation matrix**:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in SE(2)
$$

This matrix is composed of:
- A $2 \times 2$ **rotation matrix** $R(\theta)$
- A $2 \times 1$ **translation vector** $(x, y)^T$

The inverse transformation is:

$$
T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & -x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & x\sin\theta - y\cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 2.2 Constraints in Mobile Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **kinematic constraint** is a restriction on the possible velocities of the robot. They are mathematical equations that the robot's motion must satisfy.

### 2.2.1 Holonomic Constraints

A constraint is **holonomic** if it can be expressed as an explicit function of the configuration variables alone:

$$
h(q) = 0
$$

**Example:** A robot constrained to move on a circle of radius $R$:

$$
x^2 + y^2 - R^2 = 0
$$

This reduces the effective DOF (because it's an *integrable* velocity constraint).

### 2.2.2 Non-Holonomic Constraints

> [!WARNING]
> A **non-holonomic constraint** is a velocity constraint that **cannot be integrated** to give a configuration constraint. It restricts the *instantaneous* velocity but not the *reachable* position.

For a unicycle/differential-drive robot (rolling without slipping):

$$
-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

This says: **the robot cannot move sideways** (perpendicular to its wheel axis). However, the robot can still reach *any* position and orientation in the plane through careful maneuvering — so the position itself is *not* constrained, only the velocity.

### 2.2.3 Pfaffian Form

General kinematic constraints are written in **Pfaffian form**:

$$
A(q)\dot{q} = 0
$$

For a differential-drive robot:

$$
A(q) = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The number of independent rows of $A(q)$ equals the number of **non-holonomic constraints**. A system with $n$ DOF and $k$ independent non-holonomic constraints has $n - k$ controllable velocities, but **still $n$ reachable configurations** asymptotically.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table** — these are the equations you must memorize for the KTU End Semester Exam (ESE).

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---------|---------|-----------|------|
| Planar pose | $q = (x, y, \theta)$ | position + heading | m, m, rad |
| Diff. drive linear velocity | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | wheel angular speeds | m/s |
| Diff. drive angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | wheel speeds, wheelbase | rad/s |
| Forward kinematics (general) | $\dot{q} = f(q, u)$ | state, input | varies |
| Ackermann steering radius | $R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$ | wheelbase, steer angle | m |
| Body-to-world rotation | $R(\theta)$ | heading | unitless |
| Holonomic constraint | $h(q) = 0$ | configuration only | — |
| Non-holonomic constraint | $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ | involves velocity | — |
| Grübler mobility | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | links, joints | unitless |
| Differential constraint (rolling) | $-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | unicycle/2-wheel | — |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Ackermann steering kinematics determines how the steering angle translates to a turning radius. This is essential for path tracking.
> - **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** Differential-drive AMRs (e.g., Amazon Kiva robots) use kinematic models to navigate aisles.
> - **Agricultural Robots:** Tractor-trailer systems use kinematic models for precise row-following.
> - **Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance, Curiosity):** Use 6-wheel rocker-bogie kinematic models to traverse uneven terrain.
> - **Underwater ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles):** 3D pose representation in $SE(3)$ is critical for thruster control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a Differential-Drive Robot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most important derivation** for KTU exams. Master it.

### Setup

Consider a differential-drive robot with:
- Two wheels of radius $r$ mounted on a common axle
- Distance between wheel centers (wheelbase) = $L$
- Wheel angular velocities: $\omega_R$ (right), $\omega_L$ (left)
- Robot's reference point is the midpoint between wheels

### Step 1: Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)

The two wheels trace circular arcs of the **same radius** about a common center — the ICR. Let this ICR be at distance $R$ from the robot's center.

The right wheel traces an arc of radius $(R + L/2)$ and the left wheel traces an arc of radius $(R - L/2)$. Both wheels have the same linear velocity at the contact point relative to the ground (no slipping).

For the right wheel:

$$
v_R = \omega_R \cdot r
$$

For the left wheel:

$$
v_L = \omega_L \cdot r
$$

The instantaneous angular velocity of the robot is:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

**Why?** — Because the two wheels, located $L$ apart, must move at slightly different speeds to produce rotation. The "extra" velocity divided by the wheelbase gives the angular speed.

### Step 2: Linear Velocity of the Robot Center

The center of the robot moves at a speed that is the *average* of the two wheel speeds (since the center is equidistant from both wheels):

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

**Why?** — Geometric mean: the ICR is to the side, and the center sits exactly halfway between the two wheels.

### Step 3: Velocity Components in the Global Frame

The robot's center is at $(x, y)$ and heading $\theta$ in the global frame. Its body-frame velocity is $(v, 0)$ — meaning it can only move forward along its own heading axis (no sideways motion in the body frame).

To project the body-frame velocity onto the world frame, apply the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:

$$
R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos\theta - 0 \cdot \sin\theta = v \cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin\theta + 0 \cdot \cos\theta = v \sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Final Compact Form (State-Space Representation)

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = B(q)\,u\;}
$$

where $u = (v, \omega)^T$ is the control input vector.

## 3.2 Derivation: Ackermann Steering Kinematics

The Ackermann geometry models a car-like vehicle with two front steering wheels and two rear fixed wheels.

### Setup

- Wheelbase $L$ (distance between front and rear axles)
- Steering angle $\delta$ (angle of the front wheels)
- Vehicle moves at speed $v$ along its longitudinal axis

### Step 1: Turning Radius

The vehicle rotates about a point offset from the rear axle. By similar triangles in the steering geometry:

$$
\tan\delta = \frac{L}{R}
$$

Solving for the turning radius:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}
$$

### Step 2: Angular Velocity

$$
\omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{v \tan\delta}{L}
$$

### Step 3: Kinematic Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \frac{v \tan\delta}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: In Ackermann steering, $\delta$ is the **control input** (not $\omega$ directly), since the steering angle is physically limited (typically $\vert \delta \vert \le 35°$).

## 3.3 Derivation: Identifying a Constraint as Holonomic or Non-Holonomic

### Given Constraint

$$
A(q)\dot{q} = 0
$$

for a differential-drive robot:

$$
-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

### Test 1: Try to Integrate

Multiply both sides by $dt$:

$$
-\sin\theta\, dx + \cos\theta\, dy = 0
$$

Check if this is an **exact differential** of some function $h(x, y, \theta)$:

$$
\frac{\partial h}{\partial x} = -\sin\theta, \quad \frac{\partial h}{\partial y} = \cos\theta, \quad \frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta} = 0
$$

From the third equation: $h$ does not depend on $\theta$. But the first two imply $h = -x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta + C$, which **does** depend on $\theta$. **Contradiction!**

### Conclusion

The constraint **cannot be integrated** to a configuration-level equation. Therefore, it is **non-holonomic**. The robot's instantaneous motion is restricted (no sideways motion), but its reachable configuration space remains the full 3D manifold $SE(2)$.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU question** ("Compare the kinematic models of different wheeled robots"). Memorize it.

| Parameter | Differential Drive | Ackermann (Car-like) | Tricycle | Omni-Directional (Mecanum) |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|----------|---------------------------|
| DOF in C-space | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 |
| Controllable velocities | 2 (v, ω) | 2 (v, δ) | 2 (v, δ) | 3 (v_x, v_y, ω) |
| Non-holonomic constraints | 1 | 2 | 1 | 0 (holonomic) |
| Min. turning radius | 0 (turn in place) | $L / \tan\delta_{max}$ | $L / \tan\delta_{max}$ | 0 |
| Can move sideways? | No | No | No | Yes |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | Medium | High (mechanical) |
| Application | Indoor AMRs | Cars, outdoor AGVs | Research robots | Research, special use |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Position Representation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Physical World State] --> B[Sensor Measurements]
    B --> C[Odometry / Localization]
    C --> D[Configuration q equals x y theta]
    D --> E{Type of Constraint}
    E --> F[Holonomic h of q equals 0]
    E --> G[Non-Holonomic A q dot q equals 0]
    F --> H[Reduced C-space]
    G --> I[Full C-space but restricted velocities]
    H --> J[Path Planning]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Control Inputs v and omega]
    K --> L[Actuator Commands]
    L --> A
```

## 4.2 Diagram: Differential-Drive Robot Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RobotBase
        W1[Left Wheel omega_L]
        W2[Right Wheel omega_R]
        C[Center of robot pose x y theta]
    end
    W1 --- C
    W2 --- C
    W1 -->|distance L| W2
    C -->|ICR at distance R| ICR[Instantaneous Center of Rotation]
    ICR -->|Radius R| Path[Curved trajectory]
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Hierarchy of Kinematic Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mobile Robotics] --> B[Kinematic Models]
    A --> C[Dynamics Models]
    B --> D[Position Representation]
    B --> E[Velocity Representation]
    B --> F[Constraint Analysis]
    D --> D1[Pose q equals x y theta]
    D --> D2[Homogeneous Transform T]
    D --> D3[Configuration Space SE 2]
    E --> E1[Forward Kinematics dot q equals f q u]
    E --> E2[Inverse Kinematics u equals g q dot q]
    F --> F1[Holonomic]
    F --> F2[Non-Holonomic Pfaffian]
    F --> F3[Grübler Formula]
```

## 4.4 Diagram: Coordinate Frames and Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WorldFrame[Global Frame Xw Yw] -->|Translation xy| RobotFrame
    RobotFrame[Robot Frame Xb Yb] -->|Rotation theta| WorldFrame
    WorldFrame --> A[Origin at fixed reference]
    RobotFrame --> B[Origin at robot center]
    A --> C[Position is in meters]
    B --> D[Velocity is in body frame]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The body frame moves with the robot; the world frame is fixed. To convert a point from body frame to world frame, multiply by the homogeneous transformation matrix $T$. To convert back, multiply by $T^{-1}$.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Configuration Space. How is it different from Workspace?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Configuration Space (C-space):** The set of all possible configurations that a robot can attain. For a mobile robot on a plane, the C-space is $SE(2) = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$, parameterized by $q = (x, y, \theta)$.

**Workspace** is the physical region in the **real world** that the robot's end-effector or body can reach. **Configuration Space** is a higher-dimensional abstract space where each point represents a *complete state* (position + orientation) of the robot.

For a 2D point robot, workspace = C-space = $\mathbb{R}^2$. For a 3-DOF planar robot, the workspace is the physical plane, but the C-space is 3-dimensional $(x, y, \theta)$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between holonomic and non-holonomic constraints with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Holonomic | Non-Holonomic |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| Form | $h(q) = 0$ (position-level) | $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ (velocity-level) |
| Integrable? | Yes | No |
| Reduces reachable C-space? | Yes | No (asymptotically) |
| Example | Pendulum with rigid rod | Car (cannot move sideways) |
| DOF reduction | Direct | Indirect (restricts velocity only) |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`
**(a)** Derive the forward kinematic equations of a differential-drive mobile robot. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, wheelbase $L = 0.3$ m. If the right wheel rotates at $20$ rad/s and the left wheel at $15$ rad/s, calculate the linear and angular velocities of the robot. Also find the radius of curvature of its path. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
*(See SECTION 3.1 for the complete derivation. The student should:)*

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** State the geometric setup — two wheels on a common axle, ICR concept.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Derive the linear and angular velocities in terms of wheel speeds:
  - $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$
  - $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$
- **Step 3 (3 marks):** Project onto world frame using rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ and write the state-space form.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $r = 0.05$ m
- $L = 0.3$ m
- $\omega_R = 20$ rad/s
- $\omega_L = 15$ rad/s

**Step 1: Linear velocity**

$$
v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = \frac{0.05}{2}(20 + 15) = \frac{0.05}{2} \times 35 = 0.025 \times 35 = 0.875 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Angular velocity**

$$
\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.05}{0.3}(20 - 15) = \frac{0.05}{0.3} \times 5 = 0.1667 \times 5 = 0.8333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3: Radius of curvature**

The robot rotates about the ICR with angular velocity $\omega$, and the center moves at speed $v$. The radius of curvature is:

$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.875}{0.8333} = 1.05 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answer:** $v = 0.875$ m/s, $\omega = 0.833$ rad/s, $R = 1.05$ m.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the concept of homogeneous transformation matrices for representing robot pose. Derive the matrix for a 2D pose $(x, y, \theta)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car-like robot (Ackermann steering) has wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m and moves at $v = 10$ m/s. The steering angle is $\delta = 15°$. Calculate the turning radius and the time taken to complete a $90°$ turn. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Homogeneous Transformation (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Define a homogeneous transformation as a $3 \times 3$ matrix combining rotation and translation in a single algebraic object.
- **Step 2 (3 marks):** Derive the rotation matrix:
  $$
  R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
  $$
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Embed into the homogeneous matrix:
  $$
  T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
  $$
  Explain that the bottom row is fixed as $[0, 0, 1]$ for 2D rigid transforms.

**(b) Ackermann Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $L = 2.5$ m, $v = 10$ m/s, $\delta = 15°$.

**Step 1: Turning radius**

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta} = \frac{2.5}{\tan 15°} = \frac{2.5}{0.2679} = 9.33 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Angular velocity**

$$
\omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{10}{9.33} = 1.072 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3: Time for 90° turn**

Convert $90°$ to radians: $90° = \frac{\pi}{2}$ rad $\approx 1.5708$ rad.

$$
t = \frac{\theta_{turn}}{\omega} = \frac{1.5708}{1.072} = 1.465 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $R = 9.33$ m, $t \approx 1.47$ s.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Top 5 mistakes students make on this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up $L$ and $r$** — Wheelbase $L$ is between wheels, wheel radius $r$ is on the wheel itself. They are *different* quantities.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — $v$ is in m/s, $\omega$ in rad/s, angles must be in radians inside $\sin/\cos$.
> 3. **Confusing DOF reduction with configuration restriction** — Non-holonomic constraints do *not* reduce reachable configurations, only instantaneous velocities. Many students write the opposite in exams.
> 4. **Sign errors in $\omega$** — Use the right-hand rule. If $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the robot turns **left** (counter-clockwise in standard math frame).
> 5. **Treating Ackermann like differential drive** — Ackermann uses $\tan\delta$, not $\omega_R - \omega_L$. They have **different** input variables.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam!)**

- [x] **Pose = Position + Orientation** → $q = (x, y, \theta)$ in 2D.
- [x] **Configuration Space** for a planar mobile robot is $SE(2) = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$.
- [x] **Forward Kinematics of Diff. Drive:**
  - $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$
  - $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$
- [x] **Ackermann turning radius:** $R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$.
- [x] **Holonomic constraint** is *integrable*; **Non-holonomic** is *not*.
- [x] **Differential-drive non-holonomic constraint:** $-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
- [x] **Grübler mobility formula:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- [x] **Homogeneous transform** is $3 \times 3$ in 2D, $4 \times 4$ in 3D.
- [x] **Omni-directional robots are holonomic**; **car-like robots are non-holonomic**.
- [x] **Units check:** radians in trig functions, meters for distances, m/s for velocities.
- [x] **Always check** if your input variables are $\omega_R, \omega_L$ (diff. drive) or $\delta$ (Ackermann) — they lead to *different* kinematic equations.

topics.title: Forward Kinematic Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forward Kinematic Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Forward Kinematic Model (FKM)** of a mobile robot is the set of mathematical equations that maps the **control inputs** (such as wheel angular velocities or motor commands) of a robot to the **time rate of change of its posture** (configuration variables) in the world (inertial) reference frame. Formally, it is expressed as:

$$\dot{\xi}_{I} = f(\xi_{I}, u)$$

where $\xi_{I} = [x, y, \theta]^{T}$ is the robot's posture in the inertial frame and $u = [u_1, u_2, \dots, u_n]^{T}$ is the vector of control inputs. The function $f(\cdot)$ is determined by the **wheel arrangement, geometry, and mechanical constraints** of the robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Posture (Configuration)** of a 2D mobile robot has exactly **3 degrees of freedom**: $(x, y)$ position in the plane and the heading angle $\theta$ w.r.t. the $X$-axis. This is a *Holonomic* vs *Non-Holonomic* classification concept directly assessed in KTU 2024.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forward Kinematics vs. Odometry:** The **Forward Kinematic Model** uses *measured* control inputs to predict *instantaneous* motion, while **Odometry** integrates these predictions over time. FKM is the *building block* of odometry.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine driving a car. You turn the steering wheel by a certain angle and press the accelerator to a specific value. **Forward Kinematics** answers the question:
> *"If the driver turns the wheels this way and pushes the gas this hard, exactly where does the car move on the road?"*

- The **steering angle and gas pedal position** = control inputs ($u$).
- The **actual path traced on the road** = output posture rate ($\dot{\xi}$).

In the case of a **differential drive robot** (like a Roomba vacuum), instead of a steering wheel, you have two independent motors driving the left and right wheels. The combination of their speeds uniquely determines whether the robot moves straight, turns in place, or moves along an arc.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Wheel radius** $r$ : expressed in **meters (m)**
- **Distance between wheels / axle length** $2L$ or $b$ : expressed in **meters (m)**
- **Wheel angular velocity** $\dot{\phi}$ : in **radians/second (rad/s)**
- **Linear velocity** $v$ : in **meters/second (m/s)**
- **Angular velocity** $\omega$ : in **radians/second (rad/s)**
- **Assumed standard gravitational reference for planar motion** : $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ (irrelevant for kinematics, but constant in physical robotics)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Framework

The forward kinematic model rests on three pillars:

1. **Posture / Configuration Definition** – defines what we want to compute.
2. **Wheel Kinematic Constraints** – defines what the wheels physically allow.
3. **Coordinate Frame Transformation** – maps robot-frame motion to world-frame motion.

### 2.1.1 Posture of a Mobile Robot

The instantaneous pose of a rigid robot in 2D space is fully described by:

$$\xi_{I} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(x, y)$ is the position of a reference point (typically the center of the axle) and $\theta$ is the orientation of the robot's longitudinal axis measured counter-clockwise from the global $X$-axis.

### 2.1.2 Reference Frame Transformation

The transformation from the **robot frame** $R$ to the **inertial frame** $I$ is given by the rotation matrix:

$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This means the velocity of any point on the robot in the world frame is:

$$\dot{\xi}_{I} = R(\theta) \cdot \dot{\xi}_{R}$$

### 2.1.3 Wheel Kinematic Constraints (Core Concept)

Every wheel on a mobile robot imposes two fundamental constraints:

| Constraint Type | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Rolling Constraint** | Wheel cannot slip sideways; motion is purely along its plane of rotation | $J_1(\beta) R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_{I} = r \dot{\phi}$ |
| **Sliding (No-Slip) Constraint** | Wheel cannot slide along its rotational axis | $J_2(\beta) R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_{I} = 0$ |

For a fixed standard wheel, the constraint matrices $J_1$ and $J_2$ are:

$$J_1(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \sin(\alpha + \beta) & -\cos(\alpha + \beta) & -l\cos\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

$$J_2(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\alpha + \beta) & \sin(\alpha + \beta) & l\sin\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\alpha$ is the steering angle, $\beta$ is the wheel's mounting angle, $l$ is the distance from robot center to wheel contact, and $r$ is the wheel radius.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Equation** |
|---|---|---|
| $\xi_I$ | Posture in inertial frame | $\xi_I = [x, y, \theta]^T$ |
| $\dot{\xi}_I$ | Posture rate | $\dot{\xi}_I = R(\theta)\dot{\xi}_R$ |
| $R(\theta)$ | Rotation matrix | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0\\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ |
| Differential Drive Linear Velocity | $v$ | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l)$ |
| Differential Drive Angular Velocity | $\omega$ | $\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\dot{\phi}_r - \dot{\phi}_l)$ |
| Differential Drive FKM | $\dot{\xi}_I$ | $\begin{bmatrix}\dot{x}\\ \dot{y}\\ \dot{\theta}\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0\\ \sin\theta & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}v\\ \omega\end{bmatrix}$ |
| Synchronous Drive FKM | $\dot{\xi}_I$ | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\; \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\; \dot{\theta} = \dot{\phi}_s$ |
| Ackermann Steering | Turning radius | $R_c = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$ |
| Generic Wheel FKM | $\dot{\xi}_I$ | $\dot{\xi}_I = f(\xi_I) \cdot \dot{\varphi}$ |
| **Units reminder** | Linear: m/s ; Angular: rad/s ; Length: m | **SI Standard** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Pattern:** Whenever a problem states *“derive the forward kinematic model of …”*, examiners expect the final answer in the form $\dot{\xi}_I = f(\xi_I, u)$, with the explicit form of $R(\theta)$ and the Jacobian block clearly shown.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Forward kinematic models are the *spinal cord* of every autonomous mobile system:

- **Industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** : use FKM for path tracking in warehouses (e.g., Amazon Kiva robots).
- **Autonomous Cars** : use FKM of Ackermann steering for lane keeping.
- **Service Robots (vacuum cleaners, hospital logistics)** : use differential drive FKM.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance, Curiosity)** : rocker-bogie suspensions require FKM for terrain-aware motion.
- **Warehouse AMR fleets** : use the synchronous drive FKM for omnidirectional movement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematic Model of a Differential Drive Robot

A differential drive robot has two independently driven wheels mounted on a common axle, separated by distance $2L$ (so each wheel is at distance $L$ from the center).

### Step 1: Define the Posture

Let the robot's center be at $(x, y)$ with orientation $\theta$.

$$\xi_I = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Express the Robot-Frame Velocities

Let the linear velocity of the robot center be $v$ and the angular velocity be $\omega$ (both expressed in the robot frame). Then:

$$\dot{\xi}_R = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

Note: $y$ in robot frame is always zero because the robot cannot move sideways (non-holonomic constraint).

### Step 3: Apply the Frame Transformation

Multiplying by the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:

$$\dot{\xi}_I = R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_R$$

Substituting:

$$\dot{\xi}_I = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Carry out the Matrix Multiplication

Multiplying row-by-row:

$$\dot{\xi}_I = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta - 0 + 0 \\ v\sin\theta + 0 + 0 \\ 0 + 0 + \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Map Wheel Angular Velocities to $v$ and $\omega$

Let $\dot{\phi}_r$ and $\dot{\phi}_l$ be the right and left wheel angular velocities, and $r$ the wheel radius.

- The linear velocity $v$ is the average of both wheel linear speeds:
$$v = \frac{r\dot{\phi}_r + r\dot{\phi}_l}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l)$$

- The angular velocity $\omega$ is the difference of wheel speeds divided by the axle length $2L$:
$$\omega = \frac{r\dot{\phi}_r - r\dot{\phi}_l}{2L} = \frac{r}{2L}(\dot{\phi}_r - \dot{\phi}_l)$$

### Step 6: Combine into the Compact Forward Kinematic Model

$$\dot{\xi}_I = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

In expanded form, with explicit wheel control inputs:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta \cdot (\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l) \\ \dot{y} &= \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta \cdot (\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l) \\ \dot{\theta} &= \frac{r}{2L}(\dot{\phi}_r - \dot{\phi}_l) \end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is non-holonomic:** The matrix on the right has only **2 columns** but the configuration space is **3-D**. Therefore, the robot has *fewer control inputs than degrees of freedom*, making it a **non-holonomic system**. This is a guaranteed KTU 2024 14-mark question topic.

## 3.2 Derivation: Forward Kinematic Model with Single Steered Wheel

Consider a single fixed standard wheel mounted at position $(x, y)$ on the robot, with mounting angle $\beta$ and steering angle $\alpha$.

### Step 1: Rolling Constraint

The wheel must roll without slipping along its direction of motion:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sin(\alpha + \beta) & -\cos(\alpha + \beta) & -l\cos\beta \end{bmatrix} R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_I = r\dot{\phi}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Rotation Matrix

$$J_1(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \sin(\alpha + \beta) & -\cos(\alpha + \beta) & -l\cos\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying $J_1(\beta) R(\theta)$:

$$\begin{aligned} J_1(\beta) R(\theta) &= \begin{bmatrix} \sin(\alpha+\beta)\cos\theta + \cos(\alpha+\beta)\sin\theta, \ldots \\ \ldots -\sin(\alpha+\beta)\sin\theta + \cos(\alpha+\beta)\cos\theta, -l\cos\beta \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

Using the angle-addition identity $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$:

$$= \begin{bmatrix} \sin(\alpha + \beta + \theta), & -\cos(\alpha + \beta + \theta), & -l\cos\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Sliding Constraint

Perpendicular to the rolling direction, the wheel cannot slide:

$$J_2(\beta) R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_I = 0$$

This yields:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \cos(\alpha + \beta + \theta) & \sin(\alpha + \beta + \theta) & l\sin\beta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

This constraint **restricts** the robot's instantaneous motion — it can only move along a line tangent to the wheel's orientation.

## 3.3 Derivation: General Multi-Wheel Mobile Robot

For a robot with $N$ fixed standard wheels and $M$ steered standard wheels:

### Step 1: Stack All Rolling Constraints

$$J_1(\beta_F) R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_I = r\dot{\phi}_F$$

where $J_1(\beta_F)$ has dimensions $(N + M) \times 3$.

### Step 2: Stack All Sliding Constraints

$$C_\beta R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_I = 0$$

The matrix $C_\beta$ has dimensions $N \times 3$ for fixed wheels (only fixed wheels impose sliding constraints; steered wheels do not because they can reorient).

### Step 3: Solve for $\dot{\xi}_I$

For the system to be mobile, the rank of the combined constraint matrix must be **strictly less than 3**. The reachable instantaneous velocities form a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^3$ called the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** manifold.

In compact form, the FKM can be written as:

$$\dot{\xi}_I = \Sigma(\beta) \cdot \dot{\varphi}$$

where $\Sigma(\beta)$ is the **Jacobian** of the system that depends on the wheel configuration angles $\beta$, and $\dot{\varphi}$ is the vector of all wheel angular velocities.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A differential drive robot has $r = 0.05$ m, $2L = 0.3$ m, $\dot{\phi}_r = 10$ rad/s, $\dot{\phi}_l = 6$ rad/s. Find the posture rate if $\theta = \pi/4$ rad.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute $v$:

$$v = \frac{0.05}{2}(10 + 6) = 0.025 \times 16 = 0.4 \, m/s$$

Step 2: Compute $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{0.05}{0.3}(10 - 6) = 0.1667 \times 4 = 0.6667 \, rad/s$$

Step 3: Apply FKM:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= 0.4 \cos(\pi/4) = 0.4 \times 0.7071 = 0.2828 \, m/s \\ \dot{y} &= 0.4 \sin(\pi/4) = 0.4 \times 0.7071 = 0.2828 \, m/s \\ \dot{\theta} &= 0.6667 \, rad/s \end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forward Kinematic Model — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the complete information flow from physical wheel rotation to inertial-frame posture update.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WHEEL ANGULAR VELOCITY phi_dot] --> B[WHEEL KINEMATIC MODEL]
    B --> C[ROBOT FRAME VELOCITY v omega]
    C --> D[ROTATION MATRIX R theta]
    D --> E[INERTIAL FRAME POSTURE RATE xi_dot_I]
    E --> F[INTEGRATION ODOMETRY]
    F --> G[UPDATED POSTURE x y theta]
    H[ROBOT GEOMETRY r L beta alpha] --> B
    H --> D
    style A fill:f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:a9cce3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#7dcea0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style E fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style G fill:#f5cba7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style H fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

```mermaid

    A --> C[SLIDING CONSTRAINT J2]
    B --> D[VELOCITY ALONG WHEEL PLANE]
    C --> E[ZERO VELOCITY PERPENDICULAR]
    D --> F[COMBINED CONSTRAINT MATRIX]
    E --> F
    F --> G[FKM xi_dot_I equals f of xi_I and u]
    style A fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style E fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style G fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: From Control Input to Posture Update

```mermaid

    inp1[Control Input u1] --> comp[Constraint Matrices J1 and J2]
    inp2[Control Input u2] --> comp
    inp3[Control Input u3] --> comp
    comp --> robFrame[Robot Frame Velocity]
    robFrame --> rotMat[Apply R of theta]
    rotMat --> worldFrame[World Frame Posture Rate]
    worldFrame --> integ[Time Integration]
    integ --> final[New Posture x y theta]
    style inp1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
