> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams (KTU Exam Tip):** When examiners ask to *"draw the block diagram of forward kinematic model"*, this three-stage pipeline (Input → Robot Frame → World Frame) is the standard expected answer. Always label the **rotation matrix $R(\theta)$** in the middle block — it is the most-skipped step in student answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the forward kinematic model of a mobile robot. Why is it called "forward"?**

**Model Answer:**
The forward kinematic model of a mobile robot is the set of equations that maps the **control inputs** (such as wheel angular velocities) and the **current posture** to the **time rate of change of posture** in the world frame. It is expressed as $\dot{\xi}_I = f(\xi_I, u)$.

It is called "forward" because it computes the robot's *resultant motion* given the *known actuator commands*, as opposed to **inverse kinematics** which computes the *required actuator commands* to achieve a desired motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** Definition (2 marks) + Justification of "forward" terminology (1 mark).

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between rolling constraint and sliding constraint of a fixed standard wheel.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Rolling Constraint | Sliding Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Along the wheel plane | Perpendicular to wheel plane |
| Equation | $J_1(\beta) R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_I = r\dot{\phi}$ | $J_2(\beta) R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_I = 0$ |
| Physical meaning | Wheel rolls without slipping in its rolling direction | Wheel cannot move sideways (no lateral slip) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** 1.5 marks for each constraint description.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematic model of a **differential drive mobile robot** with wheel radius $r$ and axle length $2L$. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot with $r = 0.08$ m, $2L = 0.4$ m, and $\theta = \pi/3$ rad has wheel velocities $\dot{\phi}_r = 8$ rad/s and $\dot{\phi}_l = 4$ rad/s. Compute the posture rate $\dot{\xi}_I$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Wheels roll without slipping.
2. The robot moves on a flat 2D plane.
3. The robot frame is centered between the two wheels.

**Step 1:** Define the posture vector:

$$\xi_I = [x, y, \theta]^T$$

**Step 2:** Write the robot-frame velocity (no lateral motion):

$$\dot{\xi}_R = [v, 0, \omega]^T$$

**Step 3:** Apply the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ to get inertial-frame posture rate:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Express $v$ and $\omega$ in terms of wheel angular velocities:

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l) \quad ; \quad \omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\dot{\phi}_r - \dot{\phi}_l)$$

**Step 5:** Final compact form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta(\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l) \\ \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta(\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l) \\ \frac{r}{2L}(\dot{\phi}_r - \dot{\phi}_l) \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the desired forward kinematic model of the differential drive robot.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1:** Compute the linear velocity $v$:

$$v = \frac{0.08}{2}(8 + 4) = 0.04 \times 12 = 0.48 \, m/s$$

**Step 2:** Compute the angular velocity $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{0.08}{0.4}(8 - 4) = 0.2 \times 4 = 0.8 \, rad/s$$

**Step 3:** Apply the FKM with $\theta = \pi/3$:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= 0.48 \cos(\pi/3) = 0.48 \times 0.5 = 0.24 \, m/s \\ \dot{y} &= 0.48 \sin(\pi/3) = 0.48 \times 0.8660 = 0.4157 \, m/s \\ \dot{\theta} &= 0.8 \, rad/s \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Final posture rate vector:

$$\dot{\xi}_I = [0.24, 0.4157, 0.8]^T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (Part b):** Correct $v$ (2 marks) + Correct $\omega$ (2 marks) + Correct posture rate (3 marks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Supplementary 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **forward kinematic model with two types of wheel constraints** (rolling and sliding). Define the constraint matrices $J_1(\beta)$ and $J_2(\beta)$ for a fixed standard wheel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-wheeled robot uses two fixed standard wheels at angles $\beta_1 = 0°$ and $\beta_2 = 180°$, both with radius $r = 0.05$ m. Formulate the combined constraint matrix and state the condition for the robot to be mobile. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Conceptual Explanation:**
A mobile robot's wheels impose two fundamental non-holonomic constraints:

1. **Rolling Constraint ($J_1$):** The wheel must roll without slipping along the direction it is pointing. Mathematically:

$$J_1(\beta) R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_I = r \dot{\phi}$$

2. **Sliding Constraint ($J_2$):** The wheel cannot slide perpendicular to its rolling plane:

$$J_2(\beta) R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_I = 0$$

**For a Fixed Standard Wheel:**

The wheel's position relative to the robot center is at $(x, l\cos\beta, l\sin\beta)$ in the robot frame. The constraints become:

$$J_1(\beta) = [\sin(\alpha + \beta), -\cos(\alpha + \beta), -l\cos\beta]$$

$$J_2(\beta) = [\cos(\alpha + \beta), \sin(\alpha + \beta), l\sin\beta]$$

where $\alpha$ is the steering angle (0 for fixed wheel), $\beta$ is the wheel mounting angle, and $l$ is the distance from robot center to wheel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Rolling constraint explanation (2 marks) + Sliding constraint explanation (2 marks) + Matrix definitions (3 marks).

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1:** Constraint Matrix for Wheel 1 ($\beta_1 = 0°$, fixed, $\alpha = 0$):

$$J_1(\beta_1) = [\sin(0), -\cos(0), -l\cos(0)] = [0, -1, -l]$$

$$J_2(\beta_1) = [\cos(0), \sin(0), l\sin(0)] = [1, 0, 0]$$

**Step 2:** Constraint Matrix for Wheel 2 ($\beta_2 = 180°$, fixed, $\alpha = 0$):

$$J_1(\beta_2) = [\sin(180°), -\cos(180°), -l\cos(180°)] = [0, 1, l]$$

$$J_2(\beta_2) = [\cos(180°), \sin(180°), l\sin(180°)] = [-1, 0, 0]$$

**Step 3:** Stack all four constraints into a combined matrix $C(\beta) = [J_1(\beta_1); J_2(\beta_1); J_1(\beta_2); J_2(\beta_2)]$:

$$C(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & -l \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & l \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Mobility Condition:

The system is mobile if and only if the **rank of the combined constraint matrix is strictly less than 3**. From the matrix, the rank of the $J_2$ block alone is already 2 (rows 2 and 4 are linearly independent), so the rank of $C(\beta) = 4$, but the *effective rank* constraining the motion is 2. This means the robot has **one degree of mobility** (can move forward/backward) and is **non-holonomic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (Part b):** Wheel 1 matrices (1.5 marks) + Wheel 2 matrices (1.5 marks) + Combined matrix (2 marks) + Mobility condition (2 marks).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Forward Kinematics Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$** — Many students write the FKM as $\dot{\xi}_I = [v, 0, \omega]^T$ which is in the *robot frame*, not the inertial frame. Always apply $R(\theta)$. **(Lose 2-3 marks)**
>
> 2. **Sign error in angular velocity** — The convention $\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\dot{\phi}_r - \dot{\phi}_l)$ assumes counter-clockwise positive. Mixing up right/left will reverse the rotation. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Confusing "configuration" with "posture"** — Posture is $(x, y, \theta)$; configuration may also include joint angles for manipulator arms (not relevant for mobile robots). **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** — KTU 2024 strictly follows NEP 2020 outcome-based evaluation. Assumptions like "no wheel slip", "flat ground", "rigid body" must be explicitly listed. **(Lose 1-2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Skipping the Jacobian matrix derivation** — When asked to "formulate" or "derive", examiners expect the matrix form $J_1(\beta) R(\theta) \dot{\xi}_I = r\dot{\phi}$, not just the final scalar equations. **(Lose 2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Posture** of a 2D mobile robot = $\xi_I = [x, y, \theta]^T$ (**3 DOF**).
- **Forward Kinematic Model** = $\dot{\xi}_I = R(\theta) \cdot \dot{\xi}_R$ — the *core equation* of Module 1.
- **Rotation matrix** $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- **Two fundamental wheel constraints**: **Rolling** ($J_1$) and **Sliding** ($J_2$).
- **Differential Drive FKM** : $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ with $v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_r + \dot{\phi}_l)$ and $\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\dot{\phi}_r - \dot{\phi}_l)$.
- **Non-holonomic** when control inputs $<$ DOF (e.g., 2 inputs for 3 DOF in differential drive).
- **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is the unique point about which the robot rotates at any instant.
- **Synchronous drive** : all wheels steer together; separate motor controls translation and rotation.
- **Ackermann steering** : turning radius $R_c = L/\tan\delta$ where $\delta$ is the steering angle.
- **Mobility condition** : $\text{rank}(C(\beta)) < 3$ for the robot to be mobile.
- **Degrees of Mobility (M), Steerability (S), and Maneuverability** are key KTU 2024 derived concepts.
- **Assumptions checklist** for every FKM derivation: flat ground, no slip, rigid body, pure rolling.
- **Units to remember** : $r, L$ in meters; $\phi$ in rad; $v$ in m/s; $\omega$ in rad/s.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheel Kinematic Constraints for Fixed Standard Wheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wheel Kinematic Constraints for Fixed Standard Wheel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Fixed Standard Wheel** is a conventional, non-deformable, non-steerable wheel rigidly attached to the robot chassis. Its plane of rotation is fixed relative to the robot frame, and the wheel can rotate freely only about its horizontal axle. Because the wheel cannot steer, the direction of motion it permits (and restricts) is permanently locked to the chassis geometry at the time of design.

For a wheel attached at chassis coordinate $(x_b, y_b)$, the orientation of the wheel plane is described by the **fixed mounting angle** $\alpha$, measured from the robot's longitudinal $x$-axis to the wheel plane. The **steering angle** $\beta$ (the angle between the wheel axle and the chassis $x$-axis) is constrained as $\beta = 0$ for a fixed wheel, since the wheel cannot pivot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):**
> A Fixed Standard Wheel imposes **two independent kinematic constraints** simultaneously:
> 1. **Rolling Constraint** — the wheel rolls without slipping at the contact point.
> 2. **Sliding (Lateral) Constraint** — the contact point has no velocity component along the wheel plane direction perpendicular to the axle.
> Because both constraints are active, the fixed standard wheel restricts the robot's instantaneous motion to a **single degree of freedom** in the plane (pure rolling along a circular arc).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Shopping Cart Wheel:**
> Imagine pushing a shopping cart whose front wheels are locked in a straight-ahead position (they cannot turn). If you try to push the cart sideways, the locked front wheels skid violently and refuse to move. The cart can only roll forward or backward in the direction the locked wheels point. This is exactly the behaviour of a **fixed standard wheel** on a mobile robot — it refuses all motion except rolling along its own plane. If the robot wants to turn, the entire chassis must rotate; the fixed wheel can never "lean into" the turn on its own.

## 1.3 Physical Parameters and Constants

The following standard parameters govern the analysis:

- $(x, y, \theta)$ — pose of the robot in the world (inertial) frame.
- $(x_b, y_b)$ — coordinates of the wheel contact point in the **robot frame**.
- $\alpha$ — fixed angle between the wheel plane and the robot's $x$-axis (**constant**, in **radians**).
- $\beta = 0$ — steering angle of the wheel (**fixed**, equal to zero for a fixed standard wheel).
- $r$ — wheel radius (typically in **metres** or **millimetres**).
- $l$ — perpendicular distance from the robot centre to the wheel contact point.
- $\dot{\phi}$ — wheel rotation rate (in **rad/s**).
- $\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}$ — linear and angular velocities of the robot chassis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:**
> The "fixedness" of the wheel simplifies the mathematical model dramatically by eliminating $\beta$ and $\dot{\beta}$ from the constraint equations. However, this same rigidity is what makes fixed standard wheels **inferior to steered or Swedish wheels in manoeuvrability** — a robot with only fixed standard wheels cannot translate laterally (it cannot crab-walk).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition — Why Two Constraints?

A wheel rolling on the ground has a contact point $P$ that must remain instantaneously **stationary** with respect to the ground. However, the velocity at $P$ has two independent components in the plane:

1. The component **perpendicular to the wheel plane** (along the rolling direction) must be zero so that the wheel does not slide forward/backward without rotating.
2. The component **parallel to the wheel plane** (perpendicular to the axle) must also be zero so that the wheel does not skid sideways.

For a **steered** wheel, the second constraint can be relaxed by pivoting the wheel to align with the velocity vector. For a **fixed** wheel, the wheel cannot reorient itself, so **both constraints must be satisfied** simultaneously. This is the core reason a fixed standard wheel is the most restrictive type of wheel in mobile robotics.

## 2.2 Coordinate System and Sign Convention

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| World Frame $(X_I, Y_I)$ | Inertial reference, fixed to the ground |
| Robot Frame $(X_R, Y_R)$ | Origin at robot centre, rotates with chassis |
| $\theta$ | Orientation of $X_R$ w.r.t. $X_I$ (positive counter-clockwise) |
| $\alpha$ | Wheel mounting angle, fixed in robot frame |
| $\beta$ | Steering angle, $\beta = 0$ for fixed wheel |
| $r$ | Wheel radius |
| $l$ | Perpendicular distance from robot centre to contact point |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\beta = 0$ | Fixed wheel condition (no steering) | rad |
| 2 | $\alpha = \text{const}$ | Mounting angle is rigidly fixed to chassis | rad |
| 3 | $\cos(\alpha)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha)\dot{y} - r\dot{\phi} = 0$ | **Rolling constraint** for fixed standard wheel | m/s |
| 4 | $-\sin(\alpha)\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha)\dot{y} - l\dot{\theta} = 0$ | **Sliding (lateral) constraint** for fixed standard wheel | m/s |
| 5 | $\mathbf{J}_1 = [\cos(\alpha),\ \sin(\alpha),\ -l\sin(0),\ -r] = [\cos\alpha,\ \sin\alpha,\ 0,\ -r]$ | Rolling constraint Jacobian row | — |
| 6 | $\mathbf{J}_2 = [-\sin(\alpha),\ \cos(\alpha),\ -l\cos(0),\ 0] = [-\sin\alpha,\ \cos\alpha,\ -l,\ 0]$ | Sliding constraint Jacobian row | — |
| 7 | $R_{\text{ICR}} = \dfrac{\dot{\phi}\,r}{\dot{\theta}}$ | Instantaneous radius of rotation (if $\dot{\theta}\neq 0$) | m |
| 8 | $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{\cos(\alpha)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha)\dot{y}}{l}$ | Angular velocity from sliding constraint | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These two constraints are the building blocks of the robot's full kinematic Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}(\beta)$ used in trajectory planning, odometry, and motion control of differential-drive, tricycle, and Ackermann-steered platforms.

## 2.4 Geometric Interpretation

The two constraint equations can be interpreted geometrically:

- The **sliding constraint** forces the velocity at the contact point to be collinear with the wheel plane. This implies the **Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR)** lies somewhere on the line through the contact point perpendicular to the wheel plane (extended infinitely in both directions).
- The **rolling constraint** then locks the wheel's rotational rate $\dot{\phi}$ to the forward rolling speed $v_r = \cos(\alpha)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha)\dot{y}$ via $r\dot{\phi} = v_r$.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick mental check:** If the wheel plane is aligned with the robot's $x$-axis ($\alpha = 0$), the constraints reduce to $\dot{x} - r\dot{\phi} = 0$ (no slippage in rolling) and $\dot{y} - l\dot{\theta} = 0$ (no lateral slip). The first says the robot's forward speed equals the wheel's rolling speed; the second says the lateral velocity at the wheel contact equals the tangential velocity due to chassis rotation — a clean, intuitive result.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setup — Position of the Contact Point in the World Frame

Let the robot centre be at $(x, y, \theta)$ in the world frame. The wheel contact point $P$ has coordinates $(x_b, y_b)$ in the robot frame. The position of $P$ in the world frame is obtained by applying a 2D rotation and translation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_P &= x + x_b \cos(\theta) - y_b \sin(\theta) \\
y_P &= y + x_b \sin(\theta) + y_b \cos(\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating both equations with respect to time gives the velocity of the contact point $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_P &= \dot{x} - x_b \sin(\theta)\dot{\theta} - y_b \cos(\theta)\dot{\theta} \\
\dot{y}_P &= \dot{y} + x_b \cos(\theta)\dot{\theta} - y_b \sin(\theta)\dot{\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

These can be written compactly using a rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}(\theta)$ acting on the wheel-frame coordinates.

## 3.2 Direction of the Wheel Plane in the World Frame

The wheel plane is oriented at angle $\alpha$ in the robot frame and at angle $(\alpha + \theta)$ in the world frame. A unit vector along the wheel plane (direction of rolling) is:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{roll}} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\alpha + \theta) \\ \sin(\alpha + \theta) \end{bmatrix}
$$

A unit vector perpendicular to the wheel plane (direction of sliding / axle direction) is:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{slide}} = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin(\alpha + \theta) \\ \cos(\alpha + \theta) \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Rolling Constraint

The component of the contact-point velocity along the wheel plane must equal the wheel's tangential rolling speed $r\dot{\phi}$ (so that pure rolling is achieved). Projecting the contact velocity onto $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{roll}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{roll}} &= \dot{x}_P \cos(\alpha + \theta) + \dot{y}_P \sin(\alpha + \theta) \\
&= r \dot{\phi}
\end{aligned}
$$

After substituting the expressions for $\dot{x}_P$ and $\dot{y}_P$ and simplifying (using the fact that the terms $x_b, y_b$ combine to give a steering-angle-dependent contribution), the **general rolling constraint for a steered standard wheel** is:

$$
\cos(\alpha + \beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + l\sin(\beta)\dot{\theta} - r\dot{\phi} = 0
$$

For a **fixed standard wheel**, $\beta = 0$ and therefore $\sin(\beta) = 0$. Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\cos(\alpha)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha)\dot{y} - r\dot{\phi} = 0}
$$

This is the **rolling constraint** for a fixed standard wheel.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Sliding Constraint

The component of the contact-point velocity **perpendicular** to the wheel plane must be zero (no slipping sideways). Projecting onto $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{slide}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{slide}} &= -\dot{x}_P \sin(\alpha + \theta) + \dot{y}_P \cos(\alpha + \theta) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

After substituting and simplifying, the **general sliding constraint for a steered standard wheel** is:

$$
-\sin(\alpha + \beta)\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha + \beta)\dot{y} - l\cos(\beta)\dot{\theta} = 0
$$

For a **fixed standard wheel**, $\beta = 0$ and therefore $\cos(\beta) = 1$. Substituting:

$$
\boxed{-\sin(\alpha)\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha)\dot{y} - l\dot{\theta} = 0}
$$

This is the **sliding (lateral) constraint** for a fixed standard wheel.

## 3.5 Combined Matrix Form

Combining both constraints into the canonical KTU form $\mathbf{J}(\beta)\ \boldsymbol{\xi} = 0$ where $\boldsymbol{\xi} = [\dot{x},\ \dot{y},\ \dot{\theta},\ \dot{\phi}]^{T}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \cos(\alpha) & \sin(\alpha) & 0 & -r \\ -\sin(\alpha) & \cos(\alpha) & -l & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\phi} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matrix-Vector Note for KTU Exam:**
> The Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}(\beta)$ has dimensions $2 \times 4$ (two constraints, four velocity variables). It is a **rank-2** matrix (assuming $\alpha$ is not a degenerate angle), which means the **null space has dimension $4 - 2 = 2$** — i.e., the fixed standard wheel leaves the robot with **2 degrees of freedom** in its instantaneous motion (e.g., forward speed + steering-related DOF for a multi-wheel system).

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A robot has a fixed standard wheel mounted at $\alpha = 90^{\circ}$ (wheel plane perpendicular to robot's $x$-axis), at distance $l = 0.3$ m from the robot centre. The wheel radius is $r = 0.05$ m. The robot is moving with $\dot{x} = 0.1$ m/s, $\dot{y} = 0$ m/s, $\dot{\theta} = 0$ rad/s. Find the required wheel rotation rate.

**Solution:**

Substituting into the rolling constraint with $\alpha = 90^{\circ}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos(90^{\circ})\dot{x} + \sin(90^{\circ})\dot{y} - r\dot{\phi} &= 0 \\
(0)(0.1) + (1)(0) - 0.05\,\dot{\phi} &= 0 \\
-0.05\,\dot{\phi} &= 0 \\
\dot{\phi} &= 0\ \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: With $\alpha = 90^{\circ}$, the wheel plane is sideways; the rolling direction is along the robot's $y$-axis. Since the robot is moving along $x$, no rolling is induced. ✓

**Substitution Test:** Let us verify the sliding constraint with $\dot{x} = 0.1$ m/s, $\alpha = 90^{\circ}$, $l = 0.3$ m, $\dot{\theta} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\sin(90^{\circ})(0.1) + \cos(90^{\circ})(0) - 0.3(0) &= -0.1 \neq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **inconsistent** — meaning a robot with a single fixed wheel at $\alpha = 90^{\circ}$ cannot move purely in the $x$-direction. The sliding constraint enforces a coupled relationship between $\dot{x}$, $\dot{y}$, and $\dot{\theta}$. This illustrates the restrictiveness of the fixed standard wheel.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Setup — Fixed Standard Wheel on a Robot Chassis

The following Mermaid block renders the geometric configuration of a fixed standard wheel mounted on a robot chassis, showing the relevant vectors, angles, and the contact point $P$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef frameStyle fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wheelStyle fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pointStyle fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef vectorStyle fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    ICR["ICR on line through P perpendicular to wheel plane"]:::vectorStyle
    FrameX["Robot X-axis XR"]:::frameStyle
    FrameY["Robot Y-axis YR"]:::frameStyle
    WorldX["World X-axis XI"]:::frameStyle
    WorldY["World Y-axis YI"]:::frameStyle
    RobotCenter["Robot Centre origin R"]:::pointStyle
    ContactP["Contact Point P on ground"]:::pointStyle
    Wheel["Fixed Standard Wheel plane at angle alpha"]:::wheelStyle
    Axle["Wheel Axle horizontal direction"]:::wheelStyle
    RollDir["Rolling direction unit vector u_roll"]:::vectorStyle
    SlideDir["Sliding direction unit vector u_slide"]:::vectorStyle
    AlphaAng["alpha equals mounting angle constant"]:::frameStyle
    BetaAng["beta equals 0 for fixed wheel"]:::frameStyle
    Rdot["r equals wheel radius"]:::wheelStyle
    Ldist["l equals distance from R to P"]:::vectorStyle

    RobotCenter --> FrameX
    RobotCenter --> FrameY
    RobotCenter --> Wheel
    Wheel --> ContactP
    Wheel --> Axle
    Wheel --> RollDir
    Wheel --> SlideDir
    RobotCenter --> Ldist
    Ldist --> ContactP
    ContactP --> ICR
    Wheel --> AlphaAng
    Wheel --> BetaAng
    Wheel --> Rdot
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Constraint Enforcement Flow

The following Mermaid block depicts the functional flow by which the two kinematic constraints (rolling and sliding) are enforced on the robot's velocity state vector.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef inputStyle fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procStyle fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef constraintStyle fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    VelState["Velocity State Vector xi equals xdot ydot thetadot phidot"]:::inputStyle
    MountAngle["Mounting Angle alpha fixed constant"]:::inputStyle
    SteerAngle["Steering Angle beta equals 0 fixed"]:::inputStyle
    WheelRadius["Wheel Radius r"]:::inputStyle
    DistL["Distance l from centre to contact P"]:::inputStyle

    J1Compute["Compute Rolling Constraint Row J1 equals cosA sinA 0 minus r"]:::procStyle
    J2Compute["Compute Sliding Constraint Row J2 equals minus sinA cosA minus l 0"]:::procStyle
    JbetaBuild["Build 2x4 Jacobian J of beta with beta equals 0"]:::procStyle

    RollingEq["Rolling Equation cosA xdot plus sinA ydot minus r phidot equals 0"]:::constraintStyle
    SlidingEq["Sliding Equation minus sinA xdot plus cosA ydot minus l thetadot equals 0"]:::constraintStyle

    Combine["Stack J1 and J2 into 2x4 matrix J"]:::procStyle
    NullSpace["Null Space of J gives admissible motion directions"]:::outputStyle
    ICRloc["ICR lies on line through P perpendicular to wheel plane"]:::outputStyle

    VelState --> J1Compute
    MountAngle --> J1Compute
    SteerAngle --> J1Compute
    WheelRadius --> J1Compute

    VelState --> J2Compute
    MountAngle --> J2Compute
    SteerAngle --> J2Compute
    DistL --> J2Compute

    J1Compute --> Combine
    J2Compute --> Combine
    J1Compute --> RollingEq
    J2Compute --> SlidingEq
    Combine --> NullSpace
    Combine --> ICRloc
```

## 4.3 Interpretation of the Block Diagram

- The **Velocity State Vector** $\boldsymbol{\xi} = [\dot{x},\ \dot{y},\ \dot{\theta},\ \dot{\phi}]^{T}$ is the unknown.
- The **fixed geometric parameters** ($\alpha$, $\beta = 0$, $r$, $l$) feed into the computation of the two Jacobian rows $\mathbf{J}_1$ and $\mathbf{J}_2$.
- The **rolling equation** enforces the no-slip-in-rolling-direction condition.
- The **sliding equation** enforces the no-lateral-slip condition.
- The **null space** of the combined $2 \times 4$ matrix represents the set of all admissible robot motions consistent with both constraints.

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Reminder for Exam:** Whenever you draw a fixed standard wheel in your answer sheet, always mark the **wheel plane**, the **axle direction** (perpendicular to wheel plane), the **contact point $P$**, the **mounting angle $\alpha$**, and the **perpendicular distance $l$** from the robot centre. KTU examiners explicitly look for these labels to award full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
**"Define a fixed standard wheel. Why does it impose two kinematic constraints on a mobile robot?"**

**Model Answer:**

A **fixed standard wheel** is a conventional rigid wheel that is rigidly attached to the robot chassis, with its plane of rotation fixed relative to the robot frame. The wheel can rotate freely only about its horizontal axle but cannot pivot (steer) about a vertical axis.

It imposes **two kinematic constraints** because the wheel cannot reorient itself to accommodate lateral motion:
1. **Rolling constraint** — the contact point must have no velocity component perpendicular to the wheel plane (no skidding along the rolling direction).
2. **Sliding (lateral) constraint** — the contact point must have no velocity component parallel to the wheel plane (no sideways slipping).

Since both must hold simultaneously, the wheel restricts the robot's motion to a single permitted direction at any instant.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
**"State the two kinematic constraint equations for a fixed standard wheel attached to a mobile robot. Define each term."**

**Model Answer:**

The two constraints are:

1. **Rolling constraint:** $\cos(\alpha)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha)\dot{y} - r\dot{\phi} = 0$

2. **Sliding constraint:** $-\sin(\alpha)\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha)\dot{y} - l\dot{\theta} = 0$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = fixed mounting angle of the wheel plane w.r.t. the robot's $x$-axis
- $r$ = radius of the wheel
- $l$ = perpendicular distance from the robot centre to the contact point
- $\dot{x},\ \dot{y}$ = linear velocities of the robot centre
- $\dot{\theta}$ = angular velocity of the robot chassis
- $\dot{\phi}$ = rotation rate of the wheel about its axle

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the **rolling constraint** and the **sliding (lateral) constraint** for a fixed standard wheel attached to a mobile robot. Clearly state the assumptions, coordinate system, and the meaning of each variable. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive mobile robot has two fixed standard wheels mounted on a common axle of length $2l = 0.5$ m. The wheel radius is $r = 0.1$ m. Both wheels are aligned with the robot's $x$-axis ($\alpha = 0$). The right wheel rotates at $\dot{\phi}_R = 5$ rad/s and the left wheel at $\dot{\phi}_L = 3$ rad/s. Compute:
- (i) The linear velocity $\dot{x}$ of the robot centre.
- (ii) The angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$ of the robot.
- (iii) The radius of curvature $R$ of the robot's path. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Coordinate System:**
World frame $(X_I, Y_I)$ is inertial. Robot frame $(X_R, Y_R)$ is centred at the robot, with orientation $\theta$ w.r.t. world. The wheel contact point $P$ is at $(x_b, y_b)$ in the robot frame.

**Step 2 — Velocity of $P$ in World Frame:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_P &= \dot{x} - x_b \sin(\theta)\dot{\theta} - y_b \cos(\theta)\dot{\theta} \\
\dot{y}_P &= \dot{y} + x_b \cos(\theta)\dot{\theta} - y_b \sin(\theta)\dot{\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Unit Vectors:**
Rolling direction: $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{roll}} = [\cos(\alpha+\theta),\ \sin(\alpha+\theta)]^{T}$
Sliding direction: $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{slide}} = [-\sin(\alpha+\theta),\ \cos(\alpha+\theta)]^{T}$

**Step 4 — Rolling Constraint (general, then $\beta = 0$):**
The component of $\vec{v}_P$ along $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{roll}}$ must equal $r\dot{\phi}$:

$$
\cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y} + l\sin(\beta)\dot{\theta} - r\dot{\phi} = 0
$$

Setting $\beta = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\cos(\alpha)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha)\dot{y} - r\dot{\phi} = 0}
$$

**Step 5 — Sliding Constraint (general, then $\beta = 0$):**
The component of $\vec{v}_P$ along $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{slide}}$ must be zero:

$$
-\sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y} - l\cos(\beta)\dot{\theta} = 0
$$

Setting $\beta = 0$:

$$
\boxed{-\sin(\alpha)\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha)\dot{y} - l\dot{\theta} = 0}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\alpha = 0$, $l = 0.25$ m, $r = 0.1$ m, $\dot{\phi}_R = 5$ rad/s, $\dot{\phi}_L = 3$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Rolling Constraints for the Two Wheels:**

For each wheel, $\alpha = 0$:

$$
\cos(0)\dot{x} + \sin(0)\dot{y} - r\dot{\phi} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot{x} = r\dot{\phi}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\dot{x}$ from each wheel and Average (or use the standard differential-drive formula):**

From the right wheel: $\dot{x} = r\dot{\phi}_R = 0.1 \times 5 = 0.5$ m/s
From the left wheel: $\dot{x} = r\dot{\phi}_L = 0.1 \times 3 = 0.3$ m/s

The forward velocity is the **average** of the two rolling speeds (since the wheels are on a rigid axle):

$$
\dot{x} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)}{2} = \frac{0.1(5 + 3)}{2} = \frac{0.8}{2} = 0.4\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\dot{\theta}$ using the sliding constraint or differential-drive formula:**

The angular velocity is related to the **difference** of wheel speeds:

$$
\dot{\theta} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{2l} = \frac{0.1(5 - 3)}{2 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.2}{0.5} = 0.4\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Radius of Curvature:**

$$
R = \frac{\dot{x}}{\dot{\theta}} = \frac{0.4}{0.4} = 1.0\ \text{m}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\dot{x} = 0.4$ m/s
- (ii) $\dot{\theta} = 0.4$ rad/s
- (iii) $R = 1.0$ m

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the geometric meaning of the **rolling** and **sliding** constraints for a fixed standard wheel. Show that both constraints arise from the requirement that the wheel contact point be **instantaneously stationary** with respect to the ground. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fixed standard wheel is mounted on a robot chassis with $\alpha = 45^{\circ}$, $l = 0.4$ m, $r = 0.08$ m. The robot moves with $\dot{x} = 0.2$ m/s and $\dot{\theta} = 0.1$ rad/s. Determine:
- (i) The wheel rotation rate $\dot{\phi}$.
- (ii) The lateral velocity $\dot{y}$.
- (iii) Verify whether the ICR lies on the line through the contact point perpendicular to the wheel plane. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Geometric Explanation (7 Marks)

**Diagram Description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
- Draw a top-view of a circular wheel in contact with the ground at point $P$.
- Mark the wheel plane, the axle direction (perpendicular to wheel plane), the mounting angle $\alpha$ from the robot's $x$-axis, the distance $l$ from the robot centre to $P$, and the radius $r$.
- Show the unit vectors $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{roll}}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{slide}}$ at $P$.
- Indicate the **line of possible ICR locations** passing through $P$ and perpendicular to the wheel plane.

**Geometric Meaning:**

1. **Rolling constraint:** The velocity of the contact point $P$ along the wheel plane (in the direction of rolling, $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{roll}}$) must equal the tangential speed of the wheel surface, $r\dot{\phi}$. If this were violated, the wheel would slide longitudinally without rolling (or skid to a halt).

2. **Sliding constraint:** The velocity of the contact point $P$ perpendicular to the wheel plane (along $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{slide}}$) must be **zero**. If this were violated, the wheel would skid sideways across the ground.

3. **Stationarity of $P$:** Both constraints together ensure that $\vec{v}_P \cdot \hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{roll}} = r\dot{\phi}$ and $\vec{v}_P \cdot \hat{\mathbf{u}}_{\text{slide}} = 0$. Geometrically, this means the velocity of $P$ (if the wheel were free to slip) is entirely converted into rolling. The contact point is **instantaneously stationary** in the ground frame — the defining condition of pure rolling.

4. **ICR Interpretation:** The ICR must lie on the line through $P$ perpendicular to the wheel plane, at a distance $R = r\dot{\phi}/\dot{\theta}$ from $P$ (when $\dot{\theta} \neq 0$).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\alpha = 45^{\circ}$, $l = 0.4$ m, $r = 0.08$ m, $\dot{x} = 0.2$ m/s, $\dot{\theta} = 0.1$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Use the Sliding Constraint to find $\dot{y}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\sin(\alpha)\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha)\dot{y} - l\dot{\theta} &= 0 \\
-\sin(45^{\circ})(0.2) + \cos(45^{\circ})\dot{y} - 0.4(0.1) &= 0 \\
-(0.7071)(0.2) + (0.7071)\dot{y} - 0.04 &= 0 \\
-0.1414 + 0.7071\,\dot{y} - 0.04 &= 0 \\
0.7071\,\dot{y} &= 0.1814 \\
\dot{y} &= 0.2566\ \text{m/s} \approx 0.257\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the Rolling Constraint to find $\dot{\phi}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos(\alpha)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha)\dot{y} - r\dot{\phi} &= 0 \\
(0.7071)(0.2) + (0.7071)(0.2566) - 0.08\,\dot{\phi} &= 0 \\
0.1414 + 0.1814 - 0.08\,\dot{\phi} &= 0 \\
0.3228 &= 0.08\,\dot{\phi} \\
\dot{\phi} &= 4.035\ \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify ICR Location:**

The velocity of the contact point $P$ in the world frame must be perpendicular to the line from the ICR to $P$. Equivalently, the ICR is located on the line through $P$ perpendicular to the wheel plane. The wheel plane direction in the world frame is $\alpha + \theta$ (where $\theta$ is the robot's orientation). The ICR is offset from $P$ by:

$$
R = \frac{r\dot{\phi}}{\dot{\theta}} = \frac{0.08 \times 4.035}{0.1} = 3.228\ \text{m}
$$

This $R$ is the **perpendicular distance** from the ICR to $P$, along the line perpendicular to the wheel plane through $P$. The ICR exists at distance $3.228$ m from the contact point, along this perpendicular line. This confirms the geometric interpretation.

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\dot{\phi} \approx 4.04$ rad/s
- (ii) $\dot{y} \approx 0.257$ m/s
- (iii) ICR is at perpendicular distance $R = 3.228$ m from $P$ along the line perpendicular to the wheel plane. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $l\sin(\beta)$ and $l\cos(\beta)$ terms:** When deriving the general constraint and then specialising to a fixed wheel, many students drop the $\beta$-dependent terms incorrectly. The correct procedure is to first write the general constraint (with $\beta$), **then substitute** $\beta = 0$. A common error is writing the constraint with $l\dot{\theta}$ in the rolling constraint too — this is wrong; the rolling constraint has no $l\dot{\theta}$ term when $\beta = 0$.
>
> 2. **Sign convention errors:** The sliding constraint's sign on $l\dot{\theta}$ depends on the chosen convention (counter-clockwise positive). KTU accepts both conventions, but you **must** state your convention explicitly. Inconsistent signs across sub-parts of the same question will be penalised.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\beta$:** $\alpha$ is the **mounting angle** (fixed), $\beta$ is the **steering angle** (variable, = 0 for fixed). Examiners often test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers:** Always include units (m/s, rad/s, m) in the final boxed answer. A numerical answer without units typically loses **0.5 mark**.
>
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in long-answer derivations:** A 7-mark derivation question without a labelled diagram typically caps at 5–6 marks. Always draw a free-body / kinematic diagram showing the wheel, contact point, and coordinate frames.
>
> 6. **Not verifying the ICR condition:** When asked to "verify" or "show" something, you must explicitly write the verification step. Merely stating the result without working loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Fixed Standard Wheel Constraints**

- **Wheel Classification:** Fixed standard wheels are the **most restrictive** wheel type — they impose both rolling AND sliding constraints, reducing the robot's instantaneous DOF significantly.
- **Steering Angle:** For a fixed wheel, $\beta = 0$ (no steering) — this is the defining property.
- **Mounting Angle:** $\alpha$ is the constant angle between the wheel plane and the robot's $x$-axis, fixed at design time.
- **Two Constraints (Memorise):**
  - **Rolling:** $\cos(\alpha)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha)\dot{y} - r\dot{\phi} = 0$
  - **Sliding:** $-\sin(\alpha)\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha)\dot{y} - l\dot{\theta} = 0$
- **Jacobian Form:** The constraint matrix is $2 \times 4$ (rank 2), leaving a **2-DOF null space** for the combined robot system.
- **ICR Location:** Always lies on the line through the contact point $P$ perpendicular to the wheel plane.
- **Manoeuvrability Implication:** A robot with only fixed standard wheels (no steering, no Swedish wheels) **cannot translate sideways** — it can only roll along arcs determined by the wheel geometry.
- **Sign Convention:** Always declare CCW-positive convention at the start of your answer.
- **Differential-Drive Special Case:** When $\alpha = 0$ and two fixed wheels are on a common axle of length $2l$, the standard formulas are:
  - $\dot{x} = \dfrac{r(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)}{2}$
  - $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{2l}$
  - $R = \dfrac{l(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)}{\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L}$
- **Compare With Steered Wheel:** A steered standard wheel (with $\beta \neq 0$) has the SAME rolling constraint formula but with $\beta$ included; the **sliding constraint is not imposed** because the wheel can reorient to align with velocity. This is a **favourite KTU comparison question**.
- **Unit Discipline:** $\dot{x}, \dot{y}$ in m/s; $\dot{\theta}, \dot{\phi}$ in rad/s; $r, l$ in m. Mixing units is a guaranteed mark deduction.
- **Diagrams Matter:** For 7-mark derivation questions, always include a labelled kinematic diagram — KTU examiners reserve 1–2 marks specifically for the diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steered Standard Wheel and Castor Wheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steered Standard Wheel and Castor Wheel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wheel Taxonomy in Mobile Robotics

In mobile robotics, the wheel is the most fundamental locomotion element. According to standard mobile robotics taxonomy (Siegwart, Nourbakhsh, and Scaramuzza framework), wheels are classified by their **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** and the **steering mechanism** used. The two most important non-holonomic wheel types in this hierarchy are the **Steered Standard Wheel** (active steering) and the **Castor Wheel** (passive steering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PBRUT504 - Module 1):**
> Wheel Types in Mobile Robots — Steered Standard Wheel, Castor Wheel, Swedish Wheel, and Spherical Wheel. The focus of this module is the geometric placement, constraints, and kinematic modelling of these wheels on a rigid robot chassis.

---

### 1.2 Steered Standard Wheel — Definition

A **Steered Standard Wheel** is a wheel that satisfies two conditions:

1. It is **rigid** (no deformation) and has a **fixed axle** through the wheel center.
2. The entire wheel-and-axle assembly is mounted on a **steering axis** that passes through the wheel center and is **vertical to the plane of the wheel**.

The wheel has **two independently controllable degrees of freedom**:
- **Rotation** about its own axle (driven by a drive motor).
- **Rotation** about the vertical steering axis (driven by a steering motor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A Steered Standard Wheel is a wheel whose center, denoted $C$, is mounted on a steering joint whose axis is perpendicular to the wheel plane and passes through $C$. Because the steering axis passes exactly through the contact point's vertical projection, the wheel is **steered about its own contact point**, allowing zero-offset steering — this is mathematically clean and avoids slip during turning.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine the **front wheel of a car or a bicycle**. When you turn the steering wheel, the entire wheel pivots about a vertical pin located *exactly at the wheel's center*. The wheel can spin (for moving forward) *and* swivel (for changing direction). The car's *rear wheels* on the other hand are *fixed* — they only spin. A Steered Standard Wheel is essentially a powered, actively-steered bicycle front wheel mounted on a robot.

---

### 1.3 Castor Wheel — Definition

A **Castor Wheel** (often shortened to *castor* or *passively steered wheel*) is a wheel that satisfies two conditions:

1. It is rigid and has a fixed axle through the wheel center.
2. The axle is supported by a **steering joint** whose **steering axis does NOT pass through the wheel center**. Instead, it is offset by a fixed distance (the **castor offset**, denoted $d$).

The castor wheel is **passively steered** — it has *no steering motor*. It freely rotates about its steering axis, following the direction of motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Castor Offset (d):** This is the horizontal distance between the steering axis and the wheel's vertical line through the contact point. A non-zero offset is what makes a wheel a *castor* rather than a steered standard wheel. If $d = 0$, the castor degenerates into a Steered Standard Wheel.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Look at the **swiveling wheels at the back of a shopping trolley** or the **rear caster wheel of an office chair**. These wheels are not driven and not actively steered — you push the chair, and the wheel "wobbles" or "swivels" on its own to align with the direction you're pushing. That little offset between the swivel pin and the wheel contact patch is exactly the *castor offset*. This is why a heavy office chair rolls so easily in any direction — the castors always self-align.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why Castor Wheels Are Used:** Castor wheels alone **cannot drive or steer a robot** because they have no actuated DOF for direction control. They are used **purely for static stability** (preventing the robot from tipping over) while the drive and steering come from other wheels. A typical 4-wheel mobile robot uses 2 driven + steered standard wheels at the front, and 2 passive castor wheels at the rear.

---

### 1.4 The Five Reference Frames (Foundation Concept)

Before any constraint equation is written, every wheel is described using **five reference points** (this is KTU's standard formalism):

| Symbol | Name | Description |
|:-------|:-----|:------------|
| $I$ | Inertial Frame | Fixed world frame $\{X_I, Y_I, Z_I\}$ |
| $R$ | Robot Frame | Frame attached to robot chassis $\{X_R, Y_R, Z_R\}$ |
| $M$ | Wheel Center | Geometric center $C$ of the wheel |
| $S$ | Steered Axis Point | Point where steering axis meets the ground plane |
| $P$ | Contact Point | Point where wheel touches the ground |

| Parameter | Meaning | Unit |
|:----------|:--------|:-----|
| $r$ | Wheel radius (ground to center $M$) | **m** (meters) |
| $l$ | Distance from robot center to wheel center $M$ | **m** |
| $\alpha$ | Angle between $X_R$ axis and the line $SM$ (wheel mounting orientation) | **rad** |
| $\beta$ | Steering angle (rotation about steering axis) | **rad** |
| $d$ | Castor offset (horizontal distance from steering axis to contact point) | **m** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Steered Standard Wheel** is a special case of the castor wheel where the castor offset $d = 0$. The two are mathematically unified by a single set of equations — this is one of the most elegant results in the Siegwart framework.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two Fundamental Constraints

Every wheel, regardless of type, must obey two physical laws when rolling on flat ground without slip:

1. **Rolling Constraint** — The component of the wheel's velocity *along the wheel plane* (the direction of forward rolling) must equal $r\dot\phi$, where $\dot\phi$ is the angular velocity of the wheel.
2. **Sliding Constraint** — The component of the wheel's velocity *perpendicular to the wheel plane* (sideways velocity) must equal zero for a non-steerable wheel. For steerable wheels, this direction *changes with the steering angle*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these constraints matter:** Together, these two constraints reduce the mobility of the robot. A robot with $N$ wheels in a 2D plane has at most 3 DOF (forward, sideways, rotation). Each constraint removes one DOF, so a robot with $N_F$ fixed standard wheels and $N_C$ castor wheels has:
> $$\text{Mobility (DOF)} = 3 - N_F - N_C$$
> For a typical differential drive (2 fixed standard wheels), DOF = $3 - 2 = 1$ (forward only — no sideways motion), which is *non-holonomic*.

---

### 2.2 Robot Configuration Vector

The robot's instantaneous state in the plane is described by the **pose vector**:
$$\xi_I = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

with **twist** (time derivative):
$$\dot\xi_I = \begin{bmatrix} \dot x \\ \dot y \\ \dot\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

The relationship between the **robot-frame twist** and the **world-frame twist** is given by the **rotation matrix** $R(\theta)$:
$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

so that
$$\dot\xi_R = R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I$$
This transformation is essential because all wheel constraints are written in the **robot frame**, but the robot moves in the **world frame**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### (A) Steered Standard Wheel ($d = 0$)

| # | Constraint | Equation | Description |
|:-:|:-----------|:---------|:------------|
| 1 | **Rolling** | $\left[\sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ -l\cos\beta\right] R(\theta)\dot\xi_I - r\dot\phi = 0$ | Forward rolling — wheel rotation $\dot\phi$ balances component along wheel plane |
| 2 | **Sliding** | $\left[\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ \sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -l\sin\beta\right] R(\theta)\dot\xi_I = 0$ | No sideways slip — perpendicular component must vanish |

#### (B) Castor Wheel ($d \neq 0$)

| # | Constraint | Equation | Description |
|:-:|:-----------|:---------|:------------|
| 3 | **Rolling** | $\left[\sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ (d\cos\beta) - l\cos\beta\right] R(\theta)\dot\xi_I - r\dot\phi = 0$ | Same as steered wheel but the perpendicular-arm term includes the offset $d$ |
| 4 | **Sliding** | $\left[\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ \sin(\alpha+\beta),\ (d\sin\beta) - l\sin\beta\right] R(\theta)\dot\xi_I = 0$ | Sideways slip must be zero, taking offset into account |

#### (C) Master Vector Form (Unified)

For **any** wheel in the general castor configuration, both constraints can be written compactly as:

$$J_{1}(\beta)\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I - r\dot\phi = 0 \quad \text{(rolling)}$$

$$J_{2}(\beta)\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I = 0 \quad \text{(sliding)}$$

where the row vectors are:
$$J_{1}(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \sin(\alpha+\beta) & -\cos(\alpha+\beta) & d\cos\beta - l\cos\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

$$J_{2}(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\alpha+\beta) & \sin(\alpha+\beta) & d\sin\beta - l\sin\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Trick:** Setting $d = 0$ in the castor equations above yields the steered standard wheel equations exactly. This is the **K-T-U** examiner's favourite "trick question" — *always check if the castor equations reduce correctly when $d = 0$*.

---

### 2.4 Why the Offset $d$ Changes the Physics

In a steered standard wheel, the steering axis passes through the contact point. So when the wheel rotates about this axis, the contact point does **not move** — it is the *instantaneous center of rotation* for the steering motion. This means steering alone does not produce any lateral slip of the contact patch.

In a castor wheel, the steering axis is *offset* from the contact point. So when the wheel swivels, the contact point **sweeps out a circle** of radius $d$ around the steering axis. This causes unavoidable *kinematic jitter* during turning, but in practice the offset allows the wheel to freely align with the motion vector (this is why shopping trolleys feel "loose" at the back).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Wheel Configuration | Why It Is Used |
|:------------|:--------------------|:---------------|
| **Autonomous Cars (Ackermann)** | 2 Steered Standard (front) + 2 Fixed Standard (rear) | Active steering + differential drive; satisfies Ackermann geometry for low-slip turning |
| **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** | 2 Steered Standard (driving) + 4 Castor (support) | Castors stabilize heavy payloads; steered wheels provide precision |
| **Differential-Drive Robots (e.g., Pioneer 3-DX)** | 2 Fixed Standard (driven) + 1 Rear Castor (or 2) | Simplest non-holonomic drive; castor prevents tipping |
| **Service Robots (Care-O-bot, Pepper)** | 4 Swedish + 2 Castor (Swiss-Mobility / Smoother) | Omni-directional motion; castor adds payload stability |
| **Forklifts & Material Handling** | Steered Standard (front) + Castor (rear) | Tight turning radius in industrial aisles |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Steered Standard Wheel Constraints

We will derive the **rolling constraint** and **sliding constraint** for a steered standard wheel placed on a rigid robot chassis. The derivation follows the classical kinematic approach.

#### Step 1: Set up the geometric model

Consider a wheel of radius $r$ whose mounting point $M$ (the wheel center) is located at distance $l$ from the robot center, oriented at angle $\alpha$ relative to the robot's longitudinal axis $X_R$. The wheel is steered through an angle $\beta$ about a vertical axis passing through $M$.

In the **robot frame**, the position of the wheel center $M$ is:
$$\vec{r}_{RM} = \begin{bmatrix} l\cos\alpha \\ l\sin\alpha \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The unit vector **along the wheel plane** (direction of forward rolling) after steering by $\beta$ is:
$$\hat{u}_{roll} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\alpha+\beta) \\ \sin(\alpha+\beta) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The unit vector **perpendicular to the wheel plane** (sideways direction) is:
$$\hat{u}_{slide} = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin(\alpha+\beta) \\ \cos(\alpha+\beta) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** These two unit vectors are orthogonal ($\hat{u}_{roll} \cdot \hat{u}_{slide} = 0$) and form a 2D basis in the wheel plane. The angle $(\alpha + \beta)$ is the sum of the *mounting angle* and the *steering angle*, because the steering angle $\beta$ rotates the wheel plane from its original orientation $\alpha$.

#### Step 2: Express the wheel center velocity in the robot frame

The velocity of any point $M$ on a rigid body undergoing planar motion is given by:
$$\vec{v}_M = \vec{v}_{O_R} + \vec\omega \times \vec{r}_{RM}$$

where $\vec{v}_{O_R} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_R \\ \dot{y}_R \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ is the velocity of the robot center in the robot frame (which equals $[\dot x, \dot y, 0]^T$ if we work in the inertial frame's projection), and the angular velocity is $\vec\omega = [0, 0, \dot\theta]^T$.

The cross product expands to:
$$\vec\omega \times \vec{r}_{RM} = \begin{bmatrix} -\dot\theta \cdot l\sin\alpha \\ \dot\theta \cdot l\cos\alpha \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

So the velocity of the wheel center is:
$$\vec{v}_M = \begin{bmatrix} \dot x - \dot\theta\, l\sin\alpha \\ \dot y + \dot\theta\, l\cos\alpha \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The cross product $\vec\omega \times \vec{r}_{RM}$ in 2D planar motion reduces to $[\dot\theta \cdot l\sin\alpha, -\dot\theta \cdot l\cos\alpha]$ with a sign flip; the explicit expansion is shown above.

#### Step 3: Apply the rolling constraint

Rolling without slipping means the velocity of the contact point $P$ along the wheel plane must equal $r\dot\phi$. The contact point is directly below $M$ by $r$, but the vertical component does not contribute to in-plane rolling. So we project $\vec{v}_M$ onto $\hat{u}_{roll}$ and set it equal to $r\dot\phi$:

$$\hat{u}_{roll} \cdot \vec{v}_M = r\dot\phi$$

Substituting:
$$(\cos(\alpha+\beta))(\dot x - \dot\theta l\sin\alpha) + (\sin(\alpha+\beta))(\dot y + \dot\theta l\cos\alpha) = r\dot\phi$$

Expanding and grouping terms:
$$\dot x \cos(\alpha+\beta) + \dot y \sin(\alpha+\beta) - \dot\theta l\sin\alpha\cos(\alpha+\beta) + \dot\theta l\cos\alpha\sin(\alpha+\beta) = r\dot\phi$$

The two $\dot\theta$ terms combine using the identity $\sin(A-B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$:
$$\dot\theta\, l \bigl[\cos\alpha\sin(\alpha+\beta) - \sin\alpha\cos(\alpha+\beta)\bigr] = \dot\theta\, l\, \sin\beta$$

Substituting:
$$\dot x \cos(\alpha+\beta) + \dot y \sin(\alpha+\beta) + \dot\theta\, l\sin\beta = r\dot\phi$$

Reordering as a row vector (rolling form):
$$\left[\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ \sin(\alpha+\beta),\ l\sin\beta\right]\begin{bmatrix}\dot x\\ \dot y\\ \dot\theta\end{bmatrix} = r\dot\phi$$

Now transforming to the **robot frame** (where the velocity vector is $\dot\xi_R = R(\theta)\dot\xi_I$), and noting that we use the alternative form $J_1 = [\sin(\alpha+\beta), -\cos(\alpha+\beta), -l\cos\beta]$ depending on the text's convention, we obtain the canonical KTU form:

$$\boxed{\;\left[\sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ -l\cos\beta\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I - r\dot\phi = 0\;}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The two sign conventions correspond to the two perpendicular directions in the wheel plane. Both forms are correct; the KTU 2024 syllabus follows the **Siegwart form** shown in the box, which uses $\sin$ on the $X_R$ component. The simpler form above with $\cos(\alpha+\beta)$ is equivalent up to a sign flip of $\beta$.

#### Step 4: Apply the sliding constraint

For a steered standard wheel, sliding perpendicular to the wheel plane must be zero. We project $\vec{v}_M$ onto $\hat{u}_{slide}$ and set it to zero:

$$\hat{u}_{slide} \cdot \vec{v}_M = 0$$

Substituting:
$$(-\sin(\alpha+\beta))(\dot x - \dot\theta l\sin\alpha) + (\cos(\alpha+\beta))(\dot y + \dot\theta l\cos\alpha) = 0$$

After similar trigonometric simplification (using $\cos(A+B) = \cos A\cos B - \sin A\sin B$):
$$-\dot x \sin(\alpha+\beta) + \dot y \cos(\alpha+\beta) - \dot\theta\, l\cos\beta = 0$$

In the KTU / Siegwart convention, this is rewritten as:
$$\boxed{\;\left[\sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ -l\cos\beta\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I = 0\;}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Both constraints have the *same* left-hand side row vector $J_1$ in the Siegwart convention — this is a signature property of the framework, and KTU examiners test it. The rolling equation *equals* $r\dot\phi$, while the sliding equation *equals* zero.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Castor Wheel Constraints (Offset Case)

For a castor wheel, the steering axis is offset by a horizontal distance $d$ from the contact point. The wheel center $M$ and the steering-axis intersection with the ground $S$ are now **different points**.

#### Step 1: Geometry with the offset

The position of the **steering axis ground intersection** $S$ relative to the robot center is:
$$\vec{r}_{RS} = \begin{bmatrix} (l-d)\cos\alpha \\ (l-d)\sin\alpha \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The position of the **wheel center** $M$ is at distance $l$ from the robot center along the same line:
$$\vec{r}_{RM} = \begin{bmatrix} l\cos\alpha \\ l\sin\alpha \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** When the wheel swivels (i.e., $\beta$ changes), the wheel center $M$ moves on a **circle of radius $d$** about the point $S$. This circular motion contributes an *additional velocity term* proportional to $d\dot\beta$ to the wheel center's velocity.

#### Step 2: Velocity of the wheel center including the swivel

The total velocity of $M$ has **two contributions**:
1. The **chassis motion** (as in the steered standard wheel case): $\vec{v}_{chassis}$ with magnitude components $\dot x, \dot y, \dot\theta$.
2. The **swivel-induced motion** about the steering axis $S$: this is a rotation about $S$ with angular rate $\dot\beta$ (passive), producing a velocity at $M$ perpendicular to $\overrightarrow{SM}$ with magnitude $d\dot\beta$.

In component form (after expansion similar to §3.1, but keeping the offset $d$ separate from $l$):
$$\vec{v}_M = \begin{bmatrix} \dot x - \dot\theta\, (l-d)\sin\alpha + d\dot\beta\cos(\alpha+\beta) \\ \dot y + \dot\theta\, (l-d)\cos\alpha + d\dot\beta\sin(\alpha+\beta) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 3: Rolling constraint for the castor wheel

Projecting $\vec{v}_M$ onto $\hat{u}_{roll}$ and equating to $r\dot\phi$, then using the same trigonometric identity $\sin\beta$ and $\cos\beta$ groupings as before, we obtain:

$$\left[\sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ d\cos\beta - l\cos\beta\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I = r\dot\phi$$

Rearranging:
$$\boxed{\;\left[\sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ d\cos\beta - l\cos\beta\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I - r\dot\phi = 0\;}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Compare this with the steered standard wheel rolling constraint — the only difference is the moment-arm term: $-l\cos\beta$ is replaced by $(d - l)\cos\beta$. Setting $d = 0$ recovers the steered standard wheel case exactly.

#### Step 4: Sliding constraint for the castor wheel

Projecting $\vec{v}_M$ onto $\hat{u}_{slide}$ and setting to zero:

$$\boxed{\;\left[\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ \sin(\alpha+\beta),\ d\sin\beta - l\sin\beta\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I = 0\;}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The sliding constraint vanishes *identically* when the wheel is **passively aligned** with the velocity (i.e., when $\beta$ is at the equilibrium steering angle). This is precisely what a castor wheel does physically — it self-aligns so that the rolling constraint is satisfied and sliding is zero. **The castor wheel does not need a motor to track this angle — physics (low-friction swivel joint) does it for free.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot has two steered standard wheels of radius **0.05 m** mounted at distance **0.3 m** from the robot center on either side, with $\alpha = 0$ and $\beta = 0$. Find the robot's forward velocity when the right wheel rotates at $\omega_R = 10$ rad/s and the left wheel rotates at $\omega_L = 8$ rad/s.

**Solution:**

For each wheel with $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = 0$, and $d = 0$:

- Rolling constraint: $\left[\sin 0,\ -\cos 0,\ -0.3\cos 0\right]\,\dot\xi_I = r\dot\phi$
- Simplifies to: $\left[0,\ -1,\ -0.3\right]\,\dot\xi_I = 0.05\,\dot\phi$

Working in the robot frame where the velocity is $v_x$ (forward) and $\omega$ (yaw):
$$-v_y - 0.3\,\omega = 0.05\,\dot\phi$$

For a non-steering vehicle, $v_y = 0$, so:
$$-0.3\,\omega = 0.05\,\dot\phi \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot\phi = -6\,\omega$$

This negative sign indicates the convention that the wheel on the right rotates *opposite* to the body's yaw to drive forward. Substituting $\dot\phi_R = -10$ rad/s and $\dot\phi_L = -8$ rad/s:

$$-0.3\,\omega = 0.05 \times (-10) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega = \frac{0.5}{0.3} = 1.667 \text{ rad/s (right)}$$

$$-0.3\,\omega = 0.05 \times (-8) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega = \frac{0.4}{0.3} = 1.333 \text{ rad/s (left)}$$

The two wheels must agree on $\omega$, which they don't — so the robot **turns**. The turning rate is:
$$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{2L} = \frac{0.05(10 - 8)}{2 \times 0.3} = \frac{0.1}{0.6} = 0.167 \text{ rad/s}$$

The forward velocity of the chassis center is:
$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.05(18)}{2} = 0.45 \text{ m/s}$$

> **Final Answer:** The robot moves forward at **0.45 m/s** and turns at **0.167 rad/s** (counter-clockwise, since the right wheel is faster).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Constraint Summary at a Glance

| Wheel Type | Rolling Constraint | Sliding Constraint | No. of Constraints | DOF Removed |
|:-----------|:-------------------|:-------------------|:------------------|:------------|
| **Fixed Standard** ($\beta=0$, no steering) | $J_1 \cdot R\dot\xi_I = r\dot\phi$ | $J_2 \cdot R\dot\xi_I = 0$ | 2 | 2 |
| **Steered Standard** ($d=0$, active $\beta$) | $J_1(\beta)\cdot R\dot\xi_I = r\dot\phi$ | $J_2(\beta)\cdot R\dot\xi_I = 0$ | 2 (both depend on $\beta$) | 2 |
| **Castor** ($d\neq 0$, passive $\beta$) | $J_1(\beta)\cdot R\dot\xi_I = r\dot\phi$ | $J_2(\beta)\cdot R\dot\xi_I = 0$ (auto-satisfied at equilibrium) | 2 (one is free, $\beta$ chooses itself) | 1 (effectively) |
| **Swedish / Omni** | 1 rolling only | N/A (free sliding by design) | 1 | 1 |
| **Spherical** | None (ball, no constraint) | None | 0 | 0 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Wheel Classification

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the **complete taxonomy** of mobile robot wheels and the position of our two focus types in the hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[ROBOT WHEEL TYPES] --> B1[STANDARD WHEELS]
    A0 --> B2[SPECIAL WHEELS]

    B1 --> C1[Fixed Standard]
    B1 --> C2[Steered Standard]
    B1 --> C3[Castor Wheel]

    C1 --> D1[No steering axis, single DOF spin]
    C2 --> D2[Steering axis through wheel center, two actuated DOF]
    C3 --> D3[Steering axis offset by distance d, passive swivel]

    B2 --> C4[Swedish Wheel]
    B2 --> C5[Spherical Wheel]
    B2 --> C6[Mecanum Wheel]

    C4 --> E1[Passive rollers around circumference, omnidirectional]
    C5 --> E2[Ball bearing, no constraints]
    C6 --> E3[Diagonal passive rollers, holonomic drive]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Geometric Parameters of a Steered Standard Wheel

The following diagram shows the **five reference points** and the **geometric parameters** of a single steered standard wheel on a robot chassis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph World[World Frame]
        WI[Inertial Frame I]
    end

    subgraph Robot[Robot Frame]
        WR[Robot Center R]
        WM[Wheel Center M]
        WP[Contact Point P]
        WS[Steering Axis Ground Point S]
    end

    WR -- distance l, angle alpha --> WM
    WM -- vertical drop r --> WP
    WR -- chassis orientation theta --> World
    WM -- steering rotation beta --> WS

    PARAM1[PARAM l distance from R to M]
    PARAM2[PARAM r wheel radius]
    PARAM3[PARAM alpha mounting angle]
    PARAM4[PARAM beta steering angle]
    PARAM5[PARAM d castor offset equals ZERO for steered standard]

    PARAM1 --> WM
    PARAM2 --> WP
    PARAM3 --> WM
    PARAM4 --> WS
    PARAM5 --> WM
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Castor Wheel Geometry with Offset $d \neq 0$

This diagram shows the **castor offset $d$** that distinguishes a castor wheel from a steered standard wheel.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R2[Castor Wheel Geometry]
        CR[Robot Center R]
        CS[Steering Axis Point S on ground]
        CM[Wheel Center M]
        CP[Contact Point P on ground]
    end

    CR -- distance l, angle alpha --> CM
    CR -- distance l minus d, angle alpha --> CS
    CM -- offset d along wheel axis --> CS
    CM -- vertical drop r --> CP

    PARAMC1[NOTE d is the horizontal distance between steering axis and contact point vertical]
    PARAMC2[NOTE When d equals ZERO, the castor becomes a steered standard wheel]
    PARAMC3[NOTE Beta is passive, the wheel auto-aligns to the motion vector]

    PARAMC1 --> CS
    PARAMC2 --> CM
    PARAMC3 --> CM
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture: How Wheel Constraints Shape Robot Motion

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **block-level functional flow** of how individual wheel constraints combine to produce the overall robot motion (mobility, steerability, and maneuverability).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Robot Pose x, y, theta in World Frame] --> ROT[Multiply by R of theta to get Robot Frame Pose]
    ROT --> WHEEL1[Steered Standard Wheel Left]
    ROT --> WHEEL2[Steered Standard Wheel Right]
    ROT --> WHEEL3[Castor Wheel Rear Left]
    ROT --> WHEEL4[Castor Wheel Rear Right]

    WHEEL1 --> EQ1[Rolling Eq 1 and Sliding Eq 1]
    WHEEL2 --> EQ2[Rolling Eq 2 and Sliding Eq 2]
    WHEEL3 --> EQ3[Rolling Eq 3, passive sliding]
    WHEEL4 --> EQ4[Rolling Eq 4, passive sliding]

    EQ1 --> SOLVER[Combine into system J times xi dot equals b]
    EQ2 --> SOLVER
    EQ3 --> SOLVER
    EQ4 --> SOLVER

    SOLVER --> MOB[Compute Mobility DOF equals 3 minus number of independent constraints]
    SOLVER --> CMD[Compute Wheel Commands phi dot and beta dot for desired motion]
    SOLVER --> PATH[Generate Feasible Paths subject to non-holonomic constraints]

    MOB --> OUT[Final Robot Capability Profile]
    CMD --> OUT
    PATH --> OUT
```

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Degeneration $d = 0$ Limit

The following diagram illustrates the **mathematical unification** — a castor wheel collapses into a steered standard wheel when the offset $d$ is set to zero.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CASTOR[Castor Wheel with d not equal to zero] -- limit d equals zero --> STEERED[Steered Standard Wheel]
    STEERED -- no steering motor --> FIXED[Fixed Standard Wheel]
    CASTOR -- passive free swivel and no drive --> SUPPORT[Stability Support Wheel]

    J1[Constraint J1 d cos beta minus l cos beta] -- d equals zero --> J1S[Constraint J1 minus l cos beta]
    J2[Constraint J2 d sin beta minus l sin beta] -- d equals zero --> J2S[Constraint J2 minus l sin beta]

    J1 --> CASTOR
    J2 --> CASTOR
    J1S --> STEERED
    J2S --> STEERED
```
