## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> *Differentiate between a Steered Standard Wheel and a Castor Wheel. Mention one practical application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Steered Standard Wheel | Castor Wheel |
|:--------|:-----------------------|:-------------|
| **Steering axis position** | Passes through the wheel center | Offset by distance $d$ from wheel center |
| **Steering** | **Active** — uses a steering motor | **Passive** — free swivel, no motor |
| **Drive** | Usually driven (has $\dot\phi$) | Usually undriven (support wheel) |
| **Use** | Ackermann-steered car front wheels | Office-chair / trolley wheels |
| **Offset $d$** | $d = 0$ | $d \neq 0$ |
| **Application example** | Front wheels of an autonomous car | Rear support wheels of a warehouse AGV |

**Key sentence (must be in answer):**
> *"A steered standard wheel has its steering axis passing through the wheel center ($d = 0$), whereas a castor wheel has a steering axis offset from the contact point by a non-zero distance $d$."* (1 mark for distinction + 1 mark for application + 1 mark for diagram/reference)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> *State the rolling and sliding constraints for a Steered Standard Wheel mounted at distance $l$ from the robot center, with mounting angle $\alpha$ and steering angle $\beta$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a steered standard wheel with wheel radius $r$ and robot pose $\xi_I = [x, y, \theta]^T$:

**Rolling Constraint:**
$$\left[\sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ -l\cos\beta\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I - r\dot\phi = 0$$

**Sliding Constraint:**
$$\left[\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ \sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -l\sin\beta\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I = 0$$

where $R(\theta)$ is the rotation matrix between robot frame and world frame, and $\dot\phi$ is the wheel's spin rate.

> **Mark Split (1 + 1 + 1):** Rolling equation (1 mark) + Sliding equation (1 mark) + Naming the rotation matrix and variables (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the rolling and sliding constraints for a Steered Standard Wheel placed on a rigid robot chassis. Clearly state the geometric parameters and the rotation matrix used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A differential-drive robot is fitted with two steered standard wheels of radius $r = 0.08$ m, mounted at $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = 0$, and $l = 0.25$ m on each side of the chassis. The right wheel spins at $\omega_R = 15$ rad/s and the left at $\omega_L = 12$ rad/s. Find the forward velocity and angular velocity of the robot. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):** Full Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Geometric Setup (1 mark)**

Define the parameters:
- $r$ = wheel radius
- $l$ = distance from robot center to wheel center
- $\alpha$ = mounting angle relative to $X_R$ axis
- $\beta$ = steering angle (rotation about vertical axis through wheel center)
- $\theta$ = chassis orientation in world frame

**Step 2: Velocity of Wheel Center (2 marks)**

The velocity of the wheel center in the **robot frame** is:
$$\vec{v}_M = \begin{bmatrix} \dot x - \dot\theta\,l\sin\alpha \\ \dot y + \dot\theta\,l\cos\alpha \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Rolling Constraint (2 marks)**

Projecting $\vec{v}_M$ onto the wheel plane unit vector (along the rolling direction) and equating to $r\dot\phi$:

$$\left[\sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ -l\cos\beta\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I - r\dot\phi = 0$$

**Step 4: Sliding Constraint (1 mark)**

Projecting $\vec{v}_M$ onto the perpendicular unit vector and equating to zero:

$$\left[\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ \sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -l\sin\beta\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I = 0$$

**Step 5: Rotation Matrix (1 mark)**

$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):** Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Set up the rolling equation for each wheel (2 marks)**

With $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = 0$, and $l = 0.25$ m:
$$\left[\sin 0,\ -\cos 0,\ -0.25\cos 0\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I = r\dot\phi$$
$$\left[0,\ -1,\ -0.25\right]\,\dot\xi_R = 0.08\,\dot\phi$$

**Step 2: Write the linear and angular velocity components (2 marks)**

For each wheel, the $X_R$ direction gives the forward velocity, and the $Z_R$ component is the yaw rate. In the robot frame, with $v_y = 0$ (no sideways motion):
$$-0.25\,\omega = 0.08\,\dot\phi \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot\phi = -3.125\,\omega$$

**Step 3: Solve for both wheels (2 marks)**

For the **right** wheel: $\dot\phi_R = -15$ rad/s, so:
$$\omega = \frac{\dot\phi_R}{-3.125} = \frac{-15}{-3.125} = 4.8 \text{ rad/s}$$

For the **left** wheel: $\dot\phi_L = -12$ rad/s, so:
$$\omega = \frac{-12}{-3.125} = 3.84 \text{ rad/s}$$

Since the wheels must agree on $\omega$ for rigid body motion, the **robot turns** with a rate that is the *average constraint*:
$$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{2l} = \frac{0.08(15-12)}{2 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.24}{0.5} = 0.48 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Forward velocity (1 mark)**

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.08(27)}{2} = 1.08 \text{ m/s}$$

> **Final Answer:** The robot moves forward at **1.08 m/s** and rotates at **0.48 rad/s** (turning toward the slower left wheel side).

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, 14 Marks - Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a clear sketch, explain the geometric parameters of a Castor Wheel. Derive the rolling and sliding constraints for a castor wheel. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain with mathematical reasoning why a castor wheel cannot be used to drive or steer a robot. What role does it play in a typical 4-wheel mobile robot configuration? **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):** Castor Wheel Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Geometric Setup and Sketch Description (1 mark)**

The castor wheel has *five* key parameters:
- $r$ = wheel radius
- $l$ = distance from robot center to wheel center
- $d$ = **castor offset** (horizontal distance from steering axis to contact patch — *this is the defining parameter*)
- $\alpha$ = mounting angle
- $\beta$ = passive steering angle (free to vary, no motor)

**Step 2: Velocity of Wheel Center with Offset (2 marks)**

The steering axis is at distance $(l - d)$ from the robot center, but the wheel center is at distance $l$. The velocity of $M$ in the robot frame becomes:
$$\vec{v}_M = \begin{bmatrix} \dot x - \dot\theta\,(l-d)\sin\alpha + d\dot\beta\cos(\alpha+\beta) \\ \dot y + \dot\theta\,(l-d)\cos\alpha + d\dot\beta\sin(\alpha+\beta) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Rolling Constraint (2 marks)**

$$\left[\sin(\alpha+\beta),\ -\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ d\cos\beta - l\cos\beta\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I - r\dot\phi = 0$$

**Step 4: Sliding Constraint (1 mark)**

$$\left[\cos(\alpha+\beta),\ \sin(\alpha+\beta),\ d\sin\beta - l\sin\beta\right]\,R(\theta)\,\dot\xi_I = 0$$

**Step 5: Reduction to Steered Standard Wheel (1 mark)**

Setting $d = 0$ in the equations above recovers the **steered standard wheel constraints exactly**, proving the unified formalism.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b):** Why a Castor Cannot Drive/Steer (7 marks)

**Step 1: No drive DOF (2 marks)**

A castor wheel is **not connected to any drive motor**, so $\dot\phi$ is purely passive. The rolling constraint is *kinematically valid* but it only describes the wheel's spin rate — it does not **cause** the robot to move. There is no energy input to produce a controlled wheel torque.

**Step 2: No controlled steering DOF (2 marks)**

The angle $\beta$ is also passive — the wheel swivel is free. While the wheel does self-align with the motion direction, this *follows* the robot's motion rather than *determining* it. A wheel that follows cannot steer.

**Step 3: Effective DOF accounting (1 mark)**

For a castor wheel, the sliding constraint is automatically satisfied at equilibrium (no slip perpendicular to the wheel plane), so it only removes **1 effective DOF** from the robot. The other constraint is the rolling one, which also removes 1 DOF. So one castor wheel removes 2 DOF *if* it is treated as a constraint, but practically its 2 constraints are *coupled* through the auto-aligned $\beta$, leaving it with **no independent control authority**.

**Step 4: Role in a 4-wheel robot (2 marks)**

In a typical 4-wheel mobile robot (2 driven steered standard wheels at the front, 2 castor wheels at the rear):
- The **front steered standard wheels** provide both **drive** and **steering**.
- The **rear castor wheels** provide **static stability** (preventing the robot from tipping forward or backward) and act as **idler supports** for the payload.
- The castors also ensure the robot can **rotate in place** — they swivel to follow the rotation, eliminating the need to actively steer the rear.

> **Final Answer:** A castor wheel cannot drive or steer because both of its rotational DOFs ($\phi$ and $\beta$) are **passive**; it serves purely as a **stability support** and **idler wheel** in a 4-wheel configuration.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the Rotation Matrix $R(\theta)$** — Examiners expect the constraint in the **world frame** form with $R(\theta)\dot\xi_I$. Writing only the robot-frame row vector loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Sign error in the moment-arm term** — It is $-l\cos\beta$ (steered standard) or $d\cos\beta - l\cos\beta$ (castor). Writing $+l\cos\beta$ is a common mistake.
> 3. **Confusing the two constraint equations** — Rolling equation has a $-r\dot\phi$ on the right; sliding equation has a $0$ on the right. Students often write the same right-hand side for both, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Not reducing castor to steered standard** — When asked to "deduce" the steered standard wheel from the castor, students forget to set $d = 0$ and re-derive, losing 2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the 2D rotation matrix** — The 3×3 rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ must be given explicitly when transforming between world and robot frames, especially for the 14-mark derivations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision (Read Before Exam):**
>
> - [x] **Two wheel types covered:** Steered Standard ($d=0$, active) and Castor ($d \neq 0$, passive).
> - [x] **Five reference points:** $I, R, M, S, P$ — Inertial, Robot, Wheel-center, Steering-axis-on-ground, Contact.
> - [x] **Five geometric parameters:** $r$ (radius), $l$ (distance to center), $\alpha$ (mount angle), $\beta$ (steer angle), $d$ (castor offset).
> - [x] **Steered Standard Rolling:** $[\sin(\alpha+\beta), -\cos(\alpha+\beta), -l\cos\beta]\,R(\theta)\dot\xi_I - r\dot\phi = 0$.
> - [x] **Steered Standard Sliding:** $[\cos(\alpha+\beta), \sin(\alpha+\beta), -l\sin\beta]\,R(\theta)\dot\xi_I = 0$.
> - [x] **Castor Rolling:** $[\sin(\alpha+\beta), -\cos(\alpha+\beta), d\cos\beta - l\cos\beta]\,R(\theta)\dot\xi_I - r\dot\phi = 0$.
> - [x] **Castor Sliding:** $[\cos(\alpha+\beta), \sin(\alpha+\beta), d\sin\beta - l\sin\beta]\,R(\theta)\dot\xi_I = 0$.
> - [x] **Unification:** Castor equations $\xrightarrow{d=0}$ Steered Standard equations.
> - [x] **DOF removed:** Each non-holonomic wheel removes 2 DOF; mobility $= 3 - N_F - N_C$.
> - [x] **Real-world uses:** Steered Standard — car front wheels, AGV driving wheels; Castor — office chairs, AGV support wheels, shopping trolleys.
> - [x] **Why a castor cannot drive/steer:** Both $\phi$ and $\beta$ are passive — no actuation authority.
> - [x] **Common slip-ups:** Sign of $l\cos\beta$ term, omission of $R(\theta)$, mixing up rolling vs sliding RHS.
> - [x] **Examiner's favourite:** "Show that the steered standard wheel is a special case of the castor wheel with $d = 0$." (Always answer by substituting $d = 0$ in the castor equations and simplifying.)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Kinematic Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot Kinematic Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **kinematic constraint** in mobile robotics is a mathematical restriction imposed on the velocity (or higher-order motion) of a robot that limits the set of admissible velocities at a given configuration. These constraints arise from the physical interaction between the robot's wheels and the ground, the rolling-without-slipping conditions, and the mechanical linkages between the robot's chassis and its wheels.

Formally, a kinematic constraint restricts the velocity vector $\dot{\xi}$ of a rigid body to lie in a subspace of the tangent space of the configuration manifold. In the **Pfaffian form**, a set of kinematic constraints is expressed as:

$$
A(q) \cdot \dot{\xi} = 0
$$

where $A(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$ is a constraint matrix that depends on the configuration $q$, and $\dot{\xi} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the generalized velocity vector. The number of linearly independent rows in $A(q)$ determines the dimensionality of the constrained motion space.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> Students must clearly distinguish between **holonomic** and **non-holonomic** constraints. The term *holonomic* comes from the Greek word *holos* (whole) + *nomos* (law) — a "complete" constraint. Non-holonomic constraints are *inegrable only in differential form* and cannot be reduced to algebraic relations among position coordinates alone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are **parking a car** in a tight parallel-parking spot on a busy KTU campus road:

- You **cannot slide the car sideways** into the space. Even though the car has 3 logical degrees of freedom in the plane (forward/backward $x$, sideways $y$, and orientation $\theta$), the steering geometry of the front wheels restricts the velocity to always point along the car's longitudinal axis. This is a **non-holonomic constraint** — it is a *velocity-level* law, not a *position-level* law.
- The constraint says: *the sideways velocity of any wheel contact point must be zero*. This is a single linear relation among the three velocity components, and it is *not integrable* — you cannot derive a closed-form $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$ from it. That is why parallel parking requires a complex multi-step maneuver (forward-reverse-forward-reverse) rather than a single straight motion.
- In contrast, a **train on a fixed track** is holonomically constrained: its position is fully described by a single scalar (distance along the track). The track physically removes 2 degrees of freedom at the position level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams**
> - **Holonomic constraint** → reducible to a relation among coordinates only (e.g., $x^2 + y^2 = R^2$).
> - **Non-holonomic constraint** → expressed only at the velocity (differential) level, *not* integrable to a position-level law (e.g., the rolling-without-slipping condition of a wheel).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Wheel radius | $r$ | **0.05 m to 0.30 m** |
| Wheelbase (car) | $L$ or $l$ | **2.0 m to 3.0 m** |
| Degrees of Mobility | $\delta_m$ | 1, 2, or 3 |
| Degrees of Steerability | $\delta_s$ | 0, 1, or 2 |
| Degree of Maneuverability | $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | 1, 2, or 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **For a planar mobile robot moving in 2D**, the *unconstrained* configuration space has 3 degrees of freedom: $x, y, \theta$. Every kinematic constraint removes or restricts one such degree of freedom. A *fully holonomic* robot would have $\delta_m = 3$ and zero non-holonomic constraints.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Kinematic Constraints

#### 2.1.1 Holonomic Constraints (Position Constraints)

A holonomic constraint has the form:

$$
h_i(q) = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, k
$$

Differentiating with respect to time gives $\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial q_j} \dot{q}_j = 0$, which is a special case of the Pfaffian form. Holonomic constraints *reduce the dimension* of the configuration space.

- **Example**: A robot arm mounted on a prismatic rail; the prismatic joint imposes $y = c$ (constant), removing one DOF at the position level.

#### 2.1.2 Non-Holonomic Constraints (Velocity-Only Constraints)

A non-holonomic constraint is given in Pfaffian form:

$$
a_1(q) \dot{q}_1 + a_2(q) \dot{q}_2 + \cdots + a_n(q) \dot{q}_n = 0
$$

which **cannot be integrated** into a closed-form $h(q) = 0$. Non-holonomic constraints reduce the *instantaneous* feasible velocities but **do not reduce** the dimension of the reachable configuration space.

- **Example**: The rolling-without-slipping condition of a car wheel: the lateral velocity at the contact point is zero, but the car can still reach *any* position and orientation by a clever path.

### 2.2 Wheel Types and Their Constraints

KTU Mobile Robotics (following the **Campion, Bastin, D'Andrea-Novel** classification) considers five canonical wheel types.

#### 2.2.1 Fixed (Standard) Wheel

- The wheel axis is rigidly fixed to the chassis.
- It has two constraints:
  1. **Rolling constraint** (no slip in the wheel's plane of rotation)
  2. **Sliding constraint** (no slip perpendicular to the wheel's plane)

#### 2.2.2 Steered (Orientable) Wheel

- The wheel can rotate about a vertical axis passing through its mounting point.
- It has three constraints, because steering adds an additional rolling constraint for the steering angle itself.

#### 2.2.3 Castor Wheel

- The wheel axis is mounted on a horizontal pivot (offset from the contact point).
- It has two constraints (similar to fixed wheel), but the offset introduces an additional turning moment that the wheel can passively absorb.

#### 2.2.4 Swedish (Mecanum) Wheel

- Has passive rollers mounted around its circumference at angle $\gamma$ (typically $\pm 45°$).
- The rollers allow the wheel to translate sideways with negligible friction, so only the rolling constraint applies — only one constraint per wheel.

#### 2.2.5 Ball (Spherical) Wheel

- A spherical bearing; only the rolling constraint applies — only one constraint per wheel.

### 2.3 Degrees of Mobility, Steerability, and Maneuverability

These are the **most exam-relevant quantities** in KTU Module 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$)**
> $\delta_m$ is the number of degrees of freedom that can be *directly controlled* by the robot's actuators. It equals $3 - \text{rank}(C_1)$, where $C_1$ is the matrix formed by the independent rolling-type constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$)**
> $\delta_s$ is the number of degrees of freedom that can be controlled by *changing the steering angle* of steerable wheels. It is the rank of the matrix $C_2$ formed by the steering-type constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Degree of Maneuverability ($M$)**
> $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ is the total number of independent degrees of freedom of the robot in its configuration space (excluding internal wheel rotations).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Pfaffian form | $A(q)\dot{\xi} = 0$ | All kinematic constraints |
| 2 | Fixed-wheel rolling constraint | $[\sin(\alpha+\beta), -\cos(\alpha+\beta), l\cos\beta]\,\dot{\xi} = 0$ | No slip along wheel plane |
| 3 | Fixed-wheel sliding constraint | $[-\cos(\alpha+\beta), -\sin(\alpha+\beta), l\sin\beta]\,\dot{\xi} = 0$ | No slip perpendicular to wheel |
| 4 | Steered-wheel steering constraint | $[\cos(\alpha+\beta), \sin(\alpha+\beta), d]\,\dot{\xi} = 0$ | Wheel steering kinematics |
| 5 | Mobility (general) | $\delta_m = 3 - \frac{N_f + N_s}{2} + \epsilon$ | Independent controllable DOF |
| 6 | Steerability | $\delta_s = N_s$ | Number of steerable wheels |
| 7 | Maneuverability | $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Total controllable DOF |
| 8 | Number of DOF | $\delta = 3 - \frac{l - m}{2}$ | Where $l$ = total constraints, $m$ = # holonomic rolling pairs |
| 9 | ICR curvature (car) | $\kappa = \frac{\tan\phi}{L}$ | Path curvature; $\phi$ = steering angle |
| 10 | ICR radius (car) | $R = \frac{L}{2} + \frac{L}{2\tan\phi} \approx \frac{L}{\tan\phi}$ (for $| \phi \vert \ll 1$) | Radius of instantaneous turn |
| 11 | Bicycle model — body twist | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$ | Kinematic state equation |
| 12 | Differential-drive twist | $\dot{x} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Body twist from wheel speeds |
| 13 | Swedish-wheel lateral speed | $v_{\text{lat}} = v_{\text{roll}} \cdot \tan\gamma$ | Lateral velocity component |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the standard notation used is: $\alpha$ = angle of the wheel plane relative to the chassis, $\beta$ = angle between the chassis and the wheel's mounting arm, $l$ = distance from robot center to wheel contact, $d$ = offset of steered-wheel axis, $b$ = wheelbase (track width), $L$ = distance between front and rear axles, $\phi$ = steering angle.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kinematic constraints are the **backbone of motion planning and control** for every autonomous vehicle:

- **Autonomous Cars (Waymo, Tesla FSD)**: Use the bicycle model (an approximation of the car-like constraint) for high-level trajectory planning.
- **AGVs in Warehouses (Amazon Kiva, KTU smart-factory labs)**: Use differential-drive or synchro-drive kinematic models.
- **Hospital Robots (Tug, Aethon)**: Swedish-wheeled omnidirectional bases for zero-radius turning in corridors.
- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance)**: Use rocker-bogie suspensions with steerable and fixed wheels — the kinematic constraints determine the rocker angle and rocker-bogie geometry.
- **Industrial Manipulators on Mobile Bases (KUKA KMR, MiR)**: Combine a holonomic mobile base with a 6-DOF arm; the mobile-base constraints must be combined with the manipulator's joint constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fixed-Wheel Rolling Constraint

Consider a fixed wheel mounted on a mobile robot. Let:
- $(x, y, \theta)$ be the pose of the robot chassis.
- $l$ = distance from the robot's center to the wheel contact point.
- $\alpha$ = angle of the wheel's plane of rotation measured from the chassis $x$-axis.
- $\beta$ = angle between the chassis $x$-axis and the line from the robot center to the wheel contact.

The contact point $C$ of the wheel in the world frame is:

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} x + l\cos\beta \\ y + l\sin\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

The unit vector along the wheel's plane of rotation (in the world frame) is:

$$
\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\alpha+\beta) \\ \sin(\alpha+\beta) \end{bmatrix}
$$

The unit vector perpendicular to the wheel's plane is:

$$
\mathbf{n} = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin(\alpha+\beta) \\ \cos(\alpha+\beta) \end{bmatrix}
$$

The velocity of the contact point is given by the rigid-body velocity of a point at distance $l$ from the center:

$$
\dot{C} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} + \dot{\theta} \begin{bmatrix} -l\sin\beta \\ l\cos\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Rolling Constraint (no slip along $\mathbf{u}$)

The component of $\dot{C}$ along $\mathbf{u}$ must equal $r\dot{\varphi}$ (the rolling speed), so the *no-slip-along-wheel* condition is:

$$
\mathbf{u}^T \dot{C} = r\dot{\varphi}
$$

If we write the perpendicular part of this as the *sliding constraint* (next), the rolling constraint is:

$$
\mathbf{n}^T \dot{C} = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{n}^T \dot{C} &= -\sin(\alpha+\beta)[\dot{x} - l\dot{\theta}\sin\beta] + \cos(\alpha+\beta)[\dot{y} + l\dot{\theta}\cos\beta] \\
&= -\sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x} + l\sin(\alpha+\beta)\sin\beta\,\dot{\theta} \\
&\quad + \cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y} + l\cos(\alpha+\beta)\cos\beta\,\dot{\theta} \\
&= \cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y} - \sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x} + l\,[\sin(\alpha+\beta)\sin\beta + \cos(\alpha+\beta)\cos\beta]\,\dot{\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin A \sin B + \cos A \cos B = \cos(A - B)$:

$$
\sin(\alpha+\beta)\sin\beta + \cos(\alpha+\beta)\cos\beta = \cos(\alpha+\beta-\beta) = \cos\alpha
$$

Therefore the rolling constraint reduces to:

$$
[\sin(\alpha+\beta)]\,\dot{x} - [\cos(\alpha+\beta)]\,\dot{y} - [l\cos\alpha]\,\dot{\theta} = 0
$$

or equivalently (sign convention matching most KTU textbooks, where $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$):

$$
\sin(\alpha+\beta)\,\dot{x} - \cos(\alpha+\beta)\,\dot{y} + l\cos\beta\,\dot{\theta} = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact sign of the $l$ term depends on the sign convention adopted for the rigid-body rotation $\dot{\theta}$ and the orientation of the wheel arm. Both forms appear in literature; KTU commonly uses the second form shown above, with $l\cos\beta$ as the coefficient of $\dot{\theta}$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Sliding (No-Sideways-Slip) Constraint

The component of $\dot{C}$ along the wheel's plane direction $\mathbf{u}$ equals $r\dot{\varphi}$ — this is the *rolling* component. The *sliding* (or *lateral slip*) component along $\mathbf{n}$ must be zero:

$$
\mathbf{n}^T \dot{C} = 0
$$

Following the same expansion as above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{n}^T \dot{C} &= -\sin(\alpha+\beta)[\dot{x} - l\dot{\theta}\sin\beta] + \cos(\alpha+\beta)[\dot{y} + l\dot{\theta}\cos\beta] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

which after algebraic manipulation (using $\sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B = \sin(A - B)$) yields:

$$
\cos(\alpha+\beta)\,\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha+\beta)\,\dot{y} + l\sin\beta\,\dot{\theta} = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **non-holonomic** constraint. It is the *velocity-level* restriction that the wheel cannot slide sideways. This single equation is the heart of the parallel-parking analogy from Section 1.2.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Steered-Wheel Steering Constraint

A steered wheel has an additional degree of freedom — the steering angle $\phi$ measured about a vertical axis at the wheel's steering pivot, located at distance $d$ from the wheel's contact point along the wheel plane.

For a steered wheel, the wheel's plane direction $\alpha$ is no longer constant; it varies with the steering angle. The **steering constraint** enforces that the wheel's contact point is always the same distance $d$ from the pivot (no slip at the steering joint):

$$
\mathbf{u}^T \dot{C}_{\text{steer}} = 0
$$

where $\dot{C}_{\text{steer}}$ is the velocity of the steering pivot point. After analogous algebra, this yields:

$$
\cos(\alpha+\beta)\,\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha+\beta)\,\dot{y} + d\,\dot{\theta} = 0
$$

### 3.4 Degrees-of-Freedom Counting — General Formula

For a robot with $N$ wheels in total:
- $N_f$ = number of fixed (standard) wheels
- $N_s$ = number of steerable wheels
- $N_c$ = number of castor wheels
- $N_{sw}$ = number of Swedish wheels

Each type contributes a different number of constraints:

| Wheel type | # Constraints |
|---|---|
| Fixed | 2 (rolling + sliding) |
| Steered | 3 (rolling + sliding + steering) |
| Castor | 2 (rolling + sliding) |
| Swedish / Ball | 1 (rolling only) |
| Omnidirectional (Swedish) | 1 (rolling only) |

Total constraints: $l = 2N_f + 3N_s + 2N_c + N_{sw}$.

The number of independent constraints is the **rank** of the global constraint matrix $C_1$ (rolling+steering) and $C_2$ (sliding). For a generic configuration, the rank is given by a formula due to **Campion & Chung (1986)**:

$$
\text{rank}(C_1) = N_f + N_s + N_{sw}, \quad \text{rank}(C_2) = N_f + N_s + N_c
$$

The degrees of mobility and steerability are:

$$
\delta_m = 3 - \text{rank}(C_1), \quad \delta_s = \text{rank}(C_1) - \text{rank}(C_2)
$$

### 3.5 Worked Example: Differential-Drive Robot (DDR)

A differential-drive robot has:
- 2 fixed wheels on a common axle, separated by distance $b$ (track width)
- 1 or 2 passive castor wheels for balance (castor wheels add *no* rolling constraints in the active sense)

For a configuration with two fixed wheels centered along the $x$-axis of the chassis:
- Wheel 1 (left): $\alpha_1 = 0$, $\beta_1 = 0$, $l_1 = b/2$ (signed negative)
- Wheel 2 (right): $\alpha_2 = 0$, $\beta_2 = 0$, $l_2 = b/2$ (signed positive)

The two sliding (no-sideways-slip) constraints reduce to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{y} - \frac{b}{2}\dot{\theta} &= 0 \quad \text{(left wheel)} \\
\dot{y} + \frac{b}{2}\dot{\theta} &= 0 \quad \text{(right wheel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding these two equations: $2\dot{y} = 0 \Rightarrow \dot{y} = 0$. Substituting back: $\dot{\theta} = 0$ is not implied, but combining with the original gives $\dot{\theta} = 0$ from the *pair*, which is incorrect — this means the constraints are linearly *independent* only at generic configurations.

The correct rank-2 statement is that **$\dot{y} = 0$** is the only non-holonomic constraint. Thus:

- $C_1$ (rolling constraints) has rank 2
- $C_2$ (sliding constraints) has rank 1
- $\delta_m = 3 - 2 = 1$ (one controllable DOF — translation along $x$)
- $\delta_s = 2 - 1 = 1$ (one extra DOF via wheel-speed differential — rotation)
- $M = 1 + 1 = 2$ (planar position but not orientation; orientation controlled indirectly)

> [!NOTE]
> The differential-drive robot has $\delta_m = 1$ (only one *direct* mobility DOF — the average wheel speed) but achieves the appearance of $\delta_m = 2$ through the speed differential (which is essentially a steering action on the *body*). This is a subtle but commonly tested point in KTU exams.

### 3.6 Worked Example: Car-Like Robot (Ackermann Steering)

A car-like robot has 2 steerable front wheels + 2 fixed rear wheels.

For simplicity, consider the bicycle model (1 front steered wheel + 1 rear fixed wheel). The constraints are:
- Rear wheel sliding: $\dot{y}_r = 0 \Rightarrow \dot{y} = 0$ (in chassis frame) — but the chassis is moving, so in the world frame the constraint is $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
- Front wheel sliding: similar but with steering angle $\phi$, yielding $\dot{x}\sin(\theta+\phi) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta+\phi) + L\cos\phi\,\dot{\theta} = 0$.

Combining, we get the well-known bicycle kinematic model:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi
$$

The ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation) lies on the line passing through both axles, at distance $R = L/\tan\phi$ from the rear axle, perpendicular to the wheelbase.

For a car:
- $\delta_m = 1$ (forward speed $v$)
- $\delta_s = 1$ (steering angle $\phi$)
- $M = 1 + 1 = 2$ (so the car cannot translate sideways — *non-holonomic*)

### 3.7 Worked Example: Omnidirectional Robot (4 Swedish Wheels)

Four Swedish wheels at the four corners of a square chassis.

Each Swedish wheel imposes only 1 constraint (rolling). For a square arrangement with wheels at corners $\pm L/2, \pm L/2$, the rolling constraints can be written as:

$$
\cos\gamma_i\,\dot{x} + \sin\gamma_i\,\dot{y} = r\dot{\varphi}_i
$$

where $\gamma_i$ is the orientation of the $i$-th wheel's passive rollers.

For wheels at $45°$ and $-45°$ orientations, these constraints do **not** restrict $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ independently — the matrix $C_1$ has rank only 2 (not 3), so:

$$
\delta_m = 3 - 2 = 1
$$

Wait — this is a *common mistake*. Let me correct it: with **4 Swedish wheels at 45° orientations**, the rank of $C_1$ is 2, but the controllable DOFs are $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ (2 independent translations). The reason is that the $\dot{\theta}$ term does not appear in the Swedish-wheel constraint (no lateral component), so $\dot{\theta}$ is freely controllable — giving $\delta_m = 3$.

Re-examining: each Swedish wheel has the form $[\cos(\alpha_i+\beta_i), \sin(\alpha_i+\beta_i), 0]\dot{\xi} = r\dot{\varphi}_i$. The third column is **zero**, so the $3 \times 4$ matrix $C_1^T$ has zero contribution from $\dot{\theta}$. Its rank is at most 2.

But $\delta_m = 3 - \text{rank}(C_1) = 3 - 2 = 1$? No — the convention is $\delta_m = $ rank of the *controllable-velocity space*, which for 4 Swedish wheels is **3** (you can command $v_x$, $v_y$, $\omega$ independently). The Campion formula gives:

$$
\delta_m = 3 - \frac{N_f + N_s}{2} = 3 - 0 = 3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $\delta_m = 3 - (N_f + N_s)/2$ formula is the SHORTCUT**, valid only when all constraints are linearly independent and the Swedish-wheel arrangements are non-degenerate. KTU exams typically expect the *intuitive* result: a 4-Swedish-wheel robot is **fully omnidirectional** with $\delta_m = 3$, $\delta_s = 0$, $M = 3$.

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Robot Types

| Robot Type | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $M$ | Holonomic? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2WD Differential (with castors) | 2 | 0 | 2 | No | Pure translation + rotation |
| 4WD Differential (no castors) | 2 | 0 | 2 | No | 4 fixed wheels |
| Synchro-Drive (3 steered + driven) | 2 | 1 | 3 | No | All wheels steer in unison |
| Tricycle (1 steer + 2 fixed) | 2 | 1 | 3 | No | Bicycle + 2 rear wheels |
| Car (Ackermann, 2 steer + 2 fixed) | 1 | 1 | 2 | No | Non-holonomic, can park parallel |
| 4 Swedish Wheel (Mecanum) | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes** (in 2D) | True omnidirectional |
| 3 Swedish Wheel (Mecanum) | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes** (in 2D) | Triangular base |
| 4 Ball Wheel | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes** (in 2D) | Spherical bearings |

> [!NOTE]
> The distinction between "$\delta_m = 3$ in 2D" and "fully holonomic in the *position* sense" is subtle. A 4-Swedish-wheel robot can instantaneously achieve any velocity in 2D ($\delta_m = 3$), but it still has the *configuration* space $(x, y, \theta)$ — it can still rotate. In 2D planar robotics literature, this is called *omnidirectional*, but technically it is *locally holonomic* and *globally non-holonomic* (because the orientation $\theta$ can still be changed).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Mobile Robots by Kinematic Constraints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Mobile Robot in 2D Plane] --> A1[Generalized Velocity xi_dot]
    A1 --> A2{Kinematic Constraints A qi xi_dot = 0}
    A2 -->|Holonomic| A3[Position-Level h q = 0]
    A2 -->|Non-Holonomic| A4[Velocity-Level Only]
    A3 --> B1[Omnidirectional Bases]
    A3 --> B2[Sliding Manipulators]
    A4 --> C1[Unicycle DDR Type]
    A4 --> C2[Car Like Ackermann]
    A4 --> C3[Steered Wheel Robots]
    C1 --> D1[delta m 2, delta s 0, M 2]
    C2 --> D2[delta m 1, delta s 1, M 2]
    C3 --> D3[delta m 2, delta s 1, M 3]
    B1 --> E1[Swedish or Ball Wheel delta m 3]
    B2 --> E2[Holonomic Position Level]
    A0 --> F0[Wheel Type Module]
    F0 --> F1[Fixed Wheel 2 constraints]
    F0 --> F2[Steered Wheel 3 constraints]
    F0 --> F3[Castor Wheel 2 constraints]
    F0 --> F4[Swedish Wheel 1 constraint]
    F0 --> F5[Ball Wheel 1 constraint]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Constraint Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Robot Configuration q] --> S1[Wheel Position and Orientation]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Contact Point C]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Velocity of Contact Point]
    S3 --> S4[Project onto Wheel Plane and Normal]
    S4 --> S5[Enforce Rolling and Sliding Conditions]
    S5 --> S6[Build Constraint Matrix A q]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Rank of A q]
    S7 --> S8[Derive delta m and delta s]
    S8 --> S9[Maneuverability M]
    S9 --> S10[Mobility Class Output]
    S10 --> T1[Omnidirectional]
    S10 --> T2[Differential Drive]
    S10 --> T3[Ackermann Car]
    S10 --> T4[Synchro Drive]
    S10 --> T5[Custom Hybrid]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) for Car-Like Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0[Car Chassis Center] --> R1[Front Axle Point F]
    R0 --> R2[Rear Axle Point B]
    R1 --> R3[Steering Angle phi]
    R3 --> R4[ICR lies on extension of Front Wheel Direction]
    R2 --> R5[ICR lies on extension of Rear Axle]
    R4 --> R6[Intersection gives ICR Point]
    R5 --> R6
    R6 --> R7[Radius R = L over tan phi]
    R7 --> R8[Curvature kappa = tan phi over L]
    R8 --> R9[Path traces circle of radius R]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Wheel Constraint Reduction for Differential Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W0[DDR with 2 fixed wheels] --> W1[Left wheel sliding constraint: y_dot minus b over 2 theta_dot = 0]
    W0 --> W2[Right wheel sliding constraint: y_dot plus b over 2 theta_dot = 0]
    W1 --> W3[Add both equations]
    W2 --> W3
    W3 --> W4[Result: y_dot = 0 in chassis frame]
    W4 --> W5[Body twist: x_dot = v, theta_dot = omega]
    W5 --> W6[World frame: x_dot = v cos theta, y_dot = v sin theta, theta_dot = omega]
    W6 --> W7[delta m = 1 for direct forward, delta s = 1 for rotation, M = 2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above intentionally map the **logical information flow** of constraint analysis rather than attempting geometric renderings. This is the recommended KTU approach for flow-based questions on kinematic analysis.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between holonomic and non-holonomic constraints with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **holonomic constraint** is a restriction on the *configuration* of a mechanical system that can be expressed as an algebraic equation among the generalized coordinates alone, $h(q) = 0$. It reduces the dimension of the configuration space. *Example*: a point mass constrained to move on a circle $x^2 + y^2 = R^2$.

A **non-holonomic constraint** is a restriction that can only be expressed in *differential form* as $a(q)\dot{q} = 0$ and **cannot be integrated** to a position-level equation. It restricts the *instantaneous* velocities but not the reachable configurations. *Example*: the rolling-without-slipping condition of a car wheel: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. The car can still reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ by maneuvering.

(1 mark each for definitions + 1 mark for examples)

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is meant by the degree of mobility $\delta_m$ and degree of steerability $\delta_s$ of a mobile robot? Define the degree of maneuverability $M$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Degree of mobility $\delta_m$** (1 mark): The number of *degrees of freedom* of the robot that can be *directly and instantaneously* controlled by the robot's actuators. Mathematically, $\delta_m = 3 - \text{rank}(C_1)$, where $C_1$ is the matrix of independent rolling-type constraints.
- **Degree of steerability $\delta_s$** (1 mark): The number of *additional* degrees of freedom available through *changing the steering angles* of steerable wheels. Mathematically, $\delta_s = \text{rank}(C_1) - \text{rank}(C_2)$.
- **Degree of maneuverability $M$** (1 mark): $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$, representing the *total* number of independently controllable degrees of freedom.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**(a)** Derive the **Pfaffian form** of the fixed-wheel constraint for a generic wheel mounted on a mobile robot chassis. Clearly define all symbols used. Show that there are **two independent constraints** per fixed wheel. (7 marks)

**(b)** A differential-drive mobile robot has two fixed wheels of radius $r$ on a common axle of length $b$, with one castor wheel at the rear for balance. Compute the **degree of mobility**, **degree of steerability**, and **degree of maneuverability** of this robot. Write its **complete kinematic model** in the world frame. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) — Fixed-Wheel Pfaffian Form (7 marks)**

- (1 mark) Define pose vector $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ and parameters: $\alpha$ = wheel plane angle, $\beta$ = wheel mounting arm angle, $l$ = distance to contact point.
- (2 marks) Express contact point velocity $\dot{C} = [\dot{x} - l\dot{\theta}\sin\beta,\ \dot{y} + l\dot{\theta}\cos\beta]^T$.
- (2 marks) Project onto wheel-plane normal $\mathbf{n} = [-\sin(\alpha+\beta), \cos(\alpha+\beta)]^T$ to obtain the *sliding constraint*:

$$
\cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y} + l\sin\beta\,\dot{\theta} = 0
$$

- (2 marks) Project onto wheel-plane unit vector $\mathbf{u}$ to obtain the *rolling constraint* (after integration, gives wheel angular velocity; the rolling part is *holonomic* in terms of $\varphi$ and the others, but in the Pfaffian form the no-slip-along-wheel gives $r\dot{\varphi}$):

$$
\sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y} + l\cos\beta\,\dot{\theta} = 0
$$

- (Conclusion, in summary) The fixed wheel contributes **2 linearly independent constraints** in the Pfaffian matrix $A(q)$, restricting the 3-D velocity space to a 1-D subspace (one degree of mobility per wheel).

**(b) Part (b) — DDR Kinematic Model (7 marks)**

- (2 marks) Set up the geometry: two fixed wheels at $(0, \pm b/2)$ on the chassis, $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = 0$, $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = 0$, $l_1 = -b/2$, $l_2 = +b/2$.
- (1 mark) Write the two sliding constraints and reduce to $\dot{y} = 0$ in the chassis frame (i.e., $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ in world frame).
- (1 mark) Count constraints: $N_f = 2$, $N_s = 0$, $N_{sw} = 0$, $N_c = 1$ (castor). Total constraints: $l = 2 \cdot 2 + 0 + 2 \cdot 1 + 0 = 6$ raw, but with $C_1$ rank = 2 (rolling) and $C_2$ rank = 1 (sliding).
- (1 mark) Compute: $\delta_m = 3 - \text{rank}(C_1) = 3 - 2 = 1$; $\delta_s = 2 - 1 = 1$; $M = 1 + 1 = 2$.

  *Note: Some authors compute $\delta_m = 2$ for DDR by including the differential-speed DOF; either convention is accepted in KTU if justified. The most common KTU convention is $\delta_m = 1$, $\delta_s = 1$.*

- (2 marks) Kinematic model: with $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ and $\omega = \frac{r}{b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the **kinematic constraint equations** for a **steered wheel** mounted on a mobile robot. Clearly state the **three constraints** and explain why a steered wheel has *one more* constraint than a fixed wheel. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider a **car-like robot** with Ackermann steering: front wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m, track width $b = 1.6$ m, maximum steering angle $\phi_{\max} = 35°$. Compute the **minimum turning radius** and the **maximum path curvature**. Comment on the **maneuverability class** of this robot. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) — Steered-Wheel Constraints (7 marks)**

- (1 mark) Define the steering geometry: a steered wheel has a vertical pivot at distance $d$ from the contact point; the wheel plane angle $\alpha$ is now a *function of time* $\alpha(t) = \alpha_0 + \phi(t)$.
- (2 marks) Derive the **sliding constraint** at the contact point (same as fixed wheel but with $\alpha$ time-varying):

$$
\cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y} + l\sin\beta\,\dot{\theta} + d\dot{\alpha} = 0
$$

- (2 marks) Derive the **steering constraint** (the pivot point velocity perpendicular to the wheel plane is zero):

$$
\cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y} + d\dot{\theta} = 0
$$

- (2 marks) The **rolling constraint** is the third:

$$
\sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y} + l\cos\beta\,\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi}_{\text{wheel}}
$$

- (Conclusion) The three constraints of a steered wheel — *rolling, sliding, and steering* — collectively remove one more degree of freedom than a fixed wheel, hence a steered wheel reduces the controllable mobility by one additional unit.

**(b) Part (b) — Car-Like Kinematics (7 marks)**

- (1 mark) The bicycle model applies: $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$.
- (1 mark) The ICR radius: $R = L/\tan\phi$ (perpendicular distance from rear axle to ICR).
- (1 mark) Minimum turning radius at $\phi = 35°$: $R_{\min} = 2.5 / \tan(35°) = 2.5 / 0.7002 = 3.57$ m.
- (1 mark) Maximum curvature: $\kappa_{\max} = 1/R_{\min} = 0.280$ m⁻¹.
- (1 mark) Track width effect: with $b = 1.6$ m, the inner wheel must turn at a slightly larger angle $\phi_{\text{inner}} = \arctan(L/(R - b/2)) = \arctan(2.5/3.27) = 37.4°$ and the outer wheel at $\phi_{\text{outer}} = \arctan(L/(R + b/2)) = \arctan(2.5/3.87) = 32.8°$ — the Ackermann geometry ensures this.
- (2 marks) Maneuverability class: $\delta_m = 1$ (forward speed $v$), $\delta_s = 1$ (steering angle $\phi$), $M = 2$. The car is a **non-holonomic** robot — it cannot translate sideways; it can only reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ by a smooth path. The ICR always lies on the wheelbase line, restricting its path to a circle of radius at least 3.57 m.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing $\delta_m$ with total DOF**: A 4-Mecanum-wheel robot is often incorrectly said to have $\delta_m = 1$ using the rank-2 formula. The correct count is $\delta_m = 3$ (omnidirectional in 2D). KTU examiners will *not* give full credit if you only apply the rank formula without explaining the wheel-arrangement geometry.
> 2. **Sign errors in the fixed-wheel constraint**: A frequent mistake is mixing up $\sin(\alpha+\beta)$ and $\cos(\alpha+\beta)$ terms. Always draw the wheel on a Cartesian diagram and *label* the contact point, the wheel plane, and the perpendicular *before* writing the constraint.
> 3. **Forgetting the offset $d$ for steered wheels**: If the question specifies a steered wheel with offset, omitting the steering constraint $\cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y} + d\dot{\theta} = 0$ costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Stating the bicycle model without deriving it**: KTU Module 1 specifically requires the *derivation* of the bicycle model from first principles, not just its statement. The 14-mark questions often have a 4-mark subpart dedicated to this.
> 5. **Mixing up the units of curvature**: Curvature $\kappa$ has units of m⁻¹ (or rad/m), and turning radius $R$ has units of m. Do not interchange them.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Holonomic vs Non-holonomic**:
  - Holonomic = *position-level* algebraic constraint.
  - Non-holonomic = *velocity-level* Pfaffian constraint, **not** integrable to position level.
  - All wheel sliding (no-sideways-slip) conditions are **non-holonomic**.

- **Pfaffian Form**: $A(q)\dot{\xi} = 0$. The **rank** of $A(q)$ at generic configurations gives the number of independent constraints.

- **Wheel Constraint Counts**:
  - Fixed wheel → 2 constraints (rolling + sliding)
  - Steered wheel → 3 constraints (rolling + sliding + steering)
  - Castor wheel → 2 constraints
  - Swedish / Mecanum wheel → 1 constraint (rolling only)
  - Ball wheel → 1 constraint

- **Mobility Formulas**:
  - $\delta_m = 3 - \text{rank}(C_1)$
  - $\delta_s = \text{rank}(C_1) - \text{rank}(C_2)$
  - $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$
  - Quick rule: $\delta_m = 3 - (N_f + N_s)/2$, $\delta_s = N_s$ (Campion & Chung)

- **Five Canonical Robot Classes** (must memorize for KTU):
  - **Differential Drive (DDR)**: $\delta_m = 1, \delta_s = 1, M = 2$
  - **Car (Ackermann)**: $\delta_m = 1, \delta_s = 1, M = 2$ (different configuration space reachability)
  - **Synchro-Drive**: $\delta_m = 2, \delta_s = 1, M = 3$
  - **Omnidirectional (Swedish/Ball)**: $\delta_m = 3, \delta_s = 0, M = 3$
  - **Tricycle**: $\delta_m = 2, \delta_s = 1, M = 3$

- **Kinematic Models (must memorize)**:
  - **DDR**: $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = (r/b)(\omega_R - \omega_L)$
  - **Bicycle / Car**: $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$
  - **ICR radius for car**: $R = L/\tan\phi$
  - **Path curvature**: $\kappa = \tan\phi / L$

- **Important Symbols Table** (keep handy for KTU numericals):

  | Symbol | Meaning |
  |---|---|
  | $\alpha$ | Wheel plane orientation |
  | $\beta$ | Wheel mounting arm orientation |
  | $l$ | Distance from robot center to wheel contact |
  | $d$ | Steered-wheel pivot offset |
  | $r$ | Wheel radius |
  | $L$ | Wheelbase (front-rear) |
  | $b$ | Track width (left-right) |
  | $\phi$ | Steering angle |
  | $\theta$ | Robot heading |
  | $\xi = [x,y,\theta]^T$ | Robot pose vector |

- **Real-World Applications** (commonly asked in 2-mark viva questions):
  - **Cars** → Ackermann, non-holonomic
  - **AGVs in warehouses** → Differential drive or synchro-drive
  - **Hospital robots** → Omnidirectional (Swedish wheels)
  - **Mars rovers** → Rocker-bogie + Ackermann
  - **Wheelchairs (motorized)** → Differential drive
  - **Cleaning robots (Roomba)** → Differential drive

- **Holonomic Equivalence Rule**: A robot is **fully holonomic in 2D** (in the *velocity* sense) iff $\delta_m = 3$ *and* $\delta_s = 0$ *and* the rank of the constraint matrix is 2 (not 3). The 4-Swedish-wheel configuration satisfies this.

- **Path-Reachability Theorem (Brockett, 1983)**: For any non-holonomic robot with $\delta_m < 3$ in 2D, the configuration space $(x, y, \theta)$ is **completely reachable** from any initial pose by a smooth path, but the path is *never a straight line* in $(x, y)$. This is why cars need to maneuver.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Last-Minute Strategy**: For 14-mark derivations, always start with a labeled diagram showing the robot, the wheel, the contact point, the wheel plane angle $\alpha$, the mounting arm angle $\beta$, and the chassis axes. Even a rough sketch can earn you 1–2 marks of "presentation" credit. Then derive the constraint by *projection onto the wheel's normal and plane vectors* — this is the systematic KTU-recommended method.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Differential-Drive Robot (DDR)** is a non-holonomic mobile robot platform whose locomotion is achieved through **two independently actuated wheels mounted on a common axle**, supplemented by one or more passive (caster) wheels used purely for static balance. Because there is no steering mechanism, motion is generated entirely by creating a **differential in angular velocity** between the left and right drive wheels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *kinematic model* of a mobile robot specifies the **geometric and time-based relationships** between the **actuator inputs** (e.g., wheel angular velocities) and the **robot's rigid-body motion** (linear and angular velocities in the world frame), **without consideration of the forces/masses that cause the motion**.

The **kinematic constraint** defines the admissible instantaneous velocities of the robot. For a differential-drive robot, this constraint is **non-holonomic** because it is *non-integrable* — the robot cannot instantaneously translate sideways.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Office Chair**
> Sit on a wheeled office chair with both hands free. To move forward, you push both wheels equally. To turn, you push one wheel harder than the other (or hold one still). The chair **cannot move sideways** because the wheels are fixed in their rolling direction. The differential-drive robot behaves exactly like this chair, but with motors doing the pushing.

A second analogy: imagine a **wheelchair** with two large powered rear wheels and small front casters. The user adjusts the speed of each rear wheel independently. The *difference* in wheel speeds causes rotation; the *average* causes translation.

## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters (Standard Metrics)

| Symbol | Meaning | SI Unit |
|:------:|:--------|:-------:|
| $r$ | Radius of each drive wheel | **m** (metre) |
| $L$ | Distance between the two drive wheels (axle length) | **m** |
| $\omega_L$ | Angular velocity of the left wheel | **rad/s** |
| $\omega_R$ | Angular velocity of the right wheel | **rad/s** |
| $v$ | Linear (translational) velocity of the robot | **m/s** |
| $\omega$ | Angular (rotational) velocity of the robot | **rad/s** |
| $\theta$ | Heading angle of the robot w.r.t. world X-axis | **rad** |

> [!TIP]
> Always confirm with the question whether $L$ is the **full wheel separation** (between the two contact points) or **half the separation** $2L$ (centre-to-centre via the midpoint). KTU examiners commonly test this distinction.

## 1.4 Why Differential-Drive is the *Canonical* Mobile Robot

* It is the **simplest non-holonomic platform** with a closed-form kinematic solution.
* Used as the base in **iRobot Roomba**, **TurtleBot**, **Pioneer P3-DX**, and the **e-puck** educational robot.
* It is the **first example** taught in nearly every mobile-robotics course (Thrun, Siegwart, Choset) — making it extremely high-yield for KTU examinations.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Non-Holonomic Constraint (Rolling Without Slipping)

A wheel that rolls without slipping along its plane of rotation can only produce velocity **along its axle direction**, never perpendicular to it. For a differential-drive robot with both wheels aligned along the local **x-axis** of the robot frame:

$$-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This is the **kinematic constraint equation**. It restricts the robot to motion **tangential to its instantaneous heading** $\theta$. Because this constraint cannot be integrated to give a position-level restriction (the robot can reach *any* $(x,y)$ by appropriate manoeuvring), it is **non-holonomic**.

## 2.2 Configuration / Pose of the Robot

The robot's state in the 2D world frame is described by the **pose vector**:

$$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(x,y)$ is the position of a reference point on the robot (typically the midpoint of the axle) and $\theta$ is the orientation of the robot's local x-axis with respect to the world X-axis.

## 2.3 Operational Logic Steps — From Wheel Rotation to Robot Motion

1. **Each drive wheel rolls on the ground** without slipping. The contact point of the left wheel moves at speed $v_L = r\,\omega_L$ along the robot's local x-axis. Similarly, $v_R = r\,\omega_R$ for the right wheel.
2. **Linear velocity of the robot** $v$ is the *arithmetic mean* of the two wheel speeds because the robot's reference point is the midpoint of the axle:
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$
3. **Angular velocity of the robot** $\omega$ arises from the *difference* in wheel speeds. The differential speed $(v_R - v_L)$ along the axle of length $L$ produces an **Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR)** at a perpendicular distance $R = \dfrac{L(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}$ from the robot. Hence:
$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$
4. **Transform the body-frame velocity** $(v, 0, \omega)$ to the world frame using the rotation matrix:
$$\begin{bmatrix}\dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta}\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}v \\ 0 \\ \omega\end{bmatrix}$$
5. **Integrate (Euler method)** to obtain the next pose:
$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix mapping $[\omega_L, \omega_R]^T \rightarrow [v, \omega]^T$ is the **Forward Kinematic Matrix** of a differential-drive robot and is the single most frequently asked KTU question on this topic.

## 2.4 Inverse Kinematics

Given a *desired* $(v, \omega)$ for the robot, the required wheel angular velocities are:

$$\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\,\omega}{2r}$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{2v - L\,\omega}{2r}$$

This is used by the robot's **motion controller** when the user issues a command such as *"move forward at 0.3 m/s while turning at 0.2 rad/s"*.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Autonomous vacuum cleaners and warehouse AGVs** use DDR for cost-effective, mechanically simple navigation.
* **Planetary rovers** (NASA's Mars rovers *Spirit*, *Opportunity*, *Curiosity*) are essentially differential-drive (or rocker-bogie differential) platforms.
* **Swarm robotics** research platforms (e.g., Kilobots, GRITSbots) rely on DDR kinematics for distributed coordination.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **one-stop exam reference** for this topic. All quantities are in **SI units** unless stated.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Left wheel contact speed | $v_L = r\,\omega_L$ | Rolling, no slip |
| 2 | Right wheel contact speed | $v_R = r\,\omega_R$ | Rolling, no slip |
| 3 | Linear velocity (forward kinematics) | $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ | Mid-axle reference point |
| 4 | Angular velocity (forward kinematics) | $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ | $L$ = wheel separation |
| 5 | Non-holonomic constraint | $-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | No lateral motion allowed |
| 6 | Right wheel speed (inverse kinematics) | $\omega_R = \dfrac{2v + L\,\omega}{2r}$ | Used in controllers |
| 7 | Left wheel speed (inverse kinematics) | $\omega_L = \dfrac{2v - L\,\omega}{2r}$ | Used in controllers |
| 8 | Radius of Instantaneous Curvature | $R = \dfrac{L}{2}\cdot\dfrac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$ | Defined only when $\omega_R \neq \omega_L$ |
| 9 | Pose update (Euler) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t$ | Discrete-time integration |
| 10 | Pose update (Euler) | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t$ | Discrete-time integration |
| 11 | Pose update (Euler) | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t$ | Discrete-time integration |
| 12 | Forward kinematic matrix | $\begin{bmatrix}v \\ \omega\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}r/2 & r/2 \\ -r/L & r/L\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\omega_L \\ \omega_R\end{bmatrix}$ | Compact matrix form |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Kinematic Equations from First Principles

**Given:** Two wheels of radius $r$ mounted on a common axle of length $L$. The contact points move at $v_L = r\,\omega_L$ (left) and $v_R = r\,\omega_R$ (right) along the axle.

**Step 1 — Express the contact-point velocities in the body frame.**

The left contact point is at body-frame coordinates $(-L/2, 0)$ and the right at $(+L/2, 0)$. Their body-frame velocities are:

$$\mathbf{v}_L^{(body)} = \begin{bmatrix} r\,\omega_L \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{v}_R^{(body)} = \begin{bmatrix} r\,\omega_R \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Write the rigid-body velocity field.**

For a rigid body translating at $\mathbf{v}_{ref}$ and rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ about the reference point, the velocity of a point at body-frame offset $\mathbf{p}$ is:

$$\mathbf{v}(\mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{v}_{ref} + \omega\,\hat{k}\times\mathbf{p}$$

Here $\mathbf{v}_{ref} = (v, 0)$ and $\omega = (0, 0, \omega)$. The cross product $\hat{k}\times\mathbf{p} = (-p_y, p_x)$.

**Step 3 — Apply to the left wheel contact point** $\mathbf{p}_L = (-L/2, 0)$:

$$\begin{aligned} v_L^{(x)} &= v + \omega \cdot 0 = v \\ v_L^{(y)} &= 0 + \omega \cdot (-L/2) = -\frac{\omega L}{2} \end{aligned}$$

But $v_L$ is purely along the body x-axis, so $v_L^{(y)} = 0$. This gives the **transverse constraint** $-\omega L/2 = 0$, which is trivially true and does not yet give us $v$.

**Step 4 — Apply to the right wheel contact point** $\mathbf{p}_R = (+L/2, 0)$:

$$\begin{aligned} v_R^{(x)} &= v \\ v_R^{(y)} &= +\frac{\omega L}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Again the y-component must vanish (no slip in the wheel-rolling direction), giving the *constraint* $+\omega L/2 = 0$, but this is misleading — we must remember the wheel is *forced* to roll in its local x-direction by the no-slip condition. To extract $v$ and $\omega$ we must use the x-components and the kinematic fact that the contact-point speed in the world frame equals the wheel rotation rate.

**Step 5 — Use the no-slip condition properly.**

The actual rolling condition is that the wheel *rotates such that* the contact-point speed relative to the ground equals $r\omega_{wheel}$ along the wheel's rolling direction (body x-axis). Combining the two wheels:

$$r\,\omega_L = v - \frac{\omega L}{2} \quad \text{(left wheel)}$$

$$r\,\omega_R = v + \frac{\omega L}{2} \quad \text{(right wheel)}$$

These two equations arise because the body-frame x-velocity of each contact point equals the rigid-body velocity $v$ plus the contribution from rotation $\pm \omega L/2$.

**Step 6 — Solve the linear system by addition and subtraction.**

*Adding* the two equations:

$$r(\omega_L + \omega_R) = 2v \quad \Longrightarrow \quad v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}$$

*Subtracting* (right minus left):

$$r(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \omega L \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

This is the **complete forward kinematic model**.

> [!TIP]
> Examiner's favourite trick: ask for the **inverse** of the forward matrix. Make sure you can derive the matrix inverse from scratch using a determinant.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inverse Kinematic Equations

Starting from the two equations:

$$r\,\omega_L = v - \frac{\omega L}{2}, \qquad r\,\omega_R = v + \frac{\omega L}{2}$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ in terms of $v$ and $\omega$:**

$$\omega_L = \frac{v}{r} - \frac{\omega L}{2r}$$

$$\omega_R = \frac{v}{r} + \frac{\omega L}{2r}$$

**Step 2 — Put them in the standard (common) form by finding a common denominator $2r$:**

$$\omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}$$

$$\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}$$

These are the **inverse kinematics** formulae.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pose Update from Velocity Integration

**Step 1 — World-frame velocity.**

The body-frame linear velocity is $(v, 0)$. To express in the world frame, rotate by $\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= v\cos\theta - 0\cdot\sin\theta = v\cos\theta \\ \dot{y} &= v\sin\theta + 0\cdot\cos\theta = v\sin\theta \\ \dot{\theta} &= \omega \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply forward Euler integration** with time step $\Delta t$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \dot{x}_k\,\Delta t = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + \dot{y}_k\,\Delta t = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t$$

This is the **discrete-time dead-reckoning update** that all DDR wheel-encoders use to estimate pose.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem.** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheel separation $L = 0.30$ m. The left wheel spins at $\omega_L = 4$ rad/s and the right at $\omega_R = 6$ rad/s. The robot's current pose is $(x, y, \theta) = (0, 0, 0)$ and $\Delta t = 0.1$ s. Compute the new pose.

**Solution.**

*Step 1 — Linear velocity:*

$$v = \frac{0.05\,(6 + 4)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \cdot 10}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$$

*Step 2 — Angular velocity:*

$$\omega = \frac{0.05\,(6 - 4)}{0.30} = \frac{0.05 \cdot 2}{0.30} = \frac{0.10}{0.30} = 0.333 \text{ rad/s}$$

*Step 3 — Pose update:*

$$\begin{aligned} x_1 &= 0 + 0.25 \cdot \cos(0) \cdot 0.1 = 0.025 \text{ m} \\ y_1 &= 0 + 0.25 \cdot \sin(0) \cdot 0.1 = 0 \text{ m} \\ \theta_1 &= 0 + 0.333 \cdot 0.1 = 0.0333 \text{ rad} \end{aligned}$$

The robot has moved 2.5 cm forward and turned by 0.0333 rad $\approx 1.91^\circ$ counter-clockwise (positive convention).

## 3.5 Special Manoeuvres (Examiner Favourites)

| Manoeuvre | Condition | Result |
|:----------|:----------|:-------|
| **Straight-line motion** | $\omega_L = \omega_R = \omega_0$ | $v = r\omega_0$, $\omega = 0$ |
| **Pure rotation in place** | $\omega_R = -\omega_L = \omega_0$ | $v = 0$, $\omega = 2r\omega_0 / L$ |
| **Stationary** | $\omega_L = \omega_R = 0$ | $v = 0$, $\omega = 0$ |
| **Arc of radius R** | $\omega_R = \omega_L \neq 0$ and unequal | $R = L(\omega_R + \omega_L)/[2(\omega_R - \omega_L)]$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Configuration of a Differential-Drive Robot

The diagram below shows the top-view of a differential-drive robot. The two drive wheels are on the **lateral axle**; the **passive caster** at the front or rear supplies only the third contact point needed for static stability.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph RBT["DIFFERENTIAL DRIVE ROBOT TOP VIEW"]
        WHR["Right Drive Wheel radius r"]
        WHL["Left Drive Wheel radius r"]
        AXLE["Wheel Base L"]
        CAST["Passive Castor Wheel"]
        BODY["Robot Chassis"]
        MR["Motor M Right"]
        ML["Motor M Left"]
        SENSOR["Onboard Sensors IMU LIDAR Camera"]
    end
    MR --> WHR
    ML --> WHL
    WHR --- AXLE
    AXLE --- WHL
    WHR --- BODY
    WHL --- BODY
    BODY --- CAST
    BODY --- SENSOR
```

## 4.2 Kinematic Signal-Flow Block Diagram

The block diagram below shows the **forward and inverse kinematic** signal flow. The **forward kinematic** path converts measured wheel speeds to body-frame velocity; the **inverse kinematic** path converts a body-frame velocity command into the required wheel speeds.

```mermaid
graph TD
    CMD["Desired Body Velocity vc wc"] --> IK["Inverse Kinematics Block"]
    IK --> WLCMD["Required Left Wheel Speed wL"]
    IK --> WRCMD["Required Right Wheel Speed wR"]
    WLCMD --> MTRL["Left Motor Driver"]
    WRCMD --> MTRR["Right Motor Driver"]
    MTRL --> ENCL["Left Encoder Measures wL actual"]
    MTRR --> ENCR["Right Encoder Measures wR actual"]
    ENCL --> FK["Forward Kinematics Block"]
    ENCR --> FK
    FK --> VEL["Computed Body Velocity v omega"]
    VEL --> INT["Pose Integrator Euler Method"]
    INT --> POSE["Updated Pose x y theta"]
    POSE --> ODOM["Odometry Output"]
```

## 4.3 Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR) Geometry

For a differential-drive robot on a circular arc, both wheels share a common **Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR)** lying on the line through the wheel axles, extended outward. The diagram below shows the geometric relationship.

```mermaid
graph LR
    WHL["Left Wheel Contact"] --- AX["Axle of Length L"]
    AX --- WHR["Right Wheel Contact"]
    WHL --- ICR["ICR Point on Axle Extension"]
    ICR --- WHR
    RADL["Radius R minus L over 2 to ICR"] --- ICR
    RADR["Radius R plus L over 2 to ICR"] --- ICR
    RADR --- WHR
    RADL --- WHL
```

The geometric relations are:

$$R - \frac{L}{2} = \frac{v_L}{\omega} = \frac{r\,\omega_L}{\omega}$$

$$R + \frac{L}{2} = \frac{v_R}{\omega} = \frac{r\,\omega_R}{\omega}$$

Solving the two equations simultaneously yields the previously derived $R = \dfrac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$ and $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$.

## 4.4 Coordinate Frame Convention

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph WORLD["WORLD FRAME XW YW"]
        XW["X World axis fixed"]
        YW["Y World axis fixed"]
    end
    subgraph BODY["ROBOT BODY FRAME XR YR"]
        ROBOT["Robot at pose x y theta"]
        XR["X Body axis heading direction"]
        YR["Y Body axis lateral"]
    end
    XW --- ROBOT
    ROBOT --- XR
    ROBOT --- YR
    THETA["Heading Angle theta measured from XW to XR counter clockwise"] --- XR
    THETA --- XW
```
