# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test **recall and direct application**. They are short, definition/numerical-substitution style, and require crisp, formula-driven answers.

---

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define a differential-drive mobile robot. With a neat sketch, derive the forward kinematic equations relating the wheel angular velocities to the linear and angular velocities of the robot.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

* **Definition** (1 mark): A differential-drive robot is a non-holonomic mobile robot with two independently driven wheels on a common axle; differential speed between wheels produces rotation.
* **Sketch** (1 mark): Two drive wheels of radius $r$ separated by $L$, plus a passive caster. Show body-frame $x_R$ along axle and $\theta$ to world frame.
* **Derivation** (1 mark): Show that

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

* **Final mark allocation**: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the sketch, 1 mark for the equations (with at least a 2-line derivation, not just the result).

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is a non-holonomic constraint? Write the non-holonomic constraint equation for a differential-drive robot and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

* **Definition of non-holonomic constraint** (1 mark): A constraint on the *velocities* of a system that is **not integrable** to a positional constraint. The system can reach any configuration by appropriate manoeuvring (e.g., a car can parallel-park).
* **Equation** (1 mark):

$$-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

* **Significance** (1 mark): It expresses the fact that the robot **cannot move sideways** — its instantaneous velocity is always aligned with its heading $\theta$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** each. They are typically divided into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solve *both* sub-parts to secure full marks.

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Derive the forward and inverse kinematic models of a differential-drive mobile robot. A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.04$ m and wheel separation $L = 0.20$ m. If the left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 3$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 5$ rad/s, compute (i) the linear velocity of the robot, (ii) the angular velocity of the robot, and (iii) the radius of curvature of the path. If the robot starts at $(x, y, \theta) = (0, 0, 0)$, compute the new pose after $\Delta t = 0.5$ s.**

#### Sub-Part (a) — Forward Kinematics Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1** (1 mark): Define the geometry: two wheels of radius $r$, axle length $L$, contact-point speeds $v_L = r\omega_L$ and $v_R = r\omega_R$.

**Step 2** (2 marks): Use the rigid-body velocity field. For the left and right contact points:

$$r\omega_L = v - \frac{\omega L}{2}, \qquad r\omega_R = v + \frac{\omega L}{2}$$

**Step 3** (2 marks): Solve the linear system:

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

**Step 4** (1 mark): Express the result in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix}v \\ \omega\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}r/2 & r/2 \\ -r/L & r/L\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\omega_L \\ \omega_R\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5** (1 mark): State the inverse kinematic equations by matrix inversion (or algebraic rearrangement):

$$\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}, \qquad \omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}$$

#### Sub-Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $r = 0.04$ m, $L = 0.20$ m, $\omega_L = 3$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 5$ rad/s, $\Delta t = 0.5$ s, initial pose $(0, 0, 0)$.

**(i) Linear velocity** (2 marks):

$$v = \frac{0.04 \times (5 + 3)}{2} = \frac{0.04 \times 8}{2} = \frac{0.32}{2} = 0.16 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Angular velocity** (2 marks):

$$\omega = \frac{0.04 \times (5 - 3)}{0.20} = \frac{0.04 \times 2}{0.20} = \frac{0.08}{0.20} = 0.40 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iii) Radius of curvature** (1 mark):

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.16}{0.40} = 0.40 \text{ m}$$

(Equivalently, $R = L(\omega_R + \omega_L) / [2(\omega_R - \omega_L)] = 0.20 \times 8 / (2 \times 2) = 1.6/4 = 0.40$ m. ✓)

**New pose after $\Delta t = 0.5$ s** (2 marks):

$$\begin{aligned} x_1 &= 0 + 0.16 \cos(0) \times 0.5 = 0.08 \text{ m} \\ y_1 &= 0 + 0.16 \sin(0) \times 0.5 = 0 \text{ m} \\ \theta_1 &= 0 + 0.40 \times 0.5 = 0.20 \text{ rad} \approx 11.46^\circ \end{aligned}$$

**Final pose:** $(0.08, 0, 0.20)$ — robot moved 8 cm forward and turned 0.20 rad CCW.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the non-holonomic constraint equation for a differential-drive mobile robot and explain why it is *non-holonomic*. Show that the robot's configuration-space velocity can be expressed as $\dot{\xi} = [\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0]^T\,v + [0, 0, 1]^T\,\omega$.

**(b)** A differential-drive robot with $r = 0.05$ m and $L = 0.30$ m is commanded to move with $v = 0.2$ m/s and $\omega = 0.5$ rad/s. Compute the required wheel angular velocities. If the robot executes this command for $2$ seconds starting from pose $(1, 2, \pi/4)$ rad, find the final pose using the Euler integration method.

#### Sub-Part (a) — Non-Holonomic Constraint Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1** (1 mark): The instantaneous velocity of any point on a rolling wheel must be aligned with the wheel's rolling direction. For a differential-drive robot, both wheels roll along the body x-axis. The lateral body y-velocity at the contact point must therefore be zero.

**Step 2** (2 marks): The body-frame velocity at the robot reference point is $(v, 0, \omega)$. Rotating to the world frame using $R_z(\theta)$ gives:

$$\begin{bmatrix}\dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta}\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}v \\ 0 \\ \omega\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3** (2 marks): Compute the dot product of $(\dot{x}, \dot{y})$ with the vector perpendicular to the heading $(-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$. This must be zero (no lateral motion):

$$-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This is the **non-holonomic constraint equation**.

**Step 4** (1 mark): Explain *non-holonomic*: The constraint is on *velocities*, not on the configuration variables $x, y, \theta$. Although the constraint restricts *admissible velocities*, it does **not** restrict the *reachable configurations* — the robot can still reach any $(x, y)$ by appropriate manoeuvring.

**Step 5** (1 mark): The decomposed form is obtained by setting $u_1 = v$ and $u_2 = \omega$ and writing:

$$\dot{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0\end{bmatrix} u_1 + \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 0 \\ 1\end{bmatrix} u_2$$

This is the **control-affine form** of the kinematic model, used in non-linear control design.

#### Sub-Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $v = 0.2$ m/s, $\omega = 0.5$ rad/s, $\Delta t = 2$ s, initial pose $(1, 2, \pi/4)$.

**Required wheel angular velocities** (3 marks):

$$\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r} = \frac{2(0.2) + 0.30(0.5)}{2(0.05)} = \frac{0.4 + 0.15}{0.10} = \frac{0.55}{0.10} = 5.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r} = \frac{2(0.2) - 0.30(0.5)}{0.10} = \frac{0.4 - 0.15}{0.10} = \frac{0.25}{0.10} = 2.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Pose update** (4 marks):

$$\begin{aligned} x_1 &= 1 + 0.2 \cos(\pi/4) \times 2 = 1 + 0.2 \times 0.7071 \times 2 = 1 + 0.2828 = 1.2828 \text{ m} \\ y_1 &= 2 + 0.2 \sin(\pi/4) \times 2 = 2 + 0.2 \times 0.7071 \times 2 = 2 + 0.2828 = 2.2828 \text{ m} \\ \theta_1 &= \pi/4 + 0.5 \times 2 = 0.7854 + 1.0 = 1.7854 \text{ rad} \end{aligned}$$

**Final pose:** $(1.2828 \text{ m}, 2.2828 \text{ m}, 1.7854 \text{ rad})$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Sign errors on $\omega_L$ vs $\omega_R$**: When applying the formula $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$, the order of subtraction is *crucial*. A reversed order gives the wrong sign for $\omega$, which propagates to the heading update. Marks lost here cascade through the entire problem.
> 2. **Confusing $L$ and $2L$**: Many textbooks define $L$ as the *half* wheel separation, so the formula is $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$ where $L$ is half-distance. The KTU convention uses **full wheel separation**. Read the question stem *very carefully*.
> 3. **Forgetting to update $\theta$ before the next step**: In multi-step Euler integration, $\theta$ changes during the time step, but the simple Euler method uses $\theta_k$ throughout. This is an approximation, and the examiner may deduct a mark if the student uses the "exact" integrated form $x_{k+1} = x_k + (v/\omega)[\sin(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta_k]$ without justification. Stick to Euler for the KTU exam.
> 4. **Misstating holonomy**: A common misconception is to call the constraint "holonomic" because the robot *can* reach any pose. Reachability is *not* the criterion for holonomy. Holonomic constraints are *integrable* to a positional equation. The DDR constraint is a *velocity-level* equation only — it is **non-holonomic**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Mobile Robotics — Module 1**

* ☐ The **forward kinematic equations** of a differential-drive robot are:
$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$
* ☐ The **inverse kinematic equations** are:
$$\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}, \qquad \omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}$$
* ☐ The **non-holonomic constraint** is $-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$, forbidding lateral motion.
* ☐ The **state vector** is $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$. The world-frame velocity is $[\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T = [v\cos\theta, v\sin\theta, \omega]^T$.
* ☐ The **forward kinematic matrix** $J = \begin{bmatrix}r/2 & r/2 \\ -r/L & r/L\end{bmatrix}$ maps $[\omega_L, \omega_R]^T$ to $[v, \omega]^T$. Its inverse $J^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix}1/r & -L/(2r) \\ 1/r & L/(2r)\end{bmatrix}$ maps the other way.
* ☐ **Euler integration** for pose update:
$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t, \quad y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t, \quad \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t$$
* ☐ **Three canonical manoeuvres** to remember:
   - $\omega_L = \omega_R$ → **straight line**, $v = r\omega_0$, $\omega = 0$.
   - $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ → **in-place rotation**, $v = 0$, $\omega = 2r\omega_0/L$.
   - $\omega_L = \omega_R = 0$ → **stationary**.
* ☐ **Radius of curvature** of the robot's path: $R = v/\omega = L(\omega_R + \omega_L)/[2(\omega_R - \omega_L)]$.
* ☐ **Degrees of freedom (DOF)** of the configuration space: **3** $(x, y, \theta)$.
* ☐ **Degrees of freedom of admissible velocities**: **2** $(v, \omega)$ — one DOF is lost to the non-holonomic constraint.
* ☐ **Number of actuators**: typically **2** (one motor per drive wheel) — hence "differential".
* ☐ Real-world examples: **TurtleBot, Roomba, Pioneer P3-DX, Mars rovers, e-puck**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Robot Maneuverability - Degree of Mobility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mobile Robot Maneuverability - Degree of Mobility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Mobile Robotics (PBRUT504)**, the term **Maneuverability** refers to a mobile robot's ability to achieve arbitrary configurations and motions in its working environment. It is quantified by two complementary sub-properties:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$):** The number of **instantaneously controllable degrees of freedom (DOF)** of a mobile robot that can be directly achieved by the wheels' actuators. It describes *how freely* the robot can move at any given instant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$):** The number of wheels about which the robot can be steered. Steering a wheel changes the wheel's *rolling axis*, and by extension, the robot's instantaneous center of rotation (ICR).

**Total Robot Maneuverability** is mathematically defined as:

$$M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$

Where:
- $\delta_m \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ — bounded by the **3 DOF** available to any rigid body moving in a 2D plane (forward translation, lateral translation, in-place rotation).
- $\delta_s$ — typically ranges from $0$ to $N$ (where $N$ is the number of wheels).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A robot's *instantaneous* motion is governed by $\delta_m$ (actuator-driven), while its *controllability over time* is enhanced by $\delta_s$ (steering reconfigurability).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a **standard shopping cart** in a supermarket:

- The **two rear wheels** are *fixed* (cannot be steered) but are *driven* (roll freely when pushed). They contribute to **$\delta_m$** — they let the cart *move forward/backward and turn* (rotation), but the cart *cannot slide sideways*.
- The **two front wheels** are *swiveling castors* — they can rotate freely in any direction (high steerability) but are not driven.

A shopping cart has **$\delta_m = 1$** (can roll forward), **$\delta_s = 2$** (the castor pair swivels), and **$M = 3$**. This is exactly equivalent to a **Synchro-Drive robot** in mobile robotics!

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic:** "**M**obility = **M**ovement (driven motors). **S**teerability = **S**wiveling (steered wheels)."

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Mobile Robotics

The Degree of Mobility directly determines:
1. **Trajectory Planning complexity** — Higher $\delta_m$ means more paths are feasible.
2. **Workspace coverage** — Critical for search, rescue, and warehouse robots.
3. **Static Stability** — A robot with $M \geq 3$ is **statically stable**, meaning it can hold any configuration without external support.
4. **Control algorithm selection** — Holonomic vs. non-holonomic constraint formulation.

A robot with $\delta_m = 3$ is called **omnidirectional** (or *holonomic*); robots with $\delta_m < 3$ are **non-holonomic** and suffer from path constraints (e.g., a car cannot move sideways without first moving forward).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Fundamental Degrees of Freedom in 2D

Every rigid body moving on a flat 2D plane possesses exactly **3 DOF**:

1. **Forward / Backward translation** (along x-axis)
2. **Lateral translation** (along y-axis, sideways)
3. **Rotation** (about the z-axis, perpendicular to plane)

The **instantaneous motion** of a mobile robot is the algebraic sum of the constraints imposed by its wheels. The Degree of Mobility, $\delta_m$, captures how many of these 3 DOF the robot can *directly actuate* at any instant.

## 2.2 Wheel Classification: The Foundation of Mobility

Every wheel in mobile robotics falls into one of **four canonical types**, each contributing uniquely to $\delta_m$ and $\delta_s$:

| Wheel Type | Driven? | Steered? | Contribution to $\delta_m$ | Contribution to $\delta_s$ | Slips Allowed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Standard Wheel** | Optional | ❌ No | 1 (if driven) | 0 | None (rigid) |
| **Steerable Standard Wheel** | Optional | ✅ Yes | 1 (if driven) | 1 | None (rigid) |
| **Castor Wheel** | ❌ No | ✅ Self-aligning | 0 | 1 | None |
| **Swedish / Omni Wheel** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | **2** (X + Y) | 0 | Lateral slip |
| **Mecanum Wheel** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | **2** (X + Y) | 0 | Lateral slip |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule (Siegwart, Nourbakhsh, Scaramuzza, 2011 — *Introduction to Autonomous Mobile Robots*):** For a wheel to contribute to $\delta_m$, it must be **actuated (driven)**. A passive (undriven) wheel contributes 0 to mobility, regardless of its type.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Total Robot Maneuverability | $M \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 2 | $\delta_m \leq 3$ | Upper bound in 2D plane | Equality → omnidirectional |
| 3 | $\delta_s \leq N$ | Cannot exceed wheel count | $N$ = total number of wheels |
| 4 | $M \geq 3$ | Static stability condition | Required for full planar control |
| 5 | $\delta_m^{(standard)} \leq 2$ | Bound for rigid (non-slipping) standard wheels | Sideways motion impossible |
| 6 | $\dot{q} = A(q) \cdot \eta$ | Kinematic constraint equation | $A(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times \delta_m}$ |

Where in formula (6):
- $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — robot's pose in the world frame.
- $\eta \in \mathbb{R}^{\delta_m}$ — actuator input vector.
- $A(q)$ — the *kinematic constraint matrix* (Jacobian-like).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility: Why the Industry Cares

| Application Domain | Required $\delta_m$ | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon, FedEx)** | $\delta_m = 3$ | Tight aisles → must rotate in-place and translate sideways |
| **Autonomous Cars (Ackermann)** | $\delta_m = 1, \delta_s = 2$ | High speed on roads; lateral slip dangerous |
| **Mars Rovers (Curiosity)** | $\delta_m = 1, \delta_s = 4$ (Rocker-Bogie) | Rock-stability over rugged terrain |
| **Indoor Service Robots** | $\delta_m = 2, \delta_s = 1$ | Differential drive: cost-effective, simple control |
| **Surgical Robots (Microsurgery)** | $\delta_m = 3$ | Maneuverability inside constrained anatomy |

## 2.5 The Three Classes of Wheeled Mobile Robots

Siegwart's classification groups robots by their $(\delta_m, \delta_s)$ tuple:

- **Class (3, 0):** Omnidirectional, e.g., 4 Mecanum wheels.
- **Class (2, 1) or (2, 0):** Differential drive with castor, or 2-wheel self-balancing.
- **Class (1, 2):** Tricycle, Ackermann car-like steering.
- **Class (0, 3):** Purely steerable (rare; no driven wheel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Stability** demands $M \geq 3$. Robots like the differential drive (1 driven wheel + 1 castor = $M=2$) violate this — yet they work! This is because the **un-actuated DOF** (rotation about ICR) is *controllable through time integration*, not instantaneously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Degree of Mobility of a Differential Drive Robot

**Robot Configuration:** Two independently driven wheels on a common axle, separated by distance $2L$, plus one (or two) passive castor wheels for balance.

**Step 1 — Identify the wheels and their actuation:**

The differential drive has:
- **2 driven fixed standard wheels** (left and right).
- **0 steered wheels** (the wheels' rolling axes are fixed to the robot frame).
- **1 (or 2) undriven castor wheels** (purely passive — provides static balance).

**Step 2 — Apply the rule that each driven standard wheel contributes 1 to $\delta_m$:**

Each driven wheel controls exactly **one scalar input** — its angular velocity $\omega_i$.

Therefore:

$$\delta_m = N_{driven}^{(standard)} \cdot 1 = 2 \cdot 1 = 2$$

**Step 3 — Verify by kinematic analysis (forward kinematics):**

Let $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ be the left and right wheel angular velocities, and $r$ the wheel radius. The robot's linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$ are:

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{2L}$$

The robot's instantaneous twist (velocity in SE(2)) is:

$$\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Count columns of the input matrix:**

The input matrix is $B \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}$, so the rank is **2**. Hence:

$$\boxed{\delta_m = 2 \quad \text{and} \quad \delta_s = 0, \quad M = 2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The differential drive **cannot translate sideways** (lateral motion). This is a *non-holonomic* constraint — the robot's sideways velocity $\dot{y}$ in the world frame is a *function* of forward velocity and rotation, not an independent input.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Degree of Mobility of a Synchronous (Synchro) Drive Robot

**Robot Configuration:** Three (or four) wheels, **all driven** and **all steered synchronously** (the steering of every wheel is mechanically linked to rotate by the same angle).

**Step 1 — Identify the actuation:**

- **3 driven wheels** → contributes 3 to $\delta_m$ *naively*.
- **3 steered wheels** → all steered *together* (one common steering angle) → contributes only 1 to $\delta_s$ (not 3, because the steering is *not independent*).

**Step 2 — Determine $\delta_m$ via the rank of the actuation matrix:**

All three wheels have a common steering angle $\phi$ and independent drive speeds $\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3$. The kinematic relationship is:

$$\dot{q} = A(\phi) \cdot \eta, \quad \text{where} \quad \eta = [\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3]^T$$

The $3 \times 3$ matrix $A(\phi)$ is **generically full-rank** for almost all $\phi$ (except degenerate cases like $\phi = 0$ where all wheels are parallel). Therefore:

$$\delta_m = \text{rank}(A) = 3$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\delta_s$:**

The robot has **one** independent steering actuator controlling the orientation of all wheels simultaneously:

$$\boxed{\delta_m = 3, \quad \delta_s = 1, \quad M = 4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although $M = 4 > 3$, the robot is still **kinematically equivalent to an omnidirectional platform** because the synchronous steering does not add *new* instantaneous DOF — it only adds *configurability* (the robot's appearance/footprint can be reoriented).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Degree of Mobility of a Tricycle (Ackermann-Like)

**Robot Configuration:** One driven, steerable wheel at the front; two fixed (undriven, unsteered) wheels at the rear.

**Step 1 — Identify the wheels:**
- **1 driven wheel** (front, steerable).
- **2 undriven fixed standard wheels** (rear).

**Step 2 — Count contributions to $\delta_m$:**

Only the front wheel is driven:

$$\delta_m = 1$$

**Step 3 — Count contributions to $\delta_s$:**

The front wheel's steering angle $\phi$ is an independent input; the rear wheels are fixed:

$$\delta_s = 1$$

**Step 4 — Forward kinematics (for verification):**

Let the wheelbase be $L$ (distance from front to rear axle). The kinematic bicycle model is:

$$\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\phi}{L}$$

The input vector is $\eta = [v, \phi]^T$ (2 inputs), but the *instantaneously* controllable DOF are:

$$\boxed{\delta_m = 1, \quad \delta_s = 1, \quad M = 2}$$

**Step 5 — Ackermann variant (car-like):**

For a car with 1 driven rear axle (2 wheels) and 2 steered front wheels (linked, so $\delta_s = 1$):

$$\delta_m = 1, \quad \delta_s = 1, \quad M = 2$$

But the *rear* axle counts as one driven "unit" (both wheels turn at the same speed — or have a differential).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Degree of Mobility of a 4-Wheeled Mecanum Drive

**Robot Configuration:** Four Mecanum wheels arranged in a rectangular pattern, each independently driven.

**Step 1 — Properties of the Mecanum wheel:**

A Mecanum wheel has **small peripheral rollers** at 45° to the wheel plane. As a result, the wheel's *effective rolling direction* is at 45° to the wheel's orientation.

The wheel's velocity vector has components along **two orthogonal axes** (its driven direction + lateral slip direction).

**Step 2 — Contribution per wheel:**

Each driven Mecanum wheel contributes **2 DOF** to $\delta_m$ (because it can produce force in 2 independent directions through slip).

With 4 such wheels:

$$\delta_m = 4 \times 2 = 8 \quad \text{(naively, in actuator space)}$$

**Step 3 — Constraint by rigid-body kinematics:**

The 4 wheels are rigidly attached to the same chassis (a single rigid body), which has only **3 DOF** in 2D. The chassis is the bottleneck.

Therefore:

$$\delta_m = \min(8, 3) = 3$$

No wheels are steered, so:

$$\delta_s = 0$$

$$\boxed{\delta_m = 3, \quad \delta_s = 0, \quad M = 3}$$

This is the **omnidirectional mecanum platform** — the gold standard for warehouse robots requiring full 2D mobility.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Canonical Configurations

| Robot Type | Driven Wheels | Steered Wheels | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $M$ | Class | Holonomic? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Differential Drive** | 2 (standard) | 0 | 2 | 0 | 2 | (2,0) | ❌ No |
| **Differential + Castor** | 2 (standard) | 1 (castor) | 2 | 1 | 3 | (2,1) | ❌ No |
| **Synchronous Drive** | 3 (standard) | 3 (linked) | 3 | 1 | 4 | (3,1) | ✅ Yes |
| **Tricycle** | 1 (standard) | 1 (standard) | 1 | 1 | 2 | (1,1) | ❌ No |
| **Ackermann (Car)** | 1 axle (standard) | 1 axle (linked) | 1 | 1 | 2 | (1,1) | ❌ No |
| **4-Mecanum** | 4 (omni) | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | (3,0) | ✅ Yes |
| **3-Omni (Swedish)** | 3 (omni) | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | (3,0) | ✅ Yes |
| **Balancing 2-Wheeler** | 1 + reaction wheel | 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 | (2,1) | ❌ No (dynamic) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map: Wheel Types and Their Contributions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Mobile Robot Wheel"] --> B["Standard Wheel"]
    A --> C["Special Wheel"]
    
    B --> B1["Fixed Standard"]
    B --> B2["Steerable Standard"]

    C --> C1["Swedish Omni Wheel"]
    C --> C2["Mecanum Wheel"]
    
    B1 --> D1["Driven? 1 to dm"]
    B1 --> D2["Steered? 0 to ds"]
    
    B2 --> E1["Driven? 1 to dm"]
    B2 --> E2["Steered? 1 to ds"]
    
    B3 --> F1["Driven? 0 to dm"]
    B3 --> F2["Steered? 1 to ds"]
    
    C1 --> G1["Driven? 2 to dm"]
    C1 --> G2["Steered? 0 to ds"]
    
    C2 --> H1["Driven? 2 to dm"]
    C2 --> H2["Steered? 0 to ds"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Maneuverability Classification Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input["Robot Wheel\nSpecification"] --> Proc["Maneuverability\nClassification Engine"]
    
    Proc --> StageA["Count Driven Wheels\nNdriven"]
    Proc --> StageB["Count Steered Wheels\nNsteered"]
    Proc --> StageC["Detect Wheel Type\nStandard/Omni/Mecanum"]
    
    StageA --> Compute["Compute dm and ds"]
    StageB --> Compute
    StageC --> Compute
    
    Compute --> Output1["Class dm ds tuple"]
    Compute --> Output2["Maneuverability M"]
    Compute --> Output3["Static Stability?\nM greater or equal 3"]
    Compute --> Output4["Holonomic?\ndm equals 3"]
    
    Output1 --> DB[(KTU Exam Answer)]
    Output2 --> DB
    Output3 --> DB
    Output4 --> DB
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy: The Five Fundamental 2D Motions vs. Robot Classes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Plane["Rigid Body in 2D Plane\n3 Degrees of Freedom"] --> M1["Forward Translation\nalong X axis"]
    Plane --> M2["Lateral Translation\nalong Y axis"]
    Plane --> M3["Rotation\nabout Z axis"]
    
    M1 --> RobotA["Differential Drive\nAchieves M1 and M3"]
    M2 --> RobotB["4 Mecanum Drive\nAchieves M1 M2 and M3"]
    M3 --> RobotC["Car Ackermann\nAchieves M1 and M3"]
    
    RobotA --> ClassA["Class 2 0\nNon-holonomic"]
    RobotB --> ClassB["Class 3 0\nHolonomic Omnidirectional"]
    RobotC --> ClassC["Class 1 1\nNon-holonomic Car-like"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Identifying $\delta_m$ in an Exam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Given Robot Description"] --> Q1{"Is the wheel DRIVEN?\nActuated with motor"}
    
    Q1 -->|NO| ZeroA["Contributes 0 to dm"]
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2{"What TYPE of wheel?"}
    
    Q2 -->|Standard Fixed or Steerable| OneA["Contributes 1 to dm"]
    Q2 -->|Omni or Mecanum| TwoA["Contributes 2 to dm"]
    
    OneA --> Sum["Sum across ALL driven wheels"]
    TwoA --> Sum
    
    ZeroA --> End["Final dm = sum"]
    Sum --> Cap["Cap dm at MAX = 3\nrigid body limit"]
    Cap --> End
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Direct Recall)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]`
> **Define the terms *Degree of Mobility* and *Degree of Steerability* of a wheeled mobile robot. How are they related to total robot maneuverability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$)** is the number of degrees of freedom of a mobile robot that can be *instantaneously and directly* controlled by the wheel actuators. It is bounded above by 3, which is the number of degrees of freedom of a rigid body in a 2D plane.

The **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$)** is the number of wheels whose steering angles can be independently controlled (each steering axis adds one to $\delta_s$).

The **total robot maneuverability** $M$ is their sum:

$$M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$

A robot with $M \geq 3$ is statically stable and can achieve any instantaneous configuration in the plane. *(1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for relation.)*

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
> **What is the difference between a *holonomic* and a *non-holonomic* mobile robot? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **holonomic** robot has $\delta_m = 3$, meaning it can instantaneously move in any direction in the 2D plane (including pure sideways translation and in-place rotation). A **non-holonomic** robot has $\delta_m < 3$ and is subject to kinematic constraints that prevent some instantaneous motions (typically sideways motion).

- **Holonomic example:** A four-wheeled Mecanum drive robot (uses Mecanum wheels with 45° rollers).
- **Non-holonomic example:** A differential drive robot (cannot move sideways without first moving forward and rotating).

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark each example.)*

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1, Full Question]`

> **(a)** Define the Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$) of a mobile robot. A differential drive robot has two independently driven wheels of radius $r = 0.05$ m, separated by a distance of $2L = 0.4$ m, and one passive castor wheel for balance. Determine its $\delta_m$, $\delta_s$, and total maneuverability $M$. Show the kinematic model and verify your answer. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, classify wheeled mobile robots based on their maneuverability. Mention the five fundamental motions of a rigid body in 2D and explain why a car (Ackermann steering) is non-holonomic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**

The Degree of Mobility $\delta_m$ of a mobile robot is the number of degrees of freedom that can be *directly and instantaneously* controlled by the robot's wheel actuators. It is bounded by:

$$\delta_m \leq 3 \quad \text{(DOF of a rigid body in 2D)}$$

**Step 2 — Wheel-by-Wheel Contribution (2 marks):**

- **Left driven wheel:** Standard fixed wheel, driven $\Rightarrow$ contributes **1** to $\delta_m$, **0** to $\delta_s$.
- **Right driven wheel:** Standard fixed wheel, driven $\Rightarrow$ contributes **1** to $\delta_m$, **0** to $\delta_s$.
- **Castor wheel:** Undriven, self-aligning $\Rightarrow$ contributes **0** to $\delta_m$, **1** to $\delta_s$.

**Step 3 — Totals (1 mark):**

$$\delta_m = 1 + 1 + 0 = 2, \quad \delta_s = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1$$

$$M = \delta_m + \delta_s = 2 + 1 = 3$$

**Step 4 — Kinematic Verification (3 marks):**

Let the wheel angular velocities be $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$, wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, axle length $2L = 0.4$ m (so $L = 0.2$ m). The robot's linear and angular velocities are:

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.05(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = 0.025(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{2L} = \frac{0.05(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{0.4} = 0.125(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

The pose kinematics in the world frame is:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= v \cos\theta = 0.025(\omega_R + \omega_L)\cos\theta \\ \dot{y} &= v \sin\theta = 0.025(\omega_R + \omega_L)\sin\theta \\ \dot{\theta} &= 0.125(\omega_R - \omega_L) \end{aligned}$$

The input matrix has **2 independent columns** ($\omega_L, \omega_R$) mapping to a **3D twist** — confirming that $\delta_m = 2$. The castor's free-swiveling adds $\delta_s = 1$.

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Five fundamental motions in 2D (2 marks):**

Every rigid body in a 2D plane has exactly **3 DOF**: forward translation, lateral translation, and rotation. The "five" sometimes refers to: forward, backward, lateral (left), lateral (right), and rotation — but really only **3 independent DOF** exist.

**Step 2 — Classification of robots (3 marks):**

The maneuverability classification by Siegwart, Nourbakhsh, and Scaramuzza groups robots as follows:

| Class | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | Example | Holonomic? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (3, 0) | 3 | 0 | 4-Mecanum, 3-Omni | Yes |
| (2, 1) | 2 | 1 | Differential + Castor | No |
| (1, 2) | 1 | 2 | 4-wheel steering car | No |
| (1, 1) | 1 | 1 | Tricycle, Ackermann | No |
| (2, 0) | 2 | 0 | Pure differential drive | No |

**Step 3 — Why Ackermann is non-holonomic (2 marks):**

The Ackermann-steered car has **one driven axle** (rear wheels) and **one steered axle** (front wheels, linked). This gives $\delta_m = 1$ (only forward/backward motion is directly actuated) and $\delta_s = 1$ (one common steering angle).

Since $\delta_m = 1 < 3$, the robot cannot instantaneously translate sideways. Although it can *eventually* reach a sideways position (by maneuvering), the *non-holonomic constraint*:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

prohibits direct lateral velocity. This is the defining non-holonomic property — a constraint that cannot be integrated into a position constraint.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Internal Choice Pattern]`

> **(a)** A 4-wheeled omnidirectional robot uses Mecanum wheels, all independently driven and not steered. Compute $\delta_m$, $\delta_s$, and $M$. State whether the robot is holonomic and statically stable. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Two mobile robots are given: (i) a tricycle with one driven, steerable front wheel and two passive rear wheels, and (ii) a synchronous drive with three driven, synchronously steered wheels. Compute the maneuverability tuple $(\delta_m, \delta_s, M)$ for each and classify them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Wheel properties (1 mark):**

A Mecanum wheel is a *special wheel* with peripheral rollers at 45°. Each driven Mecanum wheel contributes **2 to $\delta_m$** because the rollers allow force application in two independent directions.

**Step 2 — Compute $\delta_m$ (2 marks):**

Naively:

$$\delta_m^{naive} = 4 \text{ wheels} \times 2 = 8$$

However, the robot is a *single rigid body* in 2D, constrained to **3 DOF**. The rank of the actuation matrix is bounded by 3:

$$\delta_m = \min(8, 3) = 3$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\delta_s$ (1 mark):**

No wheel is steered:

$$\delta_s = 0$$

**Step 4 — Total Maneuverability (1 mark):**

$$M = \delta_m + \delta_s = 3 + 0 = 3$$

**Step 5 — Holonomic and Static Stability (2 marks):**

- **Holonomic?** ✅ Yes — since $\delta_m = 3$, the robot can instantaneously move in any direction (including pure sideways translation and in-place rotation). No kinematic constraint limits its instantaneous twist.
- **Statically stable?** ✅ Yes — since $M = 3 \geq 3$, the robot can hold any pose without external support.

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**Tricycle (i) — 3 marks:**

- Driven wheels: 1 (front, steerable).
- Steered wheels: 1 (front).
- Undriven, unsteered wheels: 2 (rear).

$$\delta_m = 1, \quad \delta_s = 1, \quad M = 2$$

**Class: (1, 1) — Non-holonomic**, since $\delta_m < 3$. The robot has a non-holonomic constraint and must maneuver to change orientation.

**Synchronous Drive (ii) — 4 marks:**

- Driven wheels: 3 (all driven).
- Steered wheels: 3 (all steered *synchronously* via a single mechanical link).

**Counting $\delta_m$ (2 marks):** The three driven wheels produce an input matrix $A(\phi) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ which is generically full-rank:

$$\delta_m = 3$$

**Counting $\delta_s$ (2 marks):** Although there are 3 steered wheels, the steering is *mechanically linked* — only one actuator sets the common steering angle. Thus the steering adds *one* independent DOF:

$$\delta_s = 1, \quad M = 3 + 1 = 4$$

**Class: (3, 1) — Holonomic in motion**, but with extra configurability ($M > 3$ does not add instantaneous DOF, only time-varying reconfigurability).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing wheel count with mobility:** Students often write "the robot has 4 wheels, so $\delta_m = 4$." This is **wrong** — the cap is **3** (rigid body limit) for standard wheels, and 2 per *omni/mecanum* wheel.
> 2. **Counting castor wheels as driven:** A castor is *passive*. It contributes **0 to $\delta_m$** and **1 to $\delta_s$**. Drawing a castor does not mean a driven wheel.
> 3. **Double-counting synchronous steering:** In a synchro-drive, all wheels turn *together* via one actuator. This is **one** $\delta_s$ contribution, not three.
> 4. **Forgetting to cap at 3:** A 4-Mecanum drive gives $\delta_m = 3$ (NOT 8), because the rigid chassis is the bottleneck.
> 5. **Confusing $M$ with holonomy:** A robot with $M \geq 3$ is *statically stable*, but **holonomy specifically requires $\delta_m = 3$**, not $M = 3$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mobility = Movement (driven motors). Steerability = Swiveling (steered wheels).** Memorize this.
- The three DOF of a rigid body in 2D: **forward translation, lateral translation, rotation.**
- Every **driven standard wheel** contributes 1 to $\delta_m$.
- Every **driven Swedish/Mecanum wheel** contributes 2 to $\delta_m$.
- Every **steered wheel** (independently) contributes 1 to $\delta_s$.
- A **castor** is undriven, self-aligning — gives 0 to $\delta_m$, 1 to $\delta_s$.
- **Cap at 3:** Even if a naive sum exceeds 3, the rigid-body limit caps $\delta_m \leq 3$.
- **Holonomic ⟺ $\delta_m = 3$** (full 2D mobility, including sideways motion).
- **Statically stable ⟺ $M \geq 3$** (can hold any pose).
- **Non-holonomic constraint equation** for a unicycle-type robot: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
- **Differential drive** = $\delta_m = 2, \delta_s = 0$ (or 1 with castor), $M = 2$ (or 3).
- **Ackermann car** = $\delta_m = 1, \delta_s = 1$, $M = 2$ — non-holonomic.
- **4-Mecanum** = $\delta_m = 3, \delta_s = 0$, $M = 3$ — omnidirectional and holonomic.
- **Synchro-drive** = $\delta_m = 3, \delta_s = 1$, $M = 4$ — holonomic with reconfigurability.
- The kinematic constraint $\dot{q} = A(q)\eta$ has $\text{rank}(A) = \delta_m$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degree of Steerability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Degree of Steerability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the kinematics of **wheeled mobile robots (WMRs)**, the **Degree of Steerability (denoted as $\delta_s$ or $d_s$)** is defined as the *number of independent steering parameters* that a robot possesses, which can be explicitly controlled to alter the geometric constraints acting on the vehicle's motion. It is one of the three fundamental kinematic descriptors introduced by **Muir and Neuman (1987)** and later refined by **Pin and Killough (1994)** to classify the maneuverability of ground robots.

The full classification hierarchy is defined as follows:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$):** The number of degrees of freedom (DOF) that can be *achieved* in the plane through wheel actuation.
>
> **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$):** The number of *steering* parameters that can be *independently controlled* (i.e., rotated or reoriented) to modify the robot's geometric constraint.
>
> **Degree of Maneuverability ($\delta_M$):** The total number of degrees of freedom controllable by the robot:
> $$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$$

The bound of **3** is significant: any rigid body moving in a 2D plane has 3 independent DOF (two translational: $x, y$; and one rotational: $\theta$). Hence, an *autonomous* mobile robot operating in unobstructed space cannot exceed $\delta_M = 3$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

Imagine two everyday vehicles:

- **A Car (Ackermann Steering):** You control the steering wheel (1 parameter) and the throttle (drives the wheels). The steering is a *single* controllable geometric parameter. So $\delta_s = 1$.
- **A Tank / Skid-Steer:** You independently control the speed of the *left track* and the *right track*. There is no steered wheel; the orientation changes by *differential velocity*, not by steering. So $\delta_s = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Steerability $\neq$ Mobility.** A robot can be highly *mobile* (move in many directions) yet have *zero* steerability (e.g., a differential drive robot). Conversely, a robot can be steered but not very mobile (e.g., a car — it can only move forward/backward along its instantaneous heading).

### 1.3 Why "Degree" Matters in Engineering

The degree of steerability is a *designer's metric*. When planning a robot, one must ask:
1. Does the robot need to *rotate in place* (requires $\delta_m \ge 3$ and/or $\delta_s \ge 1$)?
2. Does the robot need to move *sideways* (requires $\delta_m \ge 3$)?
3. Does the application justify the added cost, control complexity, and mechanical fragility of steered wheels?

The standard **steering budget** for a planar rigid body is **3** total DOF, distributed as $\delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Wheel Constraints

Every wheel in contact with the ground imposes constraints on the robot chassis. The constraints fall into two categories:

1. **Rolling Constraint (R):** Prevents lateral slip at the contact point. A *fixed* standard wheel imposes 1 rolling constraint.
2. **Sliding / Lateral Constraint (S):** Prevents lateral motion perpendicular to the wheel plane. A *steered* wheel allows this constraint to rotate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Type Notation (after Pin & Killough):**
> - **Fixed Standard Wheel:** 1R + 1S
> - **Steered Standard Wheel:** 1R (steering removes the S-constraint directionally)
> - **Caster Wheel:** 1R + 1S (passive, free to swivel)
> - **Swedish / Mecanum / Omni Wheel:** 0R + 0S (no ground constraints — full planar freedom)

The **degree of mobility** is given by the classical formula:

$$\delta_m = 3 - N_r - N_s$$

where:
- $N_r$ = total number of rolling constraints imposed on the system
- $N_s$ = total number of sliding (lateral) constraints imposed on the system

The **degree of steerability** is:

$$\delta_s = N_s - N_c$$

where $N_c$ is the number of **non-steerable** sliding constraints (e.g., from casters or fixed omni-wheels that are not actively rotated).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Range / Units** | **Standard Value / Constraint** |
|------------|-------------|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| $\delta_m$ | Degree of Mobility (DOF in plane) | 0, 1, 2, 3 | Dimensionless integer |
| $\delta_s$ | Degree of Steerability (independent steering DOF) | 0, 1, 2, 3 | Dimensionless integer |
| $\delta_M$ | Degree of Maneuverability | 0 to 3 | $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ |
| $N_r$ | Number of rolling constraints | $\ge 0$ | Summed over all wheels |
| $N_s$ | Number of sliding constraints | $\ge 0$ | Summed over all wheels |
| $N_c$ | Non-steerable sliding constraints | $\ge 0$ | From passive casters |
| $W$ | Number of wheels | $\ge 3$ | For static stability |
| $\theta$ | Heading angle of robot | rad or deg | One component of pose $\xi$ |

> [!WARNING]
> The inequality $\delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$ is **strict** for *all* wheeled ground robots in planar motion. Any configuration claiming $\delta_M > 3$ violates rigid-body kinematics.

### 2.3 The Maneuverability Triangle

A useful visual conceptualization is the **Maneuverability Triangle** (Muir & Neuman, 1987). The three vertices of the triangle represent the three independent degrees of freedom $\{x, y, \theta\}$. A robot with full maneuverability occupies the entire triangle, while a car-like robot with $\delta_M = 2$ is constrained to a 1-DOF edge of this triangle.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

The classification is used in:

- **Robot design trade-off analysis** (cost vs. agility)
- **Path planning algorithms** (knowing which waypoints are reachable)
- **Control law synthesis** (deciding on input parameter vector dimension)
- **Standardized benchmarking** (comparing robots in industrial automation warehouses)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Maneuverability Bound $\delta_M \le 3$

A rigid body moving in a 2D plane has its pose fully described by the configuration vector:

$$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — General Motion in the Plane:**
Any instantaneous rigid-body motion in $\mathbb{R}^2$ can be expressed as a twist vector $V = (v_x, v_y, \omega)^\top$ where $v_x, v_y$ are translational velocities and $\omega$ is angular velocity.

**Step 2 — Degrees of Freedom Count:**
Counting free components: there are exactly **3** independent components. So:

$$\dim(\text{configuration space}) = 3$$

**Step 3 — Mobility via Wheel Constraints:**
Each rolling constraint removes 1 translational DOF along the contact normal direction. Each sliding constraint removes 1 translational DOF perpendicular to the wheel plane. So:

$$\delta_m = 3 - N_r - N_s$$

**Step 4 — Steerability via Constraint Relaxation:**
A steered wheel can *rotate its constraint direction*. Each actively steerable sliding constraint contributes 1 to steerability. Non-steerable sliding constraints (passive casters) do not. Therefore:

$$\delta_s = N_s - N_c$$

**Step 5 — Total Maneuverability:**
Adding Equations from Step 3 and Step 4:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_M &= \delta_m + \delta_s \\
&= (3 - N_r - N_s) + (N_s - N_c) \\
&= 3 - N_r - N_c
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Bound the Result:**
Since $N_r \ge 0$ and $N_c \ge 0$, we conclude:

$$\delta_M = 3 - N_r - N_c \le 3$$

with equality only when $N_r = 0$ and $N_c = 0$ (e.g., four Swedish/Mecanum wheels in free configuration, giving a holonomic omnidirectional robot).

### 3.2 Worked Example: Differential Drive Robot

**Configuration:** Two fixed standard drive wheels + one caster (passive).

**Step 1 — Identify constraints:**
- 2 fixed standard wheels → 2 rolling + 2 sliding constraints
- 1 caster → 1 rolling + 1 sliding constraint (non-steerable)
- Total: $N_r = 3$, $N_s = 3$, $N_c = 1$

**Step 2 — Compute mobility:**

$$\delta_m = 3 - N_r - N_s = 3 - 3 - 3 = -3$$

This negative result indicates over-constrained count — this is why we use the actual *independent* constraint count: with two co-axial fixed wheels, the effective $N_r = 2$ and $N_s = 2$ (the co-axial pair shares 1 sliding direction):

$$\delta_m = 3 - 2 - 2 = 2$$

**Step 3 — Compute steerability:**

$$\delta_s = N_s - N_c = 2 - 1 = 1$$

Wait — this includes the steerability of the *co-axial pair rotation*. More accurately, for differential drive, the pair acts as a *virtual* steered pair, so:

$$\delta_s = 0, \quad \delta_M = 2, \quad \delta_m = 2$$

**Step 4 — Verify bound:**

$$\delta_m + \delta_s = 2 + 0 = 2 \le 3 \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Worked Example: Car-Like (Ackermann) Robot

**Configuration:** 2 rear fixed drive wheels + 2 front steered wheels (mechanically linked, 1 DOF).

- $N_r = 4$ (one per wheel, simplified via Ackermann linkage)
- $N_s = 2$ (rear pair, since front pair is steered)
- $N_c = 0$ (front pair is actively steered, not passive)

$$\delta_m = 3 - 4 - 2 = -3 \quad \text{(over-constrained, reducible to } 2 \text{)}$$

Effective: $\delta_m = 2$ (forward/backward + rotation about ICR).

$$\delta_s = N_s - N_c = 2 - 0 = 2 \quad \text{(but Ackermann linkage reduces to 1)}$$

Effective: $\delta_s = 1$ (steering wheel angle). Total $\delta_M = 3$ is *not* achieved; the car can only follow curved paths of bounded turning radius.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Robot Configurations

| **Robot Type** | **$\delta_m$** | **$\delta_s$** | **$\delta_M$** | **Holonomic?** |
|----------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Unicycle (1 steered + 2 casters) | 2 | 1 | 2 | No |
| Differential Drive | 2 | 0 | 2 | No |
| Tricycle (1 steer + 2 fixed) | 2 | 1 | 2 | No |
| Car (Ackermann, 2 steer linked) | 2 | 1 | 2 | No |
| Synchronous Drive (3 steered, synced) | 2 | 2 | 3 | Yes |
| Omni (3 or 4 Swedish/Mecanum) | 3 | 0 | 3 | Yes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Maneuverability Classification Flow

The following block diagram maps the decision logic a designer follows when allocating steering DOF to a mobile robot:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design WMR] --> B{Is holonomic motion required?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Set delta_M = 3]
    C --> D{Mecanum or Swedish wheels available?}
    D -- Yes --> E[delta_m = 3, delta_s = 0]
    D -- No --> F[Synchronous drive: 3+ steered wheels]
    F --> G[delta_m = 2, delta_s = 2]
    B -- No --> H{Does robot need to rotate in place?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Differential drive or 2-wheel balance]
    I --> J[delta_m = 2, delta_s = 0]
    H -- No --> K[Car-like Ackermann]
    K --> L[delta_m = 2, delta_s = 1]
    E --> M[Validate: delta_m + delta_s <= 3]
    G --> M
    J --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Configuration]
```

### 4.2 DOF Allocation Map (The Steering Budget)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BUDGET[Total Planar DOF Budget = 3]
        MOB[Degree of Mobility delta_m]
        STE[Degree of Steerability delta_s]
    end
    MOB -- Contributes --> TOT[delta_M = delta_m + delta_s]
    STE -- Contributes --> TOT
    TOT --> BND{Bound Check: delta_M <= 3}
    BND -- Pass --> OK[Feasible Robot]
    BND -- Fail --> ERR[Infeasible: Over-constrained]
```

### 4.3 Robot Configuration Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIFF[Differential Drive]
        D1[delta_m = 2]
        D2[delta_s = 0]
        D3[delta_M = 2]
    end
    subgraph CAR[Car Ackermann]
        C1[delta_m = 2]
        C2[delta_s = 1]
        C3[delta_M = 2]
    end
    subgraph SYNC[Synchronous Drive]
        S1[delta_m = 2]
        S2[delta_s = 2]
        S3[delta_M = 3]
    end
    subgraph OMNI[Omnidirectional 4 Swedish]
        O1[delta_m = 3]
        O2[delta_s = 0]
        O3[delta_M = 3]
    end
    DIFF --> COM[Compare ICR Location]
    CAR --> COM
    SYNC --> COM
    OMNI --> COM
    COM --> OUT[Choose per Application]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Define the *Degree of Steerability* of a mobile robot. Give one example of a robot with $\delta_s = 1$ and one with $\delta_s = 2$.

**Model Answer:**
The **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$)** is the number of independent steering parameters in a wheeled mobile robot that can be actively controlled to alter the geometric constraints on the robot's motion. Formally, $\delta_s = N_s - N_c$.

- **Example with $\delta_s = 1$:** A *car-like* (Ackermann) robot — the steering wheel angle is the single steering parameter.
- **Example with $\delta_s = 2$:** A *synchronous drive* robot with three steered wheels, where each wheel can be independently steered (2 independent steering DOF after accounting for the synchronization linkage).

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** State and explain the maneuverability inequality $\delta_M \le 3$ for planar mobile robots.

**Model Answer:**
For any rigid body moving in a 2D plane, the configuration space has exactly **3** degrees of freedom: $x$, $y$ (translation) and $\theta$ (rotation). The total *controllable* DOF — the degree of maneuverability — is the sum of mobility ($\delta_m$) and steerability ($\delta_s$). Since no planar robot can exceed 3 controllable DOF:

$$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$$

The bound is tight (achievable) only for *holonomic* robots, such as those using Swedish or Mecanum wheels (with $\delta_m = 3, \delta_s = 0$) or synchronous drives (with $\delta_m = 2, \delta_s = 2$).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**
>
> **Question A:**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Differentiate between Degree of Mobility, Degree of Steerability, and Degree of Maneuverability of a wheeled mobile robot. Derive the inequality relating them.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A mobile robot has the following wheel configuration: 2 fixed standard drive wheels mounted on a common axle + 1 passive caster wheel at the front + 1 actively steered standard wheel at the rear. Compute $\delta_m$, $\delta_s$, and $\delta_M$. Justify whether this robot can rotate in place.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Definitions and Derivation:**

- **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$):** The number of degrees of freedom in the plane the robot can achieve *by virtue of wheel actuation*. Computed as $\delta_m = 3 - N_r - N_s$.
- **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$):** The number of *steering* parameters (wheel orientations) that can be independently controlled. Computed as $\delta_s = N_s - N_c$.
- **Degree of Maneuverability ($\delta_M$):** The total controllable DOF, $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$.

**Derivation of the bound:** A planar rigid body has 3 DOF. Adding the contribution from mobility (DOF achieved) and steerability (constraints relaxed by active steering):

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_M &= \delta_m + \delta_s \\
&= (3 - N_r - N_s) + (N_s - N_c) \\
&= 3 - N_r - N_c
\end{aligned}$$

Since $N_r, N_c \ge 0$, we conclude $\delta_M \le 3$. Equality holds only when no rolling or non-steerable sliding constraints exist, i.e., $N_r = N_c = 0$.

**(b) [7 Marks] Configuration Analysis:**

**Wheels and their constraints:**
- 2 fixed standard drive wheels (co-axial) → 2 rolling + 2 sliding (the 2 sliding constraints coincide along the axle — so they reduce to 1 net sliding constraint direction in 2D, but count as 2 in 1D reduction).
- 1 passive caster → 1 rolling + 1 sliding (non-steerable, $N_c$ contribution = 1).
- 1 actively steered standard wheel → 1 rolling + 0 effective sliding (the steering relaxes the lateral constraint).

**Effective counts:** $N_r = 4$ (each wheel contributes 1 rolling), $N_s = 2$ (caster + 1 active sliding), $N_c = 1$ (caster only).

**Compute:**
$$\delta_m = 3 - N_r - N_s = 3 - 4 - 2 = -3 \quad \text{(over-constrained, reducible)}$$

After reducing the over-constrained pair of fixed wheels along a shared axle: $N_r^{\text{eff}} = 2$, $N_s^{\text{eff}} = 1$ (rear caster):

$$\delta_m = 3 - 2 - 1 = 0 \quad \text{(or, treating steerable wheel as 1 fixed + 1 active steer, mobility = 2)}$$

$$\delta_s = N_s - N_c = 1 - 1 = 0 \quad \text{(rear steered wheel counts as a separate steer)}$$

Re-evaluating: with 1 actively steered wheel, $\delta_s = 1$ (from the rear steered wheel), and 2 fixed drive wheels + 1 caster give $\delta_m = 2$.

**Final result:** $\delta_m = 2$, $\delta_s = 1$, $\delta_M = 3$.

**Can it rotate in place?** **YES** — with $\delta_M = 3$ and a steerable wheel plus differential drive, the robot can perform an in-place rotation by combining the rear-wheel steering and differential wheel velocities.

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice):**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] What is the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)? Explain its role in determining the *Degree of Steerability* of a mobile robot. How does the ICR location differ between a differential drive and a car-like robot?
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A four-wheeled omnidirectional robot uses Swedish wheels at all four corners. Compute $\delta_m$, $\delta_s$, $\delta_M$. Can this robot follow an arbitrary trajectory in the plane? Justify using the maneuverability bound.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] ICR and Steerability:**

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is the point in the plane about which a rigid body is instantaneously rotating. For a wheeled mobile robot, the ICR is determined by the intersection of the *axes perpendicular to all rolling wheel directions*.

- If all wheels have *non-parallel* rolling directions, the ICR is unique.
- If the ICR lies **at infinity** (wheels roll in parallel directions), the robot translates without rotating.
- The **degree of steerability** controls *how many distinct ICR positions* the robot can access.

**Differential drive:** With two co-axial fixed wheels, the ICR always lies on the line of the axle (a 1-DOF family of ICRs along the axle). It cannot produce an ICR at infinity (pure translation) without wheel slip, and it cannot produce an ICR at the robot's center (in-place rotation) without the wheels' rolling direction being co-axial — which they are, so differential drive *can* rotate in place. $\delta_s = 0$.

**Car-like robot:** The ICR lies on the line through the rear axle, perpendicular to the rear wheels' direction. The steering angle $\phi$ continuously varies the ICR along this line. There is a minimum turning radius (when $\phi$ is at its mechanical limit). $\delta_s = 1$.

**(b) [7 Marks] Four-Wheel Swedish Configuration:**

Swedish wheels (also called **Mecanum wheels**) have passive rollers at 45° on the wheel periphery. They impose **no** rolling or sliding constraints in any direction perpendicular to the wheel axle — they are *omnidirectional*.

- $N_r = 0$ (no rolling constraints)
- $N_s = 0$ (no sliding constraints)
- $N_c = 0$ (no passive constraints)

**Compute:**
$$\delta_m = 3 - 0 - 0 = 3$$
$$\delta_s = 0 - 0 = 0$$
$$\delta_M = 3 + 0 = 3$$

**Can it follow an arbitrary trajectory?** **YES** — with $\delta_M = 3$, this robot is *fully omnidirectional* (holonomic). It can:
- Translate in any direction ($v_x, v_y$ independent),
- Rotate about its own axis ($\omega$),
- All three simultaneously without re-orienting the wheels.

The maneuverability bound $\delta_M = 3$ is *saturated*, meaning the robot achieves the theoretical maximum controllable DOF for a planar rigid body.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often confuse $\delta_m$ (mobility) with $\delta_s$ (steerability). Recall: *mobility* counts achievable DOF; *steerability* counts controllable *geometric parameters*.
> - A common mistake is to claim $\delta_M = 4$ or higher for some exotic configuration. **Never exceeds 3.**
> - When asked to compute, students frequently miscount $N_r$ and $N_s$. A **fixed standard wheel** is **1R + 1S**; a **steered standard wheel** is **1R + 0S** (steering relaxes the lateral constraint); a **caster** is **1R + 1S (non-steerable)**; a **Swedish/Mecanum wheel** is **0R + 0S**.
> - Always specify *which* wheels are passive vs. actively steered when computing $N_c$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$):** Achievable planar DOF; $\delta_m = 3 - N_r - N_s$.
- **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$):** Independently controllable steering parameters; $\delta_s = N_s - N_c$.
- **Degree of Maneuverability ($\delta_M$):** Sum: $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$.
- **Planar rigid body has exactly 3 DOF**: $x, y, \theta$.
- **Fixed standard wheel:** imposes 1 rolling + 1 sliding constraint.
- **Steered standard wheel:** imposes 1 rolling + 0 sliding (steering relaxes lateral).
- **Caster wheel:** passive, non-steerable — contributes to $N_c$.
- **Swedish/Mecanum wheel:** omnidirectional, contributes 0 to all constraints.
- **Holonomic robot:** $\delta_M = 3$ (e.g., 4 Swedish wheels, synchronous drive).
- **Differential drive:** $\delta_M = 2$, $\delta_s = 0$ — steerable by *velocity differential*, not by wheel steering.
- **Car (Ackermann):** $\delta_M = 2$, $\delta_s = 1$ — bounded turning radius.
- **Synchronous drive:** $\delta_M = 3$, $\delta_s = 2$ — holonomic with steered wheels.
- **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR):** Geometrically determined by wheel axes; its variability is linked to $\delta_s$.
- **Design trade-off:** Higher $\delta_s$ adds mechanical complexity, control cost, and failure modes, but increases path-planning flexibility.
- **Muir and Neuman (1987):** Pioneers of the maneuverability classification.
- **Pin and Killough (1994):** Refined the unified kinematic framework used in KTU syllabus.
- **Always verify:** $\delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$ at the end of any calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Maneuverability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot Maneuverability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Robot Maneuverability

**Maneuverability (δ_M)** is the total number of degrees of freedom (DOF) that a wheeled mobile robot can instantaneously achieve through its motion in the plane. It is the **sum of two orthogonal components**: mobility and steerability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Campion–Bastin–D'Andrea-Novel Framework, 1996):**
> $$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$
> where $\delta_m$ is the **degree of mobility** and $\delta_s$ is the **degree of steerability**, and the upper bound is **3** for any planar rigid body (x, y, θ).

### Component Definitions

| Symbol | Term | KTU 2024 Definition |
|--------|------|---------------------|
| **δ_m** | Degree of Mobility | The number of **independent, instantaneously controllable** DOFs of the robot chassis that affect its pose (x, y, θ) directly. |
| **δ_s** | Degree of Steerability | The number of **internal DOFs** (e.g., steering angles) that can be reconfigured to alter the **location of the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. |
| **δ_M** | Degree of Maneuverability | The total effective DOF the robot can express — always $\delta_M \le 3$. |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Shopping Mall Floor**
> Imagine three vehicles inside a mall:
> 1. **A shopping cart** (Swedish/omni wheels) — push it in ANY direction, even sideways. It is **omnidirectional** ($\delta_m = 3$).
> 2. **A wheelchair** (two large drive wheels + a front castor) — it can drive forward, reverse, and pivot, but **cannot move sideways without first rotating**. It has $\delta_m = 1$, but because the ICR can slide anywhere along the wheel axis, it has $\delta_s = 2$.
> 3. **A car** (Ackermann steering) — it can only move along arcs whose center lies on the rear axle extension. It has $\delta_m = 1$, $\delta_s = 1$ (the steering wheel angle).

The key insight: even though a wheelchair and a car both have $\delta_m = 1$, the **wheelchair is far more maneuverable** in tight spaces because it can spin in place.

---

## 1.3 Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic Robots

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holonomic Robot** → $\delta_m = 3$ → Can achieve **any** velocity vector in the plane → **No kinematic constraint** on the velocity.
> **Non-Holonomic Robot** → $\delta_m < 3$ → Velocity is **constrained**; not all pose changes are directly reachable.

| Property | Holonomic | Non-Holonomic |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| Mobility δ_m | **3** | < 3 |
| Example | Omni-drive robot | Differential drive, Car |
| Can move sideways? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Number of active constraints | 0 (velocity-level) | $\ge 1$ |
| Reaches any (x, y, θ) from any pose? | Trivially | Requires **path planning** (e.g., Reeds–Shepp, Dubins) |

---

## 1.4 The Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)

The **ICR (or ICC – Instantaneous Center of Curvature)** is the unique point in the plane about which the **entire rigid robot body** is rotating at the current instant.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **non-holonomic** robot, the ICR is **fixed by the wheel constraints** — the robot CANNOT translate sideways through the ICR. The ICR must lie at the intersection of lines perpendicular to the rolling direction of every fixed wheel.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wheel Taxonomy (KTU Wheel Classification)

Every mobile robot is built from a combination of these **five primitive wheel types**:

### 2.1.1 Fixed Standard Wheel
* **Construction:** Single rigid wheel, axle fixed to the chassis.
* **Kinematic constraints:** **2** — one rolling, one lateral no-slip.
* **Behavior:** Wheel can only roll forward/back along its fixed orientation.
* **Example:** Rear wheels of a car.

### 2.1.2 Steerable Standard Wheel
* **Construction:** Standard wheel mounted on a **pivoting fork** (can rotate about vertical axis).
* **Kinematic constraints:** **1 effective** — rolling only (lateral motion absorbed by steering DOF).
* **Steering DOF:** Adds **+1** to $\delta_s$.
* **Example:** Front wheels of a car.

### 2.1.3 Castor Wheel
* **Construction:** Standard wheel mounted in a **swiveling yoke** offset from the contact point.
* **Kinematic constraints:** **1** — rolling only (swivel absorbs lateral motion).
* **Steering DOF:** 0 (passive).
* **Example:** Front wheel of a shopping trolley, support wheels on most robots.

### 2.1.4 Swedish (Mecanum) Wheel
* **Construction:** Main hub with **passive free-spinning rollers** mounted at 45° (or 90°) around the circumference.
* **Kinematic constraints:** **1** — rolling along the active roller axis only.
* **Special property:** Allows **lateral slip-free motion** perpendicular to the wheel's natural rolling direction.
* **Example:** Omni-directional platforms in warehouses (KIVA robots).

### 2.1.5 Ball (Spherical) Wheel
* **Construction:** Full ball that can rotate about any axis.
* **Kinematic constraints:** **0** — friction provides no restriction.
* **Example:** Support balls on heavy machinery.

---

## 2.2 Counting the Degrees of Freedom

Let us define:
* $N_1$ = number of **fixed standard wheels**
* $N_2$ = number of **steerable standard wheels**
* $N_3$ = number of **castor wheels**
* $N_4$ = number of **Swedish wheels**
* $N_5$ = number of **ball wheels**

### Step-by-Step Logic for the KTU Maneuverability Equation

**Step 1 — Configuration Space:** A planar rigid body has 3 DOF: $(x, y, \theta)$.

**Step 2 — Total Constraints Imposed:**
* Each fixed wheel contributes **2 constraints** (1 rolling + 1 lateral no-slip).
* Each steerable, castor, or Swedish wheel contributes **1 constraint** (rolling only).
* Ball wheels contribute **0 constraints**.
$$C_{total} = 2N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + N_4$$

**Step 3 — Degree of Mobility (number of *unconstrained* DOFs left after wheel constraints):**
$$\delta_m = 3 - C_{total}, \quad \text{clipped to } \delta_m \ge 0$$

**Step 4 — Degree of Steerability:** The number of wheel parameters (steering angles and contact geometry) that can be **changed** to relocate the ICR:
$$\delta_s = (3 - \delta_m) \quad \text{(for a non-redundant, controllable system)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked examples** applying the KTU formula:
>
> | Robot | N₁ | N₂ | N₃ | N₄ | C_total | δ_m | δ_s | δ_M |
> |-------|----|----|----|----|---------|-----|-----|-----|
> | **Differential drive** | 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 5 → effective **3** (redundant) | **1** | **2** | **3** |
> | **Tricycle (1 steerable + 2 fixed)** | 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 5 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
> | **Ackermann car (2 steerable + 2 fixed)** | 2 | 2 | 0 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 2 | 2 |
> | **3-wheel Swedish omni** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 3 | **0** *(if 90°)* / 2 | 1 / 1 | 1 / 3 |
> | **4-wheel Swedish omni** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 3 (over-constrained) | 0 | 3 |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are board-tested. **NO vertical pipes `|` used** — `$\vert \cdot \vert$' is written with `\vert`.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Maneuverability | $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Upper bound = **3** |
| 2 | Total Constraints | $C_{total} = 2N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + N_4$ | For N₅ = 0 |
| 3 | Degree of Mobility | $\delta_m = 3 - C_{total}$ | Clipped: $\delta_m \ge 0$ |
| 4 | Differential Drive Linear Velocity | $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ | m/s |
| 5 | Differential Drive Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ | rad/s |
| 6 | ICR Radius (Differential) | $R_{ICR} = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$ | meters |
| 7 | Ackermann Condition | $\cot \phi_o - \cot \phi_i = \dfrac{W}{L}$ | $\phi_o$ outer, $\phi_i$ inner wheel |
| 8 | ICR Distance for Car | $R_{ICR} = \dfrac{L}{\tan \phi}$ | L = wheelbase |
| 9 | Pose Update (Differential) | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Continuous-time |
| 10 | Discrete Pose Update (Euler) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \Delta t \cos\theta_k$ | $y_{k+1}$, $\theta_{k+1}$ analogous | m, m, rad |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> * **Warehouse robots (KIVA/Amazon):** Use 4 Swedish wheels → $\delta_m = 3$ → can navigate tight aisles without complex turns.
> * **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** Use rocker-bogie with **6 steerable wheels** → $\delta_s$ large → can crab-walk around obstacles.
> * **Autonomous cars:** Use Ackermann geometry with $\delta_m = 1$ → require **path planners** (Reeds–Shepp, Hybrid A*) since sideways motion is impossible.
> * **AGVs in factories:** Castor + differential drives dominate due to **simplicity and cost**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Forward Kinematics of a Differential Drive Robot

### Setup
Consider a **differential drive mobile robot** with:
* Wheel radius = $r$
* Distance between wheel contact points = $L$
* Left wheel angular velocity = $\omega_L$ (rad/s)
* Right wheel angular velocity = $\omega_R$ (rad/s)
* World frame = $(X, Y)$, Robot frame = $(x_R, y_R)$
* Heading angle = $\theta$ (angle between $x_R$ and $X$)

### Step 1 — Linear Velocity of Each Wheel

Because the wheel rolls **without slipping**, the linear velocity at the contact point of each wheel is:

$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L$$
$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$$

> *Logic:* This is the standard rolling-without-slipping relation: linear velocity = radius × angular velocity.

### Step 2 — Linear Velocity of the Robot Center

The robot's center moves with the **average** of the two wheel velocities (rigid body assumption):

$$v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}$$

> *Logic:* Both wheels are equidistant from the robot's center, and the center's translation is the arithmetic mean of the wheel contact velocities.

### Step 3 — Angular Velocity of the Robot

When the two wheels have different velocities, the robot rotates about the **ICR**. The angular velocity equals the **difference in wheel velocities divided by the wheelbase**:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

> *Logic:* This follows from the rigid-body rotation formula $\omega = \Delta v / L$, where $\Delta v$ is the velocity difference across a baseline of length $L$.

### Step 4 — ICR Radius

By definition, the ICR lies perpendicular to the wheel axis at a distance $R_{ICR}$ such that:

$$R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{v_L + v_R}{v_R - v_L} \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

$$\boxed{R_{ICR} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}}$$

**Boundary cases:**
* If $\omega_R = \omega_L = \omega$: $R_{ICR} \to \infty$ → **straight-line motion** (CCW motion degenerates to translation).
* If $\omega_R = -\omega_L$: $R_{ICR} = 0$ → **pure rotation in place** (zero translation).
* If $\omega_R = 0$ or $\omega_L = 0$: $R_{ICR} = \pm L/2$ → pivot turn about the stationary wheel.

### Step 5 — Pose Kinematics (Euler Integration in World Frame)

The robot's pose evolves in the world frame as:

$$\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$$
$$\dot{y} = v \sin\theta$$
$$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$

> *Logic:* The world-frame velocity components are obtained by **projecting the robot's local velocity onto the world axes** using the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ackermann Steering Geometry

### Problem
For a four-wheeled car, the **inner and outer front wheels must turn at different angles** to avoid tire scrub during a turn. Find the correct relationship.

### Setup
* Wheelbase = $L$ (distance from front to rear axle)
* Track width = $W$ (distance between left and right wheels)
* Steering angle of **centerline** wheel = $\phi$
* Steering angle of **inner** wheel = $\phi_i$
* Steering angle of **outer** wheel = $\phi_o$

### Step 1 — All Wheels Share One ICR

In a proper Ackermann turn, **all four wheels** must roll without lateral slip, meaning all wheel axles must point toward the **same ICR** located on the rear axle extension.

### Step 2 — Geometry of Inner Wheel

The inner wheel is closer to the ICR; its steering angle is **larger** (sharper turn). From the right triangle formed by the ICR, the inner wheel, and the rear axle:

$$\tan \phi_i = \frac{L}{R - W/2}$$

### Step 3 — Geometry of Outer Wheel

The outer wheel is farther from the ICR; its steering angle is **smaller** (gentler turn):

$$\tan \phi_o = \frac{L}{R + W/2}$$

### Step 4 — Eliminate R

Take the cotangent of both equations:

$$\cot \phi_i = \frac{R - W/2}{L}, \quad \cot \phi_o = \frac{R + W/2}{L}$$

Subtract:

$$\cot \phi_o - \cot \phi_i = \frac{R + W/2}{L} - \frac{R - W/2}{L} = \frac{W}{L}$$

$$\boxed{\cot \phi_o - \cot \phi_i = \frac{W}{L}}$$

> *Logic:* This is the **Ackermann condition**. The geometric constraint is necessary for a kinematic steering system with **rigid (non-steering) rear axle**. Without it, tires scrub and the vehicle experiences tire wear and is harder to control.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ICR Locations for Standard Robots

| Robot Type | ICR Location | Degrees |
|-----------|--------------|---------|
| **Differential drive** | Anywhere on the **wheel axis** (extended) | $\delta_s = 2$ |
| **Ackermann car** | Fixed on the **rear axle**, distance $L/\tan\phi$ from car | $\delta_s = 1$ |
| **Synchronous drive (3 Swedish)** | **Controllable in 2D plane** (I can steer in 2 DOF) | $\delta_s = 1$ |
| **Omnidirectional (3+ Swedish)** | **No ICR exists** (translation is possible without rotation) | $\delta_s = 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Wheel Type Hierarchy and Constraints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheeled Mobile Robot] --> B[Wheel Types]
    B --> C1[Fixed Standard Wheel]
    B --> C2[Steerable Standard Wheel]
    B --> C3[Castor Wheel]
    B --> C4[Swedish 90 deg Wheel]
    B --> C5[Ball Spherical Wheel]

    C1 --> D1[2 constraints: rolling + lateral no slip]
    C2 --> D2[1 constraint + 1 steering DOF]
    C3 --> D3[1 constraint: rolling only]
    C4 --> D4[1 constraint along active roller axis]
    C5 --> D5[0 constraints frictionless]

    D1 --> E[Mobility delta m calculation]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E

    E --> F[delta M = delta m + delta s]
    F --> G1[Holonomic delta m = 3]
    F --> G2[Non Holonomic delta m less than 3]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Maneuverability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Count wheels of each type] --> T{Sum C_total}
    T -->|C_total equals 3| H[Holonomic delta m equals 3]
    T -->|C_total less than 3| NH[Non Holonomic delta m equals 3 minus C]
    T -->|C_total greater than 3| R[Redundantly constrained delta m clipped to 0]

    H --> EX1[Example: 3 Swedish 90 deg wheels]
    NH --> EX2[Example: Differential drive 2 fixed + 1 castor]
    NH --> EX3[Example: Ackermann 2 fixed + 2 steerable]
    R --> EX4[Example: 4 fixed wheels redundant]

    EX1 --> M1[delta M equals 3]
    EX2 --> M2[delta M equals 3]
    EX3 --> M3[delta M equals 2]
    EX4 --> M4[delta m equals 0 delta s equals 0 or 1]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — ICR Concept for Differential Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Center CG] -->|distance R ICR| B[ICR on wheel axis]
    A -->|v linear velocity| A
    A -->|omega angular velocity| C[Rotates about B]
    B --> D[Left Wheel Contact]
    B --> E[Right Wheel Contact]
    D -->|R minus L by 2| B
    E -->|R plus L by 2| B
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Forward Kinematics Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[omega L command] --> SUM1[Linear Velocity Calculation]
    IN2[omega R command] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> OUT1[v equals r times omega L + omega R over 2]
    IN1 --> SUM2[Angular Velocity Calculation]
    IN2 --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> OUT2[omega equals r times omega R minus omega L over L]
    OUT1 --> INT[Pose Integrator Euler]
    OUT2 --> INT
    IN3[theta current] --> INT
    INT --> PX[x dot equals v cos theta]
    INT --> PY[y dot equals v sin theta]
    INT --> PT[theta dot equals omega]
    PX --> WK[World Frame Pose Update]
    PY --> WK
    PT --> WK
```

## 4.5 Robot Configuration Comparison Chart (Schematic Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mobile Robot Configurations] --> B1[Differential Drive 2 fixed + 1 castor]
    A --> B2[Tricycle 1 steerable + 2 fixed]
    A --> B3[Ackermann Car 2 fixed + 2 steerable]
    A --> B4[Omnidirectional 3 or 4 Swedish]

    B1 --> C1[delta m equals 1 delta s equals 2 delta M equals 3]
    B2 --> C2[delta m equals 1 delta s equals 1 delta M equals 2]
    B3 --> C3[delta m equals 1 delta s equals 1 delta M equals 2]
    B4 --> C4[delta m equals 3 delta s equals 0 delta M equals 3]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define the term "Robot Maneuverability." Explain its two components with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Maneuverability $\delta_M$ is the total number of degrees of freedom a wheeled mobile robot can instantaneously achieve in the plane. It is given by:
$$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$
The two components are:
1. **Degree of Mobility ($\delta_m$):** The number of DOFs that directly move the robot chassis. *Example:* A differential drive has $\delta_m = 1$ — it can only move along a curve (forward or backward), not sideways.
2. **Degree of Steerability ($\delta_s$):** The number of internal variables (e.g., steering angles) that can relocate the ICR. *Example:* For the same differential drive, $\delta_s = 2$ because the ICR can be placed anywhere along the wheel axis by changing the wheel speeds.
Hence, $\delta_M = 1 + 2 = 3$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between a Holonomic and a Non-Holonomic mobile robot. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Property | Holonomic | Non-Holonomic |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| Mobility $\delta_m$ | = 3 | < 3 |
| Velocity constraints | None | At least one |
| Sideways motion | Possible | Not directly possible |
