| Path planning | Trivial | Required (Reeds–Shepp, Dubins curves) |
| **Example** | **Omnidirectional robot with 3+ Swedish wheels** | **Differential drive or Ackermann-steered car** |

A **holonomic** robot can achieve any velocity vector instantaneously; a **non-holonomic** robot cannot, even though it can still reach any pose via a sufficiently long path.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> **Note to KTU students:** In the End Semester Examination (ESE), Part B questions from a module offer an **internal choice**. Both options A and B below are exam-ready.

---

### 🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Classify the different types of wheels used in mobile robots. Explain the kinematic constraints imposed by each, with neat sketches. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:** A mobile robot's motion is determined entirely by the wheels it carries. There are **five** principal wheel types.

**1. Fixed Standard Wheel:** Wheel rigidly attached to chassis. Imposes **2 constraints** — one rolling and one lateral no-slip. Cannot change orientation relative to the robot. *Example: Rear wheels of a car.*

**2. Steerable Standard Wheel:** Mounted on a pivoting fork. Imposes **1 constraint** (rolling), but adds **1 steering DOF** to the system. *Example: Front wheels of an Ackermann car.*

**3. Castor Wheel:** Mounted in a swiveling yoke offset from the contact point. Imposes **1 constraint** (rolling); the offset causes passive alignment. *Example: Support wheel on a wheelchair.*

**4. Swedish (Mecanum) Wheel:** Hub with passive rollers at 45°/90° around the circumference. Imposes **1 constraint** along the active roller axis but allows **slip-free motion perpendicular** to it. *Example: KIVA warehouse robots.*

**5. Ball (Spherical) Wheel:** A free ball. Imposes **0 constraints** because it can rotate in any direction. *Example: Support balls on printers.*

**Table for clarity:**

| Wheel | Rolling | Lateral No-Slip | Steering | Constraints |
|-------|---------|-----------------|----------|-------------|
| Fixed Standard | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | 2 |
| Steerable Standard | ✓ | ✗ (replaced by steering) | ✓ | 1 |
| Castor | ✓ | ✗ (swivel) | ✗ | 1 |
| Swedish | ✓ | ✗ (rollers) | ✗ | 1 |
| Ball | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | 0 |

**(7 marks: 1 for intro + 5 × 1.2 for each wheel description)**

---

**(b) A differential drive mobile robot has wheel radius r = 0.05 m and wheel separation L = 0.30 m. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L$ = 2 rad/s and $\omega_R$ = 3 rad/s respectively. Calculate: (i) linear velocity, (ii) angular velocity, (iii) location of ICR, and (iv) pose update after Δt = 1 s assuming initial pose is (0, 0, 0). (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $\omega_L = 2$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 3$ rad/s, $\theta_0 = 0$, $\Delta t = 1$ s.

**(i) Linear velocity:**
$$v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times (2 + 3)}{2} = \frac{0.25}{2} = 0.125 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Angular velocity:**
$$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.05 \times (3 - 2)}{0.30} = \frac{0.05}{0.30} = 0.1\overline{6} \text{ rad/s} \approx 0.167 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iii) ICR location:**
$$R_{ICR} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L} = \frac{0.30}{2} \cdot \frac{3 + 2}{3 - 2} = 0.15 \times 5 = 0.75 \text{ m}$$

Since $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the ICR lies on the **left** side of the robot, 0.75 m from the wheel axis.

**(iv) Pose update (Euler integration):**
$$x(1) = 0 + 0.125 \times 1 \times \cos(0) = 0.125 \text{ m}$$
$$y(1) = 0 + 0.125 \times 1 \times \sin(0) = 0 \text{ m}$$
$$\theta(1) = 0 + 0.167 \times 1 = 0.167 \text{ rad} \approx 9.55°$$

**Final pose: (0.125 m, 0 m, 0.167 rad).** ✓

---

### 🔵 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) What is the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)? Derive the ICR for a differential drive mobile robot. Show that straight-line motion corresponds to R_ICR → ∞ and in-place rotation corresponds to R_ICR = 0. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The **ICR** is the unique point in the plane about which the **entire robot body** is instantaneously rotating. For a non-holonomic robot, the ICR is **fixed by the wheel constraints** (e.g., for a car, it lies on the rear-axle extension).

**Derivation:**
From the forward kinematics:
$$v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

By rigid-body rotation, the ICR radius is:
$$R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)/2}{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$

**Case 1 — Straight-line motion:** $\omega_R = \omega_L = \omega$. The denominator $\omega_R - \omega_L = 0$, so $R_{ICR} \to \infty$. The "rotation center" is at infinity, meaning the trajectory is a straight line. ✓

**Case 2 — In-place rotation:** $\omega_R = -\omega_L$. The denominator is $\omega_R - (-\omega_R) = 2\omega_R$ and the numerator is $0$, so $R_{ICR} = 0$. The ICR coincides with the robot's center, meaning it spins without translating. ✓

**Conclusion:** ICR is a powerful geometric concept that **unifies** all motions of a differential drive under one formula.

---

**(b) A tricycle-type mobile robot has 2 fixed standard rear wheels and 1 steerable standard front wheel. Calculate its degrees of mobility, steerability, and maneuverability. Identify the location of its ICR. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $N_1 = 2$ (fixed standard), $N_2 = 1$ (steerable standard), $N_3 = N_4 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Total constraints:**
$$C_{total} = 2N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + N_4 = 2(2) + 1 + 0 + 0 = 5$$

**Step 2 — Degree of mobility:**
$$\delta_m = 3 - C_{total} = 3 - 5 = -2 \rightarrow \text{clipped to } 0 \text{ (over-constrained)}$$

However, recognizing that **the two fixed rear wheels share the same axle**, the system has effectively **2 independent constraints** (1 lateral no-slip for the rear + 1 rolling from each). For a typical tricycle:
$$\delta_m = 1 \text{ (can move forward/back only)}$$

**Step 3 — Degree of steerability:** The 1 steerable wheel contributes 1 steering DOF:
$$\delta_s = 1$$

**Step 4 — Maneuverability:**
$$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s = 1 + 1 = 2$$

**ICR Location:** The ICR lies on the **rear axle extension**, at a distance $R_{ICR} = L / \tan\phi$ from the rear axle (where $L$ is the wheelbase and $\phi$ is the steering angle). When $\phi = 0$, the ICR is at infinity (straight motion); as $\phi$ increases, the ICR moves toward the robot along the rear axle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> * Students often confuse **maneuverability** with **mobility** — they are NOT the same. Always quote **all three** ($\delta_m, \delta_s, \delta_M$).
> * The 2 fixed rear wheels of a tricycle impose constraints that are **redundant** with the body. Many students incorrectly set $\delta_m = 0$; the correct practical value is $\delta_m = 1$.
> * For an Ackermann car, the formula $\delta_M = 2$ (not 3) — because $\delta_s = 1$ only (the steering wheel is one DOF).
> * **Mermaid diagrams:** If you draw wheel configurations in the exam, use **labelled boxes** with clear arrows — examiners reward clear schematics.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and tick before the exam:**

- [ ] **Maneuverability** = Mobility + Steerability: $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s \le 3$.
- [ ] **Five wheel types:** Fixed, Steerable, Castor, Swedish (Mecanum), Ball. Memorize the constraint count for each.
- [ ] **Constraint formula:** $C_{total} = 2N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + N_4$ (for $N_5 = 0$).
- [ ] **Mobility:** $\delta_m = 3 - C_{total}$ (clipped to $\ge 0$).
- [ ] **Differential drive:** $v = r(\omega_L + \omega_R)/2$; $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$.
- [ ] **ICR radius (Differential):** $R_{ICR} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$.
- [ ] **Boundary cases:** $R_{ICR} = \infty$ → straight; $R_{ICR} = 0$ → spin in place.
- [ ] **Ackermann condition:** $\cot\phi_o - \cot\phi_i = W/L$.
- [ ] **Holonomic** = $\delta_m = 3$ (can move anywhere); **Non-holonomic** = $\delta_m < 3$ (needs path planning).
- [ ] **Robot comparison:** Differential (δM=3), Car (δM=2), Omni (δM=3) — draw this table on first page of your answer booklet.
- [ ] **Pose update (Euler):** $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\Delta t \cos\theta_k$ (and similar for $y, \theta$).
- [ ] **Synchro drive** has $\delta_s = 1$ (all 3 wheels turn together) — high steerability, low mobility.
- [ ] **3-Swedish wheel (90°)** has $\delta_m = 3$ (omnidirectional); with 45° rollers it can produce pure rotation in place.
- [ ] **Common KTU error:** Forgetting to convert degrees to radians (or vice versa) in pose updates. Always keep units consistent: angles in **radians** when multiplying by time.
- [ ] **Exam tip:** Always draw a **top-view sketch** of the wheel configuration for full marks in descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Robot Workspace - Degrees of Freedom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Mobile Robot Workspace - Degrees of Freedom # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Mobile Robot Workspace > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > The **Workspace** of a mobile robot is the set of all points $(x, y)$ in the 2D plane (or $(x, y, z)$ in 3D) that the robot's **reference point** (typically the origin of the body frame) can physically reach, given the robot's mechanical structure, kinematic constraints, and steering limits. In a broader sense, the workspace is also called the **reachable configuration space**, and it is determined by: - The robot's geometry (wheelbase, track width, wheel radius) - The kinematic constraints (non-holonomic / holonomic) - The actuator limits (steering angle range, velocity limits) ### Conceptual Analogy 🧭 > [!NOTE] > Think of the **workspace** as the **"footprint" of ink** a fountain pen leaves on a sheet of paper. No matter how you tilt or move the pen, the ink only marks the spots where the pen's tip actually touches. Similarly, a mobile robot can only mark positions in the plane that its reference point can physically touch. For a **unicycle-type** robot (single steering point), the workspace is a **disk** of radius equal to the maximum path traversed. For a **differential drive**, the workspace is the entire $\mathbb{R}^2$ plane (theoretically), because the robot can execute any translation and rotation (path-wise), even though instantaneously it has only 1 Degree of Freedom. ## 1.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a mobile robot is the **minimum number of independent variables (generalized coordinates)** required to completely specify the robot's configuration (position + orientation) in its environment at any given instant. For a rigid body in a **2D plane**: $$\text{Maximum DOF} = 3 \quad (x, y, \theta)$$ For a rigid body in **3D space**: $$\text{Maximum DOF} = 6 \quad (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$$ ### Conceptual Analogy 🚗 > [!NOTE] > Imagine a **car parked in a lot**. To completely describe its state, you need **3 numbers**: how far along the North direction ($x$), how far along the East direction ($y$), and which way the nose is pointing ($\theta$). So a car has **3 positional DOF** — *but* it cannot *instantaneously* realize all three. The instantaneous mobility is **1** (move forward/backward), and reaching an arbitrary orientation requires a path (maneuver). ### Two Critical Sub-Concepts #### A. Degrees of Mobility ($M$) The number of **independent velocity inputs** the robot can command (instantaneous controllable directions in the plane). #### B. Degrees of Steerability ($S$) The number of **independent steering actuators** the robot possesses (wheels that can rotate about a vertical axis). #### C. Manoeuvrability ($\delta_M$) $$\delta_M = M + S$$ This represents the **structural richness** of the robot's motion capabilities, beyond its instantaneous path freedom. ## 1.3 Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic Robots > [!WARNING] > **High-Yield Distinction (Favourite KTU Question):** | Property | **Holonomic Robot** | **Non-Holonomic Robot** | |---|---|---| | Definition | Controllable DOF = Total DOF | Controllable DOF $<$ Total DOF | | Constraints | Only holonomic (geometric) | Has non-holonomic (velocity) constraints | | Path in plane | Can reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ directly | Must follow a path to change orientation | | Examples | Omnidirectional (Swedish/Mecanum wheels) | Differential drive, Ackermann car | | Manoeuvrability | $\delta_M = 3$ (full) | $\delta_M = 1$ or $2$ | **Real-world Analogy:** A **crab** walking sideways is *holonomic* (it can move in any direction instantly), whereas a **car** is *non-holonomic* (it cannot move sideways without first turning). ## 1.4 Wheel Inventory: The Heart of Mobile Robot Kinematics The number and type of wheels fully determine a mobile robot's DOF, workspace, and holonomic character. KTU 2024 mandates detailed knowledge of: | Wheel Type | Internal DOF | Rolling Constraint | Lateral Constraint | Steerable | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Standard Fixed Wheel** | 1 (rotation) | Yes | Yes | No | | **Standard Steered Wheel** | 2 (rotation + steering) | Yes | No (released by steering) | Yes | | **Castor Wheel** | 2 (rotation + steering) | Yes | No (passive steering) | Yes (passive) | | **Swedish (Mecanum) Wheel** | 3 (rotation + 2 passive rollers) | Configurable | Released by rollers | No | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Hook:** "*Standard = Straight-jacket (no side slip)*. *Castor = Chair wheel (rolls + swivels)*. *Swedish = Omni (active rollers at 45°)*." # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Systematic Classification of Mobile Robots by DOF The configuration of a mobile robot is uniquely described by: $$\mathbf{q} = (x, y, \theta)$$ The set of all such $\mathbf{q}$ is the **Configuration Space** $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$ (a 3D manifold). ### Wheel-by-Wheel Constraint Analysis #### (a) Standard Fixed Wheel - **Constraints imposed on robot body:** 2 1. **Rolling (no slip along contact):** velocity component along wheel direction = 0 relative motion 2. **Lateral (no skid perpendicular to wheel):** velocity component perpendicular to wheel = 0 - **DOF contributed to robot:** $-2$ #### (b) Standard Steered Wheel - **Constraints imposed:** 1 1. **Rolling** constraint only - Lateral constraint **released** because the wheel rotates to align with the velocity - **DOF contributed to robot:** $-1$ (plus 1 for the steering actuator) #### (c) Castor Wheel (Passive Steered) - **Constraints imposed:** 1 1. **Rolling** constraint only - Lateral motion is permitted by passive swivel - **DOF contributed to robot:** $-1$ (steering is passive, no actuator) #### (d) Swedish (Mecanum) Wheel - **Constraints imposed:** 0 (ideal) - Active rollers at 45° allow lateral motion - Combination of 4 such wheels gives full holonomic motion - **DOF contributed to robot:** $0$ (per wheel) ## 2.2 The Mobility Formula (Campion, Bastin, D'Andrea-Novel) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Master Equation for KTU 2024:** > > $$\boxed{\text{Mobility } (M) = 3(N - 1) - 2N_{sf} - N_{ss} - N_{cs}}$$ > > Where: > - $N$ = total number of wheels on the robot > - $N_{sf}$ = number of **Standard Fixed** wheels (each subtracts 2) > - $N_{ss}$ = number of **Standard Steered** wheels (each subtracts 1) > - $N_{cs}$ = number of **Castor** wheels (each subtracts 1) > - For Swedish wheels, use $N_{sw}$ with subtractor **0** in the ideal case The factor $3(N-1)$ emerges from Grübler's formula applied to a closed kinematic chain of $N$ wheels + 1 ground link in the plane. ### Mobility Formula — KTU Cheat Sheet 📋 | Configuration | Wheels | $N$ | $N_{sf}$ | $N_{ss}$ | $N_{cs}$ | $M$ | Character | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Differential Drive** | 2 std + 1 castor | 3 | 2 | 0 | 1 | **1** | Non-holonomic | | **Tricycle (car-like)** | 1 steered + 2 fixed | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | **1** | Non-holonomic | | **Ackermann (4W car)** | 2 steered + 2 fixed | 4 | 2 | 2 | 0 | **3** | Non-holonomic | | **Synchro Drive** | 3 steered | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | **3** | Non-holonomic | | **Omnidirectional (4 Swedish)** | 4 Swedish | 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **9** | Holonomic (cap = 3) | | **3 Swedish + 1 castor** | 3 Sw + 1 castor | 4 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **8** | Holonomic (cap = 3) | > [!WARNING] > **Subtle KTU Pitfall:** The mobility $M$ from the formula is the **structural** mobility. The **actual instantaneous controllable DOF** is bounded by 3 (the plane's own DOF). So if $M > 3$, the robot is **holonomic** with full 3-DOF control (e.g., the 9 from Swedish wheels reduces to 3, the maximum possible). ## 2.3 Manoeuvrability and Steerability > [!IMPORTANT] > $$\text{Manoeuvrability } (\delta_M) = M + S$$ > > Where $S$ is the **Degrees of Steerability** = number of wheels with a steering actuator. For a robot to be **omnidirectional (holonomic)** in the plane, it must satisfy: $$\delta_M = M + S = 3$$ This is the **holonomic sufficiency condition** for a 2D ground robot. ### Grasp Table — Steerability & Mobility | Robot Type | $M$ | $S$ | $\delta_M = M + S$ | Holonomic? | |---|---|---|---|---| | Differential Drive | 1 | 0 | 1 | ❌ | | Ackermann (2 front steer) | 3 | 2 | 5 (≥3) | ❌ (still non-holonomic) | | Synchro Drive (3 steered) | 3 | 3 | 6 (≥3) | ❌ (still non-holonomic) | | Omnidirectional (4 Swedish) | 3 | 0 | 3 | ✅ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Critical Insight:** Having $\delta_M \geq 3$ is **necessary but not sufficient** for holonomic motion. A synchro drive has $\delta_M = 6$ yet is still non-holonomic because all 3 wheels are constrained to align (no lateral slip). True holonomic motion requires **independent lateral force generation**, achievable only with Swedish/Mecanum or special active-caster systems. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of DOF Analysis | Application | Why DOF Matters | |---|---| | **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva)** | Differential drive: simple, 1 DOF, reliable | | **Autonomous Cars (Waymo)** | Ackermann: car-like, passengers familiar, 1 DOF path | | **Hospital Robots (TUG)** | Synchro drive: zero turning radius, ideal for tight corridors | | **Factory AMRs (MiR, Fetch)** | Omnidirectional: holonomic, path-optimal in cluttered spaces | | **Planetary Rovers (Curiosity)** | Rocker-bogie with 6 steerable wheels, complex DOF | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Mobility Formula from Grübler's Equation ### Step 1: Recall Grübler's Formula for Planar Mechanisms For a planar kinematic chain (all motion confined to 2D plane): $$M = 3(N - 1) - 2J_1 - J_2$$ Where: - $M$ = mobility (DOF) - $N$ = total number of links (including the fixed ground link) - $J_1$ = number of **lower pairs** (1-DOF joints, e.g., revolute, prismatic) - $J_2$ = number of **higher pairs** (2-DOF joints, e.g., cam, gear mesh) ### Step 2: Model the Mobile Robot as a Planar Mechanism Consider a robot with: - **1 ground link** (the floor) — fixed - **1 chassis (body) link** - **$N$ wheel links** — each wheel is a separate link Therefore: $$N_{total} = N_{wheels} + 2 = N + 2$$ ### Step 3: Identify the Joints #### Lower pairs ($J_1$) — Revolute joints (1-DOF): For each **standard fixed wheel**: - 1 revolute joint: wheel ↔ chassis (rotation axis) For each **steered standard wheel**: - 2 revolute joints: wheel ↔ steering knuckle (rotation), knuckle ↔ chassis (steering) - Net contribution: 2 joints For each **castor wheel**: - 2 revolute joints: wheel ↔ fork (rotation), fork ↔ chassis (steering) - Net contribution: 2 joints For each **Swedish wheel**: - 1 revolute joint: wheel ↔ chassis - Passive rollers are typically not counted as joints (they spin freely) **Total lower pairs:** $$J_1 = N_{sf} \cdot 1 + N_{ss} \cdot 2 + N_{cs} \cdot 2 + N_{sw} \cdot 1$$ #### Higher pairs ($J_2$) — Wheel-ground contact (2-DOF): Each wheel makes one contact with the ground, which is a higher pair: $$J_2 = N_{wheels} = N$$ ### Step 4: Substitute into Grübler's Formula $$M = 3(N+1) - 2 J_1 - J_2$$ $$M = 3(N+1) - 2 \left[ N_{sf} + 2N_{ss} + 2N_{cs} + N_{sw} \right] - N$$ ### Step 5: Simplify $$M = 3N + 3 - 2N_{sf} - 4N_{ss} - 4N_{cs} - 2N_{sw} - N$$ $$M = 2N + 3 - 2N_{sf} - 4N_{ss} - 4N_{cs} - 2N_{sw}$$ ### Step 6: Apply the Rolling Constraint (Inextensibility) For each standard wheel (fixed or steered) and castor wheel, the **rolling without slipping** condition removes 1 DOF. This effectively adds 1 additional lower-pair constraint per such wheel: - Each standard fixed wheel: +1 rolling constraint → effectively $-2$ - Each standard steered wheel: +1 rolling constraint → effectively $-1$ - Each castor wheel: +1 rolling constraint → effectively $-1$ - Swedish wheels: 0 rolling constraints (in ideal omnidirectional case) After applying the rolling constraints, the formula collapses to the well-known: $$\boxed{M = 3(N - 1) - 2N_{sf} - N_{ss} - N_{cs}}$$ Where $N$ is the **total number of wheels**. > [!NOTE] > **Why $N - 1$ and not $N$?** The chassis itself, when treated as a link, does not need separate counting because it is implicitly included in the $3N$ term of Grübler's formula. The $-1$ accounts for the ground link being fixed. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Differential Drive Robot A typical differential drive robot has: - 2 standard fixed wheels (left and right, independently driven) - 1 castor wheel (front or rear, for balance) **Given:** $N = 3$, $N_{sf} = 2$, $N_{ss} = 0$, $N_{cs} = 1$ **Calculation:** $$M = 3(N - 1) - 2N_{sf} - N_{ss} - N_{cs}$$ $$M = 3(3 - 1) - 2(2) - 0 - 1$$ $$M = 3(2) - 4 - 1$$ $$M = 6 - 5 = 1$$ **Conclusion:** The robot has **1 Degree of Mobility**. The 1 controllable input is the *difference* of the two wheel velocities (which causes rotation), while the *sum* controls forward motion. **Holonomic check:** $\delta_M = M + S = 1 + 0 = 1 < 3$ → **Non-holonomic** ✓ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Four-Wheel Ackermann Car A car has: - 2 standard fixed wheels (rear, driven) - 2 standard steered wheels (front, linked by steering bar) **Given:** $N = 4$, $N_{sf} = 2$, $N_{ss} = 2$, $N_{cs} = 0$ **Calculation:** $$M = 3(N - 1) - 2N_{sf} - N_{ss} - N_{cs}$$ $$M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(2) - 2 - 0$$ $$M = 3(3) - 4 - 2$$ $$M = 9 - 6 = 3$$ **Conclusion:** Structural mobility is **3**, but instantaneous path mobility is **1** (forward velocity along the car's longitudinal axis). The 3 DOF account for: 1. Forward velocity $v$ 2. Steering angle $\delta$ (kinematic input) 3. Body rotation rate $\dot{\theta}$ (derived from $v$ and $\delta$) **Holonomic check:** $\delta_M = 3 + 2 = 5 \geq 3$, but the car is still **non-holonomic** (lateral velocity is always zero). --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Omnidirectional Robot (4 Swedish Wheels) A holonomic platform with 4 Swedish (Mecanum) wheels. **Given:** $N = 4$, $N_{sf} = 0$, $N_{ss} = 0$, $N_{cs} = 0$, $N_{sw} = 4$ **Calculation (ideal case):** $$M = 3(N - 1) - 0 = 3(3) = 9$$ **Physical saturation:** Since the plane has only 3 DOF: $$M_{effective} = \min(M, 3) = 3$$ **Conclusion:** Full holonomic motion is achievable. The 4 Swedish wheels provide 3 controllable DOF: $v_x$, $v_y$, $\dot{\theta}$. --- ## 3.5 Comparative Mobility Matrix | Configuration | $N$ | $N_{sf}$ | $N_{ss}$ | $N_{cs}$ | $M$ (computed) | $M_{eff}$ | Holonomic? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Differential Drive | 3 | 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | ❌ | | Tricycle | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | ❌ | | Ackermann (4W) | 4 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 3 | 3 | ❌ | | Synchro Drive | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | 3 | ❌ | | Omni (4 Swedish) | 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 9 | 3 | ✅ | | Omni (3 Swedish + 1 castor) | 4 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 8 | 3 | ✅ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always present the **mobility formula derivation** in 2-3 lines: start from Grübler, identify joints, apply rolling constraint, and obtain the final compact form. Examiners reward explicit step-by-step reasoning. ## 3.6 Forward Kinematics of a Differential Drive (Instantaneous) The forward kinematics of a differential drive robot expresses the body twist $(v_x, v_y, \omega)$ in terms of wheel speeds $(\dot{\phi}_L, \dot{\phi}_R)$ and wheel radius $r$: $$v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)$$ $$\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)$$ Where $L$ = distance between the two wheels (wheelbase). **Velocity vector in the body frame** $(v_x, v_y, \omega)$: $$v_x = v, \quad v_y = 0, \quad \omega = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{2L}$$ Since $v_y = 0$ always, this confirms the robot is **non-holonomic**. ### Body-to-World Transformation $$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This non-integrable velocity constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ is the mathematical fingerprint of non-holonomic motion. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mobile Robot DOF Classification Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Mobile Robot
in 2D Plane] --> B[Maximum Possible DOF = 3
x, y, theta] B --> C[Apply Wheel Constraints] C --> D{Is robot holonomic?} D -- Yes, M equals 3 --> E[Holonomic Robot
Omnidirectional] D -- No, M less than 3 --> F[Non-Holonomic Robot
Path Constrained] E --> E1[4 Swedish Wheels] E --> E2[3 Swedish + 1 Castor] E --> E3[Active Castor Systems] F --> F1[M equals 1] F --> F2[M equals 2] F --> F3[M equals 3 but non-holonomic] F1 --> G1[Differential Drive] F1 --> G2[Tricycle] F2 --> H1[Two-Steer Tricycle] F3 --> I1[Ackermann Car] F3 --> I2[Synchro Drive] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315 style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style F fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 ``` ## 4.2 Wheel Type Classification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Wheel Types
in Mobile Robotics] --> B[Standard Wheel] A --> C[Castor Wheel] A --> D[Swedish Wheel] B --> B1[Fixed Standard] B --> B2[Steered Standard] B1 --> B1a[Constraints: 2
Rolling + Lateral] B2 --> B2a[Constraints: 1
Rolling only] C --> C1[Constraints: 1
Rolling only] C --> C2[Passive Steering
No actuator] D --> D1[Mecanum Wheel] D --> D2[Universal Wheel] D1 --> D3[Constraints: 0
Holonomic ideal] D2 --> D3 style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0 style B fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style C fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0 style D fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c ``` ## 4.3 Mobility Formula Derivation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Planar Mechanism] --> B[Grublers Formula
M equals 3 of N minus 1 minus 2 of J1 minus J2] B --> C[Identify Links
Ground + Chassis + N wheels] C --> D[Count Joints
Revolute J1 and Contact J2] D --> E[Apply Rolling Constraint
1 extra per standard and castor wheel] E --> F[Simplify Expression] F --> G[Final Formula
M equals 3 of N minus 1 minus 2 Nsf minus Nss minus Ncs] G --> H[Compute M for Robot Type] H --> H1[Differential Drive M equals 1] H --> H2[Ackermann M equals 3] H --> H3[Omnidirectional M equals 9] style A fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ## 4.4 Mobile Robot Configuration Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Mobile Robot Configurations] --> B[Non-Holonomic Family] A --> C[Holonomic Family] B --> B1[1 DOF Instantaneous] B --> B2[Car-Like Steering] B --> B3[Synchronous Family] B1 --> B1a[Differential Drive
2 Standard + 1 Castor] B2 --> B2a[Ackermann Car
2 Front Steered + 2 Rear Fixed] B2 --> B2b[Tricycle
1 Front Steered + 2 Rear Fixed] B3 --> B3a[Synchro Drive
3 Steered and Driven Wheels] C --> C1[Swedish Wheel Platform] C1 --> C1a[4 Mecanum Wheels
Full Holonomic 3 DOF] style A fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b style B fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DOF Analysis ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Wheel Inventory] --> B[Constraint Counter] B --> C[Mobility Formula] C --> D[M Value] D --> E[Steerability Count] E --> F[Manoeuvrability delta M] F --> G{Holonomic Test
delta M equals 3?} G -- Yes --> H[Holonomic Robot
Full 3 DOF Motion] G -- No --> I[Non-Holonomic Robot
Path Constrained] H --> H1[Workspace: Full Plane
All orientations reachable directly] I --> I1[Workspace: Full Plane
All orientations reachable via path] style A fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c style C fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style G fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c style H fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20 style I fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a mobile robot. Differentiate between holonomic and non-holonomic constraints with one example each.** **Model Answer:** The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a mobile robot is the minimum number of independent generalized coordinates required to completely specify the robot's configuration in its operating environment. For a mobile robot operating on a flat 2D plane, the maximum possible DOF is **3**, corresponding to the coordinates $(x, y, \theta)$, where $(x, y)$ represents the position of the reference point and $\theta$ represents the orientation (heading angle) of the robot. **Holonomic Constraints:** - Holonomic constraints are those that can be expressed as equations involving only the **positions and orientations** (configuration variables) of the robot, *not their time derivatives*. - They are **integrable** and reduce the **geometric** degrees of freedom. - **Example:** The distance between the two wheels of a differential drive robot is fixed — this is a holonomic constraint because it depends only on configuration, not on velocities. **Non-Holonomic Constraints:** - Non-holonomic constraints involve the **velocities** (time derivatives of configuration variables) and **cannot be integrated** to yield position-level equations. - They reduce the **instantaneous mobility** of the robot without restricting the reachable configuration space. - **Example:** A car cannot move sideways — the constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ is non-holonomic. The car can still reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ via a suitable path, but not instantaneously. **Key Distinction:** $$\text{Holonomic: } f(\mathbf{q}) = 0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Non-Holonomic: } g(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) = 0 \text{ (non-integrable)}$$ --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the three types of wheels commonly used in mobile robots with neat diagrams and mention the constraints each imposes.** **Model Answer:** The three principal types of wheels used in mobile robotics are: #### (a) Standard Wheel (Fixed) - **Construction:** A single rigid wheel mounted on an axle fixed to the chassis. The wheel can only rotate about its axle. - **DOF:** 1 (rotation about axle) - **Constraints imposed:** **2** 1. **Rolling constraint** — no slip along the direction of motion 2. **Lateral (sideways) constraint** — no skid perpendicular to the wheel plane - **Used in:** Differential drive robots, rear wheels of Ackermann cars. #### (b) Standard Steered Wheel - **Construction:** A standard wheel mounted on a fork that can rotate (steer) about a vertical axis attached to the chassis. - **DOF:** 2 (rotation + steering) - **Constraints imposed:** **1** 1. **Rolling constraint** only - Lateral constraint is released because the wheel can swivel to align with the velocity. - **Used in:** Front wheels of cars, synchro drive robots. #### (c) Castor Wheel - **Construction:** A standard wheel mounted on a fork with a vertical pivot, allowing the wheel to swivel freely (passive steering). - **DOF:** 2 (rotation + passive steering) - **Constraints imposed:** **1** 1. **Rolling constraint** only - Passive steering releases the lateral constraint. - **Used in:** Office chairs, shopping trolleys, balancing castor in differential drives. #### (Bonus: Swedish / Mecanum Wheel) - **Construction:** A standard wheel with several passive rollers placed at 45° around its circumference. - **DOF:** 3 (active rotation + 2 passive roller rotations) - **Constraints imposed:** 0 in ideal case - **Used in:** Omnidirectional holonomic platforms. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student answers **ONE full question** (with both sub-parts). Below are **TWO alternative full questions** providing internal choice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **With the help of suitable diagrams, describe the various wheel configurations used in mobile robots. Explain how the type of wheel affects the robot's degrees of freedom and manoeuvrability.** **Model Solution:** The four standard wheel configurations in mobile robotics are: **1. Differential Drive (2 standard fixed + 1 castor)** - Two independently driven wheels on either side of the chassis, plus a passive castor for balance. - **Mobility:** $M = 3(2) - 4 - 1 = 1$ - **Characteristics:** Non-holonomic, can spin in place, simple control, widely used in research (Pioneer, TurtleBot). **2. Tricycle / Car-like (1 steered + 2 fixed)** - A single steered wheel in front, two fixed driven wheels in rear (or vice versa). - **Mobility:** $M = 3(2) - 4 - 1 = 1$ - **Characteristics:** Non-holonomic, requires minimum turning radius, similar to a tricycle. **3. Ackermann Steering (2 front steered + 2 rear fixed)** - Two front wheels linked by a steering tie-rod ensuring they turn at slightly different angles (Ackermann geometry) to avoid tire slip. - **Mobility:** $M = 3(3) - 4 - 2 = 3$ - **Characteristics:** Car-like, used in autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Tesla). **4. Synchro Drive (3 steered and driven)** - Three wheels, each capable of independent steering and driving. All steered together and all driven together. - **Mobility:** $M = 3(2) - 0 - 3 = 3$ - **Characteristics:** Zero turning radius (rotate about wheel center), non-holonomic, used in cleaning robots (iRobot, Karcher). **5. Omnidirectional (4 Swedish wheels)** - Four Mecanum wheels oriented at 45° relative to chassis. - **Mobility (effective):** 3 (full holonomic) - **Characteristics:** Can move in any direction including sideways; used in heavy-payload industrial AMRs. **Effect on DOF and Manoeuvrability:** | Wheel Type | Constraints/Wheel | Effect on DOF | Effect on Manoeuvrability | |---|---|---|---| | Standard Fixed | 2 | Reduces mobility by 2 | Restricts turning capability | | Standard Steered | 1 | Reduces mobility by 1 | Adds steerability (1 DOF per wheel) | | Castor | 1 | Reduces mobility by 1 | Passive alignment, simpler control | | Swedish | 0 | No mobility reduction | Enables holonomic motion | **Conclusion:** Wheel selection is a fundamental design decision that determines the robot's mobility, steerability, manoeuvrability, and holonomic character. **(7 marks — full marks for explicit wheel-by-wheel analysis with mobility values)** #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Derive the mobility formula for a mobile robot. Using the formula, compute the mobility of a differential drive robot and an Ackermann-steered car.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Grübler's Formula for Planar Mechanism:** $$M = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$$ **Step 2: Identify Components for a Mobile Robot with $N$ wheels:** - Total links: $N + 2$ (1 ground + 1 chassis + $N$ wheels) - Lower pairs ($J_1$): Revolute joints connecting wheels to chassis - Higher pairs ($J_2$): $N$ wheel-ground contact points **Step 3: Apply Rolling Constraints:** Each standard fixed wheel adds 2 effective constraints (rolling + lateral), each steered/castor wheel adds 1 (rolling only). **Step 4: Resulting Mobility Formula:** $$M = 3(N-1) - 2N_{sf} - N_{ss} - N_{cs}$$ **Step 5: Differential Drive Calculation:** - $N = 3$, $N_{sf} = 2$, $N_{ss} = 0$, $N_{cs} = 1$ $$M = 3(2) - 2(2) - 0 - 1 = 6 - 5 = \mathbf{1}$$ **Step 6: Ackermann Car Calculation:** - $N = 4$, $N_{sf} = 2$, $N_{ss} = 2$, $N_{cs} = 0$ $$M = 3(3) - 2(2) - 2 - 0 = 9 - 6 = \mathbf{3}$$ **Step 7: Interpretation:** - Differential drive has only **1 instantaneous DOF** (control: difference of wheel speeds for rotation, sum for translation). - Ackermann has structural mobility **3** but only **1 instantaneous path DOF** (forward velocity); the other 2 are the steering angle and the resulting body rotation rate. **(7 marks — distributed as: 2 for formula derivation, 2 for differential drive, 2 for Ackermann, 1 for interpretation)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Compare holonomic and non-holonomic mobile robots. Discuss the concept of workspace and configuration space for a mobile robot with appropriate examples.** **Model Solution:** **Definition of Workspace and Configuration Space:** - **Configuration Space ($\mathcal{C}$):** The space of all possible configurations of the robot. For a 2D mobile robot, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$, parameterized by $(x, y, \theta)$. - **Workspace ($\mathcal{W}$):** The set of all points in the 2D plane that the robot's reference point (or end-effector) can reach. Mathematically, $\mathcal{W} = \{(x, y) : \exists \theta \text{ such that } (x, y, \theta) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{reachable}}\}$. **Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic Comparison:** | Parameter | Holonomic Robot | Non-Holonomic Robot | |---|---|---| | Definition | Controllable DOF = Total DOF | Controllable DOF $<$ Total DOF | | Mobility $M$ | $= 3$ (full plane mobility) | $< 3$ (restricted) | | Manoeuvrability $\delta_M$ | $= 3$ | $< 3$ (or $\geq 3$ but still constrained) | | Workspace | Entire plane (direct reach) | Entire plane (path reach) | | Configuration Space | Fully reachable | Reachable only via paths | | Velocity Constraint | Holonomic only | Includes non-integrable $\dot{q}$-constraints | | Examples | Omnidirectional (Swedish/Mecanum), active castor | Differential drive, Ackermann car, synchro drive | | Control Complexity | Higher (more DOF to coordinate) | Lower (fewer control inputs) | | Maneuver Cost | Optimal direct paths | Path-planning required (Dubins, Reeds-Shepp) | **Examples:** 1. **Holonomic Example — Swedish Wheel Robot (e.g., Kuka YouBot):** - 4 Mecanum wheels, full 3 DOF control. - Can translate in any direction (including sideways) and rotate simultaneously. - Workspace is the entire $\mathbb{R}^2$ plane, and any $(x, y, \theta)$ is reachable via a straight-line path. 2. **Non-Holonomic Example — Differential Drive (e.g., Pioneer 3-DX):** - 1 DOF instantaneous (forward velocity), $\omega$ constrained. - Cannot move sideways. - Workspace is the entire $\mathbb{R}^2$ plane, but to reach a desired $(x, y, \theta)$, a path must be computed (e.g., using Dubins curves). 3. **Non-Holonomic Example — Ackermann Car (e.g., autonomous Toyota):** - 1 DOF instantaneous. - Minimum turning radius constraint (cannot spin in place). - Workspace is the plane minus obstacles; specific poses reachable only via Reeds-Shepp paths. **Conclusion:** Both holonomic and non-holonomic robots can theoretically access the entire plane as workspace, but holonomic robots can do so *directly*, while non-holonomic robots need *path planning*. **(7 marks — distributed: 2 for definitions, 3 for comparison, 2 for examples)** #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A 4-wheel mobile robot has 2 standard fixed wheels and 2 castor wheels. Calculate its mobility using the mobility formula. If the 2 standard fixed wheels are replaced by 2 standard steered wheels, recalculate the mobility and comment on the change in holonomic character.** **Model Solution:** **Case 1: Original Configuration (2 standard fixed + 2 castor)** **Given:** - $N = 4$ (total wheels) - $N_{sf} = 2$ (standard fixed) - $N_{ss} = 0$ - $N_{cs} = 2$ (castor) **Applying the Mobility Formula:** $$M_1 = 3(N - 1) - 2N_{sf} - N_{ss} - N_{cs}$$ $$M_1 = 3(4 - 1) - 2(2) - 0 - 2$$ $$M_1 = 3(3) - 4 - 2$$ $$M_1 = 9 - 6 = 3$$ **Steerability:** $S_1 = 0$ (no wheel is actively steered) **Manoeuvrability:** $\delta_{M_1} = M_1 + S_1 = 3 + 0 = 3$ **Holonomic Check:** Although $\delta_M = 3$, the robot is still **non-holonomic** because there is no wheel capable of generating lateral force. The castor wheels passively align but cannot actively push the robot sideways. The 2 standard fixed wheels lock the chassis orientation relative to their axle direction. **Case 2: Modified Configuration (2 standard steered + 2 castor)** **Given:** - $N = 4$ - $N_{sf} = 0$ - $N_{ss} = 2$ (standard steered) - $N_{cs} = 2$ (castor) **Mobility Calculation:** $$M_2 = 3(4 - 1) - 2(0) - 2 - 2$$ $$M_2 = 3(3) - 0 - 2 - 2$$ $$M_2 = 9 - 4 = 5$$ **Steerability:** $S_2 = 2$ (2 steered wheels) **Manoeuvrability:** $\delta_{M_2} = M_2 + S_2 = 5 + 2 = 7$ **Change in Mobility:** $$\Delta M = M_2 - M_1 = 5 - 3 = +2$$ **Comments:** 1. **Mobility Increased by 2:** Replacing fixed wheels with steered wheels added 2 DOF to the structural mobility because each steered wheel releases 1 lateral constraint. 2. **Holonomic Character Still Lacking:** Despite $\delta_M = 7 \geq 3$, the robot remains **non-holonomic**. The lateral motion is still impossible instantaneously because the wheels (even when steered) cannot generate lateral force. The steered wheels only align with the velocity direction. 3. **Practical Benefit:** The new configuration allows **directional alignment without skidding**, making the robot suitable for car-like applications. The 2 castor wheels continue to provide passive lateral balance. 4. **True Holonomic Motion Would Require:** Either Swedish/Mecanum wheels or active lateral-force castors. **(7 marks — distributed: 2 for Case 1 calculation, 2 for Case 2 calculation, 3 for the commentary and holonomic discussion)** --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing $N$ with $N_{sf}$:** The total number of wheels $N$ in the formula is **not** the same as the number of standard fixed wheels. Students often substitute $N = N_{sf}$ and get wrong answers. > > 2. **Forgetting the Coefficient 2 for Standard Fixed Wheels:** The term $2N_{sf}$ in the formula uses a coefficient of **2** (not 1) because each standard fixed wheel imposes **2 constraints** (rolling + lateral). Writing $M = 3(N-1) - N_{sf} - N_{ss} - N_{cs}$ is a **frequent error**. > > 3. **Ignoring the Capping at 3 for Holonomic Robots:** When applying the formula to Swedish wheel platforms, students get $M = 9$ and report it as the answer. The *correct* answer is $M = 3$ (the structural mobility is 9, but instantaneous mobility is capped at 3). > > 4. **Mixing Mobility and Manoeuvrability:** Writing "mobility = 3" for a synchro drive is correct, but saying "the robot is holonomic" is wrong. Holonomic requires **independent lateral force generation**, not just $\delta_M \geq 3$. > > 5. **Skipping the Grübler Derivation:** KTU 2024 specifically asks for derivation of the mobility formula from Grübler's equation in 14-mark questions. Merely stating the formula without derivation attracts only 2-3 marks. > > 6. **Miscounting Ackermann DOF:** Students often claim Ackermann has "1 DOF" or "2 DOF" without distinguishing structural mobility (3) from instantaneous path mobility (1). Examiners want both clarified. ## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] **Workspace** = reachable points $(x,y)$ in the plane; differs from configuration space $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$. - [x] **DOF (Degrees of Freedom)** = minimum independent variables to specify configuration; max 3 in 2D plane. - [x] **Holonomic** = controllable DOF = total DOF; can move in any direction directly. - [x] **Non-Holonomic** = controllable DOF $<$ total DOF; has velocity constraints $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. - [x] **Standard Fixed Wheel** = 2 constraints (rolling + lateral), 1 DOF (rotation), not steerable. - [x] **Standard Steered Wheel** = 1 constraint (rolling), 2 DOF (rotation + steering). - [x] **Castor Wheel** = 1 constraint (rolling), 2 DOF (rotation + passive steering). - [x] **Swedish (Mecanum) Wheel** = 0 constraints (ideal), 3 DOF, enables holonomic motion. - [x] **Mobility Formula**: $M = 3(N-1) - 2N_{sf} - N_{ss} - N_{cs}$. - [x] **Grübler's Origin**: $M = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$; applied to mobile robot with rolling constraints. - [x] **Manoeuvrability**: $\delta_M = M + S$; need $\delta_M = 3$ for holonomic (necessary, not sufficient). - [x] **Differential Drive**: $M = 1$, non-holonomic, simplest, 1-input motion. - [x] **Ackermann Car**: $M = 3$ structural, $M = 1$ instantaneous, car-like turning. - [x] **Synchro Drive**: $M = 3$, zero turning radius, all wheels steered and driven together. - [x] **Omnidirectional (4 Swedish)**: $M_{eff} = 3$, full holonomic, 3-DOF motion. - [x] **Differential Drive Forward Kinematics**: $v = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)$, $\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)$. - [x] **Non-holonomic constraint equation**: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. - [x] **Workspace of differential drive** = entire $\mathbb{R}^2$ plane (theoretically, no obstacles). - [x] **Steerability $S$** = number of actively steered wheels (steered standard wheels count). - [x] **Holonomic test** = $\delta_M = 3$ AND independent lateral force capability. - [x] **Path planning for non-holonomic** = Dubins curves (forward only) or Reeds-Shepp (forward + reverse). - [x] **Capping rule** for Swedish wheels: $M_{eff} = \min(M_{structural}, 3)$. - [x] **KTU 2024 Common Errors**: coefficient confusion, omitting Grübler derivation, misidentifying holonomic. > **Final Note:** The Degrees of Freedom analysis is the **gateway topic** of KTU PBRUT504 Module 1. Mastering the mobility formula, wheel constraints, and holonomic distinction is essential for tackling subsequent modules on localization, mapping, and path planning.

topics.title: Holonomic Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Holonomic Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Holonomic Robot** is a mobile robotic system in which the number of *controllable degrees of freedom (DOF)* equals the *total degrees of freedom* of the robot in its operating workspace. Equivalently, a holonomic robot can instantaneously translate and rotate along **every independent axis** of its configuration space without any kinematic restriction.

Mathematically, a robot with configuration vector $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is **holonomic** if the dimension of its control input space $m$ equals $n$ (i.e., $m = n$), and there exist **no non-integrable velocity constraints** of the form $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Holonomy is fundamentally a property of the *constraint equations* acting on the system, NOT merely a property of the mechanical hardware. A robot is holonomic if every velocity constraint is integrable to a position constraint.

## 1.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — The Core Metric

The total degrees of freedom of a mobile robot operating in a 2D plane is always **3**:
$$q = [x, y, \theta]^T$$
where $x$ and $y$ are positional coordinates and $\theta$ is the orientation (heading angle).

| Robot Type | Controllable DOF ($m$) | Total DOF ($n$) | Holonomic? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Drive | 2 | 3 | ❌ No |
| Ackermann / Car-like | 2 | 3 | ❌ No |
| Omnidirectional (Mecanum) | 3 | 3 | ✅ Yes |
| Synchro Drive | 3 | 3 | ✅ Yes |
| Tracked (Skid-steer) | 2 | 3 | ❌ No |

## 1.3 Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic — The Vital Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Holonomic Constraint:** A constraint that can be expressed as an algebraic equation involving *only* the position coordinates and time (not velocities). Form: $f(q, t) = 0$. Example: A point on a rigid disc rolling on a flat surface (its distance from the floor is fixed).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Non-Holonomic Constraint:** A constraint that *must* be expressed in differential/velocity form, AND cannot be integrated into a pure position equation. Form: $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$. Example: A car cannot slide sideways — the constraint involves the *velocity* of the car.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Chair Test"

Imagine a standard wheeled **office chair** in a room:

- **Non-holonomic chair (wheels locked to roll only forward/backward):** You can drive it forward, but to move sideways toward your colleague's desk, you must first *rotate* the chair 90°, then drive forward. The chair is **incapable of instantaneous sideways translation**.

- **Holonomic chair (free-rotating casters / mecanum wheels):** You can drift it directly sideways, spin in place, or move diagonally — **all simultaneously and instantaneously**. The chair has *full maneuverability* in the plane.

This is precisely the difference: holonomic systems can execute **any 2D velocity vector $(v_x, v_y, \omega)$** at any instant, regardless of current orientation.

## 1.5 Why Holonomy Matters in Mobile Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Holonomic robots **simplify motion planning, path tracking, and dynamic obstacle avoidance** because the reachable velocity set is a *full disk* in velocity space. Non-holonomic robots require complex techniques like *Reeds-Shepp curves*, *Dubins paths*, or *nonlinear feedback linearization* to execute point-to-point motions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation — Pfaffian Constraints

A general mobile robot constraint takes the **Pfaffian form**:
$$A(q)\dot{q} = 0$$

where $A(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$ is the constraint matrix and $\dot{q}$ is the configuration velocity.

**Test for Holonomicity:**
- If $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ can be **integrated** to yield $f(q) = 0$ (a position constraint) → **Holonomic**
- If the constraint is **non-integrable** (Frobenius condition fails) → **Non-holonomic**

**Frobenius Theorem Condition:** A Pfaffian constraint $a(q)\dot{q} = 0$ (single row) is holonomic if and only if there exists an integrating factor $\mu(q) \neq 0$ such that $\mu \cdot a(q) = \nabla f(q)$ for some scalar function $f$.

## 2.2 Worked Example — The Car-Like Robot (Non-Holonomic)

For a car-like robot with configuration $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$, the sideways motion is forbidden:

$$\dot{x}\sin(\theta) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta) = 0$$

Expanding the time derivative of $\theta$, this becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}\sin(\theta) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta) &= 0 \\
\frac{d}{dt}\left[y\cos(\theta) + x\sin(\theta)\right] &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — this *does* integrate to $y\cos(\theta) + x\sin(\theta) = C$. So is the car holonomic? **No** — the correct non-holonomic constraint is the second one: $\dot{x}\cos(\theta) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta) = 0$ does NOT exist; the actual non-holonomic constraint is the **steering angle rate** equation. The key point: the car has only 2 controls (velocity $v$ and steering rate $\dot{\phi}$), so it cannot achieve any 3D velocity vector instantly.

## 2.3 Kinematic Model — Omnidirectional (Holonomic) Robot

A four-wheel **mecanum-wheeled robot** has the inverse Jacobian:

$$\omega_i = \frac{1}{r}\left(v_x \pm v_y \pm L_i \omega\right), \quad i = 1,2,3,4$$

In compact matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix}\omega_1 \\ \omega_2 \\ \omega_3 \\ \omega_4\end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & L \\ 1 & 1 & -L \\ 1 & 1 & L \\ 1 & -1 & -L \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega\end{bmatrix}$$

The matrix on the right is **invertible** (determinant $\neq 0$), proving that for *any* desired body-frame velocity $(v_x, v_y, \omega)$, there exists a unique set of wheel velocities. This is the mathematical proof of holonomy.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | Robot configuration in 2D plane | Position: $m$, Angle: $rad$ |
| $n$ | Total DOF of the robot | dimensionless, $n = 3$ for planar |
| $m$ | Number of independent control inputs | dimensionless |
| **Holonomy Index** $\rho = m/n$ | Ratio of controllable to total DOF | $\rho = 1$: holonomic; $\rho < 1$: non-holonomic |
| $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ | Pfaffian form of velocity constraint | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$ |
| $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Car sideways-sliding constraint | Non-holonomic |
| $\omega_i = \frac{1}{r}(v_x \pm v_y \pm L_i \omega)$ | Mecanum wheel inverse kinematics | $\omega_i$ in $rad/s$ |
| $L$ | Half-diagonal distance: wheel to robot center | $m$ |
| $r$ | Wheel radius | $m$ |
| Reeds-Shepp distance | Shortest non-holonomic path length | $m$ |
| Dubins curve | Shortest non-holonomic path (no reverse) | $m$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever asked "Is the robot holonomic?", count the **independent control inputs** (motors giving independent velocities) and compare with the **dimension of the configuration space**. The rule $\rho = 1$ decides it.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva, Fetch):** Holonomic robots navigate tight aisles, dock sideways onto shelves, and rotate in place for picking tasks.
- **Hospital Logistics:** Mecanum-wheeled robots deliver medications in narrow corridors.
- **Soccer Robots (RoboCup):** Holonomic drives allow rapid omnidirectional ball tracking.
- **Camera Drones (Gimbal Systems):** 3-axis gimbals are holonomic — they can achieve any 3D orientation instantaneously.
- **CNC Machine Tool Axes:** Linear stages with decoupled X/Y/Z are holonomic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a Mecanum-Wheeled Holonomic Robot

### Setup
Consider a four-wheel mecanum robot with wheels mounted at corners of a square of side $2L$ (so the wheel-center distance from the geometric center is $L$ in both X and Y). The roller orientation is at $45°$ to the wheel axle.

**Step 1 — Velocity Contribution of a Single Wheel:**
A mecanum wheel with active spinning speed $\omega$ and ground contact velocity vector decomposed into two perpendicular rollers produces a *net* force vector at $45°$ to the wheel's forward axis. For the standard left-tilted wheel (rollers angled as `/`):

$$\vec{v}_{contact} = \omega \cdot r \cdot (\cos 45°, \sin 45°)^T$$

**Step 2 — Wheel Position Vectors (relative to robot center):**
Wheel 1 (front-left): $\vec{r}_1 = (L, L)$
Wheel 2 (front-right): $\vec{r}_2 = (L, -L)$
Wheel 3 (rear-left): $\vec{r}_3 = (-L, L)$
Wheel 4 (rear-right): $\vec{r}_4 = (-L, -L)$

**Step 3 — Body-Frame Velocity at Each Wheel:**
The velocity at wheel $i$ equals the velocity at the center plus the rotational contribution:

$$\vec{v}_i = \begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \end{bmatrix} + \omega \begin{bmatrix} -y_i \\ x_i \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Project onto Wheel-Allowed Direction:**
For each wheel, only the velocity component along its roller direction is admissible. Let $\alpha_i$ be the angle of the roller. The admissible wheel velocity is the projection of $\vec{v}_i$ onto the roller axis:

$$v_{wheel,i} = \vec{v}_i \cdot \hat{u}_i$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Wheel Angular Velocities:**
Setting $v_{wheel,i} = r \cdot \omega_i$ and inverting the full system yields the matrix equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}\omega_1 \\ \omega_2 \\ \omega_3 \\ \omega_4\end{bmatrix} &= \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & -(x_1 - y_1) \\ 1 & 1 & -(x_2 - y_2) \\ 1 & -1 & -(x_3 - y_3) \\ 1 & 1 & -(x_4 - y_4) \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega\end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & -2L \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 2L \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Determinant Test for Holonomy:**
The determinant of the $4 \times 3$ pseudo-Jacobian is $\frac{8L^2}{r^3}$. Since the matrix is *full column rank* (rank = 3), the **column space spans all of $\mathbb{R}^3$**, meaning every $(v_x, v_y, \omega)$ is achievable. This **proves holonomy**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Non-Holonomic Constraint for a Differential-Drive Robot

**Step 1 — Kinematic Equations:**
For a differential-drive robot with wheel separation $b$ and wheel radius $r$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) \\
\omega &= \frac{r}{b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Express in Configuration Space:**
$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Non-Holonomic Constraint:**
Multiply the first equation by $\sin\theta$ and the second by $\cos\theta$, then subtract:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

**Step 4 — Test Integrability:**
Expanding by the chain rule:
$$\frac{d}{dt}[-x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta] = \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta - \dot{\theta}(x\sin\theta - y\cos\theta)$$

The original constraint is $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$, which integrates to $x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta = C$ (using a different sign convention carefully). The point is that while the **first** non-holonomic-looking equation *can* be integrated, the **second** constraint — namely the relationship between linear and angular velocity — is the *true* non-holonomic restriction. Since the robot has only 2 controls but lives in a 3D configuration space, it is non-holonomic.

**Step 5 — Holonomy Index:**
$$\rho = \frac{m}{n} = \frac{2}{3} < 1 \implies \text{Non-Holonomic}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Test / Property | Holonomic Robot | Non-Holonomic Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Instantaneous velocity in any direction? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (orientation-dependent) |
| $\rho = m/n$ value | $1$ | $< 1$ |
| Number of Pfaffian constraints | $0$ independent | $\geq 1$ non-integrable |
| Reachable velocity set | Full disk $v_x^2 + v_y^2 \leq v_{max}^2$ | Annulus/ring |
| Parking maneuver in tight space | ✅ Trivial | ❌ Requires multi-point turn |
| Path planning complexity | Low (straight lines) | High (Reeds-Shepp, Dubins) |
| Examples | Mecanum, Swedish, Synchro, Ball-wheel | Differential, Car-like, Tracked |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow — Classifying a Robot as Holonomic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Mobile Robot] --> B[Identify Configuration Space Dimension n]
    B --> C[n equals 3 in 2D plane]
    C --> D[Count Independent Control Inputs m]
    D --> E{m equals n?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Holonomic Robot]
    E -->|No| G[Non-Holonomic Robot]
    F --> H[Verify: All Velocity Constraints Integrable?]
    H -->|Yes| I[Confirmed Holonomic]
    H -->|No| J[Re-examine Constraints]
    G --> K[Count Non-Integrable Pfaffian Constraints]
    K --> L[Compute m and n]
    L --> M[Report Holonomy Index m over n less than 1]
    I --> N[Examples: Mecanum, Synchro Drive]
    M --> O[Examples: Differential, Ackermann]
```

## 4.2 Mecanum Wheel Velocity Decomposition — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMD[Desired Body Velocity: vx, vy, omega] --> INV[Inverse Jacobian Matrix 4x3]
    INV --> W1[Wheel 1 Angular Velocity]
    INV --> W2[Wheel 2 Angular Velocity]
    INV --> W3[Wheel 3 Angular Velocity]
    INV --> W4[Wheel 4 Angular Velocity]
    W1 --> M1[BLDC Motor 1]
    W2 --> M2[BLDC Motor 2]
    W3 --> M3[BLDC Motor 3]
    W4 --> M4[BLDC Motor 4]
    M1 --> FW1[Ground Force Vector at 45 degrees]
    M2 --> FW2[Ground Force Vector at 45 degrees]
    M3 --> FW3[Ground Force Vector at 45 degrees]
    M4 --> FW4[Ground Force Vector at 45 degrees]
    FW1 --> NET[Net Body-Frame Velocity]
    FW2 --> NET
    FW3 --> NET
    FW4 --> NET
    NET --> OUT[Achieved vx, vy, omega]
```

## 4.3 Reachable Velocity Set Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph HOLO[Holonomic Robot Reachable Velocity Set]
        H1[Full Disk of Radius vmax]
        H2[Any direction reachable instantly]
    end
    subgraph NONHOLO[Non-Holonomic Robot Reachable Velocity Set]
        N1[Velocity constrained along heading theta]
        N2[Pure lateral motion not allowed]
        N3[Forward plus rotation allowed]
        N4[Reverse plus rotation allowed]
    end
    HOLO --> COMPARE[Holonomic has strictly larger reachable set]
    NONHOLO --> COMPARE
```

## 4.4 Kinematic Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Holonomic Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REF[Reference Trajectory: xd, yd, theta d] --> CMP[Subtract Current State]
    SENS[Wheel Encoders and IMU] --> FB[State Estimation]
    FB --> EST[Estimated x, y, theta]
    EST --> CMP
    CMP --> ERR[Error Signal e]
    ERR --> CTRL[Controller: PID or LQR]
    CTRL --> INVJ[Inverse Jacobian J inverse]
    INVJ --> WVEL[Wheel Angular Velocities]
    WVEL --> MOT[Motor Drivers]
    MOT --> WH[Mecanum Wheels]
    WH --> ROBOT[Robot Body]
    ROBOT --> SENS
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a holonomic robot. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A holonomic robot is one in which the number of controllable degrees of freedom equals the total degrees of freedom of the robot in its configuration space. Equivalently, a holonomic robot can move instantaneously in *any* direction in the workspace, including pure lateral translations, regardless of its current orientation.

The mathematical condition is $\rho = m/n = 1$, where $m$ is the number of independent control inputs and $n$ is the configuration space dimension.

**Two Examples:**
1. A four-wheeled mecanum-wheeled omnidirectional robot.
2. A synchro-drive robot (where all wheels are mechanically coupled to a common steer and drive shaft).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 0.5 per example with brief justification)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between holonomic and non-holonomic constraints with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Holonomic Constraint | Non-Holonomic Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Form | Algebraic, $f(q, t) = 0$ | Differential, $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ |
| Integrable? | Yes (by definition) | No (non-integrable) |
| Reduces configuration space? | Yes | No (reduces only velocity space) |
| Example | Point on a rolling disc: contact point at fixed height | Car cannot slide sideways: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ |

*(3 marks: 1 per row of distinction, 1 for examples)*

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the inverse kinematic equations of a four-wheel mecanum-driven holonomic mobile robot. Show the matrix form and explain how it proves holonomy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Wheel Layout:** Place four mecanum wheels at the corners of a square of side $2L$ centered on the robot origin. The wheel radii are $r$, and the rollers are mounted at $\pm 45°$ to the wheel axle.

**Step 2 — Wheel Position Vectors:**
$$\vec{p}_1 = (L, L), \quad \vec{p}_2 = (L, -L), \quad \vec{p}_3 = (-L, L), \quad \vec{p}_4 = (-L, -L)$$

**Step 3 — Velocity at Each Wheel Due to Body Motion:**
$$\vec{v}_i = \begin{bmatrix} v_x - \omega y_i \\ v_y + \omega x_i \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Project Onto Roller Direction:**
For wheel $i$, the admissible wheel-rolling direction is at angle $\alpha_i$. The component of $\vec{v}_i$ along the roller direction equals $r \cdot \omega_i$:

$$\omega_i = \frac{1}{r}\left[v_x(\cos\alpha_i) + v_y(\sin\alpha_i) + \omega(x_i\sin\alpha_i - y_i\cos\alpha_i)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Standard Angles $\alpha_1 = -45°, \alpha_2 = 45°, \alpha_3 = 45°, \alpha_4 = -45°$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix}\omega_1 \\ \omega_2 \\ \omega_3 \\ \omega_4\end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & -2L \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 2L \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Proof of Holonomy:** The $4 \times 3$ matrix $J^{-1}$ has rank 3 (full column rank). Its null space is $\{0\}$, so for *any* desired $(v_x, v_y, \omega)^T$, there exists a unique set of wheel velocities $\omega_i$. Therefore, all three degrees of freedom are independently controllable, and $\rho = 3/3 = 1$, confirming the robot is **holonomic**.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for derivation, 1 for matrix, 2 for holonomy proof)*

---

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheel separation $b = 0.3$ m. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 14$ rad/s. Compute the linear velocity $v$, angular velocity $\omega$, and the radius of curvature $R$ of the path. Is this robot holonomic? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Linear Velocity:**
$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = \frac{0.05}{2}(14 + 10) = \frac{0.05 \times 24}{2} = 0.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Angular Velocity:**
$$\omega = \frac{r}{b}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.05}{0.3}(14 - 10) = \frac{0.05 \times 4}{0.3} = \frac{0.2}{0.3} = 0.6667 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Radius of Curvature:**
$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.6}{0.6667} = 0.9 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Holonomy Check:**
The differential-drive robot has $n = 3$ DOF (position $x, y$ and orientation $\theta$) but only $m = 2$ independent control inputs ($\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$). Therefore:
$$\rho = \frac{m}{n} = \frac{2}{3} < 1$$

The non-holonomic constraint is $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$, which prevents instantaneous lateral motion. Hence the robot is **non-holonomic**.

*(7 marks: 1.5 each for steps 1, 2, 3, 1.5 for holonomy justification)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** What is a non-holonomic constraint? With reference to a unicycle-type mobile robot, write the kinematic equations and derive the non-holonomic constraint. Show that this constraint restricts the robot from moving sideways. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:** A non-holonomic constraint is a velocity-level constraint that cannot be integrated into a position-level constraint. It restricts the *instantaneous* velocities a robot can achieve but does not restrict the *configuration space* it can reach over time.

**Step 2 — Unicycle Kinematic Model:**
The unicycle has configuration $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ and two control inputs: linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$. The kinematic equations are:
$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Non-Holonomic Constraint:**
Multiply the first equation by $\sin\theta$ and the second by $\cos\theta$, then subtract:
$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = v(\cos\theta\sin\theta - \sin\theta\cos\theta) = 0$$

So the constraint is:
$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation:** The velocity vector $(\dot{x}, \dot{y})$ is always parallel to the robot heading $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. The lateral component of velocity is *always zero*:
$$\vec{v}_{lateral} = (\dot{x}, \dot{y}) \cdot (-\sin\theta, \cos\theta) = -\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

Hence the robot can never slide sideways — it must first rotate, then translate.

*(7 marks: 1.5 for definition, 2 for kinematic equations, 2 for constraint derivation, 1.5 for physical interpretation)*

---

**(b)** Compare holonomic and non-holonomic mobile robots in terms of (i) controllable degrees of freedom, (ii) path planning complexity, (iii) parking maneuverability, and (iv) industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Controllable DOF:**
- Holonomic: All DOF are independently controllable ($\rho = 1$).
- Non-holonomic: Only a subset of DOF is instantaneously controllable ($\rho < 1$).

**(ii) Path Planning Complexity:**
- Holonomic: Straight-line paths in free space are always feasible. Standard A* or RRT works directly.
- Non-holonomic: Requires specialized planners — Reeds-Shepp (with reverse), Dubins (forward only), or nonlinear control to generate feasible paths. Steers the curvature continuously.

**(iii) Parking Maneuverability:**
- Holonomic: Can park sideways into a parallel slot, or rotate in place if the slot equals the robot footprint.
- Non-holonomic: Requires complex multi-point turns (e.g., 3-point turn for a car). Cannot achieve a sideways velocity component without first aligning the heading.

**(iv) Industrial Applications:**
- Holonomic: Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva), RoboCup soccer robots, hospital delivery bots, camera gimbals.
- Non-holonomic: Outdoor autonomous vehicles (Ackermann steering), surveillance ground robots, simple differential-drive indoor service robots.

*(7 marks: ~1.5 marks per category with 1 for the key distinguishing fact)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Most common mistake (–2 marks):** Students often confuse "omni-wheel" with "mecanum wheel." An omni-wheel has rollers perpendicular to the axle (allows only forward and sideways *if* combined in pairs). A mecanum wheel has rollers at **45°** to the axle, producing force vectors at 45° — these *do not* cancel when four wheels are arranged properly, giving true holonomic behavior. State the roller angle explicitly.
> 2. **Second most common mistake (–1 mark):** Forgetting that holonomy is a property of the **constraint equations**, not the wheel type. A 3-wheel omni-robot *can* be holonomic *only if* the wheel geometry is arranged correctly; otherwise it is non-holonomic.
> 3. **Numerical error (–1 mark):** In the mecanum inverse Jacobian, the sign pattern is critical. Mixing up the $+1$ and $-1$ entries in the second column (related to $v_y$) inverts the sideways motion direction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Holonomy Index Formula:** $\rho = m/n$. Holonomic if $\rho = 1$; non-holonomic if $\rho < 1$.
- ✅ **Planar Robot Configuration:** Always $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$, so $n = 3$.
- ✅ **Mecanum Wheel Signature:** Rollers at $45°$ to axle; four such wheels → full holonomy.
- ✅ **Non-Holonomic Constraint Form:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ (car/unicycle).
- ✅ **Frobenius Test:** Pfaffian constraint is holonomic iff an integrating factor exists.
- ✅ **Reachable Velocity Set:** Disk for holonomic; constrained to heading direction for non-holonomic.
- ✅ **Path Planners:** Dubins (no reverse) and Reeds-Shepp (with reverse) for non-holonomic; trivial straight lines for holonomic.
- ✅ **Inverse Jacobian of Mecanum Drive:** A $4 \times 3$ matrix with signs $\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 & -2L \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 2L\end{bmatrix}$ scaled by $1/r$.
- ✅ **Examples — Holonomic:** Mecanum, Synchro, Ball-wheel, Omni-directional with proper geometry.
- ✅ **Examples — Non-Holonomic:** Differential drive, Ackermann (car-like), Tracked/skid-steer, Unicycle.
- ✅ **Industrial Use-Case:** Holonomic robots dominate *indoor structured* warehouses; non-holonomic dominate *outdoor* and *high-speed* applications.
- ✅ **Examination Mantra:** "Count the controls, compare with the configuration space, apply $\rho = 1$ test."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Path Planning** is the computational problem of finding a geometric sequence of configurations (or states) that moves a mobile robot from an initial configuration (start pose) to a goal configuration (target pose) while avoiding collisions with obstacles in the environment and satisfying any kinematic/dynamic constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Configuration Space (C-Space) Formulation:** Path planning is formally defined as finding a continuous mapping $\tau: [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{free}$, where $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ is the obstacle-free subset of the robot's configuration space $\mathcal{C}$. This abstraction transforms the geometric motion problem into a topological search problem.

**Key Sub-Classifications in KTU Syllabus:**

| Sub-Type | Definition | Time Horizon |
|----------|------------|--------------|
| **Global Path Planning** | Offline computation assuming known static map | Pre-execution |
| **Local Path Planning** | Online reactive planning using onboard sensors | Real-time |
| **Motion Planning** | Path planning + dynamics + timing | Kinodynamic |
| **Trajectory Planning** | Path + velocity & acceleration profiles | Time-parameterized |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Blindfolded Navigator Analogy:** Imagine you are blindfolded in an unfamiliar room. You have a rough floor plan (global map) and a cane (local sensors).
> - **Global Planner** = The floor plan you memorized before being blindfolded. It tells you *roughly* which direction to walk.
> - **Local Planner** = Your cane. It detects the chair right in front of you and tells you to step sideways.
> - **Replanning** = A friend shouting "There's a new wall here!" so you update your mental floor plan and reroute.
>
> Path planning algorithms are the "brain" that combines these two layers — a *deliberative* layer (long-term goal) and a *reactive* layer (immediate avoidance).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Mobile Robotics

The following performance metrics are universally accepted in KTU 2024 evaluation:

- **Path Length** $L(\tau)$ — measured in **meters (m)**
- **Path Optimality Ratio** $\rho = L_{computed} / L_{optimal}$ — dimensionless, $\rho \geq 1$
- **Computational Complexity** — measured in **Big-O time**, e.g., $O(n \log n)$
- **Clearance** $\delta$ — minimum distance from obstacles in **meters (m)**
- **Smoothness** — integral of squared curvature, $\int \kappa(s)^2 \, ds$
- **Completeness** — boolean property; *resolution-complete* or *probabilistically complete*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Path Planning Algorithms

**Why a Hierarchy?** Different algorithms are optimal for different environments. A KTU examiner expects you to identify *which class* suits *which scenario*.

- **A. Classical / Graph-Based Methods**
  - Visibility Graphs
  - Voronoi Diagrams
  - Cell Decomposition (Trapezoidal, Boustrophedon)
  - Search Algorithms: Dijkstra, A*, D*, D*-Lite
- **B. Sampling-Based Methods**
  - Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM)
  - Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT)
  - Optimal variants: PRM*, RRT*, Informed RRT*
- **C. Reactive / Potential Field Methods**
  - Artificial Potential Fields (APF)
  - Vector Field Histogram (VFH)
  - Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)
- **D. Bio-Inspired / Optimization Methods**
  - Genetic Algorithms (GA)
  - Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)
  - Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)

### 2.2 A* Search — The Workhorse of Mobile Robotics

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. Initialize two lists: `OPEN` (priority queue, $f$-cost ascending) and `CLOSED` (visited set).
2. Push start node $s$ into `OPEN` with $g(s) = 0$.
3. Pop node $n$ with the minimum $f(n)$.
4. If $n$ is the goal, **reconstruct path** by backtracking parent pointers.
5. For each neighbor $n'$ of $n$:
   - Compute $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$
   - Compute heuristic $h(n')$
   - Compute $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$
   - If $n'$ not in `CLOSED` or new $g$ is better, update and push to `OPEN`.
6. Repeat until goal found or `OPEN` empty (failure).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why A\* is Admissible:** If the heuristic $h(n)$ is *admissible* (never overestimates the true cost $h^*(n)$), A* is guaranteed to return an optimal path. If $h$ is also *consistent* (monotonic), the first time a node is popped from `OPEN` it has its optimal $g$-value.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Algorithm / Concept | Key Formula | Admissibility / Completeness | Typical Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dijkstra's Algorithm** | $f(n) = g(n)$ | Optimal, Complete | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ naive; $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ with heap |
| **A* Search** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Optimal if $h$ admissible | $O(b^d)$, $b$ = branching factor |
| **Euclidean Heuristic** | $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$ | Admissible & Consistent | $O(1)$ per node |
| **Manhattan Heuristic** | $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_g \vert + \vert y_n - y_g \vert$ | Admissible (4-connected grids) | $O(1)$ per node |
| **Visibility Graph Path Length** | $L = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert\vert v_i - v_{i-1} \vert\vert$ | Optimal in polygon env. | $O(n^2)$ construction |
| **APF — Attractive Force** | $F_{att} = -\xi \cdot (q - q_{goal})$ | Local minima possible | $O(1)$ per step |
| **APF — Repulsive Force** | $F_{rep} = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^2} \nabla \rho$ | Local minima possible | $O(m)$ per step, $m$ = nearby obstacles |
| **RRT Growth** | $q_{new} = q_{near} + \Delta q \cdot \frac{q_{rand} - q_{near}}{\vert\vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \vert\vert}$ | Probabilistically Complete | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Reeds-Shepp Car** | Path length $L_{RS} = \sum_i \vert s_i \vert$, $s_i \in \{S, L, R\}$ | Optimal for non-holonomic | $O(1)$ lookup (48 cases) |
| **Dubins Car** | $L_{Dubins} = \rho (\vert \alpha \vert + \vert \beta \vert + \vert \gamma \vert)$ | Optimal for forward-only | $O(1)$ (6 word sequences) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX NOTE:** In exam scripts, never use the vertical bar `|` inside a `\begin{aligned}` block when it appears in a table row context. KTU examiners mark "math presentation errors" separately. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value or cardinality in tables.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva):** A* on grid maps for inventory retrieval.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo):** Hybrid A* + Reeds-Shepp for parking, RRT* for unstructured off-road.
- **AGVs in Manufacturing:** PRM for static factory floors; VFH/DWA for dynamic human avoidance.
- **Drones (UAVs):** RRT* in 3D for obstacle-rich environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Admissibility Condition for A*

**Theorem:** A* returns an optimal path if and only if $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$, where $h^*(n)$ is the true cost to goal.

**Proof Sketch (Examiner's Favorite — KTU Pattern):**

Assume, for contradiction, that A* returns a suboptimal goal $G$ with $f(G) = g(G) > C^*$, where $C^*$ is the optimal cost.

Let $n$ be an unexpanded node on an optimal path with true cost $f^* = g(n) + h^*(n) = C^*$.

Since $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$, we have $f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \leq g(n) + h^*(n) = C^*$.

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(n) &\leq C^* < g(G) = f(G)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f(n) < f(G)$, the A* priority queue would pop $n$ **before** $G$, contradicting that $G$ was the first goal popped. Hence $G$ must be optimal. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Heuristic Trick:** If a student is asked "Is Manhattan heuristic admissible for an 8-connected grid?" — the answer is **NO**, because diagonal moves violate the assumption.
