# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**  
> Define the term **localization** in mobile robotics. Differentiate between **position tracking** and **global localization**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Localization is the process of estimating the pose (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) of a mobile robot relative to a known map, given a history of sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$. Mathematically, it computes the posterior belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

**Difference (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Position Tracking | Global Localization |
|---|---|---|
| **Initial Pose** | Known (robot starts with estimate) | Unknown (uniform belief over map) |
| **Belief Shape** | Unimodal Gaussian | Multi-modal histogram |
| **Algorithms** | KF, EKF | Grid, Particle Filter |
| **Difficulty** | Easier (local corrections) | Harder (search whole space) |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**  
> List the **three canonical localization problems** and write the **Bayes filter update equation** with a brief explanation of each term.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three canonical localization problems are:
1. **Position Tracking** (1 mark)
2. **Global Localization** (1 mark)
3. **Kidnapped Robot Problem** (1 mark)

**Bayes Filter Update Equation:**

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

Where:
- $bel(x_t)$ = posterior belief over pose at time $t$
- $\eta$ = normalization constant to ensure $\int bel = 1$
- $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ = sensor measurement likelihood
- $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ = predicted belief from motion model

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**(a) Derive the Bayes filter recursion for robot localization from first principles. State the Markov assumption explicitly and show how it simplifies the recursion. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the belief (1 mark):**

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' rule (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Markov assumption to sensor model (1.5 marks):**

The current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current pose $x_t$ (not on past measurements or controls):

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

**Step 4 — Total probability for the prior (1.5 marks):**

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_t) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Markov assumption to motion model and recognize prior belief (1 mark):**

$$
p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_t) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t), \quad p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = bel(x_{t-1})
$$

**Step 6 — Final recursion (0.5 marks):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\overline{bel}(x_t) &= \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} \\
bel(x_t) &= \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

**(b) Explain the Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter) algorithm in detail. Derive the importance weight update equation and explain the resampling step with a clear diagram. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Particle Representation (1 mark):**
The belief $bel(x_t)$ is represented as a set of $M$ weighted samples (particles):

$$bel(x_t) \approx \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$$

where $\sum_{i=1}^M w_t^{[i]} = 1$.

**Step 2 — Sampling and Weight Update (2 marks):**
For each particle, draw a new pose from the motion model and update the weight using the measurement likelihood:

$$
x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)
$$

$$
w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \cdot p(x_t^{[i]} \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)
$$

**Step 3 — Normalization (0.5 marks):**

$$
\tilde{w}_t^{[i]} = \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{M} w_t^{[j]}}
$$

**Step 4 — Resampling Criterion (1 mark):**
Compute Effective Sample Size (ESS):

$$
N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (\tilde{w}_t^{[i]})^2}
$$

If $N_{eff} < M/2$, trigger resampling.

**Step 5 — Low-Variance Resampling (1.5 marks):**
Draw a random number $u \sim U[0, M^{-1}]$. Initialize cumulative sum $c = w_t^{[1]}$. For $i = 1, \ldots, M$, find the smallest $j$ such that $c \geq u + (i-1)/M$ and add $x_t^{[j]}$ to the new particle set. This avoids particle depletion while maintaining diversity.

**Step 6 — Particle Injection for Kidnapped Robot (1 mark):**
To handle the kidnapped robot problem, replace 5–10% of particles at every resampling step with samples drawn uniformly from the map. This maintains global awareness.

**Diagram (Free 1 mark — to be drawn by student):** Show a 2D map with particles scattered, then collapsing into a cluster around the true pose over iterations.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP — Alternative]**

**(a) With a clear mathematical formulation, derive the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) prediction and update steps for a 2D differential drive robot. Define all Jacobians involved. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State Space Definition (0.5 mark):**

$$x_t = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}, \quad u_t = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}, \quad z_t = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Nonlinear Motion Model (1 mark):**

$$
f(x, u) = \begin{bmatrix} x + v \Delta t \cos\theta \\ y + v \Delta t \sin\theta \\ \theta + \omega \Delta t \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Motion Jacobian $F_x$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
F_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -v \Delta t \sin\theta \\
0 & 1 & v \Delta t \cos\theta \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Control Jacobian $F_u$ (0.5 mark):**

$$
F_u = \frac{\partial f}{\partial u} = \begin{bmatrix}
\Delta t \cos\theta & 0 \\
\Delta t \sin\theta & 0 \\
0 & \Delta t
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — EKF Prediction Equations (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\mu}_t &= f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) \\
\bar{\Sigma}_t &= F_x \Sigma_{t-1} F_x^T + F_u R_t^{control} F_u^T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Nonlinear Measurement Model (0.5 mark):**

$$
h(x) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_l - x)^2 + (y_l - y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_l - y, x_l - x) - \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Measurement Jacobian $H_t$ (1 mark):**

Let $q = (x_l - x)^2 + (y_l - y)^2$:

$$
H_t = \begin{bmatrix}
-(x_l - x)/\sqrt{q} & -(y_l - y)/\sqrt{q} & 0 \\
(y_l - y)/q & -(x_l - x)/q & -1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 8 — EKF Update (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + R_t)^{-1} \\
\mu_t &= \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)) \\
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) Compare the Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter, and Particle Filter for robot localization. Provide a clear comparison table and explain which algorithm is most suitable for the kidnapped robot problem. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Kalman Filter | EKF | Particle Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Belief shape | Unimodal Gaussian | Unimodal Gaussian | Multi-modal |
| Nonlinear handling | ❌ | ✅ (1st order) | ✅ (any) |
| Kidnap recovery | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (with injection) |
| Cost | $O(n^3)$ | $O(n^3)$ | $O(M \log M)$ |
| Use case | Linear tracking | Mildly nonlinear | Global localization |

**Kidnapped Robot — Why Particle Filter Wins (3 marks):**

1. The Kalman filter assumes a **unimodal Gaussian** belief. After a sudden displacement, the true pose is far from the Gaussian's mean; the algorithm cannot represent this and will diverge.
2. The Particle Filter represents belief as a **set of hypotheses** spread over the entire state space. After kidnapping, particles scattered globally will re-converge at the new location once sensor measurements match.
3. **Random particle injection** during resampling (5–10% of particles) is critical — it preserves global awareness and prevents the particle cloud from collapsing prematurely.
4. EKF fails because the Jacobian linearization breaks down for large jumps (no valid Taylor expansion around the wrong operating point).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The Particle Filter (MCL) is the standard choice for the kidnapped robot problem in KTU-level analysis.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the orientation $\theta$** in pose — always report full $(x, y, \theta)$. Marks deducted: 1–2.
> 2. **Not stating the Markov assumption explicitly** in Bayes filter derivations. Marks deducted: 1.
> 3. **Confusing belief $bel$ and $\overline{bel}$** — the predicted belief (after motion update, before measurement) is $\overline{bel}$, NOT $bel$. Marks deducted: 1.
> 4. **Writing Particle Filter weights without normalization** — always normalize so that $\sum w_t^{[i]} = 1$. Marks deducted: 0.5.
> 5. **Forgetting to add $Q_t$ in covariance prediction** — the process noise term is MANDATORY. Marks deducted: 1.
> 6. **Choosing Kalman Filter for global localization** in long-answer questions — examiner will deduct 2–3 marks for algorithmic mismatch.
> 7. **Skipping units in the formula sheet during numerical problems** — the question paper explicitly requires units (meters, radians, seconds). Marks deducted: 0.5 per instance.
> 8. **Not mentioning the Joseph form** in EKF covariance update — full credit only for the numerically stable form.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **last-minute revision checklist** — read it 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.

- ✅ **Localization** = estimate pose $(x, y, \theta)$ of robot relative to a known map.
- ✅ Three problems: **Position Tracking**, **Global Localization**, **Kidnapped Robot Problem**.
- ✅ Bayes filter = universal umbrella; alternates **prediction** and **measurement update**.
- ✅ **Markov assumption** = future depends only on current state. Apply to BOTH motion and sensor models.
- ✅ **Kalman Filter** = linear, Gaussian, unimodal. Best for position tracking.
- ✅ **EKF** = first-order linearization of nonlinear systems via Jacobian. Best for mildly nonlinear systems.
- ✅ **Particle Filter (MCL)** = sample-based, multi-modal. Best for global localization & kidnapped robot.
- ✅ **Particle injection** (5–10% random samples per resample) = standard fix for kidnapped robot.
- ✅ Effective Sample Size $N_{eff} = 1 / \sum (w^{[i]})^2$. Resample when $N_{eff} < M/2$.
- ✅ Kalman gain $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$ — optimal under Gaussian noise.
- ✅ Joseph form $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t R_t K_t^T$ is numerically stable.
- ✅ EKF Jacobian for differential drive: $F_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v\Delta t \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & v\Delta t \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
- ✅ Beam range sensor model: $p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \mathcal{N}(z_t^k; z_{t,k}^*, \sigma^2)$ with $z_{t,k}^*$ from ray casting.
- ✅ Odometry model: $[x', y', \theta']^T = [x + \Delta s \cos(\theta + \Delta \rho/2), y + \Delta s \sin(\theta + \Delta \rho/2), \theta + \Delta \rho]^T$.
- ✅ Sensor noise and actuator drift → never trust single measurements; always use Bayesian fusion.
- ✅ Consistency = $\text{NEES} = d^2 / n$ should follow $\chi^2_n$ distribution. Overconfident estimators fail this.
- ✅ Multi-modal belief is essential for **symmetric maps** (e.g., identical corridors) — Gaussian filters fail here.
- ✅ Computational cost tradeoff: KF/EKF = $O(n^3)$ but only 1 hypothesis; PF = $O(M \log M)$ for $M$ hypotheses.
- ✅ KTU 2024 emphasizes **probabilistic approach** — if your answer doesn't mention probability distributions, you have already lost 1–2 conceptual marks.
- ✅ Always state the **assumptions** of any algorithm you apply (linearity, Gaussianity, Markov property).
- ✅ Diagrammatic explanations in long answers carry **bonus 0.5–1 mark** — always draw Bayes filter / particle filter flowcharts.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 — LOCALIZATION NOTES**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Syllabus | NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education | Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L5)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Sensing in Mobile Robotics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Sensing):**
> *Sensing is the process by which a mobile robot acquires information about its **internal state** (proprioception) and its **external environment** (exteroception) through physical transducers, converting measurable physical phenomena into electrical signals that can be processed for perception, localization, mapping, and decision-making.*

In the mobile-robotics stack, sensing is the **lowest information layer**. Without sensors, a robot is functionally blind — it cannot know *where it is* (localization), *what surrounds it* (perception), or *how its actuators are behaving* (closed-loop control). Every higher-level algorithm (SLAM, path planning, obstacle avoidance) ultimately depends on the quality and reliability of raw sensor data.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Sensing is the Robot's Five Senses"

Imagine a human walking through a dark room while wearing a blindfold but holding a **white cane**:

| Human Sense | Robot Equivalent | Underlying Physics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Touch (foot feedback) | Wheel encoders, force sensors | Mechanical/optical rotation |
| Inner-ear (balance) | IMU (gyroscope, accelerometer) | Coriolis, inertia |
| Hearing (echo-location) | Ultrasonic / Sonar sensors | Time-of-Flight (ToF) of sound |
| Sight (vision) | Camera (mono/stereo) | Light intensity / photons |
| Spatial memory (GPS) | GNSS / GPS receiver | Triangulation with satellites |
| Compass (direction) | Magnetometer, digital compass | Earth's magnetic field |

Just as a blind person integrates cane-tap echoes and inner-ear balance to walk, a mobile robot **fuses** multiple noisy sensors to estimate its pose and surroundings — a process called **sensor fusion**.

### 1.3 Two Master Categories of Sensors

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module-2 Anchor):**
> Every sensor discussed under this module belongs to one of these two master classes.

1. **Proprioceptive Sensors** — measure values *internal* to the robot.
   * Examples: wheel encoders, motor current, battery voltage, gyroscope, accelerometer.
2. **Exteroceptive Sensors** — measure values *external* to the robot (the environment).
   * Examples: sonar, laser rangefinder (LIDAR), cameras, GPS, bumpers.

A further sub-classification is **Active** vs **Passive**:
* **Active sensors** inject energy into the environment (e.g., sonar pings, laser pulses) and measure the returned signal.
* **Passive sensors** only listen to ambient energy (e.g., cameras reading sunlight, a compass reading Earth's field).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for exams:** *Proprioception = "Proprio" = one's own. Exteroception = "Exterior" = outside.*

### 1.4 Why Sensing is Hard — The Noise Problem

Real sensors do not return "the truth." They return **the truth corrupted by noise**. This noise is of two kinds:

* **Systematic error (bias)** — predictable, repeatable, e.g., a miscalibrated wheel diameter that always over-estimates distance.
* **Random error (variance)** — unpredictable, e.g., electrical jitter, photon shot noise.

> [!WARNING]
> A KTU favourite question: *"Differentiate between accuracy and precision."* — get this right!
> * **Accuracy** = closeness to the *true* value.
> * **Precision** = closeness of *repeated measurements* to each other.
> A sensor can be precise (low spread) but inaccurate (biased away from truth), and vice versa.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Performance Characteristics

A KTU examiner expects you to describe sensors using these six canonical properties. Memorise the table below — it has appeared in **previous-year Part A (3-mark)** questions.

| Characteristic | Definition | Typical Unit / Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Range** | Minimum and maximum measurable value | $d_{min}, d_{max}$ |
| **Resolution** | Smallest detectable change in input | $\Delta x_{min}$ |
| **Dynamic Range** | Ratio of maximum to minimum detectable signal, often in dB | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d_{max}}{d_{min}}\right)$ |
| **Linearity** | Degree to which output $\propto$ input (deviation = **non-linearity**) | percent of full scale |
| **Sensitivity** | $\dfrac{\partial(\text{output})}{\partial(\text{input})}$ | V/m, counts/rev |
| **Bandwidth** | Range of frequencies at which the sensor responds faithfully | Hz |
| **Accuracy** | Closeness of mean reading to true value | bias $\mu$ |
| **Precision / Repeatability** | Closeness of multiple readings to each other | std. deviation $\sigma$ |

## 2.2 The Two Big Families Discussed in KTU Module 2

### 2.2.1 Wheel / Motor Encoders (Proprioceptive)

An **optical shaft encoder** is a slotted disc that rotates with the wheel. An LED–photodiode pair counts the slots passing per unit time.

* Two channels (A and B) in **quadrature** give both *count* and *direction*.
* Resolution of the encoder: $N$ counts per revolution.

For a wheel of diameter $D$ rolling without slip, the linear distance travelled per encoder tick is:

$$ \delta_d = \frac{\pi D}{N} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental odometry equation**. It is derived fully in Section 3.

### 2.2.2 Range / Ranging Sensors (Exteroceptive)

These measure *distance* to obstacles. Three physical principles dominate the KTU syllabus:

**(a) Time-of-Flight (ToF)** — emit a wave, measure round-trip time $t$.

$$ d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} $$

* For **ultrasonic (sonar)**: $c \approx 343$ m/s in air at 20 °C.
* For **laser (LIDAR)**: $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(b) Phase-Shift Detection** — modulate a continuous carrier, measure phase lag $\phi$.

$$ d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4 \pi f} $$

**(c) Triangulation (geometric)** — project a laser spot, image it on a 1-D sensor at baseline $B$ and focal length $f$, solve the triangle.

$$ d = \frac{B \cdot f}{x} $$

where $x$ is the lateral pixel position of the spot on the detector.

## 2.3 The Complete KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every formula below has appeared — or is *very likely* to appear — in a KTU ESE question paper for PBRUT504.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Encoder linear resolution | $\delta_d = \dfrac{\pi D}{N}$ | $D$ = wheel dia, $N$ = ticks/rev |
| 2 | Encoder angular resolution | $\delta_\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ | radians per tick |
| 3 | Differential-drive forward kinematics | $\Delta x = R \dfrac{\Delta \phi_R + \Delta \phi_L}{2}$ | $R$ = wheel radius |
| 4 | Differential-drive rotation | $\Delta \theta = R \dfrac{\Delta \phi_R - \Delta \phi_L}{L}$ | $L$ = axle length |
| 5 | Sonar / LIDAR range | $d = \dfrac{c \, t}{2}$ | $t$ = round-trip time |
| 6 | Laser triangulation | $d = \dfrac{B \, f}{x}$ | $B$ = baseline, $f$ = focal length, $x$ = spot position |
| 7 | Phase-shift range | $d = \dfrac{c \, \phi}{4 \pi f}$ | $\phi$ = phase shift, $f$ = modulation freq |
| 8 | Uncertainty propagation (sum) | $\sigma_{tot} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \cdots + \sigma_n^2}$ | Independent Gaussian errors |
| 9 | Relative uncertainty (product) | $\dfrac{\sigma_y}{y} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{b}\right)^2}$ | $y = a \cdot b$ |
| 10 | Field of view footprint | $W = 2 d \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ | $\alpha$ = beam width, $d$ = range |
| 11 | GPS position via pseudo-range | $d_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2}$ | $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ = satellite $i$ |

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Industry

* **Autonomous vehicles (e.g., self-driving cars)** fuse camera + LIDAR + radar + IMU + GPS — a single sensor cannot cover blind spots, weather, and resolution simultaneously.
* **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots)** rely on 2-D LIDAR for SLAM — encoders alone drift, vision alone struggles in dust.
* **Drone swarms** use ultra-wide-band (UWB) ranging for inter-agent localisation, fusing it with on-board IMUs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every derivation below is shown in full. **No step is skipped.** This is the KTU valuation style — partial credit is awarded for every correct intermediate expression.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Linear Odometry from an Optical Shaft Encoder

**Given:** A differential-drive robot has wheels of diameter $D = 0.20$ m. Each wheel is fitted with an optical encoder giving $N = 2000$ counts per revolution. In one sampling interval the wheel reported $n = 250$ ticks.

**Find:** Linear distance $\Delta s$ travelled by the wheel and the angular rotation $\Delta \phi$ of the wheel.

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry
The wheel rolls without slip. In one full revolution, the wheel traces a circumference $C = \pi D$ on the ground.

### Step 2 — Find the angular resolution per tick
One full revolution = $2\pi$ radians = $N$ ticks. Therefore the angle rotated per single tick is:

$$ \delta_\phi \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{N} \;\;\text{[rad/tick]} $$

### Step 3 — Find the linear distance per single tick
Because the arc length traced equals the chord length for pure rolling:

$$ \delta_s \;=\; \frac{\pi D}{N} \;\;\text{[m/tick]} $$

### Step 4 — Multiply by the number of observed ticks
The wheel rotated $n$ ticks, therefore the total distance is:

$$ \Delta s \;=\; n \cdot \delta_s \;=\; n \cdot \frac{\pi D}{N} $$

### Step 5 — Substitute numerical values

$$ \Delta s \;=\; 250 \times \frac{\pi \times 0.20}{2000} $$

$$ \Delta s \;=\; 250 \times \frac{0.6283}{2000} $$

$$ \Delta s \;=\; 250 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} $$

$$ \boxed{\Delta s \;\approx\; 7.854 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \;=\; 78.54 \text{ mm}} $$

### Step 6 — Angular rotation

$$ \Delta \phi \;=\; n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N} \;=\; 250 \times \frac{2\pi}{2000} $$

$$ \boxed{\Delta \phi \;=\; 0.7854 \text{ rad} \;\approx\; 45.0^{\circ}} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Differential-Drive Forward Kinematics (in Robot Frame)

**Setup:** Two wheels — left (L) and right (R) — separated by axle length $L$. Both wheels have radius $R_w$. Let $\Delta \phi_L$ and $\Delta \phi_R$ be the rotations of left and right wheels in one interval.

### Step 1 — Linear distance rolled by each wheel

$$ \Delta s_L = R_w \, \Delta \phi_L, \qquad \Delta s_R = R_w \, \Delta \phi_R $$

### Step 2 — Linear translation of the robot's centre

The robot centre moves by the *average* of the two wheel motions:

$$ \Delta x \;=\; \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} \;=\; R_w \, \frac{\Delta \phi_R + \Delta \phi_L}{2} $$

### Step 3 — Rotational change of heading

The robot rotates around an instantaneous centre of curvature (ICC). The right wheel travels more if the robot turns left. The arc length difference $\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L$ is the arc traced at radius $L$, so:

$$ \Delta \theta \;=\; \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L} \;=\; \frac{R_w}{L}\,(\Delta \phi_R - \Delta \phi_L) $$

> [!NOTE]
> These two equations are the **most-tested kinematic pair** in PBRUT504. Memorise the exact form, including which sign corresponds to which wheel.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Uncertainty Propagation for Sonar Range

**Given:** A sonar measures round-trip time $t$ with standard deviation $\sigma_t = 0.5$ ms. Speed of sound $c = 343$ m/s with $\sigma_c = 1$ m/s.

**Find:** Uncertainty $\sigma_d$ in the computed range.

### Step 1 — State the measurement equation

$$ d \;=\; \frac{c \cdot t}{2} $$

### Step 2 — Compute partial derivatives (first-order Taylor expansion)

$$ \frac{\partial d}{\partial c} \;=\; \frac{t}{2} $$

$$ \frac{\partial d}{\partial t} \;=\; \frac{c}{2} $$

### Step 3 — Apply the linearised error-propagation law

For independent variables, the total variance is:

$$ \sigma_d^2 \;=\; \left(\frac{\partial d}{\partial c}\right)^{\!2} \sigma_c^{\,2} \;+\; \left(\frac{\partial d}{\partial t}\right)^{\!2} \sigma_t^{\,2} $$

### Step 4 — Insert numerical values (assume $t = 6$ ms, so $d \approx 1.029$ m)

$$ \left(\frac{\partial d}{\partial c}\right)^{\!2} = \left(\frac{0.006}{2}\right)^{2} = (3 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 9 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ \left(\frac{\partial d}{\partial t}\right)^{\!2} = \left(\frac{343}{2}\right)^{2} = (171.5)^{2} = 2.941 \times 10^{4} $$

$$ \sigma_d^{2} \;=\; (9 \times 10^{-6})(1)^{2} \;+\; (2.941 \times 10^{4})(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2} $$

$$ \sigma_d^{2} \;=\; 9 \times 10^{-6} \;+\; (2.941 \times 10^{4})(2.5 \times 10^{-7}) $$

$$ \sigma_d^{2} \;=\; 9 \times 10^{-6} \;+\; 7.35 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ \sigma_d^{2} \;\approx\; 7.36 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma_d \;\approx\; 8.58 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \;\approx\; 8.6 \text{ cm}} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The timing noise dominates the speed-of-sound noise by 3 orders of magnitude. This is why sonar manufacturers focus on **timing resolution**, not on knowing $c$ more precisely.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Laser Triangulation Range

**Given:** A triangulation sensor has baseline $B = 80$ mm, focal length $f = 16$ mm, and the reflected spot falls on the detector at $x = 2.4$ mm from the optical axis.

**Find:** Range $d$ of the target.

### Step 1 — State the triangulation equation

By similar triangles in the imaging geometry:

$$ d \;=\; \frac{B \cdot f}{x} $$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$ d \;=\; \frac{(80 \times 10^{-3})(16 \times 10^{-3})}{2.4 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ d \;=\; \frac{1.28 \times 10^{-3}}{2.4 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ \boxed{d \;\approx\; 0.533 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 53.3 \text{ cm}} $$

### Step 3 — Compute the sensitivity (useful for design questions)

$$ \frac{\partial d}{\partial x} \;=\; -\frac{B \cdot f}{x^{2}} $$

A small $\delta x$ on the detector produces a large $\delta d$ at long range — this is why triangulation is **most accurate at short range** and degrades quadratically with distance.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Beam Footprint (Why a 30° Sonar Cannot Resolve Two Close Objects)

**Given:** Sonar with half-beam-angle $\alpha/2 = 15°$ at range $d = 2$ m.

**Find:** Width $W$ of the sonar footprint on the obstacle.

### Step 1 — Geometry

The beam spreads as a cone; at range $d$ the radius of the footprint is $d \tan(\alpha/2)$.

### Step 2 — Full width

$$ W \;=\; 2 \, d \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) $$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$ W \;=\; 2 \times 2 \times \tan(15^{\circ}) $$

$$ W \;=\; 4 \times 0.2679 $$

$$ \boxed{W \;\approx\; 1.07 \text{ m}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Implication:** A 30°-cone sonar at 2 m illuminates a **circle roughly 1 m wide**. Two chair legs spaced 0.5 m apart look like *one* obstacle — this is a classic KTU question on sonar limitations.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison (Exam Ready)

| Property | Wheel Encoder | Sonar (ToF) | Laser (LIDAR) | Camera (Vision) | IMU (Gyro/Acc) | GPS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Type | Proprioceptive | Extero (Active) | Extero (Active) | Extero (Passive) | Proprioceptive | Extero (Active) |
| Range | — | 0.1 – 10 m | 0.1 – 200 m | 0.5 – ∞ m | — | Global |
| Resolution | $\le$ 0.1 mm | $\sim$ cm | mm–cm | pixel-limited | $\le$ 0.01 °/s | $\sim$ 1 – 5 m |
| Drift? | No (short-term) | No | No | No | **Yes** (integration drift) | No (long-term bias) |
| Works in dark? | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** (needs light) | Yes | Yes |
| Fails in fog? | Yes | **Yes** (scatters) | Partially | Partially | Yes | Yes |
| Update rate | kHz | 20–50 Hz | 10–100 Hz | 30–60 Hz | 100–1000 Hz | 1–10 Hz |
| Cost | Very low | Low | High | Medium | Medium | Medium |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Tip:** A common mistake is to claim *encoders drift*. They do **not** drift in the short term (they are incremental counters). The **drift** comes from *integrating* wheel-encoder error over time. Always state "odometric position drifts, not the encoder itself."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Sensor Classification (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Robot Sensors"]
    rootA --> propA["Proprioceptive"]
    rootA --> exterA["Exteroceptive"]
    propA --> encA["Wheel Encoders"]
    propA --> imuA["IMU Gyroscope"]
    propA --> imuB["IMU Accelerometer"]
    propA --> batA["Battery / Current Sensors"]
    exterA --> tactA["Tactile Bumpers"]
    exterA --> rangA["Ranging Sensors"]
    exterA --> visA["Vision Cameras"]
    exterA --> navA["GPS GNSS Receiver"]
    rangA --> sonA["Ultrasonic Sonar"]
    rangA --> ldrA["LIDAR Laser"]
    rangA --> irA["Infrared IR"]
    rangA --> triA["Triangulation"]
    sonA --> tofA["Time of Flight Principle"]
    ldrA --> tofB["Time of Flight Principle"]
    triA --> triB["Geometric Triangulation"]
```

## 4.2 Signal-Flow Architecture of a Sensor-Fusion Mobile Robot

```mermaid
graph LR
    s1["Wheel Encoder"] --> m1["Microcontroller DSP"]
    s2["IMU Gyro"] --> m1
    s3["IMU Accel"] --> m1
    s4["Sonar Array"] --> m1
    s5["LIDAR"] --> m2["Perception Processor"]
    s6["Camera"] --> m2
    s7["GPS"] --> m1
    m1 --> f1["Extended Kalman Filter EKF"]
    m2 --> f1
    f1 --> o1["Pose Estimate x y theta"]
    f1 --> o2["Occupancy Grid Map"]
    f1 --> o3["Object Detection List"]
    o1 --> plan["Path Planner"]
    o2 --> plan
    o3 --> plan
    plan --> ctrl["Motion Controller"]
    ctrl --> act["Motor Drivers"]
    act --> whl["Wheels"]
    whl -. feedback .-> s1
```

## 4.3 Time-of-Flight Sonar — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    aA["Trigger Pulse"] --> bA["Piezo Transmitter emits 40 kHz burst"]
    bA --> cA["Wave travels to obstacle"]
    cA --> dA["Wave reflects from obstacle"]
    dA --> eA["Piezo Receiver detects echo"]
    eA --> fA["Comparator / Threshold Detector"]
    fA --> gA["Timer captures round trip t"]
    gA --> hA["Compute d equals c t over 2"]
    hA --> iA["Report range to host processor"]
```

## 4.4 Laser Triangulation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    pA["Laser Diode projects spot"] --> pB["Spot falls on target object"]
    pB --> pC["Lens collects scattered light"]
    pC --> pD["Spot imaged on PSD or linear array"]
    pD --> pE["Read lateral position x of peak"]
    pE --> pF["Compute d equals B f over x"]
    pF --> pG["Output range in millimetres"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for KTU:** When asked *"draw a block diagram of …"*, even a hand-drawn **functional block diagram** of the above form earns full marks. The marker looks for: (i) labelled blocks, (ii) arrows showing data/signal flow, (iii) the underlying equation written beside the relevant block.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (memorise the phrasing):**

> **Proprioceptive sensors** measure the *internal state* of the robot, such as wheel rotation, joint angle, battery voltage, or heading rate. They do not depend on the environment. Examples: **(i) optical wheel encoder**, **(ii) gyroscope**.
>
> **Exteroceptive sensors** measure the *external environment*, i.e., distances, images, or signals arriving from outside the robot. Examples: **(i) ultrasonic sonar**, **(ii) CCD/CMOS camera**.
>
> The key distinction is the *reference frame* — proprioception observes the robot's own body, exteroception observes the world.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]**
*What is the difference between accuracy and precision in the context of robot sensors? Illustrate with an example.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Accuracy** is the closeness of a measured value to the *true* value — it is limited by **systematic error** (bias).
> **Precision** is the closeness of *repeated* measurements to one another — it is limited by **random error** (variance / noise).
>
> **Example:** A laser rangefinder reporting 5.01, 5.02, 5.00, 5.01 m when the true distance is 5.00 m is **both accurate and precise**. If it reports 5.20, 5.21, 5.19, 5.20 m, it is **precise** (tight cluster) but **inaccurate** (offset bias of 0.20 m). A sensor giving 4.95, 5.07, 4.92, 5.11 m is **inaccurate and imprecise**.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B carries **14 marks**, normally split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student is offered a Module-internal choice. Below are **two full alternative questions**, either of which could appear.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **operating principle of an ultrasonic sonar sensor** for distance measurement. Derive the range equation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel radius $R = 0.08$ m and axle length $L = 0.30$ m. Optical encoders report $\Delta \phi_L = 0.50$ rad and $\Delta \phi_R = 0.70$ rad in one interval.
*(i)* Compute the linear translation $\Delta x$ and rotation $\Delta \theta$ of the robot. *(ii)* The left encoder has $N_L = 1024$ ticks/rev and the right encoder $N_R = 1024$ ticks/rev; find the equivalent *number of ticks* that produced these rotations. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to A(a)**

**Block diagram (textual, as in Section 4.3):** Trigger → piezo transmitter → wave propagation in air → reflection from obstacle → piezo receiver → amplifier → threshold detector → timer → range = $c t/2$ → output.

**Operating principle:**
An ultrasonic sensor uses a **piezo-electric transducer** that converts an electrical pulse into a **mechanical pressure wave** of frequency typically **40 kHz** (above human hearing). The wave propagates through air at the **speed of sound** $c \approx 343$ m/s (at 20 °C), strikes an obstacle, and the **echo** returns to the same transducer (operating in receive mode). A counter/timer measures the **round-trip time** $t$ between transmit and receive.

**Derivation of range equation:**
The wave travels a total path $2d$ in time $t$ at speed $c$:

$$ 2d = c \, t \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; d = \frac{c \, t}{2} $$

**Key limitations** (write at least two for full marks):

1. **Beam divergence** — the sonar has a typical cone of $20°$–$30°$, so it cannot resolve objects closer than the beam width at that range.
2. **Crosstalk** — multiple sonars firing simultaneously interfere; typically fired sequentially.
3. **Specular reflection** — at grazing angles the wave bounces *away* from the sensor, producing a missed detection.
4. **Speed-of-sound variation** — $c$ depends on temperature: $c = 331.4 + 0.6 T$ (with $T$ in °C, $c$ in m/s).

---

#### **Solution to A(b)**

**(i) Linear translation and rotation:**

Using the differential-drive equations derived in Section 3.2:

$$ \Delta x \;=\; R \, \frac{\Delta \phi_R + \Delta \phi_L}{2} $$

$$ \Delta x \;=\; 0.08 \times \frac{0.70 + 0.50}{2} \;=\; 0.08 \times 0.60 \;=\; \boxed{0.048 \text{ m} \;=\; 4.8 \text{ cm}} $$

$$ \Delta \theta \;=\; \frac{R}{L} (\Delta \phi_R - \Delta \phi_L) $$

$$ \Delta \theta \;=\; \frac{0.08}{0.30} \times (0.70 - 0.50) \;=\; 0.2667 \times 0.20 \;=\; \boxed{0.0533 \text{ rad} \;\approx\; 3.06^{\circ}} $$

**(ii) Equivalent number of encoder ticks:**

The number of ticks corresponding to a rotation of $\Delta \phi$ radians on an encoder with $N$ counts/revolution is:

$$ n \;=\; \frac{\Delta \phi}{2\pi} \times N $$

*Left wheel:*

$$ n_L \;=\; \frac{0.50}{2\pi} \times 1024 \;=\; 0.0796 \times 1024 \;\approx\; \boxed{81.5 \text{ ticks}} $$

*Right wheel:*

$$ n_R \;=\; \frac{0.70}{2\pi} \times 1024 \;=\; 0.1114 \times 1024 \;\approx\; \boxed{114.1 \text{ ticks}} $$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Alternative]*

**(a)** Explain the principle of **laser triangulation** for distance measurement. Derive the range equation and state two advantages over ultrasonic sonar. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mobile robot estimates its position using a **GPS receiver** that observes four satellites at ranges $d_1, d_2, d_3, d_4$ from the known satellite positions $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ for $i=1..4$. Set up the **non-linear equations** to be solved for the unknown robot position $(x, y, z, t_b)$ where $t_b$ is the receiver clock bias. State **why four satellites are needed** for a 3-D fix. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to B(a)**

**Principle:** A laser triangulation sensor projects a **collimated laser spot** onto the target. A lens images the spot onto a position-sensitive detector (PSD) or a linear CCD. As the target moves in range, the **image of the spot** shifts laterally across the detector. The lateral shift is inversely proportional to the range — exactly the same principle as your two eyes judging distance.

**Geometry:** A laser diode at one end of a baseline $B$ emits a beam that strikes the target at range $d$. A lens of focal length $f$ at the other end of the baseline forms an image of the illuminated spot on the detector at lateral position $x$.

By similar triangles in the imaging geometry:

$$ \frac{d}{B} \;=\; \frac{f}{x} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{d \;=\; \frac{B \, f}{x}} $$

**Two advantages over ultrasonic sonar:**

1. **Much narrower beam** — laser beams diverge by milliradians rather than $20°$–$30°$, giving **far better angular resolution** and the ability to resolve small features.
2. **Speed of light vs. sound** — laser ToF can measure with **mm accuracy** at hundreds of metres, whereas sonar is cm-accurate at a few metres because of the slow speed of sound.
3. *(Bonus)* Immune to acoustic noise and ambient sound.

---

#### **Solution to B(b)**

**Range equation with clock bias:**
If the receiver clock is offset by $t_b$ (in seconds) from GPS time, the *measured* pseudo-range $\rho_i$ differs from the *true* range $d_i$ by $c \cdot t_b$:

$$ \rho_i \;=\; d_i + c \, t_b $$

$$ \rho_i \;=\; \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2} \;+\; c \, t_b $$

So the full set of four equations for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$ is:

$$ \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2} \;=\; \rho_i - c \, t_b $$

**Unknowns:** $x, y, z$ (3-D position) and $t_b$ (clock bias) → **4 unknowns**.

**Why 4 satellites:** A receiver needs one equation per unknown. To solve for 3 spatial coordinates and 1 clock bias simultaneously, **at least 4 independent pseudo-range equations** are required — hence the 4-satellite minimum for a 3-D GPS fix. With 3 satellites you can solve $x, y, z$ only if $t_b$ is already known (e.g., from an atomic clock); with 4 satellites the system is solvable as a non-linear over-determined set, typically linearised and iterated with **Newton–Raphson** or solved by **least squares**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign convention in differential-drive:** Many students write $\Delta \theta = \dfrac{R}{L}(\Delta \phi_L - \Delta \phi_R)$ — **wrong sign**, this inverts the turning direction. Use $R - L$ consistently: a positive $\Delta \theta$ means counter-clockwise (left turn) when $\phi_R > \phi_L$.
> 2. **Sonar factor of 2:** Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{2}$ (round trip) in $d = ct/2$ is the single most common arithmetic error. **Always** state "round trip" in the derivation.
> 3. **Encoder unit confusion:** Mixing degrees with radians, or ticks/rev with rad/tick. Convert **once** and write units next to every numerical answer.
> 4. **Active vs Passive confusion:** Sonar, LIDAR, RADAR are **active**. Cameras (without a flash / structured light) and compasses are **passive**.
> 5. **Forgetting the clock-bias term in GPS:** Without $t_b$, the GPS system is unsolvable — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in a Part B question.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> Tick these off the night before the exam. If you can write each line from memory, you are exam-ready.

- [ ] **Proprioceptive** = internal state (encoders, IMU). **Exteroceptive** = external world (sonar, camera, GPS).
- [ ] **Active** sensor = injects energy. **Passive** sensor = only receives ambient energy.
- [ ] **Accuracy** = closeness to truth. **Precision** = closeness of repeated measurements.
- [ ] Encoder linear resolution: $\delta_d = \dfrac{\pi D}{N}$ (in m/tick).
- [ ] Differential-drive kinematics: $\Delta x = R \dfrac{\Delta \phi_R + \Delta \phi_L}{2}$,  $\Delta \theta = \dfrac{R}{L}(\Delta \phi_R - \Delta \phi_L)$.
- [ ] Time-of-Flight range: $d = \dfrac{c \, t}{2}$ — note the **factor of 2**.
- [ ] Sonar beam footprint: $W = 2 d \tan(\alpha/2)$ — wide cone ⇒ poor lateral resolution.
- [ ] Laser triangulation: $d = \dfrac{B \, f}{x}$ — short-range accurate, long-range degrades.
- [ ] Phase-shift range: $d = \dfrac{c \, \phi}{4 \pi f}$.
- [ ] Uncertainty propagation (sum): $\sigma_{tot} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$.
- [ ] GPS needs **4 satellites** for 3-D fix because of the unknown clock bias $t_b$.
- [ ] IMU **drifts** (integration of bias); encoders do not drift in the short term — odometric *position* drifts.
- [ ] Camera needs light; LIDAR does not; sonar fails in vacuum and in soft, absorbing surfaces (curtains, foam).
- [ ] **Sensor fusion** (e.g., EKF) combines complementary sensors — IMU high-rate but drifts, GPS low-rate but absolute.
- [ ] Always quote **units** and the **physical principle** behind every formula in a derivation.

---

*End of Module 2 — Topic: Sensing (PBRUT504, KTU 2024 Scheme)*

topics.title: Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping** in mobile robotics is the process by which a mobile robot creates a spatial representation of its environment using data acquired from its onboard sensors (e.g., LiDAR, sonar, cameras, wheel odometry). The output is a **map** — a model of the environment's geometry, topology, or semantics — that the robot can subsequently use for self-localization, path planning, and autonomous decision-making.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, mapping is treated as a sub-problem of the broader **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** framework, where the mapping problem is studied *assuming* the robot's pose (position and orientation) is known.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Blindfolded Explorer in a Dark Room**
> Imagine being blindfolded and placed inside an unfamiliar room. You have a walking stick (your sensor) and a notepad (your map). Every time you tap the stick, you learn whether there's a wall, a table leg, or empty space at that point. As you shuffle around, you keep sketching the room. The notepad is your *map*; your footsteps (when you trust them) are *odometry*; the stick taps are *sensor measurements*. Mapping is precisely this: converting a stream of noisy, partial measurements into a coherent spatial picture.
>
> A more modern analogy: think of **Google Street View cars**. They drive around capturing 360° images and LiDAR sweeps. Back at the data center, algorithms stitch the scans into a unified global map. A mobile robot does the same thing — in miniature, in real-time, and entirely onboard.

### 1.3 Why Mapping Matters

- **Localization prerequisite**: A robot cannot accurately estimate "where it is" if it does not know "what the world looks like."
- **Path planning**: Maps enable algorithms like A*, RRT, and Dijkstra to find collision-free trajectories.
- **Autonomy**: Without a usable map, the robot is restricted to reactive, short-horizon behaviors.
- **Inspection/monitoring**: Maps enable repeated coverage of an area, change detection, and semantic annotation.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **Map Resolution** $r$ (m/cell) — typical values: **0.05 m** for indoor, **0.25–1.0 m** for outdoor
- **Map Coverage** $C$ (%) — fraction of environment represented
- **Mapping Error** $e_{\text{map}}$ (m) — RMSE between map cells and ground truth
- **Sensor Range** $z_{\max}$ (m) — e.g., **0.5–30 m** for LiDAR
- **Posterior Probability** threshold for occupancy — typically **0.5** (or log-odds 0)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Classification of Maps

Mobile robot maps fall into three primary categories:

| Map Type | Description | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metric (Grid)** | Discretizes space into cells (occupancy grid) or stores raw points (point cloud) | Accurate, dense | High memory; needs pose accuracy |
| **Topological** | Graph of nodes (places) and edges (paths) | Compact, scalable | Less precise geometry |
| **Hybrid (Semantic)** | Combines metric grid with topological graph or semantic labels | Best of both worlds | Complex to maintain |

### 2.2 Occupancy Grid Map — The Workhorse

The most commonly tested representation in KTU is the **2D Occupancy Grid Map (OGM)**.

**Core Idea:** Partition the 2D plane into uniform cells of size $r \times r$. Each cell $m_{ij}$ stores the probability that it is *occupied* by an obstacle.

**Mathematical Foundation — Bayes Filter for Static State:**

For a static map, the cell state does not change over time. So the standard time-update of the Bayes filter vanishes, leaving only the *measurement update*:

$$m_{ij} = \arg\max_{m} \; p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

where $m$ is the cell state (occupied/free), $z_{1:t}$ are sensor measurements up to time $t$, and $x_{1:t}$ are the robot poses (assumed known).

### 2.3 Log-Odds Representation

Storing raw probabilities $p(m)$ between 0 and 1 is numerically fragile. The KTU-standard trick is the **log-odds** transform:

$$l(m) = \log \frac{p(m)}{1 - p(m)} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad p(m) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{\,l(m)}}$$

Benefits:
- **Additive** updates (you just add log-odds increments).
- Unbounded range $(-\infty, +\infty)$ — no saturation artifacts.
- Threshold for "occupied" is simply $l(m) > 0$.

### 2.4 The Mapping Algorithm (Pseudo-formal)

Initialize: every cell $l_0(m) = 0$ (i.e., probability = 0.5, "unknown").

For each new measurement $z_t$:

$$l_t(m) = l_{t-1}(m) + \text{inv\_sensor\_model}(m, x_t, z_t) - l_0$$

Clamp $l_t(m)$ to $[l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$ to prevent over-confidence (e.g., $l_{\min} = -2$, $l_{\max} = 3.5$).

### 2.5 Inverse Sensor Model

The **inverse sensor model** is the function that, given a robot pose and a single measurement (e.g., a range scan reading $z_t = d$), tells us how to update the cells along that beam.

For a ray-cast measurement $z_t^k$ hitting distance $d$:

- Cells *before* $d$ along the beam: increase occupancy (occupied by obstacle at $d$).
- Cell *at* distance $d$: **strongly occupied** (the obstacle surface).
- Cells *beyond* $d$: free (sensor passed through them).

A canonical piecewise model (Moravec & Elfes, 1985):

$$\text{inv\_sensor}(m, x_t, z_t) = \begin{cases} l_{\text{occ}} & \text{if } m \text{ is on beam, distance } < d - \alpha/2 \\ l_{\text{free}} & \text{if } m \text{ is on beam, } d - \alpha/2 \le \text{dist} \le d + \alpha/2 \\ l_0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\alpha$ is the sensor beam-width (rad).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ | Posterior occupancy of cell $m$ | Probability $\in [0,1]$ |
| 2 | $l(m) = \log \dfrac{p(m)}{1-p(m)}$ | Log-odds transform | $\mathbb{R}$, no units |
| 3 | $p(m) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1+e^{l(m)}}$ | Inverse log-odds | Probability |
| 4 | $l_t(m) = l_{t-1}(m) + \Delta l - l_0$ | Recursive log-odds update | $l_0 = 0$ for uniform prior |
| 5 | $l_{\text{occ}} = \log \dfrac{p_{\text{occ}}}{1-p_{\text{occ}}}$ | Log-odds of occupied event | Typical $p_{\text{occ}} = 0.9$ |
| 6 | $l_{\text{free}} = \log \dfrac{p_{\text{free}}}{1-p_{\text{free}}}$ | Log-odds of free event | Typical $p_{\text{free}} = 0.3$ |
| 7 | $C = \dfrac{N_{\text{known}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Map coverage percent | Dimensionless % |
| 8 | $e_{\text{RMSE}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(d_i - \hat{d}_i)^2}$ | Mapping RMSE error | Meters (m) |
| 9 | $z_{\max}$ | Maximum sensor range | e.g., 30 m for LiDAR |
| 10 | $r$ | Grid resolution | m/cell (e.g., 0.05 m) |
| 11 | $N_{\text{cells}} = \dfrac{A}{r^2}$ | Number of cells in area $A$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $\Delta \theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{N_{\text{rays}}}$ | Angular resolution of scan | Radians |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva)**: use 2D occupancy grids built from 2D LiDAR.
- **Autonomous cars (Waymo, Cruise)**: use multi-layer 3D occupancy grids + semantic HD maps.
- **Vacuum cleaners (Roomba)**: use coarse occupancy grids + topological node graph.
- **Underwater AUVs**: use probabilistic occupancy grids with sonar models that handle specular reflection.
- **Disaster response (DARPA SubT)**: use voxel-based 3D maps with deformable / non-rigid extensions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bayes Filter for Mapping (Static State)

**Step 1 — Start with the full SLAM posterior.** We want to estimate the map $m$ given all measurements and poses:

$$p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

**Step 2 — Factor into per-cell problem.** Because each grid cell is conditionally independent given the trajectory, the joint probability factorizes:

$$p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \prod_{i,j} p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

This is the **map decomposition assumption** — the cornerstone of occupancy grid mapping.

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Rule to a single cell:**

$$p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_{ij}, x_t) \; p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize static state:** Since $m_{ij}$ does not change, the prediction step is trivial — the prior at time $t$ equals the posterior at time $t-1$:

$$p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) \quad \text{(already the filtered belief)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply recursively, with normalization $\eta$:**

$$p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \eta \; p(z_t \mid m_{ij}, x_t) \; p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$$

**Step 6 — Move to log-odds to make updates additive.** Take log of both sides:

$$\log \frac{p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \log \frac{p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})} + \log \frac{p(z_t \mid m_{ij}, x_t)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_{ij}, x_t)}$$

**Step 7 — Define the inverse sensor model in log-odds:**

$$l_t(m_{ij}) = l_{t-1}(m_{ij}) + \text{inv\_sensor\_logodds}(m_{ij}, x_t, z_t) - l_0$$

The $-l_0$ correction is included when we treat the prior as $l_0 = 0$ to prevent double-counting.

**Step 8 — Bound the log-odds** to $[l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$ (e.g., $[-2.0, 3.5]$) for numerical robustness.

This is the **complete derivation** of the standard KTU-tested occupancy grid update.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example

A robot uses a 2D laser scanner with $z_{\max} = 5$ m, beam width $\alpha = 0.5°$. Inverse sensor model log-odds increments: $l_{\text{occ}} = 0.85$, $l_{\text{free}} = -0.4$, $l_0 = 0$. Initial log-odds for all cells = $0$.

**Scenario:** Three consecutive measurements hit a particular cell at distances $d_1 = 3.2$ m, $d_2 = 3.1$ m, $d_3 = 3.0$ m, and the cell lies on the beam *at* the hit distance each time (so it accumulates $l_{\text{occ}}$ each step).

**Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_0(m) &= 0 \\
l_1(m) &= l_0(m) + l_{\text{occ}} - l_0 = 0 + 0.85 - 0 = 0.85 \\
l_2(m) &= l_1(m) + l_{\text{occ}} - l_0 = 0.85 + 0.85 = 1.70 \\
l_3(m) &= l_2(m) + l_{\text{occ}} - l_0 = 1.70 + 0.85 = 2.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Convert back to probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_3(m) &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l_3(m)}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{2.55}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 12.807} \\
&= 1 - 0.0725 \\
&= 0.9275
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** After 3 consistent "occupied" hits, the cell is judged occupied with **~92.75% confidence** — well above the 0.5 threshold.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Occupancy Grid Update Summary

| Step | Operation | Input | Output | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ray-cast from $x_t$ in direction $\theta_k$ | Pose, scan angle | List of cells on beam | $O(d/r)$ per ray |
| 2 | Apply inv. sensor model | Cells, range $z_t^k$ | Log-odds increment | $O(1)$ per cell |
| 3 | Add to existing log-odds | $l_{t-1}(m)$, $\Delta l$ | $l_t(m)$ | $O(1)$ per cell |
| 4 | Clamp $l_t(m)$ | $l_t(m)$ | $l_t^{\text{clamped}}(m)$ | $O(1)$ per cell |
| 5 | Periodic probability extraction (for display) | $l_t(m)$ | $p(m)$ | $O(N)$ |

Total cost per scan: $O(N_{\text{rays}} \cdot z_{\max}/r) = O(N_{\text{cells}}\text{ touched})$.

### 3.4 Memory Footprint Derivation

For a square area of side $L$ with grid resolution $r$:

$$N_{\text{cells}} = \left(\frac{L}{r}\right)^2$$

For a 4-byte floating-point log-odds value per cell:

$$\text{Memory} = 4 \left(\frac{L}{r}\right)^2 \text{ bytes}$$

**Example:** $L = 50$ m, $r = 0.05$ m:

$$N_{\text{cells}} = \left(\frac{50}{0.05}\right)^2 = (1000)^2 = 10^6 \text{ cells}$$

$$\text{Memory} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}$$

This is comfortably handled by modern embedded computers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: End-to-End Mapping Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Pose x_t] --> C[Ray Casting Engine]
    B[Sensor Reading z_t] --> C
    C --> D[Identify Cells on Beam]
    D --> E{Determine Cell-Band}
    E -- Cell is at hit distance d --> F[Apply inv_occ log-odds]
    E -- Cell is between sensor and d --> G[Apply inv_free log-odds]
    E -- Cell is beyond d --> H[Apply l_0 = 0]
    F --> I[Add to Log-Odds Grid l_t-1]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Clamp to l_min, l_max]
    J --> K[Updated Log-Odds Grid l_t]
    K --> L{Display / Path Planning}
    L --> M[Convert Log-Odds to Probability]
    M --> N[Visual Occupancy Map]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Occupancy Grid Map Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENS["SENSOR INPUT LAYER"]
        S1[2D LiDAR Scan z_t]
        S2[Wheel Odometry x_t]
        S3[IMU Data]
    end
    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        P1[Pre-processing and Time Sync]
        P2[Inverse Sensor Model]
        P3[Bayesian Log-Odds Update]
        P4[Saturation Clamping]
    end
    subgraph MEM["MEMORY LAYER"]
        M1[Log-Odds Grid l_t]
        M2[Pose History x_1t]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1[Probability Map p_t]
        O2[Cost Map for Planner]
        O3[Topological Graph]
    end
    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P1
    S3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    M1 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> M1
    M1 --> O1
    M1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Cell Classification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Cell Update Arrives] --> B{Is cell on scan beam?}
    B -- No --> C[No Update: l_t = l_t-1]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is cell distance d on beam?}
    D -- Greater than hit range --> E[Mark as Free: l_t = l_t-1 + l_free]
    D -- Less than hit range --> F[Mark as Unknown / Skip]
    D -- Equal to hit range --> G[Mark as Occupied: l_t = l_t-1 + l_occ]
    E --> H[Clamp l_t to l_min, l_max]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Store in Map Grid]
```

### 4.4 Concept Hierarchy: Map Representations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Robot Maps] --> B[Metric Maps]
    A --> C[Topological Maps]
    A --> D[Hybrid Maps]
    B --> B1[Occupancy Grid]
    B --> B2[Point Cloud]
    B --> B3[Feature Map]
    B --> B4[Voxel Grid 3D]
    C --> C1[Place Graph]
    C --> C2[Voronoi Diagram]
    D --> D1[Topological + Grid]
    D --> D2[Semantic Map]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an occupancy grid map. Mention the role of the inverse sensor model in occupancy grid mapping.*

**Model Answer:**
An occupancy grid map is a 2D (or 3D) discretization of the robot's environment into uniform cells, where each cell stores the probability that the cell is occupied by an obstacle. The map is represented as $p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ for each cell $(i,j)$.

The **inverse sensor model** $p(m \mid x_t, z_t)$ converts a single sensor reading into a hypothesis about the occupancy of cells along the measurement beam. It tells the mapper which cells to update and by how much (e.g., free along the beam, occupied at the hit distance). It is the bridge between the raw sensor measurement and the Bayesian update of the map.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **forward sensor model** $p(z_t \mid m, x_t)$ with the **inverse sensor model** $p(m \mid z_t, x_t)$. The KTU examiner deducts 1 mark if the direction of conditioning is reversed. The forward model predicts what the sensor *would* see; the inverse model interprets what the world *must* look like given what the sensor *did* see.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why is the log-odds representation preferred over raw probability values for occupancy grid mapping? List any two advantages.*

**Model Answer:**
In log-odds form, the multiplicative Bayesian update becomes an **additive** update:

$$l_t(m) = l_{t-1}(m) + \Delta l - l_0$$

This gives two key advantages:

1. **Computational efficiency** — Addition is faster than multiplication and normalization; it avoids repeated calls to expensive exponentials during the inner loop.
2. **Numerical robustness** — Probabilities are bounded in $[0, 1]$ and require special care near the boundaries. Log-odds live in $(-\infty, +\infty)$ and are bounded only by clamping, which is a simple saturation step.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B Questions carry **14 marks** each, usually split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both sub-parts should be answered within a single choice (i.e., choose **either** Q(A) or Q(B)). Internal choice between (a) and (b) is *not* typical — instead, the choice is between Question A and Question B as a whole.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the **occupancy grid mapping algorithm** using the Bayes filter approach. Derive the log-odds update equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Map decomposition.* We assume the world is a 2D grid of cells, and that cell states are conditionally independent given the robot's trajectory. Hence:

$$p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \prod_{i,j} p(m_{ij} \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

*Step 2 — Apply Bayes' rule* to a single cell $m$:

$$p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \eta \; p(z_t \mid m, x_t) \; p(m \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$$

The denominator $p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})$ becomes the normalization constant $\eta$.

*Step 3 — Static state assumption.* Because the map is static, the prediction step is the identity. The prior at time $t$ is the posterior at time $t-1$.

*Step 4 — Log-odds transform.* Take $\log\!\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right)$ of both sides. The normalization $\eta$ vanishes (it is symmetric in $m$ and $\neg m$). The result is the additive update:

$$\boxed{\;l_t(m) = l_{t-1}(m) + l(m, x_t, z_t) - l_0\;}$$

where $l(m, x_t, z_t)$ is the inverse sensor model in log-odds form, and $l_0$ is the prior log-odds (typically 0).

*Step 5 — Practical aspects.* The update is performed for every cell along every beam of every scan. After each update, the cell value is **clamped** to $[l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$ (e.g., $[-2.0, 3.5]$) to avoid runaway confidence.

*Diagrams:* Refer to the block diagrams in Section 4 (Block Diagram 4.1, 4.2, 4.3).

---

**(b)** A robot scans a cell **5 times**. The first 3 measurements indicate the cell is free ($l_{\text{free}} = -0.4$) and the next 2 indicate occupied ($l_{\text{occ}} = 0.9$). Prior $l_0 = 0$. **(i)** Compute the final log-odds. **(ii)** Convert to probability. **(iii)** State whether the cell is classified as occupied or free. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Step-by-step log-odds update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_0(m) &= 0 \\
l_1(m) &= 0 + (-0.4) - 0 = -0.4 \\
l_2(m) &= -0.4 + (-0.4) = -0.8 \\
l_3(m) &= -0.8 + (-0.4) = -1.2 \\
l_4(m) &= -1.2 + (0.9) = -0.3 \\
l_5(m) &= -0.3 + (0.9) = +0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Convert to probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_5(m) &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{\,0.6}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 1.8221} \\
&= 1 - 0.3543 \\
&= 0.6457
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Classification:** Since $p_5(m) = 0.6457 > 0.5$ (equivalently, $l_5(m) = +0.6 > 0$), the cell is classified as **OCCUPIED**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students frequently drop the $-l_0$ correction. Since the prior is $l_0 = 0$ in this problem, the omission does not affect the numerical answer, **but the KTU examiner will check whether you wrote the formula correctly** in part (a). Always include $-l_0$ in the boxed equation.
>
> **Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Confusing $l_{\text{free}} = -0.4$ with $l_{\text{free}} = +0.4$. Remember: free = negative log-odds (less than prior 0), occupied = positive log-odds (greater than 0). Sign errors cost 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **metric, topological, and hybrid maps** in mobile robotics. For each, give one application scenario. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Metric (Grid) Map | Topological Map | Hybrid Map |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | Cells storing occupancy probability | Graph: nodes = places, edges = traversable paths | Both — e.g., grid within each topological node |
| **Storage** | High: $O\!\left(\tfrac{L^2}{r^2}\right)$ | Low: $O(N_{\text{places}})$ | Medium–High |
| **Precision** | High (centimeter-level) | Low (qualitative) | High (locally), Low (globally) |
| **Computational cost** | Expensive updates | Cheap graph search | Moderate |
| **Robustness to noise** | Sensitive to pose errors | Robust to metric drift | Robust by design |
| **Application** | Indoor warehouse robots (Kiva), 2D LiDAR-based SLAM | Long-corridor navigation, multi-floor buildings | Large outdoor campus delivery robots, autonomous cars |
| **Example algorithm** | Occupancy grid mapping, GMapping | Voronoi roadmap, Random tree exploration | Hybrid SLAM (e.g., Topomap) |

*Applications:*
- **Metric** → Amazon warehouse robots use 2D occupancy grids for sub-centimeter path planning between shelves.
- **Topological** → A multi-floor hospital robot stores a graph where each node is a room and edges are corridors/elevators.
- **Hybrid** → Autonomous cars maintain a *semantic HD map* (metric lanes + topological intersections + traffic-rule annotations).

---

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **inverse sensor model** for a 2D range finder (laser). Explain how cells *at* the measured range, *before* the range, and *beyond* the range are updated. Use a sample numerical example with $l_{\text{occ}} = 0.85$, $l_{\text{free}} = -0.4$, $l_0 = 0$ to show the update for a single cell traversed 4 times as a free cell and then 1 time as the hit cell. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Inverse sensor model (textual block diagram):*

```
                Robot (x_t)
                    |
                    v
        [ Ray-cast in direction θ_k ]
                    |
        ------------------------
        |           |          |
   distance < d  distance = d  distance > d
        |           |          |
   FREE region    OCCUPIED     No update
   (l += l_free)  (l += l_occ)  (l unchanged)
```

*Numerical example:* Initial $l_0(m) = 0$.

- 4 free passes (cell between sensor and hit distance):
  - After pass 1: $l_1 = 0 + (-0.4) - 0 = -0.4$
  - After pass 2: $l_2 = -0.4 + (-0.4) = -0.8$
  - After pass 3: $l_3 = -0.8 + (-0.4) = -1.2$
  - After pass 4: $l_4 = -1.2 + (-0.4) = -1.6$

- 1 occupied pass (cell *at* hit distance):
  - After pass 5: $l_5 = -1.6 + 0.85 = -0.75$

- Probability:
$$p_5(m) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.75}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 0.4724} = 1 - 0.6791 = 0.3209$$

- **Classification:** Since $l_5(m) = -0.75 < 0$ and $p_5(m) = 0.3209 < 0.5$, the cell is classified as **FREE**, *despite* one occupied hit. This illustrates that the inverse sensor model typically weights the *free* update more weakly than the *occupied* update at the hit surface, but the four consistent free observations still dominate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Many students wrongly assume the entire beam is updated as occupied. The correct behavior is: **only the cell at the hit distance is "occupied"; cells between sensor and hit are "free"; cells beyond are unknown** (unupdated). Failure to distinguish these three regions costs up to 3 marks.
>
> **Examiner's Pitfall #4:** Forgetting to **clamp** the log-odds. In real implementations, $l_t$ is bounded to $[l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$, but for exam numerical problems, only compute as long as the value lies within the range. The examiner does *not* require you to clamp in short numerical problems — but it is a 1-mark bonus if you mention the saturation step.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mapping vs. SLAM:** Mapping assumes the pose is known; SLAM estimates both. Mapping is a *sub-problem* of SLAM.
- **Three map families:** metric (grids, point clouds, voxels), topological (graphs), hybrid (semantic + metric).
- **Occupancy grid** is the most frequently tested KTU map representation.
- **Bayes filter for static state:** prediction step is trivial → only measurement update.
- **Log-odds** $l(m) = \log\!\dfrac{p(m)}{1-p(m)}$ converts the multiplicative Bayesian update into an *additive* one.
- **Update equation (memorize this):**
  $$l_t(m) = l_{t-1}(m) + l_{\text{inv}}(m, x_t, z_t) - l_0$$
- **Inverse sensor model** is *not* the same as the forward sensor model — examiners test this distinction every year.
- **Three regions of a range beam:** free (before $d$), occupied (at $d$), unknown (beyond $d$).
- **Occupancy threshold:** $l > 0$ ⇒ occupied; $l < 0$ ⇒ free; $l \approx 0$ ⇒ unknown.
- **Clamping** at $[l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$ prevents numerical divergence.
- **Memory:** For an $L \times L$ area at resolution $r$, memory $\approx 4 \cdot (L/r)^2$ bytes. A 50 m × 50 m map at 5 cm resolution needs **~4 MB**.
- **Practical parameters:** indoor resolution $\sim$ 0.05 m, outdoor $\sim$ 0.25–1.0 m; LiDAR $z_{\max}$ up to 30 m; beam-width $\alpha \sim 0.25°$–$0.5°$ for high-end units.
- **Applications to mention in answers:** warehouse robots, autonomous cars, vacuum cleaners, AUVs, search-and-rescue, agriculture.
- **Common pitfalls:** sign errors in log-odds; confusing forward vs. inverse sensor model; forgetting the $-l_0$ correction; treating unknown cells as free.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simultaneous Localization and Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of an agent's (robot's) location within that map, using onboard sensors, probabilistic estimation frameworks, and recursive Bayesian inference.

Mathematically, SLAM is the problem of estimating the **posterior probability distribution** over the robot's pose trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the map $m$ given the sequence of observations $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$:

$$
p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

This is the canonical formulation that underpins nearly every modern SLAM algorithm, from **EKF-SLAM** to **Graph-SLAM** and **ORB-SLAM3**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blindfolded Explorer"

Imagine you are dropped into a completely dark, unfamiliar building wearing a blindfold. You are given a **measuring tape** (sensor), a **compass** (odometry), and a **notebook** (memory). As you shuffle forward:

- You must **remember where you are** (Localization).
- You must **draw the rooms and corridors you feel** (Mapping).
- But every step introduces **error** in your compass and tape.
- Worse, to draw a wall accurately, you must know **your exact position**, which depends on the walls you've already drawn.

This circular dependency — *"I need the map to localize, and I need to localize to build the map"* — is the **chicken-and-egg problem** of SLAM. Solving it in real time with noisy sensors is what makes SLAM one of the most celebrated achievements in modern robotics.

## 1.3 Why SLAM Matters in Mobile Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Significance** — SLAM is the foundational capability that enables:
> - **Autonomous Vehicles** (self-driving cars) to navigate unmapped urban roads.
> - **Domestic Vacuum Robots** (e.g., iRobot, Mi Robot) to clean a room efficiently.
> - **Warehouse AGVs** (Automated Guided Vehicles) in Amazon/Kuwait fulfillment centers.
> - **Aerial Drones** in GPS-denied environments (indoor inspection, search-and-rescue).
> - **Underwater AUVs** (Autonomous Underwater Vehicles) for deep-sea mapping.
> - **AR/VR Devices** (HoloLens, Meta Quest) to anchor virtual objects in physical space.

## 1.4 The SLAM Pipeline — High-Level Overview

A standard SLAM system contains four functional blocks:

| Block | Function | Example |
|-------|----------|---------|
| **Front-End (Sensor Processing)** | Raw data extraction | Feature detection (ORB, SIFT) from cameras / scan matching from LiDAR |
| **Front-End (Odometry Estimation)** | Short-term motion estimate | Visual Odometry (VO) / Wheel Odometry / LiDAR Odometry (LO) |
| **Back-End (Optimization / Filtering)** | Global consistency | EKF, Particle Filter, Pose Graph Optimization |
| **Loop Closure Detection** | Recognizes revisited places | Bag-of-Words, Scan Context, Deep Descriptors |

## 1.5 Sensor Taxonomy for SLAM

SLAM systems are categorized by the **primary exteroceptive sensor** used:

> [!NOTE]
> - **LiDAR SLAM** — High accuracy, 2D/3D point clouds. Examples: *Cartographer*, *LOAM*, *LIO-SAM*.
> - **Visual SLAM (vSLAM)** — Monocular, Stereo, or RGB-D cameras. Examples: *ORB-SLAM3*, *LSD-SLAM*, *VINS-Mono*.
> - **Visual-Inertial SLAM (VI-SLAM)** — Camera + IMU fusion. Examples: *VINS-Fusion*, *OKVIS*, *ORB-SLAM3-IMU*.
> - **Sonar / Radar SLAM** — Used underwater or in adverse weather. Examples: *RatSLAM*.
> - **Multi-Sensor Fusion SLAM** — Combines LiDAR + Camera + IMU + GPS. Examples: *FAST-LIO2*, *R3LIVE*.

## 1.6 Two Decades of SLAM Evolution

The field has transitioned through three paradigm shifts:

1. **Filtering Era (1986–2005):** EKF-SLAM, Particle Filter SLAM, FastSLAM.
2. **Optimization Era (2005–2015):** Graph-SLAM, Bundle Adjustment, $\checkmark$ g${}^{2}$o framework.
3. **Learning Era (2015–Present):** Deep learning-based feature extraction, end-to-end neural SLAM, NeRF-based mapping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SLAM Problem — Mathematical Decomposition

The full SLAM posterior is **computationally intractable** because the state space grows unboundedly with map size. SLAM algorithms therefore **factorize** this distribution strategically.

### 2.1.1 Online SLAM Posterior (Most Algorithms)

$$
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

This estimates the **current pose** $x_t$ and the **entire map** $m$ at time $t$, marginalizing over past poses $x_{1:t-1}$. Algorithms like EKF-SLAM and FastSLAM solve this.

### 2.1.2 Full SLAM Posterior (Batch Optimization)

$$
p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

Estimates the **entire trajectory** simultaneously. Graph-SLAM and Bundle Adjustment methods use this form, and then marginalize offline.

## 2.2 The Bayes Filter — Heart of Recursive SLAM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every SLAM algorithm is a **recursive Bayesian filter** with two alternating steps: **Prediction** (using motion model) and **Update / Correction** (using observation model).

### 2.2.1 Prediction Step (Time Update)

Uses the **motion model** $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$:

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

Here, $\overline{bel}$ is the predicted belief *before* incorporating the latest observation, and $bel$ is the posterior belief.

### 2.2.2 Update Step (Measurement Update)

Uses the **observation model** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ via Bayes' rule:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

where the **normalizer** $\eta$ is:

$$
\eta = \left[ \int p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t) \, dx_t \right]^{-1}
$$

## 2.3 The Three Foundational Probability Distributions

| Distribution | Symbol | Role in SLAM | Typical Form |
|--------------|--------|--------------|--------------|
| **Motion Model** | $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Predicts new pose from previous pose + control | Gaussian $+$ noise |
| **Observation Model** | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Likelihood of sensor reading given pose + map | Beam model / Camera projection |
| **Landmark Model** | $p(m_j \mid x_t, z_t)$ | Inverse observation (initialization) | Triangulation |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize every row; these equations appear in almost every KTU question paper on SLAM.

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Used In |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Full SLAM posterior | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Graph-SLAM, Batch BA |
| 2 | Online SLAM posterior | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM |
| 3 | Bayes Prediction | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | All Bayesian filters |
| 4 | Bayes Update | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | All Bayesian filters |
| 5 | EKF Linearization | $F_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{\mu_{t-1}}$ | EKF-SLAM |
| 6 | Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | EKF-SLAM |
| 7 | State Augmentation | $\mu_{t} = \begin{bmatrix} \mu_{t-1} \\ m_{j} \end{bmatrix}$, $\Sigma_t \in \mathbb{R}^{(3N+3)\times(3N+3)}$ | EKF-SLAM |
| 8 | Particle Weight | $w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m^{[i]})$ | FastSLAM |
| 9 | Graph-SLAM Information | $\Omega = \sum_t \Omega_t$, $\xi = \sum_t \xi_t$ | Graph-SLAM |
| 10 | Loop Closure Constraint | $\lVert x_i - x_j \rVert < \tau$ within descriptors | All modern SLAM |
| 11 | ICP Objective | $\min_{R,t} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert R p_i + t - q_i \rVert^2$ | LiDAR SLAM |
| 12 | Camera Projection | $z = \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \pi(K [R \vert t] X)$ | Visual SLAM |
| 13 | Triangulation (Depth) | $d = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{x_L - x_R}$ | Stereo vSLAM |
| 14 | Odometry (Differential Drive) | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$, $\Delta s = \dfrac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$ | Wheeled SLAM |
| 15 | ICP Convergence Criterion | $\lVert e_{k+1} - e_k \rVert < \epsilon$ | LiDAR scan matching |

> **Notation Note:** $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ denotes Euclidean norm; in the table above, vertical bars are represented as $\vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The actual LaTeX rendering uses $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\lVert \cdot \rVert$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Algorithm Comparison Table

| Property | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM | Graph-SLAM |
|----------|----------|----------|------------|
| **Estimator Type** | Extended Kalman Filter | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Pose Graph Optimization |
| **State Representation** | Single Gaussian over (pose + map) | Particles for pose + independent Gaussians for landmarks | Nodes (poses) + Edges (constraints) |
| **Computational Complexity** | $O(N^2)$ per step | $O(M \log N)$ per step | $O(N^3)$ batch, $O(N)$ incremental |
| **Map Size Scalability** | Poor (up to ~100s landmarks) | Moderate (1000s) | Excellent (millions) |
| **Data Association** | Maximum Likelihood | Particle-weighted ML | Robust cost functions |
| **Key Limitation** | Quadratic cost in landmarks | Particle depletion | Requires good initial guess |
| **Strength** | Mathematically elegant, optimal for linear-Gaussian | Handles non-linear, multi-modal | Globally consistent, scalable |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> - **Self-Driving Cars (Waymo, Tesla FSD, Cruise):** Use **LiDAR + Camera + Radar fusion SLAM** for centimeter-level localization in dynamic urban environments.
> - **Mars Rovers (Perseverance, Curiosity, Zhurong):** Use **Visual-Inertial SLAM** because Mars has no GPS and rocks create unreliable wheel odometry.
> - **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci XI):** Use **electromagnetic SLAM** to track instrument tip position inside the human body where line-of-sight is blocked.
> - **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva, Symbotic):** Use **2D LiDAR SLAM** with fiducial markers (AprilTags) for cm-precision navigation in highly dynamic environments.

## 2.7 Concept-Building Logic Flow

Why SLAM is hard — the **three compounding problems**:

1. **Sensor Noise** — Every measurement has Gaussian/Non-Gaussian noise, and errors **accumulate** over time (drift).
2. **Data Association Ambiguity** — Determining whether two sensor readings correspond to the **same physical landmark** is non-trivial in repetitive environments.
3. **Computational Complexity** — Joint estimation of pose + map has a state space that grows with every observation; naive solutions are $O(N^2)$ or worse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern SLAM addresses all three via: **Filtering/Optimization** (handles noise), **Loop Closure Detection** (handles drift), and **Sparse Graph Representations** (handles complexity).
