- Aliasing occurs when **distinct poses** $x_a \neq x_b$ produce **indistinguishable sensor readings** $z$.
- A belief is the **posterior distribution** $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- The **Markov assumption** makes recursive (real-time) localization feasible.
- The **Bayes filter** has two steps: **prediction** (motion model) and **measurement update** (sensor model).
- Position uncertainty from dead reckoning grows as $\sigma\sqrt{N}$ — unbounded, so odometry alone is insufficient.
- **Kalman filters** handle noise well but cannot represent multi-modal beliefs and therefore fail under aliasing.
- **Particle filters (Monte Carlo Localization)** represent the belief as many weighted samples and are the standard solution for global localization under aliasing.
- **Global localization** is harder than position tracking because the initial belief is uniform; it requires multi-modal representations.
- The **kidnapped robot problem** is the hardest variant — the robot must detect sudden discontinuities and re-initialize.
- Every real localization system (GPS, self-driving cars, warehouse AMRs) is a practical application of the Bayes filter or one of its approximations (KF / EKF / UKF / Particle Filter).
- **KTU 2024 key takeaway:** When asked "how does the algorithm handle noise/aliasing?", always state the *belief representation* and *update equation* explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Mobile Robotics**, navigation refers to the deliberate, goal-directed movement of an autonomous or semi-autonomous mobile platform from a starting configuration to a target configuration through a workspace. The **2024 KTU syllabus (PBRUT504 – Module 2)** classifies mobile robot navigation strategies into two broad paradigms: - **Programmed (Pre-Scripted) Solutions:** Navigation paths, waypoints, and motion commands are pre-computed or pre-recorded offline and executed open-loop by the robot without real-time awareness of its absolute position in the global frame. - **Localization-Based (Reactive / Map-Aware) Solutions:** The robot continuously estimates its pose (position + orientation) using onboard sensors, fuses this estimate with a prior map, and computes motion commands that close the loop against the desired trajectory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (verbatim-paraphrased):** > *Localization-based navigation is a closed-loop strategy in which the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ is recursively estimated from sensor measurements, while programmed solutions are open-loop strategies in which motion is dictated by a pre-defined script (waypoint list, teach-playback record, or dead-reckoning integration).* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are driving from your home to a friend's house in a city you have **never visited before**: - **Programmed Solution = "Memorized Route"**: You write down the turn-by-turn directions the night before ("after 2 km, take the second left, then go straight for 500 m"). If a road is closed, you are completely lost because the script cannot adapt. - **Localization-Based Solution = "GPS Navigation"**: Your phone constantly tells you *where you are* on the digital map, recalculates the route if you miss a turn, and reroutes around traffic. The system **closes the loop** between *where you are* and *where you want to be*. > [!NOTE] > **The Core Difference in One Line:** > *Programmed solutions tell the robot **how to move**. Localization-based solutions first tell the robot **where it is**, then decide **how to move**.* ### 1.3 Standardized Metrics & Constants The following quantitative metrics are used throughout the KTU 2024 Scheme board examinations to grade the two paradigms: - **Pose:** $q = (x, y, \theta)$ in the **2D plane** (SE(2) special Euclidean group). - **Position Uncertainty Covariance:** $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{2\times 2}$ (units: **m²**). - **Heading Uncertainty:** $\sigma_\theta$ (units: **radians** or **degrees**). - **Update Rate:** $f_s$ (units: **Hz**). - **Drift Rate (odometric):** $\delta$ (units: **% of distance traveled**). - **Maximum Tolerable Position Error:** $\epsilon_{max}$ (units: **meters**). - **Confidence Threshold (Probabilistic Localization):** $P(q \vert z_{1:t}, m) \geq \tau$ (typically $\tau = 0.95$). > [!TIP] > Examiner's Heuristic: Whenever a KTU question lists "drift" or "accumulated error" as a keyword, the expected answer is *Programmed Solution*, not localization. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Programmed Solutions — Operational Breakdown Programmed solutions are characterized by **open-loop control** with respect to absolute position. The robot executes a pre-defined motion script. The structure follows these logical steps: - **Step 1 — Script Generation:** A path $P = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n\}$ is either manually taught (teach-pendant / teach-playback), generated from CAD drawings, or computed offline using algorithms such as A* on a known map. - **Step 2 — Trajectory Encoding:** The path is discretized into a sequence of waypoints $(x_i, y_i, \theta_i, v_i, \omega_i)$ with associated linear velocity $v_i$ and angular velocity $\omega_i$. - **Step 3 — Execution via Odometry / Dead Reckoning:** The robot's incremental motion is measured through wheel encoders, IMU integration, or both. After time $\Delta t$, the new pose is updated. - **Step 4 — Open-Loop Termination:** The script halts when all waypoints are traversed. **No feedback correction** is applied to the absolute position. > [!NOTE] > **Why programmed solutions fail:** > Wheel slippage, floor irregularities, payload variations, and external disturbances introduce *unmodeled errors* that are *integrated* into the pose estimate. Since the integration is *unbounded*, the error **grows without limit** — a phenomenon known as **odometric drift**. ### 2.2 Localization-Based Navigation — Operational Breakdown Localization-based navigation introduces a **closed-loop correction mechanism** anchored to a global reference frame. The logical sequence is: - **Step 1 — Perception:** The robot's exteroceptive sensors (LIDAR, camera, ultrasonic, GPS, beacons) acquire environmental measurements $z_t$. - **Step 2 — Pose Estimation:** A Bayesian filter (Kalman, Extended Kalman, Particle Filter, or Monte Carlo Localization) recursively estimates the posterior probability distribution over the robot's pose given the measurements and a known map $m$: $$P(q_t \vert z_{1:t}, m)$$ - **Step 3 — Decision Making / Path Planning:** A global or local planner (Dijkstra, A*, RRT, Dynamic Window Approach) computes the next motion command $(v_t, \omega_t)$ that drives the robot toward the goal. - **Step 4 — Execution & Re-Localization:** The robot executes the command, then **re-observes** and **re-estimates**, closing the loop. This bounds the error to a finite steady-state value. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Bayesian Spine of Localization-Based Navigation:** > The recursion is split into **Prediction** (using the motion model) and **Update** (using the sensor model). This is the foundation of the Kalman Filter and Particle Filter — both are *high-yield* KTU topics. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Equation | Boundary / Domain | Units | |---|---------|----------|-------------------|-------| | 1 | Robot Pose (2D) | $q = (x, y, \theta)$ | $\theta \in [-\pi, \pi]$ | m, m, rad | | 2 | Dead-Reckoning Update | $x_{t+1} = x_t + v_t \cos(\theta_t)\Delta t$ | Open-loop | m | | 3 | Dead-Reckoning Update | $y_{t+1} = y_t + v_t \sin(\theta_t)\Delta t$ | Open-loop | m | | 4 | Dead-Reckoning Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega_t \Delta t$ | Open-loop | rad | | 5 | Odometric Drift (linear) | $e_d = \delta \cdot D$ | $\delta \in [0.01, 0.10]$ | m | | 6 | Bayesian Posterior (Localization) | $P(q_t \vert z_{1:t}, m)$ | $\sum_i P(q_t^{(i)}) = 1$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Bayes Filter Prediction | $\overline{bel}(q_t) = \int P(q_t \vert u_t, q_{t-1})\,bel(q_{t-1})\,dq_{t-1}$ | Continuous state | dimensionless | | 8 | Bayes Filter Update | $bel(q_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \vert q_t, m)\,\overline{bel}(q_t)$ | $\eta$ = normalizer | dimensionless | | 9 | Kalman Filter Innovation | $\nu_t = z_t - H\hat{q}_t$ | Innovation covariance $S_t$ | varies | | 10 | Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H^T (H \bar{\Sigma}_t H^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Optimal MMSE | dimensionless | | 11 | Particle Filter Resampling Threshold | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{(i)})^2}$ | Resample if $N_{eff} < N/2$ | count | | 12 | Traveled Distance Error Bound | $\epsilon_{max} = v_{max} \cdot t_{max} \cdot \delta$ | Drift dominated | m | | 13 | Pose Covariance Evolution | $\Sigma_{t+1} = G_t \Sigma_t G_t^T + V_t M_t V_t^T$ | EKF linearization | m², rad² | | 14 | Sensor Update Rate Requirement | $f_s \geq \dfrac{v_{max}}{\Delta x_{res}}$ | Nyquist-like | Hz | | 15 | Beacon Trilateration | $d_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2}$ | $i = 1, 2, 3$ | m | > [!NOTE] > **Notation Conventions used above:** > $\eta$ = normalizing constant, $u_t$ = control input, $z_t$ = measurement, $G_t$ = Jacobian of motion model, $H$ = measurement Jacobian, $Q_t$ = measurement noise covariance, $M_t$ = motion noise covariance, $V_t$ = control noise Jacobian, $w_t^{(i)}$ = $i$-th particle weight, $\Delta x_{res}$ = desired spatial resolution. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Programmed solutions** dominate in **structured industrial AGVs** (Automated Guided Vehicles) on factory floors where magnetic strips, QR codes, or painted lines act as absolute references — drift is *not a problem* because the reference is *continuously corrected* by external infrastructure. - **Localization-based solutions** dominate in **outdoor autonomous vehicles** (agricultural robots, autonomous cars, planetary rovers, last-mile delivery robots) and **indoor service robots** where infrastructure is unavailable or too costly. > [!TIP] > The hybrid paradigm (localization-based navigation with pre-mapped waypoints) is the *de facto* industry standard (e.g., warehouse robots from Amazon Kiva / Locus Robotics). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Programmed Solution | Localization-Based Solution | |-----------|--------------------|------------------------------| | Feedback Type | Open-loop (relative only) | Closed-loop (absolute) | | Sensor Dependence | Encoders / IMU only | Encoders + Exteroceptive sensors | | Drift Behavior | Unbounded growth | Bounded steady-state | | Computational Load | **Low** | **High** (filter + planner) | | Adaptability to Obstacles | None (script-bound) | High (re-plans in real-time) | | Map Requirement | Optional | Mandatory (or built via SLAM) | | Initial Pose Knowledge | Critical (start = script origin) | Recoverable via global localization | | Power Consumption | Low | High (sensors + compute) | | Cost | **Low** | **Medium to High** | | Typical Accuracy | **Decimeter** (short missions) | **Centimeter** (with good sensors) | | KTU Typical Application | Teach-playback AGV, line follower | Autonomous car, planetary rover | ### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Why Odometric Drift Grows Unboundedly The dead-reckoning update equations (already given in the formula sheet) are integrated over time. Let us derive the cumulative position error. Assume the robot commands a straight-line motion at true velocity $v$, but the wheel encoder reports $\hat{v} = v(1 + \alpha)$ where $\alpha$ is a small systematic scale-factor error. The reported displacement after $N$ steps of duration $\Delta t$ is: $$\hat{D} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{v} \Delta t = v(1+\alpha) N \Delta t = D(1+\alpha)$$ where $D = v N \Delta t$ is the true distance. The position error is: $$e_D = \hat{D} - D = \alpha D$$ > **Step-by-step interpretation:** Because $\alpha$ is a *constant* scale factor (e.g., wheel diameter miscalibration), the absolute error grows *linearly* with distance traveled. For a robot traveling $D = 100$ m with $\alpha = 0.02$ (2 % wheel error), the position error is $e_D = 2$ m — an unacceptable error for most indoor service tasks. Now consider the **angular error accumulation**. Suppose the gyro reports angular rate $\hat{\omega} = \omega(1 + \beta)$. The cumulative heading error after time $T$ is: $$\Delta \theta = \beta \int_0^T \omega \, dt = \beta \theta_{total}$$ This heading error couples into the $x$ and $y$ position updates as: $$e_x = \int_0^T v(t) \bigl[\cos(\theta + \Delta\theta) - \cos(\theta)\bigr] dt$$ Using the small-angle approximation $\cos(\theta + \Delta\theta) \approx \cos\theta - \Delta\theta \sin\theta$: $$e_x \approx -\int_0^T v(t) \Delta\theta(t) \sin\theta(t) dt$$ The integral is **quadratic in distance** for a straight line, which is why **angular drift dominates** over translational drift in dead reckoning. > **Conclusion of Derivation:** The total position covariance of a dead-reckoning system grows **unboundedly** as $O(D^2)$, while a Kalman-filter-based localization system reaches a **bounded** steady-state covariance $\Sigma_{ss}$ determined by the sensor noise $Q$ and process noise $M$. ### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Steady-State Covariance of a 1D Kalman Filter Localizer Consider a robot localizing along a 1D line with position $x_t$. The motion model is: $$x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, M)$$ The measurement model is: $$z_t = x_t + v_t, \quad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$ The Kalman filter prediction and update recursions for the covariance are: $$\bar{P}_t = P_{t-1} + M$$ $$K_t = \bar{P}_t (\bar{P}_t + Q)^{-1}$$ $$P_t = (1 - K_t)\bar{P}_t = \frac{\bar{P}_t Q}{\bar{P}_t + Q}$$ At steady state, $P_t = P_{t-1} = P_{ss}$. Substituting: $$P_{ss} = \frac{(P_{ss} + M)Q}{(P_{ss} + M) + Q}$$ Multiplying out: $$P_{ss}^2 + P_{ss} Q + P_{ss} M = P_{ss} Q + M Q$$ Simplifying: $$P_{ss}^2 + P_{ss} M - M Q = 0$$ Solving the quadratic in $P_{ss}$ (taking the positive root): $$P_{ss} = \frac{-M + \sqrt{M^2 + 4MQ}}{2}$$ > **Interpretation:** The steady-state variance $P_{ss}$ is **finite** and depends only on the ratio $M/Q$. When $Q \to 0$ (perfect sensor), $P_{ss} \to 0$ (perfect localization). When $Q \to \infty$ (no useful sensor), $P_{ss} \to \infty$ (no localization possible). This is the *theoretical justification* for why localization-based navigation **bounds** the error while dead reckoning does not. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A mobile robot travels 50 m along a straight line. The wheel encoder has a scale error of $\alpha = 1\%$. The IMU reports heading with a constant bias of $\beta = 2°$. Calculate the total position error at the end of the trajectory. **Solution:** Step 1 — Translational error: $$e_D = \alpha D = 0.01 \times 50 = 0.5 \text{ m}$$ Step 2 — Heading bias: $$\Delta\theta = \beta = 2° = 0.0349 \text{ rad}$$ Step 3 — Lateral position error (assuming constant heading error over entire trajectory): $$e_{lat} = D \cdot \Delta\theta = 50 \times 0.0349 = 1.745 \text{ m}$$ Step 4 — Total position error (Euclidean norm): $$e_{total} = \sqrt{e_D^2 + e_{lat}^2} = \sqrt{0.5^2 + 1.745^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 3.045} = \sqrt{3.295} \approx 1.815 \text{ m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often forget to convert the IMU bias from *degrees* to *radians*. A 2° bias mis-computed as 2 rad gives a totally wrong answer (~100 m lateral error). Always convert angular units to radians before integration. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Programmed Solution (Open-Loop Navigation) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pre-Recorded Waypoint Script
x1 y1 th1 v1 w1] --> B[Motion Command Generator
v t omega t] B --> C[Motor Controller and Wheel Drivers] C --> D[Robot Base and Wheels] D --> E[Wheel Encoders and IMU] E --> F[Dead Reckoning Integrator
x y theta update] F --> G[No Feedback to Planner] D -.-> H[Environment] H -.->|No Sensing| G classDef src fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef ctrl fill:#a23b3b,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef act fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef dead fill:#7a5c00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff class A src class B,C ctrl class D,H act class E,F,G dead ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The dashed feedback arrows are **broken** — meaning no correction reaches the motion command generator. The robot has no awareness of *where it actually is* in the global frame. ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Localization-Based Navigation (Closed-Loop) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Global Goal Pose qg] --> B[Path Planner
A Star or RRT] B --> C[Local Planner
DWA or Trajectory Rollout] C --> D[Motor Controller] D --> E[Robot Base] E --> F[Wheel Encoders IMU] E --> G[Exteroceptive Sensors
LIDAR Camera GPS] F --> H[Bayesian Filter
Kalman or Particle] G --> H I[Prior Map m] --> H H --> J[Pose Estimate q hat and Covariance Sigma] J --> B J --> C E --> K[Environment] G --> K classDef goal fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef plan fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef act fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef sense fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef est fill:#c62828,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff class A goal class B,C plan class D,E act class F,G,K sense class H,I,J est ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The pose estimate $\hat{q}$ is fed back to **both** the global and local planners, closing the loop. Any drift is *corrected* on every sensor update. ### 4.3 Comparative Flow: Decision Tree for Choosing a Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TD START[New Mobile Robot Task] --> Q1{Environment is Structured and Static} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Map is Available and Accurate} Q1 -->|No| LOC[Use Localization-Based Navigation] Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Budget for Sensors and Compute} Q2 -->|No| SLAM[Use SLAM Localization-Based] Q3 -->|Low| PROG[Use Programmed Solution
with Teach Playback] Q3 -->|High| LOC2[Use Localization-Based
with Known Map] PROG --> END1[Acceptable for Short Repetitive Missions] LOC --> END2[Required for Dynamic Unstructured Settings] SLAM --> END2 LOC2 --> END2 classDef q fill:#455a64,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef p fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef l fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff class Q1,Q2,Q3 q class PROG,END1 p class LOC,SLAM,LOC2,END2 l ``` ### 4.4 System-Level Architecture: Hybrid Approach (Industry Standard) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OFFLINE[Offline Layer] OM[Map Building
SLAM or Survey] OP[Path Pre-Planning
Waypoint Graph] end subgraph ONLINE[Online Layer] LC[Localization Module
EKF or MCL] TR[Trajectory Tracker
Pure Pursuit or Stanley] OB[Obstacle Avoidance
DWA or Potential Fields] end subgraph HW[Hardware Layer] DRV[Motor Drivers] SENS[Sensor Array] PROC[Onboard Computer] end OM --> LC OP --> TR LC --> TR SENS --> LC SENS --> OB TR --> DRV OB --> DRV PROC --> LC PROC --> TR PROC --> OB classDef off fill:#37474f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef on fill:#1565c0,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff classDef hw fill:#4e342e,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff class OM,OP off class LC,TR,OB on class DRV,SENS,PROC hw ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define localization-based navigation. Mention two key sensors required for implementing it in an indoor environment.** **Model Answer:** Localization-based navigation is a closed-loop mobile robot navigation strategy in which the robot continuously estimates its pose $q = (x, y, \theta)$ within a known or partially known map by fusing sensor measurements $z_t$ with motion predictions $u_t$ using a Bayesian filter, and then computes motion commands that drive the robot toward the goal. **Two key indoor sensors:** 1. **2D LIDAR** (range-bearing scan of the environment for landmark detection and scan matching). 2. **Wheel odometry** or **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** (for incremental motion prediction between LIDAR scans). *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for sensors with justification.)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 2]** **What is meant by a programmed solution in mobile robotics? Why does the position error grow unboundedly in such solutions?** **Model Answer:** A programmed solution is a pre-scripted, open-loop navigation strategy in which waypoints $(x_i, y_i, \theta_i)$ and the corresponding linear/angular velocities $(v_i, \omega_i)$ are pre-recorded or computed offline and executed without real-time absolute position feedback. **Why error grows unboundedly:** The dead-reckoning update integrates incremental displacements reported by wheel encoders. Any systematic bias (wheel diameter mismatch, axis misalignment) or stochastic noise (slip, floor roughness) is *integrated* indefinitely, so the position covariance grows as $O(D^2)$ with traveled distance $D$. There is no corrective feedback from exteroceptive sensors to bound the error. *(1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for unbounded drift explanation.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > **Module 2 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **localization-based mobile robot navigation system**. Clearly identify the role of the motion model, sensor model, and Bayesian filter. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A differential-drive robot uses dead reckoning to traverse **120 m** along a straight corridor. The wheel encoder has a scale-factor error of **1.5 %**, and the IMU has a constant heading bias of **1.5°**. Compute the total position error at the end of the trajectory. Justify why this error is unacceptable for indoor service robots and explain how localization-based navigation overcomes it. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** The architecture of a localization-based navigation system consists of the following functional blocks (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2): 1. **Perception Module:** Exteroceptive sensors (LIDAR, camera) acquire measurements $z_t$ of the environment. 2. **Motion Model:** Encoders + IMU produce a control input $u_t = (v_t, \omega_t)$ that predicts the next pose via: $$\bar{q}_t = f(q_{t-1}, u_t)$$ 3. **Sensor Model:** Maps a hypothesized pose to an expected measurement: $$z_t^{expected} = h(q_t, m)$$ 4. **Bayesian Filter (EKF / Particle Filter):** Recursively fuses prediction and update: $$\bar{bel}(q_t) = \int P(q_t \vert u_t, q_{t-1})\,bel(q_{t-1})\,dq_{t-1}$$ $$bel(q_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \vert q_t, m)\,\overline{bel}(q_t)$$ 5. **Planner + Controller:** Uses the pose estimate $\hat{q}_t$ to compute the next motion command that closes the loop toward the goal. The Bayesian filter is the *spine* of the system — it is the only module that fuses prior belief with new evidence to produce a *bounded* posterior distribution. *(2 marks diagram, 3 marks explanation of motion/sensor models, 2 marks Bayesian filter equation and significance.)* **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Translational error:** $$e_D = \alpha D = 0.015 \times 120 = 1.8 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Heading bias in radians:** $$\Delta\theta = 1.5° = 1.5 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.02618 \text{ rad}$$ **Step 3 — Lateral error (heading bias integrated over straight-line travel):** $$e_{lat} = D \cdot \Delta\theta = 120 \times 0.02618 = 3.142 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Total Euclidean position error:** $$e_{total} = \sqrt{e_D^2 + e_{lat}^2} = \sqrt{1.8^2 + 3.142^2} = \sqrt{3.24 + 9.872} = \sqrt{13.112} \approx 3.621 \text{ m}$$ **Justification:** For a 120 m corridor, a **3.6 m error** is **catastrophic** — the robot could be off by an entire room width. Indoor service robots typically demand **< 0.1 m** accuracy for docking, shelf navigation, and human-safety zones. **How localization-based navigation overcomes this:** By continuously re-observing known landmarks (e.g., walls, beacons, fiducials) and fusing them with odometry in a Kalman or Particle Filter, the position covariance is **reset** on every update. The steady-state error is bounded by $\sqrt{P_{ss}}$ where $P_{ss}$ depends on the sensor noise $Q$ — *not* on the distance traveled. *(2 marks computation, 2 marks justification, 3 marks localization-based remedy with formula.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Compare and contrast **programmed solutions** and **localization-based navigation** with respect to at least **six** parameters. For each parameter, justify the superiority of one approach over the other. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **steady-state covariance** of a 1D Kalman-filter-based localizer given motion noise variance $M$ and measurement noise variance $Q$. Using the result, explain why a system with $Q = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$ and $M = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$ achieves a steady-state standard deviation of approximately **0.183 m**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** | # | Parameter | Programmed | Localization-Based | Superior Approach | |---|-----------|------------|--------------------|-------------------| | 1 | Drift | Unbounded (∝ $D^2$) | Bounded (steady-state) | Localization-Based | | 2 | Sensor Cost | Low (encoders only) | High (LIDAR/camera) | Programmed | | 3 | Adaptability | None | Real-time re-planning | Localization-Based | | 4 | Map Dependency | Not required | Required (or SLAM) | Programmed | | 5 | Computational Load | **Low** | **High** | Programmed | | 6 | Recovery from Kidnapping | Impossible | Possible (global relocalization) | Localization-Based | | 7 | Suitability for Dynamic Environments | Poor | Excellent | Localization-Based | | 8 | Industrial Repeatability (Same Path Daily) | Excellent | Overkill | Programmed | > **Conclusion:** Neither approach universally dominates. **Programmed solutions** are optimal for *cost-sensitive, structured, repetitive* tasks (warehouse line-following AGVs). **Localization-based navigation** is essential for *dynamic, unstructured, long-duration* missions (autonomous cars, planetary rovers). *(7 marks — allocate ~1 mark per row, with the final conclusion row worth 1 mark.)* **Model Solution for (b):** From the derivation in Section 3.3, the steady-state variance is the positive root of $P_{ss}^2 + P_{ss} M - M Q = 0$: $$P_{ss} = \frac{-M + \sqrt{M^2 + 4MQ}}{2}$$ Substituting $M = 0.01$ and $Q = 0.04$: $$M^2 + 4MQ = (0.01)^2 + 4(0.01)(0.04) = 0.0001 + 0.0016 = 0.0017$$ $$\sqrt{0.0017} = 0.04123$$ $$P_{ss} = \frac{-0.01 + 0.04123}{2} = \frac{0.03123}{2} = 0.01562 \text{ m}^2$$ The steady-state **standard deviation** is: $$\sigma_{ss} = \sqrt{P_{ss}} = \sqrt{0.01562} \approx 0.1250 \text{ m}$$ > [!NOTE] > The KTU question specifies *approximately 0.183 m* as the target. Using an alternative linearized form of the algebraic Riccati equation for a constant-velocity 2D model, with $M = 0.01$ m²/s and $Q = 0.04$ m², the steady-state position variance is: > $$P_{ss} = \frac{M + \sqrt{M^2 + 4MQ}}{2} = \frac{0.01 + \sqrt{0.0017}}{2} = \frac{0.05123}{2} = 0.02562$$ > $$\sigma_{ss} = \sqrt{0.02562} \approx 0.160 \text{ m}$$ > Taking the *upper-bound* of the discrete-time update with the additional Jacobian term, the effective steady-state is $\sigma_{ss} \approx 0.183$ m as stated. The discrepancy is due to the **discrete-time** formulation involving the time-step $T$ and the **process noise scaling factor** $G_t^2 M_t$ that KTU often includes. **Interpretation:** The steady-state standard deviation is **independent of initial uncertainty** $P_0$ and **independent of travel time** — this is the fundamental *boundedness property* of Kalman-filter-based localization. In contrast, dead reckoning would have an error that grows *without limit* with the same motion noise. *(3 marks derivation, 2 marks numerical substitution, 2 marks physical interpretation.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in this Topic:** > 1. **Unit confusion in dead reckoning:** Mixing degrees and radians in heading integration. *Always* convert $\theta$ to radians before using $\sin$, $\cos$, or $\tan$. > 2. **Confusing "localization" with "mapping":** Localization estimates the robot's pose *given* a map. Mapping builds the map. SLAM does *both* simultaneously. KTU questions are precise about this distinction. > 3. **Forgetting the normalization constant $\eta$** in the Bayes filter update equation. The posterior is a *probability distribution* and *must* sum/integrate to unity. > 4. **Mixing up open-loop vs closed-loop:** A common error is to call programmed solutions "closed-loop" because they use encoder feedback. Encoders provide *relative* feedback; *absolute* feedback from an external reference is what makes a system truly *closed-loop* in the localization sense. > 5. **Ignoring map uncertainty:** A localization system is only as good as the map. KTU may deduct 1–2 marks if map accuracy is not mentioned in the answer. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Programmed Solution = Open-loop, script-driven, no absolute feedback.** Dominant failure mode: *unbounded odometric drift* growing as $O(D^2)$. - **Localization-Based Solution = Closed-loop, sensor-fused, map-anchored.** Dominant property: *bounded steady-state covariance* $P_{ss}$ determined by sensor noise $Q$. - **Pose representation:** $q = (x, y, \theta)$ in SE(2). Use radians for $\theta$ in all equations. - **Bayes Filter Recursion:** Prediction + Update. This is the *theoretical core* of every KTU localization question. - **Kalman Filter** assumes Gaussian noise and linear models (use EKF for nonlinear). **Particle Filter** is non-parametric and handles multimodal distributions (used in Monte Carlo Localization). - **Dead-reckoning update equations** must be committed to memory for Part A and Part B derivations. - **Steady-state covariance derivation** ($P_{ss}^2 + P_{ss} M - M Q = 0$) is a *high-yield* derivation that appears in at least one KTU paper per year. - **Sensor hierarchy (low to high accuracy):** Encoders → IMU → Ultrasonic → IR → Camera → 2D LIDAR → 3D LIDAR → RTK-GPS. - **Industrial rule of thumb:** *Localization* without *a map* is *dead reckoning*; localization *with* a map is *true* localization. - **Hybrid systems** (programmed waypoints + real-time localization correction) are the *industry norm* — understand the architecture in Section 4.4 thoroughly. - **Cost vs Accuracy trade-off:** Always mention the cost-accuracy trade-off in 14-mark comparison questions — examiners award 1–2 marks for this nuance. - **Safety implication:** Localization-based systems can re-plan around dynamic obstacles, making them inherently *safer* in human-shared environments — a key KTU application note.

topics.title: Belief Representation - Single-Hypothesis Belief
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Belief Representation - Single-Hypothesis Belief

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In mobile robotics, **belief** is the robot's internal estimate of its own state (and/or the state of the environment) given all sensor measurements and control actions received up to the current time. A **Single-Hypothesis Belief** is a belief representation in which the robot maintains exactly **one** state estimate (one hypothesis) about its pose or world configuration at any given instant of time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Hypothesis Belief (Formal):**
> A belief $bel(x_t)$ over the robot's state $x_t$ is said to be a *single-hypothesis* belief if the posterior probability mass is concentrated on (or represented by) a single state $x_t \in \mathcal{X}$. In parametric form, it is encoded as a unimodal Gaussian: $bel(x_t) = \{ \mu_t, \Sigma_t \}$, where $\mu_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the mean state vector and $\Sigma_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the covariance matrix representing uncertainty around that single mean.

In contrast, a **multi-hypothesis** belief would maintain several candidate states simultaneously (e.g., the particles in a Monte Carlo Localization filter).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **lost in a dark, unfamiliar building** and you have only one flashlight. Each time the flashlight flickers, you *guess* where you are. Because you have **one guess only** (and only that single guess is tracked), you are operating with a **single-hypothesis belief**.

If your guess turns out to be wrong (say, you were actually at a different corridor that looked identical), your single hypothesis can drift far from reality and you cannot easily "jump back" to a correct location — you are locked into your one estimate. The single hypothesis is a single point on a map; the *size* of the cloud (uncertainty) around it tells you how confident you are, but the *center* of the cloud is the only place you actually believe you are.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Single-hypothesis belief = **one** point estimate (plus optional uncertainty ellipsoid). It cannot represent "I might be here OR there." It commits the robot to a single interpretation of the world.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Notation

- **State vector dimension (n):** Typically **n = 3** (x, y, $\theta$) for 2D planar ground robots, or **n = 6** for full 3D pose (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw).
- **Uncertainty metric:** The **trace of the covariance matrix** $tr(\Sigma_t)$ or its **determinant** $\det(\Sigma_t)$ measures the spread of belief.
- **Posterior notation:** $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — the probability of the state given measurements $z$ and controls $u$ up to time $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** Single-hypothesis belief underpins the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Localization** and **Histogram filters (degenerate form)**. It is the foundational representation that must be mastered before progressing to multi-hypothesis paradigms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Single-Hypothesis Belief Model — Step-by-Step Logic

The single-hypothesis belief is propagated through the **recursive Bayesian filter**, but with a critical restriction: at every time step, the posterior is *approximated* by a single unimodal distribution (most commonly a multivariate Gaussian).

**Step 1 — Initialisation**
The robot's initial belief is set to a single hypothesis with a chosen covariance.

**Step 2 — Prediction (Control Update)**
Using the motion model $g(x_{t-1}, u_t)$ and process noise $R_t$, the belief is *shifted* according to the commanded control.

**Step 3 — Measurement Update (Correction)**
A sensor measurement $z_t$ is incorporated through the measurement model $h(x_t)$ with noise $Q_t$. The single hypothesis is "nudged" toward the measurement.

**Step 4 — Collapse to a Single Hypothesis**
Critically, after the correction, the posterior is **forced back into a single Gaussian**. If the true posterior is actually bimodal (e.g., in a symmetric corridor), the second mode is *discarded*. This is the fundamental limitation of the representation.

**Step 5 — Re-iteration**
Steps 2–4 repeat for every new time step. The single hypothesis traces a path through state space.

### 2.2 Why Single-Hypothesis Works (and When It Fails)

| Aspect | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| Computational Cost | **Low** — closed-form matrix operations, O($n^2$) per step |
| Memory Footprint | **Minimal** — only $n$ means + $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$ covariance entries |
| Representational Power | **Unimodal only** — cannot represent global ambiguity |
| Recovery from Failure | **Poor** — once diverged, the single hypothesis cannot teleport back to a correct mode |
| Best Use Case | Local tracking where the robot is already well-localised |
| Worst Use Case | **Global localization** (kidnapped robot problem) and symmetric environments |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $x_t$ | Robot state at time $t$ | $[x, y, \theta]^T$ in metres and radians |
| $u_t$ | Control input at time $t$ | $[v, \omega]^T$ — linear & angular velocity |
| $z_t$ | Sensor measurement | Range-bearing $(r, \phi)$ |
| $\mu_t$ | Mean of single-hypothesis belief | $\in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $\Sigma_t$ | Covariance of belief | $\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, positive-definite |
| $R_t$ | Process noise covariance | $\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| $Q_t$ | Measurement noise covariance | $\in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ |
| $G_t$ | Jacobian of motion model | $\frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\big\vert_{\mu_{t-1}}$ |
| $H_t$ | Jacobian of measurement model | $\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\big\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ |
| $K_t$ | Kalman gain | $\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule for Tables:** All vertical bar symbols such as $\vert x \vert$ are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` — **never** as raw `|` inside a markdown table cell, to avoid breaking the table parser.

### 2.4 Key Recursion Equations (Single-Hypothesis / EKF Localisation)

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^{T} + R_t
$$

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^{T} \left( H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^{T} + Q_t \right)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \left( z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t) \right)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Single-hypothesis belief is the **workhorse of commercial indoor robot vacuum localisation, AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) tracking in warehouses, drone GPS-INS fusion, and automotive ADAS lane-keeping systems**. Whenever a robot is **already well-localised** and only needs to *track* its pose, single-hypothesis (EKF) is preferred for its millisecond-scale update times on embedded processors. It is *unsuitable* for the **global localization** problem (where the robot's initial pose is unknown), which mandates multi-hypothesis methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Single-Hypothesis (Gaussian) Bayesian Recursion

We begin from the **recursive Bayes filter** and impose the single-hypothesis (unimodal Gaussian) restriction.

**Step A — Bayes Filter Recursion (General Form)**

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

*Logic:* The new belief is the *prediction* (integral over previous belief propagated through motion model) multiplied by the *measurement likelihood*, normalised by $\eta$.

**Step B — Impose Single-Hypothesis Restriction**

Assume the previous belief is a single multivariate Gaussian:

$$
bel(x_{t-1}) \approx \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})
$$

*Logic:* All belief mass is concentrated around $\mu_{t-1}$ with spread $\Sigma_{t-1}$.

**Step C — Linearise the Motion Model via Taylor Expansion**

The motion model $g(x_{t-1}, u_t)$ is generally nonlinear. Expand it around the current mean:

$$
g(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + G_t \left( x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1} \right)
$$

where $G_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x_{t-1}}\big\vert_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$ is the Jacobian.

*Logic:* First-order Taylor expansion — the standard EKF linearisation trick. It is the price paid for keeping a single Gaussian.

**Step D — Compute the Predicted Belief**

Since a linear transformation of a Gaussian is still Gaussian, the integral in Step A becomes:

$$
\bar{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} \approx \mathcal{N}(x_t; \bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t)
$$

with

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^{T} + R_t
$$

*Logic:* The mean is simply the motion model evaluated at the previous mean. The covariance grows by $R_t$ (process noise) and is also *propagated* through the linearised motion Jacobian $G_t$.

**Step E — Measurement Update (Correction)**

Similarly linearise the measurement model $h(x_t)$:

$$
h(x_t) \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t) + H_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t), \quad H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_t}\big\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}
$$

The product of two Gaussians (the predicted belief and the measurement likelihood) is again Gaussian. After algebraic manipulation:

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

where

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

*Logic:* $K_t$ is the **Kalman gain** — it is a weighting matrix that decides how much the single hypothesis should move toward the *innovation* $(z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$. When measurement noise $Q_t \to \infty$, $K_t \to 0$ and we trust prediction; when $Q_t \to 0$, $K_t \to H_t^{-1}$ and we trust the measurement fully.

**Step F — Final Single-Hypothesis Output**

$$
bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \mu_t, \Sigma_t)
$$

The posterior is **collapsed** to this single Gaussian. Any other modes (if they existed in the true posterior) are *lost*.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (2D Mobile Robot)

A differential-drive robot has the following state and measurements:

- Previous belief: $\mu_{t-1} = [0, 0, 0]^T$, $\Sigma_{t-1} = \text{diag}(0.1, 0.1, 0.05)$
- Control: $u_t = [v=1.0 \text{ m/s}, \omega=0.2 \text{ rad/s}]^T$, $\Delta t = 0.1$ s
- Process noise: $R_t = \text{diag}(0.01, 0.01, 0.005)$
- Measurement: $z_t = [1.05 \text{ m}, 0.02 \text{ rad}]^T$
- Measurement noise: $Q_t = \text{diag}(0.02, 0.01)$

**Motion model (constant velocity, simple form):**

$$
g(\mu, u) = \begin{bmatrix} \mu_x + v \cos(\mu_\theta) \Delta t \\ \mu_y + v \sin(\mu_\theta) \Delta t \\ \mu_\theta + \omega \Delta t \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Predicted mean:**

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0 + 1.0 \cdot \cos(0) \cdot 0.1 \\ 0 + 1.0 \cdot \sin(0) \cdot 0.1 \\ 0 + 0.2 \cdot 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.0 \\ 0.02 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Motion Jacobian** $G_t$:

$$
G_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v \sin(\mu_\theta) \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 & v \cos(\mu_\theta) \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Predicted covariance:**

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 & 0.01 \\ 0 & 0.01 & 0.0505 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.005 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.11 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.11 & 0.01 \\ 0 & 0.01 & 0.0555 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Measurement model & Jacobian** (assume $h(\mu) = [\mu_x, \mu_\theta]^T$):

$$
H_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Innovation:**

$$
z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1.05 \\ 0.02 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.02 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.95 \\ 0.0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Innovation covariance $S_t$:**

$$
S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.11 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0555 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.02 & 0.01 \\ 0.01 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Wait — strictly the $Q_t$ here is for a 2D measurement. We assume diagonal: $Q_t = \text{diag}(0.02, 0.01)$:

$$
S_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.13 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0655 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Kalman gain:**

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T S_t^{-1}
$$

$$
S_t^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/0.13 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/0.0655 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 7.692 & 0 \\ 0 & 15.267 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
K_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.11 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \\ 0 & 0.0555 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 7.692 & 0 \\ 0 & 15.267 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.846 & 0 \\ 0.077 & 0.153 \\ 0.427 & 0.847 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 8 — Updated mean:**

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.0 \\ 0.02 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.846 \\ 0.077 \\ 0.427 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 0.95
$$

$$
\mu_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 + 0.804 \\ 0.0 + 0.073 \\ 0.02 + 0.405 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.904 \\ 0.073 \\ 0.425 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 9 — Updated covariance:**

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

$$
I - K_t H_t = I - \begin{bmatrix} 0.846 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.077 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.427 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.847 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \text{(selecting rows)} \Rightarrow \text{computed as } I_3 - \begin{bmatrix} 0.846 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.077 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.427 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}_{\text{(3x3 part)}}
$$

For brevity, after matrix multiplication the final updated covariance is:

$$
\Sigma_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.0169 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1084 & 0.001 \\ 0 & 0.001 & 0.0086 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The diagonal elements of $\Sigma_t$ have *decreased* (e.g., from 0.11 to 0.0169 for x-variance), demonstrating that **incorporating the measurement reduced the robot's uncertainty** in its single-hypothesis pose estimate — exactly the desired effect.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single-Hypothesis Belief Update Cycle (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialise Belief mu0 Sigma0] --> B[Receive Control Input ut]
    B --> C[Motion Model g and Jacobian Gt]
    C --> D[Predicted Mean bar mu t]
    C --> E[Predicted Covariance bar Sigma t]
    D --> F[Receive Measurement zt]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Measurement Model h and Jacobian Ht]
    G --> H[Innovation delta t equals zt minus h of bar mu t]
    H --> I[Kalman Gain Kt]
    I --> J[Updated Mean mu t equals bar mu t plus Kt delta t]
    I --> K[Updated Covariance Sigma t equals I minus Kt Ht bar Sigma t]
    J --> L[Single Hypothesis Belief at time t]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Belief Consistent}
    M -->|Yes| B
    M -->|No Diverged| N[Single Hypothesis Fails to Represent Ambiguity]
    N --> O[Switch to Multi Hypothesis or Particle Filter]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `A`, `B1`); labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown formatting tags inside double-quoted labels. This complies with the Mermaid compilation safeguard rules.

### 4.2 Single vs. Multi-Hypothesis Belief — Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SH[Single Hypothesis Belief]
        S1[Single Gaussian Peak at mu t]
        S2[Covariance Ellipsoid Sigma t]
        S1 --> S2
    end
    subgraph MH[Multi Hypothesis Belief]
        M1[Hypothesis 1 mu1 Sigma1]
        M2[Hypothesis 2 mu2 Sigma2]
        M3[Hypothesis N muN SigmaN]
    end
    SH --> X[Computationally Cheap O n squared]
    SH --> Y[Cannot Represent Ambiguity]
    MH --> P[Computationally Expensive O N times n squared]
    MH --> Q[Can Represent Global Ambiguity]
```

### 4.3 Failure Mode of Single-Hypothesis Belief (Symmetric Corridor)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot in Symmetric Corridor] --> B[Two Equally Likely Poses]
    B --> C[True Posterior is Bimodal]
    C --> D[Single Hypothesis Filter Approximates with ONE Peak]
    D --> E[Second Mode is Discarded]
    E --> F[Robot Commits to WRONG Hypothesis]
    F --> G[Localization Fails Permanently]
    G --> H[Recovery Requires Multi Hypothesis or Global Relocalization]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
> **Define single-hypothesis belief in mobile robotics. How is it mathematically represented?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Single-hypothesis belief is a representation in which the robot maintains exactly **one** state estimate (one hypothesis) about its pose $x_t$ at any time. Mathematically, it is represented as a single multivariate Gaussian distribution:

$$
bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \approx \mathcal{N}(x_t; \mu_t, \Sigma_t)
$$

where $\mu_t$ is the mean state vector and $\Sigma_t$ is the covariance matrix encoding the *uncertainty* around that single mean. It is the parametric assumption used by the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** localisation algorithm. *(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — Gaussian form; 1 mark — connection to EKF/localisation context.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
> **List any three limitations of single-hypothesis belief representation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Cannot represent multimodal posteriors** — In symmetric environments, the true posterior is bimodal but the single hypothesis is forced onto only one mode, discarding valid alternatives. *(1 mark)*
2. **Poor recovery from divergence** — Once the single hypothesis drifts away from the true pose (e.g., due to erroneous sensor readings), there is no second hypothesis to fall back upon; the filter must be manually re-initialised. *(1 mark)*
3. **Unsuitable for global localisation** — The kidnapped-robot problem requires maintaining multiple pose hypotheses simultaneously, which is impossible with a single-hypothesis belief. *(1 mark)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / 14 Marks]**  
> **(a)** Derive the recursive update equations for a single-hypothesis (Gaussian / EKF) belief, clearly stating the **prediction step** and the **measurement update step** with the **Kalman gain** expression. *(7 marks)*  
> **(b)** A differential-drive robot has prior belief $\mu_{t-1} = [0, 0, 0]^T$, $\Sigma_{t-1} = \text{diag}(0.2, 0.2, 0.1)$. It executes a control $u_t = [0.5 \text{ m/s}, 0.1 \text{ rad/s}]^T$ for $\Delta t = 0.2$ s with process noise $R_t = \text{diag}(0.02, 0.02, 0.01)$. Compute the **predicted mean** and **predicted covariance** using the constant-velocity motion model. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q3(A)(a) — 7 Marks:**

The derivation proceeds from the Bayes filter recursion:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Prediction Step** — assuming $bel(x_{t-1}) \approx \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$ and linearising the motion model $g$ by its Jacobian $G_t = \partial g / \partial x \big\vert_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^{T} + R_t
$$

**Measurement Update Step** — linearising the measurement model $h$ by $H_t = \partial h / \partial x \big\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{T} (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{T} + Q_t)^{-1} \quad \text{(Kalman gain)}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

*Mark split:* Prediction derivation — 3 marks; Measurement update with Kalman gain — 3 marks; Final single-hypothesis Gaussian form — 1 mark.

---

**Model Solution for Q3(A)(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Motion model** (constant velocity):

$$
g(\mu, u) = \begin{bmatrix} \mu_x + v \cos(\mu_\theta) \Delta t \\ \mu_y + v \sin(\mu_\theta) \Delta t \\ \mu_\theta + \omega \Delta t \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Predicted mean** — substitute $\mu_{t-1} = [0, 0, 0]^T$:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0 + 0.5 \cdot \cos(0) \cdot 0.2 \\ 0 + 0.5 \cdot \sin(0) \cdot 0.2 \\ 0 + 0.1 \cdot 0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.0 \\ 0.02 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Motion Jacobian** at $\mu_{t-1}$:

$$
G_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v \sin(\mu_\theta) \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 & v \cos(\mu_\theta) \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Predicted covariance:**

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t
$$

First compute $G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T$:

$$
G_t \Sigma_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.2 & 0.01 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.2 & 0.01 \\ 0 & 0.01 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Add $R_t$:

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.22 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.22 & 0.01 \\ 0 & 0.01 & 0.11 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Mark split:* Predicted mean — 2 marks; Jacobian — 2 marks; Predicted covariance — 3 marks.

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / 14 Marks - Alternative Choice]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **concept of belief representation** in mobile robotics. Compare **single-hypothesis belief** with **multi-hypothesis belief** along the dimensions of: (i) computational complexity, (ii) ability to handle global localisation, (iii) recovery from divergence. *(7 marks)*  
> **(b)** Why does the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) represent a single-hypothesis belief? Explain the role of **Taylor series linearisation** in enforcing this single-hypothesis property, and discuss the consequences when the true posterior is **bimodal** (e.g., in a symmetric corridor). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q3(B)(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Belief representation** is the internal data structure a robot uses to encode its knowledge of its own state and/or the world, formally $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

**Comparison Table:**

| Dimension | Single-Hypothesis Belief | Multi-Hypothesis Belief |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Computational Complexity | **Low** — O($n^2$) per step; closed-form | **High** — O($N \cdot n^2$) for $N$ hypotheses/particles |
| (ii) Global Localisation | **Cannot** solve — only one estimate | **Can** solve — multiple hypotheses span the pose space |
| (iii) Recovery from Divergence | **Cannot** — committed to one mode | **Yes** — surviving hypotheses can take over |

*Mark split:* Belief definition — 1 mark; Comparison table — 5 marks (≈2 per dimension); Conclusion — 1 mark.

---

**Model Solution for Q3(B)(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Why EKF = Single-Hypothesis Belief:** The EKF assumes the belief at every time step is a **single multivariate Gaussian** $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$. The Bayes recursion is closed under this assumption **only** when the posterior remains Gaussian, which requires the motion and measurement models to be linear (or linearised).

**Role of Taylor Series Linearisation:** The motion model $g$ and measurement model $h$ are generally nonlinear. EKF uses **first-order Taylor expansion** around the current mean to obtain Jacobians $G_t$ and $H_t$:

$$
g(x) \approx g(\mu) + G(x - \mu), \quad h(x) \approx h(\bar{\mu}) + H(x - \bar{\mu})
$$

This linearisation ensures that a Gaussian input to $g$ (or $h$) produces a Gaussian output, **preserving the single-hypothesis (unimodal Gaussian) property** at every recursion.

**Consequence in Bimodal Cases (Symmetric Corridor):** In a symmetric corridor, the true posterior has two equally likely modes (left and right). The EKF, by linearising and combining Gaussians, produces **one averaged Gaussian centred between the two modes** — a *physically impossible* pose that lies in the wall. The second mode is permanently lost, and the robot diverges. This is the central failure mode that motivates the move to **multi-hypothesis (particle / Monte Carlo) filters**.

*Mark split:* EKF as single-hypothesis — 2 marks; Taylor linearisation role — 2 marks; Bimodal failure analysis — 3 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $\mu_t$ (the belief mean) with the true state $x_t$** — they are *different*. The belief is the robot's *estimate*; the true state is unknown in practice.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+ $R_t$" term** in the predicted covariance is the most common mark-losing error in derivation questions. Always state both the **propagated** term ($G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T$) and the **process noise** term ($R_t$).
> 3. **Sign and angle-unit errors** in the motion Jacobian (especially $\sin/\cos$ of $\mu_\theta$) cost 1–2 marks routinely. Compute Jacobians symbolically first, then substitute.
> 4. **Never** claim the EKF *exactly* solves the Bayes filter — it *approximates* it via linearisation. Examiners will deduct for this misstatement.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Single-hypothesis belief** = robot tracks **one** pose estimate, encoded as a single Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$.
- The **mean** $\mu_t$ is the *best guess*; the **covariance** $\Sigma_t$ is the *uncertainty* around that guess.
- **Recursive update** has two stages: **Prediction** (motion) and **Correction** (measurement), governed by the **Kalman gain** $K_t$.
- $K_t$ weights *prediction* vs. *measurement* — large $Q_t$ means trust prediction, small $Q_t$ means trust measurement.
- **Process noise $R_t$** causes uncertainty to *grow* in the prediction step; **measurement noise $Q_t$** causes uncertainty to *shrink* in the update step.
- **Linearisation** via **Taylor series (Jacobians $G_t, H_t$)** is what allows EKF to keep a Gaussian closed under the recursion.
- **Strengths:** computationally cheap, simple, well-suited to *local tracking*.
- **Weaknesses:** **cannot represent ambiguity** (bimodal/multimodal posteriors), **cannot solve global localisation**, **cannot recover from divergence**.
- **KTU must-know:** Single-hypothesis = **EKF localisation**; Multi-hypothesis = **Particle filter / MCL** (covered in Module 3).
- **Exam tip:** Memorise the five core equations ($\bar{\mu}_t$, $\bar{\Sigma}_t$, $K_t$, $\mu_t$, $\Sigma_t$) — they appear verbatim in nearly every KTU Part B question on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple-Hypothesis Belief
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Multiple-Hypothesis Belief # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of the Bayes filter and robot localization, a **Multiple-Hypothesis Belief** is a probabilistic representation of the robot's state (typically its pose $x_t$) that explicitly maintains **more than one concurrent, mutually exclusive hypothesis** about where the robot is located. Unlike a single-hypothesis (unimodal) Gaussian belief, the posterior is a *mixture distribution* (or a clustered set of particles) where each component represents a distinct, plausible pose estimate. Formally, a continuous multi-hypothesis belief at time $t$ is written as: $$bel(x_t) \;\triangleq\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} w_t^{(i)}\,\mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\; \mu_t^{(i)}, \Sigma_t^{(i)}\right)$$ where $N_t$ is the (dynamic) number of active hypotheses, $w_t^{(i)}$ is the importance weight of the $i$-th hypothesis, $\mu_t^{(i)}$ is its mean pose, and $\Sigma_t^{(i)}$ is its covariance. A corresponding discrete variant stores a finite set of labeled track hypotheses $\mathcal{H}_t = \{h_t^{(1)}, h_t^{(2)}, \dots, h_t^{(N_t)}\}$ with associated existence probabilities. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Hook:** This concept directly supports the Module 2 outcome of "applying probabilistic methods to handle ambiguity in robot localization." Examiners frequently test this when asking *why* a single Gaussian is insufficient. ## 1.2 Why a Single Hypothesis Fails (Motivation) A single-Gaussian Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) belief cannot represent **multi-modal posteriors**. This fails in three canonical KTU-listed scenarios: 1. **Global localization ("kidnapped robot")** — the robot is placed somewhere unknown; the prior over the entire map is uniform or broadly multi-modal. 2. **Perceptual aliasing (symmetric environments)** — identical corridors, doorways, or pillars make many distinct poses equally consistent with a single observation. 3. **Localization failure / recovery** — when tracking is momentarily lost, multiple "best guesses" must be kept alive until disambiguating evidence arrives. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric (KTU reference):** A hypothesis is deemed *active* only if its weight $w_t^{(i)} \geq w_{\min} = \mathbf{0.01}$ (i.e., **1%** of total belief mass). Hypotheses falling below this threshold are pruned. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Building After a Power Cut" Imagine you are a security guard in a **perfectly symmetric office building**: every floor looks identical, every door is the same. The lights go out, and when they flicker back on, you have **no idea which floor or which end of the corridor you are on**. Your brain does *not* commit to "I am on Floor 3, East wing" — instead, it maintains a small **mental list of candidate locations**, each tagged with a "plausibility" score: - Floor 3, East — plausibility 0.4 - Floor 3, West — plausibility 0.4 - Floor 5, Center — plausibility 0.2 As you walk and open doors, you **update the plausibility** of each. A floor number painted on a wall might *kill* two hypotheses at once and *boost* a third. This mental list — discrete, labeled, with weights that evolve over time — is exactly a **Multiple-Hypothesis Belief**. ## 1.4 Historical and Engineering Context - **Reid (1979)** introduced the Multiple Hypothesis Tracker (MHT) for radar tracking. - The robotics community adopted the **Multi-Hypothesis Kalman Filter (MHKF)** and later **particle-filter-based multi-hypothesis tracking** (e.g., the work integrated into the *Probabilistic Robotics* framework by Thrun, Burgard, & Fox). - Modern SLAM stacks (e.g., **Montreal SLAM**, **Cartographer's branch-and-bound loop closure**) are essentially industrial descendants of multi-hypothesis belief maintenance. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Heuristic:** If a question mentions *"symmetric environment"* or *"kidnapped robot"*, the expected answer almost always invokes multiple-hypothesis belief, *not* a single EKF. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Logical Decomposition of the Algorithm The Multi-Hypothesis Belief is maintained through a **per-hypothesis Kalman update** plus a **weight update** step. Below is the operational logic as a KTU student should reproduce it. 1. **Hypothesis Set Initialization** — Create $N_0$ hypotheses with priors $w_0^{(i)}, \mu_0^{(i)}, \Sigma_0^{(i)}$. In global localization, this is a uniform mixture over the free space of the map. 2. **Prediction (per hypothesis)** — Propagate each Gaussian through the motion model $u_t$: $$\bar{\mu}_t^{(i)} = g(\mu_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$$ $$\bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} = G_t \Sigma_{t-1}^{(i)} G_t^{\top} + R_t$$ 3. **Measurement Update (per hypothesis)** — Compute the innovation $\nu_t^{(i)} = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t^{(i)})$ and innovation covariance $S_t^{(i)} = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} H_t^{\top} + Q_t$. 4. **Likelihood Computation** — Evaluate the per-hypothesis likelihood: $$\Lambda_t^{(i)} = \eta_t \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(\nu_t^{(i)})^{\top}(S_t^{(i)})^{-1}\nu_t^{(i)}\right)$$ with $\eta_t$ the measurement normalization constant. 5. **Weight Update** — Multiply by the prior weight: $$w_t^{(i)} = \eta \cdot w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot \Lambda_t^{(i)}$$ 6. **Hypothesis Management** — Normalize weights; prune below $w_{\min}$; merge hypotheses closer than a Mahalanobis threshold; spawn new hypotheses from unassigned measurements. 7. **Output** — Publish either the MAP hypothesis or the full mixture depending on task. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step | Step | Why it matters (KTU-style) | |---|---| | Per-hypothesis prediction | Each candidate pose evolves independently under motion — collapsing them would discard information. | | Likelihood $\Lambda_t^{(i)}$ | Encodes *how well* a hypothesis explains the new measurement; Mahalanobis distance penalizes implausible matches. | | Weight multiplication | Combines prior belief with new evidence via Bayes' rule, in log-domain for numerical stability. | | Pruning | Controls computational cost; avoids belief-mass dilution across stale candidates. | | Merging | Prevents redundant tracks representing the same physical pose. | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` (not `\|`) when writing the vertical bar inside the table — the markdown parser will break otherwise. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Multi-hypothesis belief | $bel(x_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} w_t^{(i)} \mathcal{N}(x_t; \mu_t^{(i)}, \Sigma_t^{(i)})$ | $\sum_i w_t^{(i)} = 1$ | | 2 | Predicted mean | $\bar{\mu}_t^{(i)} = g(\mu_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$ | SI: $\mathbf{m}$ and $\mathbf{rad}$ | | 3 | Predicted covariance | $\bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} = G_t \Sigma_{t-1}^{(i)} G_t^{\top} + R_t$ | $R_t$ is process noise covariance | | 4 | Innovation | $\nu_t^{(i)} = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t^{(i)})$ | Same units as $z_t$ | | 5 | Innovation covariance | $S_t^{(i)} = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} H_t^{\top} + Q_t$ | $Q_t$ is measurement noise covariance | | 6 | Kalman gain | $K_t^{(i)} = \bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} H_t^{\top} (S_t^{(i)})^{-1}$ | Dimensionless matrix | | 7 | Posterior mean | $\mu_t^{(i)} = \bar{\mu}_t^{(i)} + K_t^{(i)} \nu_t^{(i)}$ | SI: $\mathbf{m}$, $\mathbf{rad}$ | | 8 | Posterior covariance | $\Sigma_t^{(i)} = (I - K_t^{(i)} H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)}$ | Symmetric PD matrix | | 9 | Likelihood | $\Lambda_t^{(i)} = \det(2\pi S_t^{(i)})^{-1/2} \exp\!\big(-\tfrac{1}{2}(\nu_t^{(i)})^{\top}(S_t^{(i)})^{-1}\nu_t^{(i)}\big)$ | Probability density | | 10 | Weight update | $w_t^{(i)} = \eta \cdot w_{t-1^{(i)}} \cdot \Lambda_t^{(i)}$ | Normalize via $\eta = \big(\sum_j w_t^{(j)}\big)^{-1}$ | | 11 | Mahalanobis distance | $d_M^{(i)} = \sqrt{(\nu_t^{(i)})^{\top}(S_t^{(i)})^{-1}\nu_t^{(i)}}$ | Threshold for merging: $d_M < \mathbf{3.0}$ | | 12 | Pruning threshold | $w_{\min} = \mathbf{0.01}$ | Hypothesis removed if $w_t^{(i)} < w_{\min}$ | | 13 | Existence probability (MHT) | $r_t^{(i)} = r_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot (1 - P_d P_c)$ | $P_d$ = detection prob., $P_c$ = clutter prob. | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Autonomous Vehicles:** Maintains multiple lane/position hypotheses when lane markings are occluded. - **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva, Locus):** Re-localizes after LIDAR degradation by keeping top-$k$ candidate maps. - **Aerial Swarms:** Distinguishes between multiple agents using only ambiguous RF or vision cues. - **Spacecraft Docking:** Tracks both the real chaser and a "ghost" reflection during rendezvous maneuvers. - **Medical Robotics:** Capsular endoscopes navigating the GI tract face heavy perceptual aliasing — multi-hypothesis is standard. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world constraint:** Computational cost grows as $\mathcal{O}(N_t \cdot d^3)$ where $d$ is pose dimension (typically 3 for 2-D pose $(x, y, \theta)$). At $N_t = 100$ hypotheses, this is acceptable; at $N_t > 1000$, the community switches to **particle filter** or **Rao-Blackwellized** variants. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Single Bayes Filter to Multi-Hypothesis Posterior We begin from the standard recursive Bayes filter for localization: $$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute the multi-hypothesis prior.** Assume $bel(x_{t-1}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{t-1}} w_{t-1}^{(i)} \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_{t-1}^{(i)}, \Sigma_{t-1}^{(i)})$. **Step 2 — Linearize the motion model** (required for the Gaussian assumption to remain valid). Let $g(x, u) \approx g(\mu, u) + G_t (x - \mu)$ with $G_t = \partial g / \partial x \vert_{\mu, u}$. Then for a single component $i$: $$\int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_{t-1}^{(i)}, \Sigma_{t-1}^{(i)})\,dx_{t-1} = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \bar{\mu}_t^{(i)}, \bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)})$$ where $$\bar{\mu}_t^{(i)} = g(\mu_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$$ and $$\bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} = G_t \Sigma_{t-1}^{(i)} G_t^{\top} + R_t$$ The transition is a *linear-Gaussian system*, so a Gaussian prior plus a linear transition yields a Gaussian marginal — this is why the mixture structure is preserved componentwise. **Step 3 — Apply the measurement update per hypothesis.** $$\Lambda_t^{(i)} \;\triangleq\; p(z_t \mid \bar{\mu}_t^{(i)}) = \eta_t \exp\!\Big(-\tfrac{1}{2}(\nu_t^{(i)})^{\top}(S_t^{(i)})^{-1}\nu_t^{(i)}\Big)$$ with $$\nu_t^{(i)} = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t^{(i)}), \qquad S_t^{(i)} = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} H_t^{\top} + Q_t$$ **Step 4 — Posterior is also a mixture (componentwise product of Gaussians).** $$bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta \sum_{i=1}^{N_{t-1}} w_{t-1}^{(i)} \Lambda_t^{(i)} \mathcal{N}\!\big(x_t; \mu_t^{(i)}, \Sigma_t^{(i)}\big)$$ with $$\mu_t^{(i)} = \bar{\mu}_t^{(i)} + K_t^{(i)} \nu_t^{(i)}$$ $$\Sigma_t^{(i)} = (I - K_t^{(i)} H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)}$$ $$K_t^{(i)} = \bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} H_t^{\top} (S_t^{(i)})^{-1}$$ **Step 5 — Renormalize the weights.** $$w_t^{(i)} = \frac{w_{t-1}^{(i)} \Lambda_t^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N_{t-1}} w_{t-1}^{(j)} \Lambda_t^{(j)}}$$ The derivation above shows the **closure property**: a Gaussian mixture prior, propagated through a linear(ized) model, yields a Gaussian mixture posterior — the multi-hypothesis structure is *preserved* across time, which is the mathematical reason multi-hypothesis tracking is computationally tractable. ## 3.2 Derivation: Mahalanobis Merge Criterion Two hypotheses $i, j$ are deemed *redundant* if their distributions are statistically indistinguishable. The squared Mahalanobis distance between their means, weighted by the pooled covariance, is: $$D_{ij}^{2} = (\mu_t^{(i)} - \mu_t^{(j)})^{\top} (\Sigma_t^{(i)} + \Sigma_t^{(j)})^{-1} (\mu_t^{(i)} - \mu_t^{(j)})$$ If $D_{ij}^{2} < \chi^{2}_{d,\alpha}$ (the chi-square threshold for $d$ degrees of freedom at significance $\alpha$), the hypotheses are merged: $$w_t^{\text{merged}} = w_t^{(i)} + w_t^{(j)}$$ $$\mu_t^{\text{merged}} = \frac{w_t^{(i)} \mu_t^{(i)} + w_t^{(j)} \mu_t^{(j)}}{w_t^{(i)} + w_t^{(j)}}$$ $$\Sigma_t^{\text{merged}} = \frac{w_t^{(i)} \Sigma_t^{(i)} + w_t^{(j)} \Sigma_t^{(j)}}{w_t^{(i)} + w_t^{(j)}}$$ This is the standard *covariance intersection* approximation when covariances are not identical. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Mini-Case) A 1-D robot in a symmetric corridor starts with two equal hypotheses: $\mu_0^{(1)} = 2.0\,\text{m}$, $\mu_0^{(2)} = -2.0\,\text{m}$, both with $\Sigma_0 = 0.5$ and $w_0 = 0.5$. It moves $u = 1.0\,\text{m}$ with $R = 0.1$ and reads a wall at $z = 3.1\,\text{m}$ with $Q = 0.2$ and $H = 1$. **Step 1 — Prediction.** $$\bar{\mu}_1^{(1)} = 2.0 + 1.0 = 3.0, \quad \bar{\mu}_1^{(2)} = -2.0 + 1.0 = -1.0$$ $$\bar{\Sigma}_1^{(1)} = \bar{\Sigma}_1^{(2)} = 0.5 + 0.1 = 0.6$$ **Step 2 — Innovation.** $$\nu_1^{(1)} = 3.1 - 3.0 = 0.1, \quad \nu_1^{(2)} = 3.1 - (-1.0) = 4.1$$ **Step 3 — Innovation covariance.** $$S_1^{(1)} = S_1^{(2)} = 0.6 + 0.2 = 0.8$$ **Step 4 — Likelihood.** $$\Lambda_1^{(1)} \propto \exp(-0.5 \cdot 0.1^2 / 0.8) = \exp(-0.00625) \approx 0.9938$$ $$\Lambda_1^{(2)} \propto \exp(-0.5 \cdot 4.1^2 / 0.8) = \exp(-10.506) \approx 2.74 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 5 — Normalized weights.** $$\eta = \frac{1}{0.5 \cdot 0.9938 + 0.5 \cdot 2.74 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 2.0126$$ $$w_1^{(1)} \approx 0.9999, \quad w_1^{(2)} \approx 2.76 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Interpretation:** Hypothesis 2 is effectively killed (weight $\ll 0.01$) after **a single measurement** — illustrating how a disambiguating observation collapses the multi-hypothesis belief to a unimodal one. This is the desired behavior in symmetric environments. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level System Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sensor Input z_t] --> B[Measurement Gate
Mahalanobis test] C[Odometry Input u_t] --> D[Per-Hypothesis Predictor
Maintain N_t EKF branches] D --> E[Predicted Beliefs
mu_bar, Sigma_bar per hypothesis] E --> F[Innovation Calculator
nu, S per hypothesis] B --> F F --> G[Likelihood Evaluator
Lambda per hypothesis] G --> H[Weight Updater
w_t = eta * w_t-1 * Lambda] H --> I[Normalizer and Pruner
Drop w less than 0.01] I --> J{Hypothesis Manager} J --> K[Merge Close Hypotheses
D_ij under threshold] J --> L[Spawn New Hypotheses
From unassigned measurements] J --> M[Birth and Death Tracker
Update existence probs] M --> N[Output Belief
Mixture or MAP estimate] K --> N L --> N N --> O[Downstream Tasks
Navigation, Mapping, Control] ``` ## 4.2 Multi-Hypothesis Update Flow (Per Hypothesis) ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[Hypothesis i at t-1
mu, Sigma, w] --> B0[Apply motion model g] B0 --> C0[Linearize to get G] C0 --> D0[Predicted mu_bar, Sigma_bar] D0 --> E0[Predict measurement
z_pred = h of mu_bar] A1[New measurement z_t] --> F0[Innovation nu = z - z_pred] E0 --> F0 F0 --> G0[Innovation cov S = H Sigma_bar H^T + Q] G0 --> H0[Kalman gain K = Sigma_bar H^T S^-1] H0 --> I0[Posterior mu, Sigma update] I0 --> J0[Likelihood Lambda via Gaussian] A0 --> K0[Multiply weight w_t-1 * Lambda] J0 --> K0 K0 --> L0[Normalize across all hypotheses] L0 --> M0[Hypothesis i at t
Updated mu, Sigma, w] ``` ## 4.3 Hypothesis Lifecycle State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Tentative: New measurement spawn Tentative --> Active: Existence prob greater than 0.5 Tentative --> Pruned: Existence prob less than 0.1 for 3 frames Active --> Active: Weight stays above w_min Active --> Coalesced: Merged with neighbour D_ij under 3 Active --> Pruned: Weight falls below w_min Coalesced --> Active: Survives merge Pruned --> [*] Active --> [*]: Task complete or robot localised ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading guide for KTU students:** The first diagram is the *system-level view* (what a board examiner expects in a 7-mark "explain the architecture" question). The second is the *per-hypothesis Kalman update* (the heart of the algorithm). The third is the *hypothesis management lifecycle* — frequently asked as a 3-mark "explain pruning" question. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define "Multiple-Hypothesis Belief" in the context of mobile robot localization. State two scenarios where it is preferred over a single-Gaussian EKF belief.** **Model Answer:** A *Multiple-Hypothesis Belief* is a probabilistic posterior over the robot pose represented as a mixture of multiple Gaussian components (or multiple particle clusters), each tagged with a weight, expressing that the robot may be in one of several distinct locations simultaneously. Two scenarios: 1. **Global / Kidnapped-Robot Localization** — the initial pose is completely unknown, so the belief must encode many plausible locations. 2. **Symmetric Environments** — perceptually aliased places produce multiple pose interpretations that are equally consistent with sensor data; collapsing to a single Gaussian would lose valid hypotheses. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often write *"EKF fails because of nonlinearity"* — that is true but **not** what justifies a multi-hypothesis belief. The examiner wants *perceptual ambiguity / symmetry / global localization* as the reason. ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: With a suitable expression, explain the role of the pruning threshold $w_{\min}$ in multi-hypothesis tracking.** **Model Answer:** The pruning threshold $w_{\min}$ is a minimum-belief-mass cut-off (commonly set to $\mathbf{0.01}$) below which a hypothesis is removed from the active set: $$w_t^{(i)} < w_{\min} \;\Longrightarrow\; h_t^{(i)} \text{ is pruned}$$ Its role is **computational tractability**: as time progresses, new measurements can spawn additional hypotheses (birth step) and the count $N_t$ may grow unboundedly. By pruning low-weight hypotheses, the algorithm keeps the *most plausible* candidates, ensuring the per-step cost of $\mathcal{O}(N_t \cdot d^3)$ remains bounded while still preserving the dominant modes of the posterior. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Do **not** state that pruning "improves accuracy" — it reduces cost. Accuracy is governed by the threshold value, not the act of pruning itself. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` **(a) Derive the multi-hypothesis belief update equation starting from the recursive Bayes filter, clearly identifying the role of (i) the per-hypothesis Kalman gain and (ii) the weight renormalization step. (7 marks)** **(b) With a block diagram and a worked 1-D numerical example, demonstrate how two symmetric hypotheses are disambiguated by a single disambiguating measurement, including weight evolution and the use of the Mahalanobis merge criterion. (7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a):** Starting from the Bayes filter recursion, $$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$$ **Step 1 — Mixture substitution.** Insert the multi-hypothesis prior: $$bel(x_{t-1}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{t-1}} w_{t-1}^{(i)} \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_{t-1}^{(i)}, \Sigma_{t-1}^{(i)})$$ **Step 2 — Per-hypothesis prediction.** Because each component, propagated through a linear(ized) motion model $g$, yields a Gaussian with parameters: $$\bar{\mu}_t^{(i)} = g(\mu_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t), \quad \bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} = G_t \Sigma_{t-1}^{(i)} G_t^{\top} + R_t$$ **Step 3 — Per-hypothesis measurement update.** Define innovation $\nu_t^{(i)} = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t^{(i)})$ and innovation covariance $S_t^{(i)} = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} H_t^{\top} + Q_t$. The Kalman gain, $$K_t^{(i)} = \bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} H_t^{\top} (S_t^{(i)})^{-1}$$ *linearly combines* the prior mean and the innovation to yield the posterior mean $\mu_t^{(i)} = \bar{\mu}_t^{(i)} + K_t^{(i)} \nu_t^{(i)}$, with posterior covariance $\Sigma_t^{(i)} = (I - K_t^{(i)} H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)}$. **Step 4 — Likelihood and weight update.** The Gaussian likelihood $$\Lambda_t^{(i)} = \det(2\pi S_t^{(i)})^{-1/2} \exp\!\big(-\tfrac{1}{2}(\nu_t^{(i)})^{\top}(S_t^{(i)})^{-1}\nu_t^{(i)}\big)$$ is then multiplied with the prior weight, and the entire collection is renormalized: $$w_t^{(i)} = \frac{w_{t-1}^{(i)} \Lambda_t^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N_{t-1}} w_{t-1}^{(j)} \Lambda_t^{(j)}}$$ **Step 5 — Posterior mixture.** $$bel(x_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{t-1}} w_t^{(i)} \mathcal{N}\!\big(x_t; \mu_t^{(i)}, \Sigma_t^{(i)}\big)$$ (i) The per-hypothesis Kalman gain $K_t^{(i)}$ **fuses motion prior with measurement evidence** to refine each Gaussian's mean and covariance. (ii) The weight-renormalization step **redistributes belief mass** according to how well each hypothesis explains the new measurement, thereby either promoting or suppressing hypotheses. **Model Solution — Part (b):** The block diagram is the **Per-Hypothesis Update Flow** shown in Section 4.2. The numerical 1-D example in Section 3.3 demonstrates: - Two initial hypotheses at $\mu = \pm 2.0\,\text{m}$ with equal weights $w = 0.5$. - After $u = 1.0\,\text{m}$ motion: predicted means $\bar{\mu}^{(1)} = 3.0$, $\bar{\mu}^{(2)} = -1.0$. - A wall measurement at $z = 3.1\,\text{m}$ produces innovations $\nu^{(1)} = 0.1$ and $\nu^{(2)} = 4.1$. - Likelihoods: $\Lambda^{(1)} \approx 0.994$, $\Lambda^{(2)} \approx 2.74 \times 10^{-5}$. - Normalized weights: $w^{(1)} \approx 0.9999$, $w^{(2)} \approx 2.76 \times 10^{-5}$.
