The Mahalanobis distance between the two posterior means, using the pooled covariance, is: $$D_{12}^{2} = \frac{(\mu^{(1)} - \mu^{(2)})^{2}}{\Sigma^{(1)} + \Sigma^{(2)}} = \frac{(3.09 - (-0.94))^{2}}{0.6 + 0.6} \approx \frac{16.24}{1.2} \approx 13.5$$ Since $D_{12}^{2} = 13.5 \gg \chi^{2}_{1, 0.99} = 6.63$, the hypotheses are **not merged** at this stage. After the next measurement (or after $w^{(2)}$ falls below $0.01$), hypothesis 2 is pruned — a clean demonstration of the disambiguation pipeline. --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a) Compare single-hypothesis EKF localization with multi-hypothesis Kalman filter (MHKF) localization along the dimensions of: (i) belief representation, (ii) handling of perceptual aliasing, (iii) computational complexity, and (iv) recovery from localization failure. (7 marks)** **(b) Explain the operations of hypothesis birth, hypothesis merging, and hypothesis pruning with their respective mathematical criteria. State the typical numerical thresholds used in mobile robotics. (7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a):** | Dimension | Single-Hypothesis EKF | Multi-Hypothesis Kalman Filter (MHKF) | |---|---|---| | (i) Belief representation | One Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(x; \mu, \Sigma)$ | Mixture $\sum_i w_i \mathcal{N}(x; \mu_i, \Sigma_i)$ | | (ii) Perceptual aliasing | Collapses to one mode — can latch onto the wrong pose | Maintains all consistent modes; survives aliasing | | (iii) Computational complexity | $\mathcal{O}(d^3)$ per step | $\mathcal{O}(N_t \cdot d^3)$ per step, $N_t$ = #hypotheses | | (iv) Recovery from failure | Must be re-initialized externally (kidnap recovery) | Spawns new hypotheses automatically from unassigned measurements | **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Birth** — A new hypothesis is created for each measurement $z_t$ that is **not explained** by any existing hypothesis within a Mahalanobis gate $d_M < d_{\text{gate}}$ (typically $d_{\text{gate}} = \mathbf{5.0}$). The new hypothesis is initialized at $h(z_t)$ (the inverse sensor model) with covariance equal to the measurement covariance $Q_t$ and a small initial weight $w_{\text{birth}} = \mathbf{0.05}$. **Merging** — Two hypotheses $i, j$ with pooled distance $$D_{ij}^{2} = (\mu_i - \mu_j)^{\top}(\Sigma_i + \Sigma_j)^{-1}(\mu_i - \mu_j) < \chi^{2}_{d, \alpha}$$ (with $\alpha = 0.99$, i.e. $\chi^{2}_{3, 0.99} = \mathbf{11.34}$ in 2-D pose) are statistically indistinguishable and are replaced by a single weighted-average hypothesis: $$w_{\text{new}} = w_i + w_j, \quad \mu_{\text{new}} = \frac{w_i \mu_i + w_j \mu_j}{w_i + w_j}$$ **Pruning** — A hypothesis is removed if its normalized weight falls below the threshold: $$w_t^{(i)} < w_{\min} = \mathbf{0.01}$$ or, in MHT formulations, if its existence probability $r_t^{(i)} < 0.1$ for **3 consecutive frames**. Pruning bounds the hypothesis count and prevents belief-mass dilution across stale candidates. > [!WARNING] > **Common 14-mark Pitfalls (KTU Examiner's Pattern):** > 1. Writing $\Sigma$ in the wrong place (predicting with $R$ instead of adding $R$). > 2. Confusing *birth* (from unassigned measurement) with *initialization* (at $t=0$). > 3. Forgetting to **renormalize** the weights after the update — leading to $\sum w \neq 1$. > 4. Citing the wrong chi-square value: 2-D pose uses $d = 3$ (x, y, $\theta$), not $d = 2$. > 5. Skipping the **linearization** step in the derivation — KTU deducts marks for treating the motion model as linear without justification. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print this before the exam):** - A *Multiple-Hypothesis Belief* is a **Gaussian mixture posterior**; each component is a *pose candidate*. - Total hypothesis weight is **always normalized to 1**: $\sum_i w_t^{(i)} = 1$. - The two update steps are **(i) per-hypothesis Kalman update** and **(ii) weight multiplication + renormalization**. - The mixture structure is preserved through linear(ized) motion — *closure property*. - **Birth** is triggered by an *unassigned* measurement; **pruning** drops $w < \mathbf{0.01}$; **merging** uses Mahalanobis $D_{ij}^{2} < \chi^{2}_{d, 0.99}$. - Kalman gain for hypothesis $i$: $K_t^{(i)} = \bar{\Sigma}_t^{(i)} H_t^{\top} (S_t^{(i)})^{-1}$. - Posterior mean for hypothesis $i$: $\mu_t^{(i)} = \bar{\mu}_t^{(i)} + K_t^{(i)} \nu_t^{(i)}$. - Weight update: $w_t^{(i)} = \eta \cdot w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot \Lambda_t^{(i)}$ with $\eta$ as the normalizer. - **Computational cost** scales as $\mathcal{O}(N_t \cdot d^3)$ where $d = 3$ for 2-D pose. - The technique is **mandatory** for global localization, symmetric environments, and post-failure recovery. - For 2-D mobile robots, use $\chi^{2}_{3, 0.99} = \mathbf{11.34}$ as the merge threshold. - The Mahalanobis gate for measurement-to-hypothesis association is typically $d_{\text{gate}} = \mathbf{5.0}$. - The **disambiguating power** of a single measurement can collapse a symmetric multi-modal belief to near-unimodal in one step — quantify via likelihood ratio $\Lambda^{(i)} / \Lambda^{(j)}$. - Closely related algorithms: **MHT** (Reid, 1979), **JPDA** (Joint Probabilistic Data Association), **Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter** for SLAM. - Always **linearize** $g(\cdot)$ and $h(\cdot)$ before applying the Kalman update — KTU examiners specifically look for $G_t$ and $H_t$.

topics.title: Differences Between Markov and Kalman Localization (Detailed Explanation Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differences Between Markov and Kalman Localization (Detailed Explanation Not Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Localization Paradigms: Markov vs. Kalman

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Markov Localization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Markov Localization** is a **probabilistic, recursive state-estimation technique** in which the robot's pose belief $bel(x_t)$ is propagated through the environment using the **Markov assumption** (i.e., the future state is conditionally independent of the past given the present state). The belief is represented as an **arbitrary probability distribution** over the entire state space, allowing it to handle **multi-modal, ambiguous, or globally uncertain situations**.

Mathematically, the recursive belief update is:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

where $\eta$ is the normalization constant, $z_t$ is the measurement, $u_t$ is the control input, and $x_t$ is the robot pose.

### 1.2 Kalman (EKF) Localization — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kalman Localization** is a specialization of Bayesian filtering that approximates the robot's belief by a **single multivariate Gaussian distribution** $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$, where $\mu_t$ is the mean pose estimate and $\Sigma_t$ is the covariance matrix. The **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** linearizes the non-linear motion and measurement models using **first-order Taylor expansion (Jacobians)**, making the filter tractable for continuous state spaces.

The belief is fully characterized as:

$$bel(x_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Finding a Friend in a Mall:**
> - **Markov Localization** behaves like a person who *knows they could be on any floor of the mall* and maintains a *mental map of probabilities for every floor* (a multi-modal belief). If they hear a familiar song, they up-weight that floor; if they don't, they down-weight it. This works even if they are utterly lost.
> - **Kalman Localization** behaves like a person who *is already certain they are on the 3rd floor near the food court* and only refines that belief by **narrowing the uncertainty radius** with every observation — a *single, unimodal, Gaussian-shaped* confidence region. If the friend is actually on the 5th floor, this person will never find them because their single-hypothesis belief cannot recover.

**Key Constants / Metrics Referenced:**
- Standard deviation: typically **$\sigma \in [0.01, 5.0]$ m** for indoor odometry noise.
- Process noise covariance $Q$ and measurement noise covariance $R$ are the **tuning knobs** of the EKF.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown — Markov Localization

The Markov approach decomposes localization into two recursive steps:

1. **Prediction Step (Motion Update):** Propagate belief forward using the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$.
   - The integral term $\int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ **convolves** the previous belief with the motion model.
2. **Measurement Update (Correction Step):** Re-weight the predicted belief using the perceptual model $p(z_t \mid x_t)$.
   - Multiplication by $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ sharpens ambiguous regions.
   - The normalizer $\eta$ rescales so the total belief sums to 1.

**Variants of Markov Localization:**
- **Grid-based:** Discrete grid cells store probabilities; computationally heavy.
- **Topological:** Discrete nodes represent places; efficient for large spaces.
- **Monte Carlo (Particle Filter):** Samples represent the belief — most widely used in practice.

## 2.2 Structural Breakdown — Kalman (EKF) Localization

The EKF performs two steps per iteration:

1. **Prediction:** Propagate $\mu$ and $\Sigma$ using linearized motion model $A_t$ and process noise $Q_t$.
2. **Update:** Compute the **Kalman Gain** $K_t$ using the linearized measurement model $H_t$ and measurement noise $R_t$, then correct the estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Why** behind linearization: real robots have non-linear kinematics (e.g., $\sin\theta$, $\cos\theta$). The EKF approximates these with Jacobians evaluated at the current mean, allowing the *linear* Kalman equations to be applied iteratively.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | Highway lane tracking (EKF) vs. full GPS-denied recovery (Markov/MCL) |
| Warehouse AMRs | Position tracking on predefined paths (EKF); global recovery after docking errors (MCL) |
| Underwater AUVs | Compass + DVL fusion via EKF; terrain-aided navigation via particle filters |
| Domestic Vacuum Robots | Markov localization on occupancy grids for global relocalization |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Markov belief (continuous) | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | $\eta$ is dimensionless normalizer |
| 2 | Markov belief (discrete grid) | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \sum_{x_{t-1}} p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1})$ | Used in grid-based variants |
| 3 | EKF prediction — mean | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ | $g(\cdot)$ is non-linear motion model |
| 4 | EKF prediction — covariance | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^T + Q_t$ | $G_t$ is Jacobian of $g$ w.r.t. $x$ |
| 5 | EKF Kalman gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, (H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$ | Dimensionless matrix |
| 6 | EKF update — mean | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$ | $h(\cdot)$ is measurement model |
| 7 | EKF update — covariance | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t \, H_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Always positive semi-definite |
| 8 | Belief shape (Kalman) | $bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_t \mid \mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ | Gaussian; unimodal |
| 9 | Belief shape (Markov) | Arbitrary distribution over $X$ | Multi-modal allowed |
| 10 | Complexity (Markov, grid) | $O(\vert X \vert)$ per step | $\vert X \vert$ is number of grid cells |
| 11 | Complexity (EKF) | $O(n^3)$ per step | $n$ = state dimension (typically 3) |
| 12 | Recovery from failure | Markov = **Yes**; EKF = **No** | Critical exam point |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Comparison Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Recursive Belief — Markov Form

The derivation starts from Bayes' rule and the theorem of total probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
bel(x_t) &= p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \\[4pt]
&= \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \\[4pt]
&= \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot p(x_t \mid u_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \quad \text{(Markov assumption on measurements)} \\[4pt]
&= \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} \\[4pt]
&= \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each transition:**
- Line 1 → 2: Apply Bayes' rule with $\eta$ as the normalizer.
- Line 2 → 3: Apply the **Markov assumption** — given $x_t$, the measurement $z_t$ is independent of past measurements and controls.
- Line 3 → 4: Marginalize over all possible previous poses $x_{t-1}$ using the **law of total probability**.
- Line 4 → 5: Substitute $bel(x_{t-1}) = p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})$.

## 3.2 Deriving the EKF Update Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Predicted mean:}\quad \bar{\mu}_t &= g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) \\[4pt]
\text{Predicted covariance:}\quad \bar{\Sigma}_t &= G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^T + Q_t, \quad G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t} \\[4pt]
\text{Innovation:}\quad \nu_t &= z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t) \\[4pt]
\text{Innovation covariance:}\quad S_t &= H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + R_t, \quad H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t} \\[4pt]
\text{Kalman gain:}\quad K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1} \\[4pt]
\text{Updated mean:}\quad \mu_t &= \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, \nu_t \\[4pt]
\text{Updated covariance:}\quad \Sigma_t &= (I - K_t \, H_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each transition:**
- The first two equations are the **prediction**, propagating state and uncertainty through the (linearized) motion model.
- The innovation $\nu_t$ measures the discrepancy between the predicted and actual measurement.
- $S_t$ is the total uncertainty in the innovation space (prior + sensor noise).
- $K_t$ optimally blends the prediction with the innovation — large $R_t$ (noisy sensor) reduces $K_t$, trusting the prediction more.
- The last two equations perform the **update**, fusing the prediction with the measurement.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Markov Localization | Kalman (EKF) Localization |
|---|---|---|
| **Belief representation** | Arbitrary distribution (any shape) | Single Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ |
| **Modality** | Multi-modal supported | Strictly **unimodal** |
| **State space** | Continuous **or** discrete (grids) | Continuous only |
| **Global localization** | **Supported** natively | Not supported (requires known initial pose) |
| **Kidnapped robot problem** | **Solved** | Cannot recover |
| **Computational cost** | High (grid: $O(\vert X \vert)$; MCL: $O(M)$) | Low: $O(n^3)$ for $n$-dim state |
| **Sensor noise model** | Any $p(z \mid x)$ | Gaussian, zero-mean |
| **Linearity requirement** | None | Requires linearization (Jacobians) |
| **Data association** | Naturally handles ambiguity | Sensitive to wrong correspondences |
| **Memory footprint** | High (entire grid) | Low (only $\mu$ and $\Sigma$) |
| **Theoretical optimality** | **Bayes-optimal** | Approximate (linearization error) |
| **Failure mode** | Slow convergence in large spaces | Divergence if non-linearity is strong |
| **Typical use case** | Global relocalization, ambiguous envs. | Local tracking, known initial pose |
| **Real-world example** | Roomba's grid-based localization | GPS-IMU fusion in drones |
| **Key assumption** | Markov property of state | Gaussian noise + linearizability |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Belief Propagation Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Robot State x_t-1]) --> belA[Previous Belief bel x_t-1]
    belA --> predA[Prediction: p x_t / u_t, x_t-1]
    predA --> convA[Convolve with Motion Model]
    convA --> belBarA[Predicted Belief bel bar x_t]
    belBarA --> measA[Apply Measurement Model p z_t / x_t]
    measA --> normA[Normalize using eta]
    normA --> belAnew[Updated Belief bel x_t - Arbitrary Shape]
    belAnew -->|Multi-modal possible| globalA[Supports Global Localization]
    belAnew -->|Kidnapped robot| recoverA[Recovers from Failure]

    startB([Robot State x_t-1]) --> muB[Mean mu_t-1]
    startB --> sigmaB[Covariance Sigma_t-1]
    muB --> gFunc[Non-linear Motion g]
    sigmaB --> jacB[Compute Jacobian G_t]
    gFunc --> muBar[Predicted Mean mu bar_t]
    jacB --> sigmaBar[Predicted Covariance Sigma bar_t]
    muBar --> hFunc[Non-linear Measurement h]
    hFunc --> innov[Innovation nu_t = z_t - h mu bar]
    sigmaBar --> kalmanG[Kalman Gain K_t = Sigma bar H^T S^-1]
    kalmanG --> muUpd[Updated Mean mu_t]
    innov --> muUpd
    sigmaBar --> sigmaUpd[Updated Covariance Sigma_t]
    muUpd --> gauss[Belief is Gaussian N mu, Sigma]
    sigmaUpd --> gauss
    gauss -->|Unimodal only| localB[Local Tracking Only]
    gauss -->|No recovery| failB[Cannot Recover from Global Failure]

    classDef markov fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1
    classDef kalman fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C
    class belA,predA,convA,belBarA,measA,normA,belAnew,globalA,recoverA markov
    class muB,sigmaB,gFunc,jacB,muBar,sigmaBar,hFunc,innov,kalmanG,muUpd,sigmaUpd,gauss,localB,failB kalman
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Which Localizer to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Is initial pose known?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Is environment ambiguous?}
    q1 -->|No| markovAns[Use Markov / Monte Carlo Localization]
    q2 -->|Yes| markovAns
    q2 -->|No| q3{Is real-time critical on embedded hardware?}
    q3 -->|Yes| kalmanAns[Use EKF Localization]
    q3 -->|No| hybridAns[Use Particle Filter or Hybrid Approach]
```

## 4.3 Belief Shape Evolution Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Step 0: Uniform over map] --> s2[Step 1: After 1 measurement - Sharp peaks]
    s2 --> s3[Step 2: After motion - Spread + multiple peaks]
    s3 --> s4[Step 3: After next measurement - Converges]

    note1[Markov: Multi-modal preserved at step 3]
    note2[EKF: Forced into single peak at step 3]

    s3 -.- note1
    s3 -.- note2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Markov localization and Kalman localization with respect to belief representation and ability to handle the kidnapped robot problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Belief representation (1.5 marks):** Markov localization represents the robot's belief as an *arbitrary probability distribution* over the state space and can be multi-modal, whereas Kalman (EKF) localization represents the belief as a *single Gaussian* $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$, which is strictly **unimodal**.
- **Kidnapped robot problem (1.5 marks):** Markov localization can **recover from global localization failure** because the belief may carry mass over the whole map; the robot re-localizes once it gathers new evidence. EKF localization **cannot recover** because a unimodal Gaussian will simply track the wrong estimate and never re-explore alternative hypotheses.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State any three differences between Markov and EKF localization in terms of computational complexity, linearity assumption, and sensor noise model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Computational complexity (1 mark):** Markov localization scales with the *size of the state space* $O(\vert X \vert)$ (grid) or number of particles $O(M)$, whereas EKF scales as $O(n^3)$ where $n$ is the state dimension (typically 3). EKF is therefore far cheaper per step.
2. **Linearity assumption (1 mark):** Markov localization places *no linearity requirement* on motion or measurement models. EKF linearizes non-linear models using *first-order Jacobians*, which is an approximation.
3. **Sensor noise model (1 mark):** Markov localization accepts *any* sensor model $p(z \mid x)$. EKF assumes *additive, zero-mean Gaussian* measurement noise with known covariance $R$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the recursive belief update equation of **Markov localization**. State the Markov assumption explicitly and explain its role in the derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **two-step (predict-update) cycle of EKF localization**. List the Jacobians involved and the role of the Kalman gain.

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The recursive belief update is:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

- **(2 marks)** **Markov assumption:** The future state is *conditionally independent of the past given the present*. Concretely, $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, x_{t-2}, \ldots) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$. This allows the integral to involve only $x_{t-1}$, not the full history.
- **(2 marks)** **Prediction step:** The integral $\int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ **convolves** the prior belief with the motion model — it propagates uncertainty forward through the kinematic model.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Update step:** Multiplication by $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ re-weights the predicted belief using the current measurement; $\eta$ ensures the result is a valid probability distribution.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Significance:** The recursion makes localization **$O(1)$ memory** in time (only the previous belief is needed) and supports any distribution shape.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Predict step (2 marks):**
- $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$
- $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^T + Q_t$, where $G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\big\vert_{\mu_{t-1}}$

**Update step (2.5 marks):**
- Innovation: $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$
- Kalman gain: $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, (H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$, where $H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\big\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$
- $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, \nu_t$
- $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t \, H_t) \, \bar{\Sigma}_t$

**Role of Kalman gain (1.5 marks):** $K_t$ optimally blends prediction and measurement. When $R_t$ is large (noisy sensor), $K_t$ is small and the filter trusts the prediction. When $Q_t$ is large (uncertain motion), $K_t$ is large and the filter trusts the measurement.

**Jacobians (1 mark):** $G_t$ linearizes the motion model $g(\cdot)$; $H_t$ linearizes the measurement model $h(\cdot)$.

### **Question B — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Markov localization and EKF localization** under the following heads: (i) belief representation, (ii) handling of ambiguity, (iii) global localization capability, (iv) computational cost, (v) sensor noise assumption, (vi) optimality, (vii) real-time feasibility.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mobile robot is **kidnapped** during operation and placed in an unknown location. Which localization technique — Markov or EKF — would you recommend and why? Justify with a scenario.

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 marks (1 mark each):**

| # | Head | Markov | EKF |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Belief representation | Arbitrary distribution; multi-modal | Single Gaussian; unimodal |
| (ii) | Handling ambiguity | Naturally represents multiple hypotheses | Collapses to single mode — wrong if ambiguous |
| (iii) | Global localization | Natively supported | Requires known initial pose |
| (iv) | Computational cost | High — $O(\vert X \vert)$ or $O(M)$ | Low — $O(n^3)$ |
| (v) | Sensor noise | Any $p(z \mid x)$ | Zero-mean Gaussian |
| (vi) | Optimality | Bayes-optimal (exact) | Approximate due to linearization |
| (vii) | Real-time feasibility | Difficult on large grids | Excellent on embedded hardware |

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Recommendation: Markov / Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) (1 mark)**

**Justification (6 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** Upon kidnapping, the robot's true pose is completely unknown. The prior belief must therefore allow mass over the *entire* map — a multi-modal distribution.
- **(2 marks)** EKF cannot handle this because its Gaussian belief is centered at the *old* (now wrong) pose; the covariance may grow briefly but the filter has no mechanism to re-introduce hypotheses at distant locations. The robot will **permanently diverge**.
- **(2 marks)** Markov localization (specifically MCL) handles kidnapping elegantly: when the measurement likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ is low for *all* particles (low total belief), the algorithm triggers **random resampling**, injecting fresh particles across the map. The robot then re-localizes globally. Examples: museum tour guides, warehouse AMRs, and Roomba vacuums all use this paradigm.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in this topic (verified against KTU valuation key patterns):**
> 1. **Writing "Markov localization uses Bayes' theorem" only (½ mark).** Examiners expect the *full recursive equation* with $\eta$, the integral/sum, and the explicit Markov assumption. A bare statement loses 1–1.5 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Kalman localization with the basic Kalman Filter.** Students often write the linear KF equations (with $A$, $B$ matrices) and forget the **Jacobians $G_t$, $H_t$** — this costs 1–2 marks in Part B.
> 3. **Omitting the kidnapped robot discussion.** This is a **favourite 3-mark sub-question**. A vague answer like "Markov is better" scores 1; you must explicitly state that MCL *randomly resamples* particles across the map to recover.
> 4. **Stating EKF "cannot global localize" without reason.** The correct reason is the *unimodal Gaussian assumption*, not "EKF is bad." A precise answer citing $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ scores full marks.
> 5. **Mixing units or symbols.** $bel(x_t)$ and $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ must be written with proper notation; sloppy notation loses 0.5 mark per occurrence.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Markov vs. Kalman Localization**

- ⭐ **Markov localization** = general Bayes filter; belief is **arbitrary**; supports **multi-modal** beliefs; **globally localizes**; solves the **kidnapped robot problem** (via random particle injection in MCL).
- ⭐ **EKF localization** = special case assuming **Gaussian** belief; **unimodal**; requires **known initial pose**; **cannot recover** from global failure; cheaper at $O(n^3)$.
- ⭐ **Markov assumption:** $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, x_{t-2}, \ldots) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$ — the present summarizes the past.
- ⭐ **Recursive belief update:** $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$.
- ⭐ **EKF predict:** $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ and $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + Q_t$.
- ⭐ **EKF update:** $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$, then $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$.
- ⭐ **Jacobians:** $G_t = \partial g / \partial x$ and $H_t = \partial h / \partial x$ — first-order linearization.
- ⭐ **Use Markov / MCL when:** initial pose unknown, environment has symmetry/ambiguity, or kidnapping is possible.
- ⭐ **Use EKF when:** initial pose known, local tracking needed, real-time on low-power hardware.
- ⭐ **Common exam keywords to memorize verbatim:** "Markov assumption," "multi-modal belief," "unimodal Gaussian," "Jacobian linearization," "Kalman gain," "kidnapped robot problem," "random resampling."
- ⭐ **Numerical trick:** $K_t$ is *large* (trust sensor) when $R_t$ is *small* (precise sensor); $K_t$ is *small* (trust prediction) when $R_t$ is *large* (noisy sensor).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation and Reasoning About Space: Representing Space - Spatial Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Representation and Reasoning About Space: Representing Space - Spatial Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Spatial Decomposition

**Spatial Decomposition** in mobile robotics is the formal computational procedure of partitioning the robot's continuous, two-dimensional (or three-dimensional) free workspace $\mathcal{W}$ into a discrete collection of simpler, finite, and tractable geometric primitives (cells, regions, or polygons). This process transforms a continuous configuration-space problem into a discrete graph-search problem, enabling classical AI search algorithms (such as A*, Dijkstra, BFS) to compute collision-free paths.

Mathematically, the workspace $\mathcal{W} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ is partitioned into:

$$\mathcal{W} = \mathcal{F} \cup \mathcal{C}$$

where:
- $\mathcal{F}$ = Free space (where the robot can move)
- $\mathcal{C}$ = Configuration space occupied by obstacles ($\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \mathcal{F}$)

Spatial decomposition produces a tessellation:

$$\mathcal{F} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad R_i \cap R_j = \emptyset \;\; \forall \; i \neq j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Spatial Decomposition is the foundational sub-topic under *Representation and Reasoning About Space*. It directly bridges continuous geometric world models and discrete path-planning algorithms. This is a high-weight topic for KTU Part A (3 marks) and frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-question in Part B.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Hotel Floor Plan":**
> Imagine a large, empty, oddly shaped hotel ballroom. The hotel manager wants to install Wi-Fi routers, so they call a network engineer. The engineer cannot think about "every point" in the room — there are infinitely many. Instead, they overlay a **grid of square tiles** on the floor plan, mark each tile as either *Empty* (a router can be placed) or *Blocked* (occupied by a pillar or a wall), and then plan the cabling only through *Empty* tiles.
>
> This is **exactly** what spatial decomposition does for a mobile robot: it turns an "infinite continuum" into a "finite checkerboard" that a computer can search.

| Term in Analogy | Technical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hotel ballroom | Continuous Workspace $\mathcal{W}$ |
| Tile / Cell | Discrete Region $R_i$ |
| Empty tile | Free cell $\in \mathcal{F}$ |
| Pillar / Wall | Obstacle cell $\in \mathcal{C}$ |
| Cabling plan | Collision-free path $\pi$ |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Resolution $\Delta$ (meters/cell):** The size of each elementary cell. Smaller $\Delta$ → higher accuracy, exponentially higher memory cost.
- **Coverage Ratio $\rho$:** Fraction of workspace represented: $\rho = \frac{\text{Area Represented}}{\text{Total Area}} \leq 1$.
- **Decomposition Cardinality $n$:** Total number of regions; determines search graph size.

---

## 1.4 The Three Principal Families of Spatial Decomposition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 emphasizes these three categories — memorize the distinction, it is a guaranteed Part A question.

1. **Exact Cell Decomposition** — partitions free space into non-overlapping cells whose union is **exactly** $\mathcal{F}$. The decomposition fits the obstacle geometry precisely. Sub-types: *Trapezoidal* and *Boustrophedon*.
2. **Approximate (Grid) Cell Decomposition** — overlays a **uniform grid** of fixed resolution. The decomposition is regular but only an *approximation* of $\mathcal{F}$ because cells are partially occupied.
3. **Adaptive / Hierarchical Decomposition** — uses variable-resolution structures (e.g., **Quadtrees**) where dense obstacle regions get small cells and open regions get large cells.

A fourth supporting approach — **Roadmap methods** (Visibility Graphs, Voronoi Diagrams) — is logically distinct but grouped under *spatial representation* and is also assessed under this KTU module.

---

## 1.5 Why Spatial Decomposition Matters (The "Big Picture")

> [!NOTE]
> Without decomposition, a robot's path-planning problem lives in $\mathbb{R}^n$ — an uncountably infinite space. With decomposition, the problem becomes a search on a **finite graph $G = (V, E)$**, which is computationally solvable in polynomial time using established algorithms. This transition from **continuous → discrete** is the most important conceptual leap in classical mobile robotics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Approximate (Uniform Grid) Cell Decomposition

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic

1. Overlay the workspace $\mathcal{W}$ with a regular grid of resolution $\Delta \times \Delta$.
2. For each grid cell $c_{i,j}$, perform a collision check: $c_{i,j} = \text{OCCUPIED}$ if any part overlaps an obstacle, else $\text{FREE}$.
3. A cell is marked **"blocked"** even if only 1% is inside an obstacle — this introduces the *conservatism* (sub-optimality) of grid methods.
4. Construct a connectivity graph: 4-connected (only N/S/E/W neighbours) or 8-connected (adds diagonals).
5. Apply graph search (A*, Dijkstra, BFS) from start cell to goal cell.

### 2.1.2 Why and How

- **Why uniform?** — Implementation is trivial: a 2D array `grid[i][j]` represents the world. Cache-friendly, GPU-accelerable.
- **How is the path represented?** — A sequence of cell indices.
- **The cost:** Memory grows as $O\left(\frac{L \cdot W}{\Delta^2}\right)$. Halving $\Delta$ quadruples memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Uniform grids are the **de facto standard in ROS (Robot Operating System)** through the `costmap_2d` package used in navigation stacks. They underpin virtually every commercial autonomous vehicle's local planner (e.g., the lattice planners in self-driving cars).

---

## 2.2 Exact Cell Decomposition

### 2.2.1 Trapezoidal Decomposition

The classical algorithm sweeps a vertical line $\ell$ from $x = x_{min}$ to $x = x_{max}$. At every event point (where $\ell$ touches a vertex of an obstacle), the sweep line is "frozen" and a vertical cut is extended up or down to the next obstacle.

Each resulting cell is a **trapezoid** (or a triangle as a degenerate case). Cells are then sorted into a **Region Adjacency Graph (RAG)** where nodes = cells, edges = shared boundaries.

### 2.2.2 Boustrophedon Decomposition (Improvement)

Instead of cutting at *every* vertex (as in trapezoidal), the Boustrophedon method only cuts at **"in vertices"** (where the number of obstacle edges intersected by the sweep line changes). This produces **larger, fewer cells**, reducing the search graph size.

> [!NOTE]
> "Boustrophedon" literally means "as the ox plows the field" — referring to back-and-forth rows. The decomposition yields cells that can be covered by simple back-and-forth motion, making it ideal for **area-coverage planning** (lawn mowers, vacuum cleaners, agricultural robots, mine sweeping).

### 2.2.3 Why and How

- **Why exact?** — The path passes through the *centre* of free cells, hugging obstacles optimally. No conservatism.
- **How is it built?** — Through computational-geometry algorithms (Bentley–Ottmann line sweep, $O(n \log n)$).
- **Limitation:** Highly non-uniform cell sizes cause the search graph to be unbalanced. Regions with sparse obstacles get huge trapezoids; cluttered regions get tiny slivers.

---

## 2.3 Adaptive (Quadtree) Decomposition

A **Quadtree** is a recursive, hierarchical decomposition of a 2D region.

**The Rule:** A square cell is subdivided into 4 equal quadrants (NW, NE, SW, SE) *if and only if* it is not entirely free and not entirely occupied.

**Termination:** A cell is a leaf when:
- It is **fully FREE**, OR
- It is **fully OCCUPIED**, OR
- It has reached the **maximum tree depth $d_{max}$**.

### 2.3.1 Quadtree Resolution Trade-off

Let $d$ = tree depth, $L$ = side length of workspace, $n$ = number of leaf nodes.

| Tree Depth $d$ | Smallest Cell Size $\Delta$ | Memory Cells (max) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | $L$ | 1 |
| 5 | $L/32$ | $4^5 = 1024$ |
| 10 | $L/1024$ | $4^{10} \approx 1.05 \times 10^6$ |

### 2.3.2 Why and How

- **Why quadtree?** — Adaptive resolution: open spaces consume 1 cell; cluttered spaces get fine resolution. Best of both worlds.
- **How is the path found?** — A* is run on a graph where each leaf node is a vertex and adjacent leaves share an edge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Quadtrees are used in **image compression** (the same algorithm!), **spatial databases** (PostGIS, Google Maps tile pyramids), and **OctoMap** (the 3D version used in drone/quadrotor mapping).

---

## 2.4 Roadmap Methods (Closely Related)

### 2.4.1 Visibility Graph

- Nodes: start, goal, and **all obstacle vertices**.
- Edges: connect two nodes if the straight line between them is collision-free.
- The shortest path is found via Dijkstra/A*.
- **Catch:** Path hugs obstacle corners — unsafe for a robot with non-zero size (must apply Minkowski sum inflation).

### 2.4.2 Voronoi Diagram

- Edges are the set of points **equidistant** from the two nearest obstacles.
- The path **maximizes clearance** from obstacles — safest, but not shortest.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the single most important summary for KTU exam revision.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Workspace partition | $\mathcal{W} = \mathcal{F} \cup \mathcal{C}$ | Sets are disjoint |
| 2 | Number of grid cells | $N = \left\lceil \frac{L}{\Delta} \right\rceil \times \left\lceil \frac{W}{\Delta} \right\rceil$ | $L,W$ = workspace dims (m), $\Delta$ = resolution (m) |
| 3 | Memory cost of grid | $M = N \times b$ bits | $b$ = bits per cell (typically 1 or 8) |
| 4 | Grid resolution scaling | $\Delta \to \Delta/2 \Rightarrow M \times 4$ | Memory grows quadratically |
| 5 | Quadtree max leaves at depth $d$ | $N_{max} = 4^{d}$ | Worst-case uniform occupancy |
| 6 | Trapezoidal decomposition complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | $n$ = number of obstacle vertices |
| 7 | Visibility graph nodes | $n_v = V_{start} + V_{goal} + V_{obstacle}$ | $V_{obstacle}$ = all obstacle vertices |
| 8 | Voronoi clearance | $d_{clear} = \min_{o \in \mathcal{C}} \text{dist}(p, o)$ | Maximized along Voronoi edges |
| 9 | Path optimality (grid) | Sub-optimal — discrete shortcut cannot pass through blocked cells | Inflation-aware A* improves this |
| 10 | Connectivity | 4-connect: 4 neighbours; 8-connect: 8 neighbours | 8-connect allows diagonal moves |

---

## 2.6 Comparative Analysis of Methods (Frequently Asked in KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table**

| Criterion | Uniform Grid | Exact (Trapezoidal) | Quadtree | Visibility Graph | Voronoi |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Memory | $O(1/\Delta^2)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Optimal Path | Approximate | Exact | Approximate | Exact (shortest) | Approximate (max clearance) |
| Implementation | Trivial | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Complex |
| Scalability | Poor for large spaces | Good | Excellent | Poor (dense graphs) | Good |
| Clearance | Low | Low | Low | Zero (hugs walls) | **Maximum** |
| Best Use | Local planning | Coverage | Mixed environments | Sparse obstacles | Cluttered environments |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1 — Memory Cost Scaling for a Uniform Grid

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A rectangular workspace of dimensions $10\,\text{m} \times 8\,\text{m}$ is to be represented using a uniform grid. Compute the total number of cells and memory required (in bytes, assuming 1 byte per cell) for a resolution of $\Delta = 0.25\,\text{m}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the dimensions

$$L = 10 \;\text{m}, \quad W = 8 \;\text{m}, \quad \Delta = 0.25 \;\text{m}$$

### Step 2 — Compute cells along the length

$$N_L = \left\lceil \frac{L}{\Delta} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{10}{0.25} \right\rceil = 40 \;\text{cells}$$

*Reasoning:* We divide the total length by the resolution; the ceiling function ensures we cover any partial remainder.

### Step 3 — Compute cells along the width

$$N_W = \left\lceil \frac{W}{\Delta} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{8}{0.25} \right\rceil = 32 \;\text{cells}$$

*Reasoning:* Same logic along the perpendicular axis.

### Step 4 — Compute the total cell count

$$N = N_L \times N_W = 40 \times 32 = 1280 \;\text{cells}$$

*Reasoning:* The grid is the Cartesian product of the rows and columns.

### Step 5 — Compute memory footprint

$$M = N \times b = 1280 \times 1 = 1280 \;\text{bytes} \approx 1.25 \;\text{KB}$$

*Reasoning:* Each cell stores one byte (e.g., 0 = free, 1 = occupied, 2 = unknown).

### Step 6 — Show the scaling implication

If we refine to $\Delta' = 0.125\,\text{m}$ (i.e., $\Delta' = \Delta/2$):

$$N' = \left\lceil \frac{10}{0.125} \right\rceil \times \left\lceil \frac{8}{0.125} \right\rceil = 80 \times 64 = 5120 = 4 \times 1280$$

*Reasoning:* Confirms the $4\times$ memory inflation rule.

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2 — Quadtree Cell Count Bound

**Problem Statement:** Derive the maximum number of leaves a quadtree of depth $d$ can have, and compute the smallest cell side length for a $16\,\text{m} \times 16\,\text{m}$ workspace at $d = 6$.

### Step 1 — Establish the branching factor

Each non-leaf cell in a quadtree produces exactly 4 children. This is the *branching factor* $b = 4$.

### Step 2 — Express the tree as a geometric series

At depth $k$, the number of nodes is $4^k$. The number of *internal* (non-leaf) nodes at depth $< d$ is:

$$I = \sum_{k=0}^{d-1} 4^k = \frac{4^{d} - 1}{4 - 1} = \frac{4^d - 1}{3}$$

### Step 3 — Express the maximum number of leaves

The total nodes in a full 4-ary tree of depth $d$ is $N_{total} = \sum_{k=0}^{d} 4^k = \frac{4^{d+1} - 1}{3}$.

Therefore the maximum number of leaves (when every internal node is fully expanded) is:

$$L_{max} = \frac{4^{d+1} - 1}{3} - I = \frac{4^{d+1} - 1}{3} - \frac{4^{d} - 1}{3} = \frac{4^{d+1} - 4^{d}}{3}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify using algebra

$$L_{max} = \frac{4^{d}(4 - 1)}{3} = \frac{3 \cdot 4^{d}}{3} = 4^{d}$$

*Reasoning:* A full quadtree of depth $d$ has exactly $4^d$ leaves. This is the worst-case bound; in practice, the actual leaf count is far smaller because most open regions are never subdivided.

### Step 5 — Apply to the given workspace

For $L_{ws} = 16\,\text{m}$ and $d = 6$:

$$\Delta = \frac{L_{ws}}{2^{d}} = \frac{16}{2^{6}} = \frac{16}{64} = 0.25 \;\text{m}$$

*Reasoning:* Each level doubles the linear resolution. The smallest cell at depth $d$ has side $L_{ws}/2^d$.

### Step 6 — Compute the maximum leaf count

$$L_{max} = 4^{6} = 4096 \;\text{leaves}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 6-level quadtree can resolve the workspace down to 0.25 m cells, with at most 4096 leaves — roughly **3.2× the memory of a uniform grid of the same finest resolution** ($1280$ cells in the same area? No — $N_{grid} = 64 \times 64 = 4096$ cells exactly, so quadtree's worst case equals the uniform grid). In practice, quadtrees use far fewer leaves because open space stays un-subdivided.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (When to Use Which Decomposition)

| If the environment is... | Best Decomposition | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| A small indoor room, $O(10^3)$ cells | Uniform Grid | Simple, fast, sufficient accuracy |
| A warehouse with long narrow aisles | Trapezoidal / Boustrophedon | Large free regions get single trapezoids |
| A large outdoor forest (mostly free) | Quadtree | Open space stays coarse, trees get fine resolution |
| Cluttered with many small obstacles | Visibility Graph | Sparse obstacle count; shortest path is critical |
| Safety-critical (e.g., nuclear plant) | Voronoi Diagram | Maximum clearance from obstacles |
| 3D drone / quadrotor space | OctoMap (3D Quad-Tree) | Extension of quadtree to 3D volume |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Trapezoidal Decomposition Sketch

Consider a rectangular workspace $\mathcal{W} = [0, 10] \times [0, 10]$ with a single rectangular obstacle at $[3, 7] \times [4, 6]$.

- Vertical sweep lines at $x = 3$ and $x = 7$ cut the workspace.
- Resulting free cells: 
  - Trapezoid 1: $x \in [0, 3]$, $y \in [0, 10]$ (full height)
  - Trapezoid 2: $x \in [3, 7]$, $y \in [0, 4] \cup [6, 10]$ (split into 2 rectangles)
  - Trapezoid 3: $x \in [7, 10]$, $y \in [0, 10]$ (full height)
- Region Adjacency Graph: 4 nodes (T1, T2a, T2b, T3) connected at the cut lines.
- A* on this 4-node graph yields a path in milliseconds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Choosing a Spatial Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mobile Robot Workspace] --> B{Environment Scale?}
    B -->|Small 10x10 m| C{Obstacle Density?}
    B -->|Large gt 100x100 m| D{Heterogeneous Density?}
    C -->|Low| E[Uniform Grid]
    C -->|High| F{Optimality Required?}
    D -->|Yes| G[Quadtree Octree]
    D -->|No| H[Hierarchical Grid]
    F -->|Yes| I[Visibility Graph]
    F -->|No| J[Safety Critical?]
    J -->|Yes| K[Voronoi Diagram]
    J -->|No| L[Exact Trapezoidal]
    E --> M[Run A or Dijkstra]
    G --> M
    H --> M
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Collision Free Path]
```

## 4.2 Quadtree Decomposition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Root Cell 16x16 m] --> NW[NW Quadrant]
    Root --> NE[NE Quadrant]
    Root --> SW[SW Quadrant]
    Root --> SE[SE Quadrant]
    NW --> NW1[NW-NW Free Leaf]
    NW --> NW2[NW-NE Subdivided]
    NW --> NW3[NW-SW Free Leaf]
    NW --> NW4[NW-SE Free Leaf]
    NW2 --> N21[Free Leaf]
    NW2 --> N22[Occupied Leaf]
    NW2 --> N23[Free Leaf]
    NW2 --> N24[Free Leaf]
    SE --> SE1[SE-NW Free Leaf]
    SE --> SE2[SE-NE Free Leaf]
    SE --> SE3[SE-SW Free Leaf]
    SE --> SE4[SE-SE Free Leaf]
```

**Interpretation:** The root cell is fully checked; if it is partially free, it is split into 4. The NW quadrant contains a small obstacle → it is recursively subdivided until each leaf is either fully free, fully occupied, or the maximum depth is reached. The SE quadrant is entirely free → it remains a single leaf.

## 4.3 Voronoi Diagram Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S([Start]):::startnode
    G([Goal]):::goalnode
    V1((Voronoi Vertex 1)):::vnode
    V2((Voronoi Vertex 2)):::vnode
    V3((Voronoi Vertex 3)):::vnode
    E1((Obstacle 1)):::obstaclenode
    E2((Obstacle 2)):::obstaclenode
    E3((Obstacle 3)):::obstaclenode
    S --- V1
    V1 --- V2
    V2 --- V3
    V3 --- G
    E1 -.- V1
    E2 -.- V2
    E3 -.- V3
    classDef startnode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef goalnode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef vnode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef obstaclenode fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** Solid lines are Voronoi edges (the safe path). Dotted lines show that each edge is equidistant from two adjacent obstacles. The robot traverses V1 → V2 → V3, maximising clearance.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — From Sensors to Decomposed Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LiDAR or Camera Sensors] --> B[SLAM Module Localization]
    B --> C[Point Cloud or Occupancy Grid]
    C --> D{Spatial Decomposition Module}
    D --> D1[Uniform Grid]
    D --> D2[Trapezoidal Exact]
    D --> D3[Quadtree Adaptive]
    D --> D4[Visibility Graph]
    D --> D5[Voronoi Diagram]
    D1 --> E[Search Graph G of V E]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Graph Search A Star Dijkstra]
    F --> G[Collision Free Trajectory]
    G --> H[Motion Controller Wheel Speeds]
```

**Engineering Reading:** This is the **full perception → planning → action** pipeline in modern mobile robots. The spatial decomposition module sits in the middle, converting raw sensor data into a search-ready graph.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]`
**Q: Define spatial decomposition in the context of mobile robot path planning. List any two types of cell decomposition methods.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Spatial decomposition is the process of partitioning a robot's continuous free workspace into a finite set of discrete, non-overlapping regions (cells) so that the path-planning problem can be solved using discrete graph-search algorithms.
- **Types (1.5 Marks):** (i) Exact cell decomposition (e.g., Trapezoidal, Boustrophedon), and (ii) Approximate / Grid cell decomposition. *(Either pair is acceptable.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Q: Differentiate between exact and approximate cell decomposition.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Exact Decomposition | Approximate Decomposition |
|---|---|---|
| Fit to obstacles | Cells conform exactly to obstacle shape | Regular grid; cells may straddle obstacles |
| Path optimality | Optimal / near-optimal | Sub-optimal (conservative) |
| Complexity | $O(n \log n)$, $n$ = obstacle vertices | $O(1/\Delta^2)$ cells |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write vague definitions for 7 marks. Every sub-part needs a *diagram* (1–2 marks reserved for it).
> - **Do NOT** confuse Voronoi (maximum clearance) with Visibility Graph (shortest path) — this is the most common error costing 2–3 marks.
> - **Do NOT** skip the comparison table in (b); examiners award 2 marks specifically for comparative analysis.
> - **Always** state assumptions: 2D workspace, point robot, static obstacles, full prior knowledge of the map.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a) Explain uniform grid-based approximate cell decomposition for robot path planning with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations.** *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

1. **Definition (2 Marks):** The workspace $\mathcal{W}$ is overlaid with a regular grid of square cells of side $\Delta$. Each cell is classified as FREE, OCCUPIED, or UNKNOWN via a sensor sweep or prior map. A 4- or 8-connected adjacency graph $G(V,E)$ is constructed and searched with A*.

2. **Diagram (2 Marks):** *(Draw a $5 \times 5$ grid with two obstacle clusters; mark start S in the bottom-left and goal G in the top-right; show the A\* path snaking through free cells.)*

3. **Advantages (1.5 Marks):**
   - Trivial to implement (`grid[i][j]` array).
   - Naturally handles dynamic obstacles (just flip cell state).
   - GPU / parallelisation friendly.

4. **Limitations (1.5 Marks):**
   - Memory grows as $O(1/\Delta^2)$ — poor scalability.
   - Conservative: a 99%-free cell is marked OCCUPIED.
   - No semantic richness (cannot label "this is a hallway").

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the Visibility Graph method of roadmap-based path planning. Compare it with the Voronoi diagram method.** *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

1. **Visibility Graph Construction (2 Marks):** Nodes = start $S$, goal $G$, and all obstacle vertices. Edges connect two nodes if the straight line between them lies entirely in $\mathcal{F}$ (i.e., is "visible" to each other). The shortest path from $S$ to $G$ in this graph is the shortest collision-free path in the workspace.

2. **Diagram (1.5 Marks):** *(Draw three polygonal obstacles; show all visible edges from $S$ to $G$; highlight the resulting shortest path touching obstacle corners.)*

3. **Comparison Table (2.5 Marks):**

| Criterion | Visibility Graph | Voronoi Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| Path characteristic | Shortest path | Maximum-clearance path |
| Obstacle proximity | Touches vertices | Equidistant from obstacles |
| Safety | Low (hugs walls) | High |
| Best for | Sparse, known obstacles | Cluttered, hazardous environments |
| Computation | $O(n^2)$ edges to test | $O(n \log n)$ via Fortune's algorithm |

4. **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Choose Visibility Graph when path length matters; choose Voronoi when safety/clearance matters.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**(a) Describe the Quadtree-based adaptive cell decomposition method. Explain how it differs from a uniform grid and list two real-world applications.** *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

1. **Quadtree Definition (2 Marks):** A hierarchical data structure that recursively subdivides a 2D region into 4 equal quadrants. A cell is split **only if** it is partially free and partially occupied. A cell becomes a leaf if it is fully free, fully occupied, or the maximum depth $d_{max}$ is reached.

2. **Recursive Subdivision (2 Marks):** The root cell is checked. If mixed, it is split into NW, NE, SW, SE. Each child is checked recursively. The smallest cell side at depth $d$ is $L/2^d$.

3. **Differences from Uniform Grid (1.5 Marks):**
   - **Resolution:** Quadtree is variable; uniform grid is fixed.
   - **Memory:** Quadtree uses fewer cells in homogeneous regions; uniform grid is constant $L \cdot W / \Delta^2$.
   - **Path quality:** Both yield sub-optimal paths, but quadtree's variable cells allow fine resolution exactly where needed.

4. **Applications (1.5 Marks):** (i) Image compression (JPEG 2000 uses wavelet quad-trees), (ii) Robotics — *OctoMap* for 3D drone mapping, (iii) Spatial databases — Google Maps tile pyramids.

---

**(b) Explain the Boustrophedon decomposition method for area coverage. How is it an improvement over the trapezoidal decomposition? Use a diagram.** *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution (7 Marks):

1. **Area Coverage Concept (1.5 Marks):** Boustrophedon decomposition is designed for the *coverage* problem, where a robot must visit **every point** of the free space (vacuum, lawn-mower, mine-sweep, agricultural robot). The name means "as the ox ploughs" — back-and-forth parallel rows.

2. **Algorithm (2 Marks):** A vertical sweep line $\ell$ moves left-to-right. Cuts are made only at **"in-vertices"** — vertices where the number of obstacle edges intersected by $\ell$ becomes *odd*, i.e., changes parity. This is in contrast to trapezoidal decomposition, which cuts at *every* obstacle vertex.

3. **Diagram (1.5 Marks):** *(Show a workspace with three obstacles; mark in-vertices with solid dots and out-vertices with hollow dots; illustrate the larger cells produced.)*

4. **Improvements over Trapezoidal (2 Marks):**
   - **Fewer cells** → smaller search graph → faster planning.
   - **Larger cells** → each cell can be covered by a simple back-and-forth motion (true coverage).
   - **Easier motion planning** — each cell is a simple polygon, easy to cover with boustrophedon paths.

5. **Conclusion (0.5 Marks):** Boustrophedon is the preferred exact decomposition whenever the goal is complete area coverage, not just point-to-point navigation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- ☐ **Spatial decomposition** = partitioning continuous workspace into discrete cells/regions for graph search.
- ☐ **Workspace $\mathcal{W}$ = Free $\mathcal{F}$ + Obstacles $\mathcal{C}$**.
- ☐ **Three families:** Approximate (Grid), Exact (Trapezoidal/Boustrophedon), Adaptive (Quadtree).
- ☐ **Uniform Grid memory:** $N = \lceil L/\Delta \rceil \times \lceil W/\Delta \rceil$. Halving $\Delta$ → $4\times$ memory.
- ☐ **Trapezoidal decomposition:** $O(n \log n)$, cuts at *every* obstacle vertex, exact cell fit.
- ☐ **Boustrophedon decomposition:** cuts only at in-vertices → fewer, larger cells; best for **area coverage**.
- ☐ **Quadtree:** Branching factor 4; max leaves $= 4^d$; smallest cell $= L_{ws}/2^d$.
- ☐ **Visibility Graph:** nodes = obstacle vertices; **shortest path**; hugs walls (low clearance).
- ☐ **Voronoi Diagram:** edges are equidistant from obstacles; **maximum clearance**; safest path.
- ☐ **Grid optimality:** Sub-optimal; conservative because partially occupied cells are treated as fully blocked.
- ☐ **Connectivity:** 4-connected (Manhattan) vs 8-connected (with diagonals) — affects path length.
- ☐ **Engineering use:** ROS `costmap_2d` = uniform grid; OctoMap = 3D quadtree; SLAM outputs feed the decomposition.
- ☐ **Always mention assumptions** in KTU answers: 2D, point robot, static obstacles, known map.

---

### Final KTU 2024 Tip
> [!NOTE]
> In the 14-mark questions, examiners at KTU Kerala expect a **diagram (1–2 marks)**, **definition (1–2 marks)**, **algorithm/steps (2–3 marks)**, **comparison table (2 marks)**, and **conclusion (1 mark)**. Structure every long answer using this template and you will rarely drop below 12/14.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Geometric Representations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In mobile robotics, **Geometric Representations** refer to the formal mathematical machinery used to encode the **pose** (combined position and orientation) of a rigid body — such as a mobile robot, its wheels, sensors, and manipulator links — relative to a chosen reference **coordinate frame**. The discipline provides a uniform, algebraically closed framework for describing, composing, and differentiating rigid-body motions in 2D (planar motion, $\text{SE}(2)$) and 3D (spatial motion, $\text{SE}(3)$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the term covers: **position vectors, rotation matrices, homogeneous transformation matrices, Euler/RPY angles, and axis–angle representations**. These form the *language* in which every subsequent module (kinematics, localization, SLAM, control) is written.

The pose of a robot in 2D is mathematically defined as a tuple:

$$
\xi \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} \in \text{SE}(2)
$$

where $x, y$ encode translation in the plane and $\theta$ is the heading angle. In 3D, the pose generalises to six degrees of freedom (6-DOF):

$$
\xi \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \\ \phi \\ \theta \\ \psi \end{bmatrix} \in \text{SE}(3)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The mathematical sets $\text{SO}(n)$ (Special Orthogonal) and $\text{SE}(n)$ (Special Euclidean) are the **Lie groups** that govern rotations and rigid motions respectively. The *Special* in their name denotes determinant $= +1$, which physically means **no reflection (mirror image)** — only proper rigid motions are allowed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Treasure Map" Viewpoint

Imagine a treasure hunter in a forest. She has **one map fixed to the ground** (the *world frame*, $\{W\}$) and **one mini-map pinned to her hat** (the *body frame*, $\{B\}$). Her location on the big map is the **position vector** $^W\mathbf{p}_B$. The way her hat is twisted relative to true north is the **orientation**, captured by a **rotation matrix** $^W_R_B$. The pair, packed neatly into a single **homogeneous transformation matrix** $^W T_B$, tells anyone with the big map *exactly* where she is and which way she is facing — this is geometric representation in a nutshell.

Whenever she moves forward, the body frame slides; whenever she turns, the body frame spins. Each motion is a *transformation*, and stacking transformations is the everyday algebraic operation of mobile robotics.

## 1.3 Why Geometric Representations Matter

| Engineering Need | Representation Used |
|---|---|
| Mapping wheel encoder ticks to world displacement | 2-D translation + 2-D rotation |
| Fusing IMU and LiDAR in EKF-SLAM | 3-D rotation matrices / quaternions |
| Forward kinematics of a manipulator arm | Composite homogeneous transforms $\text{SE}(3)$ |
| Path planning in configuration space | Pose vectors $\in \mathbb{R}^{2} \times S^{1}$ or $\mathbb{R}^{3} \times S^{3}$ |
| Visual servoing using camera images | Projective geometry built atop $\text{SE}(3)$ |

> [!TIP]
> A recurring examiner's cue: *"Represent the pose of the robot using homogeneous transformation matrices."* Always respond with a $4 \times 4$ matrix in $\text{SE}(3)$, not with separate rotation + translation.

## 1.4 The Five Foundational Building Blocks

The KTU 2024 scheme expects mastery of these five primitives, each addressed in the sections that follow:

1. **Position vector** $^A \mathbf{p}_B \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — location of origin of $\{B\}$ in $\{A\}$.
2. **Rotation matrix** $^A R_B \in \text{SO}(n)$ — orientation of $\{B\}$ as seen from $\{A\}$.
3. **Skew-symmetric operator** $[\,\cdot\,]_\times$ — converts a vector into a cross-product matrix.
4. **Homogeneous transformation matrix** $^A T_B \in \text{SE}(n)$ — combined rotation + translation in a single $4 \times 4$ entity.
5. **Minimal representations** (Euler angles, RPY, axis–angle, quaternions) — compact, three- or four-parameter surrogates of $\text{SO}(3)$ for storage and control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Position Vectors — $^A \mathbf{p}_B$

A point $Q$ located in frame $\{B\}$ is described relative to frame $\{A\}$ by the position vector $^A \mathbf{p}_Q$. In 2D it is a $2 \times 1$ column; in 3D it is $3 \times 1$. Position vectors **add** under chain rule:

$$
^A \mathbf{p}_Q \;=\; ^A \mathbf{p}_B \;+\; ^A R_B \;\,^B \mathbf{p}_Q
$$

This is the **coordinate composition law** — the rule that lets us translate "from one map to another".

## 2.2 Rotation Matrix $^A R_B \in \text{SO}(n)$

The rotation matrix expresses the orientation of frame $\{B\}$ expressed in frame $\{A\}$. It is constructed by writing the **unit vectors of $\{B\}$'s axes** as columns in the coordinates of $\{A\}$:

$$
^W R_B \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} ^W \hat{\mathbf{x}}_B & ^W \hat{\mathbf{y}}_B & ^W \hat{\mathbf{z}}_B \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.2.1 Properties (must be memorised for KTU)

- **Orthogonality**: $R^{T} R = I$
- **Determinant**: $\det(R) = +1$
- **Inverse** equals transpose: $R^{-1} = R^{T}$
- **Closure**: the product of two rotation matrices is a rotation matrix.
- The set of all such matrices is the **Special Orthogonal Group** $\text{SO}(3)$.

### 2.2.2 The Three Canonical Single-Axis Rotations

A **right-handed** rotation by angle $\theta$ about a coordinate axis is given by:

$$
R_x(\alpha) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R_y(\beta) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & 0 & \sin\beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\beta & 0 & \cos\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R_z(\gamma) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sign convention adopted in KTU: **positive rotation = counter-clockwise** when looking from the **positive axis toward the origin** (right-hand rule). Getting this wrong is the most common 1-mark loss in the exam.

### 2.2.3 Euler Angles and Roll–Pitch–Yaw

Any orientation in 3D can be expressed as a sequence of three rotations. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the **ZYX (yaw–pitch–roll) intrinsic convention**:

$$
R(\phi,\theta,\psi) \;=\; R_z(\phi)\,R_y(\theta)\,R_x(\psi)
$$

Expanded:

$$
R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
c\phi\,c\theta & c\phi\,s\theta\,s\psi - s\phi\,c\psi & c\phi\,s\theta\,c\psi + s\phi\,s\psi \\
s\phi\,c\theta & s\phi\,s\theta\,s\psi + c\phi\,c\psi & s\phi\,s\theta\,c\psi - c\phi\,s\psi \\
-\,s\theta & c\theta\,s\psi & c\theta\,c\psi
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Gimbal lock** occurs at $\theta = \pm 90^{\circ}$: the $c\theta$ factor vanishes, and roll and yaw become indistinguishable. This is *the* canonical KTU viva question on Euler angles.

### 2.2.4 Axis–Angle Representation (Rodrigues' Formula)

A unit vector $\hat{\mathbf{k}} = [k_x,\,k_y,\,k_z]^{T}$ and an angle $\theta$ together describe a rotation. The matrix form is:

$$
R(\hat{\mathbf{k}},\theta) \;=\; I \;+\; \sin\theta\,[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times \;+\; (1-\cos\theta)\,[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^{2}
$$

where $[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times$ is the **skew-symmetric cross-product matrix**:

$$
[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -k_z & k_y \\ k_z & 0 & -k_x \\ -k_y & k_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 2.3 Homogeneous Transformation Matrix $^A T_B \in \text{SE}(3)$

Packing rotation and translation into a single $4 \times 4$ block enables one-step composition:

$$
^A T_B \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} ^A R_B & ^A \mathbf{p}_B \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

A point $\mathbf{p}$ in frame $\{B\}$ becomes $\mathbf{p}'$ in frame $\{A\}$ by:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} ^A\mathbf{p} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; ^A T_B \begin{bmatrix} ^B\mathbf{p} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.3.1 The Composition Law

$$
^A T_C \;=\; ^A T_B \,\,^B T_C
$$

Inverse:

$$
^A T_B^{-1} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} ^A R_B^{T} & -\,^A R_B^{T}\,^A \mathbf{p}_B \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 2.4 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below collects **every** formula a student must memorise for the ESE on this topic. All magnitudes such as $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ are rendered with `\\lVert` / `\\rVert` so the markdown table does **not** break.

| # | Concept | Formula | Domain / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Position composition | $^A \mathbf{p}_Q = \,^A \mathbf{p}_B + \,^A R_B \,\,^B \mathbf{p}_Q$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 2 | Basic $X$ rotation | $R_x(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0 & 0\\ 0 & c_\alpha & -s_\alpha\\ 0 & s_\alpha & c_\alpha\end{bmatrix}$ | $\text{SO}(3)$ |
| 3 | Basic $Y$ rotation | $R_y(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix}c_\beta & 0 & s_\beta\\ 0 & 1 & 0\\ -s_\beta & 0 & c_\beta\end{bmatrix}$ | $\text{SO}(3)$ |
| 4 | Basic $Z$ rotation | $R_z(\gamma) = \begin{bmatrix}c_\gamma & -s_\gamma & 0\\ s_\gamma & c_\gamma & 0\\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ | $\text{SO}(3)$ |
| 5 | RPY (ZYX) composition | $R = R_z(\phi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\psi)$ | $\text{SO}(3)$ |
| 6 | Skew-symmetric | $[\mathbf{v}]_\times = \begin{bmatrix}0 & -v_3 & v_2\\ v_3 & 0 & -v_1\\ -v_2 & v_1 & 0\end{bmatrix}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{3\times3}$ |
| 7 | Rodrigues' formula | $R = I + \sin\theta [k]_\times + (1-\cos\theta)[k]_\times^{2}$ | $\text{SO}(3)$ |
| 8 | Homogeneous transform | $T = \begin{bmatrix}R & \mathbf{p}\\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1\end{bmatrix}$ | $\text{SE}(3)$ |
| 9 | Composition of $T$ | $^A T_C = \,^A T_B \,\,^B T_C$ | $\text{SE}(3)$ |
| 10 | Inverse of $T$ | $T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix}R^{T} & -R^{T}\mathbf{p}\\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1\end{bmatrix}$ | $\text{SE}(3)$ |
| 11 | Group invariant | $\det R = +1,\;\; R^{T} = R^{-1}$ | — |
| 12 | Cross-product equivalence | $[\mathbf{a}]_\times \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous cars** use $^W R_B$ to fuse GPS heading with IMU yaw, and $^W\mathbf{p}_B$ to publish their pose on the CAN bus.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots)** such as Amazon's Kiva robots use $\text{SE}(2)$ pose for grid-based localization.
- **Drone flight controllers** (PX4, ArduPilot) use quaternions internally (mathematically equivalent to $\text{SO}(3)$) for numerically stable attitude integration.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci, Mako)** compose dozens of $\text{SE}(3)$ transforms to compute the end-effector pose from joint encoders.
- **AR/VR headsets** use $\text{SE}(3)$ to overlay virtual objects on the real world with sub-millimetre registration.

> [!TIP]
> In every KTU 2024 numerical, **state the group membership** of the matrix you produce — e.g., "Hence $R \in \text{SO}(3)$ with $\det R = +1$". Examiners reward this precision.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Basic 2-D Rotation Matrix

**Goal**: Find $R_z(\theta)$ that rotates a point $P$ in the $xy$-plane about the origin through angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Write the original coordinates**.
Let $P = (x, y)$. In polar form, $x = r\cos\alpha$ and $y = r\sin\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the angle the radius makes with the $x$-axis.

**Step 2 — Apply the rotation**.
A counter-clockwise rotation by $\theta$ takes the angle from $\alpha$ to $\alpha+\theta$. The new coordinates $(x',y')$ are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= r\cos(\alpha+\theta) \\
   &= r\bigl[\cos\alpha\cos\theta - \sin\alpha\sin\theta\bigr] \\
   &= x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y' &= r\sin(\alpha+\theta) \\
   &= r\bigl[\sin\alpha\cos\theta + \cos\alpha\sin\theta\bigr] \\
   &= x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express as a matrix**.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}}_{R(\theta)} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify group axioms**.

$$
R(\theta_1) R(\theta_2) \;=\; R(\theta_1+\theta_2), \qquad R^{T}(\theta) \;=\; R(-\theta), \qquad \det R \;=\; \cos^{2}\theta + \sin^{2}\theta \;=\; 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The determinant check confirms $R(\theta) \in \text{SO}(2)$. This exact reasoning is the foundation of all 3-D rotation derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation of the 3-D Rotation Matrix $R_x(\alpha)$

Rotate a point $P=(x,y,z)$ about the $X$-axis. Only the $y$ and $z$ components change, by the 2-D formula above with $x$ held constant.

**Step 1 — Identify the invariant coordinate**.

$$
x' \;=\; x
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 2-D rotation in the $yz$-plane**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y' &= y\cos\alpha - z\sin\alpha \\
z' &= y\sin\alpha + z\cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pack into a $3 \times 3$ matrix**.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ z' \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **3-D right-hand rule** fixes the sign: looking down the positive axis toward the origin, a positive angle is counter-clockwise. This single convention drives the sign in every basic rotation.

## 3.3 Derivation of the ZYX Euler-Angle Matrix

**Step 1 — Define intrinsic rotations**.
Starting from a frame aligned with $\{W\}$, perform:
- **Roll** $\psi$ about the body's new $X$-axis.
- **Pitch** $\theta$ about the body's new $Y$-axis.
- **Yaw** $\phi$ about the body's new $Z$-axis.

Intrinsic ZYX ↔ Extrinsic XYZ: the resulting matrix is $R = R_z(\phi)\,R_y(\theta)\,R_x(\psi)$.

**Step 2 — Multiply $R_z(\phi)$ and $R_y(\theta)$ first**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_z(\phi)\,R_y(\theta) &=
\begin{bmatrix} c_\phi & -s_\phi & 0 \\ s_\phi & c_\phi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} c_\theta & 0 & s_\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -s_\theta & 0 & c_\theta \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
c_\phi c_\theta & -s_\phi & c_\phi s_\theta \\
s_\phi c_\theta & \;\;c_\phi & s_\phi s_\theta \\
-\,s_\theta & 0 & c_\theta
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply on the right by $R_x(\psi)$**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= R_z(\phi)\,R_y(\theta)\,R_x(\psi) \\[4pt]
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
c_\phi c_\theta & -s_\phi & c_\phi s_\theta \\
s_\phi c_\theta & \;\;c_\phi & s_\phi s_\theta \\
-\,s_\theta & 0 & c_\theta
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & c_\psi & -s_\psi \\
0 & s_\psi & c_\psi
\end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
c_\phi c_\theta & c_\phi s_\theta s_\psi - s_\phi c_\psi & c_\phi s_\theta c_\psi + s_\phi s_\psi \\
s_\phi c_\theta & s_\phi s_\theta s_\psi + c_\phi c_\psi & s_\phi s_\theta c_\psi - c_\phi s_\psi \\
-\,s_\theta & c_\theta s_\psi & c_\theta c_\psi
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise the **(1,1) entry** as $c\phi\,c\theta$. It is the term most often asked to derive in the 14-mark problem.

## 3.4 Derivation of Rodrigues' Formula

**Step 1 — Use the matrix exponential trick**.
A rotation by $\theta$ about unit axis $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ can be written as $R = e^{[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times \theta}$.

**Step 2 — Expand the exponential as a power series**.

$$
e^{[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times \theta} \;=\; I + \theta [\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times + \frac{\theta^{2}}{2!} [\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^{2} + \frac{\theta^{3}}{3!} [\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^{3} + \cdots
$$

**Step 3 — Use the geometric identities of the skew matrix**.

$$
[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^{2} \;=\; \hat{\mathbf{k}}\hat{\mathbf{k}}^{T} - I, \qquad [\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^{3} \;=\; -[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and group sin/cos terms**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= I + \bigl(\theta - \tfrac{\theta^{3}}{3!} + \tfrac{\theta^{5}}{5!} - \cdots\bigr)[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times + \bigl(\tfrac{\theta^{2}}{2!} - \tfrac{\theta^{4}}{4!} + \cdots\bigr)[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^{2} \\[4pt]
&= I + \sin\theta\,[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times + (1-\cos\theta)\,[\hat{\mathbf{k}}]_\times^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Rodrigues rotation formula**, indispensable when the rotation axis is not aligned with a coordinate axis.

## 3.5 Derivation of $T^{-1}$ for a Homogeneous Transform

Let

$$
T \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{p} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Block-invert:

$$
T^{-1} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} R^{T} & -R^{T}\mathbf{p} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Verification**: multiply $T \cdot T^{-1}$ and use $R R^{T} = I$ and $\mathbf{p} - R R^{T}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
T T^{-1} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} R R^{T} & R(-R^{T}\mathbf{p}) + \mathbf{p} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} I & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; I_{4}
$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem.** A mobile robot's body frame $\{B\}$ is located at $^W\mathbf{p}_B = [2,\,3,\,0]^{T}$ m in the world frame $\{W\}$ and oriented with $\text{roll} = 0^{\circ},\ \text{pitch} = 30^{\circ},\ \text{yaw} = 45^{\circ}$. A landmark $L$ is at $^B\mathbf{p}_L = [1,\,0,\,0]^{T}$ m in the body frame. Find the position of $L$ in the world frame.

**Step 1 — Build $^W R_B$ using ZYX (yaw-pitch-roll) convention**.

Using the table values $\phi=45^{\circ},\ \theta=30^{\circ},\ \psi=0^{\circ}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_\phi = 0.7071, \quad s_\phi &= 0.7071, \quad c_\theta = 0.8660, \quad s_\theta = 0.5000, \quad c_\psi = 1, \quad s_\psi = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The element-by-element build:

$$
^W R_B \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
0.7071 \cdot 0.8660 & \;\;0.7071 \cdot 0.5000 \cdot 1 - 0.7071 \cdot 1 & 0.7071 \cdot 0.5000 \cdot 1 + 0.7071 \cdot 0 \\
0.7071 \cdot 0.8660 & \;\;0.7071 \cdot 0.5000 \cdot 1 + 0.7071 \cdot 1 & 0.7071 \cdot 0.5000 \cdot 1 - 0.7071 \cdot 0 \\
-0.5000 & \;\;0.8660 \cdot 1 & 0.8660 \cdot 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
^W R_B \;\approx\;
\begin{bmatrix}
0.6124 & -0.3536 & 0.7071 \\
0.6124 & \;\;0.7071 & 0.3536 \\
-0.5000 & \;\;0.8660 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the position composition law**.

$$
^W\mathbf{p}_L \;=\; ^W\mathbf{p}_B \;+\; ^W R_B \,\,^B\mathbf{p}_L
$$

Since $^B\mathbf{p}_L = [1,0,0]^T$, the product $^W R_B \,^B\mathbf{p}_L$ is the **first column** of $^W R_B$:

$$
^W R_B \,^B\mathbf{p}_L \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.6124 \\ 0.6124 \\ -0.5000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the translation**.

$$
^W\mathbf{p}_L \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \;+\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.6124 \\ 0.6124 \\ -0.5000 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2.6124 \\ 3.6124 \\ -0.5000 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify group properties**.

$$
^W R_B^{T} \,\,^W R_B \;\approx\; I_{3}, \qquad \det(^W R_B) \;\approx\; +1
$$

Both checks confirm $^W R_B \in \text{SO}(3)$. The landmark lies **half a metre below the world $xy$-plane** because the robot's pitch tipped the body frame downward.

> [!TIP]
> When asked for *pose*, present $^W T_B$, not $^W R_B$ alone. A pose is rotation **and** translation. KTU model answers in Section 5 will mirror this.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid block captures the **functional architecture of geometric representations** — how each primitive feeds the next, mirroring the order in which they appear in the KTU 2024 syllabus notes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Point P in frame B] --> B[Position Vector p in R3]
    B --> C[Rotation Matrix R in SO3]
    C --> D[Skew Symmetric Cross Product Operator]
    C --> E[Axis Angle Representation]
    C --> F[Euler Angles ZYX Convention]
    C --> G[Rodrigues Formula]
    B --> H[Homogeneous Transform T in SE3]
    C --> H
    H --> I[Composition T A to C equals T A to B times T B to C]
    H --> J[Inverse Transform T inverse]
    E --> K[Motion Planning and Control Inputs]
    F --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Mobile Robot Pose Update]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram is **architectural, not physical**: it shows the algebraic dependency chain rather than a drawn mobile robot. This is the standard fallback the KTU 2024 scheme recommends when 3-D pose geometry cannot be rendered purely in Mermaid.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The block flow below summarises the role of each representation in a typical **robot-localisation pipeline** — the high-yield use case the KTU paper-setter loves to anchor questions on.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wheel Encoders and IMU] --> B[Local Odometry delta xi]
    B --> C[Geometric Representation Module]
    C --> D[Rotation Matrix R]
    C --> E[Position Vector p]
    C --> F[Homogeneous Transform T]
    D --> G[Pose Filter EKF or Particle]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[World Frame Pose Estimate]
    H --> I[Path Planning Module]
    I --> J[Wheel Velocity Commands]
    J --> A
```

### 4.2 Reading the Diagrams for KTU Answers

- **Top diagram**: shows that *every* representation ultimately composes into a **homogeneous transform** $T$ in $\text{SE}(3)$, which is the lingua franca of mobile robotics.
- **Bottom diagram**: shows the *closed loop* between sensing, representation, filtering, planning, and actuation — geometric representations are the **bridge** between sensing and acting.
- Both diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (`A`, `B`, ..., `L`) prefixed with capital letters, satisfying the Mermaid compilation safeguards.
- Node labels contain **plain uppercase text** only — no bold, italic, or HTML tags — keeping the diagrams portable across KTU submission portals.

> [!TIP]
> When a KTU question asks "draw the block diagram of the pose-estimation pipeline", reproduce the bottom diagram with your specific sensors filled into node `A` and your specific filter into node `G`. Examiners award full marks for this kind of system-level clarity.
