| 12 | Sampling Density (PRM) | $n \ge \dfrac{\ln(1/p_f)}{\ln(1 - \pi r^2 / \mu(\mathcal{C}_{free}))}$ | $p_f$ = failure probability, $r$ = connection radius | samples |
| 13 | Holonomic Constraint | $f(q) = 0$ | Reduces DOF by 1 | dimensionless |
| 14 | Non-holonomic Constraint | $f(q, \dot{q}) = 0$ | Cannot reduce DOF by integration | dimensionless |
| 15 | Workspace Velocity Transform | $\dot{x} = J(q) \dot{q}$ | $J$ = Jacobian matrix | $m/s$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Notation:** For absolute value or cardinality expressions in the formula sheet, the vertical bar `|` was deliberately replaced with `\Vert \dots \Vert` (norm) to preserve the markdown table integrity. Students should write $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ in exams when referring to magnitudes, not single pipes.

## 2.3 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Roadmap Construction

The construction of a probabilistic roadmap exemplifies the simplification process:

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Generate a uniform random sample $q_{rand}$ from $\mathcal{C}$.
- **Step 2 — Collision Check:** Verify $q_{rand} \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$ using a geometric test (e.g., distance computation).
- **Step 3 — Neighbor Search:** Identify all existing nodes $q_i$ within radius $r$ of $q_{rand}$.
- **Step 4 — Local Planning:** For each neighbor $q_i$, verify that the straight-line path $\overline{q_i q_{rand}} \subset \mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- **Step 5 — Edge Addition:** Add valid edges to the graph $G = (V, E)$.
- **Step 6 — Termination:** Repeat until $N_{samples}$ is reached or coverage exceeds threshold.

## 2.4 Why the C-Obstacle Transform Simplifies Planning

The transform $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{B} = \mathcal{B} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$ fundamentally reduces the planning problem:

- **Before Transform:** Compute collision between a moving shape $\mathcal{A}(q)$ and static obstacles for every $q \in \mathcal{C}$ — $O(m \cdot n)$ per query.
- **After Transform:** Compute point-in-polygon test for a single point $q$ against a pre-computed CSO — $O(\log n)$ per query.

This reduction in per-query complexity is the cornerstone of practical C-Space simplification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** The Minkowski-sum CSO transform is used in industrial CNC machining for tool-path planning, in autonomous vehicle trajectory planning (driveable corridor computation), and in computational geometry libraries such as CGAL.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Construction of C-Obstacle for a Circular Robot

**Problem Setup.** Consider a circular robot of radius $r$ with reference point at its geometric center. The robot must avoid a convex polygonal obstacle $\mathcal{P}$ with vertices $V_1, V_2, \dots, V_n$ in the workspace.

**Step 1 — Define the Robot Geometry.**
The robot is a closed disk:
$$\mathcal{A} = \{(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid x^2 + y^2 \le r^2\}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Negative Reflection.**
The reflection through the origin yields:
$$-\mathcal{A} = \{(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid (-x)^2 + (-y)^2 \le r^2\} = \mathcal{A}$$

For a circle, the reflection is identical to the original — a critical symmetry.

**Step 3 — Apply the Minkowski Sum.**
By definition:
$$\mathcal{C}\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{P} \oplus (-\mathcal{A}) = \{(p_x + a_x, p_y + a_y) \mid p \in \mathcal{P}, a \in \mathcal{A}\}$$

Substituting the parametric form $a = (r\cos\phi, r\sin\phi)$ for $\phi \in [0, 2\pi)$:
$$\mathcal{C}\mathcal{P} = \{(p_x + r\cos\phi, p_y + r\sin\phi) \mid p \in \mathcal{P}, \phi \in [0, 2\pi)\}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret Geometrically.**
Each point $p$ on the boundary $\partial\mathcal{P}$ traces a circle of radius $r$ centered at $p$. The union of all such circles forms the C-Obstacle boundary, which is a **polygon offset outward by distance $r$**.

$$\partial \mathcal{C}\mathcal{P} = \{q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \text{dist}(q, \partial\mathcal{P}) = r\}$$

**Step 5 — Final Simplified Form.**
For a convex polygon, this is computationally equivalent to:
$$\mathcal{C}\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{P} \oplus r \cdot \mathbb{B}^2$$
where $\mathbb{B}^2$ is the unit ball. The result is a polygon with the same number of vertices, but each edge displaced outward by $r$ along its outward normal.

## 3.2 Derivation II: C-Obstacle for a Polygonal Robot

**Step 1 — Define the Polygonal Robot.**
Let $\mathcal{A}$ be a convex polygon with vertices $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_m$ in counter-clockwise order.

**Step 2 — Reflect Through Origin.**
$$-\mathcal{A} = \{(-a_x, -a_y) \mid a \in \mathcal{A}\}$$
The reflected polygon has vertices $-A_1, -A_2, \dots, -A_m$ in clockwise order.

**Step 3 — Compute Minkowski Sum via Edge Merging.**
For two convex polygons, the Minkowski sum's boundary is a convex polygon whose edges are the sorted angular sequence of the input polygons' edge normals. Algorithmically:

$$\partial(\mathcal{P} \oplus \mathcal{Q}) = \text{ConvexHull}\left(\bigcup_{i,j} \{V_i^{\mathcal{P}} + V_j^{\mathcal{Q}}\}\right)$$

For convex inputs, the sum has at most $m + n$ vertices:
$$|\partial(\mathcal{C}\mathcal{P})| \le m + n$$

**Step 4 — Algorithmic Complexity.**
The brute-force vertex-addition is $O(m \cdot n)$, but the **rotating calipers** technique reduces it to $O(m + n)$.

$$\boxed{T_{Minkowski}(\text{convex}) = O(m + n)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation III: Sampling Complexity for PRM

**Setup.** We want to determine the number of samples $n$ required to ensure that the probability of failing to connect two configurations (within a connection radius $r$) is below $p_f$.

**Step 1 — Probability of Connection.**
For a single sample in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ (with measure $\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})$), the probability that it lies within radius $r$ of a query point is:
$$p_{conn} = \frac{\pi r^2}{\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})}$$
(for 2-D; generalized to $d$-D with $\pi r^d / \mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})$).

**Step 2 — Probability of Failure.**
The probability that no sample is within radius $r$ of the query point is:
$$(1 - p_{conn})^n$$

**Step 3 — Apply Confidence Constraint.**
Setting $(1 - p_{conn})^n \le p_f$:
$$n \ln(1 - p_{conn}) \le \ln(p_f)$$

Since $\ln(1 - x) < 0$ for $x \in (0, 1)$:

$$n \ge \frac{\ln(p_f)}{\ln(1 - p_{conn})} = \frac{\ln(1/p_f)}{\ln\left(\frac{1}{1 - p_{conn}}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Final Form (approximated for small $p_{conn}$).**
Using $\ln(1 - x) \approx -x$ for small $x$:

$$\boxed{n \ge \frac{\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free}) \cdot \ln(1/p_f)}{\pi r^2}}$$

This derivation shows the **sampling density** scales linearly with the C-Space volume and logarithmically with the inverse failure probability.

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Cell Count in Approximate Cell Decomposition

**Step 1 — Discretization Assumption.**
A $d$-dimensional C-Space bounded region of side length $L$ is decomposed into hypercubes of side $\epsilon$.

**Step 2 — Cell Count Computation.**
The number of cells along each dimension is $L/\epsilon$, giving:

$$N_{cells} = \left(\frac{L}{\epsilon}\right)^d$$

**Step 3 — Memory Complexity.**
Each cell requires $O(1)$ storage, hence memory is:
$$M = \left(\frac{L}{\epsilon}\right)^d \cdot k \text{ bits}$$
where $k$ is the bits per cell.

**Step 4 — Example Calculation.**
For $L = 10\,m$, $\epsilon = 0.01\,m$, $d = 3$ (x, y, $\theta$ discrete):

$$N_{cells} = \left(\frac{10}{0.01}\right)^3 = 10^9 \text{ cells}$$

This is the **curse of dimensionality** in action — discretization at centimeter resolution of a 3-D C-Space already requires a billion cells.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Table

| Method | Completeness | Optimality | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Best Use Case |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Visibility Graph | Exact | Optimal | $O(N^2 \log N)$ | $O(N^2)$ | Sparse 2-D polygonal maps |
| Voronoi Diagram | Exact | Maximally clear | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N)$ | Wide-open environments |
| Trapezoidal Decomp. | Exact | Near-optimal | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N)$ | Bounded 2-D workspaces |
| Boustrophedon Decomp. | Exact | Coverage-optimal | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N)$ | Coverage path planning |
| PRM | Probabilistic | Resolution-optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Multi-query, static env. |
| RRT | Probabilistic | Suboptimal | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Single-query, high-DOF |
| Potential Field | None | Local optimum | $O(1)$ per step | $O(N)$ | Real-time reactive control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation clarity:** $N$ = number of workspace features (obstacles/vertices); $n$ = number of samples. The hyphen in "2-D" is intentional to avoid the parser misinterpreting the dash as a markdown list bullet.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 C-Space Transformation Block Diagram

The following block-level functional architecture diagram illustrates the C-Space simplification pipeline from workspace input to motion plan output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workspace W] --> B[Robot Geometry A]
    A --> C[Obstacles B]
    B --> D[Minkowski Sum Transform]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Configuration Space C]
    E --> F[Compute CSO = B minus A]
    F --> G{Method Selection}
    G --> H[Roadmap Method]
    G --> I[Cell Decomposition]
    G --> J[Sampling Method PRM]
    G --> K[Potential Field]
    H --> L[Graph Search A star]
    I --> M[Adjacency Graph]
    J --> N[Probabilistic Connectivity]
    K --> O[Gradient Descent]
    L --> P[Path Extraction]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Feasible Path in C-free]
    Q --> R[Inverse Transform to Workspace]
    R --> S[Motion Plan Output]
```

## 4.2 Roadmap vs Cell Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RoadmapParadigm
        R1[Sampling Nodes] --> R2[Connect Valid Pairs]
        R2 --> R3[Graph G with V and E]
        R3 --> R4[Search Start to Goal]
    end
    subgraph CellDecompParadigm
        C1[Decompose C-free] --> C2[Label Cells Free or Obstacle]
        C2 --> C3[Build Cell Adjacency]
        C3 --> C4[Search Start to Goal]
    end
    R4 --> Outcome1[Collision-free Path]
    C4 --> Outcome1
```

## 4.3 PRM Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> S1[Sample q-random from C]
    S1 --> S2{q in C-free?}
    S2 -- No --> S1
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Add to Vertex Set V]
    S3 --> S4[Find Neighbors within radius r]
    S4 --> S5[Check Local Path in C-free]
    S5 -- Valid --> S6[Add Edge to E]
    S5 -- Invalid --> S4
    S6 --> S7{Stop Condition Met?}
    S7 -- No --> S1
    S7 -- Yes --> End([Roadmap Ready for Query])
```

## 4.4 Potential Field Force Vector Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ForceComputation
        Goal[Goal Configuration qg] --> Attr[Attractive Force F-att]
        Obst[C-Obstacles] --> Rep[Repulsive Force F-rep]
        Attr --> Sum[Vector Sum F-total]
        Rep --> Sum
    end
    Sum --> Grad[Negative Gradient Descent]
    Grad --> Move[Update Configuration q]
    Move --> Check{Collides?}
    Check -- Yes --> Stop[Stuck at Local Minimum]
    Check -- No --> Goal
```

## 4.5 Configuration Space Partition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C[Configuration Space C] --> Free[Free Space C-free]
    C --> Obs[Obstacle Space C-obs]
    C --> Boundary[Boundary Partial C-obs]
    Free --> Path1[Path 1 feasible]
    Free --> Path2[Path 2 feasible]
    Free --> Path3[Path 3 infeasible due to C-obs]
    Boundary --> Critical[Critical Configurations]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic note:** All node identifiers in the Mermaid blocks above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `R1`, `C2`, `S5`) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard rules. No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Configuration Space and list its subsets. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Configuration Space (C-Space)** of a robot is the set of all possible configurations that the robot can attain. For a mobile robot moving in a 2-D plane, the C-Space is given by $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$, where $S^1$ represents the angular orientation $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$. The C-Space is partitioned into two mutually exclusive subsets:

1. **Free Space ($\mathcal{C}_{free}$):** All configurations where the robot does not collide with any obstacle.
2. **Obstacle Space ($\mathcal{C}_{obs}$):** All configurations where the robot intersects an obstacle.

Mathematically: $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}_{free} \cup \mathcal{C}_{obs}$, with $\mathcal{C}_{free} \cap \mathcal{C}_{obs} = \emptyset$.

### Question 2: What is the role of the Minkowski Sum in C-Space simplification? `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Minkowski Sum transform converts a complex collision-detection problem into a simple point-in-polygon query. For a robot $\mathcal{A}$ and obstacle $\mathcal{B}$, the C-Obstacle is defined as $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{B} = \mathcal{B} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$. This effectively reduces the robot to a single reference point and "grows" the obstacles to compensate. The benefit is a per-query complexity reduction from $O(m \cdot n)$ (shape-shape intersection) to $O(\log n)$ (point-in-polygon), enabling real-time motion planning.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: C-Obstacle Computation and Simplification

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Define Configuration Space and C-Obstacles. Explain with neat diagrams the procedure to construct a C-Obstacle for a circular robot moving in a 2-D workspace. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **Visibility Graph** method of roadmap construction for C-Space simplification. Discuss its completeness and complexity. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Definition (2 marks):** The Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all possible configurations of the robot. The C-Obstacle $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{B}_i$ corresponding to a workspace obstacle $\mathcal{B}_i$ is the set of all configurations in which the robot $\mathcal{A}(q)$ intersects $\mathcal{B}_i$.

**Procedure (5 marks):** For a circular robot of radius $r$:

1. Identify the convex hull of the workspace obstacle polygon.
2. Compute the outward normal at each edge.
3. Translate each edge outward by distance $r$ along its outward normal.
4. Connect the translated edges to form the new polygon.
5. The resulting polygon is the C-Obstacle $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{B}$.

Formally: $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{B} = \mathcal{B} \oplus \mathbb{B}^2(r)$, where $\mathbb{B}^2(r)$ is the disk of radius $r$.

**Diagram description:** A square obstacle of side $s$ becomes a C-Obstacle of side $s + 2r$ with rounded corners (quarter circles of radius $r$). The reference point of the robot is now treated as a point, and the obstacle is "inflated" by the robot's radius.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Construction (4 marks):** The Visibility Graph is a graph $G = (V, E)$ where:

- **Vertices ($V$):** Start configuration $q_s$, goal configuration $q_g$, and all vertices of all C-Obstacles.
- **Edges ($E$):** Connect two vertices with a straight line if the entire line segment lies in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

A path is found by running **$A^*$** (or Dijkstra) on this graph from $q_s$ to $q_g$. The shortest path in the visibility graph corresponds to a collision-free path that is piecewise linear, with breakpoints at obstacle vertices.

**Properties (3 marks):**

- **Completeness:** Complete — finds a path if one exists.
- **Optimality:** Optimal in path length.
- **Complexity:** Construction is $O(N^2 \log N)$ using rotating calipers; graph search is $O(N^2)$ with $A^*$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget that the Visibility Graph edges must be tested for collision — direct vertex-to-vertex connections can pass *through* obstacles. Always verify $\overline{q_i q_j} \subset \mathcal{C}_{free}$ before adding an edge. Also, the Visibility Graph does **not** work for non-convex obstacles without first decomposing them into convex pieces.

---

### Question B: Sampling-Based Simplification Methods

`[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM)** algorithm for C-Space simplification. Describe its construction and query phases with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the **PRM** and **RRT** algorithms in terms of completeness, optimality, and computational complexity. State one application scenario where each is most suitable. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Overview (1 mark):** PRM is a multi-query, sampling-based planner that constructs a graph in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ for repeated use across multiple start-goal queries.

**Construction Phase (4 marks):**

1. Sample $n$ configurations $q_i$ uniformly at random from $\mathcal{C}$.
2. Discard samples in $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ (collision check).
3. For each valid sample $q_i$, find all neighbors $q_j$ within connection radius $r$.
4. For each neighbor pair, verify the local path is in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.
5. Add valid edges to the graph.

**Query Phase (2 marks):**

1. Given $q_s$ and $q_g$, attempt to connect both to the existing graph.
2. Run $A^*$ or Dijkstra on the augmented graph.
3. Extract the path and convert to a sequence of robot trajectories.

**Diagram description:** Nodes (filled circles) scattered in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ with edges (lines) connecting nearby collision-free pairs. Start/goal shown as larger markers connected to the nearest valid nodes.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

| Criterion | PRM | RRT |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Completeness** | Probabilistically complete | Probabilistically complete |
| **Optimality** | Asymptotically optimal (with rewiring) | Not optimal; can be post-processed |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n \log n)$ construction + $O(\log n)$ query | $O(n \log n)$ per query |
| **Space** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Best For** | Multi-query, static environments | Single-query, dynamic or unknown environments |

**Application (1 mark each):** PRM suits warehouse robots with repeated tasks in static layouts. RRT suits planetary rovers navigating unknown terrain with single mission plans.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly confuse the **construction phase** (offline) with the **query phase** (online) of PRM. Clearly distinguishing them is worth 1–2 marks. Also, do not claim PRM is "complete" — it is only **probabilistically complete**, meaning the probability of finding a path approaches 1 as $n \to \infty$ if a path exists.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for C-Space Simplification**

- **Definition:** C-Space $\mathcal{C}$ is the manifold of all possible robot configurations. For planar rigid robots, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ with $dim = 3$.
- **Partition:** $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}_{free} \cup \mathcal{C}_{obs}$, with $\mathcal{C}_{free} \cap \mathcal{C}_{obs} = \emptyset$.
- **Minkowski Sum Transform:** $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{B} = \mathcal{B} \oplus (-\mathcal{A})$ reduces shape-shape collision to point-in-polygon.
- **Four Simplification Paradigms:** Roadmaps, Cell Decomposition, Sampling, Potential Fields.
- **Visibility Graph:** Complete, optimal, $O(N^2 \log N)$ construction, vertices = obstacles + start + goal.
- **Voronoi Diagram:** Complete, maximizes clearance, $O(N \log N)$ construction, ideal for open spaces.
- **Trapezoidal Decomposition:** $O(N \log N)$, exact cell decomposition, well-suited for polygonal workspaces.
- **PRM:** Multi-query, probabilistically complete, $O(n \log n)$ construction, ideal for static environments.
- **RRT:** Single-query, probabilistically complete, Voronoi-biased exploration, ideal for high-DOF or dynamic.
- **Potential Field:** Real-time, gradient-based, $O(1)$ per step, suffers from local minima.
- **Curse of Dimensionality:** Cell count grows as $(L/\epsilon)^d$ — discretization fails for high-$d$ C-Space.
- **Holonomic vs Non-holonomic:** Holonomic constraints reduce DOF; non-holonomic constraints do not.
- **Formula to Memorize:** $n \ge \dfrac{\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free}) \cdot \ln(1/p_f)}{\pi r^2}$ for PRM sampling bound.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw the workspace and the transformed C-Space side by side. Marks are awarded for *both* the diagram and the explanation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Word of Advice:** In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, part (a) of 14-mark questions typically tests definitions and basic procedures (Bloom's *Remember/Understand* levels), while part (b) tests analytical comparison and trade-off analysis (Bloom's *Analyze/Evaluate* levels). Structure your answer accordingly: definitions first, then derivations, then comparative analysis with tables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free-Space Path versus Semi-Free-Space Path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Free-Space Path versus Semi-Free-Space Path ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition In mobile robotics, the concept of a **Configuration Space (C-space)** is the mathematical framework within which all motion planning problems are formulated. Every distinct pose (position + orientation) of the robot corresponds to a single point in this multidimensional space. The C-space is partitioned into three mutually exclusive subsets: 1. **Free Space ($\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$):** The set of all robot configurations in which the robot does **not** collide with any obstacle in the workspace. 2. **Obstacle Space ($\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$):** The complement of free space — configurations where the robot physically intersects one or more obstacles. 3. **Boundary / Contact Space ($\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$):** Configurations where the robot is in exact tangential contact with an obstacle's boundary. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Free-Space Path (FSP):** A continuous mapping $\tau : [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ such that $\tau(0) = q_{\text{start}}$, $\tau(1) = q_{\text{goal}}$, and for every $s \in [0,1]$, the configuration $\tau(s)$ lies strictly inside the free space (i.e., $\tau(s) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Semi-Free-Space Path (SFSP):** A continuous mapping $\tau : [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ such that $\tau(0) = q_{\text{start}}$, $\tau(1) = q_{\text{goal}}$, and for every $s \in [0,1]$, the configuration $\tau(s)$ lies in the free space **OR** in exact tangential contact with an obstacle boundary. SFSP permits the robot to "graze" obstacle walls but never to penetrate them. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are walking a large, rectangular sofa through a narrow hallway. - A **Free-Space Path** is like instructing someone to move the sofa through the hallway such that, at every instant, there is a comfortable gap (a strict interior clearance) between the sofa and **every** wall. The sofa must always be safely floating inside the clear corridor. - A **Semi-Free-Space Path** is like saying: "You may slide the sofa along the wall — touching is fine, crashing through is not." The sofa can skim the wall, touch the door frame, or rotate while in contact, as long as it never actually penetrates (crashes into) a solid object. > [!NOTE] > In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this distinction is fundamental to motion-graph algorithms like the **Visibility Graph** and **Voronoi Diagram**, where semi-free paths are exploited to produce **shortest-length trajectories** that are not achievable when strict free-space-only constraints are imposed. ### Standard Metrics Used - **Path Length** $\mathcal{L}(\tau)$ — measured in meters (m) or workspace units. - **Clearance** $\delta(q)$ — minimum Euclidean distance from the robot to the nearest obstacle surface, measured in meters (m). A **safe** free-space path requires $\delta(q) > 0$ for all $q \in \tau$. - **Workspace dimension** $W \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{2}$ (or $\mathbb{R}^{3}$) — usually meters squared ($\mathbf{m^2}$). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Set-Theoretic Decomposition of C-Space The complete configuration space can be expressed as: $$ \mathcal{C} \;=\; \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \;\cup\; \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} $$ with the disjointness property: $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \;\cap\; \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} \;=\; \emptyset $$ The semi-free space is the topological closure of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ within $\mathcal{C}$: $$ \mathcal{C}_{\text{semi-free}} \;=\; \overline{\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}} \;=\; \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} $$ ### 2.2 Classification of Paths - A **Free-Space Path (FSP)** requires: $$ \forall\, s \in [0,1] : \tau(s) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} $$ - A **Semi-Free-Space Path (SFSP)** requires: $$ \forall\, s \in [0,1] : \tau(s) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} $$ - Every FSP is also an SFSP, but the converse is not true. Formally: $$ \{\text{FSP}\} \;\subsetneq\; \{\text{SFSP}\} $$ ### 2.3 Operational Logic — Why SFSP Matters 1. **Shortest path guarantee:** In a polygonal 2D workspace, the **shortest collision-free path** between two configurations is **always a Semi-Free-Space Path**, not a strict FSP. The optimal trajectory is built from straight-line segments whose vertices lie on obstacle vertices (or on the start/goal). 2. **Boundary tangency is physically realizable:** A real robot can roll along a wall — its body in contact with the wall surface is still a feasible configuration. 3. **Strict free-space constraints inflate path length** because any infinitesimal clearance from obstacles adds detour length, and rounding corners introduces unnecessary curvature. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Constraint / Condition | Units | |---|---------|-------------------|------------------------|-------| | 1 | Configuration Space | $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | Disjoint union | — | | 2 | Semi-free space | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{semi-free}} = \overline{\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}}$ | Closed set | — | | 3 | Free-Space Path | $\tau:[0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | $\delta(\tau(s)) > 0$ for all $s$ | — | | 4 | Semi-Free-Space Path | $\tau:[0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | $\delta(\tau(s)) \geq 0$ for all $s$ | — | | 5 | Path length | $\mathcal{L}(\tau) = \int_{0}^{1} \left\| \frac{d\tau}{ds} \right\| ds$ | Integral of norm of tangent | m | | 6 | Clearance | $\delta(q) = \min_{o \in \mathcal{O}} d(\mathcal{R}(q), o)$ | Min distance to obstacle | m | | 7 | Minkowski sum (C-obstacle) | $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_{i} = \mathcal{O}_{i} \ominus \mathcal{R}$ | Robot swept backwards | m or $\mathbf{m^2}$ | | 8 | Forbidden region | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \bigcup_{i} \mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_{i}$ | Union of C-obstacles | — | > [!NOTE] > In the table above, $\left\| \cdot \right\|$ denotes the Euclidean norm; $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ is Euclidean distance; $\mathcal{R}$ is the robot body and $\mathcal{O}_{i}$ is the $i$-th workspace obstacle. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Autonomous vehicles:** Lane-keeping and curb-following behaviours are semi-free paths (vehicle wheel may graze the lane marking). - **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Operating in tight aisles, the shortest path between two racks often involves sliding the AGV along a rack edge. - **Surgical robotics:** Instrument tip navigation along tissue surface exploits SFSP principles. - **Drone swarms:** Wall-following flight in indoor environments is naturally an SFSP problem. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparing FSP and SFSP | Property | Free-Space Path (FSP) | Semi-Free-Space Path (SFSP) | |----------|------------------------|------------------------------| | Mathematical image | $\tau([0,1]) \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | $\tau([0,1]) \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | | Clearance condition | $\delta(\tau(s)) > 0 \; \forall s$ | $\delta(\tau(s)) \geq 0 \; \forall s$ | | Wall contact allowed? | **No** (strictly interior) | **Yes** (tangential contact permitted) | | Optimality in length | May be **non-minimal** | **Shortest-length** in polygonal C-space | | Existence guarantee | Requires $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ to be open-connected | Requires $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ to be path-connected | | Constructive method | Cell decomposition, RRT, PRM | Visibility graph, shortest-path roadmaps | | Safety margin | High (built-in clearance) | Zero minimum (must add safety buffer externally) | | Control complexity | Simpler (open-loop feasible) | Higher (requires contact-aware control) | ### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Path Length of an SFSP Visibility-Graph Edge Consider a 2D polygonal workspace with a polygonal obstacle having vertices $V_i = (x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. A semi-free path is composed of straight-line segments connecting the start $q_s$, a sequence of obstacle vertices $V_{i_1}, V_{i_2}, \ldots, V_{i_k}$, and the goal $q_g$. The **total length** of such a path is the sum of the Euclidean distances of consecutive segments: $$ \mathcal{L}(\tau) \;=\; \sum_{j=0}^{k} \left\| P_{j+1} - P_{j} \right\| $$ where $P_0 = q_s$, $P_{k+1} = q_g$, and $P_j = V_{i_j}$ for $j = 1, \ldots, k$. **Step-by-step expansion of a single segment length** (segment from $P_j$ to $P_{j+1}$): $$ \left\| P_{j+1} - P_j \right\| \;=\; \sqrt{(x_{j+1} - x_j)^2 + (y_{j+1} - y_j)^2} $$ **Derivation of the shortest-path condition.** For an SFSP, the path is a candidate only if every intermediate vertex $P_j$ lies on the convex-hull boundary or on an obstacle vertex such that the segment $P_{j-1}P_{j+1}$ does not intersect any $\mathcal{C}$-obstacle interior. This is the **tangency-conditional shortest path** property. The key analytical statement is: > In a polygonal environment, the **shortest** collision-free path between $q_s$ and $q_g$ is **always** a semi-free path whose intermediate nodes are vertices of the C-obstacle polygon(s). This follows from the **triangle inequality applied to the broken-line SFSP** vs. the straight-line FSP: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}(\text{straight FSP}) &\;\geq\; \mathcal{L}(\text{any broken SFSP via } V_{i}) \\ &\;\geq\; \min_{V_{i} \in \text{vertices}} \left( \left\| q_s - V_{i} \right\| + \left\| V_{i} - q_g \right\| \right) \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Existence Test for a Free-Space Path A free-space path from $q_s$ to $q_g$ exists **if and only if** $q_s$ and $q_g$ belong to the same **connected component** of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. This can be tested using a graph-search algorithm (BFS, DFS) over a sampled roadmap of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. ### 3.4 Existence Test for a Semi-Free-Space Path A semi-free path exists if $q_s$ and $q_g$ belong to the same connected component of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. Note that this set is **larger** than $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ alone, so an SFSP may exist where an FSP does not — for example, in a maze whose only exit has zero clearance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Insight:** The difference between FSP and SFSP existence is a topological one. SFSP existence is governed by the path-connectedness of the **closed** set $\overline{\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}}$, while FSP existence is governed by the path-connectedness of the **open** set $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. A narrow corridor of zero width admits SFSP (a robot the exact width of the corridor) but **not** FSP. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Set-Theoretic Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["C-space Total"] nodeB["Free Space C_free"] nodeC["Obstacle Space C_obs"] nodeD["Boundary dC_obs"] nodeE["FSP exists if q_s, q_g in same component of C_free"] nodeF["SFSP exists if q_s, q_g in same component of C_free union dC_obs"] nodeG["Visibility Graph uses SFSP shortest path"] nodeH["Voronoi Diagram maximises clearance but uses FSP"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeG ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Path Planner ```mermaid flowchart LR inputA["Workspace Map W"] inputB["Robot Geometry R"] inputC["Start Config q_s"] inputD["Goal Config q_g"] proc1["C-space Constructor
C_obs = Union of C-Obstacles
via Minkowski Difference"] proc2["Path Existence Test
Connectivity of C_free
vs C_free union Boundary"] proc3["Path Type Selector
Strict FSP or SFSP"] proc4["Path Generator
Visibility Graph / RRT / PRM"] outA["Free-Space Path tau_F"] outB["Semi-Free-Space Path tau_SF"] inputA --> proc1 inputB --> proc1 inputC --> proc2 inputD --> proc2 proc1 --> proc2 proc2 --> proc3 proc3 --> proc4 proc3 --> outA proc4 --> outB ``` ### 4.3 Decision-Flow Diagram — FSP vs SFSP Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD s1["Receive q_s and q_g"] s2["Build C_obs and C_free"] s3{"Are q_s and q_g
in same connected
component of C_free?"} s4["Generate FSP
e.g. via Cell Decomposition or RRT"] s5{"Are q_s and q_g
in same component of
C_free union Boundary?"} s6["Generate SFSP
e.g. via Visibility Graph"] s7["No path exists
Report Failure"] s1 --> s2 s2 --> s3 s3 -- YES --> s4 s3 -- NO --> s5 s5 -- YES --> s6 s5 -- NO --> s7 ``` > [!NOTE] > These diagrams map the **algorithmic / set-theoretic** flow used in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 motion planning. They translate the abstract set relations into a tractable engineering pipeline that the student should be able to reproduce in a written examination. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** Define a *Free-Space Path* and a *Semi-Free-Space Path* in the context of configuration space. State the key mathematical condition that distinguishes them. **Model Answer:** A Free-Space Path (FSP) is a continuous mapping $\tau:[0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ such that $\tau(0)=q_s$ and $\tau(1)=q_g$, with the image entirely inside the open free space. A Semi-Free-Space Path (SFSP) is a continuous mapping $\tau:[0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$, permitting tangential contact with obstacle boundaries. The distinguishing condition is the **clearance inequality**: FSP requires $\delta(\tau(s)) > 0$ for all $s$, whereas SFSP allows $\delta(\tau(s)) \geq 0$. **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** Give one example where a Semi-Free-Space Path exists but a strict Free-Space Path does not. Justify briefly. **Model Answer:** Consider a mobile robot whose width exactly matches a narrow corridor between two obstacles, with start and goal configurations on opposite ends of the corridor. The corridor has **zero width clearance** for the robot interior, so no strict FSP exists ($\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is disconnected across the corridor). However, an SFSP exists by sliding the robot in perfect contact with the corridor walls, so $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ remains connected across the passage. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, illustrate the configuration space of a circular mobile robot translating in a 2D polygonal workspace containing two polygonal obstacles. Clearly mark the free space, the obstacle region, and the boundary. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain why the shortest collision-free path in a polygonal 2D environment is always a Semi-Free-Space Path. Use the visibility-graph construction as the basis of your argument. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1:** Define the workspace $W \subset \mathbb{R}^{2}$ with two polygonal obstacles $\mathcal{O}_1$ and $\mathcal{O}_2$ and a circular robot of radius $r$. **Step 2:** Compute the C-obstacle for each workspace obstacle using the Minkowski difference: $$ \mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_{i} \;=\; \mathcal{O}_{i} \;\ominus\; \mathcal{R} \;=\; \{ \, q \in \mathcal{C} \;\mid\; \mathcal{R}(q) \cap \mathcal{O}_{i} \neq \emptyset \,\} $$ For a circular robot, this is equivalent to **inflating** the workspace obstacle by $r$ (taking the morphological dilation by a disc of radius $r$). **Step 3:** Identify the three sets: - $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = W \setminus \bigcup_i \mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_{i}$ (drawn as white region) - $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \bigcup_i \mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_{i}$ (drawn shaded) - $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ (drawn as the bold outline of each shaded region) **Step 4:** Mark the start $q_s$ and goal $q_g$ inside $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, and show two illustrative paths: one strict FSP (curved, with clearance) and one SFSP (straight, grazing a vertex). ##### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1:** Recall the **Visibility Graph** construction: nodes are $q_s$, $q_g$, and **all vertices of all C-obstacles**. An edge connects two nodes if the straight line between them lies entirely within $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ (i.e., it does not pass through the **interior** of any C-obstacle). **Step 2:** The shortest path from $q_s$ to $q_g$ over the visibility graph, computed via Dijkstra's or A* algorithm, gives the length-minimising semi-free path. This path consists of straight-line segments that may terminate on obstacle vertices; each endpoint of each segment is a **boundary configuration** $\in \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. **Step 3:** Argue optimality: by the **funnel / elastic-band principle**, any shortest collision-free curve in a polygonal environment can be "snapped" to obstacle vertices without increasing length. The result is piecewise-linear with breakpoints on the obstacle boundary — exactly an SFSP. **Step 4:** Conclude that no strict FSP can be shorter, because any strict FSP with $\delta > 0$ can be perturbed onto obstacle boundaries, monotonically decreasing its length until it becomes an SFSP. #### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme Pattern — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Derive the condition under which a free-space path does **not** exist between two configurations, but a semi-free-space path does. State a real-world scenario that exhibits this behaviour. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare Free-Space Path and Semi-Free-Space Path in terms of (i) clearance, (ii) optimal path length, (iii) planner algorithm used, and (iv) safety guarantee. Present your answer in a structured comparative table. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1:** The condition is that $q_s$ and $q_g$ lie in **different connected components** of the open set $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, but in the **same connected component** of the closed set $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. **Step 2:** Formally: an FSP does not exist iff $q_s$ and $q_g$ are separated by an obstacle of positive thickness that fully blocks any open clearance. An SFSP exists iff the only route between $q_s$ and $q_g$ involves configurations touching an obstacle's boundary tangentially. **Step 3:** **Real-world example:** a refrigerator of width exactly equal to a doorway width. The fridge cannot pass through the doorway with any clearance (no strict FSP), but it can pass through in sliding contact with the door frame (SFSP exists). Equivalently in robotics, a snake-like manipulator negotiating a tube of inner diameter equal to the manipulator's outer diameter. ##### Model Solution for (b) | Aspect | Free-Space Path | Semi-Free-Space Path | |--------|------------------|------------------------| | (i) Clearance | Strictly positive, $\delta(\tau(s)) > 0$ | Non-negative, $\delta(\tau(s)) \geq 0$ | | (ii) Optimal length | Generally **non-minimal** | **Minimal** in polygonal C-space | | (iii) Planner algorithm | Cell decomposition, RRT, PRM, Voronoi diagram | Visibility graph, shortest-path roadmap | | (iv) Safety guarantee | High (built-in clearance buffer) | Low (zero minimum clearance — external safety margin required) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do not confuse "free space" with "empty workspace".** Free space is a property of the configuration space, not the workspace. Students who write "free space = empty area in the room" lose marks. > 2. **Always state the clearance inequality explicitly** ($\delta > 0$ vs. $\delta \geq 0$). Omitting the inequality loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Visibility graph yields SFSP, not FSP.** A common error is to claim the visibility graph produces free-space paths. The edges terminate on obstacle vertices, making the path a **semi-free** path by definition. > 4. **Forgetting the Minkowski difference** when drawing the C-obstacle for a robot of finite size. A point-robot C-obstacle equals the workspace obstacle, but a circular/rectangular robot's C-obstacle is the **inflated** obstacle. > 5. **SFSP ⊃ FSP, not the reverse.** Always write the inclusion correctly. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is open; $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is closed.** This topological distinction dictates which paths can exist. - **FSP** $\Rightarrow$ **SFSP** (every free-space path is also semi-free), but not vice versa. - **Shortest collision-free path in a polygonal C-space is always an SFSP**, generated by the **visibility graph**. - **Strict FSP may not exist** even when an SFSP does, when corridors have zero width clearance. - **Clearance inequality** is the defining mathematical test: $\delta > 0$ for FSP, $\delta \geq 0$ for SFSP. - **Minkowski difference** $\ominus$ is the operation that converts workspace obstacles into configuration-space obstacles, accounting for robot geometry. - **Visibility graph** nodes = $\{q_s, q_g\} \cup \{\text{all obstacle vertices}\}$; edges are tangent-permitting straight lines. - **Voronoi diagram** maximises clearance (pure FSP approach) but does **not** give shortest paths. - **Engineering trade-off:** FSP = safer, longer; SFSP = shorter, requires external safety margins. - **Real-world examples:** warehouse AGVs in narrow aisles, surgical tools along tissue, autonomous vehicles lane-keeping.

topics.title: Non-Homotopic Paths versus Homotopic Paths
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Non-Homotopic Paths versus Homotopic Paths # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Path in Configuration Space Let $\mathcal{C}$ denote the **Configuration Space (C-space)** of the robot. A **path** is defined as a continuous mapping from the unit interval into C-space: $$\tau : [0, 1] \longrightarrow \mathcal{C}$$ where $\tau(0) = q_{start}$ and $\tau(1) = q_{goal}$ are the initial and goal configurations, respectively. A path is said to be **collision-free** (or **feasible**) if and only if $\tau(s) \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$ for all $s \in [0, 1]$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A path is a *one-dimensional curve* embedded in the (typically high-dimensional) configuration space. The path itself is *not* the trajectory in the workspace — it is a sequence of robot configurations. ## 1.2 Formal Definition of Homotopy Two collision-free paths $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ (sharing the same start and goal configurations) are said to be **homotopic** if one can be continuously deformed into the other without leaving the free space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$. Formally, there exists a continuous function $$h : [0, 1] \times [0, 1] \longrightarrow \mathcal{C}_{free}$$ called a **homotopy**, satisfying the boundary conditions: $$h(s, 0) = \tau_1(s), \quad h(s, 1) = \tau_2(s)$$ $$h(0, t) = q_{start}, \quad h(1, t) = q_{goal}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Homotopy Class:** An *equivalence class* of paths under the homotopy relation. The set of all such equivalence classes for loops (closed paths) at a given basepoint forms the **Fundamental Group** $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{free})$. ## 1.3 Homotopic vs Non-Homotopic Paths — The Distinction | Property | Homotopic Paths | Non-Homotopic Paths | |----------|----------------|---------------------| | Continuous deformation | One can be smoothly morphed into the other | No continuous deformation exists | | Topological obstruction | None (same equivalence class) | Obstacles in C-space prevent deformation | | Winding number | Identical winding around all obstacles | Different winding numbers | | Free-space connectivity | Both lie in the same connected component of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ | They may wrap around obstacles differently | ## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **Think of it this way:** Imagine two hikers starting at point A and ending at point B on a mountain that has a circular lake in the middle. One hiker walks clockwise around the lake (Path 1), the other walks counter-clockwise (Path 2). No matter how much the hikers "wobble" their paths closer to or farther from the lake, **one can never be continuously deformed into the other without crossing the lake** (the obstacle). Hence, these are **non-homotopic paths**. However, two paths that both go on the *same side* of the lake are **homotopic** — you can smoothly slide one into the other. In mobile robotics, the "lake" corresponds to obstacles in configuration space, and the "hikers" represent feasible robot paths. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation: Properties of Homotopy The homotopy relation $\simeq$ is an **equivalence relation** on the set of all paths in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reflexivity:** Every path is homotopic to itself via the identity homotopy $h(s, t) = \tau(s)$. > > **Symmetry:** If $\tau_1 \simeq \tau_2$ via $h$, then $\tau_2 \simeq \tau_1$ via $h'(s, t) = h(s, 1-t)$. > > **Transitivity:** If $\tau_1 \simeq \tau_2$ via $h_1$ and $\tau_2 \simeq \tau_3$ via $h_2$, then $\tau_1 \simeq \tau_3$ via the concatenation $$h(s, t) = \begin{cases} h_1(s, 2t) & 0 \leq t \leq \frac{1}{2} \\ h_2(s, 2t - 1) & \frac{1}{2} \leq t \leq 1 \end{cases}$$ ## 2.2 The Concept of Homotopy Classes The set of equivalence classes $\{\tau\}$ under homotopy forms a partition of the path space. A common way to **distinguish homotopy classes** is by: 1. **Winding Number** — An integer-valued topological invariant counting how many times a loop winds around an obstacle. 2. **Fundamental Group** — The algebraic structure $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{free})$ that captures all distinct loops. 3. **Reeb Graph / Hasse Diagram** — Graph structures that encode homotopy classes of paths in a region. ## 2.3 Why Non-Homotopic Paths Matter in Mobile Robotics > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In cluttered environments, multiple *qualitatively different* paths exist between two configurations (e.g., going left vs. right of an obstacle). These are non-homotopic. The robotics engineer must: > 1. Identify all *topologically distinct* path classes. > 2. Compute the **optimal representative** within each class (e.g., shortest path). > 3. Return the globally optimal solution. This problem is formalized as the **Homotopic Path Planning Problem** and is foundational to advanced planners like **Homotopic Roadmap Planning**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | |---|---------|------------------------|-------------| | 1 | Path definition | $\tau : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}$ | Continuous map from interval to C-space | | 2 | Boundary conditions | $\tau(0) = q_{s}, \; \tau(1) = q_{g}$ | Start and goal configurations | | 3 | Homotopy function | $h : [0,1] \times [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{free}$ | Continuous 2-parameter deformation | | 4 | Homotopy equivalence | $\tau_1 \simeq \tau_2$ | Paths lie in same equivalence class | | 5 | Concatenation | $\tau_1 * \tau_2$ | Joining two paths end-to-start | | 6 | Reversal | $\bar{\tau}(s) = \tau(1-s)$ | Reverse traversal of a path | | 7 | Winding number | $n(\tau, p) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \oint_{\tau} d\theta$ | Integer count of encirclements of point $p$ | | 8 | Fundamental group | $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{free}, q_0)$ | Group of loop classes at basepoint $q_0$ | | 9 | Number of classes | $\vert \pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{free}) \vert$ | Cardinality of distinct homotopy classes | | 10 | Simply connected condition | $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{free}) = \{e\}$ | All loops contractible to a point | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Examples ## 3.1 Example 1: Two Paths Around a Point Obstacle in 2D Consider a point robot in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with a circular obstacle $\mathcal{O}$ of radius $r$ centered at the origin. Two paths $\tau_1$ (going above) and $\tau_2$ (going below) connect $q_s = (-d, 0)$ to $q_g = (d, 0)$ with $d > r$. **Step 1: Parametrize the two paths.** $$\tau_1(s) = \begin{pmatrix} -d + 2ds \\ r \end{pmatrix}, \quad s \in [0, 1]$$ $$\tau_2(s) = \begin{pmatrix} -d + 2ds \\ -r \end{pmatrix}, \quad s \in [0, 1]$$ **Step 2: Attempt to construct a homotopy.** Suppose we try $$h(s, t) = \begin{pmatrix} -d + 2ds \\ (1 - 2t)r \end{pmatrix}$$ **Step 3: Check the obstacle constraint.** The y-coordinate of $h$ is zero when $t = 1/2$. At this moment, the path passes through the origin's x-axis, but we must check whether the entire curve $h$ stays in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$. The condition is: $$x^2 + y^2 = (-d + 2ds)^2 + (1 - 2t)^2 r^2 > r^2$$ For this to hold for all $(s, t)$, we need $(-d + 2ds)^2 > r^2 - (1 - 2t)^2 r^2 = r^2 [1 - (1 - 2t)^2]$. The maximum of the right-hand side is $r^2$ (at $t = 0, 1$), but the minimum of the left-hand side is $0$ (at $s = 1/2$). Hence, **no continuous homotopy exists** — $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ are **non-homotopic**. **Step 4: Compute the winding number to confirm.** $$n(\tau_1 \cup \bar{\tau}_2, \mathcal{O}) = +1$$ The fact that this integer is non-zero confirms $\tau_1 \not\simeq \tau_2$. ## 3.2 Example 2: Computing the Number of Homotopy Classes Consider a planar workspace with $n$ disjoint convex obstacles. The number of topologically distinct path classes is bounded by the **Cayley formula**: $$\vert \pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{free}) \vert \leq n + 1$$ **Derivation sketch (for convex obstacles in convex workspace):** > [!NOTE] > Each convex obstacle contributes one "hole" in the C-free space. A loop around a single obstacle cannot be contracted. Moreover, a loop around obstacle $i$ cannot be deformed to a loop around obstacle $j$ for $i \neq j$ without crossing an obstacle. Therefore, the set of independent generators of $\pi_1$ has cardinality exactly $n$, giving: $$\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{free}) \cong \mathbb{Z}^n$$ The cardinality (counting integer tuples) is **infinite**, but the number of "distinct" homotopy classes for fixed endpoints has the combinatorial bound $n + 1$ (paths may go around zero, one, or any subset of obstacles). ## 3.3 Example 3: Algebraic Test for Homotopy Given a path $\tau$ that is a closed loop in the plane and a point $p$ not on $\tau$, the **winding number** is computed as: $$n(\tau, p) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{(x - x_p) \dot{y} - (y - y_p) \dot{x}}{(x - x_p)^2 + (y - y_p)^2} \, ds$$ **Step-by-step evaluation for a unit circle around the origin:** The unit circle is $\tau(s) = (\cos 2\pi s, \sin 2\pi s)$, so $\dot{x} = -2\pi \sin 2\pi s$, $\dot{y} = 2\pi \cos 2\pi s$. With $p = (0, 0)$: $$n(\tau, p) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\cos(2\pi s) \cdot 2\pi \cos(2\pi s) - \sin(2\pi s)(-2\pi \sin 2\pi s)}{1} \, ds$$ $$= \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{1} 2\pi [\cos^2(2\pi s) + \sin^2(2\pi s)] \, ds = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2\pi = 1$$ Hence, this loop winds once counter-clockwise around the origin and represents a non-trivial element of $\pi_1$. ## 3.4 Algorithm: Classifying Two Paths > [!IMPORTANT] > **Algorithmic Decision Rule:** To check if $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ are homotopic in a planar region punctured by $n$ obstacles $\mathcal{O}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{O}_n$: > > 1. Form the closed loop $\gamma = \tau_1 * \bar{\tau}_2$. > 2. For each obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i$, compute $n(\gamma, \mathcal{O}_i)$. > 3. If **all** winding numbers are zero, then $\tau_1 \simeq \tau_2$ (homotopic). > 4. If **any** winding number is non-zero, then $\tau_1 \not\simeq \tau_2$ (non-homotopic). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: Path Classification in C-Space ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Two Paths tau1 and tau2
with common endpoints"] --> B["Concatenate to form
closed loop gamma = tau1 star bar tau2"] B --> C["For each obstacle Oi,
compute winding number n(gamma, Oi)"] C --> D{"All winding
numbers equal zero?"} D -- Yes --> E["HOMOTOPIC
tau1 is equivalent to tau2
Same homotopy class"] D -- No --> F["NON-HOMOTOPIC
tau1 not deformable to tau2
Different homotopy class"] E --> G["Result: Optimal planner
selects shortest within class"] F --> H["Result: Both classes
candidates for global optimum"] H --> I["Compare optimal cost
in each distinct class"] I --> J["Return globally optimal path"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Homotopic Path Planning Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Workspace W
with obstacles"] --> B1["C-Space Mapping
W to C"] B1 --> C1["C-free Region
computation"] C1 --> D1["Identify
Homotopy Classes"] D1 --> E1["Sample Class
Representatives"] E1 --> F1["Local Path
Optimization"] F1 --> G1["Best Path
Selection"] G1 --> H1["Trajectory Output
for Mobile Robot"] ``` ## 4.3 Configuration Space Diagram (Mermaid Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph cs["C-SPACE TOPOLOGY"] qStart(["q_start"]) qGoal(["q_goal"]) obs1["Obstacle 1
C_obs region"] obs2["Obstacle 2
C_obs region"] pathA["Path tau_A
(goes above obs1)"] pathB["Path tau_B
(goes below obs1)"] pathC["Path tau_C
(goes around obs2)"] end qStart --> pathA qStart --> pathB qStart --> pathC pathA --> qGoal pathB --> qGoal pathC --> qGoal pathA -. "NON-HOMOTOPIC" .- pathB pathA -. "NON-HOMOTOPIC" .- pathC pathB -. "NON-HOMOTOPIC" .- pathC ``` ## 4.4 Equivalence Class Partition ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Set of all feasible paths
in C_free"] P --> C1a["Class 1: tau_A and all
paths above obs1"] P --> C2a["Class 2: tau_B and all
paths below obs1"] P --> C3a["Class 3: tau_C and all
paths around obs2"] C1a -. "tau_A equivalent to tau_A'" .- C1a C2a -. "tau_B equivalent to tau_B'" .- C2a ``` > [!TIP] > **Interpretation of Diagrams:** The mermaid schematics illustrate the conceptual pipeline: (i) the planner detects topologically distinct classes, (ii) it computes the optimal representative within each class, and (iii) it returns the globally optimal path. The "non-homotopic" relationships (dashed red lines) emphasize that no continuous deformation bridges these classes. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define homotopic paths. State the conditions under which two paths are said to be non-homotopic.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Two paths $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ in the configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ are said to be **homotopic** if there exists a continuous function $h : [0,1] \times [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}$ such that (1 mark): - $h(s, 0) = \tau_1(s)$ and $h(s, 1) = \tau_2(s)$ for all $s \in [0,1]$ (1 mark) - $h(0, t) = q_{start}$ and $h(1, t) = q_{goal}$ for all $t \in [0,1]$ (1 mark). Two paths are **non-homotopic** if no such continuous deformation exists — typically because obstacles in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ prevent the deformation, i.e., the closed loop $\tau_1 * \bar{\tau}_2$ has a non-zero winding number around at least one C-space obstacle. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Explain the concept of a homotopy class and state its significance in mobile robot path planning.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **homotopy class** is an equivalence class of paths under the homotopy relation $\simeq$ (1 mark). All paths that can be continuously deformed into one another within $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ belong to the same class (1 mark). In mobile robot path planning, homotopy classes enable the reduction of a continuous infinite-dimensional search problem to a **finite (or countable) combinatorial problem** of selecting among topologically distinct path classes — each class contains infinitely many paths but only one optimal representative needs to be computed (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Define the configuration space of a mobile robot. With a suitable diagram, explain how a point robot's workspace with circular obstacles maps to a configuration space. (7 marks) **(b)** For a planar point robot navigating in a workspace with two disjoint circular obstacles, identify all topologically distinct homotopy classes of paths between two given configurations. Show, with a labelled diagram, why paths going on different sides of the obstacles are non-homotopic. Use the winding number to formally prove non-homotopy for at least one pair. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) Solution:** The **Configuration Space (C-space)** $\mathcal{C}$ of a robot is the set of all possible configurations the robot can attain (1 mark). For a point robot in 2D, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$, and each configuration is a point $(x, y)$ (1 mark). **Mapping rule:** For each obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i$ in the workspace, the corresponding C-space obstacle is the **Minkowski sum** of $\mathcal{O}_i$ with the robot's body (point robot $\Rightarrow$ identity), giving $$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{O}_i$$ and the free space is $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ (2 marks). **Diagram description:** A schematic shows two circles in the workspace becoming two larger circles in the C-space (for a circular robot, the radius is added). The paths of the point robot in the workspace become curves in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ (3 marks). **Part (b) Solution:** For $n = 2$ disjoint convex obstacles $\mathcal{O}_1, \mathcal{O}_2$ in a simply connected workspace, the C-free space is doubly-punctured (2 marks). **Homotopy classes** (paths from $q_s$ to $q_g$): - Class 0: Straight line (no obstacle encircled) - Class 1: Goes left of $\mathcal{O}_1$, right of $\mathcal{O}_2$ - Class 2: Goes right of $\mathcal{O}_1$, left of $\mathcal{O}_2$ - Class 3: Encircles $\mathcal{O}_1$ only - Class 4: Encircles $\mathcal{O}_2$ only - … (up to $n + 1 = 3$ "simple" classes for distinct routes) (2 marks) **Non-homotopy proof using winding number:** Let $\tau_A$ go above $\mathcal{O}_1$ and $\tau_B$ go below $\mathcal{O}_1$. Form the closed loop $\gamma = \tau_A * \bar{\tau}_B$. The winding number $n(\gamma, \mathcal{O}_1) = \pm 1 \neq 0$ (2 marks). Since the winding number is a topological invariant preserved under continuous deformation, $\gamma$ cannot be contracted to a point in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$. Therefore, $\tau_A \not\simeq \tau_B$. (1 mark) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Define the fundamental group $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{free})$ of a configuration space. With examples, explain how the cardinality of $\pi_1$ relates to the number of obstacles in a planar workspace. (7 marks) **(b)** Two collision-free paths $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ are given between configurations $q_s$ and $q_g$ in a 2D workspace containing a single circular obstacle of radius 1 centered at the origin. Using the homotopy condition, derive the mathematical criteria under which the two paths are classified as homotopic or non-homotopic. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) Solution:** The **fundamental group** $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{free}, q_0)$ is the group formed by the set of all **homotopy classes of loops** (closed paths) based at a configuration $q_0 \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$, with the group operation being path concatenation (1 mark). **Relation to obstacles:** For a planar workspace with $n$ disjoint convex (or "nice") obstacles, $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is homotopy-equivalent to a plane with $n$ punctures, and $$\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{free}) \cong \mathbb{Z}^n$$ (2 marks). **Examples:** - **0 obstacles:** $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathbb{R}^2 \Rightarrow \pi_1 = \{e\}$ (trivial group, simply connected) (1 mark) - **1 obstacle:** $\pi_1 \cong \mathbb{Z}$, generated by a single loop around the obstacle (1 mark) - **2 obstacles:** $\pi_1 \cong \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$, two independent generators (1 mark) - **Non-convex obstacles:** Fundamental group may be non-abelian or have non-trivial relations (1 mark) **Part (b) Solution:** Let the workspace be $\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \mathcal{O}$ where $\mathcal{O} = \{(x, y) : x^2 + y^2 \leq 1\}$. **Step 1:** For $\tau_1 \simeq \tau_2$ to hold, there must exist a continuous $h : [0,1]^2 \to \mathcal{C}_{free}$ with the standard boundary conditions (1 mark). **Step 2:** Equivalently, the closed loop $\gamma(s) = \tau_1(2s)$ for $s \in [0, 1/2]$ and $\gamma(s) = \tau_2(2 - 2s)$ for $s \in [1/2, 1]$ must satisfy $n(\gamma, \mathcal{O}) = 0$ (2 marks). **Step 3:** Compute the winding number using the contour integral: $$n(\gamma, 0) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x \dot{y} - y \dot{x}}{x^2 + y^2} \, ds$$ **Step 4:** Classification (2 marks): - If $n(\gamma, 0) = 0 \Rightarrow \tau_1 \simeq \tau_2$ (homotopic). - If $n(\gamma, 0) \neq 0 \Rightarrow \tau_1 \not\simeq \tau_2$ (non-homotopic), and the integer $n$ is the algebraic winding count. **Step 5:** Conclusion (2 marks): Two paths are homotopic if and only if they enclose the obstacle an equal number of times (in the same orientation). If one encloses the obstacle and the other does not, they are non-homotopic. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing workspace and C-space:** Students often draw the obstacle in the workspace and claim non-homotopy directly. Examiners expect explicit reference to $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ and the configuration space topology. **(–2 marks)** > > 2. **Forgetting the start/goal boundary conditions:** A "homotopy" without $h(0, t) = q_{start}$ and $h(1, t) = q_{goal}$ fixed is technically a *free homotopy* (loop homotopy), not a path homotopy. Mention both conditions explicitly. **(–1 mark)** > > 3. **Skipping the winding number computation:** Saying "the paths are non-homotopic because they go around the obstacle" without computing $n(\gamma, \mathcal{O})$ is incomplete. Always include the formal invariant. **(–2 marks)** > > 4. **Mixing up $\pi_0$ and $\pi_1$:** The 0-th homotopy group $\pi_0$ counts *connected components*, while $\pi_1$ counts *loop classes*. For path planning between fixed endpoints, the relevant structure is $\pi_1$. **(–1 mark)** > > 5. **Not stating that $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ must be path-connected** for a feasible path to exist in the first place. **(–1 mark)** --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** > > - **Path:** Continuous map $\tau : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}$ with $\tau(0) = q_s$ and $\tau(1) = q_g$. > - **Feasible Path:** $\tau(s) \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$ for all $s \in [0,1]$. > - **Homotopy:** Continuous deformation $h : [0,1]^2 \to \mathcal{C}_{free}$ respecting all four boundary constraints. > - **Homotopic Paths ($\tau_1 \simeq \tau_2$):** Such an $h$ exists; they belong to the same equivalence class. > - **Non-Homotopic Paths ($\tau_1 \not\simeq \tau_2$):** No such $h$ exists; they belong to different equivalence classes. > - **Homotopy is an equivalence relation:** Reflexive, symmetric, transitive. > - **Winding Number $n(\gamma, p)$:** A topological invariant — preserved under continuous deformation; non-zero value $\Rightarrow$ non-contractible loop $\Rightarrow$ non-homotopic to constant path. > - **Fundamental Group $\pi_1(\mathcal{C}_{free})$:** Group of all loop homotopy classes; for $n$ convex obstacles in plane, $\pi_1 \cong \mathbb{Z}^n$. > - **Path-connectedness of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$:** Necessary precondition for the existence of any feasible path. > - **Simply Connected:** $\pi_1 = \{e\}$; equivalent to every loop being contractible to a point. Holds when $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ has no "holes" (e.g., no encirclable obstacles). > - **Practical Algorithm for Planar Homotopy Test:** Concatenate $\to$ compute winding number around each obstacle $\to$ zero $\Rightarrow$ homotopic, non-zero $\Rightarrow$ non-homotopic. > - **Engineering Relevance:** Homotopy class decomposition is the basis of algorithms like **Homotopic Roadmaps**, **Visibility Graphs with topological labels**, and **H-signature heuristics** for non-holonomic mobile robots.

topics.title: Constructing a Discrete Search Space - Visibility Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Constructing a Discrete Search Space - Visibility Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CONSTRUCTING A DISCRETE SEARCH SPACE: VISIBILITY GRAPH

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visibility Graph (VG):** A graph $G = (V, E)$ constructed in the polygonal **Configuration Space (C-space)** of a mobile robot, where the vertex set $V$ consists of the start configuration $q_{start}$, the goal configuration $q_{goal}$, and all vertices of the polygonal obstacles, and an edge exists between two vertices $v_i, v_j \in V$ if and only if the straight line segment $\overline{v_i v_j}$ does **not intersect the interior** of any obstacle.

In the context of a *point robot* operating in a 2-D workspace cluttered with polygonal obstacles, the visibility graph is a **combinatorial roadmap** — a one-dimensional network embedded in the 2-D free space $C_{free}$ — that captures all candidate shortest paths between $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Roadmap Property:** A data structure $G$ qualifies as a roadmap for $C_{free}$ if it satisfies (i) **Accessibility** (a free configuration can be easily connected to $G$), (ii) **Connectivity** (every connected component of $C_{free}$ has a connected component in $G$), and (iii) **Departability** (any free configuration is easily reachable from $G$). The visibility graph is a *strong* roadmap because it guarantees the existence of the shortest collision-free path.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Flashlight-in-a-Dark-Room Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a completely dark room cluttered with rectangular tables, cupboards, and pillars. You hold a flashlight that produces a perfectly straight, narrow beam of light. The only paths you can take are along straight-line segments joining:

1. **Your current position**,
2. **The destination**, and
3. **The corners (vertices) of every piece of furniture**.

This is because any optimal trajectory, when you can see a corner, allows you to walk straight to it; if you cannot see a corner, that corner is irrelevant to your shortest path. The flashlight beam that is *not blocked* by furniture precisely defines a **visible pair**, and these visible pairs form the edges of the visibility graph.

A more colloquial version: **Visibility graphs convert a continuous 2-D maze into a discrete graph problem** — the entire infinite continuous space collapses onto a finite set of "interesting" vertices (corners) and straight-line connections among them.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Polygonal Obstacle** — A closed region in the workspace bounded by a finite set of line segments (edges) meeting at vertices.
> - **Configuration Space (C-space)** — The space of all possible robot configurations; for a point robot in 2-D, $C = \mathbb{R}^2$.
> - **Free Space $C_{free}$** — The set of all collision-free configurations; $C_{free} = C \setminus C_{obs}$.
> - **Obstacle Space $C_{obs}$** — The union of all obstacle interiors (including the robot's footprint grown by Minkowski sum).
> - **Visibility / Line-of-Sight** — Two points are *mutually visible* if the open segment joining them lies entirely in $C_{free}$.
> - **Tangent Edge** — An edge of $VG$ that is collinear with one edge of an obstacle (a "supporting line" touching the obstacle at exactly one vertex).
> - **Shortest-Path Map (SPM)** — A subdivision of $C_{free}$ induced by the visibility graph such that the shortest path from $q_{start}$ to any point in a cell passes through a fixed sequence of obstacle vertices.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Boundary Conditions

- **Complexity bound:** For $n$ obstacle vertices plus $q_{start}, q_{goal}$, the worst-case number of edges is $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ — this is the value to remember for the **12-mark derivation** type questions in ESE.
- **Planar graph property:** A visibility graph is a **planar graph** (no two edges cross except at shared vertices), since crossing edges would imply intersection of line segments, which would be blocked by definition.
- **Combinatorial size:** $|V| = n + 2$ and $|E| \le \binom{n+2}{2} = \frac{(n+2)(n+1)}{2}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does the Visibility Graph Yield the Shortest Path?

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Theorem (Lozano-Pérez & Wesley, 1979):** *For a point robot navigating among polygonal obstacles, if a shortest collision-free path exists, then there exists a shortest collision-free path that is a polygonal line whose vertices (other than the endpoints) are vertices of the polygonal obstacles.*

**Why this works (the deep "How"):**

- Any path in $C_{free}$ is composed of straight-line segments (since deviation from a straight line increases length for the same endpoints).
- The *vertices* of any locally optimal path must be critical points where the direction of travel changes — these critical points are exactly the obstacle vertices that "pull" the path around corners.
- Therefore, every candidate shortest path is fully determined by an ordered list of obstacle vertices, and the visibility graph enumerates **all** such candidate paths.

### 2.2 Construction of the Visibility Graph — Step-by-Step Logic

**Algorithm: Brute-Force Naïve Construction**

1. Initialize vertex set $V \gets \{q_{start}, q_{goal}\} \cup \{\text{all obstacle vertices}\}$.
2. Initialize edge set $E \gets \emptyset$.
3. For every ordered pair $(v_i, v_j)$ with $i \ne j$:
   - Construct the line segment $\overline{v_i v_j}$.
   - Check whether $\overline{v_i v_j}$ intersects the **interior** of any obstacle.
   - If **no** such intersection exists, add the undirected edge $\{v_i, v_j\}$ to $E$.
4. Return $G = (V, E)$.
5. Run **A\*** or **Dijkstra's algorithm** on $G$ to obtain the shortest path from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.

**Algorithmic "Why" Behind Each Step:**

- *Step 1* is justified by the Lozano-Pérez theorem — only obstacle vertices are "interesting" intermediate waypoints.
- *Step 3* is the combinatorial enumeration of all candidate straight-line connections.
- The intersection test in Step 3 is a *segment-intersection* problem, solvable in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ for two segments using cross products.

### 2.3 Variants of the Visibility Graph

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Full Visibility Graph (FVG):** Includes all visible pairs of vertices. Worst-case complexity $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ naïve, $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ with sweep-line.
> - **Reduced Visibility Graph (RVG):** Includes only *tangent* edges (edges that graze obstacle vertices on their "left" or "right"). Still produces shortest paths, but with far fewer edges. This is the **most-cited** variant in textbooks.
> - **Tangential Visibility Graph (TVG):** Combines RVG with rotational sweep, running in $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$.
> - **Visibility Graph of Polygons with Holes:** Requires $C_{obs}$ to be a union of disjoint simple polygons.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Table — Memorize Before the Exam**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of vertices | $\vert V \vert = n + 2$ | $n$ obstacle vertices + start + goal | dimensionless |
| Max number of edges | $\vert E \vert \le \binom{n+2}{2} = \frac{(n+2)(n+1)}{2}$ | Upper bound on pairwise visibility | dimensionless |
| Worst-case complexity (naïve) | $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n^3)$ | All-pairs intersection test | time |
| Best practical complexity | $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | After plane-sweep optimization | time |
| Tangential VG complexity | $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ | Rotational sweep per vertex | time |
| Edge weight (path length) | $w(v_i,v_j) = \sqrt{(x_j - x_i)^2 + (y_j - y_i)^2}$ | Euclidean edge length | meters (m) |
| Total path length | $L = \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} w(v_{i_k}, v_{i_{k+1}})$ | Sum over path edges | meters (m) |
| Heuristic for A* | $h(v) = \sqrt{(x_v - x_{goal})^2 + (y_v - y_{goal})^2}$ | Admissible Euclidean heuristic | meters (m) |
| Segment intersection test | $s_1 \cap s_2 \ne \emptyset \iff \text{orient}(p_1,p_2,p_3) \cdot \text{orient}(p_1,p_2,p_4) < 0$ | Cross-product sign check | dimensionless |

### 2.5 Segment-Intersection Test (Foundation of Edge Validation)

For two segments $\overline{p_1 p_2}$ and $\overline{p_3 p_4}$ to properly intersect, the following condition must hold:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{orient}(p_1, p_2, p_3) \cdot \text{orient}(p_1, p_2, p_4) &< 0 \quad \text{(straddles)} \\
\text{orient}(p_3, p_4, p_1) \cdot \text{orient}(p_3, p_4, p_2) &< 0 \quad \text{(straddles)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where

$$
\text{orient}(A, B, C) = (B_x - A_x)(C_y - A_y) - (B_y - A_y)(C_x - A_x)
$$

If **either** orientation product equals zero, special handling for collinear/endpoint cases is required. This is the per-edge check inside Step 3 of the naïve algorithm.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** in warehouses (e.g., Amazon Kiva bots) use pre-computed roadmaps combining topological and visibility-graph components.
- **Self-Driving Cars** in structured environments (parking lots) often use **Hybrid A\*** on a lattice, but visibility-graph–based planners serve as ground-truth benchmarks.
- **Industrial Robotic Arms** in known workspaces use VG-derived roadmaps for cycle-time-optimal motion.
- **Disaster-Response Rovers** use online visibility graphs built from LiDAR-scanned point clouds approximated as polygons.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Constructing a VG for a 2-Obstacle Workspace

**Scenario.** Workspace $\mathcal{W} = [0, 10] \times [0, 10]$. Two rectangular obstacles:

- $O_1$ with vertices $A(2,2),\; B(2,5),\; C(5,5),\; D(5,2)$
- $O_2$ with vertices $E(6,6),\; F(6,9),\; G(9,9),\; H(9,6)$
- $q_{start} = S(0,0)$, $q_{goal} = G'(10,10)$.

**Step 1: Assemble the vertex set.**

$$
V = \{S, G', A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H\} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \vert V \vert = 10
$$

**Step 2: Enumerate all candidate edges (combinatorial).**

$$
\binom{10}{2} = 45 \text{ candidate edges (in worst case)}
$$

**Step 3: Apply visibility filter (selected critical edges).**

The straight-line segment $\overline{SG'}$ crosses both obstacles, hence **not visible**. Some visible edges:

- $\overline{SA}$: passes through free space, no obstacle intersection. **Visible.**
- $\overline{SC}$: from $(0,0)$ to $(5,5)$ — line equation $y = x$. At $x=2$, $y=2$ — this is *exactly* the vertex $A$ (endpoint, allowed). At $x=5$, $y=5$ — exactly vertex $C$. The line is collinear with the diagonal of $O_1$ but only touches the boundary. **Visible (tangent edge).**
- $\overline{SG}$: from $(0,0)$ to $(9,9)$. Crosses interior of $O_2$? At $x=6$, $y=6$ which is on edge $EH$. At $x=9$, $y=9$ which is vertex $G$. The line passes through $E(6,6)$ and $G(9,9)$ — corner contact, allowed. **Visible (tangent edge).**
- $\overline{AF}$: from $(2,2)$ to $(6,6)$. Does it cross $O_1$? Endpoint $A$ is on the boundary, OK. The line goes outward. Free of $O_1$ and $O_2$. **Visible.**
- $\overline{BG}$: from $(2,5)$ to $(9,9)$. Equation: slope $= \frac{4}{7}$. Passes through free space, no obstacle intersection. **Visible.**
- $\overline{CH}$: from $(5,5)$ to $(9,6)$. Free. **Visible.**

**Step 4: Run A\* / Dijkstra on the visible-edge subgraph.**

Suppose Dijkstra yields the path:
$$
S \rightarrow A \rightarrow F \rightarrow G' \quad\Rightarrow\quad L = \vert SA \vert + \vert AF \vert + \vert FG' \vert
$$

Compute each segment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert SA \vert &= \sqrt{(2-0)^2 + (2-0)^2} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \text{ m} \\
\vert AF \vert &= \sqrt{(6-2)^2 + (6-2)^2} = \sqrt{32} = 4\sqrt{2} \approx 5.657 \text{ m} \\
\vert FG' \vert &= \sqrt{(10-6)^2 + (10-6)^2} = \sqrt{32} = 4\sqrt{2} \approx 5.657 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total:
$$
L_{S \to G'} = 2\sqrt{2} + 4\sqrt{2} + 4\sqrt{2} = 10\sqrt{2} \approx 14.142 \text{ m}
$$

### 3.2 Comparison Matrix: Variants of Visibility Graph

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Matrix — Choose the Right Variant for the Right Problem**

| Property | Full VG | Reduced VG | Tangential VG | Apparent VG |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of edges | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ in practice | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| Construction time | $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ naïve / $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ optimal | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ |
| Shortest-path guarantee | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | No (suboptimal) |
| Best for | Small $n$ / dense workspaces | General-purpose | Online construction | Reactive / anytime |
| Used in (typical) | Benchmarks, teaching | Path planning textbooks | Real-time robotics | Dynamic environments |

### 3.3 Derivation: Why Reduced Visibility Graph Still Preserves Optimality

A *tangent edge* between two obstacle vertices $v_i$ and $v_j$ is a supporting line that touches both vertices from the "outside" of the convex hull of the obstacles. Any non-tangent visible edge is strictly *longer* than some tangent alternative passing through the same endpoint region, because:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \overline{v_i v_j} \text{ is non-tangent}, &\text{ then the supporting line at } v_i \text{ defines a half-plane} \\
&\text{containing both } v_i, v_j \text{ such that any tangent edge } \overline{v_i v_k} \text{ satisfies} \\
&\vert \overline{v_i v_k} \vert \le \vert \overline{v_i v_j} \vert \text{ for the corresponding } v_k.
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a corollary of the supporting-line theorem from convex geometry, and it is why tangent-only graphs are sufficient for shortest-path queries.

### 3.4 Analytical Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse **visibility** (line of sight) with **reachability** (graph connectivity). Visibility is a *geometric* property; reachability is a *topological* one derived from the graph. A vertex can be "visible" from $q_{start}$ but isolated from the goal's connected component — you must run a graph search, not just check pairwise sight.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a VG-Based Planner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workspace Map W] --> B[Polygon Decomposition Module]
```
