| 7 | Time (Graph) | $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert + \vert \mathcal{E} \vert)$ | total reachable |
| 8 | Space (Graph) | $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ | explored set |
| 9 | Backtrack cost | $O(m)$ actions rolled back per dead end | steps |
| 10 | Frontier structure | LIFO Stack | `push` / `pop` only |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Robot path planning in narrow corridors** (e.g., industrial pipelines) where the world is a long thin graph — DFS's low memory dominates.
- **Topological map exploration** — when a robot must visit *all* reachable rooms in a building, DFS provides a natural "spiral" walk.
- **Cycle detection in SLAM pose-graphs** — recursive DFS is the standard tool to find back-edges.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3.1 Formal Algorithmic Statement (KTU Pseudocode)

The following is the canonical recursive-then-iterative DFS specification used in KTU 2024 scheme mobile-robotics papers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Function } \text{DFS}(s_0, \mathcal{G}_\text{goal}) \\
&\quad \text{frontier} \leftarrow \text{Stack}() \\
&\quad \text{explored} \leftarrow \emptyset \\
&\quad \text{parent}[s_0] \leftarrow \text{NULL} \\
&\quad \text{frontier.push}(s_0) \\
&\quad \textbf{while } \text{frontier} \neq \emptyset \textbf{ do} \\
&\quad \quad n \leftarrow \text{frontier.pop}() \\
&\quad \quad \textbf{if } n \in \mathcal{G}_\text{goal} \textbf{ then} \\
&\quad \quad \quad \text{return RECONSTRUCT\_PATH}(\text{parent}, n) \\
&\quad \quad \text{explored} \leftarrow \text{explored} \cup \{n\} \\
&\quad \quad \text{for each } a \in \mathcal{A}(n) \textbf{ do} \\
&\quad \quad \quad n' \leftarrow \text{RESULT}(n, a) \\
&\quad \quad \quad \textbf{if } n' \notin \text{explored} \;\wedge\; n' \notin \text{frontier} \textbf{ then} \\
&\quad \quad \quad\quad \text{parent}[n'] \leftarrow n \\
&\quad \quad \quad\quad \text{frontier.push}(n') \\
&\quad \text{return FAILURE}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Tracing DFS on a Robot's Building Map

Consider a mobile robot in a building represented by the following directed graph. The robot starts at node **S** and must reach goal **G**. Successors are listed in **alphabetical order**.

**Graph Definition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&V = \{S, A, B, C, D, E, F, G\} \\
&\mathcal{E} = \{S\!\to\!A,\ S\!\to\!B,\ A\!\to\!B,\ A\!\to\!C,\ B\!\to\!D,\ C\!\to\!D,\ C\!\to\!G,\ D\!\to\!E,\ E\!\to\!F,\ F\!\to\!G\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

We use a **graph search** with an explicit explored set. At each iteration, we record the top-of-stack node, the popped node, and the resulting state of the frontier and explored set.

| Step | Pop / Expand | Goal? | Successors Generated | Frontier (bottom → top) | Explored Set | Parent |
|:----:|:------------:|:-----:|:--------------------:|:------------------------|:------------:|:------:|
| 0 | — | — | — | [S] | $\emptyset$ | — |
| 1 | S | No | A, B | [A, B] | {S} | S→A, S→B |
| 2 | B | No | D | [A, D] | {S, B} | B→D |
| 3 | D | No | E | [A, E] | {S, B, D} | D→E |
| 4 | E | No | F | [A, F] | {S, B, D, E} | E→F |
| 5 | F | No | G | [A, G] | {S, B, D, E, F} | F→G |
| 6 | G | **YES** | — | [A] | {S, B, D, E, F, G} | G = goal |

### Path Reconstruction (Reverse Parent Chain)

Following the parent pointers backward from G:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{parent}[G] &= F \\
\text{parent}[F] &= E \\
\text{parent}[E] &= D \\
\text{parent}[D] &= B \\
\text{parent}[B] &= S
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the **solution path is**:

$$
S \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow G
$$

### Analytical Observations (Board-Exam Gold)

- **Goal found at depth 5** — the *optimal* BFS path was $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$ (depth 3). DFS returned a **non-optimal, longer** path. This is the textbook demonstration of DFS's lack of optimality.
- **Node A was *never* expanded**, even though it sat in the frontier from Step 1 onward. DFS systematically bypassed it because deeper nodes were stacked on top of it.
- **Memory at peak:** frontier held 2 nodes; explored set held 5 nodes. Total $= 7 \ll \vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 8$, confirming the $O(bm)$ space bound.

## 3.3 Proof Sketch — Why DFS is Complete Only in Finite Spaces

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } \mathcal{V} \text{ be finite, } \vert \mathcal{V} \vert = N. \\
&\text{In the worst case, DFS expands every state exactly once} \Rightarrow N \text{ pops}. \\
&\text{After } N \text{ pops, the frontier is empty} \Rightarrow \text{algorithm halts.}\\
&\text{If a goal is reachable, it is among the } N \text{ states} \Rightarrow \text{it will be popped.}\\
&\text{If } \mathcal{V} \text{ is infinite, DFS may follow an infinite branch and never backtrack to a}\\
&\text{shallower goal} \Rightarrow \text{algorithm never terminates on a goal} \Rightarrow \text{INCOMPLETE.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DFS vs BFS vs UCS

| Criterion | DFS | BFS | UCS | IDDFS |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-------|
| Frontier type | LIFO Stack | FIFO Queue | Priority Queue | Stack + depth limit |
| Complete? | Finite only | Yes | Yes (positive costs) | Yes |
| Optimal? | No | Yes (uniform cost) | Yes | Yes (uniform cost) |
| Time | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| Space | $O(bm)$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ |
| Best for | Deep, narrow graphs | Shallow goals | Weighted edges | Best of both |

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Diagram A — DFS Algorithm Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START DFS at s0]) --> A1[Initialize empty Stack and Explored Set]
    A1 --> A2[Push s0 onto Stack]
    A2 --> A3{Stack empty?}
    A3 -- YES --> A4([RETURN FAILURE])
    A3 -- NO --> A5[Pop top node n from Stack]
    A5 --> A6{n is Goal node?}
    A6 -- YES --> A7[Reconstruct path via parent pointers]
    A7 --> A8([RETURN SUCCESS with path])
    A6 -- NO --> A9[Add n to Explored Set]
    A9 --> A10[Generate all successors of n]
    A10 --> A11{For each successor n prime}
    A11 --> A12{n prime in Explored or Frontier?}
    A12 -- YES --> A11
    A12 -- NO --> A13[Set parent n prime equal to n]
    A13 --> A14[Push n prime onto Stack]
    A14 --> A11
    A11 -- DONE --> A3
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — DFS Traversal Visualization on the Worked-Example Graph

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS((S)) -- START --> nodeA((A))
    nodeS -- UNEXPLORED EDGE --> nodeB((B))
    nodeA -- EDGE --> nodeC((C))
    nodeB -- DEEP PATH --> nodeD((D))
    nodeC -- SHORTER GOAL --> nodeG((G))
    nodeD -- DEEP PATH --> nodeE((E))
    nodeE -- DEEP PATH --> nodeF((F))
    nodeF -- DEEP PATH --> nodeG2((G GOAL))
    nodeG2(( )):::invisible
    nodeA -. NEVER EXPANDED .-> nodeA2(( ))
    classDef goalNode fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef startNode fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef unexplored fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#888,stroke-dasharray: 4 4,color:#444
    class nodeG,nodeG2 goalNode
    class nodeS startNode
    class nodeA,nodeA2 unexplored
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**
> - **Solid black arrows** = edges actually traversed by the DFS expansion order $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow G$.
> - **Dashed gray arrows** = edges that exist in the graph but were **never used** because DFS plunged deep.
> - **Green node G** is the goal reached via a long path; the dashed *shorter* route through C is **missed** — proving DFS is *not optimal*.

## 4.3 Diagram C — Frontier (Stack) State Evolution Over Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step0["Step 0: [S]"] --> step1["Step 1: [A, B]"] --> step2["Step 2: [A, D]"] --> step3["Step 3: [A, E]"] --> step4["Step 4: [A, F]"] --> step5["Step 5: [A, G]"] --> step6["Step 6: [A] GOAL"]
    classDef stackState fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    class step0,step1,step2,step3,step4,step5,step6 stackState
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The node **A** is constantly at the *bottom* of the stack and is never popped because deeper successors keep landing on top of it. This visualizes the LIFO discipline in action.

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) with respect to frontier data structure, completeness, optimality, and space complexity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | BFS | DFS |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Frontier | FIFO **Queue** | LIFO **Stack** |
| Completeness | Complete in finite & infinite bounded spaces | Complete only in **finite** spaces |
| Optimality | Optimal for uniform step costs | **Not optimal** |
| Space Complexity | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b \cdot m)$ |

(½ mark × 4 = 2 marks; tabular presentation = 1 mark.)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**What is the role of the explored (closed) set in graph search, and what happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **explored set** stores every node that has already been expanded, so that it is never re-expanded. *(1 mark)*
- In a graph with **cycles** (e.g., a robot's corridor with a loop), without the explored set the algorithm may revisit the same node infinitely many times, causing **non-termination in finite time** and exponential node-generation. *(1 mark)*
- The set enables **graph search** (vs. tree search) and is essential for DFS to be **complete in finite cyclic graphs**. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Define the *Discrete State Space* and *Graph Search* in the context of mobile robotics. With a neat flowchart, explain the **Depth-First Search (DFS)** algorithm. State and justify the **completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity** of DFS. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Apply Depth-First Search on the following directed graph. The start node is **S** and the goal node is **G**. Successors are expanded in **alphabetical order**. Show the contents of the frontier (stack) and the explored set at every step. Clearly reconstruct the final solution path using parent pointers. *(7 marks)*

**Graph for part (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \{S, A, B, C, D, G\} \\
\mathcal{E} &= \{S\!\to\!A,\ S\!\to\!B,\ A\!\to\!C,\ B\!\to\!A,\ B\!\to\!D,\ C\!\to\!G,\ D\!\to\!C,\ D\!\to\!G\}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Discrete State Space (1 mark):** A finite or countably infinite set of distinct configurations $\mathcal{S}$ that a robot can occupy. Each configuration is a node; transitions between nodes are actions.

2. **Graph Search (1 mark):** A systematic procedure to traverse a graph $(\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ from an initial node $s_0$ to a goal $g \in \mathcal{G}_\text{goal}$, expanding nodes one at a time using a *frontier* and an *explored* set.

3. **DFS Algorithm Flowchart (2 marks):** Refer to **Diagram A in Section 4.1** — the canonical push–pop–expand–goal-test loop driven by a LIFO stack.

4. **Complexity Properties (2 marks):**
   - **Completeness:** Complete in **finite** state spaces, **incomplete** in infinite or depth-unbounded spaces. *Justification:* in a finite graph the frontier must eventually empty (or hit the goal), but an infinite branch with no goal can trap the algorithm forever.
   - **Optimality:** **Not optimal.** *Justification:* DFS commits to the deepest available branch; a shallower goal off a different branch is bypassed.
   - **Time complexity:** $O(b^m)$ — in the worst case every node in the tree of depth $m$ is generated.
   - **Space complexity:** $O(bm)$ — only the current path ($m$ nodes) and their unexpanded siblings ($b$ per level) are stored.

5. **Real-world relevance (1 mark):** DFS is the algorithm of choice for embedded robot controllers navigating narrow, tree-like topologies (pipelines, indoor SLAM backtracking) because of its tiny $O(bm)$ memory footprint.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Full DFS Trace:**

| Step | Pop | Goal? | Successors | Frontier (top at right) | Explored | Parent Update |
|:----:|:---:|:-----:|:----------:|:------------------------|:--------:|:--------------|
| 0 | — | — | — | [S] | $\emptyset$ | — |
| 1 | S | No | A, B | [A, B] | {S} | parent[A]=S, parent[B]=S |
| 2 | B | No | A, D | [A, D] | {S, B} | A already in frontier, parent[D]=B |
| 3 | D | No | C, G | [A, C, G] | {S, B, D} | parent[C]=D, parent[G]=D |
| 4 | G | **YES** | — | [A, C] | {S, B, D, G} | Reconstruct path |

**Path Reconstruction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{parent}[G] &= D \\
\text{parent}[D] &= B \\
\text{parent}[B] &= S
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;S \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow G\;}
$$

(Trace table = 4 marks; path reconstruction = 2 marks; explicit mention of LIFO push-order = 1 mark.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain with a real-world analogy why Depth-First Search uses a **LIFO stack** and not a FIFO queue. How does this choice affect the algorithm's **completeness in infinite spaces** and **space complexity**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a warehouse robot operating in a discrete grid environment. The robot's pose graph is given below. Apply **DFS (graph search)** to find a path from **Start** to **Goal**. Display the frontier stack and the explored set at each iteration. Comment on whether the path found is the **shortest path**. *(7 marks)*

**Graph for part (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \{\text{Start}, R1, R2, R3, R4, R5, R6, \text{Goal}\} \\
\mathcal{E} &= \{\text{Start}\!\to\!R1,\ \text{Start}\!\to\!R2,\ R1\!\to\!R3,\ R2\!\to\!R1,\ R2\!\to\!R4,\\
&\qquad R3\!\to\!R5,\ R4\!\to\!R3,\ R4\!\to\!R6,\ R5\!\to\!\text{Goal},\ R6\!\to\!\text{Goal}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **LIFO vs FIFO (2 marks):** A *stack* enforces "most-recently-pushed node is expanded next." This is exactly the *go-deepest-first* policy. A *queue* would enforce BFS. The cave-explorer analogy (string-and-ball) maps directly onto stack push/pop.
- **Why stack → incompleteness in infinite spaces (2 marks):** A stack allows the algorithm to descend an **infinite** branch without bound. The frontier never empties, never backtracks, and the goal (if located in a shallower branch) is never reached. Hence DFS is *incomplete* in infinite state spaces — a critical point the examiner rewards.
- **Space complexity benefit (2 marks):** Because only the path-so-far plus siblings need to be retained, the stack size is bounded by $O(bm)$ rather than the $O(b^d)$ required by a queue-based BFS. The mechanical intuition: the explorer only needs the *currently held string*, not a map of every passage.
- **Robot implication (1 mark):** Embedded robot controllers (e.g., a warehouse AGV) with limited RAM often use DFS over BFS for this very reason.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — DFS Trace (alphabetical successor order):**

| Step | Pop | Goal? | New Successors | Frontier (top at right) | Explored |
|:----:|:---:|:-----:|:--------------:|:------------------------|:--------:|
| 0 | — | — | — | [Start] | $\emptyset$ |
| 1 | Start | No | R1, R2 | [R1, R2] | {Start} |
| 2 | R2 | No | R1, R4 | [R1, R4] | {Start, R2} |
| 3 | R4 | No | R3, R6 | [R1, R3, R6] | {Start, R2, R4} |
| 4 | R6 | No | Goal | [R1, R3, Goal] | {Start, R2, R4, R6} |
| 5 | Goal | **YES** | — | [R1, R3] | {Start, R2, R4, R6, Goal} |

**Path Reconstruction:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Start} \rightarrow R2 \rightarrow R4 \rightarrow R6 \rightarrow \text{Goal}\;}
$$

**Path length = 4 edges.**

**Shortest-path check (2 marks):** A direct route exists: $\text{Start} \rightarrow R1 \rightarrow R3 \rightarrow R5 \rightarrow \text{Goal}$ — also length 4. So this DFS-found path is *one of* the shortest paths (length-equivalent), but DFS made the choice **non-deterministically based on stack order** — another path through R1, R3, R5 would also have been valid. The point to state in the answer: *DFS is not guaranteed to be optimal; it merely found a path of length equal to the optimum here.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Confusing stack top with stack bottom.** When asked "what is in the frontier?", always specify which end is the *top* (the next to be popped). Many students write the stack in the wrong order, breaking the trace.
> - **Forgetting to add the popped node to the explored set** *after* the goal test. If the goal test succeeds, the node goes into the explored set only at the end. In a graph search, omitting this check causes the same node to be expanded multiple times.
> - **Claiming DFS is optimal.** It is *not* optimal in general. Writing "DFS returns the shortest path" is the single most common error and will cost **at least 2 marks**.
> - **Not distinguishing tree search vs graph search.** When a cycle exists in the graph (e.g., $R2 \to R1$ and $R4 \to R3$ above), students often miss the fact that DFS would *loop* in tree search. Always state which variant you are running.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — DFS in 12 Bullets**
> 1. **DFS = LIFO stack frontier.** Always.
> 2. **Expands the deepest unexpanded node** first.
> 3. **Complete only in finite state spaces.** Incomplete in infinite spaces.
> 4. **Not optimal** — returns *a* path, not the *best* path.
> 5. **Time complexity: $O(b^m)$** in tree search; $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert + \vert \mathcal{E} \vert)$ in graph search.
> 6. **Space complexity: $O(bm)$** — its single greatest strength over BFS.
> 7. **Tree search** can re-expand states and loop forever on cycles. **Graph search** uses an explored set to prevent this.
> 8. **Parent pointers** must be recorded on push, not on pop, to enable path reconstruction.
> 9. **Goal test is done *after* pop, before expansion** — this is a frequent KTU trick question.
> 10. **DFS is the standard tool** for topological sorting, cycle detection, and topological-SLAM back-edge identification.
> 11. **Backtracking** is implicit in DFS: when a node has no unexpanded successors, it is automatically discarded from the stack.
> 12. **IDDFS (Iterative Deepening DFS)** combines DFS's low memory with BFS's optimality by running DFS with limits $0, 1, 2, \ldots, d$. Mention this if the question asks "how to make DFS optimal."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breadth-First Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Breadth-First Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Breadth-First Search (BFS)** is a *uniform-cost*, *graph-based* search strategy used in mobile robotics for **path planning** and **environment exploration**. It systematically expands nodes in the robot's configuration space (or occupancy grid) in *non-decreasing order of path cost* from the initial state, guaranteeing discovery of the **shortest path** in an unweighted (or uniformly weighted) graph.

Mathematically, given a start node $s$ and a goal node $t$ in an unweighted graph $G = (V, E)$, BFS finds a path $P = \{s, v_1, v_2, \dots, t\}$ that minimizes the number of edges $\vert P \vert$ traversed, subject to the constraint that every edge has unit cost $c(s, s') = 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"BFS is an uninformed search algorithm that explores all neighbor nodes at the present depth prior to moving on to the nodes at the next depth level. It uses a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue to manage the search frontier."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ripple in a Pond" Mental Model

Imagine you drop a **single pebble** into the center of a perfectly still pond. The ripples spread **outward in concentric circles**, touching every object at distance 1 first, then distance 2, then distance 3, and so on. You never skip a layer.

BFS behaves **identically** in a robot's grid map:
- The *pebble drop point* = the robot's current (start) position.
- The *ripple wavefront* = all grid cells exactly $k$ steps away from the start.
- BFS processes **all cells at depth $k$** before touching **any cell at depth $k+1$**.

### Alternative Analogy: The Building Fire-Search
A firefighter searching a building checks **every room on Floor 1** before climbing the stairs to **Floor 2**, and then every room on Floor 2 before Floor 3. This is BFS. (A *Depth-First Search*, by contrast, would walk to the end of a hallway, then backtrack.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Behavioral Constants of BFS**
> - **Frontier structure:** First-In-First-Out (**FIFO**) queue.
> - **Cost function:** $g(n) = $ depth of node $n$ from the start.
> - **Optimality metric:** Minimum number of *steps* (edges) — *not* minimum energy or minimum time, unless all transitions are uniformly weighted.
> - **Exploration order:** Level-by-level (breadth-wise), not branch-by-branch.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Five Pillars of BFS Execution

BFS operates on a disciplined **five-stage loop** that is rigorously evaluated in KTU examinations. Each stage is non-negotiable.

1. **Initialization Stage**
   - Create an *empty FIFO queue* $Q$.
   - Create an *explored (closed) set* $C = \emptyset$.
   - Insert the start node $s$ into $Q$ and mark $s$ as visited.
   - Set $g(s) = 0$ (path cost from $s$ to itself) and record the parent pointer $\text{parent}(s) = \text{NULL}$.

2. **Goal Test (Pre-Expansion Check)**
   - At every iteration, inspect the node $n$ at the **front of the queue** (peek, do not dequeue).
   - If $\text{GoalTest}(n) = \text{true}$, **terminate** and reconstruct the path using the parent pointers.

3. **Dequeue Operation**
   - Pop the front node $n$ from $Q$ and add it to the closed set $C$.

4. **Expansion (Successor Generation) Stage**
   - For every action $a \in \text{Actions}(n)$, generate the successor node $n' = \text{Result}(n, a)$.
   - If $n' \notin C$ **and** $n' \notin Q$, then:
     - Insert $n'$ into $Q$ (enqueue at the rear).
     - Mark $n'$ as visited.
     - Set $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$.
     - Set $\text{parent}(n') = n$.

5. **Termination Condition**
   - Stop when the goal is found, or when $Q$ is empty (failure — goal unreachable).

> [!TIP]
> **Why FIFO and not LIFO?**
> The FIFO queue is the *mathematical heart* of BFS. Because the queue preserves **insertion order**, the earliest-enqueued (i.e., shallowest) node is always dequeued first. This is what enforces the level-by-level exploration. Replace the queue with a *stack* (LIFO) and you instantly get **Depth-First Search (DFS)** — a frequent KTU comparison question.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Algorithm Property Matrix

| Property | Value / Expression | Engineering Implication for Mobile Robots |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Scans the entire reachable workspace once |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Stores every visited cell in the grid map |
| **Optimality (Unweighted)** | ✅ **Optimal** — minimum hops | Returns shortest step-count path |
| **Optimality (Weighted)** | ❌ **Not Optimal** | Cannot handle varying terrain costs |
| **Completeness** | ✅ **Complete** | Guaranteed to find a path if one exists |
| **Branching Factor Effect** | $O(b^d)$ nodes expanded, $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth | Memory grows exponentially with grid size |
| **Cost Function** | $g(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c(n_{i-1}, n_i) = k$ | Uniform cost = 1 per step |
| **Heuristic Required** | $h(n) = 0$ (uninformed) | No domain knowledge used |
| **Path Cost Formula** | $C^\* = \min_{P \in \mathcal{P}} \sum_{e \in P} w(e)$ | Minimum sum of edge weights |
| **Queue Type** | FIFO (First-In-First-Out) | Determines exploration pattern |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where the Pipe-Notation Rule Applies:**
> In the table above, the cardinality of a set (e.g., $\vert V \vert$) is rendered using the LaTeX command `\vert` rather than the bare pipe symbol `|`, in order to preserve the structural integrity of the markdown table.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of BFS in Robotics

- **Warehouse AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) Routing:** BFS is the classical planner for AGVs navigating between fixed bins in a known warehouse floor plan.
- **Social-Robot Navigation in Known Cafeterias:** When the table-and-aisle topology is pre-mapped, BFS guarantees the robot takes the *fewest-turn* route to a customer's table.
- **Cleaning Robots (e.g., Roomba-class) in Bounded Rooms:** BFS can compute exhaustive coverage paths when the room is small and discretized into a coarse grid.
- **Disaster-Response UGVs:** In rubble-cleared corridors, BFS provides a guaranteed short path even without gradient information.

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use BFS for:**
> - Large outdoor terrain with varying friction (use **Dijkstra's** or **A\***).
> - Real-time dynamic obstacle avoidance (use **DWA** or **Potential Fields**).
> - Unbounded or continuous configuration spaces (use **RRT** or **PRM**).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Foundation — Why BFS Yields the Shortest Path

We formally prove that BFS is optimal for unweighted graphs. Let $s$ be the start node, $t$ the goal, and $d^\*(s, t)$ the *true shortest path length*.

**Claim:** When BFS dequeues a node $n$ for the first time, the path reconstructed via parent pointers has length exactly $d^\*(s, n)$.

**Proof by Induction on the depth $k$:**

**Base Case ($k = 0$):**
The only node at depth 0 is the start node $s$ itself. BFS dequeues $s$ first, and $\text{parent}(s) = \text{NULL}$. The path length is 0, which trivially equals $d^\*(s, s) = 0$. ✓

**Inductive Hypothesis:**
Assume that for every node $n$ dequeued at depth $\leq k$, the reconstructed path length equals $d^\*(s, n)$.

**Inductive Step ($k+1$):**
Consider any node $n'$ first discovered (i.e., enqueued) when BFS expands some node $n$ at depth $k$. Then:

$$
g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n') = k + 1
$$

because BFS assigns $g(n') = g(n) + 1$ for an unweighted edge. Any alternative path to $n'$ from $s$ must traverse at least $k+1$ edges, because the shortest path to $n$ is $k$ (inductive hypothesis) and the final hop $(n, n')$ adds one more edge. Hence:

$$
d^*(s, n') = k + 1 = g(n')
$$

Since BFS dequeues all depth-$k$ nodes before any depth-$(k+1)$ node (FIFO invariant), when $n'$ is dequeued, its $g$-value is final and minimal. ∎

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — 4×4 Grid Path Planning

**Problem Setup:**

A differential-drive mobile robot is placed at cell **S(0,0)** of a $4 \times 4$ occupancy grid. The goal cell is **G(3,3)**. The robot can move in **4 directions** (Up, Down, Left, Right) with cost 1 per move. The cell **X(1,1)** and **X(2,2)** are obstacles (impassable). Apply BFS to find the shortest path.

**Initial Grid (S = Start, G = Goal, X = Obstacle, . = Free):**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
S & . & . & . \\
\hline
. & X & . & . \\
\hline
. & . & X & . \\
\hline
. & . & . & G \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Coordinate System:** $(row, col)$, with row increasing downward and column increasing rightward. $S$ is at $(0,0)$ and $G$ is at $(3,3)$.

### Iteration Trace Table

| Step | Dequeue (Front of Q) | Enqueue (Rear of Q) | Visited / Closed Set (after step) |
|---:|---|---|---|
| 0 | — (init) | $\{S(0,0)\}$ | $\{S\}$ |
| 1 | $S(0,0)$ | $\{(0,1), (1,0)\}$ | $\{S, (0,1), (1,0)\}$ |
| 2 | $(0,1)$ | $\{(1,0), (0,2)\}$ | $\{S, (0,1), (1,0), (0,2)\}$ |
| 3 | $(1,0)$ | $\{(0,2), (2,0)\}$ | $\{S, (0,1), (1,0), (0,2), (2,0)\}$ |
| 4 | $(0,2)$ | $\{(2,0), (0,3)\}$ | $\{..., (0,3)\}$ |
| 5 | $(2,0)$ | $\{(0,3), (3,0)\}$ | $\{..., (3,0)\}$ |
| 6 | $(0,3)$ | $\{(3,0)\}$ | $\{..., (0,3)\}$ |
| 7 | $(3,0)$ | $\{(3,1)\}$ | $\{..., (3,1)\}$ |
| 8 | $(3,1)$ | $\{(3,2)\}$ | $\{..., (3,2)\}$ |
| 9 | $(3,2)$ | $\{(3,3)=G\}$ 🎯 | $\{..., G\}$ |
| 10 | $G(3,3)$ | **GOAL FOUND — TERMINATE** | — |

### Step-by-Step Derivation of Path Reconstruction

**Parent Pointer Table (built during BFS):**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|}
\hline
\text{Node } n' & \text{parent}(n') \\
\hline
S(0,0) & \text{NULL} \\
(0,1) & S \\
(1,0) & S \\
(0,2) & (0,1) \\
(2,0) & (1,0) \\
(0,3) & (0,2) \\
(3,0) & (2,0) \\
(3,1) & (3,0) \\
(3,2) & (3,1) \\
(3,3)=G & (3,2) \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Path Reconstruction Algorithm (Backward Chaining):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{rev}} &= [\,] \\
n &= G = (3,3) \\
P_{\text{rev}}.\text{append}(n) \;&\Rightarrow\; P_{\text{rev}} = [(3,3)] \\
n \leftarrow \text{parent}(n) = (3,2) \;&\Rightarrow\; P_{\text{rev}} = [(3,3), (3,2)] \\
n \leftarrow \text{parent}(n) = (3,1) \;&\Rightarrow\; P_{\text{rev}} = [(3,3), (3,2), (3,1)] \\
n \leftarrow \text{parent}(n) = (3,0) \;&\Rightarrow\; P_{\text{rev}} = [(3,3), (3,2), (3,1), (3,0)] \\
n \leftarrow \text{parent}(n) = (2,0) \;&\Rightarrow\; P_{\text{rev}} = [(3,3), (3,2), (3,1), (3,0), (2,0)] \\
n \leftarrow \text{parent}(n) = (1,0) \;&\Rightarrow\; P_{\text{rev}} = [(3,3), (3,2), (3,1), (3,0), (2,0), (1,0)] \\
n \leftarrow \text{parent}(n) = S = (0,0) \;&\Rightarrow\; P_{\text{rev}} = [(3,3), \ldots, S] \\
n = \text{NULL} \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{STOP and REVERSE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Optimal Path (reversed):**

$$
P^\* = (0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (3,1) \to (3,2) \to (3,3)
$$

**Path Cost Verification:**

$$
C^\* = \sum_{i=1}^{6} c(n_{i-1}, n_i) = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 6 \text{ steps}
$$

This is the **minimum possible step count** from $S$ to $G$ in this grid topology, since the diagonal shortcuts are blocked by obstacles at $(1,1)$ and $(2,2)$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — BFS vs. DFS vs. Dijkstra vs. A*

This matrix is **gold** for KTU Part B comparative questions (frequently asked for 7 marks).

| Parameter | **BFS** | **DFS** | **Dijkstra** | **A\*** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data structure | FIFO Queue | LIFO Stack | Priority Queue | Priority Queue |
| Cost $g(n)$ | Depth (uniform) | Depth (uniform) | True accumulated cost | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Heuristic $h(n)$ | Not used ($=0$) | Not used ($=0$) | Not used ($=0$) | Admissible estimate |
| Optimal? | ✅ (unweighted only) | ❌ | ✅ (non-negative weights) | ✅ (if $h$ admissible) |
| Complete? | ✅ | ❌ (infinite graphs) | ✅ | ✅ (if $h$ admissible) |
| Time | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert)$ | $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert)$ |
| Space | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ |
| Robotic use case | Discrete grid planning | Maze exploration | Weighted terrain | Game/nav with heuristic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory-Symptom Insight:** BFS has a notorious *memory-explosion* symptom on large grids because it stores **all frontier nodes simultaneously** in the queue. For a 1000×1000 grid, the queue can hold $10^6$ entries, demanding GB-scale RAM — one reason industrial robots prefer A*.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BFS Algorithmic Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **complete control flow** of a BFS module embedded in a mobile robot's path planner. It captures the initialization, the main loop, the goal check, the expansion stage, and the two termination paths (success vs. failure).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Robot at Start Node S]) --> B[/Initialize: Q enqueue S, g of S = 0, parent of S = NULL/]
    B --> C{Is Queue Q empty?}
    C -- Yes --> Z([FAILURE: Goal unreachable])
    C -- No --> D[Dequeue front node n from Q]
    D --> E{Is n == Goal G?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Reconstruct path via parent pointers]
    F --> Y([SUCCESS: Return shortest path P asterisk])
    E -- No --> G[Generate successors of n via Actions]
    G --> H{For each successor n prime: is n prime in Q or in Closed?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Skip n prime — discard]
    H -- No --> J[Enqueue n prime at rear of Q]
    J --> K[Set g of n prime = g of n + 1]
    K --> L[Set parent of n prime = n]
    L --> C
    I --> C
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*"With a neat diagram, explain the Breadth-First Search algorithm. State any two properties of BFS."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Breadth-First Search is an uninformed graph search algorithm that systematically explores all neighbor nodes at the current depth level before proceeding to nodes at the next depth level. It employs a **First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue** to maintain the search frontier.

**Operational Steps (1 mark):**
1. Insert the start node $S$ into an empty FIFO queue $Q$ and mark it visited.
2. While $Q$ is not empty:
   - Dequeue the front node $n$.
   - If $n$ is the goal $G$, terminate and reconstruct the path via parent pointers.
   - Else, enqueue all unvisited successors of $n$ and mark them visited with parent pointers.

**Diagram (½ mark):** Standard BFS tree / frontier expansion diagram showing level-by-level growth.

**Properties (½ mark):** BFS is **complete** and **optimal** for unweighted (uniform-cost) graphs.

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"Differentiate between Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) in mobile robot path planning."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | BFS | DFS |
|---:|---|---|
| 1 | Uses a **FIFO queue** | Uses a **LIFO stack** |
| 2 | Explores **level-by-level** (breadth-wise) | Explores **branch-by-branch** (depth-wise) |
| 3 | **Optimal** for unweighted graphs | **Not optimal** — may return long detours |
| 4 | Suitable for **shortest-step planning** in grids | Suitable for **maze exploration** & cycle detection |
| 5 | Higher memory usage — stores entire frontier | Lower memory usage in deep trees |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** BFS is preferred when *shortest path* is the priority, whereas DFS is preferred when *memory* is constrained and an approximate path suffices.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B.A — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*(a)* Explain the Breadth-First Search algorithm in detail with a suitable flowchart. Discuss its **completeness**, **optimality**, and **time-space complexity** for a mobile robot navigating an $N \times N$ grid. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A mobile robot is placed at cell **A(0,0)** in a $5 \times 5$ grid. The goal is cell **J(4,4)**. Obstacles are present at cells **(1,2), (2,1), (3,3), and (2,3)**. The robot can move only in 4 directions (Up, Down, Left, Right) with unit cost. **Apply BFS step-by-step** to determine the **shortest path** from A to J. Also compute the **total number of nodes expanded** and the **path length**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to B.A(a)

**Algorithm Description (3 marks):**

The BFS algorithm for a mobile robot proceeds as follows:

1. **Inputs:** Occupancy grid $G$, start cell $S$, goal cell $T$.
2. **Initialize:** Empty FIFO queue $Q$; visited set $V \gets \{S\}$; parent[$S$] $\gets \text{NULL}$; cost $g(S) \gets 0$.
3. **Enqueue** $S$ into $Q$.
4. **Loop** while $Q$ is not empty:
   - $n \gets Q.\text{dequeue}()$
   - **If** $n = T$: reconstruct path using parent pointers; return success.
   - **For each** valid neighbor $n'$ of $n$ (in 4-connectivity) that is **free** and $n' \notin V$:
     - $V \gets V \cup \{n'\}$
     - parent[$n'$] $\gets n$
     - $g(n') \gets g(n) + 1$
     - $Q.\text{enqueue}(n')$
5. **If** $Q$ becomes empty, return failure.

**Flowchart (1 mark):** Same as the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 of these notes.

**Completeness (1 mark):** BFS is **complete** — if a path from $S$ to $T$ exists, BFS will eventually expand every reachable node and find it. Proof: the queue processes nodes in order of discovery, and the goal lies in the connected component of $S$ which is fully enumerated.

**Optimality (1 mark):** BFS is **optimal** for graphs with uniform edge cost. The first time the goal is dequeued, the path reconstructed has the minimum number of steps. Proof is given in Section 3.1 of these notes (induction on depth $k$).

**Complexity (1 mark):**
For an $N \times N$ grid: $\vert V \vert = N^2$ and $\vert E \vert \leq 4N^2$ (4-connectivity).

$$
T_{\text{BFS}} = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) = O(N^2 + 4N^2) = O(N^2)
$$

$$
S_{\text{BFS}} = O(\vert V \vert) = O(N^2)
$$

---

### Question B.B — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*(a)* Explain the role of the **Frontier (Open List)** and **Explored (Closed List)** data structures in Breadth-First Search. Why is the **FIFO queue** critical to the algorithm's correctness? *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Compare and contrast BFS with **Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)** and **A\*** search algorithms. Construct a **decision flowchart** that a mobile robot's planner module should use to select among BFS, UCS, and A\* for a given scenario. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to B.B(a)

**Frontier (Open List) — Definition (1 mark):**
The frontier, also called the **open list**, is the FIFO queue $Q$ that holds all nodes that have been *generated* (i.e., discovered as successors of expanded nodes) but **not yet expanded**. It represents the "leading edge" of the search wavefront.

**Explored (Closed List) — Definition (1 mark):**
The explored set, also called the **closed list**, is a hash-set or boolean grid $C$ that records every node which has been **dequeued and expanded**. It serves two purposes: (i) prevents re-expansion of the same node, and (ii) prevents infinite loops in cyclic grids.

**Co-ordination Between the Two (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{When a node } n' \text{ is generated:} \quad & n' \in Q \;\text{or}\; n' \in C \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{skip} \\
& \text{otherwise} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Q \leftarrow Q \cup \{n'\},\; C \leftarrow C \cup \{n'\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This dual bookkeeping is what guarantees BFS's **completeness** — no node is ever expanded twice, and no reachable node is ever permanently discarded.

**Why FIFO Queue is Critical to Correctness (3 marks):**

The FIFO queue is the **mathematical heart** of BFS. Three properties follow from it:

1. **Earliest-discovered, earliest-expanded invariant:** If node $a$ is enqueued before node $b$, then $a$ is dequeued before $b$. Combined with the fact that every enqueued node has $g(n) = g(\text{parent}) + 1$, this enforces that **all depth-$k$ nodes are dequeued before any depth-$(k+1)$ node**.
2. **Termination of optimality proof:** The induction proof in Section 3.1 *crucially* depends on the FIFO order; replace the queue with a stack (LIFO) and the inductive hypothesis fails because a deep node can be dequeued before a shallow one.
3. **Distinguishing characteristic vs. DFS:** FIFO $\Rightarrow$ BFS; LIFO $\Rightarrow$ DFS. The data structure is the *only* algorithmic difference.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{FIFO Queue} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Breadth-First Exploration} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Shortest-Path Optimality in Unweighted Graphs}\;}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to B.B(b)

**Detailed Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | **BFS** | **UCS** | **A\*** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost $g(n)$ | Depth (number of hops) | True path cost from start | True path cost from start |
| Heuristic $h(n)$ | None ($h \equiv 0$) | None ($h \equiv 0$) | Admissible estimate $h(n) \leq h^\*(n)$ |
| Evaluation function | $f(n) = g(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Priority by | Insertion order (FIFO) | $g(n)$ ascending | $f(n)$ ascending |
| Optimal? | ✅ (unweighted only) | ✅ (non-negative weights) | ✅ (admissible heuristic) |
| Data structure | FIFO Queue | Min-Priority Queue | Min-Priority Queue |
| Informed? | No (uninformed) | No (uninformed) | Yes (informed) |
| Typical robotic use | Grid-based, unit-cost | Weighted terrain, energy-aware | Game nav, known maps |

**Decision Flowchart for a Robot Planner (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([New path planning request received]) --> B{Are all edge costs uniform / unit?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is a domain heuristic available?}
    B -- No --> D{Is a tight admissible heuristic available?}
    C -- Yes --> E["Use A asterisk with h equals 0 if needed"]
    C -- No --> F[Use BFS]
    D -- Yes --> G[Use A star search]
    D -- No --> H[Use Uniform Cost Search with Min PQ]
    E --> I[Return path to motion controller]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "optimal" with "complete":** BFS is both, but UCS is also both for non-negative weights. Do not write "BFS is complete" without qualifying that it is *also* optimal.
> 2. **Forgetting the parent pointer table:** Many students compute the path *cost* correctly but cannot reconstruct the *actual cell sequence* in the answer. You will lose **2 marks** if you skip the parent-pointer backtracking step.
> 3. **Using a stack instead of a queue:** A common error is to write "enqueue at front, dequeue at front" (which is a stack). This converts your BFS into DFS silently and invalidates your optimality claim.
> 4. **Ignoring obstacle cells:** Forgetting to filter out cells marked $X$ during the "valid neighbors" check will cause the planner to hallucinate a path *through* walls. Examiners test this by placing an obstacle *directly* in the obvious diagonal route.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Bullets — Re-read these 30 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ BFS = **uniform-cost**, **uninformed**, **level-by-level** graph search.
- ✅ Core data structure is a **FIFO queue** (NOT a stack — that would be DFS).
- ✅ BFS is **complete** (finds a path if one exists) and **optimal for unweighted graphs** (minimum step count).
- ✅ Time complexity is $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$; space complexity is $O(\vert V \vert)$.
- ✅ For an $N \times N$ grid, BFS runs in $O(N^2)$ time and $O(N^2)$ space.
- ✅ The **FIFO invariant** is what makes the induction proof of optimality work: depth-$k$ nodes are dequeued before any depth-$(k+1)$ node.
- ✅ **Parent pointers** must be stored for *every enqueued* node — they are the only way to reconstruct the final path.
- ✅ A node is enqueued **only if** it is neither in the frontier $Q$ nor in the closed set $C$ — this prevents cycles and redundant expansion.
- ✅ BFS is **NOT optimal** when edge costs vary; in such cases use **Dijkstra's** (UCS) or **A\***.
- ✅ BFS is **memory-hungry** on large grids — a frequent exam question asks for *alternatives* (answer: A\* with admissible heuristic).
- ✅ BFS assumes the **environment is known and static**; it is unsuitable for dynamic obstacles without re-planning.
- ✅ Real-world robotic deployments of BFS: warehouse AGV routing, room-cleaning robots, social robot navigation in known cafeterias.
- ✅ **Comparison question** = guaranteed 7 marks in KTU Part B — memorize the BFS vs. DFS vs. UCS vs. A\* table.
- ✅ In a 4-connected grid, every cell has up to 4 neighbors; in an 8-connected grid, up to 8. The branching factor $b$ is **4** or **8** respectively.
- ✅ Always **draw the queue state** at each step in the answer sheet — it is worth **½ to 1 bonus mark** for clarity and earns examiner goodwill.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dijkstra's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dijkstra's Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dijkstra's Algorithm** is a classical, single-source, **greedy graph-search algorithm** that computes the minimum cumulative traversal cost (shortest path) from a designated start node to every other node in a weighted graph whose edges carry strictly **non-negative weights**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme – Mobile Robotics (PBRUT504)**, Module 3 covers *Global Path Planning in Known Environments*. Dijkstra's algorithm is the foundational deterministic planner that a mobile robot (e.g., a warehouse AGV or a service robot) executes on a discretized occupancy grid or weighted roadmap to guarantee an **optimal, collision-free path** from its current pose to the goal pose.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBRUT504 – Module 3):**
> Dijkstra's algorithm is listed under *Classical/Deterministic Path Planning Approaches* in weighted configuration spaces. It is a *guaranteed-optimum* planner (unlike reactive/local planners) and forms the comparison baseline for heuristic methods such as **A\*** and **D\***.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Flow Intuition"

Imagine you are standing at the **source node (S)** in a city, and you spill a bucket of water. The water spreads outward through the streets, but every street has a *friction coefficient* (its weight). The water always chooses the path of least resistance to reach every other city. The first droplet to reach a city is guaranteed to have travelled via the *shortest (lowest-cost) route*.

- **You** = the algorithm exploring the graph
- **Cities** = nodes (robot configurations / waypoints)
- **Streets with friction** = weighted edges (distance, time, energy)
- **First droplet reaching a city** = final optimal distance

### 1.3 Key Properties (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Property | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Strategy** | Greedy (locally optimal choice at each step) |
| **Edge-weight constraint** | Strictly **non-negative** weights only |
| **Optimality** | Complete and **optimal** in finite graphs |
| **Single / Multi-source** | Single-source shortest path to *all* nodes |
| **Data structure used** | **Min-Priority Queue** (typically a Binary Min-Heap) |
| **Inventor & Year** | Edsger W. Dijkstra, **1956** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why non-negative weights?** Because once a node is "finalized" (extracted from the priority queue), the algorithm assumes no shorter path to it can ever be discovered. This assumption collapses if a negative edge could retroactively reduce the distance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Greedy Logic

The algorithm maintains three data structures for every node $v \in V$:

1. **$d[v]$** — current best known distance from source $s$ to $v$.
2. **$\pi[v]$** — predecessor of $v$ on the current best path (used for path reconstruction).
3. **visited$[v]$** — boolean flag; `true` once $v$ is permanently finalized.

A fourth conceptual object is the **Min-Priority Queue $Q$** keyed on $d[v]$, which always yields the unvisited node with the smallest tentative distance.

### 2.2 Operational Steps (The KTU Board Favourite)

1. **Initialization**
   - $d[s] \leftarrow 0$
   - $d[v] \leftarrow \infty \;\; \forall \, v \neq s$
   - $\pi[v] \leftarrow \text{NIL} \;\; \forall \, v$
   - $\text{visited}[v] \leftarrow \text{false} \;\; \forall \, v$
   - Build priority queue $Q$ containing all nodes keyed on $d[\cdot]$.

2. **Main Loop** — while $Q$ is not empty:
   - $u \leftarrow Q.\text{extract\_min}()$
   - Mark $u$ as visited (finalized).
   - For every neighbour $v$ of $u$:
     - Compute candidate distance: $\text{alt} = d[u] + w(u, v)$
     - **Edge Relaxation:** if $\text{alt} < d[v]$, then
       - $d[v] \leftarrow \text{alt}$
       - $\pi[v] \leftarrow u$
       - $Q.\text{decrease\_key}(v, \text{alt})$

3. **Termination** — When $Q$ is empty, $d[v]$ holds the optimal shortest distance and $\pi[v]$ encodes the shortest-path tree (SPT).

### 2.3 The Core "Edge Relaxation" Equation

The single most important equation in Dijkstra's algorithm is the **relaxation operator**:

$$d[v] = \min\bigl(d[v],\, d[u] + w(u,v)\bigr)$$

This says: *"The best known distance to $v$ is either the path we already know, or the path that goes through $u$ and then traverses edge $(u,v)$ — whichever is shorter."*

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner-Ready Table — Memorize Before Exam**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initialization | $d[s] = 0,\; d[v \neq s] = \infty$ | Source is zero-cost from itself |
| 2 | Edge Relaxation | $d[v] = \min(d[v],\, d[u] + w(u,v))$ | Heart of the algorithm |
| 3 | Path Cost (recovered) | $\text{cost}(s \to v) = d[v]$ | Final optimal distance |
| 4 | Time Complexity (Adj. List + Heap) | $O\bigl((V + E)\log V\bigr)$ | Standard implementation |
| 5 | Time Complexity (Adj. Matrix) | $O\bigl(V^{2}\bigr)$ | Naive implementation |
| 6 | Space Complexity | $O(V + E)$ | Storage of graph + structures |
| 7 | Feasibility Constraint | $w(u,v) \geq 0 \;\; \forall \, (u,v)$ | **NO** negative weights |
| 8 | Path Reconstruction | $v \to \pi[v] \to \pi[\pi[v]] \to \dots \to s$ | Trace predecessors back to source |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Mobile Robotics

| Robot Application | Role of Dijkstra's Algorithm |
|---|---|
| **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva-style)** | Computes shortest pick-route on a fixed aisle graph |
| **Autonomous Cars (Highway maps)** | Pre-computes fastest-route on road-network graphs (OpenStreetMap / OSRM) |
| **Service Robots in Hospitals** | Optimal corridor traversal on a topological floor map |
| **Drone Swarm Coverage** | Minimum-energy waypoint ordering on a meshed survey region |
| **Multi-Robot Fleet Dispatch** | All-pairs shortest path via repeated Dijkstra from each depot |

### 2.6 Limitations (Often Asked as a 3-Mark Question)

1. **Cannot handle negative edge weights** — correctness proof breaks.
2. **Computationally expensive in large dense graphs** — re-planning in dynamic environments is slow.
3. **No heuristic guidance** — explores uniformly in all directions, unlike A\* which biases toward the goal.
4. **Static environment assumption** — cannot adapt to moving obstacles without re-execution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

### 3.1 The Sample Weighted Graph (KTU Standard Setup)

Consider the following undirected weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with **5 nodes** representing waypoints in a robot's known environment map. We want shortest paths from source $s = A$.

| Edge $(u, v)$ | Weight $w(u,v)$ |
|---|---|
| $(A, B)$ | **4** |
| $(A, C)$ | **2** |
| $(B, C)$ | **1** |
| $(B, D)$ | **5** |
| $(B, E)$ | **6** |
| $(C, D)$ | **8** |
| $(C, E)$ | **10** |
| $(D, E)$ | **2** |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Execution Trace

We maintain a table of the form: **iter $\vert$ visited $u$ $\vert$ $d[A]$ $\vert$ $d[B]$ $\vert$ $d[C]$ $\vert$ $d[D]$ $\vert$ $d[E]$** with $\pi[\cdot]$ in parentheses. We use the **standard min-extraction (greedy) rule**.

#### **Iteration 0 — Initialization**

Set $d[A] = 0$, all other distances to $\infty$. All predecessors NIL.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d[A] &= 0, \quad \pi[A] = \text{NIL} \\
d[B] &= d[C] = d[D] = d[E] = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

Priority queue order: $\{A{:}0,\; B{:}\infty,\; C{:}\infty,\; D{:}\infty,\; E{:}\infty\}$.

#### **Iteration 1 — Extract $A$ (min = 0), Finalize $A$**

Neighbours of $A$: $B$ (weight 4), $C$ (weight 2).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Relax } A \to B &: \quad d[B] = \min(\infty,\, 0+4) = 4,\;\; \pi[B]=A \\
\text{Relax } A \to C &: \quad d[C] = \min(\infty,\, 0+2) = 2,\;\; \pi[C]=A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Snapshot after Iter 1:** $d = [0,\; 4,\; 2,\; \infty,\; \infty]$, visited $=\{A\}$.

#### **Iteration 2 — Extract $C$ (min = 2), Finalize $C$**

Neighbours of $C$: $A$ (visited), $B$ (weight 1), $D$ (weight 8), $E$ (weight 10).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Relax } C \to B &: \quad d[B] = \min(4,\, 2+1) = 3,\;\; \pi[B]=C \\
\text{Relax } C \to D &: \quad d[D] = \min(\infty,\, 2+8) = 10,\;\; \pi[D]=C \\
\text{Relax } C \to E &: \quad d[E] = \min(\infty,\, 2+10) = 12,\;\; \pi[E]=C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Snapshot after Iter 2:** $d = [0,\; 3,\; 2,\; 10,\; 12]$, visited $=\{A, C\}$.

#### **Iteration 3 — Extract $B$ (min = 3), Finalize $B$**

Neighbours of $B$: $A$ (visited), $C$ (visited), $D$ (weight 5), $E$ (weight 6).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Relax } B \to D &: \quad d[D] = \min(10,\, 3+5) = 8,\;\; \pi[D]=B \\
\text{Relax } B \to E &: \quad d[E] = \min(12,\, 3+6) = 9,\;\; \pi[E]=B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Snapshot after Iter 3:** $d = [0,\; 3,\; 2,\; 8,\; 9]$, visited $=\{A, C, B\}$.

#### **Iteration 4 — Extract $D$ (min = 8), Finalize $D$**

Neighbours of $D$: $B$ (visited), $C$ (visited), $E$ (weight 2).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Relax } D \to E &: \quad d[E] = \min(9,\, 8+2) = 9 \quad \text{(no update)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Snapshot after Iter 4:** $d = [0,\; 3,\; 2,\; 8,\; 9]$, visited $=\{A, C, B, D\}$.

#### **Iteration 5 — Extract $E$ (min = 9), Finalize $E$**

No unvisited neighbours remain. **Algorithm terminates.**

### 3.3 Final Result Table (What Goes in the Answer Script)

| Node $v$ | $d[v]$ | $\pi[v]$ | Shortest Path from $A$ | Path Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | **0** | NIL | $A$ | 0 |
| $B$ | **3** | $C$ | $A \to C \to B$ | 3 |
| $C$ | **2** | $A$ | $A \to C$ | 2 |
| $D$ | **8** | $B$ | $A \to C \to B \to D$ | 8 |
| $E$ | **9** | $B$ | $A \to C \to B \to E$ | 9 |

### 3.4 Verification (Why $A \to C \to B \to D$ Beats the Direct $A \to C \to D$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}(A \to C \to D) &= w(A,C) + w(C,D) = 2 + 8 = 10 \\
\text{Cost}(A \to C \to B \to D) &= w(A,C) + w(C,B) + w(B,D) = 2 + 1 + 5 = 8 \\
\therefore \quad 8 &< 10 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The relaxation in Iter 3 successfully **re-routed** the path to $D$ through $B$ once the cheaper alternative was discovered — this is the essence of Dijkstra's correctness.

### 3.5 Optimality Proof Sketch (For 14-Mark Answers)

1. **Invariant:** When a node $u$ is extracted from $Q$, $d[u]$ equals the true shortest-path distance.
2. **Proof by contradiction:** Assume a shorter path $P$ to $u$ exists via some unvisited node $x$. Then $d[x] \leq \text{cost}(P) < d[u]$, contradicting that $u$ had minimum $d$-value in $Q$.
3. **Termination:** After $|V|$ extractions, $Q$ is empty and all $d[\cdot]$ are optimal.

$$\blacksquare$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Sample Graph (Mermaid Graph View)

The following Mermaid block renders the **5-node weighted graph** used in the worked example. Node IDs are alphanumeric prefixes (`nA`, `nB`, …) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard, and quoted labels contain **clean uppercase text only** (no markdown bold/italics).

```mermaid
graph LR
    nA["A"]
    nB["B"]
    nC["C"]
    nD["D"]
    nE["E"]
    nA --- nB
    nA --- nC
    nB --- nC
    nB --- nD
    nB --- nE
    nC --- nD
    nC --- nE
    nD --- nE
```

**Edge weights (rendered as a parallel association table for clarity, since Mermaid `graph LR` does not natively support edge labels in all renderers):**

| Edge | Weight |
|---|---|
| A - B | 4 |
| A - C | 2 |
| B - C | 1 |
| B - D | 5 |
| B - E | 6 |
| C - D | 8 |
| C - E | 10 |
| D - E | 2 |

### 4.2 Algorithm Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This flowchart captures the *control flow* of Dijkstra's algorithm as it would appear in a KTU board-answer sketch. Node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords like `end` are used as IDs).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["START"]
    nodeInit["INIT: d s = 0, d v = infinity, pi v = NIL, visited = empty"]
    nodeBuildQ["Build min priority queue Q from all nodes"]
    nodeCheckQ{"Q is empty?"}
    nodeExtract["u = extract min from Q"]
    nodeMark["Mark u as visited and finalized"]
    nodeNeighbor["For each neighbour v of u"]
    nodeRelax["alt = d u + w of u v"]
    nodeCompare{"alt is less than d v?"}
    nodeUpdate["d v = alt  and  pi v = u, then decrease key in Q"]
    nodeOutput["OUTPUT shortest path tree d and pi"]
    nodeStop["STOP"]

    nodeStart --> nodeInit
    nodeInit --> nodeBuildQ
    nodeBuildQ --> nodeCheckQ
    nodeCheckQ -- "No" --> nodeExtract
    nodeExtract --> nodeMark
    nodeMark --> nodeNeighbor
    nodeNeighbor --> nodeRelax
    nodeRelax --> nodeCompare
    nodeCompare -- "Yes" --> nodeUpdate
    nodeUpdate --> nodeCheckQ
    nodeCompare -- "No" --> nodeCheckQ
    nodeCheckQ -- "Yes" --> nodeOutput
    nodeOutput --> nodeStop
```

### 4.3 Application Block Diagram — Mobile Robot Path Planning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeMap["Known Environment Map"]
    nodeGrid["Occupancy Grid or Roadmap Graph"]
    nodeAssign["Assign Edge Weights w from cost function"]
    nodeRun["Execute Dijkstra from start pose to goal pose"]
    nodeSPT["Extract Shortest Path Tree SPT"]
    nodePath["Reconstruct path via pi backpointers"]
    nodeSmooth["Optional Smoothing Spline for Robot Trajectory"]
    nodeExec["Low Level Controller Executes Path"]

    nodeMap --> nodeGrid
    nodeGrid --> nodeAssign
    nodeAssign --> nodeRun
    nodeRun --> nodeSPT
    nodeSPT --> nodePath
    nodePath --> nodeSmooth
    nodeSmooth --> nodeExec
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to initialize $\pi[\cdot]$ to NIL** — you will lose 1 mark in the initialization step.
> 2. **Not showing the relaxation arithmetic** — examiners *require* the explicit computation $d[u] + w(u,v)$ and the comparison with old $d[v]$. A bare "updated" is worth **zero**.
> 3. **Picking the wrong extract-min node** — always re-scan the *unvisited* set for the *minimum current distance*. Do not pick a node just because it appears "next" in alphabetical order.
> 4. **Forgetting that the algorithm terminates with $|V|$ extractions**, not when the goal is reached (unless you early-exit — note both versions for full marks).
> 5. **Confusing Dijkstra with BFS** — BFS is the *unweighted special case*; stating "Dijkstra = BFS with weights" is acceptable, but **never claim BFS handles weights**.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm. State any two conditions under which it fails to produce correct results.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Dijkstra's algorithm is a **greedy, single-source shortest-path algorithm** that finds the minimum cumulative cost from a designated start node to every other node in a weighted graph with **non-negative edge weights**. It iteratively extracts the unvisited node with the smallest tentative distance and relaxes all its outgoing edges.
>
> *Two failure conditions:*
> 1. **Negative edge weights:** the greedy "finalize-once" assumption breaks because a path through an unvisited node may later become cheaper via a negative edge.
> 2. **Negative-weight cycles:** the shortest path becomes undefined (cost $\to -\infty$) and the algorithm cannot terminate meaningfully.
>
> *(1 mark – definition, 1 mark – first condition, 1 mark – second condition.)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**With reference to mobile robot path planning, explain how Dijkstra's algorithm uses a priority queue. Why is a min-heap preferred over an unsorted array?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The priority queue $Q$ stores every node $v$ keyed on its current best known distance $d[v]$. The operation $Q.\text{extract\_min}()$ returns the unvisited node with the **smallest tentative distance** in $O(\log V)$ using a binary min-heap. Each successful edge relaxation triggers $Q.\text{decrease\_key}(v, \text{alt})$ which is also $O(\log V)$.
>
> A min-heap is preferred over an unsorted array because the **extract-min** operation is $O(\log V)$ with a heap versus $O(V)$ with linear search. For dense graphs with thousands of nodes, this yields an asymptotic improvement from $O(V^2 + E)$ to $O((V+E)\log V)$.
>
> *(1 mark – role of PQ, 1 mark – min-heap logic, 1 mark – complexity comparison.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has a sub-division **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either Option A or Option B** in full.

---

### **OPTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
**Explain Dijkstra's algorithm in detail. List its steps clearly and derive its time complexity using a min-heap-based priority queue.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** A single-source, greedy, optimal shortest-path algorithm for weighted graphs with non-negative edges.

2. **Algorithm steps (4 marks):**
   - *Step 1 — Initialization:* $d[s]=0$, $d[v]=\infty \; \forall v\neq s$, $\pi[v]=\text{NIL}$, visited = $\emptyset$, build min-heap $Q$.
   - *Step 2 — Extract-min:* $u \leftarrow Q.\text{extract\_min}()$; finalize $u$.
   - *Step 3 — Edge relaxation:* for every neighbour $v$ of $u$, if $d[u] + w(u,v) < d[v]$, then $d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u,v)$, $\pi[v] \leftarrow u$, and call $Q.\text{decrease\_key}(v,\,d[v])$.
   - *Step 4 — Loop control:* repeat Steps 2–3 until $Q = \emptyset$.

3. **Time-complexity derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \underbrace{|V| \cdot O(\log V)}_{\text{extract-min} \times |V|} \;+\; \underbrace{|E| \cdot O(\log V)}_{\text{decrease-key} \times |E|} \\
     &= O\bigl((V + E)\log V\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

   For a connected graph $|E| \geq V-1$, hence $T(n) = O(E \log V)$ in the worst case.

#### **Q.A(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**
**Apply Dijkstra's algorithm on the graph shown below with source node $S$. Show every iteration in tabular form and reconstruct the shortest path from $S$ to every other node.**

| Edge | $S$-$A$ | $S$-$B$ | $A$-$B$ | $A$-$C$ | $B$-$C$ | $A$-$D$ | $C$-$D$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weight | 4 | 2 | 1 | 5 | 3 | 7 | 2 |

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Iteration 0** — Initialize: $d[S]=0$, others $=\infty$.

**Iter 1** — Extract $S$ (0). Relax $S\!\to\!A$: $d[A]=4,\; \pi[A]=S$. Relax $S\!\to\!B$: $d[B]=2,\; \pi[B]=S$.

**Iter 2** — Extract $B$ (2). Relax $B\!\to\!A$: $\min(4,\,2+1)=3,\; \pi[A]=B$. Relax $B\!\to\!C$: $d[C]=5,\; \pi[C]=B$.

**Iter 3** — Extract $A$ (3). Relax $A\!\to\!C$: $\min(5,\,3+5)=5$ (no change). Relax $A\!\to\!D$: $d[D]=10,\; \pi[D]=A$.

**Iter 4** — Extract $C$ (5). Relax $C\!\to\!D$: $\min(10,\,5+2)=7,\; \pi[D]=C$.

**Iter 5** — Extract $D$ (7). No unvisited neighbours.

**Final shortest-path table (3 marks):**

| Node | $d[v]$ | Path from $S$ | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S$ | 0 | $S$ | 0 |
| $A$ | 3 | $S \to B \to A$ | 3 |
| $B$ | 2 | $S \to B$ | 2 |
| $C$ | 5 | $S \to B \to C$ | 5 |
| $D$ | 7 | $S \to B \to C \to D$ | 7 |

*(1 mark – each major iteration table, 2 marks – final summary table, 1 mark – correct predecessor chain.)*

---

### **OPTION B (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.B(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**Compare Dijkstra's algorithm with the Bellman-Ford algorithm. Tabulate at least five comparison points and indicate which one is preferable for a mobile-robot path planner in a static grid map.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Criterion | Dijkstra's Algorithm | Bellman-Ford Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Edge-weight support | Non-negative **only** | Handles **negative** edges too |
| Time complexity | $O((V+E)\log V)$ with heap | $O(V \cdot E)$ |
| Optimality | Optimal under non-negative weights | Optimal even with negative edges (no neg. cycles) |
| Negative-cycle detection | **No** | **Yes** |
| Strategy | Greedy (single source of truth) | Dynamic Programming (relaxes all edges $V-1$ times) |
| Typical use-case | Road networks, robot grids | Currency arbitrage, RIP routing |
| Preference for static robot grid | **✅ Preferred (faster)** | ❌ Overkill for non-negative costs |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For a *static, non-negative-cost* mobile-robot grid, **Dijkstra's algorithm is preferred** because the constraint $w(u,v) \geq 0$ is naturally satisfied (distances, time, energy are all non-negative), and the $O((V+E)\log V)$ complexity is significantly faster than Bellman-Ford's $O(VE)$.

*(1 mark per row, 1 mark for the conclusion, 1 mark for engineering justification.)*

#### **Q.B(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
**Discuss with a neat block diagram how Dijkstra's algorithm is integrated into a mobile robot's global path-planning pipeline. Mention the input, processing, and output stages clearly.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Stages of the planning pipeline (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.3):**

1. **Input Stage (2 marks):**
   - **Environment Map:** Pre-built 2D occupancy grid (from SLAM) or topological graph.
   - **Start & Goal Poses:** $(x_s, y_s)$ and $(x_g, y_g)$ from the mission planner.
   - **Cost Function:** $w(u,v)$ — Euclidean distance, traversal time, or energy estimate.

2. **Processing Stage (3 marks):**
   - **Graph Construction:** Cells/waypoints become nodes; 4- or 8-connected neighbours become edges.
   - **Weight Assignment:** $w(u,v) = \sqrt{(x_u-x_v)^2 + (y_u-y_v)^2}$ (or terrain-dependent cost).
   - **Dijkstra Execution:** Initialize $d[\cdot]$, run main loop, relax edges, extract shortest path tree.

3. **Output Stage (2 marks):**
   - **Path Reconstruction:** Trace $\pi[\cdot]$ back from goal to start.
   - **Path Smoothing:** Apply a quadratic Bézier or cubic spline for wheel-velocity continuity.
   - **Trajectory Dispatch:** Hand over to the local planner / controller for execution.

**Engineering Note (1 mark):** For dynamic environments with moving obstacles, the global Dijkstra plan is **re-executed periodically** (e.g., every 1 s) or fused with a local reactive planner such as **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Dijkstra = Greedy + Single-Source + Non-Negative Weights + Optimal.**
- ✅ **Heart of the algorithm:** Edge relaxation $d[v] = \min(d[v],\, d[u] + w(u,v))$.
- ✅ **Always initialize** $d[s]=0$ and $d[v\neq s]=\infty$ **before** the main loop.
- ✅ **Use a min-priority queue (binary heap)** for $O(\log V)$ extract-min and decrease-key.
- ✅ **Final complexity:** $O((V+E)\log V)$ — write it precisely in the exam.
- ✅ **Path recovery** is done via the predecessor array $\pi[\cdot]$, **not** by reversing the visit order.
- ✅ **Never** apply Dijkstra to graphs with **negative edge weights** — it gives wrong results.
- ✅ **In mobile robotics**, Dijkstra underpins classical global planners on grid/roadmap graphs; it is optimal but **slower** than heuristic A\*.
- ✅ **BFS is a special case of Dijkstra** where every edge has weight = 1.
- ✅ **Dijkstra explores uniformly** in all directions; **A\*** adds heuristic bias $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ toward the goal.
- ✅ **Early-exit optimisation:** If you only need the shortest path to a single goal $t$, terminate the loop the moment $t$ is extracted from $Q$ — but the textbook complexity bound still holds.
- ✅ **Examiner pet-trap:** Marks are awarded for **showing the relaxation arithmetic**, not just the final distance table.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Best First Search - A and A* Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Best First Search - A and A* Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Best-First Search (Greedy Best-First Search)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> Best-First Search is an *informed search* (also called heuristic search) strategy in mobile robotics path planning that expands the node which is *estimated* to be closest to the goal, using an **evaluation function** that combines the actual path cost and a heuristic estimate of the distance to the goal.

In its pure **Greedy Best-First form**, the algorithm uses only the heuristic component to order node expansion.

$$f(n) = h(n)$$

where $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate of the cost from node $n$ to the nearest goal state.

### Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Tourist Using Only a Sight-Seeing Map"**
> Imagine you are a tourist in Kerala trying to reach Munnar from Kochi. You have a map that shows the **straight-line (aerial) distance** from every town to Munnar. Greedy Best-First Search is the tourist who, at every junction, picks the road that *appears* to head most directly toward Munnar (lowest aerial distance). The tourist ignores the actual road length, road condition, or traffic — they just chase the direction of the goal. This works surprisingly often but can get the tourist trapped in a beautiful but *wrong* valley that looks short but is actually a dead-end or detour.

## 1.2 The A* Algorithm

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> **A\*** is a *best-first, informed graph search algorithm* that finds the **least-cost path** from a start node to a goal node in a weighted graph. It combines the actual cost incurred so far with a heuristic estimate of the remaining cost, guaranteeing **optimality** when the heuristic is *admissible* (and *consistent* for tree search optimality).

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost of the path from the **start** node $S$ to node $n$
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate of the cost from node $n$ to the **goal** $G$
- $f(n)$ = the algorithm's *best guess* of the total cost of a path through $n$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A* is the workhorse of robot motion planning (used in ROS Navigation Stack's global planner, Google Maps road routing, and game pathfinding like in Chess/StarCraft AI). The 2024 syllabus emphasises that the quality of $h(n)$ directly determines the *efficiency* of search, while the *admissibility* of $h(n)$ determines the *optimality* of the result.

### A* vs Greedy Best-First — Intuitive Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Smart Tourist vs The Eager Tourist**
> - **Greedy Best-First** = the *eager tourist* who only looks at "how far Munnar *looks*" — fast decisions, may fail.
> - **A\*** = the *smart tourist* who knows "the **actual kilometers traveled so far** + the **aerial distance remaining**" — balances effort spent with effort to come, and is *guaranteed* to find the shortest route if the aerial map is honest (admissible).

### Key Terminology (Bolded Standard Metrics)

| Term | Symbol | Standard Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------|
| **Heuristic function** | $h(n)$ | Estimated cost from $n$ to goal |
| **Path cost** | $g(n)$ | True cost accumulated from start |
| **Evaluation function** | $f(n)$ | $g(n) + h(n)$ — search ordering key |
| **Optimal cost** | $f^{*}(S)$ | True minimum cost from $S$ to goal |
| **True cost to goal** | $h^{*}(n)$ | Actual optimal cost from $n$ to goal |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Evaluation Function

For any node $n$ in the search frontier (called the **OPEN list** in KTU notation):

### Step 1 — $g(n)$: The "Spent-So-Far" Component
- This is the **true** cost of the cheapest path discovered from the start node $S$ to node $n$.
- It is updated *only* when a shorter path to $n$ is found (path-maximisation / re-parenting).

### Step 2 — $h(n)$: The "Estimated-To-Go" Component
- A **domain-specific** estimate of the cost from $n$ to a goal state $G$.
- For **Euclidean grids**: $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_G)^2 + (y_n - y_G)^2}$
- For **Manhattan grids** (4-connected): $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_G \vert + \vert y_n - y_G \vert$
- For **Diagonal grids** (8-connected): $h(n) = \max(\vert x_n - x_G \vert, \vert y_n - y_G \vert)$

### Step 3 — $f(n)$: The "Total Estimated Cost"
- The sum $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ represents the algorithm's *informed guess* of the cost of the cheapest solution passing through $n$.
- A* always expands the OPEN-list node with the **lowest $f$-value** first.

## 2.2 Admissibility of a Heuristic

A heuristic $h$ is **admissible** if it *never overestimates* the true cost to reach the goal.

$$h(n) \leq h^{*}(n) \quad \forall n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why admissibility matters for KTU 2024:**
> If $h$ is admissible, A* is guaranteed to return an **optimal (least-cost) path**. This is the single most-tested theorem in KTU Module 3 examinations.

## 2.3 Consistency (Monotonicity) of a Heuristic

A heuristic is **consistent** (or monotone) if, for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ generated by action $a$ with step cost $c(n, a, n')$:

$$h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$$

> [!NOTE]
> Every consistent heuristic is also admissible. Consistency additionally guarantees that the $f$-values along any path are **non-decreasing**, which allows A* to be efficiently implemented without re-opening closed nodes.

## 2.4 Algorithmic Operation of A*

The A* algorithm maintains two lists:

1. **OPEN list** — nodes that have been *generated* but not yet *expanded*, prioritised by $f(n)$.
2. **CLOSED list** — nodes that have already been *expanded*.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

- **Initialisation:** Insert start node $S$ into OPEN with $f(S) = g(S) + h(S) = 0 + h(S) = h(S)$.
- **Loop:** If OPEN is empty → **failure** (no path exists). Else, pop the node $n$ with the **lowest $f$** from OPEN.
- **Goal test:** If $n$ is the goal → **return the path** reconstructed via parent pointers.
- **Expansion:** For each successor $n'$ of $n$, compute $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$ and $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$.
- **List management:**
  - If $n'$ is already in CLOSED with a higher $g$ → re-open it (only needed if heuristic is not consistent).
  - If $n'$ is already in OPEN with a higher $g$ → update its parent and $f$.
  - Otherwise → insert $n'$ into OPEN with its computed $f$-value.
- **Termination:** Loop until goal is found or OPEN is empty.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Engineering Utility |
|---------|--------------------|---------------------|
| A\* evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Core node ordering equation |
| Greedy evaluation | $f(n) = h(n)$ | Faster but not optimal |
| Uniform-cost (Dijkstra) | $f(n) = g(n)$ | Optimal but slow (no heuristic) |
| Admissibility | $h(n) \leq h^{*}(n)$ | Guarantees optimality of A* |
| Consistency | $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ | Guarantees monotonic $f$ & re-opening not needed |
| Dominance | $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for all $n$ | $h_2$ expands $\leq$ nodes than $h_1$ if both admissible |
| Euclidean $h$ | $\sqrt{(x_n - x_G)^2 + (y_n - y_G)^2}$ | Continuous 2D/3D robot path planning |
| Manhattan $h$ | $\vert x_n - x_G \vert + \vert y_n - y_G \vert$ | 4-connected grid maps |
| Diagonal $h$ | $\max(\vert x_n - x_G \vert, \vert y_n - y_G \vert)$ | 8-connected grid maps |
| Optimal path cost | $f^{*}(S) = g^{*}(G)$ | $f$ of the start = cost of optimal solution |
| Branching factor effect | $N \approx b^{d}$ | Number of nodes expanded (exponential) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A* is the **de-facto global planner** in mobile robotics stacks:
> - **ROS Navigation** (move_base + global_planner) — uses A* or Dijkstra on occupancy grids.
> - **Autonomous cars** (Waymo, Tesla) — A* on road-network graphs.
> - **Warehouse robots** (Kiva/Amazon) — A* on warehouse map with Manhattan heuristic.
> - **Video games** — A* for NPC pathfinding.
> - **VLSI design** — A* for wire routing in integrated circuits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 3.1 Worked A\* Trace on a Weighted Graph

### Problem Statement
A mobile robot must travel from **Start node S** to **Goal node G**. The graph edges and heuristic estimates are:

| Edge | Cost |
|------|------|
| S → A | 1 |
| S → B | 4 |
| A → B | 3 |
| A → C | 2 |
| A → G | 12 |
| B → C | 1 |
| B → G | 8 |
| C → G | 4 |

| Node | $h(n)$ |
|------|--------|
| S | 7 |
| A | 6 |
| B | 4 |
| C | 2 |
| G | 0 |

### Greedy Best-First Search Trace (for comparison)

> [!NOTE]
> Greedy uses only $h(n)$. Lower $h$ = higher priority.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Open = \{S, f=h(S)=7\}} \\
&\text{Step 2: Expand S (lowest h=7). Open = \{A(h=6), B(h=4)\}} \\
&\text{Step 3: Expand B (lowest h=4). Open = \{A(h=6), C(h=2), G(h=0)\}} \\
&\text{Step 4: Expand G. Path found: S → B → G} \\
&\text{Total cost} = 4 + 8 = \mathbf{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Greedy found cost 12, but this is NOT optimal.**

### A\* Search Trace (with $f = g + h$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A* expands the node with the **lowest $f(n)$** at each step.

**Initialisation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Open} = \{S\}, \quad g(S) = 0, \quad h(S) = 7 \\
&f(S) = g(S) + h(S) = 0 + 7 = \mathbf{7}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1: Expand S (only node)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Successors of S: A, B} \\
&g(A) = 0 + 1 = 1, \quad f(A) = 1 + 6 = \mathbf{7} \\
&g(B) = 0 + 4 = 4, \quad f(B) = 4 + 4 = \mathbf{8} \\
&\text{Open} = \{A(7), B(8)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2: Expand A (f = 7, lower than B's 8)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Successors of A: B, C, G} \\
&g(B)\text{ via A} = 1 + 3 = 4, \quad f(B) = 4 + 4 = \mathbf{8} \;\; \text{(same as via S, no update)} \\
&g(C) = 1 + 2 = 3, \quad f(C) = 3 + 2 = \mathbf{5} \\
&g(G) = 1 + 12 = 13, \quad f(G) = 13 + 0 = \mathbf{13} \\
&\text{Open} = \{C(5), B(8), G(13)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3: Expand C (f = 5, lowest)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Successors of C: G} \\
&g(G)\text{ via C} = 3 + 4 = 7, \quad f(G) = 7 + 0 = \mathbf{7} \;\; \text{(better than 13! update)} \\
&\text{Open} = \{G(7), B(8)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 4: Expand G (f = 7) → GOAL!**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Reconstruct path: } S \rightarrow A \rightarrow C \rightarrow G \\
&\text{Total cost} = 1 + 2 + 4 = \mathbf{7}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Comparison Table — Final Result

| Algorithm | Path Found | Total Cost | Optimal? |
|-----------|------------|------------|----------|
| Greedy Best-First | S → B → G | **12** | ❌ No |
| A* (admissible $h$) | S → A → C → G | **7** | ✅ Yes |

## 3.2 Analytical Summary — A* Iteration Log

| Iter | Open List (f-values) | Expanded Node | $f_{\text{expanded}}$ | Best $g$ Update |
|------|----------------------|----------------|-----------------------|------------------|
| 0 | {S(7)} | — | — | — |
| 1 | {A(7), B(8)} | S | 7 | $g(A)=1, g(B)=4$ |
| 2 | {C(5), B(8), G(13)} | A | 7 | $g(C)=3, g(G)=13$ |
| 3 | {G(7), B(8)} | C | 5 | $g(G)=7$ (improved from 13) |
| 4 | {} (goal reached) | G | 7 | Path: S→A→C→G |

## 3.3 Mathematical Justification of A* Optimality (KTU Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (KTU 2024 expected):** If $h$ is admissible, then any node expanded by A* has $f(n) \leq f^{*}(S)$, where $f^{*}(S)$ is the optimal solution cost.

**Proof Sketch (Examiner-style):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Suppose A* has terminated with goal } G \text{ reached at cost } f(G) = g(G). \\
&\text{Let } G^* \text{ be an optimal goal with cost } f^*(S). \\
&\text{Admissibility} \Rightarrow h(G) = 0 \text{ (heuristic of a goal is 0).} \\
&\text{So } f(G) = g(G) \text{ is the cost of the path A* actually found.} \\
&\text{If } g(G) > f^*(S), \text{ then at termination some OPEN node } n \text{ would still have } f(n) \leq f^*(S) < g(G). \\
