&\text{But A* always expands the minimum-} f \text{ OPEN node, so it would have expanded } n \text{ before declaring success.} \\
&\text{Contradiction.} \therefore g(G) = f^*(S). \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of A*

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional pipeline** of the A* algorithm, mapping the interaction between the Open List, Closed List, the heuristic oracle, and the cost accumulator. This is the architecture examiners expect students to reproduce in KTU 2024 ESE answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start A Star]) --> B1[Initialise OPEN with Start Node S]
    B1 --> B2[Set g S equal to 0 and compute f S = h S]
    B2 --> C1{Is OPEN Empty}
    C1 -->|Yes| F1[Return Failure No Path Exists]
    C1 -->|No| D1[Pop node n with minimum f n from OPEN]
    D1 --> E1{Is n the Goal Node}
    E1 -->|Yes| G1[Reconstruct path via parent pointers and Return Success]
    E1 -->|No| H1[Insert n into CLOSED list]
    H1 --> I1[Generate all valid successors n prime of n]
    I1 --> J1[For each successor compute g n prime = g n + c n n prime]
    J1 --> K1[Query Heuristic Oracle to obtain h n prime]
    K1 --> L1[Compute f n prime = g n prime + h n prime]
    L1 --> M1{Is n prime already in CLOSED with lower g}
    M1 -->|Yes| N1[Skip this successor]
    M1 -->|No| O1{Is n prime already in OPEN with lower g}
    O1 -->|Yes| P1[Skip this successor]
    O1 -->|No| Q1[Insert n prime into OPEN with parent pointer to n]
    N1 --> C1
    P1 --> C1
    Q1 --> C1
    F1 --> Z1([End])
    G1 --> Z1
```

## 4.2 Information Flow Block Diagram (Three-Layer Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[INPUT LAYER]
        P1[Weighted Graph G]
        P2[Start Node S]
        P3[Goal Node G star]
        P4[Heuristic Function h]
    end
    subgraph L2[ALGORITHM CORE]
        C1[Open List Priority Queue sorted by f]
        C2[Closed List Hash Set]
        C3[Cost Accumulator g n]
        C4[Parent Pointer Map]
    end
    subgraph L3[OUTPUT LAYER]
        R1[Reconstructed Optimal Path]
        R2[Path Cost g star]
        R3[Nodes Expanded Count for Performance Analysis]
    end
    P1 --> C1
    P2 --> C1
    P4 --> C1
    P3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> R1
    C3 --> R2
    C4 --> R1
    C1 --> R3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Convention:** When asked to draw the "search tree" in ESE, students should label every node with its $(g, h, f)$ triple and draw parent-pointer arrows. Examiners explicitly look for the *expansion order* being numerically correct.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define heuristic function. State the condition for a heuristic to be admissible with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **heuristic function** $h(n)$ is a domain-specific estimate of the cost of the cheapest path from node $n$ to a goal state, computed using problem-specific knowledge. (1 mark)

A heuristic is **admissible** if it *never overestimates* the true cost to the goal, i.e., for every node $n$:

$$h(n) \leq h^{*}(n)$$

where $h^{*}(n)$ is the actual optimal cost from $n$ to goal. (1 mark)

**Example:** For a robot navigating a 2D grid from cell $(x_n, y_n)$ to goal $(x_G, y_G)$ in 4-connected (Manhattan) motion, $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_G \vert + \vert y_n - y_G \vert$ is admissible because Manhattan distance never exceeds the true shortest path length on a unit-cost grid. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Compare Uniform Cost Search and A\* algorithm in terms of evaluation function and optimality.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Uniform Cost Search | A\* Algorithm |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| Evaluation function | $f(n) = g(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Uses heuristic | No | Yes |
| Optimality | Always optimal | Optimal *if* $h$ is admissible |
| Completeness | Yes (with non-negative costs) | Yes (with non-negative costs) |
| Speed | Slow — expands in all directions | Fast — heuristic guides toward goal |

(3 marks — 1 mark each for any 3 distinct correct comparison points)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the A\* search algorithm with a neat flowchart. Define the evaluation function and discuss the role of the heuristic function in detail.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A* is an informed best-first search algorithm that finds the least-cost path in a weighted graph. Its evaluation function is $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, where $g(n)$ is the cost of the path from start to $n$ and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate of the cost from $n$ to the goal.

**Flowchart (3 marks):** Show the A* loop (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram converted to a hand-drawn version in the answer script). Key boxes: Initialise OPEN with S → Test for goal → Expand min-f node → Generate successors → Compute g, h, f → Update OPEN/CLOSED → Loop.

**Role of heuristic (2 marks):** The heuristic biases the search toward the goal, drastically reducing the number of nodes expanded (compared to uniform-cost search). A *good* heuristic — one that is admissible, consistent, and dominates others — yields faster convergence while preserving optimality.

**(b) Apply A\* algorithm to find the shortest path from S to G for the following graph. Show all iterations with OPEN and CLOSED lists.** (7 marks)

| Edge | Cost |
|------|------|
| S → A | 3 |
| S → B | 5 |
| A → C | 4 |
| A → G | 10 |
| B → C | 2 |
| B → D | 6 |
| C → G | 5 |
| D → G | 2 |

| Node | $h$ |
|------|-----|
| S | 7 |
| A | 5 |
| B | 4 |
| C | 3 |
| D | 2 |
| G | 0 |

**Model Solution Trace (7 marks):**

**Iter 0:** OPEN = {S(0+7=7)}, CLOSED = {}
**Iter 1:** Expand S(7). Successors: A(g=3,f=3+5=8), B(g=5,f=5+4=9). OPEN = {A(8), B(9)}, CLOSED = {S}
**Iter 2:** Expand A(8). Successors: C(g=3+4=7, f=7+3=10), G(g=3+10=13, f=13+0=13). OPEN = {B(9), C(10), G(13)}, CLOSED = {S, A}
**Iter 3:** Expand B(9). Successors: C(g=5+2=7, f=7+3=10) — equal, skip update; D(g=5+6=11, f=11+2=13). OPEN = {C(10), D(13), G(13)}, CLOSED = {S, A, B}
**Iter 4:** Expand C(10). Successor: G(g=7+5=12, f=12+0=12) — **better than 13!** Update. OPEN = {G(12), D(13)}, CLOSED = {S, A, B, C}
**Iter 5:** Expand G(12) → GOAL REACHED.

**Optimal Path:** S → A → C → G
**Optimal Cost:** $3 + 4 + 5 = 12$

(1 mark per iteration, 1 mark for final path, 1 mark for total cost)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a) Differentiate between Greedy Best-First Search and A\* algorithm. Show with an example where Greedy Best-First fails but A\* succeeds.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Differences (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Greedy Best-First | A\* |
|--------|--------------------|------|
| Evaluation function | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Uses path cost | No | Yes |
| Optimal | Not necessarily | Optimal (with admissible $h$) |
| Speed | Faster in lucky cases | Slightly slower but reliable |
| Susceptible to local minima | Yes | No (g(n) anchors the search) |

**Counter-example (3 marks):** (Re-use the Section 3.1 graph) Greedy chose S→B→G with cost 12, but A* found S→A→C→G with cost 7. This demonstrates Greedy's failure mode: it commits to node B because $h(B) = 4$ is small, but the actual path through B is long. A* avoids this trap because $g(B) = 4$ is already large, making $f(B) = 8$ unattractive compared to other open nodes.

**(b) Define admissible and consistent heuristics. Prove that every consistent heuristic is admissible, with a clear derivation.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- $h$ is **admissible** if $h(n) \leq h^{*}(n)$ for all $n$.
- $h$ is **consistent** if $h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ for every edge $(n, a, n')$.

**Proof that consistent → admissible (5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } P: n = n_0 \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_k = G \text{ be any optimal path from } n \text{ to } G. \\
&\text{By consistency applied recursively along } P: \\
&h(n_0) \leq c(n_0, n_1) + h(n_1) \\
&h(n_1) \leq c(n_1, n_2) + h(n_2) \\
&\quad \vdots \\
&h(n_{k-1}) \leq c(n_{k-1}, n_k) + h(n_k) \\
&\text{Substituting and telescoping: } \\
&h(n) \leq c(n_0, n_1) + c(n_1, n_2) + \cdots + c(n_{k-1}, n_k) + h(G) \\
&\text{Since } h(G) = 0 \text{ for any goal } G: \\
&h(n) \leq h^{*}(n) \\
&\therefore h \text{ is admissible.} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting $h(G) = 0$:** Many students omit the boundary condition $h(\text{goal}) = 0$, which is essential for the consistency→admissibility proof. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing admissibility with optimality of A\*:** Admissibility is a property of the *heuristic*, while optimality is a property of the *algorithm given an admissible heuristic*. Examiners test this distinction.
> 3. **Not showing the parent-pointer reconstruction:** When tracing A*, students often give the path cost but forget to explicitly write the node sequence (e.g., S → A → C → G). Both are required.
> 4. **Inconsistent updates:** In the trace, when re-opening a CLOSED node with a better $g$, students must re-insert it into OPEN. Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Best First Search & A\* Algorithm: High-Yield Revision Bullets**

- ✅ **A\* evaluation function:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — the single most important equation.
- ✅ **Greedy Best-First:** $f(n) = h(n)$ — fast but **not optimal**.
- ✅ **Uniform Cost Search:** $f(n) = g(n)$ — optimal but **slow** (no heuristic guidance).
- ✅ **Admissibility condition:** $h(n) \leq h^{*}(n)$ — *never overestimates*.
- ✅ **Consistency condition:** $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ — implies monotonic $f$-values.
- ✅ **Theorem:** Consistent → Admissible (proved via telescoping sum).
- ✅ **OPEN list:** priority queue of generated-but-not-expanded nodes, sorted by $f$.
- ✅ **CLOSED list:** set of expanded nodes.
- ✅ **Heuristic dominance:** If $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for all $n$ and both admissible, $h_2$ is *more informed* and expands $\leq$ nodes.
- ✅ **Common heuristics on grids:** Manhattan (4-connected), Euclidean (continuous), Diagonal/Chebyshev (8-connected).
- ✅ **A\* optimality:** Guaranteed iff heuristic is admissible (and the search graph has non-negative edge costs).
- ✅ **A\* completeness:** Yes, on finite graphs with non-negative costs and at least one goal reachable.
- ✅ **Practical use in robotics:** ROS global planner, warehouse AMRs, autonomous vehicles, VLSI routing.
- ✅ **Examiner's mantra:** Always show $(g, h, f)$ for every node in the OPEN list at each iteration; always reconstruct the path explicitly; always state the heuristic admissibility before claiming A* is optimal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Searching a Continuous State Space - Potential Fields
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Searching a Continuous State Space - Potential Fields

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Potential Field (APF) Method** is a reactive, gradient-based local path planning technique in which the robot's configuration space (C-space) is treated as a **scalar potential energy landscape**. The goal configuration acts as a global minimum (gravitational sink), while obstacles act as repulsive peaks. The robot navigates by following the **negative gradient** of the total potential, which corresponds to the resultant artificial force acting on it.

The method was formally introduced by **Oussama Khatib in 1986** as a real-time obstacle avoidance framework for manipulators and mobile platforms. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, this is classified under **local navigation / continuous state space search**, where the configuration is a continuous vector **q ∈ ℝⁿ** (typically 2D or 3D for mobile robots).

**Mathematical essence:**

$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$$

$$\mathbf{F}(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

The robot is commanded to move along the direction of **F**, executing a continuous **gradient descent** on the potential landscape.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Ball Rolling on a Hilly Terrain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine placing a marble on a smooth rubber sheet stretched over a tabletop.** The goal point is a **valley** (lowest point) where the marble naturally rolls to rest. Every obstacle is represented as a **sharp upward bump** (hill) that pushes the marble away. If you tilt the rubber sheet so the goal is the deepest point and the obstacles are raised bumps, the marble will roll toward the goal while automatically swerving around the obstacles.

This is precisely the **Potential Field** metaphor:
- **Attractive field** = slope pulling the robot toward the goal (like gravity).
- **Repulsive field** = bumps pushing the robot away from obstacles.
- **Path of robot** = trajectory a ball would take rolling downhill on this energy landscape.

The advantage: **no explicit path search** is required — the path *emerges* from the physics of the field. This makes the technique extremely fast and ideal for real-time execution on embedded robot controllers (typical control loop: **10 Hz – 100 Hz**).

## 1.3 Why Continuous State Space Search?

In discrete grids (Module 1/2 — A*, Dijkstra, BFS), the world is **cell-based**. However, real robots have **continuous pose variables** (x, y, θ). The potential field approach operates directly in ℝⁿ, avoiding discretization artifacts such as:
- Resolution bias (paths locked to grid axes)
- Memory blow-up for high-resolution grids
- Discontinuities at cell boundaries

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Configuration q** | A point in the robot's C-space (e.g., (x, y) for a point robot) |
| **Potential U(q)** | Scalar function assigning energy to every configuration |
| **Artificial Force F** | Vector field derived as the negative gradient of U |
| **Goal configuration q_goal** | Global minimum of U_att |
| **Obstacle region C_obs** | Region of infinite potential (or capped repulsive zone) |
| **Free space C_free** | Region where the robot can move |
| **Local minimum** | Configuration where ∇U = 0 but q ≠ q_goal — a failure mode |
| **Reactive control** | Control law computed directly from sensor readings each cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Potential Fields fall under **"Searching a Continuous State Space"** in Module 3, alongside **Randomized planners (RRT, PRM)**. The key distinction: Potential Fields are **reactive and local**; RRT/PRM are **sampling-based and global**. Examiners love asking for this contrast.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The potential field planner executes the following algorithmic loop each control cycle:

1. **Sense** the current configuration `q = (x, y)` from odometry / localization.
2. **Compute the distance** to the goal: `ρ_goal = ||q − q_goal||`.
3. **Compute the distance** to the nearest obstacle: `ρ_obs = ||q − q_obs||`.
4. **Evaluate** the attractive potential `U_att(q)`.
5. **Evaluate** the repulsive potential `U_rep(q)` for every sensed obstacle within the influence radius.
6. **Sum** to get total potential: `U(q) = U_att(q) + Σ U_rep_i(q)`.
7. **Compute the gradient**: `F(q) = −∇U(q)`.
8. **Command** the robot to move in the direction of `F` with a bounded velocity `v = k·F` (capped at max velocity).
9. **Repeat** at the next control tick.

The "search" of the continuous state space is thus **implicit** — the gradient descent trajectory *is* the search, with no explicit enumeration of states.

## 2.2 The Two Constituent Fields

### 2.2.1 Attractive Potential

The attractive potential pulls the robot toward the goal. Two common forms exist:

**Form 1 — Conic (Linear Far, Quadratic Near):**

$$U_{att}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\, d^2 & \text{if } d \le d^*_{goal} \\ d^*_{goal}\,\zeta\, d - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\, (d^*_{goal})^2 & \text{if } d > d^*_{goal} \end{cases}$$

**Form 2 — Pure Quadratic (Simpler, used in textbooks):**

$$U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\, \rho^2_{goal}(q)$$

where `d = ||q − q_goal||`, **ζ (zeta)** is the attractive gain (units: N/m or 1/m), and `d*_goal` is a threshold distance.

The corresponding **attractive force** is the negative gradient:

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\zeta\,(q - q_{goal})$$

This force grows linearly with distance from the goal, guaranteeing the goal is the unique global minimum.

### 2.2.2 Repulsive Potential

The repulsive potential creates a "shield" around each obstacle. The classical Khatib formulation:

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 & \text{if } \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

where:
- `ρ(q)` = shortest distance from `q` to the obstacle
- `ρ₀` = **influence radius** of the obstacle (zero outside this radius)
- **η (eta)** = repulsive gain (positive scalar)

The corresponding **repulsive force** pushes the robot away from the obstacle:

$$F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \eta \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \dfrac{1}{\rho^2(q)} \nabla\rho(q) & \text{if } \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The force **diverges to infinity** as `ρ → 0`, guaranteeing collision avoidance (in theory) when the obstacle is treated as a point.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 1/ρ singularity** is the *guarantee* of collision avoidance for point obstacles. For extended (non-point) obstacles, the gradient `∇ρ` must be replaced by the gradient of distance to the closest point on the obstacle boundary.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table of All KTU-Exam-Relevant Equations for Potential Fields**

| # | Equation | Meaning / Role | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$ | Total potential (superposition) | J (Joules) |
| 2 | $\mathbf{F}(q) = -\nabla U(q)$ | Artificial force as negative gradient | N (Newtons) |
| 3 | $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\, \rho^2_{goal}$ | Quadratic attractive potential | J |
| 4 | $\mathbf{F}_{att}(q) = -\zeta\,(q - q_{goal})$ | Attractive force (linear in distance) | N |
| 5 | $U_{rep}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ | Repulsive potential (Khatib 1986) | J |
| 6 | $\mathbf{F}_{rep}(q) = \eta\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\tfrac{1}{\rho^2}\nabla\rho$ | Repulsive force vector | N |
| 7 | $\rho(q) = \min_{q' \in C_{obs}} \lVert q - q' \rVert$ | Distance function to obstacle | m |
| 8 | $\nabla\rho(q) = \tfrac{q - q_{obs}}{\lVert q - q_{obs} \rVert}$ | Unit vector toward obstacle | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\rho_0$ | Influence radius of obstacle | m |
| 10 | $\zeta, \eta$ | Attractive & repulsive gains (design params) | N/m |
| 11 | $\mathbf{v}_{cmd} = k \cdot \mathbf{F}(q)$, $\lVert \mathbf{v}_{cmd} \rVert \le v_{max}$ | Velocity command with saturation | m/s |
| 12 | $\mathbf{F}_{total} = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{F}_{rep,i}$ | Net force from N obstacles | N |
| 13 | $K(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}(q - q_{goal})^T K (q - q_{goal})$ | Generalized quadratic form (non-spherical goals) | J |

**Note on design trade-off:** If `η ≫ ζ`, the robot becomes overly cautious and may not reach the goal. If `ζ ≫ η`, the robot may collide with obstacles. A typical KTU/standard ratio is **η : ζ ≈ 1 : 1** to **5 : 1**, with `ρ₀` set to **1.5 × robot radius + safety margin**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Potential field methods are deployed in:
- **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots)** — Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics — for local obstacle avoidance in known map corridors.
- **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicles)** in factories — combined with B-spline smoothing.
- **Robotic surgery (da Vinci)** — real-time manipulator obstacle avoidance in cluttered anatomy.
- **Drone swarms** — decentralized collision avoidance with virtual potential fields between UAVs.
- **NASA Mars rovers** — supplementary reactive layer on top of global path planners.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Attractive Force from Quadratic Potential

We begin with the canonical attractive potential used in KTU textbooks:

$$U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\, \rho^2_{goal}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\,(q - q_{goal})^T (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 1 — Expand the squared distance term.**

We treat `q` as a 2D vector `q = (x, y)` and `q_goal = (x_g, y_g)`. The squared Euclidean distance expands as:

$$(q - q_{goal})^T (q - q_{goal}) = (x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the potential.**

$$U_{att}(x, y) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta \left[(x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Take partial derivatives (the gradient).**

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = \zeta\,(x - x_g)$$

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = \zeta\,(y - y_g)$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the gradient vector.**

$$\nabla U_{att} = \begin{bmatrix} \zeta\,(x - x_g) \\ \zeta\,(y - y_g) \end{bmatrix} = \zeta\,(q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 5 — Apply the negative gradient to obtain the force.**

$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -\zeta\,(q - q_{goal}) = \zeta\,(q_{goal} - q)$$

**Interpretation:** The force vector points from the robot's current configuration directly toward the goal, with magnitude `ζ·||q − q_goal||`. As the robot approaches the goal, the force shrinks linearly, ensuring a smooth arrival.

## 3.2 Derivation: Repulsive Force (Khatib 1986)

We start with the Khatib repulsive potential for a single obstacle:

$$U_{rep}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2, \quad \rho(q) \le \rho_0$$

**Step 1 — Define a substitution to simplify differentiation.**

Let `u = 1/ρ(q) − 1/ρ₀`. Then `U_rep = (η/2)·u²`.

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to ρ (chain rule).**

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial \rho} = \eta\, u \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial \rho}$$

Since `u = ρ⁻¹ − ρ₀⁻¹`, we have `∂u/∂ρ = −ρ⁻²`. Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial \rho} = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{\rho^2}\right) = -\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^2}$$

**Step 3 — Convert ρ-derivative to vector gradient using `∇U = (∂U/∂ρ)·∇ρ`.**

We know that for a point obstacle at `q_obs`, the distance gradient is the unit vector toward the obstacle:

$$\nabla \rho(q) = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\|q - q_{obs}\|}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the repulsive gradient.**

$$\nabla U_{rep} = -\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^2}\,\nabla \rho$$

**Step 5 — Apply the negative sign to obtain the repulsive force.**

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep} = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^2}\,\nabla \rho$$

**Step 6 — Examine limiting behaviour.**

- As `ρ → 0` (collision imminent): `(1/ρ − 1/ρ₀) → ∞` and `1/ρ² → ∞`, so `‖F_rep‖ → ∞` — guaranteed avoidance for point obstacles.
- As `ρ → ρ₀` (boundary of influence): `(1/ρ − 1/ρ₀) → 0`, so `F_rep → 0` — smooth handoff to free-space.
- As `ρ > ρ₀`: `U_rep = 0`, no influence.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A point robot is at `q = (2, 3)` m. The goal is at `q_goal = (10, 8)` m. An obstacle (treated as a point) is at `q_obs = (4, 4)` m. Given `ζ = 1 N/m`, `η = 2 N/m`, `ρ₀ = 3 m`. Compute the **net artificial force** on the robot.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Compute distance to goal.**

$$\rho_{goal} = \sqrt{(10-2)^2 + (8-3)^2} = \sqrt{64 + 25} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.43 \text{ m}$$

**Step B — Compute distance to obstacle.**

$$\rho_{obs} = \sqrt{(4-2)^2 + (4-3)^2} = \sqrt{4+1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24 \text{ m}$$

**Step C — Attractive force.**

$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = \zeta\,(q_{goal} - q) = 1 \cdot (8, 5) = (8, 5) \text{ N}$$

Magnitude: `‖F_att‖ = √(64+25) = √89 ≈ 9.43 N`. The direction is along the line from robot to goal.

**Step D — Repulsive force.**

Since `ρ_obs = 2.24 m < ρ₀ = 3 m`, the obstacle is in range.

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{obs}} = 0.447, \quad \frac{1}{\rho_0} = 0.333$$

$$\left(\frac{1}{\rho_{obs}} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) = 0.114$$

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{obs}^2} = 0.200$$

$$\text{Scalar coefficient} = \eta \cdot 0.114 \cdot 0.200 = 2 \cdot 0.0228 = 0.0456$$

**Unit vector toward obstacle:**

$$\nabla\rho = \frac{q_{obs} - q}{\|q_{obs} - q\|} = \frac{(2, 1)}{\sqrt{5}} = (0.894, 0.447)$$

**Repulsive force vector:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = 0.0456 \cdot (0.894, 0.447) \approx (0.0408, 0.0204) \text{ N}$$

**Step E — Net force.**

$$\mathbf{F}_{net} = \mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep} = (8.041, 5.020) \text{ N}$$

Magnitude: `‖F_net‖ ≈ 9.48 N`. The repulsive contribution is small (robot is far from obstacle relative to its influence) but it **deflects** the robot slightly away from the obstacle direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step F — Velocity command with saturation.** Assuming `k = 0.1 m/(s·N)` and `v_max = 1.5 m/s`:
>
> $$\mathbf{v}_{cmd} = 0.1 \cdot (8.041, 5.020) = (0.804, 0.502) \text{ m/s}$$
>
> Magnitude: `0.937 m/s < 1.5 m/s` ⇒ no saturation. The robot moves at `0.937 m/s` in the net force direction.

## 3.4 Local Minima Problem (Critical Failure Mode)

The fundamental limitation of APF is the **local minima problem**. A local minimum occurs at any configuration `q* ≠ q_goal` where:

$$\nabla U(q^*) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad U(q^*) > U(q_{goal})$$

**Common scenarios that create local minima:**

1. **Obstacle aligned with goal:** When the obstacle lies directly between the robot and goal, the repulsive force exactly cancels the attractive force before the goal is reached. Mathematically:
   $$\mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep} = 0 \quad \text{at } q^* \neq q_{goal}$$

2. **GNRON (Goal Non-Reachable with Obstacles Nearby):** If the goal is in a concave pocket of obstacle influence, the robot may stabilize at a configuration where it can neither reach the goal nor escape the obstacle's field.

3. **Narrow passages:** The opposing forces from two close walls can trap the robot at a saddle point that is not the goal.

## 3.5 Solutions to the Local Minima Problem

| Method | Mechanism | Pros / Cons |
|---|---|---|
| **Random walk** | When `‖F‖ < ε`, inject random motion | Simple; non-deterministic |
| **Virtual obstacles** | Add a small repulsive blob at the local min | Requires detection first |
| **Harmonic potential fields** (Connolly et al., 1990) | Solve Laplace's equation `∇²U = 0`; only the goal is a minimum | Mathematically elegant, no local minima; computationally heavy |
| **Navigation functions** (Rimon & Koditschek, 1992) | Carefully designed scalar function with goal as unique min | Strong theoretical guarantee; hard to construct in arbitrary maps |
| **Superposition + rotational fields** | Add a tangential force component near obstacles | Breaks local minima; can introduce oscillations |
| **Hybrid: APF + global planner** | Use A*/RRT for global plan, APF for local refinement | Industry standard; complementary strengths |
| **Wall-following** | Detect trap, then follow obstacle boundary | Heuristic; works in practice |

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners commonly ask:** "Compare Potential Fields with RRT/PRM." Here is the table to memorize:

| Criterion | Potential Fields | RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree) | PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Completeness** | Not complete (can get stuck) | Probabilistically complete | Probabilistically complete |
| **Optimality** | Locally optimal | Asymptotically optimal (RRT*) | Asymptotically optimal (PRM*) |
| **Computation** | Very fast, O(N) per step | Moderate, O(n log n) | Heavy preprocessing |
| **Memory** | Constant | Tree size grows with workspace | Roadmap graph stored |
| **Reactive use** | Excellent (real-time) | Poor (planning phase required) | Poor |
| **Local minima** | Yes — major weakness | No (random exploration avoids) | No |
| **Dynamic env.** | Excellent — recompute each tick | Needs replanning | Needs replanning |
| **Holonomic robots** | Works directly | Works | Works |
| **Non-holonomic** | Needs modification (steering functions) | Works | Works |

## 3.7 Mathematical Statement: Gradient Descent Convergence

Under the assumption that the potential `U` is **strictly convex** with a unique global minimum at `q_goal`, the discrete-time update rule:

$$q_{k+1} = q_k - \alpha_k \nabla U(q_k)$$

with a sufficiently small learning rate `α_k > 0` (Armijo / Lipschitz condition satisfied), converges to `q_goal` as `k → ∞`. However, for non-convex potentials (the realistic case with multiple obstacles), **convergence to a local minimum is not guaranteed to reach the goal** — hence the local minima problem.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Potential Field Control Loop

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a real-time potential field controller as it would be implemented on an embedded robot.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Layer: LiDAR / Sonar / Camera] --> B[Perception Module: Obstacle Map]
    B --> C[Configuration Estimator: q equals x, y, theta]
    C --> D[Goal Configuration: q_goal]
    D --> E[Attractive Field Module: U_att]
    B --> F[Repulsive Field Module: U_rep for each obstacle i]
    E --> G[Total Potential: U equals U_att plus sum of U_rep]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Gradient Computation: F equals minus gradient of U]
    H --> I[Velocity Saturation: v equals k times F, bounded by v_max]
    I --> J[Robot Actuator: Wheel Motors]
    J --> K[New Configuration q_new at next timestep]
    K --> C
    H --> L{Local Minimum Detected: gradient magnitude less than epsilon}
    L --> M[Escape Strategy Module: Random Walk or Wall Follow]
    M --> C
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Potential Field Topology (Conceptual)

The following diagram shows a **functional / topological** view of how the potential field is structured. It does not depict a physical 2D map but rather the **energy surface abstraction** the controller operates on.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Goal Configuration: Global Minimum] --> nodeB[Attractive Bowl: Quadratic Well]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Robot Position: Gradient Descent Trajectory]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Obstacle Influence Zone: Repulsive Hill]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Free Space: Zero Potential Plateau]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Total Field: Superposition of Bowls and Hills]
    nodeF --> nodeA
```

## 4.3 State Machine — Local Minima Escape Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SearchPhase
    SearchPhase --> GradientDescent: Normal Operation
    GradientDescent --> SearchPhase: Gradient magnitude greater than epsilon
    GradientDescent --> TrapDetected: Gradient magnitude less than epsilon for T consecutive steps
    TrapDetected --> RandomWalk: Inject Stochastic Motion
    RandomWalk --> GradientDescent: Gradient magnitude greater than epsilon
    TrapDetected --> WallFollowing: Random walk failed for N attempts
    WallFollowing --> GradientDescent: Boundary cleared
    GradientDescent --> GoalReached: q equals q_goal
    GoalReached --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Functional Decomposition of the Attractive + Repulsive Force Superposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AttractiveComponent
        A1[Goal q_goal] --> A2[Distance rho_goal]
        A2 --> A3[Quadratic Well U_att]
        A3 --> A4[Force F_att: Points Toward Goal]
    end
    subgraph RepulsiveComponent
        B1[Obstacle q_obs] --> B2[Distance rho]
        B2 --> B3{rho less than rho_0?}
        B3 --> B4[Repulsive Hill U_rep]:::active
        B3 --> B5[Zero Influence]:::inactive
        B4 --> B6[Force F_rep: Points Away from Obstacle]
        B5 --> B6
    end
    A4 --> C1[Vector Sum: F_total]
    B6 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Velocity Command v_cmd]
    classDef active fill:#ffd166,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    classDef inactive fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#666,color:#333
```

**Reading guide for the diagrams above:**
- The first diagram (control loop) shows the **real-time data flow** in a deployed APF system.
- The second diagram (topology) illustrates that the controller is **not searching a graph** — it is **rolling a particle down a continuous energy surface**.
- The state machine captures the **escape logic** that addresses the local minima failure mode.
- The functional decomposition makes the **superposition principle** explicit and shows that the influence radius `ρ₀` is the **switch** between active repulsion and inactive regions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define the Artificial Potential Field (APF) method for mobile robot navigation. State its main advantage and main disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Artificial Potential Field method** is a reactive local path planning technique in which the robot's configuration space is modelled as a scalar potential energy field. The goal acts as an attractive potential minimum, and obstacles generate repulsive potential peaks. The robot moves along the negative gradient of the total potential, effectively performing gradient descent on the energy landscape. The **main advantage** is real-time computational efficiency (per-cycle cost is linear in the number of sensed obstacles), making it ideal for dynamic environments. The **main disadvantage** is the **local minima problem** — the robot can become trapped at configurations where the net force is zero but the goal has not been reached. (3 marks: 1 definition + 1 advantage + 1 disadvantage)

---

**Q2. Write the mathematical expression for the repulsive potential in the Khatib (1986) formulation and explain the role of the influence radius `ρ₀`.**

**Model Answer:**

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0 & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

The **influence radius `ρ₀`** defines the spatial extent of an obstacle's repulsive effect. For configurations with `ρ > ρ₀`, the obstacle exerts no influence (`U_rep = 0`). For `ρ ≤ ρ₀`, the potential grows as the robot approaches the obstacle, reaching a singularity as `ρ → 0`. This parameter bounds the computational cost of obstacle evaluation — only obstacles within `ρ₀` of the robot need to be considered. (3 marks: 1 equation + 2 explanation)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 3 — Question Paper Style (Internal Choice Between A and B)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With neat mathematical expressions, explain the **Attractive** and **Repulsive** potential field components used in the Artificial Potential Field method. Clearly define each variable and discuss the physical interpretation of the resulting force vector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A point robot is at position `q = (1, 2)` m. The goal is at `q_goal = (9, 7)` m. A single circular obstacle of influence radius `ρ₀ = 2 m` is centred at `q_obs = (3, 3)` m. Use gains `ζ = 1.0 N/m` and `η = 5.0 N/m`. Calculate the net artificial force on the robot and the corresponding velocity command if `k = 0.1 (m/s)/N` and `v_max = 2.0 m/s`. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The **attractive potential** models the goal as a quadratic well:

$$U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\, \|q - q_{goal}\|^2$$

- **ζ** = positive attractive gain (N/m)
- `||q − q_goal||` = Euclidean distance from robot to goal

The **attractive force** is the negative gradient:

$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -\zeta\,(q - q_{goal}) = \zeta\,(q_{goal} - q)$$

This force always points from the robot toward the goal with magnitude proportional to the distance — physically analogous to a linear spring anchored at the goal.

The **repulsive potential** models each obstacle as an inverse-distance barrier:

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0 & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

- **η** = positive repulsive gain (N/m)
- `ρ(q)` = distance from robot to nearest point on the obstacle
- `ρ₀` = influence radius beyond which the obstacle has no effect

The **repulsive force** is:

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep} = \eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\dfrac{1}{\rho^2}\,\nabla \rho$$

This force points away from the obstacle and grows without bound as `ρ → 0`, guaranteeing collision avoidance for point obstacles.

**Total field** by superposition: `U(q) = U_att(q) + U_rep(q)` and the net force: `F = −∇U(q)`. (7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1)

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Distance to goal.**

$$\rho_{goal} = \sqrt{(9-1)^2 + (7-2)^2} = \sqrt{64+25} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.43 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Attractive force.**

$$\mathbf{F}_{att} = \zeta\,(q_{goal} - q) = 1.0 \cdot (8, 5) = (8, 5) \text{ N}$$

Magnitude: `9.43 N`.

**Step 3 — Distance to obstacle.**

$$\rho_{obs} = \sqrt{(3-1)^2 + (3-2)^2} = \sqrt{4+1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ m}$$

Since `2.236 > ρ₀ = 2`? **Yes, 2.236 > 2.0**, so `ρ_obs > ρ₀`.

Therefore **the obstacle is outside the influence radius**, and `F_rep = 0`. The robot experiences only the attractive force.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (1 mark lost):** Students often compute `F_rep` without first checking the condition `ρ ≤ ρ₀`. Always verify the influence condition **before** applying the repulsive formula. If `ρ > ρ₀`, the repulsive contribution is exactly zero — the robot moves in a straight line to the goal.

**Step 4 — Net force.**

$$\mathbf{F}_{net} = (8, 5) + (0, 0) = (8, 5) \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Velocity command with saturation.**

$$\mathbf{v}_{cmd} = 0.1 \cdot (8, 5) = (0.8, 0.5) \text{ m/s}$$

Magnitude: `√(0.64 + 0.25) = √0.89 ≈ 0.943 m/s`. Since `0.943 < v_max = 2.0 m/s`, **no saturation occurs**.

**Final answer:** `F_net = (8, 5) N`, `v_cmd = (0.8, 0.5) m/s`. (7 marks: 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Alternative]**

**(a)** Discuss the **local minima problem** in Artificial Potential Field methods. Illustrate with a diagram the scenario where a robot gets trapped between a goal and an obstacle. Suggest **two** methods to overcome this limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method with the **Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT)** planner along the following axes: (i) completeness, (ii) optimality, (iii) computational cost per planning cycle, (iv) suitability for dynamic environments, and (v) handling of local minima. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

The **local minima problem** is the principal failure mode of APF methods. A local minimum is a configuration `q* ≠ q_goal` where the net artificial force vanishes:

$$\mathbf{F}(q^*) = -\nabla U(q^*) = 0$$

**Classic trap scenario:** When an obstacle lies *directly between* the robot and the goal, the repulsive force from the obstacle (pointing away from the obstacle, i.e., back toward the robot) exactly cancels the attractive force (pointing from the robot toward the goal, i.e., forward). At the equilibrium point, the robot halts because the net commanded velocity is zero — but the goal is still unreachable.

```
    [Goal]  ←— F_attractive —←  [Robot stuck at q*]  —→ F_repulsive —→  [Obstacle]
```

(Visual: Robot is trapped at a point where F_att and F_rep cancel each other, and the goal lies beyond the obstacle.)

**Two methods to overcome local minima:**

1. **Random Walk / Brownian Motion Escape:** When the gradient magnitude falls below a threshold `ε` for a sustained duration, the controller injects a small random velocity command. This perturbs the robot out of the equilibrium, after which gradient descent resumes. Simple, but non-deterministic.

2. **Harmonic Potential Fields (Connolly, 1990):** Replace the Khatib potential with a solution to Laplace's equation `∇²U = 0` with the goal as a Dirichlet boundary condition. The only minimum of a harmonic function on a bounded domain with one boundary minimum is the goal itself — guaranteeing no spurious local minima. Computationally expensive (requires numerical PDE solver).

(Mention of **Navigation Functions** by Rimon-Koditschek or **wall-following** are also valid alternatives.) (7 marks: 2 problem description + 1 diagram description + 2 × 2 marks for two solutions)

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

| Axis | Artificial Potential Field (APF) | Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Completeness** | **Not complete** — can fail in local minima. | **Probabilistically complete** — probability of finding a solution (if one exists) approaches 1 as iterations → ∞. |
| **(ii) Optimality** | Locally optimal (gradient descent reaches nearest local min). | Asymptotically optimal only in **RRT\*** variant; vanilla RRT is not optimal. |
| **(iii) Computational cost per cycle** | **O(N)** where N = number of obstacles in influence radius. Extremely cheap, real-time. | Moderate; each iteration involves collision checks, nearest-neighbour search O(log n). Not real-time without precomputation. |
| **(iv) Suitability for dynamic env.** | **Excellent** — recompute forces every sensor cycle; adapts instantly. | **Poor** — requires replanning from scratch when environment changes. |
| **(v) Local minima handling** | **Vulnerable** — primary failure mode. | **Robust** — random sampling explores the space, escaping traps naturally. |

(7 marks: 5 axes × 1.4 marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **For APF questions:** Students often **forget the influence radius condition** `ρ ≤ ρ₀` when computing repulsive forces. Always check this first; if `ρ > ρ₀`, set `F_rep = 0` immediately. (1–2 marks lost)
> - **For local minima questions:** Do not just name the problem — **draw the trap scenario** (robot–obstacle–goal collinear) and **show the force cancellation** mathematically: `F_att + F_rep = 0`. Verbal-only answers receive partial credit at best.
> - **For comparison questions:** Use a **table format** with explicit axis names. Bullet-only or paragraph answers lose marks for missing structure.
> - **For gradient derivation:** Show the chain rule expansion `∂U/∂ρ · ∂ρ/∂x` explicitly. Skipping the chain rule step costs 1 mark.
> - **Common conceptual error:** Confusing **navigation function** (Rimon-Koditschek) with **harmonic potential** (Connolly). They are different solutions — harmonic solves Laplace's equation; navigation function is a specific analytic construction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Potential Fields**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ APF models C-space as a **scalar potential landscape**; goal is a minimum, obstacles are repulsive peaks.
- ☐ **Superposition principle**: `U(q) = U_att(q) + U_rep(q)`.
- ☐ **Force as negative gradient**: `F(q) = −∇U(q)`.
- ☐ Robot executes **gradient descent** on the potential surface.
- ☐ Method is **reactive and real-time** — no explicit path search.

### Must-Memorize Equations
- ☐ `U_att = ½·ζ·ρ²_goal`, `F_att = −ζ·(q − q_goal)`
- ☐ `U_rep = ½·η·(1/ρ − 1/ρ₀)²` for `ρ ≤ ρ₀`; else `0`.
- ☐ `F_rep = η·(1/ρ − 1/ρ₀)·(1/ρ²)·∇ρ`
- ☐ Influence condition: `ρ ≤ ρ₀` is the **switch**.

### Failure Modes
- ☐ **Local minima** — robot halts at non-goal equilibrium.
- ☐ **GNRON** — goal non-reachable with obstacles nearby.
- ☐ **Oscillations** in narrow corridors.
- ☐ **No passage between closely spaced obstacles.**

### Design Parameters
- ☐ `ζ` = attractive gain (linear force growth).
- ☐ `η` = repulsive gain (controls obstacle "aggressiveness").
- ☐ `ρ₀` = influence radius (bounds computational cost).
- ☐ Typical ratio: `η : ζ ≈ 1:1` to `5:1`; `ρ₀ ≈ 1.5 × robot radius + safety`.

### Mitigations
- ☐ Random walk, virtual obstacles, wall-following, harmonic potentials, navigation functions, **hybrid with global planner** (industry standard).

### Contrast with Sampling-Based Methods (Module 3 Comparison)
- ☐ APF: **local, reactive, fast, prone to local minima**.
- ☐ RRT/PRM: **global, slower, probabilistically complete, no local minima**.
- ☐ KTU answer template: **"APF is suitable for real-time local avoidance; RRT/PRM for global planning in complex maps."**

### Examination Strategy
- ☐ Always state **units** of gains and forces.
- ☐ Always **check the influence condition** before computing `F_rep`.
- ☐ Always **show the chain rule** in gradient derivations.
- ☐ Always **draw a diagram** for local minima scenarios.
- ☐ Use **markdown tables** for comparison answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector Field Histogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vector Field Histogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** is a real-time, local obstacle-avoidance algorithm for mobile robots, originally proposed by **Borenstein and Koren (1991)**. It is a refinement of the earlier **Virtual Force Field (VFF)** method and operates by compressing a two-dimensional Cartesian **certainty (occupancy) grid** of the robot's surroundings into a one-dimensional **polar histogram**. This histogram is divided into angular sectors, where each sector's magnitude represents the **polar obstacle density** — essentially the cumulative effect of obstacle proximity along that bearing. The robot then selects a steering direction by identifying "candidate valleys" in this histogram that are closest to the target heading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** VFH is classified under **Local Path Planning / Reactive Obstacle Avoidance** in Module 3 of the Mobile Robotics syllabus. It is a landmark algorithm bridging classical potential fields and modern sampling-based planners. Expect 14-mark analytical questions on its architecture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded in a cluttered room** and given a compass pointing toward a doorway (the goal). You have a cane that can sense obstacles within arm's reach.

- **Step 1 (Certainty Grid):** Every time your cane touches something, you mentally mark that point on a 2D paper map.
- **Step 2 (Polar Histogram):** Instead of reading the whole 2D map, you imagine drawing rays outward from your current position. You count "how much stuff" lies along each ray direction and assign it a "crowding score."
- **Step 3 (Windowing):** You ignore the far-away clutter and only consider obstacles within an **active window** around you.
- **Step 4 (Thresholding):** You mark all directions whose crowding score is below a "safe threshold" as **candidate valleys**.
- **Step 5 (Steering):** You walk through the **valley closest to the doorway**, because that's the path of least resistance that still gets you toward your goal.

This is exactly how a VFH-controlled robot navigates — it trades a detailed 2D map for a compact 1D angular representation, allowing **real-time control at high speeds** with minimal computational overhead.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Certainty Grid** | 2D Cartesian map of cells, each holding a numerical certainty value (0 = free, 1 = occupied) |
| **Active Window** | A rectangular sub-region of the certainty grid centered on the robot |
| **Cell Weighting Function** | Function that scales obstacle influence based on distance from the robot |
| **Polar Histogram** | 1D array of $n$ angular sectors with magnitudes $h_k$ |
| **Threshold $\tau$** | Minimum allowed polar obstacle density for a sector to be "safe" |
| **Candidate Valley** | A contiguous run of safe sectors near the target direction |
| **Steering Direction $\theta_{steer}$** | Final selected heading for the next control cycle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (Borenstein & Koren 1991):** Default sector count $n = 72$ (each sector = $5°$), active window size typically $= 61 \times 61$ cells, and default threshold $\tau$ chosen experimentally based on robot radius and safety margin.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage VFH Pipeline

The VFH algorithm executes in a **strictly sequential three-stage pipeline** at every control tick:

1. **Stage 1 — Grid Update & Windowing:** Update cell certainty values from sensor readings (e.g., sonar, lidar) and crop the certainty grid to an **active window** of size $(2W+1) \times (2W+1)$ centered on the robot.

2. **Stage 2 — Polar Histogram Construction:** Project every occupied cell within the window onto angular sectors using a **cell weighting function** $m_{i,j}$, then accumulate sector densities.

3. **Stage 3 — Steering Decision:** Apply threshold $\tau$ to find candidate valleys, and select the steering direction based on the goal heading and robot dynamics.

### 2.2 Cell Weighting Function (Why & How)

The cell weighting function $m_{i,j}$ models the **inverse relationship** between obstacle influence and distance. Closer cells must exert far greater influence than distant cells, otherwise the robot would treat a faraway wall the same as an adjacent obstacle — which is dynamically unsafe.

A common, smooth choice is:

$$m_{i,j} = \left(c^{\star}\right)^{2} \left( a - b \cdot d_{i,j} \right)$$

where $c^{\star}$, $a$, and $b$ are positive constants, and $d_{i,j}$ is the Euclidean distance from cell $(i,j)$ to the robot's center. The constants are tuned so that $m_{i,j}$ is large near the robot and small far away. The squared term $c^{\star}$ sharpens the response.

> [!NOTE]
> The original Borenstein formulation uses $c^{\star} = c_{i,j}$ as a *certainty scaling factor* that incorporates the maturity (age) of the cell evidence, but in KTU exam settings it is treated as a constant near 1 for mature cells.

### 2.3 Polar Histogram Magnitude Formula

Each sector $k$ (with $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$) accumulates obstacle density from all cells $(i,j)$ whose angular bearing from the robot lies within that sector:

$$h_k = \sum_{i,j} m_{i,j} \cdot \mathbb{1}\!\left[ \beta_{i,j} \in \text{sector } k \right]$$

where $\beta_{i,j}$ is the angular bearing of cell $(i,j)$ from the robot, and $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function (1 if true, 0 otherwise).

In practice, the mapping from cell to sector is:

$$k = \mathrm{int}\!\left( \frac{\beta_{i,j}}{n^{-1} \cdot 360°} \right)$$

### 2.4 Thresholding & Candidate Valley Detection

For each sector $k$, define a binary **safety mask**:

$$c_k = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } h_k < \tau \quad (\text{sector } k \text{ is safe}) \\ 0 & \text{if } h_k \geq \tau \quad (\text{sector } k \text{ is blocked}) \end{cases}$$

A **candidate valley** is a maximal contiguous run of consecutive safe sectors $\{k_s, k_s+1, \dots, k_e\}$ in the binary mask $c$.

The valley that is **closest in angular distance to the goal direction** $\theta_{goal}$ is selected. The valley's center or its sector closest to the goal is chosen as the steering direction $\theta_{steer}$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_{i,j} = \sqrt{(i - i_0)^2 + (j - j_0)^2}$ | Euclidean distance from cell to robot center | cells |
| 2 | $\beta_{i,j} = \mathrm{atan2}(j - j_0, \; i - i_0)$ | Angular bearing of cell | radians / degrees |
| 3 | $m_{i,j} = (c^{\star})^{2}(a - b \cdot d_{i,j})$ | Cell weighting function | dimensionless |
| 4 | $h_k = \sum_{i,j} m_{i,j} \cdot \mathbb{1}[\beta_{i,j} \in \text{sector } k]$ | Polar histogram sector magnitude | dimensionless |
| 5 | $c_k = \mathbb{1}[h_k < \tau]$ | Sector safety mask | $\in \{0,1\}$ |
| 6 | $k = \mathrm{int}(\beta_{i,j} \cdot n / 360°)$ | Cell-to-sector mapping | integer index |
| 7 | $\theta_{steer} = \arg\min_{\theta_v} \vert \theta_v - \theta_{goal} \vert$ | Steering direction (valley closest to goal) | degrees |
| 8 | $n = 72$ (default) | Number of angular sectors | count |
| 9 | $W$ (active window half-width) | Defaults vary (e.g., 5 m, 30 cells) | meters / cells |
| 10 | $\tau$ (smoothing threshold) | Tunable; higher = more conservative | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Reminder:** In KTU answer sheets, **always state the units** of the threshold and histogram values (dimensionless) and clearly justify the choice of $n = 72$ as a balance between angular resolution and computational cost.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** VFH-style reactive control is the de-facto standard for fast-moving logistics robots (e.g., Amazon Kiva / Proteus systems) where sub-50 ms control loops are mandatory.
- **Cleaning Robots:** Domestic vacuum robots (iRobot, Xiaomi) use VFH derivatives for room traversal.
- **Disaster-Response UGVs:** Real-time obstacle avoidance in unstructured rubble, where global maps are unreliable.
- **Service Robots in Hospitals:** Smooth, predictable motion around humans in narrow corridors.

### 2.7 Limitations of Vanilla VFH (Sets up VFH+ and VFH*)

- Cannot handle **narrow passages** smaller than the robot's turning radius (no kinematic awareness).
- Suffers from **local minima** — can get stuck in symmetric dead-ends.
- **Threshold sensitivity:** Poor $\tau$ selection causes oscillation or unsafe behavior.
- The **steering decision** is purely reactive — no look-ahead to detect dead-ends that emerge a few meters ahead.

> These limitations motivated **VFH+** (Ullrich & Akella, 1995, with robot kinematics) and **VFH\*** (Ulrich & Borenstein, 1998, with A\*-based look-ahead verification).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation: From 2D Grid to 1D Polar Histogram

The objective of this derivation is to rigorously show how a 2D certainty grid is *deterministically* reduced to a 1D polar histogram through angular binning and weighted accumulation.

**Step 1 — Define the certainty grid.** Let the local occupancy map be a 2D array of cells $\mathcal{G}$ indexed by $(i,j)$ where $i,j \in \mathbb{Z}$. Each cell holds a certainty value $\mathrm{CC}_{i,j} \in [0, 1]$, and the robot occupies cell $(i_0, j_0)$ at the center of the active window.

$$
\mathcal{G} = \{ (i,j, \mathrm{CC}_{i,j}) : |i - i_0| \le W,\; |j - j_0| \le W \}
$$

The active window size is $(2W+1)^2$ cells. The parameter $W$ defines the *look-ahead radius* of the robot in cell units.

**Step 2 — Compute angular bearing of each cell.** For any occupied cell $(i,j)$ with $\mathrm{CC}_{i,j} > 0$, its bearing from the robot is:

$$
\beta_{i,j} = \arctan2\!\left( j - j_0,\; i - i_0 \right) \quad \text{(returns } \beta \in (-180°, 180°]\text{)}
$$

Here we use the standard 2-argument arctangent to preserve quadrant information; a naive $\arctan$ would lose sign and quadrant.

**Step 3 — Apply the cell weighting function.** The contribution of cell $(i,j)$ to the obstacle field is:

$$
m_{i,j} = (c^{\star}_{i,j})^{2} \left( a - b \cdot d_{i,j} \right), \quad d_{i,j} = \sqrt{(i-i_0)^{2} + (j-j_0)^{2}}
$$

The constants satisfy $a, b > 0$ and are tuned so that $m_{i,j} > 0$ for cells within the active window and $m_{i,j} \to 0$ at the window edge:

$$
m_{i,j} > 0 \iff d_{i,j} < \frac{a}{b}
$$

Hence the effective sensing range is $d_{\max} = a/b$. We choose $W \cdot \Delta x < d_{\max}$ where $\Delta x$ is the cell size in meters.

**Step 4 — Discretize the bearing into sector index.** With $n$ sectors spanning $[0°, 360°)$, sector width is $\alpha = 360°/n$. For $n = 72$, $\alpha = 5°$. The sector index is:

$$
k_{i,j} = \mathrm{int}\!\left( \frac{\beta_{i,j}}{\alpha} \right) \mod n
$$

**Step 5 — Accumulate the polar histogram.** For each sector $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n-1\}$:

$$
h_k = \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{G}} m_{i,j} \cdot \mathbb{1}\!\left[ k_{i,j} = k \right] \cdot \mathbb{1}\!\left[ \mathrm{CC}_{i,j} > 0 \right]
$$

This is the **master equation** of VFH. It says: *for every sector, sum the weighted contributions of all occupied cells whose bearing falls within that sector*.

**Step 6 — Smooth the histogram (optional but recommended).** To suppress single-cell noise and avoid jitter, a 1D convolution with a smoothing kernel of size $l$ is applied:

$$
h_k^{smooth} = \frac{1}{2l+1} \sum_{j=-l}^{l} h_{k+j}
$$

This rolling mean eliminates the "spiky" behavior of raw histograms caused by isolated sensor returns.

**Step 7 — Apply binary threshold.** Define safety mask:

$$
c_k = \begin{cases} 1, & h_k^{smooth} < \tau \\ 0, & h_k^{smooth} \ge \tau \end{cases}
$$

**Step 8 — Identify candidate valleys.** A candidate valley is a maximal contiguous block of safe sectors:

$$
V = \{ k_s, k_s+1, k_s+2, \dots, k_e \} \quad \text{with } c_{k_s-1} = c_{k_e+1} = 0
$$

**Step 9 — Select the best valley.** Among all candidate valleys, choose the one whose angular midpoint is closest to $\theta_{goal}$:

$$
\theta_{steer} = \frac{(k_s + k_e) \cdot \alpha}{2}, \quad \text{from } \arg\min_{V} \left\vert \frac{(k_s + k_e)}{2} \cdot \alpha - \theta_{goal} \right\vert
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Intuition:** A *narrow* valley (small $k_e - k_s$) implies a tight corridor the robot should align precisely with. A *wide* valley (large $k_e - k_s$) gives the robot freedom to choose any sector inside it; VFH typically picks the sector nearest to $\theta_{goal}$ for shortest path progress.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: How VFH Reacts to Scenarios

| Scenario | Histogram Shape | Valley Selection | Robot Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open space, target ahead | Single wide low-amplitude hill opposite to target | One wide valley at $\theta_{goal}$ | Drives straight to goal |
| Wall directly ahead | Tall peak at $\beta \approx 0°$ | Two valleys at $\beta \approx \pm 90°$ | Turns left or right, whichever is nearer to $\theta_{goal}$ |
| Single obstacle to the left | Peak at $\beta \approx -45°$ | Valley spans roughly $0°$ to $90°$ | Curves right around the obstacle |
| U-shaped trap | Two peaks symmetric about $\theta_{goal}$ | Wide valley *behind* the goal direction | Moves backward (or oscillates — VFH limitation!) |
| Narrow corridor | Two tall peaks, narrow valley between | One narrow valley aligned with corridor axis | Funnels through the corridor |
| Goal in a dense cluster | Multiple peaks with no clear valley pointing at goal | Best-available valley (not at $\theta_{goal}$) | Deviates from direct path but maintains safety |

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

Suppose a robot has certainty grid with **4 occupied cells** at relative positions (in meters) and certainty $c^{\star} = 1$:

| Cell | $(i - i_0,\; j - j_0)$ (m) | $d_{i,j}$ (m) | $\beta_{i,j}$ (deg) | $\mathrm{CC}_{i,j}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | $(1.0,\; 0.0)$ | 1.0 | $0°$ | 0.9 |
| B | $(0.5,\; 0.5)$ | 0.707 | $45°$ | 0.8 |
| C | $(0.0,\; 1.0)$ | 1.0 | $90°$ | 0.95 |
| D | $(-1.0,\; 0.0)$ | 1.0 | $180°$ | 0.7 |

**Parameters:** $a = 5$, $b = 4$, $n = 72$ (sector width $\alpha = 5°$).

**Step 1 — Compute $m_{i,j}$:**

$$
m_{i,j} = (1)^{2}(5 - 4 \cdot d_{i,j}) = 5 - 4 d_{i,j}
$$

- $m_A = 5 - 4(1.0) = 1.0$
- $m_B = 5 - 4(0.707) = 5 - 2.828 = 2.172$
- $m_C = 5 - 4(1.0) = 1.0$
- $m_D = 5 - 4(1.0) = 1.0$

**Step 2 — Determine sector indices $k_{i,j}$:**

$$
k_{i,j} = \mathrm{int}(\beta_{i,j} / 5°) \mod 72
$$

- A: $k_A = \mathrm{int}(0/5) = 0$
- B: $k_B = \mathrm{int}(45/5) = 9$
- C: $k_C = \mathrm{int}(90/5) = 18$
- D: $k_D = \mathrm{int}(180/5) = 36$

**Step 3 — Build the polar histogram $h_k$:**

$$
h_0 = m_A = 1.0
$$

$$
h_9 = m_B = 2.172
$$

$$
h_{18} = m_C = 1.0
$$

$$
h_{36} = m_D = 1.0
$$

All other $h_k = 0$.

**Step 4 — Smooth with $l = 1$ (rolling window of 3):**

$$
h_0^{smooth} = (h_{71} + h_0 + h_1)/3 = (0 + 1.0 + 0)/3 = 0.333
$$

$$
h_9^{smooth} = (h_8 + h_9 + h_{10})/3 = (0 + 2.172 + 0)/3 = 0.724
$$

$$
h_{18}^{smooth} = (h_{17} + h_{18} + h_{19})/3 = 0.333
$$

$$
h_{36}^{smooth} = (h_{35} + h_{36} + h_{37})/3 = 0.333
$$

**Step 5 — Apply threshold $\tau = 1.0$:** All four peaks are below $\tau$, so all four sectors are safe. But these are *isolated* peaks, and surrounding sectors are deeply safe. The **dominant safe arc** is the wide valley from sector $k = 0$ through $k = 72$ (wrapping around), excluding the few peak regions.

**Step 6 — Goal selection:** Suppose the goal is at $\theta_{goal} = 45°$, which corresponds to sector $k = 9$. The peak at $k = 9$ is the *closest* sector to the goal. Because $h_9 < \tau$, the sector is safe. The robot selects:

$$
\theta_{steer} = 9 \times 5° = 45° = \theta_{goal}
$$

Hence, **the robot drives directly toward the goal**, treating the local peaks as navigable because they are below the safety threshold. If $\tau$ were reduced to $0.5$, sector $k = 9$ would become blocked (since $h_9^{smooth} = 0.724 > 0.5$), and the robot would divert to the nearest safe sector (say, $k = 0$ or $k = 18$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away:** The threshold $\tau$ is the **single most influential tuning parameter** in VFH. Low $\tau$ = aggressive (robot hugs obstacles); high $\tau$ = conservative (robot avoids even distant clutter). KTU exams frequently test this trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete functional flow of the VFH algorithm, from sensor ingestion to actuator command.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sonar / Lidar / IR Sensors] --> B[Certainty Grid Map Update]
    B --> C[Active Window Extraction\nHalf-width W centered on robot]
    C --> D[Compute Cell Distances\nd_ij and Bearings beta_ij]
    D --> E[Apply Cell Weighting Function\nm_ij = c-star squared times a minus b d_ij]
    E --> F[Angular Binning into n Sectors\nk_ij = int beta_ij over alpha]
    F --> G[Polar Histogram Accumulation\nh_k = sum of m_ij per sector]
    G --> H[1D Smoothing Filter\nRolling mean over 2l plus 1 sectors]
    H --> I[Binary Thresholding\nc_k = 1 if h_k less than tau]
    I --> J[Candidate Valley Detection\nMaximal runs of c_k = 1]
    J --> K{Goal Direction in any Valley?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Select Valley Nearest to theta_goal]
    K -- No --> M[Select Best-Available Valley\nMinimize angular distance to goal]
    L --> N[Compute Steering Direction\ntheta_steer = valley midpoint or edge]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Low-Level Motor Controller\nDifferential drive / Ackermann]
    O --> P[Robot Motion]
    P --> A
```

### 4.2 Data-Flow View: 2D Grid to 1D Histogram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[2D Certainty Grid\nsize 2W+1 by 2W+1] --> W[Active Windowing\nCrop to local region]
    W --> B[Bearing Computation\nbeta_ij = atan2 of delta j, delta i]
    B --> M[Weighting\nm_ij function of distance]
    M --> A[Sector Binning\nk index for each cell]
    A --> H[1D Polar Histogram\narray of n sector magnitudes]
    H --> S[Smoothing Kernel\n1D convolution]
    S --> T[Threshold tau applied]
    T --> V[Valley Set\nlist of safe angular runs]
    V --> D[Steering Decision\ntheta_steer output]
```

### 4.3 State Machine of the Steering Decision

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ReadSensors
    ReadSensors --> UpdateGrid
    UpdateGrid --> BuildHistogram
    BuildHistogram --> SmoothHistogram
    SmoothHistogram --> ApplyThreshold
    ApplyThreshold --> DetectValleys
    DetectValleys --> GoalInValley
    GoalInValley --> SteerToGoal : Yes
    GoalInValley --> SteerToBest : No
    SteerToGoal --> SendCommand
    SteerToBest --> SendCommand
    SendCommand --> ReadSensors
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The Mermaid diagrams use alphanumeric node identifiers (e.g., `A`, `B`, `W`) and **plain uppercase text inside quoted labels** — no bold/italic tags — to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards.

### 4.4 Schematic of the Polar Histogram Geometry

A polar histogram is a 1D function $h(\beta)$ plotted in polar form, where the radius at angle $\beta$ equals the obstacle density. The figure below depicts the conceptual geometry — three obstacle cells in the certainty grid and their accumulated sector magnitudes.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Grid[2D Certainty Grid View]
        O[Robot at center] --- X1[Cell at 0 deg, 1 m, weight 1.0]
        O --- X2[Cell at 45 deg, 0.7 m, weight 2.17]
        O --- X3[Cell at 90 deg, 1 m, weight 1.0]
        O --- X4[Cell at 180 deg, 1 m, weight 1.0]
    end
    subgraph Hist[1D Polar Histogram View]
        H0[Bar h_0 = 1.0 at 0 deg]
        H9[Bar h_9 = 2.17 at 45 deg]
        H18[Bar h_18 = 1.0 at 90 deg]
        H36[Bar h_36 = 1.0 at 180 deg]
    end
    X1 --> H0
    X2 --> H9
    X3 --> H18
    X4 --> H36
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
Define the **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** algorithm. State the role of the **active window** and the **polar histogram threshold $\tau$** in determining the robot's steering direction.

**Model Answer:**

The **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** is a real-time, reactive obstacle-avoidance method introduced by **Borenstein and Koren (1991)**. It compresses a 2D Cartesian **certainty (occupancy) grid** into a 1D **polar histogram** divided into $n$ angular sectors (default $n = 72$, sector width $= 5°$).

- The **active window** is a rectangular sub-region of size $(2W+1) \times (2W+1)$ centered on the robot. It restricts obstacle consideration to a bounded local area, ensuring real-time performance and making the algorithm robust to distant, irrelevant clutter.
- The **threshold $\tau$** is applied to the smoothed polar histogram magnitudes $h_k^{smooth}$: sectors with $h_k^{smooth} \ge \tau$ are declared **blocked**, while those with $h_k^{smooth} < \tau$ are **safe**. The robot selects the safe valley (contiguous run of safe sectors) whose angular midpoint is closest to the goal heading $\theta_{goal}$. The threshold thus directly controls the trade-off between **aggressiveness** (low $\tau$, robot hugs obstacles) and **conservativeness** (high $\tau$, robot detours around distant clutter). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
Distinguish between the **Virtual Force Field (VFF)** and **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** methods of obstacle avoidance. Why does VFH generally outperform VFF in cluttered environments?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | VFF | VFH |
|---|---|---|
| Representation | Continuous 2D vector field | 1D polar histogram (sector-wise) |
| Data structure | Per-cell force vectors | Per-sector scalar magnitudes $h_k$ |
| Computation | O(N²) per cycle | O(N) per cycle after binning |
| Susceptibility to local minima | High (force vectors cancel) | Lower (valley selection is robust) |
| Real-time performance | Slower for large grids | Faster; suitable for high-speed robots |

VFH outperforms VFF in cluttered environments because VFF suffers from **force cancellation** when obstacles are arranged symmetrically, trapping the robot in local minima. VFH, in contrast, builds a *scalar* polar histogram, eliminating vector-cancellation issues and enabling faster, more predictable steering decisions. Additionally, the **windowing** and **thresholding** stages in VFH suppress noise from far-field clutter that VFF cannot filter. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B typically carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both alternatives are provided.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-stage architecture** of the Vector Field Histogram (VFH) algorithm. Discuss the role of the **cell weighting function** $m_{i,j}$ in the second stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The VFH algorithm is organized into three sequential stages executed at every control cycle:

**Stage 1 — Grid Update and Windowing:** Sensor readings (sonar, lidar, IR) update the certainty values $\mathrm{CC}_{i,j}$ of cells in a 2D Cartesian occupancy grid. A rectangular **active window** of half-width $W$ is extracted, centered on the robot. The active window size is $(2W+1)^2$ cells. This stage ensures that only *locally relevant* obstacles influence the decision, and it bounds the computational cost of subsequent stages.

**Stage 2 — Construction of the Polar Histogram:**
- For each cell $(i,j)$ within the active window, compute its Euclidean distance $d_{i,j} = \sqrt{(i - i_0)^2 + (j - j_0)^2}$ and angular bearing $\beta_{i,j} = \mathrm{atan2}(j - j_0, i - i_0)$.
- Apply the **cell weighting function**:

$$m_{i,j} = (c^{\star}_{i,j})^{2} (a - b \cdot d_{i,j})$$

- Discretize the bearing into sector index $k_{i,j} = \mathrm{int}(\beta_{i,j} \cdot n / 360°)$ and accumulate the histogram:

$$h_k = \sum_{i,j} m_{i,j} \cdot \mathbb{1}[k_{i,j} = k] \cdot \mathbb{1}[\mathrm{CC}_{i,j} > 0]$$

- Apply 1D smoothing: $h_k^{smooth} = \frac{1}{2l+1} \sum_{j=-l}^{l} h_{k+j}$.

**Role of $m_{i,j}$:** The cell weighting function $m_{i,j}$ models the *physical intuition* that obstacles closer to the robot must dominate the decision. It scales each cell's contribution by an inverse-linear function of distance, so that an obstacle at $d = 0.5$ m contributes roughly **twice** as much as one at $d = 1.0$ m. The squared certainty factor $c^{\star 2}_{i,j}$ further amplifies the influence of *well-evidenced* cells (those updated by many sensor readings). Without $m_{i,j}$, the histogram would treat all cells equally, producing erratic, jittery motion. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** A mobile robot using VFH has a 2D certainty grid with the following **five occupied cells** in its active window (robot at origin):

| Cell | $(x, y)$ in meters | $\mathrm{CC}_{i,j}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(0.0, 0.8)$ | 0.9 |
| 2 | $(0.6, 0.6)$ | 0.85 |
| 3 | $(0.8, 0.0)$ | 0.95 |
| 4 | $(-0.5, 0.5)$ | 0.8 |
| 5 | $(0.0, -0.7)$ | 0.7 |

The robot uses $a = 4$, $b = 3$, $c^{\star} = 1$ for all cells, and $n = 72$ sectors. **Compute the polar histogram magnitudes $h_k$ for the cells listed, and determine which sectors are safe if $\tau = 1.5$.** If the goal is at $\theta_{goal} = 90°$, suggest a suitable steering direction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $d_{i,j}$, $\beta_{i,j}$, and $m_{i,j}$ for each cell:**

For cell 1: $(x,y) = (0.0, 0.8)$

$$d_1 = \sqrt{0^2 + 0.8^2} = 0.8 \text{ m}, \quad \beta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(0.8, 0) = 90°$$

$$m_1 = (1)^2 (4 - 3 \cdot 0.8) = 4 - 2.4 = 1.6$$

For cell 2: $(x,y) = (0.6, 0.6)$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{0.6^2 + 0.6^2} = 0.849 \text{ m}, \quad \beta_2 = \mathrm{atan2}(0.6, 0.6) = 45°$$

$$m_2 = 4 - 3 \cdot 0.849 = 4 - 2.546 = 1.454$$

For cell 3: $(x,y) = (0.8, 0.0)$

$$d_3 = 0.8 \text{ m}, \quad \beta_3 = \mathrm{atan2}(0, 0.8) = 0°$$

$$m_3 = 4 - 3 \cdot 0.8 = 1.6$$

For cell 4: $(x,y) = (-0.5, 0.5)$

$$d_4 = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} = 0.707 \text{ m}, \quad \beta_4 = \mathrm{atan2}(0.5, -0.5) = 135°$$

$$m_4 = 4 - 3 \cdot 0.707 = 4 - 2.121 = 1.879$$

For cell 5: $(x,y) = (0.0, -0.7)$

$$d_5 = 0.7 \text{ m}, \quad \beta_5 = \mathrm{atan2}(-0.7, 0) = -90°$$

$$m_5 = 4 - 3 \cdot 0.7 = 4 - 2.1 = 1.9$$

**Step 2 — Compute sector indices $k_{i,j}$ with $\alpha = 5°$:**

$$
k_1 = \mathrm{int}(90/5) = 18, \quad k_2 = \mathrm{int}(45/5) = 9
$$

$$
k_3 = \mathrm{int}(0/5) = 0, \quad k_4 = \mathrm{int}(135/5) = 27
$$

$$
k_5 = \mathrm{int}(-90/5) = -18 \mod 72 = 54
$$

**Step 3 — Build the polar histogram $h_k$ (before smoothing):**

$$
h_0 = m_3 = 1.6, \quad h_9 = m_2 = 1.454
$$

$$
h_{18} = m_1 = 1.6, \quad h_{27} = m_4 = 1.879
$$

$$
h_{54} = m_5 = 1.9
$$

**Step 4 — Apply threshold $\tau = 1.5$:**

$$
h_0 = 1.6 > 1.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{blocked}
$$

$$
h_9 = 1.454 < 1.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{safe}
$$

$$
h_{18} = 1.6 > 1.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{blocked}
$$

$$
h_{27} = 1.879 > 1.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{blocked}
$$

$$
h_{54} = 1.9 > 1.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{blocked}
$$

**Step 5 — Candidate valleys:** The only safe sector is $k = 9$ (at $\beta = 45°$). It forms a single-sector valley.

**Step 6 — Steering decision:** Goal direction $\theta_{goal} = 90°$ corresponds to sector $k = 18$, which is *blocked*. The closest safe sector to $k = 18$ is $k = 9$ (9 sectors away vs. going the long way around: 63 sectors). Hence:

$$
\theta_{steer} = 9 \times 5° = 45°
$$

The robot steers at **45° (NE direction)** to maintain safety while making best-effort progress toward the goal at 90°. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **limitations of the original VFH algorithm**. How do **VFH+** and **VFH\*** address these limitations? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of vanilla VFH:**

1. **No kinematic awareness:** VFH assumes the robot can instantaneously change heading. In practice, non-holonomic robots (car-like, differential-drive) have minimum turning radii. VFH may command infeasible directions, causing oscillation in narrow spaces.
2. **Local minima / dead-ends:** In symmetric U-shaped or box canyons, the "best valley" may lead away from the goal with no look-ahead to detect the trap.
3. **Threshold sensitivity:** Small changes in $\tau$ cause dramatic behavioral shifts; manual tuning is laborious.
4. **No obstacle motion handling:** Static grid assumption; moving obstacles can cause erratic behavior unless the grid is updated fast enough.
5. **Uniform sector treatment:** Does not account for sector width versus robot width; narrow valleys may be physically passable but algorithmically rejected.

**VFH+ improvements (Ullrich & Akella, 1995):**

- Introduces a **"blocked" sector concept** that incorporates the robot's **physical dimensions and minimum turning radius**.
- Adds a **primary and secondary set of candidate sectors**, accounting for the fact that sectors near a blocked one are also *transitionally* blocked due to robot geometry.
- Considers the **robot's current direction of travel**, not just the target bearing, enabling smooth, kinematically feasible trajectories.

**VFH\* improvements (Ulrich & Borenstein, 1998):**

- Adds a **look-ahead verification** step using the **A\*** search algorithm on the polar histogram to confirm that the selected valley does not lead to a dead-end.
- If a candidate valley is identified as a dead-end, the algorithm re-evaluates alternative valleys.
- Combines the *reactive speed* of VFH with the *deliberative foresight* of A\*, yielding smooth, near-globally-optimal trajectories in cluttered environments.

**Summary table:**

| Algorithm | Kinematics | Look-ahead | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| VFH | No | No | Very low |
| VFH+ | Yes | No | Low |
| VFH* | Yes | Yes (A*) | Moderate |

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the polar histogram magnitude** $h_k$ from the 2D certainty grid. State clearly the role of the **certainty value $\mathrm{CC}_{i,j}$**, the **distance $d_{i,j}$**, and the **indicator function** in the formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the 2D certainty grid $\mathcal{G} = \{(i,j,\mathrm{CC}_{i,j})\}$, the polar histogram magnitude $h_k$ for sector $k$ is:

$$h_k = \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{G}} (c^{\star}_{i,j})^{2} \left( a - b \cdot d_{i,j} \right) \cdot \mathbb{1}\!\left[ k_{i,j} = k \right] \cdot \mathbb{1}\!\left[ \mathrm{CC}_{i,j} > 0 \right]$$

**Role of each term:**

- **$\mathrm{CC}_{i,j}$ (certainty value):** Represents the *evidential strength* of an obstacle at cell $(i,j)$. It grows toward 1 with repeated sensor detections and decays otherwise. The indicator $\mathbb{1}[\mathrm{CC}_{i,j} > 0]$ ensures only *positively-evidenced* cells contribute. Without it, free-space cells (uncertainty = 0) would erroneously add to obstacle density.

- **$d_{i,j}$ (Euclidean distance):** Modulates the influence of an obstacle by proximity. The factor $(a - b \cdot d_{i,j})$ ensures close cells contribute more. The constants $a$ and $b$ are chosen such that $d_{\max} = a/b$ defines the effective sensing radius. The choice of *linear* decay (rather than quadratic) reflects a balance between aggressive avoidance and far-field awareness.

- **$c^{\star}_{i,j}$ (scaled certainty):** Squaring it amplifies the difference between mature and immature cells. A cell with $c^{\star} = 0.9$ contributes $0.81$ times its base weight, while a cell with $c^{\star} = 0.5$ contributes only $0.25$ — suppressing noisy, transient detections.

- **Indicator function $\mathbb{1}[k_{i,j} = k]$:** Performs the **angular binning**. It selects, for each cell, exactly one sector to which its contribution is added. This is the *dimensionality reduction* step — collapsing a 2D distribution into a 1D polar profile.

- **Indicator function $\mathbb{1}[\mathrm{CC}_{i,j} > 0]$:** Filters out free or unknown cells, ensuring $h_k$ represents **obstacle density**, not occupancy. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Traps in VFH Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the certainty grid update step:** Many students describe VFH as *only* the polar histogram construction. Stage 1 (grid update + windowing) carries independent marks.
> 2. **Using $\arctan$ instead of $\mathrm{atan2}$:** This loses quadrant information and is a guaranteed **1–2 mark penalty** in 14-mark derivations.
> 3. **Omitting units:** Threshold $\tau$, cell weights $m_{i,j}$, and histogram magnitudes are dimensionless. Always state this explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing VFH with VFH+ or VFH\*:** If the question says "VFH," do **not** include A\* look-ahead or kinematic constraints unless explicitly asked. Read the question stem carefully.
> 5. **Skipping the smoothing step:** KTU examiners frequently award a separate 1–2 mark line item for "1D smoothing / convolution filter" — don't miss it.
> 6. **Not justifying the choice of $n = 72$:** Always state that $n = 72$ balances angular resolution ($5°$) and computational tractability.
> 7. **Local minima oversimplification:** Do not just say "VFH gets stuck in local minima." Explain *why* — symmetric obstacle configurations cause two equally attractive valleys on either side of the goal, and the algorithm has no look-ahead to choose the correct one.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VFH = 2D grid → 1D polar histogram → thresholding → valley-based steering.** Three stages, executed in real time at every control cycle.
- **The three-stage pipeline** is: (1) Grid Update + Windowing, (2) Polar Histogram Construction, (3) Thresholding + Valley Selection + Steering.
