- **Cell weighting function** $m_{i,j} = (c^{\star}_{i,j})^2 (a - b d_{i,j})$ — closer and more certain cells dominate.
- **Polar histogram** has $n$ sectors (default $n = 72$, $\alpha = 5°$); magnitudes are $h_k = \sum m_{i,j} \cdot \mathbb{1}[k_{i,j} = k] \cdot \mathbb{1}[\mathrm{CC}_{i,j} > 0]$.
- **Threshold $\tau$** is the single most important tuning knob: low $\tau$ = aggressive, high $\tau$ = conservative.
- **Smoothing** with a 1D rolling mean suppresses sensor noise and prevents jitter.
- **Candidate valleys** are maximal contiguous runs of safe sectors.
- **Steering direction** = sector (in best valley) closest to $\theta_{goal}$.
- **Use $\mathrm{atan2}$, not $\arctan$**, when computing cell bearings — quadrant preservation is essential.
- **Limitations of vanilla VFH:** no kinematic awareness, prone to local minima, threshold-sensitive, no look-ahead.
- **VFH+** adds kinematic feasibility; **VFH\*** adds A\* look-ahead verification.
- **Real-world applications:** warehouse AGVs, domestic cleaning robots, disaster-response UGVs, hospital service robots.
- **Original reference (no fabricated edition):** Borenstein, J., & Koren, Y. (1991). *The Vector Field Histogram — Fast Obstacle Avoidance for Mobile Robots*. IEEE Transactions on Robotics and Automation.
- **Exam trick:** If a question asks for a *numerical* polar histogram value, always show the calculation of $d_{i,j}$, $\beta_{i,j}$, and $k_{i,j}$ *first* — KTU examiners award step-marks generously when intermediate steps are shown.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bug 1, Bug 2, and Tangent Bug Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bug 1, Bug 2, and Tangent Bug Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Bug Family of Algorithms — A Foundational Concept in Reactive Path Planning

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bug Algorithms** are a family of simple, sensor-based, reactive (reactive = the robot decides its next action solely based on the currently sensed local environment, without remembering a global map) path-planning algorithms used in mobile robotics to navigate a planar (2D) environment containing unknown or partially known obstacles. A robot, treated as a *point mass* (i.e., a robot whose physical size is treated as zero, with obstacles "grown" by the robot's radius for collision checking), moves from a start configuration to a goal configuration using only two primitives: **motion-to-goal** and **boundary following (wall following)**.

The three principal members studied in the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PBRUT504 – Mobile Robotics (Module 3)** are:

1. **Bug 1** – A naive, exhaustive boundary follower that searches for the *closest leave point*.
2. **Bug 2** – A directionally-aware boundary follower that searches for a leave point on the **m-start-to-goal line (M-line)**.
3. **Tangent Bug** – A sensor-rich, range-based extension that anticipates obstacles using distance readings and switches to boundary following only at an *infinity point* (called the **minimal distance point**, where the distance to the obstacle is smallest before collision).

The **standard performance metric** in KTU board questions is the worst-case path length expressed as a multiple of the shortest path, $L^* = d(s, g) + \sum_i p_i$, where $d(s, g)$ is the straight-line start-to-goal distance and $p_i$ is the perimeter of the $i$-th obstacle encountered.

---

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine walking across a dark, unfamiliar room to reach a lamp on the opposite wall.**

| Real-World Situation | Robotic Primitive |
|---|---|
| You walk straight toward the lamp, eyes forward. | **Motion-to-Goal** |
| You bump your shin on a table. | **Hit Point (H)** — obstacle detected by contact/proximity |
| You put your hand on the table edge and walk around it to find the closest spot where, letting go, you could head toward the lamp. | **Boundary Following** → **Leave Point (L)** (Bug 1 strategy) |
| You put your hand on the table edge and walk around it *in a fixed direction* until you cross the imaginary line from where you started to the lamp. | **Boundary Following** → **M-Line Re-encounter** (Bug 2 strategy) |
| You don't bump anything — instead, you use a flashlight that scans ahead. When the beam grazes the table's edge, you decide *now* to start hugging the table, picking the direction that lets you leave earliest. | **Tangent Bug** — uses a **range sensor** (sonar/laser) and the **continuous M-line** concept |

The "bug" metaphor comes from the earliest robotics literature: a robotic insect that bumps into walls and crawls along them.

---

## 1.2 Assumptions Common to All Three Algorithms

For KTU board questions, the following assumptions are **non-negotiable**:

> [!NOTE]
> - The workspace $W \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ is **bounded** and contains a finite set of obstacles.
> - The robot is a **point** (or the workspace is configuration-space grown).
> - The robot is equipped with a **contact sensor** (or a 1-D range sensor for Tangent Bug) that returns a *Boolean hit* or a *distance value* $d$.
> - The robot has perfect **localization** — it always knows its own position $(x, y)$ and heading $\theta$.
> - The goal is a single point $q_{goal}$ of known coordinates.
> - The robot can measure the **straight-line distance to the goal** at every instant: $\rho = \sqrt{(x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2}$.

---

## 1.3 What the KTU 2024 Examiner Wants You to Know

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Board Concepts:**
> 1. The **state machine** structure of each algorithm (states: *Go-to-Goal* and *Follow-Boundary*).
> 2. The **transition conditions** (when to leave an obstacle).
> 3. The **worst-case path-length bound** for each algorithm.
> 4. A clear sketch of the **trajectory** for a given start–goal–obstacle configuration.
> 5. Differences between **contact-sensor Bug** variants and the **range-sensor Tangent Bug**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bug 1 Algorithm — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Algorithm Description (Decomposed into Bulleted Logic Steps)

**Goal:** Move from start $q_{start}$ to goal $q_{goal}$ in an unknown 2-D environment.

- **Step 1 — Initialize:** Head straight from $q_{start}$ toward $q_{goal}$ (motion-to-goal).
- **Step 2 — Detect Obstacle:** When the contact sensor fires, mark the current position as the **Hit Point** $H_i$ and record the *closest distance to the goal achieved so far* as $d_{\min} = d(q_{start}, q_{goal})$ initially, then updated.
- **Step 3 — Boundary Following (Circumnavigation):** Switch to *follow-boundary* mode. Traverse the entire boundary of the obstacle in a chosen direction (say, counter-clockwise) until the robot returns to $H_i$.
- **Step 4 — Identify Leave Point:** While circumnavigating, continuously record the position $L_i$ on the boundary where the distance $d(q, q_{goal})$ is **minimized**. This is the candidate leave point.
- **Step 5 — Depart:** Resume motion-to-goal from $L_i$ toward $q_{goal}$.
- **Step 6 — Termination:** Stop when the robot reaches $q_{goal}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Bug 1 is *memoryless in the choice of leave point* — it always exhaustively explores the entire obstacle perimeter before deciding where to leave. This is computationally wasteful but guarantees a correct (if suboptimal) solution.

### 2.1.2 Worst-Case Path Length Bound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bug 1 Worst-Case Performance Bound:**
> $$L_{Bug1} \le d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + 1.5 \cdot \sum_{i} p_i$$
> where $p_i$ is the perimeter of the $i$-th obstacle encountered. The constant **1.5** arises because, in the worst case, the robot walks the **entire perimeter** of an obstacle plus returns to the hit point before leaving.

---

## 2.2 Bug 2 Algorithm — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Algorithm Description

- **Step 1 — Define the M-line:** The M-line (Motion line) is the straight line segment connecting $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$. It is computed once and stored.
- **Step 2 — Motion-to-Goal:** Move straight toward $q_{goal}$ along the M-line.
- **Step 3 — Detect Hit:** When the contact sensor fires at point $H_i$, switch to *follow-boundary* mode.
- **Step 4 — Boundary Following with M-line Tracking:** Traverse the obstacle boundary in a **pre-chosen direction** (left-hand or right-hand rule). Record the position whenever the robot's path **re-crosses the M-line** at a point $L_i$ (a *leave point*).
- **Step 5 — Departure Decision:** If the robot reaches $q_{goal}$, stop. If, while boundary-following, the robot **re-encounters $H_i$** (i.e., completes a full loop without finding the goal), then the goal is **unreachable** — declare failure.
- **Step 6 — Resume Motion-to-Goal:** Leave the obstacle at the first qualifying $L_i$ on the M-line and head back toward $q_{goal}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from Bug 1:** Bug 2 leaves an obstacle as soon as the M-line is re-encountered, without doing a global search for the shortest-distance leave point. This is faster on average but can produce longer paths in some geometries.

### 2.2.2 Worst-Case Path Length Bound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bug 2 Worst-Case Performance Bound:**
> $$L_{Bug2} \le d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + \sum_{i} \frac{n_i \cdot p_i}{2}$$
> where $n_i$ is the number of times the M-line intersects the $i$-th obstacle. In the worst case, $n_i$ is unbounded, so Bug 2 has **no finite constant multiplier** for the perimeter term.

---

## 2.3 Tangent Bug Algorithm — Operational Theory

### 2.3.1 The Range-Sensor Enhancement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Tangent Bug algorithm replaces the *binary contact sensor* with a **360° range sensor** (e.g., a laser rangefinder or a ring of sonars). The sensor returns a **discrete set of range readings** $d(\alpha_k)$ at angles $\alpha_k$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$, typically evenly spaced.

### 2.3.2 The Continuous M-line Concept

Instead of a single M-line, Tangent Bug maintains a **continuously updated line** from the **current position** $q$ to $q_{goal}$:

$$M(q) = \{(1-t)\,q + t\,q_{goal} \mid t \in [0, 1]\}$$

This means the M-line *rotates and translates* as the robot moves. This is the central innovation of Tangent Bug.

### 2.3.3 Algorithm Description

- **Step 1 — Motion-to-Goal:** Drive in the direction that **minimizes the distance to the goal**, $\rho = d(q, q_{goal})$, subject to all sensed range readings being greater than a **minimum clearance threshold**.
- **Step 2 — Detect Imminent Collision:** When the minimum range reading $d_{\min} = \min_k d(\alpha_k)$ falls below a threshold, switch to *follow-boundary* mode. The point on the obstacle boundary closest to the robot is called the **minimal distance point**, $O_{\min}$.
- **Step 3 — Boundary Following with Look-Ahead:** As the robot follows the boundary, continuously compute two key values:
    - $d_{\text{reach}}$: the distance from the goal-grazing ray's endpoint (the robot would re-enter motion-to-goal here) to $q_{goal}$, given current sensor data.
    - $d_{\text{followed}}$: the actual distance the robot must travel along the boundary to reach that endpoint.
- **Step 4 — Leave Criterion:** Leave the boundary following mode **as soon as** $d_{\text{reach}} \le d_{\text{followed}}$. Intuitively: if the robot can reach the goal sooner by leaving *now* than by continuing along the boundary, it leaves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Tangent Bug *anticipates* the boundary-following cost. It does not commit to wall-following until it is the locally optimal choice. This is why the algorithm is called "Tangent" — it uses the **tangent directions** to obstacle edges (the directions of the sensor beams at local range minima) to plan.

### 2.3.4 Worst-Case Path Length Bound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tangent Bug Worst-Case Performance Bound:**
> $$L_{TB} \le d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + \sum_{i} \left(p_i + 2 \cdot d_{\text{reactive}, i}\right)$$
> where $d_{\text{reactive}, i}$ is the maximum **reactive distance** for obstacle $i$ — the distance over which the robot's range sensor can see and react to the obstacle. In dense environments with short sensor range, this bound approaches the **Bug 2** bound; in sparse environments, it is nearly optimal.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for the 2-minute revision pass before the exam.**

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Euclidean distance to goal | $\rho$ | $\rho = \sqrt{(x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2}$ | meters (m) | All three |
| Shortest path length | $L^*$ | $L^* = d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + \sum_i p_i$ | m | Benchmark |
| Bug 1 worst case | $L_{B1}$ | $L_{B1} \le d + 1.5 \sum_i p_i$ | m | Bug 1 |
| Bug 2 worst case | $L_{B2}$ | $L_{B2} \le d + \sum_i \tfrac{n_i p_i}{2}$ | m | Bug 2 |
| Tangent Bug worst case | $L_{TB}$ | $L_{TB} \le d + \sum_i (p_i + 2 d_{r,i})$ | m | Tangent Bug |
| M-line equation | $M$ | $(1-t) q_{start} + t \, q_{goal}, \; t \in [0,1]$ | dimensionless | Bug 2, Tangent |
| Tangent heading angle | $\psi$ | $\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(y_g - y, \; x_g - x)$ | radians (rad) | Motion-to-Goal |
| Beam direction angle | $\alpha_k$ | $\alpha_k = \theta + k \cdot \Delta\alpha$ | rad | Tangent Bug |
| Leave criterion | $L_c$ | $L_c: d_{\text{reach}} \le d_{\text{followed}}$ | dimensionless | Tangent Bug |
| Workspace dimension | $W$ | $W \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ | m² | All three |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation key:**
> - $d$ or $d(q, q_{goal})$ — Euclidean distance between two points.
> - $p_i$ — perimeter of the $i$-th obstacle encountered.
> - $n_i$ — number of times the M-line intersects the $i$-th obstacle.
> - $d_{r,i}$ — maximum reactive sensor distance for obstacle $i$.
> - $\Delta\alpha$ — angular resolution of the range sensor (e.g., $1°$ for a 360-beam laser).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where are these algorithms used in industry?**
> - **Autonomous Vacuum Robots (e.g., iRobot Roomba legacy modes):** Simple boundary-following with random exploration is a degenerate Bug variant.
> - **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Bug 2 style "follow the magnetic line until you hit an obstacle, then go around" is the backbone of many legacy logistics systems.
> - **Mars Rovers (NASA JPL):** The Autonomous Navigation (AutoNav) subsystem on **Curiosity** and **Perseverance** uses a *Tangent Bug*-style algorithm as a low-level reactive layer beneath the global path planner, with stereo cameras as the range sensor.
> - **Disaster-Response Robots:** In unknown rubble, the simplest algorithm that *works* is often the Bug 1 algorithm, because it makes the fewest assumptions.
> - **Underwater AUVs (Autonomous Underwater Vehicles):** Multi-beam sonar provides the 360° range scan required for Tangent Bug to perform well.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bug 1 Worst-Case Bound

We will derive the factor **1.5** in the Bug 1 worst-case path length bound from first principles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Let the robot start at $q_{start} = (0, 0)$ and the goal at $q_{goal} = (d, 0)$ along the positive $x$-axis. Suppose there is a single convex obstacle $O$ of perimeter $p$ whose boundary intersects the M-line at two points, $H$ (the hit point) and $F$ (the far intersection).

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Direct path from start to first hit.**
The robot moves along the M-line from $q_{start}$ until it hits the obstacle at $H$. The distance traveled is simply the projection of $\overrightarrow{q_{start} H}$ onto the M-line:

$$L_1 = d(q_{start}, H)$$

In the worst case, $H$ can be anywhere on the obstacle boundary that is encountered by the line of motion.

**Step 2 — Boundary-following distance.**
Bug 1 requires the robot to follow the **entire perimeter** $p$ of the obstacle before deciding on the leave point. Therefore:

$$L_2 = p$$

This is the defining cost of Bug 1 — it is committed to a full circumnavigation.

**Step 3 — Distance from the optimal leave point to the goal.**
After circumnavigation, the robot leaves at the point $L^*$ that minimizes $d(L^*, q_{goal})$. In the worst case, this leave point is at the *furthest* point of the obstacle from the goal, requiring:

$$L_3 = d(L^*, q_{goal})$$

**Step 4 — Total path length.**
Summing the three legs:

$$L_{Bug1} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = d(q_{start}, H) + p + d(L^*, q_{goal})$$

**Step 5 — Worst-case upper bound.**
The shortest path $L^*$ from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$ that *touches* the obstacle is bounded below by:

$$L^* \ge d(q_{start}, q_{goal})$$

Now, we relate $L_1 + L_3$ to the obstacle geometry. By the triangle inequality applied to the path $q_{start} \to H \to L^* \to q_{goal}$:

$$L_1 + d(L^*, q_{goal}) \le d(q_{start}, L^*) + d(L^*, q_{goal}) \le d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + 2 \cdot d_{\max}(O)$$

where $d_{\max}(O)$ is the maximum distance from the obstacle to the line of motion. For a convex obstacle of perimeter $p$, a classical geometric bound gives:

$$d_{\max}(O) \le \frac{p}{4}$$

**Step 6 — Substituting.**
Plugging this in:

$$L_1 + L_3 \le d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + 2 \cdot \frac{p}{4} = d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + \frac{p}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$L_{Bug1} \le d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + \frac{p}{2} + p = d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + 1.5 \, p$$

This confirms the **1.5** factor. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** The derivation is rarely required in full, but the final bound $L_{Bug1} \le d + 1.5 \sum p_i$ is a high-frequency board question. The factor **1.5** comes from the geometric fact that a convex shape's furthest point from a line is at most $p/4$ away.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bug 2 Worst-Case Bound

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Same as above. Bug 2 leaves the obstacle as soon as the M-line is re-encountered.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Departure point identification.**
At hit point $H$, the robot begins boundary following. It travels along the perimeter and re-encounters the M-line at some point $L$. The length of boundary traversed is bounded by the perimeter:

$$L_2 = \text{arc length from } H \text{ to } L \text{ along the boundary} \le p$$

**Step 2 — Counting M-line crossings.**
In the worst case, the M-line may **windingly intersect** the obstacle boundary many times, with $n$ crossings. Bug 2 will exit at the **first** crossing encountered while following the boundary. However, on a complex (non-convex) obstacle, the boundary may loop back, and the robot may traverse a substantial fraction of the perimeter.

**Step 3 — Bounding the boundary traversal.**
The robot traverses at most $n/2$ "loops" of the boundary, each of length up to $p$. The worst-case boundary-following distance is therefore:

$$L_2 \le \frac{n \cdot p}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Total path length.**

$$L_{Bug2} = d(q_{start}, H) + L_2 + d(L, q_{goal})$$

Using the same geometric bounds as in the Bug 1 derivation, we obtain:

$$L_{Bug2} \le d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + \frac{n \cdot p}{2}$$

Summing over multiple obstacles:

$$L_{Bug2} \le d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + \sum_i \frac{n_i \cdot p_i}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Why this is worse than Bug 1 in pathological cases.**
For a single convex obstacle, $n = 2$ crossings, so $L_{Bug2} \le d + p$, which is **better** than Bug 1's $d + 1.5p$. But for a *concave* obstacle shaped like a maze (a "comb" obstacle), $n$ can be arbitrarily large, making Bug 2's bound arbitrarily worse than Bug 1's. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Result:** Bug 1 has a **uniform** 1.5× worst-case bound; Bug 2 is better in the average case but has no uniform bound in the worst case. This is a classic KTU question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Tangent Bug Leave Criterion

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** The robot is in *follow-boundary* mode. The range sensor returns readings $d(\alpha_k)$ at angles $\alpha_k$. The robot is at position $q$ with heading $\theta$.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Identify the goal-grazing ray.**
Among all the range readings, identify the beam with the **smallest** range, $d_{\min} = d(\alpha^*)$. The corresponding point on the obstacle is:

$$O^* = q + d_{\min} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta + \alpha^*) \\ \sin(\theta + \alpha^*) \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the "leave-now" line.**
The current M-line (continuously updated) is $M(q)$. The robot could resume motion-to-goal from $q$ directly. Define:

$$d_{\text{reach}} = d(q, q_{goal}) = \rho$$

**Step 3 — Compute the "follow-then-leave" cost.**
If the robot continues boundary-following until it can leave, the cumulative distance is:

$$d_{\text{followed}} = d_{\text{boundary, current}} + d_{\text{future leave point to goal}}$$

where $d_{\text{boundary, current}}$ is the distance the robot has already followed, and $d_{\text{future leave point to goal}}$ is the projected distance from the next candidate leave point to the goal, estimated using the **current sensor data** by projecting along the boundary.

**Step 4 — Leave criterion.**
The robot leaves the boundary when:

$$d_{\text{reach}} \le d_{\text{followed}}$$

This is the **greedy local optimality** condition: the immediate cost of leaving (which is the distance to the goal from the current position) is no more than the cost of following further. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is elegant:** Tangent Bug never commits to boundary following unless it is locally optimal. Bug 1 and Bug 2 commit to boundary following **at the hit point**, which is a more myopic decision.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most frequently asked KTU comparison question.**

| Feature | Bug 1 | Bug 2 | Tangent Bug |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensor type** | Contact (binary) | Contact (binary) | Range (360° scan) |
| **Boundary-following commitment** | Full perimeter always | Until M-line re-encounter | Until local optimum |
| **Leave point strategy** | Min distance to goal | First M-line re-cross | First $d_{\text{reach}} \le d_{\text{followed}}$ |
| **Memory requirement** | Records $H$ and min-distance point | Records $H$ and M-line | Stores full range scan + history |
| **Worst-case bound** | $d + 1.5 \sum p_i$ | $d + \sum (n_i p_i / 2)$ | $d + \sum (p_i + 2 d_{r,i})$ |
| **Failure detection** | Cannot fail (always terminates) | Can fail (detects M-line loop) | Cannot fail with bounded sensor |
| **Computational cost** | $O(p)$ per obstacle | $O(p)$ per obstacle | $O(N)$ per scan cycle |
| **Best for** | Unknown maze-like env. | Known simple env. | Sensor-rich real robots |
| **Path smoothness** | Jagged (full perimeter) | Smoother | Smoothest (look-ahead) |
| **Practical use** | Educational baseline | Educational, simple AGVs | Mars rovers, modern AUVs |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level State Machine (Common to All Bug Variants)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S_MotionGoal : Initialize at q_start
    S_MotionGoal : MOTION TO GOAL
    S_MotionGoal : Drive toward q_goal
    S_MotionGoal : Update M line continuously
    S_FollowBoundary : FOLLOW BOUNDARY
    S_FollowBoundary : Traverse obstacle perimeter
    S_FollowBoundary : Evaluate leave condition
    S_MotionGoal --> S_FollowBoundary : Hit point detected H_i
    S_FollowBoundary --> S_MotionGoal : Leave point L_i found
    S_MotionGoal --> [*] : Goal reached q_goal
    S_FollowBoundary --> [*] : Goal unreachable
    note right of S_FollowBoundary
      Leave conditions differ:
      Bug 1: minimize d(q, q_goal)
      Bug 2: re-encounter M line
      Tangent: d_reach less or equal d_followed
    end note
```

---

## 4.2 Bug 1 Algorithm — Trajectory Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start at q_start] --> B[Set d_min equal to d q_start q_goal]
    B --> C[Move toward q_goal]
    C --> D{Obstacle detected?}
    D -->|No| C
    D -->|Yes| E[Record hit point H]
    E --> F[Begin boundary following CCW]
    F --> G[Traverse one full perimeter]
    G --> H{Returned to H?}
    H -->|No| F
    H -->|Yes| I[Identify L at min d L q_goal]
    I --> J[Resume motion to goal from L]
    J --> K{Goal reached?}
    K -->|No| C
    K -->|Yes| L[STOP]
```

---

## 4.3 Bug 2 Algorithm — Trajectory Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start at q_start] --> B[Compute M line from q_start to q_goal]
    B --> C[Move along M line toward q_goal]
    C --> D{Obstacle detected?}
    D -->|No| C
    D -->|Yes| E[Record hit point H]
    E --> F[Begin boundary following fixed direction]
    F --> G{Path crosses M line?}
    G -->|No| H{Returned to H without crossing?}
    H -->|No| F
    H -->|Yes| I[DECLARE FAILURE Goal unreachable]
    I --> Z[STOP]
    G -->|Yes| J[Record leave point L on M line]
    J --> K[Resume motion to goal from L]
    K --> L{Goal reached?}
    L -->|No| C
    L -->|Yes| M[STOP]
```

---

## 4.4 Tangent Bug Algorithm — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[360 degree Range Sensor] --> B[Range Scan Buffer d alpha_k]
    B --> C[Min Range Finder]
    C --> D{d_min less than threshold?}
    D -->|No| E[Motion to Goal Controller]
    D -->|Yes| F[Boundary Follower Module]
    E --> G[Robot Actuators]
    F --> H[Compute d_reach and d_followed]
    H --> I{d_reach less or equal d_followed?}
    I -->|No| F
    I -->|Yes| E
    E --> J{Goal reached?}
    J -->|No| B
    J -->|Yes| K[STOP]
```

## 4.6 Conceptual Sketch of Bug 1 Trajectory (Convex Obstacle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q0((q_start)) -->|Motion to Goal| H1((H1 Hit))
    H1 -->|Circumnavigate full perimeter| A((A))

    B --> C((C))
    C --> D((D))
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Format:** Part A questions are 3-mark direct-concept questions, typically answered in **30–60 seconds** with a precise 3–4 sentence response. The model answer below exceeds the typical student answer to demonstrate the depth expected for a full-marks response.

### Question 1

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBRUT504]**  
> *Differentiate between Bug 1 and Bug 2 algorithms based on the leave-point selection strategy.*

**Model Answer:**

Bug 1 and Bug 2 differ fundamentally in **when** and **how** they decide to leave an obstacle.

- **Bug 1** uses an *exhaustive search* strategy: upon hitting an obstacle at point $H$, the robot follows the **entire boundary** of the obstacle and selects the leave point $L$ as the point that **minimizes the distance** $d(L, q_{goal})$ to the goal.
- **Bug 2** uses an *event-driven* strategy: it defines an M-line from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$ and leaves the obstacle at the **first point** where the boundary-following path **re-crosses** this M-line.

As a result, Bug 1 always walks the full perimeter (worst case $d + 1.5 \sum p_i$) while Bug 2 may leave earlier but has no uniform worst-case bound because of pathological concave-obstacle geometries where the M-line intersects the boundary many times.

---

### Question 2

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PBRUT504]**  
> *What is the role of the range sensor in the Tangent Bug algorithm? How does it improve upon the contact-sensor-based Bug variants?*

**Model Answer:**

The range sensor in Tangent Bug provides a **discrete set of distance measurements** $d(\alpha_k)$ over a 360° field of view, enabling the robot to **perceive obstacles before physical contact**. This transforms the algorithm from *reactive* (responding after collision) to *anticipatory* (planning before collision).

The key improvement is the **continuous M-line** concept: as the robot moves, the M-line from its current position to the goal is recomputed at every step. The robot can identify the **minimal distance point** on the obstacle boundary — the tangent direction closest to the goal — and switch to boundary following only when this is the **locally optimal** action, governed by the leave criterion $d_{\text{reach}} \le d_{\text{followed}}$.

In contrast, Bug 1 and Bug 2 commit to boundary following **at the moment of contact**, which is a more myopic decision and can lead to longer paths.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Format:** Part B questions are 14-mark questions with **internal choice** (the student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B). Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, often involving (a) a descriptive/derivation component and (b) a sketch/comparison component.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBRUT504, Module 3]**  
> **(a)** Describe the **Bug 1 algorithm** with the help of a state transition diagram. Clearly define the *hit point* and *leave point* concepts, and state the worst-case path length bound with justification. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A point robot starts at $q_{start} = (0, 0)$ and must reach $q_{goal} = (10, 0)$. There is a single **circular obstacle** of radius $r = 1$ centered at $(5, 0)$. Sketch the **Bug 1 trajectory** and compute the **total path length** traveled by the robot. Use $\pi \approx 3.14$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Bug 1 Algorithm Description:**

The Bug 1 algorithm operates as a **two-mode state machine**:

1. **State 1 — Motion-to-Goal:** The robot drives in a straight line from its current position toward $q_{goal}$. The heading angle is computed as:

$$\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(y_g - y, \, x_g - x)$$

2. **State 2 — Boundary Following:** When the contact sensor fires, the robot switches to this state. It traverses the obstacle boundary in a pre-chosen direction (say, counter-clockwise) and records:
   - **Hit point $H$:** The first contact point with the obstacle.
   - **Leave point $L$:** The point on the boundary that minimizes $d(L, q_{goal})$.

The **transition back to State 1** occurs only after a **full perimeter traversal** has been completed, ensuring that the global minimum-distance point is found.

**Worst-Case Path Length Bound:**

$$L_{Bug1} \le d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + 1.5 \sum_{i} p_i$$

This is derived by noting that the longest leg of the robot's path within an obstacle encounter is the perimeter $p_i$, plus a geometric bound on the start-hit and leave-goal distances of $p_i / 2$ each.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Sketch of Trajectory:**

The robot starts at $(0,0)$ and moves along the $x$-axis toward $(10, 0)$. It hits the circular obstacle at the first point of intersection, $H_1 = (4, 0)$, since the circle of radius 1 is centered at $(5, 0)$ and spans $x \in [4, 6]$.

It then follows the boundary **counter-clockwise** through angles $\theta = \pi$ (the hit point) all the way around to $\theta = \pi$ again (back to $H_1$). It then identifies the point on the circle closest to $q_{goal} = (10, 0)$, which is $L_1 = (6, 0)$ (the rightmost point of the circle), and departs.

**Path Length Calculation:**

**Leg 1 — From $q_{start}$ to $H_1$:**

$$L_1 = d((0,0), (4,0)) = 4 \, \text{m}$$

**Leg 2 — Full perimeter traversal:**

$$L_2 = 2\pi r = 2 \times 3.14 \times 1 = 6.28 \, \text{m}$$

**Leg 3 — From $L_1$ to $q_{goal}$:**

$$L_3 = d((6, 0), (10, 0)) = 4 \, \text{m}$$

**Total Path Length:**

$$L_{total} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 4 + 6.28 + 4 = 14.28 \, \text{m}$$

**Verification of the bound:**

$$L_{total} = 14.28 \le d + 1.5 p = 10 + 1.5 \times 6.28 = 10 + 9.42 = 19.42 \, \text{m} \quad \checkmark$$

The bound holds, and the actual path is 14.28 m versus the shortest possible path of 10 m, giving an inefficiency ratio of **1.428**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PBRUT504, Module 3]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **Tangent Bug algorithm** in detail. Define the *continuous M-line*, the *minimal distance point*, and the leave criterion $d_{\text{reach}} \le d_{\text{followed}}$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **Bug 1, Bug 2, and Tangent Bug** algorithms in a tabular form with respect to **at least six parameters** including sensor type, leave-point strategy, worst-case path length, memory, computational cost, and practical applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Tangent Bug Algorithm — Detailed Explanation:**

Tangent Bug is a **range-sensor-based reactive planner** that improves upon the contact-sensor Bug variants by using a 360° range scan to *anticipate* obstacle encounters.

**Key Definitions:**

1. **Continuous M-line $M(q)$:** Unlike Bug 2's static M-line, Tangent Bug recomputes the line from the *current* position $q$ to $q_{goal}$ at every time step:

$$M(q) = \{(1-t) q + t \, q_{goal} \mid t \in [0, 1]\}$$

2. **Minimal distance point $O_{\min}$:** The point on the obstacle boundary closest to the robot's current position, identified by the sensor beam with the **smallest range reading**:

$$O_{\min} = q + d_{\min} \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\theta + \alpha^*) \\ \sin(\theta + \alpha^*) \end{pmatrix}$$

where $d_{\min} = \min_k d(\alpha_k)$ and $\alpha^*$ is the corresponding beam angle.

3. **Leave criterion $d_{\text{reach}} \le d_{\text{followed}}$:** The robot compares the cost of leaving the boundary *now* (which is the Euclidean distance $d_{\text{reach}} = d(q, q_{goal})$) with the cost of *continuing* boundary following (which is $d_{\text{followed}}$, the projected total path length). The boundary is abandoned as soon as leaving becomes the locally optimal action.

**Algorithmic Flow:**

- **Phase 1 — Motion-to-Goal:** Drive in the direction of $q_{goal}$ until the minimum range reading falls below a threshold $d_{\text{reactive}}$.
- **Phase 2 — Boundary Following with Look-Ahead:** Follow the obstacle boundary while continuously evaluating the leave criterion.
- **Phase 3 — Departure:** Resume motion-to-goal when $d_{\text{reach}} \le d_{\text{followed}}$.

**Worst-Case Bound:**

$$L_{TB} \le d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) + \sum_{i} (p_i + 2 d_{r,i})$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table (6+ parameters):**

| Parameter | Bug 1 | Bug 2 | Tangent Bug |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensor type** | Contact (binary) | Contact (binary) | Range (360° scan) |
| **Leave-point strategy** | Global min-distance after full perimeter | First M-line re-encounter | Local $d_{\text{reach}} \le d_{\text{followed}}$ |
| **Worst-case path length** | $d + 1.5 \sum p_i$ | $d + \sum (n_i p_i / 2)$ | $d + \sum (p_i + 2 d_{r,i})$ |
| **Memory requirement** | $H$ and min-distance point | $H$ and M-line | Full range scan + history |
| **Computational cost** | $O(p)$ per obstacle | $O(p)$ per obstacle | $O(N)$ per scan cycle |
| **Practical applications** | Educational baseline, simple maze robots | Legacy AGV systems, warehouse robots | Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance), modern AUVs |
| **Failure detection** | Cannot fail | Detects M-line loop | Cannot fail with bounded sensor |
| **Boundary-following commitment** | Full perimeter always | Until M-line crossed | Until locally optimal |

**Concluding Remarks:**

Tangent Bug represents the **theoretical apex** of the Bug family under idealized conditions, but it requires **expensive sensors** (laser rangefinders, multi-beam sonar). Bug 2 offers the best **practical trade-off** for legacy systems with only contact sensors. Bug 1 remains relevant for **low-cost, low-computation** platforms.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "1.5" factor in Bug 1's bound:** Students often write $L_{Bug1} \le d + \sum p_i$, which is the *shortest* path with obstacles, not the *worst-case* path. The correct bound is $L_{Bug1} \le d + 1.5 \sum p_i$. *(Lose 2 marks if forgotten.)*
>
> 2. **Confusing "leave point" with "goal":** The leave point $L$ is on the *obstacle boundary*, not at the goal. Many students sketch trajectories where the robot teleports from the boundary to the goal. Always show the *second motion-to-goal leg* explicitly.
>
> 3. **Claiming Bug 2 "always outperforms Bug 1":** This is false. Bug 2 outperforms Bug 1 on **average** and on **convex** obstacles, but Bug 1 has a **uniform 1.5×** worst-case bound while Bug 2 has **no uniform bound** in the worst case (concave "comb" obstacles). *(Lose 1–2 marks for overgeneralization.)*
>
> 4. **Drawing the M-line as static in Tangent Bug:** The M-line in Tangent Bug is **continuously updated** as the robot moves — this is a key innovation. Drawing it as a single line from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$ is the **Bug 2** M-line, not Tangent Bug's. *(Lose 1 mark for this conceptual error.)*
>
> 5. **Forgetting the leave criterion in Tangent Bug:** Writing "the robot follows the boundary until it can leave" without specifying the $d_{\text{reach}} \le d_{\text{followed}}$ criterion is **incomplete**. The criterion is the heart of the algorithm.
>
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Always state path lengths in **meters (m)** and angles in **radians (rad)** or **degrees (°)** consistently throughout your answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

### Core Concepts
- [x] Bug algorithms are **reactive** (decision based on local sensing) **path planners** for **unknown 2D environments**.
- [x] The robot is treated as a **point mass** in a configuration space grown by the robot's radius.
- [x] All Bug algorithms have **two modes**: **Motion-to-Goal** and **Boundary Following**.

### Bug 1
- [x] Sensor: **Contact** (binary).
- [x] Strategy: Follow the **full perimeter** of the obstacle, then leave at the point **closest to the goal**.
- [x] Worst-case bound: $L_{Bug1} \le d + 1.5 \sum p_i$.
- [x] Cannot fail; always terminates.
- [x] High cost on large obstacles.

### Bug 2
- [x] Sensor: **Contact** (binary).
- [x] **M-line**: static line from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.
- [x] Strategy: Follow the boundary in a **fixed direction** until the M-line is **re-crossed**; leave at the first re-crossing.
- [x] Worst-case bound: $L_{Bug2} \le d + \sum (n_i p_i / 2)$ — **no uniform multiplier**.
- [x] **Can fail** (declares goal unreachable) if the M-line loops back to $H$ without re-crossing.
- [x] Better than Bug 1 for convex obstacles; worse for pathological concave geometries.

### Tangent Bug
- [x] Sensor: **Range** (360° scan, $N$ beams at angles $\alpha_k$).
- [x] **Continuous M-line** $M(q)$: recomputed at every step.
- [x] **Minimal distance point** $O_{\min}$: identified by the sensor beam with the smallest range.
- [x] **Leave criterion**: $d_{\text{reach}} \le d_{\text{followed}}$.
- [x] Worst-case bound: $L_{TB} \le d + \sum (p_i + 2 d_{r,i})$.
- [x] Used in **NASA Mars rovers** and modern AUVs.

### Quick-Fire Comparison Mnemonics
- [x] **Bug 1** = "**S**earch **E**ntire **B**oundary" (S-E-B).
- [x] **Bug 2** = "**M**-line **C**rossing **R**elease" (M-C-R).
- [x] **Tangent Bug** = "**L**ocal **O**ptimality **O**n **R**ange" (L-O-O-R).

### Board-Question Triggers
- [x] "**State** the worst-case path length bound" → write the formula and the **derivation logic** (1.5 from $p/2 + p$).
- [x] "**Sketch** the trajectory" → always label $q_{start}$, $q_{goal}$, **all hit points $H_i$**, **all leave points $L_i$**, and the **M-line** (if applicable).
- [x] "**Compare** the three algorithms" → use a **6+ row table**; do not write a paragraph.
- [x] "**Differentiate** Bug 1 and Bug 2" → emphasize the **leave-point strategy** (full perimeter vs. M-line re-crossing).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Path Planning - RPP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBRUT504 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Path Planning - RPP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Randomized Path Planner (RPP)** is a probabilistically complete, single-query motion planning algorithm introduced by Barraquand and Latombe (1991) that hybridizes a classical **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method with **randomized exploration** to escape local minima. RPP operates in the *configuration space* (C-space) of the robot and uses a two-tier strategy: a deterministic gradient descent of an attractive/repulsive potential field for greedy local motion, combined with **random walks** executed whenever the robot becomes trapped in a local minimum of the potential function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *Probabilistic Path Planning refers to a class of motion planning algorithms that use random sampling in the configuration space to find a collision-free path from a start configuration to a goal configuration. RPP is the canonical example, which augments potential field-based greedy descent with randomized escapes from local minima, providing probabilistic completeness rather than deterministic completeness.*

### 1.2 Related Algorithmic Variants (KTU Module 3 Context)

- **PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap Method)** – multi-query, builds a graph then queries
- **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)** – incremental single-query, tree-based
- **RPP (Randomized Path Planner)** – potential-field-guided single-query planner

> [!NOTE]
> RPP is **historically the first** practical probabilistic motion planner and the conceptual ancestor of modern sampling-based methods like RRT and EST (Expansive Space Trees).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a hiker trying to walk down a mountain in dense fog toward a known valley (the goal) while avoiding cliffs (obstacles). The hiker can always feel the local slope and walks downhill (**gradient descent on a potential field**). However, what if the hiker reaches a small pond on the mountainside that is *lower than the surrounding terrain but not the actual goal*? — this is a **local minimum**. 

The hiker's smart strategy is: when stuck, do a **random walk** in some direction for a random distance, then resume walking downhill. If the random walk reaches a new position where the slope leads toward the true goal, the hiker has successfully "escaped" the local minimum and resumes the descent.

- **Mountain slope = Negative gradient of the attractive potential** pulling the robot toward the goal
- **Cliffs = Repulsive potential** from obstacles
- **Pond = Local minimum** (a configuration where net force = 0 but goal not reached)
- **Random walk in fog = Brownian-motion-like randomized escape**
- **Goal valley = Goal configuration** $q_{goal}$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** RPP trades deterministic completeness for **probabilistic completeness** — given enough random escapes and time, the probability of *not* finding a path (if one exists) approaches **zero**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- The **convergence rate** of RPP depends on the **dimension** $n$ of C-space and is exponential: $P(\text{success after } k \text{ trials}) = 1 - (1-p)^k$, where $p$ is the per-trial success probability.
- Standard parameters: step size $\Delta$, random-walk length $L_{rw}$, maximum number of random walks $N_{max}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition: The Two-Layer RPP Architecture

RPP's algorithm consists of **two interleaved layers**:

#### Layer A — Deterministic Descent (Potential Field)
1. Compute total potential $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$ at current configuration $q$.
2. Take a small step in the direction of the **negative gradient** $-\nabla U(q)$.
3. If the step is collision-free, accept; else backtrack.
4. Repeat until either the goal is reached or a **local minimum** is detected (i.e., $\Vert \nabla U(q) \Vert \leq \varepsilon$ for a small threshold $\varepsilon$).

#### Layer B — Randomized Escape (Random Walk)
1. When a local minimum is detected, perform a **random walk** of length $L_{rw}$ in a random direction.
2. If the random walk ends in a region where descent is possible (i.e., the gradient is non-zero and points toward the goal), resume Layer A.
3. If still stuck, perform **backtracking** to the previous milestone and try a different random direction.
4. Terminate after $N_{max}$ random walks or when goal is reached.

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of the Potential Field

**Attractive Potential** (typically quadratic near the goal, conic far away):

$$
U_{att}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} \, \xi \, \rho_{goal}^{2}(q) & \text{if } \rho_{goal}(q) \leq \rho_0 \\[2mm] \xi \, \rho_0 \, \rho_{goal}(q) - \dfrac{1}{2} \, \xi \, \rho_0^{2} & \text{if } \rho_{goal}(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

where:
- $\xi > 0$ is the **attractive gain**
- $\rho_{goal}(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$ is the Euclidean distance to the goal
- $\rho_0$ is the threshold distance for switching between quadratic and conic forms

The attractive force is:

$$
F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = \begin{cases} -\xi (q - q_{goal}) & \text{if } \rho_{goal}(q) \leq \rho_0 \\[2mm] -\xi \rho_0 \dfrac{q - q_{goal}}{\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert} & \text{if } \rho_{goal}(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

**Repulsive Potential** (from obstacles):

$$
U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} \, \eta \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2} & \text{if } \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\[2mm] 0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

where:
- $\eta > 0$ is the **repulsive gain**
- $\rho(q)$ is the minimum distance from $q$ to any obstacle
- $\rho_0$ is the **influence distance** of the obstacle

### 2.3 Why Local Minima Occur — The 'Why' Behind RPP

In a smooth potential field, the robot follows $\dot{q} = -\nabla U(q)$. At a **critical point**, $\nabla U(q) = 0$. This includes:
- The global minimum (goal) — desirable
- **Local minima** — undesirable traps
- **Saddle points** — unstable; rare in 2-D but common in higher dimensions

The deterministic gradient descent **cannot escape** local minima without perturbation. This is the central motivation for the randomized component.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield RPP Formula Reference Table** (KEE 2024 Scheme Board Exam)

| # | Concept | Equation / Expression | Key Parameters | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Potential | $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i} U_{rep}^{(i)}(q)$ | $\xi, \eta, \rho_0$ | Scalar energy; $\dim = [Energy]$ |
| 2 | Attractive (quadratic) | $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}\xi \rho_{goal}^{2}$ | $\xi$ = gain | Valid for $\rho_{goal} \leq \rho_0$ |
| 3 | Attractive (conic) | $U_{att} = \xi \rho_0 \rho_{goal} - \tfrac{1}{2}\xi \rho_0^{2}$ | $\rho_0$ = threshold | Valid for $\rho_{goal} > \rho_0$ |
| 4 | Repulsive | $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}$ | $\eta$ = gain | Zero outside $\rho_0$ neighborhood |
| 5 | Gradient Descent Step | $q_{k+1} = q_k - \alpha \nabla U(q_k)$ | $\alpha$ = step size | Discrete Euler integration |
| 6 | Random Walk Step | $q' = q + \Delta \cdot \vec{v}$ | $\vec{v} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ or uniform on $S^{n-1}$ | $\Delta$ = step length |
| 7 | Probabilistic Completeness | $P(\text{success in } k \text{ trials}) = 1 - (1-p)^{k}$ | $p$ = per-trial success | Approaches 1 as $k \to \infty$ |
| 8 | Local Minima Condition | $\Vert \nabla U(q_{min}) \Vert \leq \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon$ > 0 small | Triggers random walk |
| 9 | C-space Dimension | $n = $ DOF of robot | Typically 2 to 6 | Curse of dimensionality |
| 10 | Backtrack Threshold | $N_{back}$ = max backtrack count | Integer | Prevents infinite loops |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exam answers, always state the **dimension** $n$ of C-space explicitly. Most board exam questions are for $n = 2$ (point robot in 2-D) or $n = 3$ (3-D point) for tractability.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial manipulators** in cluttered work-cells (welding, painting) — modern descendants (RRT*, PRM*) are used.
- **Autonomous vehicles** in dynamic parking lots — RPP-style planners are precursors to RRT-based motion planners.
- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** — Amazon Kiva systems and similar use sampling-based planners.
- **Surgical robotics** — needle steering in tissue uses potential field + RRT hybrid methods.
- **Drone swarms** in GPS-denied environments — local APF + global RPP-style escape.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gradient of the Attractive Potential

We derive $\nabla U_{att}(q)$ in the **quadratic region** ($\rho_{goal}(q) \leq \rho_0$).

**Starting point:** the attractive potential in the quadratic region is defined as:

$$
U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, \xi \, \rho_{goal}^{2}(q)
$$

**Step 1:** Express the distance function in expanded form. Let $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and $q_{goal} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. Then:

$$
\rho_{goal}^{2}(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2} = (q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal})
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute back into the potential:

$$
U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, \xi \, (q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal})
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the gradient operator $\nabla_{q}$ using the standard matrix-calculus identity $\nabla_{x}(x^T A x) = (A + A^T)x$. For scalar case with $A = I$:

$$
\nabla U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, \xi \, (I + I^T)(q - q_{goal}) = \xi \, (q - q_{goal})
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore the **attractive force** is:

$$
F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\xi \, (q - q_{goal})
$$

This is a **linear restoring force** that pulls the robot toward $q_{goal}$, with magnitude proportional to the distance. The constant of proportionality is the **attractive gain** $\xi$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Gradient of the Repulsive Potential

We derive $\nabla U_{rep}(q)$ inside the obstacle's influence region ($\rho(q) \leq \rho_0$).

**Starting point:** the repulsive potential is:

$$
U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}
$$

**Step 1:** Define an auxiliary function for clarity:

$$
u(q) = \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}
$$

So that $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta \, u^{2}(q)$.

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule:

$$
\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \eta \, u(q) \cdot \nabla u(q)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\nabla u(q)$. Note that $\rho_0$ is a constant, so:

$$
\nabla u(q) = \nabla \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} \right) = -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \nabla \rho(q)
$$

**Step 4:** For a point robot at $q$ and an obstacle point $p_{obs}$ (closest obstacle point), the distance gradient is:

$$
\nabla \rho(q) = \frac{q - p_{obs}}{\Vert q - p_{obs} \Vert} = \frac{q - p_{obs}}{\rho(q)}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$
\nabla u(q) = -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \cdot \frac{q - p_{obs}}{\rho(q)} = -\frac{q - p_{obs}}{\rho^{3}(q)}
$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$
\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \left( -\frac{q - p_{obs}}{\rho^{3}(q)} \right)
$$

**Step 7:** The **repulsive force** is:

$$
F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{q - p_{obs}}{\rho^{3}(q)}
$$

This force points **away from the obstacle** (along $q - p_{obs}$) and grows without bound as $\rho(q) \to 0$, which physically prevents the robot from penetrating the obstacle.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Probabilistic Completeness Bound

**Claim:** RPP is probabilistically complete.

**Setup:** Let $A$ be the event "RPP fails to find a path in $k$ random walks" and let $p$ be the (constant) per-walk probability that a random walk leads to a new region from which the goal is reachable. We assume each walk is independent (a reasonable approximation in open C-space regions).

**Step 1:** Probability of **failure** in a single walk:

$$
P(\text{single walk fails}) = 1 - p
$$

**Step 2:** Probability of failure in $k$ independent walks:

$$
P(A) = (1-p)^{k}
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore probability of **success** in at most $k$ walks:

$$
P(\text{success}) = 1 - (1-p)^{k}
$$

**Step 4:** Take the limit as $k \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{k \to \infty} P(\text{success}) = 1 - \lim_{k \to \infty}(1-p)^{k} = 1 - 0 = 1
$$

**Step 5:** Since the bound is **independent of the start configuration** and depends only on the existence of a free path (which guarantees $p > 0$ in non-degenerate problems), RPP is **probabilistically complete**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Probabilistic completeness is a **weaker** guarantee than completeness, but **stronger** than heuristic methods. Modern descendants (PRM*, RRT*) provide **asymptotic optimality** in addition to completeness.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (RPP vs Other Planners)

| Property | RPP | PRM | RRT | APF (pure) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Completeness** | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | None (local minima) |
| **Optimality** | No | Asymptotic (PRM*) | Asymptotic (RRT*) | No |
| **Single/Multi-query** | Single | Multi | Single | Single |
| **Local Minima Handling** | Random walks | N/A (graph) | N/A (tree) | None |
| **Speed** | Fast in low-dim | Medium | Fast | Very fast |
| **Memory** | Low (milestones) | High (graph) | Medium (tree) | Very low |
| **Dimensionality Limit** | ~4-5 | ~7 | ~7+ | ~3 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RPP Algorithm Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: q_start, q_goal] --> B[Compute U_att and U_rep]
    B --> C[Set q_current = q_start]
    C --> D{q at goal?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Return Path - SUCCESS]
    D -- No --> F[Gradient Descent Step]
    F --> G{Step collision-free?}
    G -- No --> H[Backtrack and reduce step]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[Update q_current]
    I --> J{Is q at local minimum?}
    J -- No --> D
    J -- Yes --> K[Perform Random Walk]
    K --> L{New position collision-free?}
    L -- No --> M[Backtrack to previous milestone]
    M --> K
    L -- Yes --> N[Reset descent from new q]
    N --> O{Max random walks exceeded?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Return FAILURE]
    O -- No --> D
```

### 4.2 Potential Field Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Input Layer"]
        S1[Start Config q_s]
        G1[Goal Config q_g]
        O1[Obstacle Map]
    end

    subgraph Potential["Potential Field Computation"]
        PA[Attractive Potential U_att]
        PR[Repulsive Potential U_rep]
    end

    subgraph Total["Total Potential"]
        SUM[U_total = U_att + U_rep]
        GRAD[Compute Gradient nabla U]
    end

    subgraph Motion["Motion Generation"]
        GD[Gradient Descent Step]
        RW[Random Walk Module]
    end

    S1 --> PA
    G1 --> PA
    O1 --> PR
    PA --> SUM
    PR --> SUM
    SUM --> GRAD
    GRAD --> GD
    GD --> RW
    RW --> GD
```

### 4.3 State Machine for RPP Planner

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DESCEND
    DESCEND --> DESCEND : step accepted, not at min
    DESCEND --> SUCCESS : q reaches goal
    DESCEND --> RANDOM_WALK : local min detected
    RANDOM_WALK --> DESCEND : escape to new region
    RANDOM_WALK --> BACKTRACK : collision during walk
    BACKTRACK --> RANDOM_WALK : try new direction
    RANDOM_WALK --> FAILURE : max walks exceeded
    SUCCESS --> [*]
    FAILURE --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["High Level: Task Planner"]
```
