# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Define **untethered communication** in the context of mobile robotics. List **any four advantages** it offers over tethered (wired) communication.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Untethered communication refers to the wireless exchange of data and control signals between a mobile robot and remote entities (operator, base station, or other robots) using electromagnetic or acoustic waves, without any physical cable connection.
>
> **Advantages (0.5 each, any four):**
> 1. **Mobility:** The robot is not constrained by cable length, enabling free navigation.
> 2. **Extended Range:** Communication can span meters to thousands of kilometers.
> 3. **Safety:** Essential for operating in hazardous environments (radiation, fire, bomb disposal).
> 4. **Multi-Robot Coordination:** Enables swarm-based cooperation and data sharing.
> 5. **No Cable Wear:** Eliminates mechanical fatigue and tangling issues.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Differentiate between **Radio Frequency (RF)** and **Infrared (IR)** communication in mobile robotics. State **one limitation** of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | RF Communication | IR Communication |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier | EM waves (3 kHz – 300 GHz) | Near-IR light (300 GHz – 400 THz) |
| Line-of-Sight | Not required | **Required** |
| Range | Up to km (LoRa: 15 km) | < 10 m |
| Interference | Susceptible to EMI | Immune to EMI, but blocked by obstacles |

> **Limitation of RF:** Susceptible to electromagnetic interference and security sniffing.
> **Limitation of IR:** Requires strict line-of-sight and is blocked by opaque obstacles.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **confuse Wi-Fi and Bluetooth frequencies** — both operate at 2.4 GHz, but Wi-Fi has much higher bandwidth.
> - When asked to "compare," students list features but **forget to state which is preferred for the given application** — this costs 2–3 marks.
> - Numerical problems on the **Friis equation** require careful unit conversion; $f$ must be in **Hz**, not **GHz**, otherwise the answer will be wrong by **9 orders of magnitude**.
> - Always draw a **labeled block diagram** when explaining any communication system — it secures 1–2 extra marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **functional architecture of an untethered communication subsystem** in a mobile robot. Clearly identify the roles of the **sensor layer, processing layer, transceiver stack, and external entities**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **Friis Free-Space Transmission Equation**. A mobile robot receives a signal at **−70 dBm** from a base station. If the transmitted power is **1 W**, the transmit antenna gain is **10 dBi**, the receive antenna gain is **5 dBi**, and the operating frequency is **915 MHz**, find the **maximum operating distance** of the link. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the subsystem.

> An untethered communication subsystem in a mobile robot is a multi-layered architecture that converts onboard sensor data into wireless signals and transmits them to remote entities such as base stations, cloud servers, or peer robots.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Identify the four layers.

1. **Sensor Layer:** LIDAR, Cameras, IMU, GPS, ultrasonic sensors — generates raw data.
2. **Processing Layer:** MCU/SoC performs data fusion, packet encapsulation, encryption, and protocol formatting.
3. **Transceiver Stack:** Protocol stack (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, 5G) drives the RF modem and antenna to convert digital packets into EM waves.
4. **External Entities:** Operators, cloud servers, other robots — receive data and send back control commands.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Explain bidirectional flow.

> Data flows **upstream** (robot → operator) and control commands flow **downstream** (operator → robot). The transceiver must support **full-duplex** or **half-duplex** operation.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Draw and label a clean block diagram (similar to Section 4.2 above). Mark arrows indicating direction of data flow.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Derivation of Friis equation (4 marks)**

Start with the isotropic power flux density at distance $d$:

$$
\text{PFD} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

Multiply by the effective aperture of the receiving antenna $A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}
$$

Substitute $\lambda = c / f$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{c}{4\pi d f}\right)^2\,}
$$

**Step 2: Numerical solution (3 marks)**

Given:
- $P_r = -70$ dBm $= 10^{-7}$ W
- $P_t = 1$ W
- $G_t = 10$ dBi $\Rightarrow 10$ linear
- $G_r = 5$ dBi $\Rightarrow 3.162$ linear
- $f = 915$ MHz $= 9.15 \times 10^8$ Hz
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Rearranging Friis for distance $d$:**

$$
d = \frac{c}{4\pi f} \sqrt{\frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r}{P_r}}
$$

**Substitute values:**

$$
\frac{c}{4\pi f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4\pi \times 9.15 \times 10^8} = 0.0261 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\frac{1 \times 10 \times 3.162}{10^{-7}}} = \sqrt{3.162 \times 10^8} = 17{,}780.9
$$

**Final Calculation:**

$$
d = 0.0261 \times 17{,}780.9 \approx 464.1 \text{ m}
$$

> **Answer:** The maximum operating distance is approximately **464 m** (in free space).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** Classify the **different types of untethered communication** used in mobile robotics. Briefly describe any **three types** with their operating frequency, typical range, and one robotic application each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A swarm of **20 agricultural robots** must coordinate over a **2 km × 2 km farmland**. They need to exchange only **small status packets (≤ 50 bytes)** every **10 seconds**, with battery life being critical. Recommend the **most suitable untethered communication technology** and justify your choice with **at least four reasons**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Classification heading.

> Untethered communication can be classified into:
> 1. Radio Frequency (RF) — Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa, Cellular, Satellite
> 2. Optical — Infrared, Laser/FSO
> 3. Acoustic — Ultrasonic
> 4. Inductive — NFC, RFID

**Step 2 (6 marks — 2 marks each for any three):**

**Type 1 — Wi-Fi (RF)**
- Frequency: **2.4 GHz / 5 GHz**
- Range: ~100 m indoor
- Robot Application: Indoor service robots, warehouse AGVs

**Type 2 — LoRa (RF)**
- Frequency: **868 / 915 MHz** (sub-GHz ISM)
- Range: up to **15 km** (line-of-sight)
- Robot Application: Outdoor farm robots, environmental monitoring

**Type 3 — Infrared (Optical)**
- Frequency: **300 GHz – 400 THz**
- Range: < 10 m, requires LOS
- Robot Application: TV remote-controlled consumer robots, docking alignment

**Type 4 — Ultrasonic (Acoustic)** *(if chosen)*
- Frequency: **20 kHz – 1 MHz** acoustic
- Range: ~10 m air, km-scale water
- Robot Application: Underwater AUVs, ROVs

**Type 5 — Satellite** *(if chosen)*
- Frequency: **L/S/Ku/Ka bands**
- Range: Global
- Robot Application: UAVs, planetary exploration rovers

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the requirement profile.

- Coverage area: **4 km²** (2 km × 2 km)
- Payload: **≤ 50 bytes** (very small packets)
- Update rate: every **10 seconds** (low duty cycle)
- Priority: **battery life**

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the chosen technology.

> **Recommended Technology: LoRa (Long Range) in the sub-GHz ISM band (e.g., 868 MHz or 915 MHz).**

**Step 3 (4 marks — justification with at least four reasons):**

1. **Range Suitability:** LoRa provides **up to 15 km** range in rural/outdoor environments, easily covering the **2 km × 2 km** farm from a single gateway.
2. **Ultra-Low Power Consumption:** LoRa transceivers consume only **~10–50 mA** during transmission, enabling multi-month battery operation. Critical for solar-powered farm robots.
3. **Low Data Rate Compatibility:** The required **50 bytes / 10 s = 5 bytes/s** is well within LoRa's **0.3 to 50 kbps** data rate envelope. Bandwidth is not wasted.
4. **Robustness to Interference:** LoRa uses **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** modulation, providing immunity to multipath fading common in farmland with crops and trees.
5. **Mesh & Scalability:** Supports **star-of-stars topology** with thousands of nodes, perfect for 20-robot swarms and future expansion.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Concluding statement.

> Hence, **LoRa is the optimal untethered communication technology** for this agricultural swarm robotics application, offering the best trade-off between range, power efficiency, cost, and payload suitability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Untethered = Wireless = No physical cable** between the robot and the external system.
- ✅ **Seven major types**: RF, Optical (IR/Laser), Acoustic (Ultrasonic), Cellular, Satellite, NFC, RFID.
- ✅ **RF is the most common**, with sub-types Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa.
- ✅ **Friis Equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ — memorized in linear and dB form.
- ✅ **Path loss** $\propto d^2$ (free space) or $\propto d^4$ (two-ray ground).
- ✅ **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c/f$ — higher frequency → smaller antenna, more attenuation.
- ✅ **LoRa** = best for low-power, long-range, low-data-rate outdoor robots.
- ✅ **Wi-Fi 5 GHz** = best for high-bandwidth indoor service robots.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic** = mandatory for underwater AUVs/ROVs.
- ✅ **Satellite** = mandatory for global UAV and planetary rover communication.
- ✅ **IR** = requires Line-of-Sight; blocked by walls.
- ✅ **NFC** = range < 10 cm; used for docking, payload ID, payment.
- ✅ **5G latency** < 1 ms enables **tele-surgery** and **real-time V2X**.
- ✅ **Shannon Capacity** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — the theoretical max data rate.
- ✅ **Constant to memorize:** $20 \log_{10}(4\pi/c) = -147.55$ dB (for FSPL).
- ✅ **Speed of EM wave** in vacuum: $3 \times 10^8$ m/s; in fiber ~ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **Speed of sound** in air: 343 m/s; in water: 1500 m/s.
- ✅ **Exam Mantra:** Always pair any communication type with its **(Frequency, Range, Data Rate, Robot Use Case)** tuple.
